label: VP Operations
content: VP Operations in Executive Leadership. Responsible for overall manufacturing operations, capacity planning, and strategic make-buy decisions. In multi-shift, high-mix environments with variable lead times: review capacity-constraint escalations from Production Scheduler (queue backlog, shift starvation signals); approve investments in process improvements, equipment, or outsourcing to reduce lead-time variability. Oversee Kanban system performance through quarterly management reviews; monitor service-level achievement (target 95%+), inventory-turn improvement, and stockout trends. Approve demand-tier adjustments for seasonal demand swings; ensure supply-chain and manufacturing teams have resources (space, tooling, supplier contracts) to support Kanban pull signals.
summary: Role: VP Operations in Executive Leadership

label: Manufacturing capacity planning
content:
VP Operations responsibility for manufacturing capacity planning across multi-program environments. Key decisions: (1) Approve tiered capacity allocation — committed production gets base load priority, pilot validation at 80% demand confidence, prototype engineering at 60% demand confidence. (2) Equipment investment gates — require multi-tier ROI justification (not single-program payoff); approve phased investment (buy base capability now, defer automation until pilot FAI confirms Ppk ≥1.67). (3) Utilization targets — 60–75% for multi-tier environments (lower than traditional 85% to absorb demand variability and design-change rework). (4) Capacity escalation protocol — queue depth thresholds trigger overtime/subcontract/investment decisions. (5) Quarterly business review — equipment utilization trend, MTBF vs. target, obsolescence status, capital ROI reconciliation, technology roadmap updates. (6) Facility layout strategy — approve three-zone model (hazardous isolated, shared production floor, dedicated program cells) balancing process isolation against resource sharing; review zone allocation annually against program mix changes. (7) Capital facility investment prioritization — rank competing facility improvements by constraint-relief impact (schedule acceleration, bottleneck reduction) using priority scoring; reserve 40-50% of annual capital budget for contingency/emergency equipment repair.

Action thresholds: Utilization <40% for 4+ weeks → consolidate equipment or defer purchases. Utilization >85% → prototype engineering starved, approve capacity expansion. MTBF <1,000 hrs → escalate to equipment OEM for reliability improvement. Facility-driven schedule deferral impacting Tier 0 critical path → immediate escalation to Plant Manager for contingency activation.
summary: Task: Manufacturing capacity planning

label: Analyze production schedules and capacity models
summary: System: Analyze production schedules and capacity models

label: Approve capacity investments or outsourcing decisions
summary: Decision: Approve capacity investments or outsourcing decisions

label: Issue capacity plan with make-buy decisions

curves, identified capacity constraints, and recommended investments (equipment, tooling,
facilities). Include make-buy analysis per FAR 15.407-2 and AS9100 Section 8.4 external

summary: Output: Issue capacity plan with make-buy decisions

label: VP Business Development
content: B&P budget authority: VP BD approves B&P commitment for opportunities >$500K estimated value at Stage 2 gate; CEO approves cumulative single-opportunity B&P spend >$500K.
summary: Role: VP Business Development in Executive Leadership — capture management, win theme development, competitive intelligence, CRM (DoD/primes/international), color team reviews, and strategic teaming across defense/aerospace BD lifecycle

label: Pipeline management and capture strategy
content: VP BD responsibility for managing the full BD pipeline and executing capture strategy per Shipley BD Lifecycle. Pipeline management: maintain tiered pipeline register (Identified → Qualified → Capture → Proposal → Award); apply PWIN scoring at qualification gate (see #1482); maintain Qualified+ pipeline coverage ≥3× annual revenue target; update PWIN-weighted revenue forecast monthly for CFO. Capture strategy: initiate capture planning ≥18 months before RFP drop on strategic opportunities and re-competes; assign dedicated capture manager for must-win pursuits and PWIN ≥35%; develop capture plans covering customer intelligence profile, competitive landscape, win themes and discriminators, teaming strategy, and B&P budget requirement. Gate discipline: mandatory go/no-bid screen before B&P commitment (five criteria per #1482); VP BD chairs color team reviews (Pink→Red→Gold) at proposal milestones; CEO reviews capture brief on must-win programs. Pipeline metrics (see #1481): B&P efficiency target ≥$8M awarded per $1M B&P; win rate disaggregated by contract type, customer segment, and NAICS; book-to-bill target ≥1.0 (two consecutive quarters <0.8 triggers SMC BD strategy review). Re-compete priority: all re-competes on programs >10% of annual revenue treated as must-win; capture initiated 18 months before expiry; CEO briefed with competitive assessment at 12-month mark.
summary: Task: VP BD pipeline management and Shipley capture strategy — tiered pipeline register, PWIN scoring, capture plan execution, color team gate reviews, and re-compete must-win prioritization

label: Review opportunity pipeline in CRM and SAM.gov feeds
content: VP BD system for daily SAM.gov opportunity pipeline monitoring and CRM population. Daily workflow: (1) Configure saved searches in beta.SAM.gov by NAICS code, set-aside type (SB/SDVOSB/8(a)/HUBZone/unrestricted), agency/sub-agency, and PSC code; set automated email alerts to BD team distribution list per saved search. (2) Triage inbound notices daily — pre-solicitation notices, Sources Sought, RFIs, and draft RFPs each trigger different BD response actions; notice type determines urgency and responsible action. (3) RFI/Sources Sought response: VP BD reviews within 24 hours of receipt; opportunities estimated ≥$500K with NAICS match require formal BD response within posting window; responses position capability and past performance without disclosing proprietary pricing data or revealing competitive strategy in multi-vendor settings. (4) Industry day notices: flag all industry day announcements on pipeline-aligned programs; attendance is a positional relationship action regardless of informational value — register within 24 hours. (5) Pre-solicitation tracking: create CRM opportunity record within 1 business day of identification; set stage to Identified; attach SAM.gov solicitation number and URL; set follow-up alert 7 days before anticipated RFP drop. (6) RFP receipt: route immediately to VP BD; apply five-criterion go/no-bid screen (#1482) within 5 business days; advance CRM to Proposal stage if proceeding. CRM integration: every SAM.gov-identified opportunity linked to CRM record with solicitation number, NAICS code, set-aside designation, agency/program office, estimated value, anticipated RFP date, and assigned BD owner. Pipeline completeness audit: VP BD reviews weekly to confirm target-NAICS SAM.gov search results are fully reflected in CRM — coverage gaps signal pipeline blindspots requiring search configuration adjustment. FPDS-NG cross-reference: cross-reference SAM.gov pre-solicitation notices with FPDS-NG incumbent award data to distinguish re-competitions (known incumbent, known pricing posture) from true new starts — enables 6–18 month early positioning advantage before RFP. Agency forecast tools: supplement beta.SAM.gov with agency acquisition forecast data and GovWin IQ (Deltek) for opportunity maturity ratings and projected RFP timelines on high-priority programs.
summary: System: Review opportunity pipeline in CRM and SAM.gov feeds

label: Validate GD&T per ASME Y14.5 and manufacturing feasibility
content: Validate that GD&T callouts on released drawings are both standards-compliant and manufacturing-feasible. Decision inputs: (1) DRF selection rationale — primary datum = largest stable mating surface, secondary/tertiary constrain remaining DOF per assembly function; verify 3-2-1 constraint is complete. (2) Tolerance stack-up — each GD&T tolerance verified against assembly functional requirement using WC, RSS, or Monte Carlo; design margin ≥10% WC or Cpk ≥1.33 RSS. (3) Process capability check — each tolerance mapped to intended manufacturing process; verify process σ supports Cpk ≥1.33 (tolerance ≥ 8σ); flag infeasible tolerances for design negotiation or process upgrade. (4) Measurement feasibility — each GD&T callout must be measurable with available equipment at 4:1 TUR minimum; complex profile or composite position callouts require CMM program feasibility confirmation. (5) Material condition modifier review — MMC/LMC applied only where functional gaging or bonus tolerance is justified; verify virtual condition is calculable. Decision gate: drawing proceeds to release only when all tolerances are both standards-compliant (Y14.5 checklist) and manufacturing-feasible (Cpk ≥1.33 demonstrated or risk-accepted with mitigation). If infeasible, return to design for tolerance relaxation, process upgrade, or datum restructuring.
summary: Decision: Validate GD&T per ASME Y14.5 and manufacturing feasibility

Release engineering drawings per AS9100 Section 8.3.4 design and development controls.

surface finish requirements, and special process notes (heat treat, NDT). Process through
PLM workflow: designer → checker → stress → DER (if applicable) → release. Assign
configuration item numbers per CM plan.

label: Manufacturing Engineer
content: Manufacturing Engineer in Engineering. Responsible for process design, capability assessment, and production system optimization. In high-mix environments: analyze lead-time variability by measuring process mean lead times and standard deviation across 10+ occurrences per upstream process per shift (accounting for changeover, inspection holds, queue effects). Support pull-system design by quantifying realistic lead-time buffers. Recommend process improvements (SMED, mistake-proofing, inspection-queue reduction) that reduce lead-time variance and enable Kanban assumptions to hold. Collaborate with Production Scheduler on shift-by-shift lead-time distribution and update safety-buffer calculations when process changes are implemented.
summary: Role: Manufacturing Engineer in Engineering

label: Process planning and work instruction development
summary: Task: Process planning and work instruction development

label: Create manufacturing plans in MES per AS9100 Section 8.5
summary: System: Create manufacturing plans in MES per AS9100 Section 8.5

label: Select manufacturing methods and validate process capability
content: AS9100D Section 8.5.1(f) and AS9145 Section 7 (IQ/OQ/PQ framework for aerospace manufacturing equipment qualification — no aerospace-specific IQ/OQ/PQ standard exists equivalent to pharma ASTM E2500; AS9100D 8.5.1(f) and AS9145 are the governing references); ISO 230-1/ISO 230-2/ISO 10791 (machine tool geometric and positioning acceptance testing for OQ phase);
summary: Decision: Select manufacturing methods and validate process capability

label: Release manufacturing plan with process sheets and tooling list
content:
Develop manufacturing plan per AS9100D Section 8.5.1 production and service provision. Include operation sequence, process sheets with step-by-step instructions, tooling and fixture requirements, inspection points (in-process and final), special process callouts per applicable NADCAP audit criteria (verify current AC codes for your specific processes—welding, NDT, chemical processing, etc.), and material handling requirements. Reference AS9145 APQP Phase 3 process design deliverables. For low-rate initial production (LRIP), use staged documentation release: pilot builds 1-3 use draft procedures with mandatory deviation capture, builds 4-6 baseline formal work instructions from deviation data, builds 7+ operate under full change-controlled documentation. This prevents over-documenting unstable processes while building toward AS9102 FAI compliance. CRITICAL FOR WORKFORCE: Manufacturing plans must be written from observed shop floor practice, not solely from engineering intent. Baseline procedures by shadowing experienced operators during actual builds, capturing decision points and adaptations they perform implicitly, then formalizing into work instructions. Include photo sequences of equipment setup, troubleshooting decision trees for common deviations, and explicit escalation criteria. This makes procedures trainable for new technicians rather than reference documents only experts can interpret.

Standards references: AS9100D Section 8.5.1 (Control of Production — manufacturing plan documentation requirements); AS9145 Section 6.3 (Process Flow Diagram) and 6.5 (Control Plan — manufacturing plan structure); AS9102 (First Article Inspection — FAI requirements for new manufacturing plans); NADCAP audit criteria (verify specific AC codes applicable to your facility's special processes); SAE AS6500 Section 5.1 (Process Documentation — manufacturing plan content requirements); ASME Y14.100 (Engineering Drawing Practices — drawing references in process sheets).
summary: Output: Release manufacturing plan with process sheets and tooling list

label: Composite fabrication process development
content: COMPOSITE FABRICATION PROCESS DEVELOPMENT for aerospace structures: (1) LAYUP PROCESS: Manual hand layup (prototype/LRIP) vs. automated fiber placement (AFP) or automated tape laying (ATL) for production rate. Manual layup typical for LRIP quantities <20 units; labor-intensive (15-40 hrs per major structural panel) but low tooling investment. (2) CURE PROCESS: Autoclave cure (standard for primary structure, 250-350°F, 85-100 psi) vs. out-of-autoclave (OOA) vacuum-bag-only cure for secondary structure. Cure cycle development requires 3-5 qualification panels before production; each material/geometry combination needs separate qualification per NADCAP AC7118. (3) KEY PROCESS VARIABLES: Ply orientation accuracy (±1° for primary structure), debulk frequency, vacuum integrity, cure temperature uniformity (±5°F across tool surface), resin flow/bleed. (4) COMMON DEFECTS (hand layup): Porosity (>2% void content typically rejectable per ASTM D2734), delamination, fiber waviness, FOD, ply drops/gaps exceeding drawing limits. NDT methods: ultrasonic C-scan (primary), tap testing (screening), thermography (large panels). AFP-SPECIFIC DEFECT MODES (not present in hand layup): fiber steering gaps from jaw wear, tow restart bridging/resin-rich voids at cut/restart locations, machine-induced out-of-plane fiber waviness from premature compaction, tow contamination from open fiber path, and head collision damage. These AFP-specific modes require dedicated PFMEA entries and control plan inspection points not carried over from hand-layup PFMEA. (5) TOOLING: Composite cure tools (Invar, steel, or composite) must match part CTE; Invar preferred for tight-tolerance primary structure (CTE matches carbon/epoxy). Tool cost $50-200K for major structural tools; tool life 50-200 cycles depending on material and cure temperature. (6) LRIP CONSIDERATIONS: Process capability data limited; expect 10-20% rework rate on early builds due to layup operator learning, cure cycle optimization, and tool surface condition. Track defect types by location on part to identify systematic process issues vs. random operator errors. CITATION WARNING: ASTM D6775 is a tensile test standard for woven textile tapes — it does NOT govern AFP machine capability or process qualification. Do not cite it as an AFP qualification reference; doing so will be flagged as a nonconforming citation by customer auditors.
summary: Task: Composite fabrication process development

label: Program autoclave/ATP machines per material specification cure cycles
summary: System: Program autoclave/ATP machines per material specification cure cycles

label: Validate layup sequence and cure parameters against process spec
content: Standards references: AS9100D Section 8.5.1(f) (Validation of Special Processes — composite cure process validation); NADCAP AC7118 (Composites — composite fabrication accreditation); SAE AMS specifications (e.g., AMS 3892 — carbon fiber prepreg material specifications); ASTM D5528/D7136/D7264 (Composite Test Methods — laminate characterization); AS9103 (Variation Management — cure parameter key characteristics control); CMH-17 (Composite Materials Handbook) Chapter 8 — the governing reference for statistical equivalency demonstration when transitioning between composite fabrication processes (e.g., hand layup to AFP); defines basis value methods (B-basis, A-basis) and equivalency testing protocols for mechanical property comparison between a new process and a qualified baseline. MEASUREMENT SYSTEM PREREQUISITE: Before any Cpk or Ppk value is reported as evidence of process capability, the measurement system used to generate that data must be validated per MSA (Measurement System Analysis) or Gage R&R study. A Cpk ≥ 1.67 claim built on a measurement system with >30% gauge R&R contribution to total variation is statistically invalid and will not survive customer or DCMA audit scrutiny. Conduct MSA on all KC measurement systems (void content UT, fiber waviness optical measurement, dimensional CMM) before the PQ capability study begins.
summary: Decision: Validate layup sequence and cure parameters against process spec

label: Issue composite fabrication traveler with NDI requirements
content:
Prepare composite fabrication traveler including material kitting instructions (prepreg out-time tracking), layup sequence per engineering ply table, vacuum bag configuration, autoclave cure cycle parameters per material spec (e.g., Hexcel 8552/AS4 prepreg or Toray T800), NDI requirements per ASNT SNT-TC-1A (ultrasonic C-scan, thermography), and dimensional inspection requirements. Reference NAS 410 for NDT personnel certification. MATERIAL SYSTEM DISAMBIGUATION: Hexcel 8552 is an epoxy matrix used with dry fiber reinforcements including AS4 carbon fiber (designated Hexcel 8552/AS4 — a prepreg). Hexcel RTM6 is a completely separate single-component RTM epoxy resin system used in resin transfer molding — it is NOT a variant of 8552 and is not used in prepreg layup or AFP processes. Do not conflate these two product lines; citing 'Hexcel 8552 RTM6' is technically incorrect and will undermine credibility with customer engineers and auditors. For AFP process qualification, the applicable material TDS is Hexcel 8552/AS4 (or the specific prepreg tow form procured for AFP). AFP FAI NOTE: When transitioning from hand layup to AFP, repair procedures qualified for hand-layup panels may not be pre-approved for AFP-manufactured panels. Repair procedure qualification is a common FAI exposure — confirm with the customer DER whether existing repair substantiation extends to AFP panels or requires supplemental analysis before FAI submission.

Standards references: AS9100D Section 8.5.2 (Identification and Traceability — material lot traceability in composite travelers); NADCAP AC7118 (Composites — fabrication process accreditation requirements); NAS 410 / EN 4179 (NDT Personnel Certification — Level II/III requirements for NDI methods); ASNT SNT-TC-1A (Personnel Qualification — NDT operator certification); AS9102 (First Article Inspection — composite part FAI requirements); ASTM E2580 (Ultrasonic Testing of Flat Panel Composites); SAE ARP5606 (Composite Honeycomb NDI).
summary: Output: Issue composite fabrication traveler with NDI requirements

Prepare structural test report per MIL-STD-1530 ASIP requirements. Include test article
description, instrumentation layout (strain gauges, LVDTs, load cells), load application
method, test sequence, raw data plots, comparison to FEA predictions (target <10% deviation),
and failure mode documentation if applicable. Calculate test-adjusted margins and update

content:
Document environmental qualification test results per MIL-STD-810H and/or DO-160G.
Include test categories performed (vibration Method 514.8, shock Method 516.8, altitude
Method 500.6, temperature Method 501.7/502.7, humidity Method 507.6, EMI per MIL-STD-461G),
test profiles with actual vs. specified parameters, functional test results at each

label: Contracts & Compliance
summary: Department: Contracts & Compliance

label: Contract Analyst
content: Contract Analyst is responsible for FAR/DFARS compliance on active contracts, CDRL tracking and submission, contract modification processing (FAR 43.103/43.201), monthly CLIN risk reviews, and flow-down clause verification. Single-point-of-failure risk is high in small orgs — cross-training and documented SOP coverage are critical. Key fluency areas: cost accounting standards (CAS) applicability thresholds, DCAA audit readiness, incremental funding limits, and Anti-Deficiency Act exposure on government cost-type vehicles.
summary: Role: Contract Analyst in Contracts & Compliance

label: Monthly compliance risk review
summary: Task: Monthly compliance risk review

label: Query SAP for CLINs near due date and review obligation status
summary: System: Query SAP for CLINs near due date and review obligation status

label: Sort by cost times urgency, identify top risks and required actions
summary: Decision: Sort by cost times urgency, identify top risks and required actions

label: Email risk report to PM and contracts lead in specified format
content:
Generate monthly CLIN compliance risk report. Query SAP for all active CLINs with
delivery dates within 60 days. Calculate risk score = (remaining cost / total CLIN value)
× (1 / days remaining). Sort descending. For top 10 risks, document: CLIN number,
deliverable description, current status, funding status (fully funded/incrementally funded),
LOE vs. deliverable type, and recommended action. Cross-reference with DCMA surveillance
findings. Format as Excel with conditional formatting and email to PM within 3 business days.
summary: Output: Email risk report to PM and contracts lead in specified format

label: Contract modification processing
summary: Task: Contract modification processing

label: Review modification requests in contract management system
summary: System: Review modification requests in contract management system

label: Validate scope alignment with existing terms and FAR compliance
summary: Decision: Validate scope alignment with existing terms and FAR compliance

label: Prepare contract modification package with revised SOW and pricing
content:
Process contract modification per FAR 43.103 (bilateral) or FAR 43.201 (unilateral).
Document scope change description, delta pricing with basis of estimate, updated CDRL
list if applicable, period of performance adjustment, and revised contract value. Ensure
DFARS 252.204-7012 (CMMC/cybersecurity) and 252.227-7013 (data rights) clauses flow
correctly. Route through legal review and obtain CO signature.
summary: Output: Prepare contract modification package with revised SOW and pricing

label: CDRL tracking and deliverable management
summary: Task: CDRL tracking and deliverable management

label: Maintain CDRL tracker with submission dates and government acceptance status
summary: System: Maintain CDRL tracker with submission dates and government acceptance status

label: Prioritize overdue CDRLs and coordinate with technical leads
summary: Decision: Prioritize overdue CDRLs and coordinate with technical leads

label: Submit CDRL packages through government portal with transmittal letter
content:
Manage CDRL submissions per DI-MGMT-80368 Data Item Description format. Track each CDRL
by exhibit line item number, DID number, submission frequency, distribution list, and
government review period. Prepare transmittal letter referencing contract number, CDRL
exhibit, and data item. Submit through designated government portal (e.g., PIEE/WAWF).
Track government acceptance within 30-day review period per FAR 52.247-34.
summary: Output: Submit CDRL packages through government portal with transmittal letter

label: Export Control Officer
content: Export Control Officer is responsible for ITAR (22 CFR Parts 120-130) and EAR (15 CFR Parts 730-774) compliance across the organization. Core duties: DDTC registration maintenance; commodity jurisdiction and ECCN classification; export license and agreement management (DSP-5, DSP-73, DSP-85, TAA, MLA); Technology Control Plan development and maintenance; deemed export controls for foreign persons; restricted party screening; voluntary self-disclosure; and ITAR/EAR training program. Key risk areas: unlicensed deemed exports to foreign national employees, technical data sharing without CJ determination, lapsed DDTC registration, and unreported violations. Penalty exposure: criminal penalties up to $1M per violation and 20 years imprisonment (ITAR); civil penalties up to $1.3M per violation (EAR). Debarment from federal contracting is a collateral consequence of serious violations.
summary: Role: Export Control Officer in Contracts & Compliance

label: ITAR/EAR classification and licensing
content: ITAR/EAR classification and licensing covers: (1) Jurisdiction determination — ITAR (22 CFR Parts 120-130, USML categories) vs. EAR (15 CFR Parts 730-774, CCL/ECCN); submit CJ request to DDTC if jurisdiction is ambiguous; (2) License requirement — ITAR: TAA, MLA, or DSP-5 depending on activity type; EAR: identify ECCN and applicable license exception (GOV, TSR, TMP, etc.); (3) Denied party screening — screen all foreign parties against SAM.gov exclusions, BIS Entity List, OFAC SDN list, and DDTC debarment list before any export or deemed export; (4) Recordkeeping — ITAR: 22 CFR 122.5 (5 years); EAR: 15 CFR 762.2 (5 years); both clocks run from date of export or license expiration.
summary: Task: ITAR/EAR classification and licensing

label: Screen items against USML and CCL using DECCS classification tool
summary: System: Screen items against USML and CCL using DECCS classification tool

label: Determine jurisdiction (ITAR/EAR) and required license type
content: Decision framework for determining ITAR vs EAR jurisdiction and required export authorization type. STEP 1: Identify item — classify by technical characteristics, performance specifications, and intended end-use against USML Categories I–XXI. STEP 2: Apply 'specially designed' test (22 CFR 120.41) — catches items designed/modified for military application; releases items on CCL, commercially available, or in public domain. STEP 3: If not USML, classify against CCL by ECCN; check 600-series for Export Control Reform transitioned items. STEP 4: If no CCL match, designate EAR99 (residual restrictions apply for embargoed destinations and denied parties). STEP 5: Determine authorization type — ITAR: DSP-5 (permanent export), DSP-73 (temporary export), TAA (technical data/defense services), MLA (manufacturing license); EAR: license application to BIS or applicable license exception (STA, TMP, GOV, TSR). STEP 6: For foreign manufacturing subcontracts — ITAR Manufacturing License Agreement (MLA) is typically required when a foreign entity will manufacture ITAR-controlled defense articles; MLA requires DDTC approval and may include provisos restricting re-export, third-party access, and end-use. FOREIGN SUBCONTRACTOR CRITICAL PATH: CJ determination → if ITAR, assess whether MLA is obtainable (consider country, end-use, foreign policy restrictions per 22 CFR 126.1) → if MLA not feasible, foreign subcontracting is blocked; if EAR, determine ECCN and license requirement by destination country using Commerce Country Chart (15 CFR 738 Supp. 1).
summary: Decision: Determine jurisdiction (ITAR/EAR) and required license type

label: Issue commodity jurisdiction determination or license application
content:
Perform commodity jurisdiction determination per 22 CFR 120.3. If ITAR-controlled,
identify USML category and determine if Technical Assistance Agreement (TAA), Manufacturing
License Agreement (MLA), or DSP-5 export license is required. If EAR-controlled, determine
ECCN and applicable license exception (e.g., GOV, TSR, TMP). Document screening results
including denied party screening (SAM.gov, BIS Entity List, OFAC SDN). Maintain Technology
Control Plan per ITAR 22 CFR 125.
summary: Output: Issue commodity jurisdiction determination or license application

label: Deemed export management
content: Deemed exports are transfers of technology or source code to foreign persons in the United States, treated as an export to that person's most recent country of citizenship per EAR 15 CFR 734.13 and ITAR 22 CFR 120.17. Process: (1) Technology inventory — identify all ECCNs and USML categories applicable to technical data, source code, and equipment in the workplace; (2) Personnel screening — obtain citizenship and immigration status for any person who may access controlled technology (new hires, visitors, subcontractors); (3) License determination — apply EAR license exceptions (TMP, TSR) or confirm TAA/DSP-5 covers the transfer; ITAR has no deemed export license exception for nationals of ITAR 22 CFR 126.1 embargoed countries; (4) Access controls — IT restrictions, need-to-know controls, and physical access limitations on controlled technology areas; (5) Deemed export log — record all authorized transfers with license or exception basis, date, technology description, and recipient nationality. Risk example: providing ECCN 9E003 turbine engine technology to a foreign national engineer without a TAA is an unlicensed deemed export.
summary: Task: Deemed export management

label: Screen foreign national employees against ITAR restricted countries
summary: System: Screen foreign national employees against ITAR restricted countries

label: Approve or restrict facility access based on citizenship and program classification
summary: Decision: Approve or restrict facility access based on citizenship and program classification

label: Issue access determination letter with required controls
content:
Process deemed export determination per EAR 15 CFR 734.13 and ITAR 22 CFR 120.17.
Verify foreign national citizenship against restricted country lists (ITAR 22 CFR 126.1).
If access approved, document required Technology Control Plan provisions including
information barriers, computer access restrictions, meeting attendance limitations, and
facility access controls. Issue determination letter with annual review date.
summary: Output: Issue access determination letter with required controls

label: FAR/DFARS Specialist
content: FAR/DFARS Specialist interprets regulatory requirements, analyzes RFP clauses, and ensures flow-down compliance. Key competency areas: FAR Parts 12, 15, 31, 42, 52; DFARS Parts 215, 225, 227, 242, 252; data rights clauses (DFARS 252.227-7013 technical data, 7014 noncommercial software, 7015 technical data — commercial items); CAS applicability and Disclosure Statement obligations; export controls interface (ITAR/EAR clause triggers). Reviews all subcontract terms for required flow-downs per FAR 52.244-6. Monitors FAR/DFARS case rule changes via Federal Register. Provides clause interpretation guidance to program managers and contract analysts. Prepares deviation and waiver requests when contract clauses conflict with company capability.
summary: Role: FAR/DFARS Specialist in Contracts & Compliance

label: Clause compliance review and flowdown
summary: Task: Clause compliance review and flowdown

label: Parse RFP Section I clauses against FAR/DFARS compliance matrix
content: FAR/DFARS flow-down clause verification for government-funded aerospace supplier agreements. KEY CLAUSES TO FLOW DOWN: (1) FAR 52.244-6 (Subcontracts for Commercial Items): require applicable FAR clauses in all sub-tier commercial item agreements; supplier responsible for enforcement at all tiers. (2) DFARS 252.244-7001 (Subcontracts for Commercial Items—DoD): prohibits undisclosed foreign subcontractors on DoD contracts; supplier certifies subcontractor list quarterly. (3) DFARS 252.245-7000 (Government-Furnished Information): perpetual inventory accountability by serial/lot; monthly certification of non-loss status; return within 15 days of contract termination. (4) CAS Compliance: thresholds—$2M+ contract and >$700K CAS-covered contract triggers full CAS; supplier cost pool must segregate direct labor (touch labor only), direct material (per BOM), special processes (heat treat, plating, NDT), and overhead; monthly cost reports with >5% variance investigation. (5) DCAA Audit Rights: supplier maintains cost records accessible for 3 years post-delivery; government may conduct unannounced audit. (6) Anti-Kickback Act FAR 52.203-7: certify no kickbacks in subcontracting. (7) Buy American Act / DFARS 252.225 domestic sourcing: foreign source requires justification and contracting officer approval. SUBCONTRACTOR MANAGEMENT DOCUMENTATION: maintain subcontractor register updated quarterly; identify foreign subcontractors with ITAR/EAR cognizance; certify no restricted-component manufacturing by foreign entities. IMPLEMENTATION CHECKPOINTS: Pre-award—obtain CAS disclosure statement, verify subcontractor list; Kickoff—confirm cost pool structure, establish monthly reporting; Quarterly—audit supplier-reported costs vs. historical benchmarks; flag >10% variance.
summary: System: Parse RFP Section I clauses against FAR/DFARS compliance matrix

label: Identify high-risk clauses and required flowdown to subcontractors
summary: Decision: Identify high-risk clauses and required flowdown to subcontractors

label: Prepare clause compliance matrix with risk assessment
content:
Review RFP Section I for all incorporated FAR/DFARS clauses. Map each clause to internal
compliance procedures. Flag high-risk clauses: DFARS 252.225-7001 (Buy American), DFARS
252.225-7012 (specialty metals), DFARS 252.204-7012 (CMMC/cybersecurity), FAR 52.222-50
(human trafficking), FAR 52.219-9 (small business subcontracting plan). Determine required
flowdown per FAR 52.244-6. Prepare compliance matrix with responsible party and evidence requirements.
summary: Output: Prepare clause compliance matrix with risk assessment

label: Cost Accounting Standards compliance
summary: Task: Cost Accounting Standards compliance

label: Review cost accounting practices against CAS 401-420
summary: System: Review cost accounting practices against CAS 401-420

label: Identify potential CAS violations and corrective actions
summary: Decision: Identify potential CAS violations and corrective actions

label: Issue CAS compliance assessment with remediation recommendations
content:
Conduct CAS compliance review per 48 CFR 9904. Verify consistency of cost accounting
practices (CAS 401), allocation of home office expenses (CAS 403), capitalization of
assets (CAS 404), allocation of IR&D/B&P (CAS 420). Document any disclosed practices
that deviate from published Disclosure Statement. Recommend corrective actions and
estimate potential cost impact of any noncompliance. Coordinate with DCAA auditor.
summary: Output: Issue CAS compliance assessment with remediation recommendations

label: Quality Auditor
content: Quality Auditor owns the internal audit program (AS9100D §9.2.2), supplier qualification and surveillance (§8.4), CAPA system management, and NCR classification. Primary risk in aerospace prototype orgs: audits become paper exercises disconnected from operational reality. Key competency gaps: distinguishing major vs. minor NCRs correctly, applying 5-Why and Ishikawa rigorously, verifying corrective action effectiveness (not just closure), and maintaining NADCAP-specific surveillance cadence for special process suppliers.
summary: Role: Quality Auditor in Contracts & Compliance

label: Internal audit program management
content: Internal audit program management per AS9100D §9.2.2. Key activities: (1) Develop annual audit schedule covering all QMS clauses, weighted by risk — high-risk processes (supplier controls §8.4, production §8.5, nonconformance §10.2) audited 2×/year minimum. (2) Assign auditors independent of area being audited; maintain auditor competency records (ISO 19011 training, process knowledge). (3) Audit checklist tailored by process type — machining audits check tool wear logs, fixture calibration, first-piece verification; assembly audits check torque records, FOD controls, configuration verification. (4) Track audit findings in CAPA system with 30-day closure target for minor NCRs, 15-day containment for major NCRs. (5) Report audit program effectiveness at management review: finding trends, closure rates, repeat finding rate (<10% target). (6) In LRIP multi-program environments: audit shared resources (equipment, operators, measurement systems) as cross-program elements, not per-program silos.
summary: Task: Internal audit program management

label: Set supplier audit frequency per AS9100D §9.2 — risk, performance, and process type criteria
content: Execute audit schedule per AS9100D Section 9.2.2. Audit frequency must consider process importance, prior performance, and risk. For new suppliers or those with recent NCRs, minimum quarterly touch — not annual. Internal audits must cover Section 8.4 (supplier controls) as a standalone audit element. Audit checklist must be tailored to process type: heat treat suppliers require additional checks on temperature uniformity survey (TUS) logs, pyrometer calibration records, and process specification traveler approvals.
summary: System: Execute audit schedule per AS9100 Section 9.2 requirements

label: Classify findings as major/minor NCR and determine root cause
content: Classify audit findings per AS9100D severity framework. MAJOR NCR: Absence or total breakdown of a required QMS element (e.g., no calibration program, no document control for drawings, no material traceability). Also: any finding that could result in shipment of nonconforming product. Requires 15-day containment action + 60-day root cause + corrective action. MINOR NCR: Isolated instance of non-compliance or incomplete implementation (e.g., one gage overdue calibration, single missing signature on traveler). Requires 30-day corrective action. OBSERVATION: Potential weakness that could become NCR if not addressed (e.g., trending toward late calibrations). No formal CAR required but tracked. ROOT CAUSE METHODS: 5-Why analysis for straightforward failures (single causal chain); Ishikawa/fishbone for complex failures with multiple potential causes (categorize: Man, Machine, Method, Material, Measurement, Environment). CRITICAL DISTINCTION: Do not classify systemic documentation gaps as minor — if the same type of missing record appears across multiple lots or operators, escalate to major even if each individual instance seems minor.
summary: Decision: Classify findings as major/minor NCR and determine root cause

label: Issue audit report with findings and corrective action requests
content:
Conduct internal quality audit per AS9100D Section 9.2.2. Prepare audit checklist
covering applicable clauses, review objective evidence, interview personnel, and
document findings. Classify nonconformities: Major (system breakdown, missing required
element) or Minor (isolated instance, incomplete implementation). Issue CARs per 8D
methodology with root cause analysis (5-Why, Ishikawa), containment actions, corrective
actions, and effectiveness verification date. Track in CAPA system.
summary: Output: Issue audit report with findings and corrective action requests

label: Supplier quality assessment
content: Supplier quality assessment task encompasses: (1) Pre-award capability evaluation — process capability (trial-run Ppk), measurement system adequacy (Gage R&R), QMS maturity baseline, capacity assessment, and delivery reliability. (2) Ongoing surveillance — scheduled audits per risk tier, SPC data review, NCR trending, corrective action effectiveness verification. (3) Supplier scoring — weighted scorecard (Quality 30%, Delivery 25%, QMS 25%, Cost 20%) updated monthly. (4) Disposition decisions — Approved, Conditionally Approved (with improvement plan + milestones), Probation (100% incoming inspection + biweekly audits), or Disqualified. (5) Supplier development — joint FMEA, process improvement projects, training support for immature suppliers. For LRIP with non-aerospace suppliers: assess actual process discipline rather than certification status; use tiered qualification (Phase 1: capability trial + baseline assessment, 4-8 weeks → Phase 2: joint FMEA + onsite audit + conditional approval, 4-6 weeks → Phase 3: FAI + control plan + SPC baseline → full production approval, 4-6 weeks). TIER TRANSITION CRITERIA: Upgrade Tier B→A after 6 months with FTY ≥98%, escape rate ≤0.3%, declining audit findings. Downgrade A→B if escape >1% or repeat NCR rate >15% over 2 months. Disqualify if >2 major NCRs unresolved + escape >5%. PROTOTYPE vs PRODUCTION GATES: Prototype authorization requires Pp/Ppk ≥1.0 + Gage R&R ≤25% + preliminary Control Plan; production authorization requires Ppk ≥1.33 + Gage R&R ≤10%/20% + final Control Plan + FAI sign-off + SPC baseline established.
summary: Task: Supplier quality assessment

label: Review supplier quality data and conduct on-site assessments
content: Review supplier quality data including: AS9100D cert scope and expiration, NADCAP accreditation commodity and expiration, OASIS database confirmation of active registration, last CB surveillance audit date and findings, open CAR/NCR count and age, process capability indices (Cpk) for critical characteristics, and on-time delivery rate (trailing 12 months). For new suppliers with <12 months performance history, increase surveillance cadence to quarterly. Flag any certification lapse — even a 1-day gap — as a major risk indicator requiring escalation.
summary: System: Review supplier quality data and conduct on-site assessments

label: Approve, conditionally approve, or disqualify suppliers
content: Supplier approval tiers: Approved (full use), Conditionally Approved (use with enhanced inspection or corrective action plan in place, re-evaluate within 90 days), Disqualified (no new POs, transition existing work). For conditionally approved suppliers, document specific deficiencies and measurable exit criteria. AS9100D Section 8.4.1 requires maintaining criteria for evaluation, selection, monitoring, and re-evaluation. Any change in certification body, scope reduction, or suspension requires immediate re-evaluation before continued use.
summary: Decision: Approve, conditionally approve, or disqualify suppliers

label: Issue supplier assessment report with approval status
content: Conduct supplier quality assessment per AS9100D Section 8.4 (externally provided processes, products, and services). For special process suppliers (heat treating, NDT, welding), verify NADCAP accreditation in addition to AS9100D cert. Validate cert currency via OASIS database (oasis.sae-international.org) independently of supplier-provided documents. Score using Yggdrasil Aero supplier scorecard (QMS: 25%, delivery: 25%, quality: 30%, cost: 20%). Issue approval determination: Approved, Conditionally Approved (with improvement plan), or Disqualified. Maintain in Approved Supplier List with cert expiration dates tracked.
summary: Output: Issue supplier assessment report with approval status

label: Safety Officer
summary: Role: Safety Officer in Contracts & Compliance

label: System safety analysis
summary: Task: System safety analysis

label: Perform hazard analysis per MIL-STD-882E using fault tree analysis
summary: System: Perform hazard analysis per MIL-STD-882E using fault tree analysis

label: Classify hazards by severity/probability and determine risk acceptance
summary: Decision: Classify hazards by severity/probability and determine risk acceptance

label: Publish system safety assessment report
content:
Conduct system safety assessment per MIL-STD-882E. Perform Preliminary Hazard List (PHL),
Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA), Subsystem Hazard Analysis (SSHA), System Hazard Analysis
(SHA), and Operating & Support Hazard Analysis (O&SHA). Classify hazards using risk
assessment matrix (Severity I-IV × Probability A-E). Document risk mitigation actions
and residual risk acceptance. Maintain Safety Assessment Report (SAR) with hazard tracking
log. Present to System Safety Working Group for approval.
summary: Output: Publish system safety assessment report

label: Mishap investigation and reporting
summary: Task: Mishap investigation and reporting

label: Conduct mishap investigation per company safety procedures
summary: System: Conduct mishap investigation per company safety procedures

label: Determine causal factors and corrective actions using HFACS framework
summary: Decision: Determine causal factors and corrective actions using HFACS framework

label: Submit mishap report to safety review board and applicable agencies
content:
Investigate mishap/incident per MIL-STD-882E Section 4.10. Apply Human Factors Analysis
and Classification System (HFACS) to identify organizational influences, unsafe supervision,
preconditions, and unsafe acts. Document sequence of events, physical evidence, witness
statements, and contributing factors. Prepare mishap report including corrective actions
with implementation timeline. Submit to Safety Review Board and applicable agencies (FAA,
DoD) per reporting timeframes (24-hour preliminary, 30-day final).
summary: Output: Submit mishap report to safety review board and applicable agencies

label: Manufacturing & Operations
summary: Department: Manufacturing & Operations

label: Production Manager
summary: Role: Production Manager in Manufacturing & Operations

label: Production scheduling and workflow management
content: Production scheduling and workflow management for low-rate multi-program aerospace manufacturing. CORE CHALLENGE: 8-15 units per variant with concurrent programs at different maturity levels competing for shared facility resources. SCHEDULING HIERARCHY: (1) Master Production Schedule (MPS)—monthly horizon, capacity allocation by program tier priority. (2) Weekly dispatch schedule—frozen 1-2 weeks ahead for committed production, rolling daily for prototype. (3) Daily shop floor execution—shift-level task assignment against dispatch. WORKFLOW MANAGEMENT: Track each unit through routing steps (machining → sub-assembly → assembly → test → inspection); flag holds for missing material, design change, or quality findings. Manage rework loops as re-entry to routing (not separate workflow) to maintain schedule visibility. KEY INPUTS: design release dates, supplier material dock dates, equipment availability (including PM windows), labor availability by skill certification. KEY OUTPUTS: production sequence, resource assignments, material pull signals, schedule variance reports. COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Over-scheduling prototype work that displaces committed deliveries; (2) Invisible rework loops consuming planned capacity; (3) Design changes invalidating frozen schedule windows without formal replan trigger.
summary: Task: Production scheduling and workflow management

label: Manage production schedule in MES with capacity constraints
summary: System: Manage production schedule in MES with capacity constraints

label: Sequence work orders based on priority, tooling availability, and material readiness
summary: Decision: Sequence work orders based on priority, tooling availability, and material readiness

label: Publish weekly production schedule with shift assignments
content:
Generate weekly production schedule including work order sequence, station assignments,
shift coverage, tooling allocation, and material pull schedules. Apply Theory of Constraints
to identify and manage bottleneck stations. Include overtime authorization requests if
needed. Track schedule performance index (SPI) against program milestones. Format per
Yggdrasil Aero production planning template and distribute to floor supervisors by Thursday COB.
summary: Output: Publish weekly production schedule with shift assignments

label: Non-conformance management and MRB process
content: Manage nonconforming material from detection through final disposition, minimizing production impact while ensuring systemic issues are corrected. DETECTION AND CONTAINMENT: Upon nonconformance detection, quarantine affected material/WIP immediately; document NCR with description, location, quantity, operation, operator, and date; notify QC and Engineering within 2 hours for Tier 1 nonconformances. MRB PROCESS: Convene Material Review Board (Quality + Engineering + Production) for disposition per AS9100D §8.7 — Use-As-Is (engineering analysis required), Rework (to drawing spec per approved procedure), Repair (approved deviation), Scrap. Production Manager role: assess schedule and cost impact of each option; disposition authority rests with Engineering and Quality. REWORK SCHEDULING: Incorporate approved rework into shop floor routing as re-entry to affected operations (not a separate workflow); update WO to reflect rework hours; track rework as a separate cost element. SCHEDULE RECOVERY: NCR hold time impacts schedule — expedite MRB dispositions for Tier 0 program-critical items (target <24 hrs for use-as-is/rework; <72 hrs for complex engineering analysis). NCR METRICS: Track monthly NCR count by operation/part/root cause, MRB cycle time, rework cost as % of production cost, repeat NCR rate. Systemic NCR trends (same defect type ≥3 occurrences) → escalate to formal CAPA process with Quality Manager.
summary: Task: Non-conformance management and MRB process for prod_mgr

label: Disposition NCRs in quality system per AS9100 Section 8.7
summary: System: Disposition NCRs in quality system per AS9100 Section 8.7

label: Determine disposition (use-as-is, repair, rework, scrap) with engineering concurrence
summary: Decision: Determine disposition (use-as-is, repair, rework, scrap) with engineering concurrence

label: Process MRB action with cost impact and schedule recovery plan
content:
Manage nonconforming product per AS9100 Section 8.7. Document nonconformity description,
affected parts/lots, containment actions, and root cause. Convene Material Review Board
(MRB) for disposition: use-as-is (with engineering analysis), repair (per approved repair
procedure), rework (to drawing requirements), or scrap. Calculate cost impact and schedule
recovery plan. Notify customer if contractually required per FAR 52.246-2.
summary: Output: Process MRB action with cost impact and schedule recovery plan

label: Quality Manager
content: Quality Manager in aerospace/defense manufacturing. Responsible for AS9100D QMS compliance, supplier quality oversight, NADCAP customer approval maintenance, and nonconformance disposition. In LRHM environments: own the inspection strategy (100% vs. sampling justification), drive PFMEA-to-control-plan linkage, maintain process capability records per customer flow-down, and ensure measurement system capability (Gage R&R) before relying on 100% inspection results.
summary: Role: Quality Manager in Manufacturing & Operations

label: Quality management system maintenance
content: Maintain QMS per AS9100D. Key subsystems: document control (Section 7.5), internal audit program (Section 9.2), CAPA process (Section 10.2), management review (Section 9.3), and supplier evaluation (Section 8.4). In LRHM: QMS must accommodate part-number proliferation without procedure explosion — use tiered work instructions, family-of-parts control plans, and process-centric rather than part-number-centric SPC to remain scalable.
summary: Task: Quality management system maintenance

label: Maintain QMS documentation per AS9100D requirements
content: Maintain QMS documentation per regulatory requirements, including Kanban procedures. Document: standard container quantities, card count calculation method (including lead-time safety-buffer formula), seasonal adjustment logic, escalation triggers (queue-depth threshold, lead-time variance notification). Update work instructions when process lead times change >10% or seasonal demand tiers shift. Conduct quarterly document review to verify calculations align with current measured lead-time distribution and demand forecast. Include Kanban delivery requirements in supplier purchase-order quality clauses.
summary: System: Maintain QMS documentation per AS9100D requirements

label: Review and approve QMS changes through document control process
content: Review and approve Kanban system changes (card-count adjustments, new product-family introduction to pull system, supermarket layout modifications) through document control process. Evaluate impact on: lead-time buffer adequacy (vs. current demand forecast and lead-time variability), capacity constraints (can upstream processes support reduced WIP?), supplier delivery readiness. Approve with effective date; communicate to all shifts and Production Scheduler. Document approval rationale for traceability in management review.
summary: Decision: Review and approve QMS changes through document control process

label: Release updated quality manual and procedures
content:
Maintain Quality Management System per AS9100D. Manage documented information including
quality manual, quality policy, quality objectives, procedures, work instructions, and
forms. Process document changes through review/approval workflow per Section 7.5. Conduct
management review per Section 9.3 including audit results, customer feedback, process
performance, nonconformity trends, and improvement opportunities. Prepare for CB
surveillance audits per annual schedule.
summary: Output: Release updated quality manual and procedures

label: First Article Inspection coordination
content: Coordinate First Article Inspection per AS9102 Rev C (supersedes AS9102B). FAI required for: new part numbers, engineering changes affecting form/fit/function, supplier changes, production breaks >2 years, and process changes (e.g., manual to CNC). Partial FAI (PFAI) permitted for minor changes — PFAI documents only the characteristics affected by the change, referencing the prior full FAI for unchanged characteristics. FAI IS A SINGLE FIRST-ARTICLE INSPECTION of one production-representative article (or small defined lot per customer requirement) — it is NOT a batch capability study. AS9102 defines three forms: Form 1 (Part Number Accountability), Form 2 (Product Accountability — raw material, special processes, functional tests), Form 3 (Characteristic Accountability — dimensional results). Process capability data collected during trial-run phase BEFORE FAI (n ≥ 30 parts recommended) should demonstrate KCs meet contractual Ppk thresholds. Common aerospace thresholds: Ppk ≥ 1.33 standard, ≥ 1.67 for safety-critical or Special Characteristics — but specific thresholds are CONTRACT-DEPENDENT, not universally mandated by AS9102 itself. AS13003 (Process Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) addresses KC identification and variation management but does not itself set universal Ppk thresholds. FAI measurements confirm conformance on first production article; FAI supplements but does not replace trial-run capability study. IMPORTANT: In LRIP programs where n≥30 trial-run is impractical, document capability assessment limitations and establish risk-accepted inspection plans with customer concurrence.
summary: Task: First Article Inspection coordination

label: Coordinate FAI per AS9102 requirements
content: Coordinate FAI per AS9102 Rev C. System steps: (1) Identify FAI-triggering events — new part number, supplier change, engineering change affecting form/fit/function, production break >2 years, process change. (2) Issue FAI workorder to supplier with: drawing revision, KC list from FMEA, measurement method requirements, Form 1/2/3 templates. (3) Supplier produces FAI article(s) on production-representative tooling/process (same CNC program, fixtures, materials as intended production — reject hand-fit or prototype-method FAI articles). (4) Supplier completes Form 1 (part number accountability), Form 2 (material certs, special process results, functional tests), Form 3 (dimensional results for every characteristic). (5) Quality Engineer reviews: all characteristics measured and conforming, material/special-process certs traceable, measurement system adequate (Gage R&R documented). (6) Sign-off gate: Quality + Engineering approve; document any risk-acceptance notes (e.g., capability below target with 100% inspection mitigation). LRIP ADAPTATION: For programs with n<30 trial-run parts, attach capability assessment (Pp/Ppk with confidence intervals) alongside FAI conformance results; document assessment limitations and customer concurrence on risk-accepted inspection plans.
summary: System: Coordinate FAI per AS9102 requirements

label: Verify FAI completeness and approve for production release
content: Verify FAI completeness before production release. Decision criteria: (1) All Form 3 characteristics measured and within spec — any nonconformance requires disposition (Use-As-Is with engineering justification, or reject/rework). (2) Form 2 material certs traceable to mill test reports; special process certs (heat treat, plating, NDT) reference accredited processor. (3) Trial-run capability data (Pp/Ppk) meets contractual thresholds — standard: Ppk≥1.33 for CC, ≥1.67 for SC; if below target, document risk-acceptance with mitigation (typically 100% inspection for first N units). (4) Measurement system verified: Gage R&R <10% of tolerance for tight tolerances (excellent), 10-30% acceptable for wider tolerances with documented justification, >30% unacceptable — upgrade equipment or fixture. Measurement uncertainty U95 must satisfy discrimination ratio: U95 ≤ tolerance/4 (4:1 per AIAG MSA) or ≤ tolerance/10 (10:1 per ANSI/ASME B89.7.3.1 for high-confidence applications). (5) Control Plan approved with SPC chart type, sampling frequency, and reaction plan. PRODUCTION RELEASE CONDITIONS: Approve with conditions (e.g., '100% inspection first 50 units', 'SPC charting every 5th part', 'monthly supplier SPC data review'). Require Quality Manager + Engineering + Program Manager sign-off. For Partial FAI (PFAI): verify only changed characteristics are re-measured and prior full FAI is referenced for unchanged characteristics. PFAI SAMPLE SIZE: n=3 minimum for single-feature changes; n=5-8 for multiple features or tolerance stack-up involvement.
summary: Decision: Verify FAI completeness and approve for production release

label: Approve and archive FAI package in quality records
content:
Coordinate First Article Inspection per AS9102B. Ensure FAI package includes Form 1
(Part Number Accountability), Form 2 (Product Accountability — raw material, special
process, functional test), and Form 3 (Characteristic Accountability — all drawing
dimensions with actual measured values). Verify all characteristics meet drawing
requirements. Obtain customer approval if required. Archive in quality records with
part number and revision traceability.
summary: Output: Approve and archive FAI package in quality records

label: Supply Chain Manager
content: Supply Chain Manager in Manufacturing & Operations. Responsible for supplier performance, procurement strategy, and inventory coordination. In high-mix manufacturing: manage supplier delivery cadence to align with Kanban pull signals; ensure purchase orders specify pull-system delivery frequency, right-of-access for supermarket audits, and quality acceptance criteria. Coordinate 12-week material forecasts with Production Scheduler. Monitor supplier on-time delivery and lead-time consistency; escalate variability as a constraint signal to Operations. Own supplier scorecards and periodic reviews; ensure suppliers can sustain Kanban delivery cadence.
summary: Role: Supply Chain Manager in Manufacturing & Operations

label: Supplier management and procurement
content: Supplier management and procurement for aerospace LRIP. SUPPLIER PERFORMANCE SCORECARD (monthly): On-time delivery ≥95%, lead-time consistency (std dev ≤2 weeks of stated), quality AQL compliance ≥99%, expedite responsiveness <48-hour quote turnaround. SINGLE-SOURCE RISK: Budget 5-10% premium to maintain secondary source qualification for critical materials; justify against $500K+ production halt cost from single-source delay. Maintain secondary suppliers in 'warm standby' with 1-2 qualification orders per year. SUPPLIER VISIBILITY (tiered): Critical suppliers (long lead, single-source) — monthly availability + lead-time inquiry (~30 min/month supplier effort); Standard suppliers — quarterly status; Commodity — ERP demand plan only, no supplier forecasting burden. LEAD-TIME MANAGEMENT: 16-26 week aerospace material lead times require procurement triggers at lead-time minus 2-4 weeks depending on tier confidence. Track supplier lead-time distribution from recent 20-30 POs to calculate safety stock variability inputs. Do NOT request 12-month forecasts from suppliers (creates bullwhip effect); share only Tier 1 confirmed purchases and quarterly Tier 2 planning quantities.
summary: Task: Supplier management and procurement

label: Manage purchase orders and supplier performance in ERP
summary: System: Manage purchase orders and supplier performance in ERP

label: Select suppliers based on quality, cost, delivery, and AS9100 compliance
content: Supplier selection for LRIP with immature supply base (job shops without aerospace QMS experience). Decision factors: (1) Quality system baseline — ISO 9001 or equivalent documented QMS (do NOT require AS9100D certification as gate; use as development roadmap). Minimum: documented procedures for revision control, material traceability, NCR closure, and calibration. (2) Process capability — trial-run Pp/Ppk on representative geometry (n=10-15 acceptable for LRIP; target Ppk≥1.33; accept 1.0-1.33 with 100% inspection mitigation). (3) Measurement capability — Gage R&R <20% for wider tolerances, <10% for tight (<±0.01"). Reject if >25%. (4) Capacity to scale from prototype to 10-50 units/variant. (5) Lead-time commitment with ±σ variability range. (6) Willingness to participate in joint P-FMEA and accept on-site audits. (7) NADCAP roadmap feasibility if special processes (12-18 months post-award). TIERED CLASSIFICATION: Tier A (Ppk≥1.33, R&R<10%, QMS baseline) → full production approval after FAI. Tier B (Ppk 1.0-1.33, R&R<20%, OR immature QMS but strong process discipline) → conditional approval, 100% incoming inspection first 30 units, weekly SPC reviews. Do Not Award (Ppk<1.0, R&R>25%, no traceability discipline, audit-resistant). TIERED QUALIFICATION PHASES: Phase 1 (pre-award, 4-8 weeks) — capability trial + baseline assessment. Phase 2 (8-12 weeks) — joint P-FMEA, onsite audit, conditional approval for FAI. Phase 3 — FAI sign-off + Control Plan approval = full production release. Risk-accept suppliers with documented mitigation rather than waiting for full aerospace certification. PROTOTYPE-TO-PRODUCTION TRANSITION VALIDATION: Critical distinction — suppliers executing prototype orders on development equipment must demonstrate transition to stable production processes before LRIP commitment. Validation requires: (a) FAI on production equipment with production operators (reject hand-fit or prototype-method FAI articles); (b) confirmed production lead times vs. prototype lead times (production often longer due to shared equipment scheduling); (c) assessment of whether supplier capacity model assumes dedicated or shared equipment for your program; (d) verification that supplier SPC/quality controls are implemented on production line, not just engineering lab. Red flag: supplier quotes production pricing but hasn't transitioned from prototype tooling/equipment.
summary: Decision: Select suppliers based on quality, cost, delivery, and AS9100 compliance

label: Issue purchase orders with quality and compliance flowdowns
content:
Process procurement per AS9100 Section 8.4 and FAR/DFARS flowdown requirements. Issue
purchase orders with applicable quality clauses (right of access, first article requirements,
special process certifications), material specifications, DFARS clauses (252.225-7001 Buy
American, 252.225-7012 specialty metals if applicable), and delivery requirements. Maintain
supplier performance scorecards and conduct periodic supplier reviews.
summary: Output: Issue purchase orders with quality and compliance flowdowns

label: Material planning and inventory control
content: Material Planning and Inventory Control: Balance cost, customer service, and inventory turns in multi-shift, high-mix environments. Hybrid approach: use Kanban (pull) for high-velocity items (typically 70–80% of volume, 20% of SKU count); use MRP for slow-moving and long-lead-time items. Kanban sizing: Cards = (D × (LT + k×σ_LT)) / Container_Qty + 1 batch_card. D = average daily demand; LT = mean lead time in shifts; σ_LT = lead-time standard deviation (measure across ≥10 occurrences per shift); k = 1.65 for 95% service level. Adjust card counts monthly per demand forecast; implement tiered Kanban (red/yellow/green) for seasonal peaks/troughs. Monitor: inventory turns, stockout events (<2% target for aerospace), WIP age (detect excess buffer creep daily).
summary: Task: Material planning and inventory control

label: Run MRP in ERP system for material requirements planning
content: Run MRP in ERP system for material requirements planning: MRP is best suited for low-velocity SKUs (bottom 20–30% by volume), long-lead-time procurements, and seasonal/campaign items with lumpy demand. MRP calculates planned orders; output is a procurement forecast. In hybrid multi-mix strategy: use MRP for supplier planning (12-week window); use Kanban for fast-moving internal pull. Do NOT run MRP on items already under Kanban (creates double-ordering and inventory confusion). Review MRP exceptions (late orders, obsolete stock) weekly against Kanban pull signals.
summary: System: Run MRP in ERP system for material requirements planning

label: Optimize inventory levels balancing carrying cost vs. stockout risk
content: Optimize inventory levels balancing carrying cost vs. stockout risk: In Kanban systems, inventory is tuned via card counts, not ERP safety-stock parameters. Calculate baseline cards: Cards = (D × (LT + k×σ_LT)) / Container_Qty + 1, where k=1.65 for 95% service level. Pilot one product family with Monte Carlo simulation (vary upstream lead time by ±1σ; count stockout events). Adjust bi-weekly: increase cards if stockouts exceed 2%/day; decrease if containers sit full >3 days. Seasonal tiering: reduce cards during slow periods (cut carrying cost); increase during peak demand (prevent stockouts). Document each adjustment; conduct quarterly audit for dead cards (never pulled in 30 days, indicating obsolete SKU).
summary: Decision: Optimize inventory levels balancing carrying cost vs. stockout risk

label: Generate material requirements plan with lead time analysis
content:
Execute MRP run to generate material requirements plan. Include long-lead items with
procurement timelines, safety stock calculations, shelf-life management for perishable
materials (prepreg, adhesives, sealants), and lot traceability requirements per AS9100
Section 8.5.2. Flag items requiring government-furnished material (GFM) coordination.
Identify single-source risks and develop mitigation strategies.
summary: Output: Generate material requirements plan with lead time analysis

label: Facilities Manager
summary: Role: Facilities Manager in Manufacturing & Operations

label: Facility maintenance and capital planning
content: Develop and execute preventive and predictive maintenance (PPM) programs for all manufacturing facility systems (HVAC, compressed air, electrical, cranes, process equipment). Manage capital improvement projects through full lifecycle: scope definition, budgeting, contractor procurement, execution, and closeout. Track facility condition assessments (FCA) to prioritize capital investment and justify budget requests. Plan and schedule major equipment and infrastructure upgrades with 3–5 year capital forecast. Coordinate maintenance windows with Production Manager to minimize production downtime; schedule major PM activities during planned production breaks. Maintain CMMS (computerized maintenance management system) work-order records for AS9100D §7.1.3 infrastructure compliance.
summary: Task: Facility maintenance and capital planning for aerospace manufacturing facilities

label: Monitor environmental controls and calibration schedules
content: Monitor environmental controls and calibration schedules for laboratory and manufacturing process areas. ENVIRONMENTAL MONITORING: Log temperature and humidity in CMM/measurement room at ≥1-hour intervals via BAS or standalone data logger; alert threshold ±2°F from 68°F setpoint (precision measurement room) and ±5% RH deviation from target. Flag all measurements taken during excursions as suspect pending Quality Manager review. If 3 consecutive out-of-range readings, escalate to HVAC maintenance. CALIBRATION SCHEDULE MANAGEMENT: Maintain equipment calibration register (equipment ID, serial number, calibration due date, assigned vendor, cert file reference). Issue 30-day advance reminders (ERP or calendar alert) for each instrument due. Pull out-of-calibration instruments from service immediately; apply RED OUT-OF-CALIBRATION tag; restrict from productive use until recertified by accredited source. OUT-OF-CALIBRATION IMPACT ASSESSMENT: If instrument found expired, audit all measurements since last valid calibration; if any accepted result falls within U95 of the tolerance limit, issue nonconformance and re-inspect affected parts. RECORDS RETENTION: Environmental logs ≥3 years; calibration certificates ≥6 years (ISO 17025 §7.5 minimum) or contract-specified duration (FAR 52.246-15 requires 15 years for FAI records — use the longer period). Coordinate with Quality Manager before any environmental system maintenance that interrupts monitoring continuity.
summary: System: Monitor environmental controls and calibration schedules

label: Schedule maintenance to minimize production impact
summary: Decision: Schedule maintenance to minimize production impact

label: Issue facility maintenance report and compliance certificates
content:
Manage facility maintenance per AS9100 Section 7.1.3 infrastructure requirements. Monitor
cleanroom classifications per ISO 14644 (particle counts, temperature, humidity). Maintain
calibration program per ISO 17025 / ANSI Z540.3. Schedule preventive maintenance during
non-production windows. Document ESD control per ANSI/ESD S20.20. Maintain facility
security per NISPOM 32 CFR 117 for classified programs.
summary: Output: Issue facility maintenance report and compliance certificates

Ensure completion of required training: cybersecurity awareness (annual), insider threat

summary: Output: Issue onboarding package with security briefing acknowledgments

label: Workforce planning and compliance
content: Aerospace manufacturing workforce planning requires balancing skilled labor scarcity against variable production demand. KEY PLANNING ELEMENTS: (1) Staffing model — target 70-75% technician utilization for low-rate production (not 85%); remaining 25-30% absorbs training, rework, setup variability, and cross-training rotation. Formula: Base Technicians = Annual Production Hours / (2000 hrs/yr × 0.72 utilization); add 10-15% buffer for demand variability >±30% month-to-month. (2) Skill inventory — maintain competency matrix tracking each technician's certification level per process/equipment; update monthly; flag single-point-of-failure processes (<2 qualified operators). (3) Hiring pipeline — experienced aerospace technicians have 3-6 month recruitment cycles; maintain rolling job postings for critical specializations; internal development from non-aerospace backgrounds requires 8-12 week ramp to independent operation. (4) Compliance — AS9100D Section 7.2 requires documented competence evidence for personnel affecting product quality; maintain training records, certification dates, and recertification schedules. (5) Demand alignment — limit concurrent program assignments to 2 per technician to reduce context-switching overhead; each technician change on complex assemblies can add 15-30% time. COMMON PITFALL: Staffing to average demand creates chronic overtime during peaks and underutilization during troughs; staff to 75th percentile demand and use trough periods for cross-training and documentation.
summary: Task: Workforce planning and compliance
