label: Finance
summary: Department: Finance

label: Financial Analyst
summary: Role: Financial Analyst in Finance

label: Program financial analysis and EVM reporting
content: Program financial analysis and EVM reporting for aerospace/defense programs. KEY ACTIVITIES: (1) Maintain Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB) per ANSI/EIA-748 — PMB = sum of all control account budgets + undistributed budget; Management Reserve (MR) held outside PMB at program level. (2) Collect monthly EVM data: BCWP by earned value method, ACWP from accounting system, BCWS from time-phased IMS. (3) Compute cost and schedule variances at WBS element, control account, and total program level. (4) Prepare IPMR per DI-MGMT-81861A: Format 1 (WBS cost/schedule), Format 2 (Organizational), Format 3 (Baseline changes and MR status), Format 4 (Staffing), Format 5 (Problem Analysis narratives with CAPs for significant variances). (5) Support Integrated Baseline Review (IBR): validate PMB logic, resource loading, and EV method assignment per DCMA-MAN 3101. (6) Compare independent EAC to contractor self-assessment; flag divergence >10%. (7) Monitor MR burn rate — MR < 5% BAC triggers escalation and potential OTB evaluation. (8) OTB variance preservation rule: cumulative cost variance at rebaselining must be isolated in an over-target budget bucket, not absorbed into new PMB.
summary: Task: Program financial analysis and EVM reporting

label: Generate EVM metrics from cost/schedule data in project controls system
content: Generate EVM metrics from cost/schedule data in project controls system. METRIC GENERATION WORKFLOW: (1) Load Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB) from IMS export — map each WBS control account to its earned value method: Discrete effort (tangible measurable output — hardware builds, tests, inspections; BCWP = % complete × BAC or weighted milestone credit), Level of Effort/LOE (ongoing support — PM, configuration management, quality surveillance; BCWP = BCWS by definition, never shows SV — overuse masks schedule problems), Apportioned Effort (budgeted as fixed ratio to a related discrete base task — e.g., quality inspection at 15% of production WO labor; BCWP = ratio × BCWP_base_task), Milestone (100% credit on defined contractual event — CDRL delivery, review completion, test article acceptance). (2) Compute variances: CV = BCWP − ACWP (negative = over cost); SV = BCWP − BCWS (negative = behind schedule). (3) Compute indices: CPI = BCWP/ACWP; SPI = BCWP/BCWS; TCPI = (BAC − EV)/(BAC − AC) to complete to original budget; TCPI > 1.10 signals recovery is unrealistic. (4) EAC methods: BAC/CPI (stable performers), AC + (BAC−EV)/(CPI×SPI) (schedule-critical programs), AC + independent bottom-up ETC (program re-estimate). (5) VAC = BAC − EAC; negative VAC requires customer notification when |VAC| > 10% BAC.
summary: System: Generate EVM metrics from cost/schedule data in project controls system

label: Analyze cost and schedule variances, project estimate at completion
content: Analyze cost and schedule variances at control account and WBS element level. Steps: (1) Compute CV = BCWP − ACWP and SV = BCWP − BCWS for current period and cumulative. (2) Flag significant variances (typically |CV| or |SV| >10% of BAC or contract threshold) for root-cause narrative in IPMR Format 5. (3) Distinguish cause categories: cost overrun (material price, rework, labor inefficiency, design-change-driven), schedule slip (resource constraint, supplier delay, engineering risk). (4) Project EAC using multiple methods: EAC = BAC/CPI (historical efficiency continues), EAC = BAC/(CPI×SPI) (schedule slip affects cost), or independent bottoms-up ETC + ACWP. (5) Compute TCPI = (BAC − BCWP)/(BAC − ACWP) for original budget and (BAC − BCWP)/(EAC − ACWP) for revised EAC. TCPI >1.10 signals unrealistic recovery — evaluate OTB need. (6) Track CPI/SPI trends over 3+ months; cumulative CPI stabilizes by ~20% completion. (7) Develop Corrective Action Plans targeting measurable CPI/SPI recovery, not just narrative explanation.
summary: Decision: Analyze cost and schedule variances, project estimate at completion

label: Publish monthly EVM report (CPR/IPMR Format 1-5)
content: Prepare Integrated Program Management Report (IPMR) per DI-MGMT-81861A (supersedes CPR DI-MGMT-81466A). Five formats: Format 1 (WBS) — cost and schedule performance by WBS element through at least three levels; mandatory columns: BCWS, BCWP, ACWP, CV, SV, EAC, VAC per element. Format 2 (Organizational) — same metrics by performing organization (IPT or functional). Format 3 (Baseline) — PMB change log: MR draws, BCPs authorized, undistributed budget movements, OTB/OTS authorizations. Format 4 (Staffing) — direct labor hours by skill category, actual vs. plan. Format 5 (Problem Analysis) — required for WBS elements where |CV| > threshold (typically ±$100K or ±10% of control account budget): root cause, corrective action plan (CAP) with owner and recovery date, risk of further slippage. IPMDAR (DI-MGMT-81861B) delivers the same data via structured XML/JSON submission for automated DAMIR/EVM Central ingestion — supersedes manual Format 1-5 for programs using a certified contractor EVM system. THRESHOLDS: Alert CPI < 0.95 or SPI < 0.90; CPI < 0.90 requires formal CAP with OTB assessment. Significance threshold for variance narrative: |CV| or |SV| > 10% of control account BAC. VAC = BAC − EAC; |VAC| > 10% BAC requires customer notification and re-estimate justification.
summary: Output: Publish monthly EVM report (CPR/IPMR Format 1-5)

label: Indirect rate development and monitoring
summary: Task: Indirect rate development and monitoring

label: Calculate actual indirect rates against provisional billing rates
summary: System: Calculate actual indirect rates against provisional billing rates

label: Recommend rate adjustments based on actual vs. planned spending
summary: Decision: Recommend rate adjustments based on actual vs. planned spending

label: Issue indirect rate analysis report with forward pricing recommendations
content: Monitor indirect rates (fringe, overhead, G&A, material handling) against provisional billing rates approved by DCMA. Calculate actual rates monthly and project year-end rates. Prepare forward pricing rate proposal per FAR 42.1701 and DFARS 242.703-1. Document rate base allocation methodology per CAS Disclosure Statement (required at modified CAS threshold: $2M; full CAS threshold: $50M single award). Flag significant rate variances for internal management review — note: no regulation specifies a fixed percentage deviation as an automatic DCAA audit trigger; DCAA scrutiny is risk-based. Support annual incurred cost audit per FAR 42.705.
summary: Output: Issue indirect rate analysis report with forward pricing recommendations

label: Cost Accountant
summary: Role: Cost Accountant in Finance

label: Cost pool management and allocation
summary: Task: Cost pool management and allocation

label: Maintain cost pools and allocation bases in accounting system
summary: System: Maintain cost pools and allocation bases in accounting system

label: Ensure consistent allocation methodology per CAS and Disclosure Statement
summary: Decision: Ensure consistent allocation methodology per CAS and Disclosure Statement

label: Generate cost pool summary with allocation calculations
content:
Maintain cost pool structure per approved Disclosure Statement and CAS requirements.
Manage direct cost pools (by contract/WBS), indirect pools (engineering overhead,
manufacturing overhead, material handling, G&A), and service center allocations.
Verify allocation bases (direct labor dollars, total cost input, material costs) are
consistently applied per CAS 418. Prepare monthly cost pool summary with allocation
calculations. Support DCAA audits with documentation trails.
summary: Output: Generate cost pool summary with allocation calculations

label: Contract cost-to-complete analysis
summary: Task: Contract cost-to-complete analysis

label: Compile actual costs and commitments from accounting system
content: Compile Actual Cost of Work Performed (ACWP) from the accounting system for EVM reporting. Key steps: (1) Extract direct labor charges by charge number/WBS element from payroll or timekeeping system. (2) Add applied indirect costs (overhead, G&A, fringe) at contractually established billing rates. (3) Capture subcontractor costs from accounts payable — use accruals for invoices not yet received to avoid ACWP lag. (4) Add material costs: direct material issued to job + subcontractor material; include scrap and rework costs in the period incurred. (5) Capture open commitments (purchase orders not yet invoiced) — commitments are not ACWP but required for EAC completeness and LOC monitoring (FAR 52.232-20/52.232-22). (6) Reconcile ACWP to general ledger by WBS charge number before submitting EVM data to program office. (7) Flag timing mismatches between accounting cutoff and IMS performance period — a common source of period-level CPI distortion.
summary: System: Compile actual costs and commitments from accounting system

label: Develop estimate to complete based on technical progress and burn rate
summary: Decision: Develop estimate to complete based on technical progress and burn rate

label: Submit cost-to-complete report with risk-adjusted projections
content:
Prepare contract cost-to-complete analysis including actual costs to date (by cost
element), open commitments, estimate to complete (ETC) by WBS element, and estimate
at completion (EAC). Apply risk-adjusted projection methodology considering technical
progress, burn rate trends, and identified risks. Compare EAC to contract ceiling and
funded value. Flag contracts approaching 85% of ceiling for management attention.
Report per FAR 52.232-20 (Limitation of Cost) and FAR 52.232-22 (Limitation of Funds).
summary: Output: Submit cost-to-complete report with risk-adjusted projections

label: Program Management
summary: Department: Program Management

label: Program Manager
content: Program Manager in aerospace/defense programs. Responsible for integrated program planning (IMS per DI-MGMT-81650), earned value management (EVM per ANSI/EIA-748), risk management (MIL-STD-882E), contract deliverable management (CDRLs), configuration management (per MIL-HDBK-61B, supersedes MIL-STD-973), and customer relationship. In LRIP environments: own the production readiness review cadence, chair Engineering Change Review Board (ECRB), manage configuration baseline freeze milestones (DB-1 prototype, DB-2 pilot, DB-3 low-rate production), and balance design evolution pace against supplier qualification schedule. Key tension in LRIP: design maturity vs. production commitment — PM must enforce frozen design windows to prevent supplier requalification spiral while allowing necessary flight-test-driven changes.
summary: Role: Program Manager in Program Management

label: Integrated program planning and execution
content: Integrated program planning for multi-program environments where prototype, pilot, and mature production share resources. Key activities: (1) Maintain resource-loaded IMS per DI-MGMT-81650 with program-tier priority flags (Tier 0=mature production, Tier 1=pilot/trial-run, Tier 1B=prototype). (2) Coordinate cross-program resource conflicts at weekly Production Control Review (30 min Monday, owner=Shop Floor Supervisor). (3) Enforce frozen schedule windows per tier: Tier 0=6-week freeze, Tier 1=4-week freeze with weekly replan, Tier 1B=2-week rolling. (4) Maintain Heijunka level-loaded schedule template: 40% capacity reserved Tier 0, 30% Tier 1 (including SPC data collection), 20% flex (Tier 1B + rework), 10% constraint buffer (setups, PM, tool changes). (5) Link IMS milestones to manufacturing readiness gates: FAI completion, Ppk validation, operator certification level achieved. RESOURCE CONFLICT DECISION RULES: When Tier 1B change request arrives: (a) check Tier 0 frozen window—reject if impacted; (b) check Tier 1 FAI/capability phase—defer Tier 1B if active; (c) check operator utilization—if >85% defer/scale Tier 1B, 65-85% conditional approve with weekly monitoring, <65% approve immediately; (d) verify changeover time budget—if cumulative weekly changeover >20 hours, escalate to PM. LRIP TENSION: Balance design evolution pace (Tier 1B) against supplier qualification schedule and production commitment (Tier 0). PM enforces frozen design windows to prevent FAI/supplier requalification spiral while accommodating flight-test-driven changes.
summary: Task: Integrated program planning and execution

label: Maintain IMS in Microsoft Project/Primavera per DI-MGMT-81650
content: Maintain IMS in MS Project or Primavera with multi-program resource loading. DI-MGMT-81650 (Integrated Master Schedule CDRL) defines IMS delivery requirements for DoD contracts but does not prescribe internal scheduling methods—adapt its WBS/milestone structure to your facility's needs. System steps: (1) Define resource pools (equipment, operators by certification level, measurement systems) as shared resources across all programs. (2) Assign program tier priority (Tier 0 committed > Tier 1 pilot > Tier 1B prototype) as resource leveling constraint. (3) Build weekly schedule template with capacity allocation proportional to commitment level—example starting point: 40% Tier 0, 30% Tier 1, 20% flex/rework, 10% maintenance/setup buffer. Adjust ratios based on actual demand mix. (4) Run critical path analysis weekly; flag resource conflicts where prototype ECOs overlap committed delivery windows. (5) Publish rolling schedule with frozen windows scaled by tier (longer freeze for committed, shorter for prototype); distribute via constraint dashboard + ERP. (6) Track schedule variance with tier-appropriate targets (tighter for committed production, looser for prototype); measure prototype progress by engineering gate adherence rather than calendar dates.
summary: System: Maintain IMS in Microsoft Project/Primavera per DI-MGMT-81650

label: Analyze critical path and authorize schedule recovery actions
content: Analyze critical path across concurrent programs sharing constrained resources. Decision framework: (1) Identify weekly bottleneck using Capacity Constraint Protocol (equipment utilization %, operator availability, material supply status, measurement queue depth, facility space). (2) If critical path runs through constraint resource, apply Theory of Constraints exploitation: schedule highest-priority Tier 0 work on bottleneck first, subordinate all other work to bottleneck pace, elevate constraint only after exploitation exhausted. (3) Schedule recovery options by tier: Tier 0=authorize overtime, parallel-route to secondary machine, expedite material; Tier 1=compress trial-run schedule (risk: wider Ppk confidence intervals if n reduced); Tier 1B=defer to next available window or split across shifts. (4) Recovery cost authorization: Shop Floor Supervisor up to $2K, Program Manager up to $10K, Plant Manager above $10K. (5) Document recovery decision with root-cause analysis in shift log; feed into monthly management review for pattern detection. ESCALATION PROTOCOL: Step 1—Shop Floor Supervisor initial triage (30 min): is request within Tier 1B scope with no cross-tier impact? Step 2—Production Scheduler feasibility check (1 hr): equipment, operator, material, changeover time all available? Step 3—Production Control Review (30 min weekly): Supervisor + QM + PM evaluate trade-offs using EVM variance, Ppk status, utilization %, PM windows, material days-on-hand. Decision outcomes: Approved, Approved-with-conditions, or Deferred-to-date. Step 4—PM formal approval + stakeholder notification (1 day). Step 5—Weekly monitoring of approved work for utilization drift.
summary: Decision: Analyze critical path and authorize schedule recovery actions

label: Publish monthly program status report with EVM dashboard
content:
Manage integrated master schedule per DI-MGMT-81650 and NDIA PASEG guidelines. Maintain
resource-loaded schedule with critical path analysis. Publish monthly program status
report including: program overview, technical progress by WBS, schedule status (milestone
tracker, critical path analysis), cost status (EVM metrics, variance narratives), risk
register update (per MIL-STD-882E risk matrix), and action item tracker. Present at
monthly Program Management Review (PMR) with customer.
summary: Output: Publish monthly program status report with EVM dashboard

label: Risk and opportunity management
content: Risk and opportunity management per MIL-STD-882E and PMI framework. Maintain risk register with probability/impact scoring, mitigation plans, and residual risk assessment. LRIP-SPECIFIC RISKS: (1) Design-change-driven FAI restart — mitigate with frozen design windows and PFAI for minor changes. (2) Immature supplier quality escape — mitigate with tiered inspection (100% first 25 units, sampling thereafter). (3) Small-sample capability uncertainty — mitigate with confidence intervals on Ppk and 100% inspection buffer. (4) Supplier transition mid-program — mitigate with configuration traceability matrix linking serial ranges to suppliers. (5) NADCAP/AS9100D certification gaps — mitigate with risk-accepted qualification + development roadmap. (6) Cost overrun from immature cost baselines — mitigate with phased cost targets aligned to design/supplier maturity gates (prototype targets ≠ production targets). (7) Unattributed quality costs buried in overhead — mitigate with CoQ tracking by supplier, process, and defect category. (8) Material obsolescence from design changes during long-lead procurement — mitigate with design-freeze policy ≥120 days before production material commitment, scrap-cost tracking by ECO, and inventory-on-hand targets matched to design stability (lower inventory when design unstable). (9) Prototype-to-production staffing gap — mitigate with operator competency validation on pilot lot under production conditions (no engineering support), cross-training matrix with single-point-of-failure elimination. (10) Design-change cascade risk — when single design change triggers supplier requalification + fixture modification + WIP rework simultaneously, mitigate by requiring manufacturing impact assessment before ECO approval; if combined impact exceeds schedule buffer or cost threshold, defer change to next production lot boundary. (11) Design-change-introduced manufacturing risk — design changes may introduce new failure modes not covered by existing PFMEA; mitigate by requiring PFMEA update for any design change affecting process routing, tooling interface, or material specification; update Control Plan and SPC monitoring accordingly. IMPORTANT: Specific Ppk thresholds, cost thresholds, and buffer durations referenced in mitigation plans are program-dependent recommendations, not universal standards — always validate against contract requirements and customer flow-down. Monthly risk review: assess supplier capability trending, design maturity status, traceability audit findings, cost variance trending by category, material obsolescence exposure, operator competency trends, and design-change rate trending by program maturity level.
summary: Task: Risk and opportunity management

label: Maintain risk register with probability, impact, and mitigation plans
content: Maintain risk register with multi-program resource conflict risks. System: (1) Score risks using P×I matrix (1-5 each) per MIL-STD-882E. (2) LRIP multi-program specific risk categories: R1=Equipment bottleneck cascade (constraint machine failure delays all tiers), R2=Operator skill gap (cross-training incomplete when program ramps), R3=Measurement system saturation (CMM queue blocks FAI/SPC data collection), R4=Material supply disruption (long-lead alloy shortage affects multiple programs), R5=Engineering change collision (Tier 1B ECO requires same equipment during Tier 0 frozen window), R6=Maintenance deferral accumulation (>3 deferred PMs creates equipment reliability risk). (3) Mitigation strategies: R1=qualify backup machine + cross-train operators; R2=maintain operator rotation matrix with 12-week certification pipeline; R3=deploy portable gages for loose-tolerance SPC; R4=dual-source critical materials; R5=escalation protocol with decision matrix; R6=cap deferred PMs at 20% per month, escalate to Plant Manager if exceeded. (4) Monthly review: assess constraint drift pattern, supplier trending, certification pipeline adequacy.
summary: System: Maintain risk register with probability, impact, and mitigation plans

label: Accept, mitigate, transfer, or avoid risks based on program tolerance
content: Risk disposition decision for multi-program resource conflicts. Decision criteria: (1) ACCEPT risk when: probability <2 AND impact <2, or residual risk after mitigation is within program tolerance (e.g., 1-day Tier 1B schedule slip with no engineering gate impact). (2) MITIGATE when: P×I ≥6 and mitigation cost <10% of risk exposure; examples: add portable gage to reduce CMM bottleneck risk, schedule operator cross-training to reduce skill gap risk. (3) TRANSFER when: supplier-owned risk (material lead time, special process certification); transfer via contract quality clauses, supplier development roadmap with milestone penalties. (4) AVOID when: risk threatens Tier 0 customer delivery or safety-critical quality; examples: refuse to defer critical equipment calibration, refuse to assign uncertified operator to Tier 0 work. ESCALATION: If risk disposition requires budget >$5K or customer communication, escalate to Program Manager + Plant Manager joint decision. Document disposition rationale for AS9100D management review traceability.
summary: Decision: Accept, mitigate, transfer, or avoid risks based on program tolerance

label: Update risk register and present at risk review board
content:
Manage program risk per MIL-STD-882E and PMI risk management framework. Maintain risk
register with: risk ID, description, root cause, probability (1-5), impact (1-5),
risk score (P×I), risk response strategy (accept/mitigate/transfer/avoid), mitigation
actions with owners and due dates, and residual risk assessment. Identify opportunities
(positive risks) for schedule acceleration or cost savings. Present top 10 risks at
monthly risk board. Maintain risk burn-down chart.
summary: Output: Update risk register and present at risk review board

label: Contract deliverable management
content: Contract deliverable management in multi-program environment. Key activities: (1) Maintain CDRL tracker with due dates, responsible IPT leads, review status, and submission dates per contract exhibit. (2) Coordinate technical content across programs when deliverables share common processes (e.g., manufacturing readiness assessment covering multiple part families on shared equipment). (3) Ensure production status reports accurately reflect multi-program schedule performance—on-time delivery %, process capability trending, constraint status. (4) Manage customer expectations when Tier 1B prototype schedule flexibility impacts Tier 1 pilot production milestones visible in CDRLs. (5) Process deliverables through internal quality review (Quality Manager sign-off) before customer submission. (6) Track government review cycles and acceptance; maintain action item log from customer feedback. (7) Proactively address CPARS ratings by presenting multi-program execution KPIs (on-time delivery, quality escape rate, corrective action closure) at regular performance discussions.
summary: Task: Contract deliverable management

label: Track CDRLs, milestones, and contractual obligations in program tracker
content: Track CDRLs, milestones, and contractual obligations in program tracker system. System steps: (1) Enter all CDRLs from contract exhibit into tracker with: DI number, title, frequency (one-time/recurring), due date, responsible engineer/IPT lead, review chain (Engineering → Quality → PM → customer). (2) Link CDRL due dates to IMS milestones—flag when production schedule slip threatens deliverable timeline. (3) For recurring deliverables (monthly status reports, quarterly reviews): auto-generate reminder 10 business days before due; assign content owner. (4) Track review status: Draft → Internal Review → PM Approval → Submitted → Customer Accepted/Returned. (5) Maintain revision history for resubmitted deliverables. (6) Dashboard: overdue CDRLs (red), due within 10 days (yellow), on track (green). (7) Integrate with risk register: if CDRL at risk due to multi-program resource conflict, flag in weekly Production Control Review for priority resolution.
summary: System: Track CDRLs, milestones, and contractual obligations in program tracker

label: Prioritize deliverables based on contractual deadlines and customer feedback
content: Prioritize deliverables when multi-program resource conflicts affect CDRL timelines. Decision criteria: (1) Contractual penalty exposure: prioritize deliverables with liquidated damages or CPARS impact over informational submittals. (2) Customer gate dependency: if CDRL feeds customer milestone decision (e.g., production readiness review, Milestone C), prioritize over routine status reports. (3) Cross-program data dependency: if deliverable requires production data from Tier 1 trial-run still in progress, negotiate submission timeline with customer rather than submitting incomplete data. (4) Quality review bottleneck: if Quality Manager review queue is saturated, triage by penalty exposure + gate dependency score. (5) Resource conflict protocol: if CDRL preparation competes with production execution for same engineer's time, Production Control Review decides allocation; default = production execution wins unless CDRL penalty exceeds production delay cost.
summary: Decision: Prioritize deliverables based on contractual deadlines and customer feedback

label: Submit contract deliverables with quality review and customer coordination
content:
Manage contract deliverables per CDRL exhibit and Data Item Descriptions. Coordinate
technical content development with IPT leads. Process through internal quality review
prior to submission. Track government review and acceptance per contract terms. Manage
customer relationship including status meetings, action item resolution, and performance
feedback. Ensure CPARS ratings are addressed proactively through regular performance discussions.
summary: Output: Submit contract deliverables with quality review and customer coordination

label: Heat treat subprocess qualification — AMS 2750 and AMS 2759/x verification
content: For heat treating special process suppliers, cert verification must go beyond AS9100D to include: (1) NADCAP AC7102 accreditation (heat treating commodity) — verify scope covers the specific process (vacuum, atmosphere, induction, etc.); (2) AMS 2750 (Pyrometry) compliance — confirm supplier has current Temperature Uniformity Survey (TUS) and System Accuracy Test (SAT) records; pyrometer calibration currency is a common lapse point and a direct quality escape risk; (3) AMS 2759/x approval — verify the supplier is approved to the specific drawing-called process spec (e.g., AMS 2759/1 for steel, AMS 2759/3 for precipitation hardening, AMS 2759/7 for overaging). Approval to the QMS cert alone does not confirm approval to the specific process spec.
summary: System: Heat treat cert verification beyond AS9100D — AMS 2750 pyrometry (TUS/SAT currency), AMS 2759/x drawing-specific process spec approval, and NADCAP AC7102 scope confirmation

label: OASIS database — independent AS9100 cert verification
content: Verify AS9100D certification independently via OASIS (oasis.sae-international.org), the SAE International database for aerospace QMS certifications. Do not rely solely on supplier-provided certificate PDFs — certs can be expired, suspended, or have scope exclusions that the PDF does not reflect. OASIS search by company name returns current certificate status, scope, expiration date, and certifying body. For NADCAP, use the eAuditNet portal (eauditnet.com) to verify accreditation commodity, job site, and expiration. Both checks take under 5 minutes and provide customer-independent evidence for the supplier qualification record.
summary: Decision: Use OASIS database and eAuditNet for independent, real-time AS9100D and NADCAP cert status verification — do not rely only on supplier-provided documents

label: Heat treat / special process supplier review cadence — new vs. established
content: For new special process suppliers (heat treating, NDT, welding, plating) with <12 months performance history, apply elevated surveillance: Month 0 (pre-award or contract kickoff): on-site assessment, verify process certs, review TUS/SAT records and process spec approvals. Months 1–6: monthly process performance report (yield, rework rate, cert status). Month 6: formal mid-year review — if 0 escapes and stable cert status, reduce to quarterly. Month 12: annual assessment decision — if performance stable, move to standard annual cadence; if any NCR or cert issue, extend elevated surveillance for additional 6 months. Document cadence rationale in supplier qualification record to satisfy AS9100D Section 8.4.1 monitoring requirements.
summary: Output: Elevated first-year surveillance cadence for new special process suppliers — monthly reports, mid-year review, annual assessment with exit criteria for standard cadence

label: Mid-contract cert lapse response — heat treat and NADCAP suppliers
content: If a heat treat supplier's AS9100D, NADCAP, or AMS 2759/x approval lapses during an active contract: (1) immediately place affected part numbers on hold — do not accept new certs of conformance until re-verification is complete; (2) notify the supplier in writing and request corrective action plan with reinstatement timeline; (3) pull any in-process or recently delivered hardware for review — cert lapse creates a retroactive quality escape risk depending on lapse duration; (4) for NADCAP lapse, notify your customer if AS9100D §8.4 or flow-down clauses require it; (5) document in supplier qualification record and reset surveillance cadence to elevated (monthly) upon reinstatement. Do not assume a reinstated cert retroactively covers work performed during the lapse period.
summary: Output: Immediate hold and corrective action steps when a heat treat or NADCAP supplier cert lapses mid-contract — hardware disposition, customer notification, and surveillance reset

label: CMMC compliance and CUI protection program
content: CMMC compliance program management covers: (1) CUI identification — document what data qualifies as CUI per the CUI Registry categories, where it is stored, processed, and transmitted (data flow diagram required for SSP); (2) SSP maintenance — update System Security Plan when system boundary, technology stack, or control implementation changes occur; (3) DFARS 252.204-7012 flow-down — required in all subcontracts where subcontractor will handle CUI; subcontractor must also post independent SPRS score; (4) POA&M tracking — maintain open items with target dates, responsible parties, and closure evidence; closed items must retain evidence; (5) Annual review — document internal review of full 800-171 compliance posture with responsible party sign-off.
summary: Task: CMMC compliance program management — CUI identification, SSP maintenance, DFARS 252.204-7012 flow-down, and POA&M tracking

label: CMMC Level 2 — SSP, POA&M, and DFARS 252.204-7012 operational requirements
content: CMMC Level 2 applies to any contract involving Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI). Operational requirements: (1) System Security Plan (SSP) — document all 110 NIST SP 800-171 controls, system boundary, and responsible parties; (2) Plan of Action & Milestones (POA&M) — track any control not yet fully implemented with remediation timeline; (3) DFARS 252.204-7012 — requires adequate security on all covered contractor information systems, 72-hour cyber incident reporting to DoD, and preservation of images for 90 days; (4) SPRS score submission — self-assessed score must be posted before contract award. C3PAO Assessment: assessors validate control implementation against all NIST SP 800-171A assessment objectives; there is no published minimum passing control count — all deficiencies are documented in the final assessment report and must be remediated before certification is issued. Cloud CUI: cloud service providers must meet FedRAMP Moderate equivalent authorization. Flow-down: DFARS 252.204-7012 must be passed to all subcontractors handling CUI. Common failure modes: incomplete CUI identification, SSP not updated after system changes, SPRS score not posted, flow-down omitted in subcontracts.
summary: System: CMMC Level 2 operational controls — SSP, POA&M, DFARS 252.204-7012, SPRS score posting, and CUI flow-down requirements

label: Workforce knowledge retention and tribal knowledge risk
content: Aerospace prototype orgs face acute knowledge retention risk: processes, customer quirks, tooling setups, and supplier relationships live in individuals rather than systems. Critical gaps: (1) no documented work instructions for recurring but infrequent processes; (2) engineering tribal knowledge not captured in the QMS; (3) single points of failure on cleared personnel (no cleared backup for key roles); (4) no structured onboarding for contract compliance knowledge — new hires learn FAR/DFARS by osmosis. Mitigation: knowledge capture sessions before retirements/departures, role-based procedure documentation tied to QMS, and cross-training matrices for critical cleared and regulatory roles. Knowledge loss is a root cause for repeat NCRs and audit findings that appear unrelated on the surface.
summary: Task: Workforce knowledge retention — capturing tribal knowledge, cleared personnel single points of failure, and compliance cross-training gaps in prototype orgs

label: ITAR Technology Control Plan — operational implementation vs. paper compliance
content: Most small-to-mid aerospace firms have an ITAR Technology Control Plan (TCP) that was templated at business formation and never operationalized. Functional TCP requires: (1) CJ (commodity jurisdiction) and ECCN classification documented for all products and technical data; (2) physical and electronic access controls for export-controlled data — not just a policy statement; (3) foreign person authorization tracking — all visitors, subcontractors, and employees require nationality verification; (4) license determination before any technical data sharing with foreign persons or export of hardware; (5) annual TCP review with documented acknowledgment by all staff with CUI/ITAR access. Recordkeeping: 22 CFR 122.5 requires all ITAR export records (licenses, agreements, shipping documents, technical data disclosures) to be retained for 5 years from date of export or expiration of license — not 6 years. Subcontractor reporting: no specific regulatory timeframe mandates a 24-hour sub-to-prime disclosure window; DDTC notification timelines are prime-to-government obligations. Penalty structure: criminal penalties up to $1M per violation and 20 years imprisonment; administrative debarment from federal contracting. Common failure: engineers share CAD files or specs with foreign vendors without export classification determination.
summary: System: ITAR TCP operational implementation — classification, foreign person controls, license determination, and debarment risk for aerospace prototype orgs

label: Short-run SPC and process capability for high-mix low-volume manufacturing
content: LRHM SPC CHALLENGE: Traditional X-bar/R charts require subgroups of n≥3-5 at same conditions — impossible when producing 5-50 parts/year per part number. STRATEGY SELECTION: (1) Per-part-number I-MR chart when volume ≥25 parts/year and single feature family; (2) DNOM (Deviation from Nominal) chart when multiple part numbers share same process/machine but differ in nominal — plot (X - Nominal) to overlay on single chart, accumulating n across part family; (3) Z-MR chart when part numbers differ in both nominal and tolerance — plot Z = (X - Nominal) / (Tolerance/k) where k=4 or 6; (4) Pre-control chart when lot size <10 and setup qualification is primary concern — 5 consecutive green (within ±T/4 of nominal) qualifies setup; (5) CUSUM (Cumulative Sum) chart when design changes shift process target mid-production — plot cumulative deviations from new target to detect drift separate from legitimate design shift. GAUGE RESOLUTION WARNING: When gauge resolution ≥25% of tolerance, measurements become near-discrete — variables charts (I-MR, X-bar/R) produce artificially 'chunky' control limits that make every point appear in-control. In this regime, switch to attribute charts: np/p chart (conforming vs. nonconforming) or c/u chart (defects per unit) are statistically sounder. Example: 0.01" resolution on 0.02" tolerance (0.04"–0.06") yields only 3 possible in-tolerance values — use p chart tracking fraction nonconforming instead of I-MR. DUAL MEASUREMENT STRATEGY: When contract mandates low-resolution device but process monitoring requires finer discrimination, use a higher-precision internal gauge (e.g., digital micrometer to 0.0001") for SPC charting while reserving the contractual device exclusively for acceptance inspection. Document both measurement systems in the Control Plan with distinct purposes; confirm with customer that SPC internal gauge is permissible for process monitoring (not acceptance). CONTROL LIMIT BOOTSTRAPPING: For first 10-15 parts, use tolerance-based control limits (UCL/LCL at ±2/3 of tolerance from nominal) as interim; transition to data-driven X-bar ± 2.66×MR-bar after 25+ measurements. PROCESS-CENTRIC SPC: When part-dimension sample sizes remain <10 indefinitely, supplement with machine parameter monitoring (spindle load, feed force, temperature) which generates continuous data stream regardless of part volume. TECHNICIAN ROTATION: When technicians rotate across production runs, perform ANOVA on trial-run data grouped by technician; if p<0.05, technician effect is significant — either establish technician-specific control limits or (preferred) standardize procedure/tooling to eliminate technician dependence before charting. AS9100D COMPLIANCE: Document SPC method selection rationale in Control Plan; reference AIAG SPC manual Section III for short-run techniques.
summary: Task: SPC strategy for LRHM environments where per-part-number lot sizes are too small for traditional control charts

label: I-MR charts and short-run SPC techniques for LRIP
content: For LRHM manufacturing where subgroup size n=1: use Individuals-Moving Range (I-MR) charts. Control limits: X-bar ± 3σ where σ is estimated from MR-bar/d2 (d2=1.128 for n=2 moving range). Complement with short-run techniques: DNOM (Deviation from Nominal) charts normalize measurements to nominal, enabling overlay of multiple part numbers on one chart. Z-MR charts standardize by process sigma for mixed-part families. Pre-control (green/yellow/red zones at ±T/4 and ±T/2 from nominal) requires only 5 consecutive green parts to qualify a setup — practical for LRIP. Process-centric SPC (spindle load, torque, temperature, feed rate) supplements part-dimension SPC when lot sizes preclude meaningful control limits.
summary: System: I-MR, DNOM, Z-MR, and pre-control chart selection and setup for low-volume individual-measurement SPC

label: Small-sample process capability estimation for LRHM
content: When n<25, use Pp/Ppk (overall performance indices using sample std dev) not Cp/Cpk (which assume stable, in-control process with sufficient data). Comparison: Cp/Cpk require Shewhart stability, n≥25 recommended; Pp/Ppk valid for any sample but confidence intervals are wide at n<10. For n<10: (1) Report Ppk with 95% CI using chi-squared method: CI_lower = Ppk × sqrt(chi2_lower/(n-1)); (2) Bootstrap resampling for Ppk CI with n=5–15; (3) Tolerance interval approach — verify that X% of population falls within spec at Y% confidence (e.g., 99%/95% per MIL-STD-1916). Targets: Ppk≥1.33 for CC at FAI, Ppk≥1.67 for SC. Pool capability across process family (same machine, same operation, similar geometry) to increase effective n when per-part-number samples are too small.
summary: System: Pp/Ppk vs Cp/Cpk selection, confidence intervals, and tolerance-interval alternatives for n<25 LRHM capability studies

label: Map AS9100D clause requirements to LRHM inspection and SPC controls
content: AS9100D clause-to-LRHM control mapping: §8.5.1 (production control) → process parameter monitoring + I-MR charts on key process inputs; §8.5.1.2 (validation of special processes) → NADCAP approval + qualification run records; §8.5.2 (identification and traceability) → serialized traveler per unit with op-by-op sign-off; §8.6 (release of products and services) → 100% inspection record with variable measurement data (not just pass/fail); §8.7 (nonconforming outputs) → MRB disposition logged with operation, operator, machine, material lot for escape Pareto; §9.1.1 (monitoring, measurement, analysis) → Pp/Ppk records at FAI and documented requalification triggers (setup change, tooling replacement, material lot change).
summary: Decision: Map AS9100D clauses 8.5–9.1 to specific SPC, inspection, and traceability controls for LRHM aerospace manufacturing

label: DFARS 252.227-7013 vs. 7014 — technical data and software rights framework
content: DFARS 252.227-7013 (Technical Data — Noncommercial Items): Three-tier structure based on funding origin. Unlimited Rights: data developed exclusively with government funds — government may release or disclose to any party. Government Purpose Rights (GPR): data developed with mixed funding — government use permitted internally and for competitive reprocurement, but commercial disclosure prohibited; standard restriction period is 5 years from contract award. Limited Rights: data developed exclusively at private expense — government internal use only; cannot be used for competitive reprocurement or disclosed externally. Contractor must assert Limited Rights by affixing DFARS-prescribed marking at time of delivery or rights default to unlimited. DFARS 252.227-7014 (Noncommercial Computer Software): Four-tier structure. Unlimited Rights (government-funded). Government Purpose Rights (mixed funding, same 5-year default). Restricted Rights: software developed exclusively at private expense — government may use one copy on one computer for internal purposes only; no reverse engineering or sublicense. SBIR data rights: separate protection period applies. Key distinction: 7013 uses 'Limited Rights'; 7014 uses 'Restricted Rights' — the permitted government use scope and remedies differ. Both clauses: failure to assert restrictions with proper markings at delivery constitutes waiver of rights.
summary: System: DFARS 252.227-7013 (technical data) vs. 7014 (noncommercial software) — funding-based three/four-tier rights allocation, assertion and marking requirements, and government use limitations

label: Corrective Action Report — AS9100 Rev D clause 10.2 quality escape requirements
content: Corrective Action Report (CAR) requirements are governed by AS9100 Rev D clause 10.2 (Nonconformity and Corrective Action) — not AS9102 or AS9103, which cover First Article Inspection (FAI) only. Required CAR sections: (1) Problem Description — specific failure with measured values vs. specification limits, escape point identification (where in the process was the nonconformance introduced and where was it not detected?), customer/program impact, and discovery date; (2) Containment — immediate actions: hold and segregate affected stock, 100% inspection of affected lot, customer notification if product already delivered; (3) Root Cause Analysis — use 5-Why or fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram; identify systemic root cause, not operator error proximate cause; CRITICAL: reject responses stating 'will be more careful' or 'operator retrained' without identifying the process-level enabling condition — require evidence of what PROCESS element changed; (4) Corrective Action — specific procedural, design, or training changes that eliminate the root cause; named responsible party and target completion date; (5) Effectiveness Verification — defined criteria proving correction worked; verification must be based on production data (Ppk ≥1.33 on post-CAR lot n≥30, witnessed production audit, Gage R&R confirming measurement system stability), NOT just supplier-documented response — documentation can be fabricated, witnessed production cannot; (6) Closure Evidence — test reports, rework records, training sign-offs retained in quality record system. SUPERFICIAL CAR PATTERN: Common failure mode — supplier documents corrective action addressing proximate cause (e.g., 'operator retrained') without process-level change (procedure revision number unchanged, SPC control limits not recalculated, offset-adjustment policy not documented). Evidence of systemic closure: revised procedure with new revision number posted at workstation, SPC chart updated with new control limits, measurement system re-verified via Gage R&R. Government CARs are discoverable in DCMA audits; premature or inadequate closure is an audit finding that can trigger production halt.
summary: System: CAR quality escape requirements per AS9100 Rev D clause 10.2 — escape point analysis, containment, root cause (5-Why/fishbone), corrective action, effectiveness verification, and closure evidence

label: Wrap rate calculation — cost-plus indirect rate stacking methodology
content: Wrap rate formula: Direct Labor Rate × (1 + Fringe%) × (1 + Overhead%) × (1 + G&A%). Rates must align with DCMA-approved provisional billing rates or a negotiated Forward Pricing Rate Agreement (FPRA) per FAR 42.1701; absent an FPRA, contractor proposes rates with cost or pricing data. Rate base methodology: fringe applied to direct labor only; overhead applied to burdened labor (or labor + direct materials depending on pool structure); G&A applied to total cost input or value-added base per Disclosure Statement election. Material handling rate (if applicable) applied to subcontract and direct material costs. CAS applicability: contracts ≥$2M trigger modified CAS coverage (selected standards); single-award contracts ≥$50M trigger full CAS coverage requiring a Disclosure Statement. TINA (Truth in Negotiations Act) requires certified cost or pricing data for proposals ≥$2M unless a commercial item, catalog price, or FPRA exception applies per FAR 15.403-1. There is no regulatory percentage deviation that automatically triggers a DCAA challenge; variance scrutiny is risk-based.
summary: System: Wrap rate stacking formula (fringe → overhead → G&A), FPRA alignment requirements, CAS applicability thresholds, and TINA certified cost data obligations for cost-plus proposals

label: Proposal cost and pricing strategy development
content: Develop proposal pricing strategy per FAR 15.407 (cost or pricing data thresholds and requirements). Assess competitive pricing landscape through market research and past performance data. Conduct build-up cost estimate by WBS with labor rates (wrap rates including fringe/overhead/G&A per forward pricing rate agreements), material costs, subcontractor quotes, and profit margin targets. Evaluate pricing model (fixed-price vs. cost-plus applicability), identify cost drivers and value levers, and recommend pricing positioning relative to government estimate and competitive set. Identify low-price opportunities where margin can be absorbed to improve win probability vs. no-bid scenarios.
summary: Task: Proposal cost and pricing strategy development

label: RFP analysis and proposal strategy mapping
content: Parse RFP for evaluation criteria (technical, past performance, cost), scoring weights, and source selection approach (Best Value, Lowest Price Technically Acceptable). Extract key customer requirements from Sections B/C (Statement of Work), L (Instructions), and M (Evaluation Criteria). Develop proposal strategy document mapping RFP evaluation factors to Yggdrasil Aero discriminators, win themes, and technical approach. Identify compliance red lines and proposal volume structure. Conduct competitive gap analysis: incumbent strengths/weaknesses, known competitor capabilities, estimated proposal themes. Recommend bid/no-bid gates and resource allocation strategy for proposal effort.
summary: Task: RFP analysis and proposal strategy mapping

label: Competitive intelligence and bid risk analysis
content: Gather and analyze competitive intelligence to support bid/no-bid and capture strategy decisions. Conduct Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) requests for incumbent contract data (past performance, contract value, task order history). Track competitor win rates, proposal themes, and past performance in CPARS/publicly available databases. Analyze incumbent performance vulnerabilities (cost growth, schedule slips, quality issues). Assess Yggdrasil Aero competitive positioning relative to known competitors (experience, capabilities, customer relationships, pricing). Develop competitive risk assessment with estimated win probability (Pwin) ranges, identify competitive discriminators, and recommend capture priorities and resource investments.
summary: Task: Competitive intelligence and bid risk analysis

label: Subcontractor proposal coordination and compliance
content: Coordinate proposal development with teaming partners and subcontractors per defined work share agreements. Track subcontractor proposal deliveries (technical content, past performance data, pricing input) against proposal milestones. Review subcontractor compliance with Yggdrasil Aero proposal standards, win themes, and RFP requirements. Integrate subcontractor content into master proposal with consistent formatting and messaging. Ensure subcontractors provide required certifications (small business status, ITAR compliance, security clearances, conflict-of-interest statements). Manage subcontractor color team review participation. Verify compliance with FAR Part 15 representation and certification requirements (cost or pricing data certifications).
summary: Task: Subcontractor proposal coordination and compliance

label: Competitive win probability tracking and incumbent performance database
content: Maintain competitive database tracking incumbent contract performance: contract numbers, periods of performance, estimated values, task order history (from FPDS.gov/SAM.gov), CPARS ratings, and publicly available performance data. Track competitor win rates and proposal themes across industry. Use database queries to assess Yggdrasil Aero competitive positioning (gaps/strengths relative to incumbents) and estimate win probability by opportunity category. Integrate FOIA data, industry conference intelligence, and customer feedback to refine competitive assessments. Update quarterly with SAM.gov contract data feeds and CPARS releases.
summary: System: Competitive win probability tracking and incumbent performance database

summary: Role: Data Engineer in Engineering

label: Delta Live Tables (DLT) pipeline development
content: DLT pipeline structure: @dlt.table or @dlt.view decorates a function that RETURNS a DataFrame — used for transformations only. dlt.create_streaming_table() declares a target table with no function body. dlt.apply_changes() is a top-level side-effecting call that wires a CDC source into a target table — it returns None and must NOT appear inside a @dlt.table function and must NOT be returned. Correct pattern: call dlt.create_streaming_table(name='target') then call dlt.apply_changes(target='target', source=...) as two separate top-level module-level statements. Anti-pattern: def my_table(): return dlt.apply_changes(...) — this is wrong; the decorator expects a DataFrame, not None, and apply_changes never fires correctly in this form.
summary: Task: Build declarative DLT pipelines with expectations, continuous/triggered modes, and SCD support

label: Change data capture and slowly changing dimensions
summary: Task: Implement CDC ingestion patterns and SCD Type 1/2 merge logic for historical tracking

label: Data quality and testing
summary: Task: Define and enforce data quality expectations, validations, and monitoring across pipelines

label: Performance tuning and cost optimization
summary: Task: Tune cluster configurations, leverage Photon, and apply adaptive query execution to reduce cost and latency

label: Ingest landing zone files using Auto Loader cloudFiles source
content: Auto Loader reads new files incrementally from cloud storage (ADLS Gen2, S3, GCS) using Structured Streaming with format('cloudFiles'). Set cloudFiles.format to json, csv, or parquet to match the landing zone. Auto Loader tracks ingested files in an internal checkpoint, guaranteeing exactly-once processing without re-scanning the full directory. Combine with a Delta Lake sink for durable bronze layer writes.
summary: System: Use Auto Loader cloudFiles source to incrementally ingest JSON/CSV/Parquet files from a landing zone into Delta Lake

label: Choose Auto Loader vs COPY INTO for landing zone ingestion
content: Use Auto Loader (Structured Streaming) when: files arrive continuously or at high frequency, file counts exceed tens of thousands, schema evolution is required, or the pipeline feeds a continuous DLT/streaming job. Use COPY INTO when: ingestion is a simple scheduled batch, total file count is small (<10k), and streaming overhead is undesirable. Auto Loader is the recommended default for production bronze layer ingestion in Databricks lakehouse architectures.
summary: Decision: Select Auto Loader for continuous/large-scale ingestion; COPY INTO for simple scheduled batch loads

label: Auto Loader readStream code pattern for JSON/CSV/Parquet landing zone
content:
df = (spark.readStream
  .format('cloudFiles')
  .option('cloudFiles.format', 'json')          # or 'csv', 'parquet'
  .option('cloudFiles.schemaLocation', '/mnt/checkpoints/schema/orders')
  .option('cloudFiles.inferColumnTypes', 'true')
  .load('/mnt/landing/orders/'))

(df.writeStream
  .format('delta')
  .option('checkpointLocation', '/mnt/checkpoints/bronze/orders')
  .option('mergeSchema', 'true')
  .trigger(availableNow=True)                    # or processingTime='5 minutes'
  .toTable('bronze.orders'))
summary: Output: Structured Streaming Auto Loader pattern reading cloudFiles and writing to a Delta bronze table

label: Configure Auto Loader schema inference and evolution with schemaLocation
content: Auto Loader infers schema on the first batch and persists it to cloudFiles.schemaLocation. On subsequent runs it reuses the stored schema. When new columns arrive, Auto Loader detects the mismatch and can automatically evolve the schema using mergeSchema=true on the Delta write, or raise a schema evolution exception for manual review. Set cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode to 'addNewColumns' (default), 'rescue', 'failOnNewColumns', or 'none'.
summary: System: Persist inferred schema to schemaLocation and control column additions with schemaEvolutionMode

label: When to use mergeSchema vs failOnNewColumns for schema evolution
content: Use schemaEvolutionMode='addNewColumns' + mergeSchema=true when upstream producers may add optional columns and downstream consumers can tolerate nullable additions (typical bronze layer). Use failOnNewColumns when the downstream schema is strictly contract-bound and new columns indicate a breaking upstream change requiring intervention. Use 'rescue' mode to route unknown columns into _rescued_data instead of failing or silently adding them. Never use 'none' if source schema is not 100% stable.
summary: Decision: Match schemaEvolutionMode to contract strictness — addNewColumns for flexible bronze, failOnNewColumns for strict contracts, rescue to quarantine unknowns

label: Schema evolution configuration with schemaLocation and mergeSchema
content:
# Schema inference + addNewColumns evolution
df = (spark.readStream
  .format('cloudFiles')
  .option('cloudFiles.format', 'parquet')
  .option('cloudFiles.schemaLocation', '/mnt/checkpoints/schema/events')
  .option('cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode', 'addNewColumns')  # or 'rescue' / 'failOnNewColumns'
  .option('cloudFiles.inferColumnTypes', 'true')
  .load('/mnt/landing/events/'))

(df.writeStream
  .format('delta')
  .option('checkpointLocation', '/mnt/checkpoints/bronze/events')
  .option('mergeSchema', 'true')   # required when addNewColumns introduces new cols
  .toTable('bronze.events'))

# To reset schema after breaking change:
# dbutils.fs.rm('/mnt/checkpoints/schema/events', recurse=True)
summary: Output: Auto Loader schema location and mergeSchema configuration with reset instructions

label: Select Auto Loader file discovery mode: file notification vs directory listing
content: Auto Loader supports two file discovery modes. Directory listing mode (default) lists the cloud storage path on each trigger and diffs against previously processed files — simple to set up but scales poorly beyond ~1M files. File notification mode subscribes to cloud storage event notifications (Azure Event Grid, AWS SNS/SQS, or GCS Pub/Sub) and queues new file events, enabling sub-second discovery at any scale. Notification mode requires cloud infrastructure setup but is the recommended approach for high-volume landing zones.
summary: System: Directory listing is simple/default; file notification mode uses cloud events for scalable, low-latency file discovery

label: When to switch from directory listing to file notification mode
content: Use file notification mode when: landing zone accumulates >100k files, file arrival rate exceeds ~1000 files/hour, low-latency discovery (<30 seconds) is required, or directory listing triggers cloud API throttling errors. Stay with directory listing for: prototyping, small landing zones, or environments where Event Grid/SNS/Pub/Sub cannot be provisioned. On Azure, notification mode requires an Event Grid subscription and an Azure Queue Storage queue; Databricks Auto Loader can create these automatically with appropriate permissions.
summary: Decision: Switch to notification mode at >100k files or >1k files/hour; use directory listing for simple/small workloads

label: Enable file notification mode with useNotifications option
content:
# File notification mode (Azure ADLS Gen2 example)
# Databricks auto-creates Event Grid + Queue with cloudFiles.subscriptionId
df = (spark.readStream
  .format('cloudFiles')
  .option('cloudFiles.format', 'csv')
  .option('cloudFiles.useNotifications', 'true')
  .option('cloudFiles.subscriptionId', '<azure-subscription-id>')
  .option('cloudFiles.resourceGroup', '<resource-group>')
  .option('cloudFiles.tenantId', '<tenant-id>')
  .option('cloudFiles.clientId', '<service-principal-client-id>')
  .option('cloudFiles.clientSecret', dbutils.secrets.get('kv', 'sp-secret'))
  .option('cloudFiles.schemaLocation', '/mnt/checkpoints/schema/telemetry')
  .load('abfss://landing@<account>.dfs.core.windows.net/telemetry/'))

# AWS S3: omit Azure options, set cloudFiles.region instead
# GCS: set cloudFiles.project instead
summary: Output: Auto Loader file notification mode configuration for Azure ADLS Gen2 with service principal auth

label: Tune Auto Loader throughput with maxFilesPerTrigger and format options
content: Auto Loader exposes trigger-level and format-level configuration options. maxFilesPerTrigger caps the number of files processed per micro-batch (default unlimited), allowing rate control for downstream Delta writers. cloudFiles.maxBytesPerTrigger limits by bytes. cloudFiles.format accepts json, csv, parquet, avro, text, binaryFile, and orc. CSV-specific options (cloudFiles.header, cloudFiles.delimiter) and JSON options (cloudFiles.multiLine) are passed through directly. Use cloudFiles.pathGlobFilter to restrict ingestion to a filename pattern.
summary: System: Control batch size with maxFilesPerTrigger/maxBytesPerTrigger and pass format-specific options through cloudFiles namespace

label: When to set maxFilesPerTrigger vs rely on default unlimited batching
content: Set maxFilesPerTrigger when: Delta write latency spikes due to large micro-batches, downstream tables need predictable commit frequency, or cluster memory is constrained by large concurrent file reads. Leave unlimited when catching up on a large backlog (use trigger(availableNow=True) for one-shot processing instead). Set maxBytesPerTrigger for variable-size files where file count is a poor proxy for load. Do not set both maxFilesPerTrigger and maxBytesPerTrigger simultaneously — whichever limit is hit first applies.
summary: Decision: Use maxFilesPerTrigger for predictable commit cadence or memory control; leave unlimited for backfill with availableNow trigger

label: Auto Loader configuration reference with maxFilesPerTrigger and format options
content:
df = (spark.readStream
  .format('cloudFiles')
  .option('cloudFiles.format', 'csv')
  # Throughput control
  .option('cloudFiles.maxFilesPerTrigger', 1000)     # process ≤1000 files/batch
  # .option('cloudFiles.maxBytesPerTrigger', '10g')  # alternative: cap by bytes
  # CSV format passthrough options
  .option('header', 'true')
  .option('delimiter', ',')
  .option('cloudFiles.inferColumnTypes', 'true')
  # File filtering
  .option('cloudFiles.pathGlobFilter', '*.csv')      # only .csv files
  .option('cloudFiles.includeExistingFiles', 'true') # include files present at stream start
  .option('cloudFiles.schemaLocation', '/mnt/checkpoints/schema/sales')
  .load('/mnt/landing/sales/'))

# Trigger options:
# .trigger(processingTime='10 minutes')  — micro-batch every 10 min
# .trigger(availableNow=True)            — process all pending files, then stop
summary: Output: Auto Loader option reference for maxFilesPerTrigger, format passthrough, pathGlobFilter, and trigger modes

label: Handle malformed records with Auto Loader _rescued_data column
content: When cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode is set to 'rescue' (or the rescue column is enabled alongside other modes), Auto Loader appends a _rescued_data column of type STRING to the output. Any record field that cannot be parsed against the inferred/declared schema — including extra columns, type mismatches, and corrupt values — is captured as a JSON string in _rescued_data rather than dropping the row. The column also includes _rescued_data._file_path for traceability. Rows with no rescue content have null in the column.
summary: System: _rescued_data column captures unparseable fields as JSON strings, preventing row loss from malformed or schema-mismatched records

label: When to monitor _rescued_data for data quality alerting
content: Monitor _rescued_data when: bronze tables receive data from multiple upstream producers with inconsistent schemas, CSV/JSON sources are not schema-validated at the source, or the pipeline must maintain 100% record capture (no silent drops). Alert on non-null _rescued_data counts exceeding a threshold (e.g., >0.1% of records per batch) as this indicates upstream schema drift or data corruption. Do not use _rescued_data as a long-term storage pattern — quarantine rescued records to a dedicated bad-records Delta table and route to a data quality review process.
summary: Decision: Alert when non-null _rescued_data exceeds threshold; quarantine rescued records to a dedicated bad-records table for review

label: Enable and query _rescued_data for malformed record handling
content:
# Enable rescue mode during ingestion
df = (spark.readStream
  .format('cloudFiles')
  .option('cloudFiles.format', 'json')
  .option('cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode', 'rescue')   # routes unknowns to _rescued_data
  .option('cloudFiles.schemaLocation', '/mnt/checkpoints/schema/api_events')
  .load('/mnt/landing/api_events/'))

# Write all records including rescued ones to bronze
(df.writeStream
  .format('delta')
  .option('checkpointLocation', '/mnt/checkpoints/bronze/api_events')
  .toTable('bronze.api_events'))

# Quarantine query — run as separate DLT expectation or scheduled job:
bad_records = spark.sql("""
  SELECT _rescued_data, input_file_name() AS source_file, current_timestamp() AS rescued_at
  FROM bronze.api_events
  WHERE _rescued_data IS NOT NULL
""")
bad_records.write.format('delta').mode('append').saveAsTable('bronze.api_events_rescued')

# Data quality metric:
# SELECT COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE _rescued_data IS NOT NULL) / COUNT(*) AS rescue_rate
# FROM bronze.api_events
summary: Output: Auto Loader rescue mode configuration, bronze write pattern, and _rescued_data quarantine query

label: Configure Kafka readStream with broker and topic subscription options
content: Spark Structured Streaming reads from Kafka via readStream.format('kafka'). Required options: kafka.bootstrap.servers (comma-separated broker:port list) and exactly one topic selector — subscribe (comma-separated topic names), subscribePattern (Java regex matching topic names), or assign (JSON of {"topic":[partition,...]} for explicit partition targeting). Output schema always contains: value (binary), key (binary), topic, partition, offset, timestamp, timestampType. Cast value to STRING or use from_json for structured parsing. Optional tuning: startingOffsets ('latest' for live, 'earliest' for backfill, or per-partition JSON), maxOffsetsPerTrigger to cap throughput per micro-batch, failOnDataLoss=false to tolerate topic/offset deletion in non-production environments.
summary: System: Configure Kafka readStream broker connection, topic subscription mode, and output schema for Structured Streaming ingestion

label: Understand Structured Streaming trigger modes: availableNow, processingTime, continuous
content: Structured Streaming supports four trigger modes. processingTime('interval') runs micro-batches on a fixed wall-clock interval; if a batch exceeds the interval the next starts immediately. availableNow=True processes all currently pending data in one or more micro-batches (respecting maxOffsetsPerTrigger/maxFilesPerTrigger) then stops — this is the modern replacement for the deprecated Trigger.Once(). continuous('checkpoint-interval') is an experimental low-latency mode that uses asynchronous epoch checkpointing and targets sub-millisecond latency but supports only stateless map/filter operations. Default (no trigger specified) starts the next micro-batch immediately after the prior one completes, maximizing throughput with no inter-batch pause.
summary: System: Overview of Structured Streaming trigger modes — processingTime, availableNow (replaces Trigger.Once), continuous, and default

label: Configure checkpointLocation and understand streaming recovery semantics
content: checkpointLocation is a required writeStream option pointing to a durable path (ADLS, S3, DBFS). The checkpoint stores three things: committed source progress (Kafka offsets or processed file list), the serialized query plan (validated on restart for compatibility), and any stateful operator state stores (aggregation buffers, join state). On restart after failure, Spark replays from the last committed checkpoint. Combined with a Delta Lake sink, this guarantees exactly-once output — Delta's idempotent writes reject duplicate batch IDs. Each streaming query must have a unique checkpointLocation; sharing checkpoints between queries or between source changes corrupts state. Checkpoint size grows with stateful operations (windowed aggregations, stream-stream joins).
summary: System: checkpointLocation configuration, what is stored in the checkpoint, and exactly-once recovery semantics with Delta Lake

label: Apply withWatermark to handle late-arriving events in Structured Streaming
content: withWatermark(eventTimeCol, delayThreshold) declares the maximum expected event lateness. The watermark tracks: watermark_t = max(event_time seen so far) - delayThreshold. State stores for windowed aggregations and stream-stream joins are retained only until the watermark advances past the window/join boundary — events arriving after the watermark is exceeded are silently dropped. In Append output mode, aggregated window results are emitted only after the watermark passes the window end, guaranteeing completeness. In Update mode, partial results are emitted as events arrive and updated when late events arrive within the watermark. Without withWatermark, Spark retains all state indefinitely, causing unbounded memory growth for any stateful streaming operation.
summary: System: withWatermark mechanics — watermark tracking formula, state eviction, late event handling, and output mode interaction

label: Chain streaming-to-streaming jobs for bronze→silver multi-hop architecture
content: Multi-hop streaming reads a Delta table written by an upstream streaming job as a new Structured Streaming source using spark.readStream.format('delta').table('bronze.X'). Delta's transaction log serves as the durable queue — the downstream job consumes committed Delta versions as ordered micro-batches. Each hop maintains its own checkpointLocation and trigger, providing independent failure isolation and cadence control. Set ignoreChanges=true on the Delta readStream to tolerate OPTIMIZE, ZORDER, or DELETE rewrites on the bronze table that rewrite existing files without changing logical data. This pattern replaces intermediate Kafka topics for intra-lakehouse hops and preserves Delta's ACID exactly-once guarantees across the chain.
summary: System: Multi-hop streaming using Delta as streaming source between bronze and silver, with independent checkpoints per hop

label: Choose subscribe vs subscribePattern vs assign for Kafka topic selection
content: Use subscribe when consuming a fixed, enumerated set of topics (e.g., 'orders,shipments') — simplest setup, correct for stable topic names. Use subscribePattern when topic names follow a predictable naming convention and new matching topics should be auto-discovered without pipeline changes (e.g., 'ops_.*' picks up ops_apac, ops_emea automatically) — appropriate for multi-tenant or environment-segmented topic layouts. Use assign for precise partition-level replay or offset-targeted recovery, specifying topic-partition-offset triples as JSON — use only when explicit offset control is required (e.g., replaying a specific partition after data corruption). Do not mix the three selectors in a single readStream. For most production pipelines consuming a well-known topic set, subscribe is the default and correct choice.
summary: Decision: subscribe for fixed topics, subscribePattern for auto-discovery by naming convention, assign for explicit partition/offset control

label: Select Structured Streaming trigger mode for the workload
content: Use processingTime when: downstream consumers need data on a predictable cadence (e.g., dashboard refresh every 5 minutes), cluster cost must be bounded by limiting active compute time, or the stream shares a cluster with batch jobs — choose an interval matching the latency SLA. Use availableNow when: running a scheduled one-shot catch-up job (e.g., hourly Workflow triggering a stream that drains new files then stops) — always prefer availableNow over the deprecated Trigger.Once(); availableNow processes data in multiple rate-limited micro-batches, avoiding OOM on large backlogs. Use continuous only when: end-to-end latency must be sub-second, the pipeline is entirely stateless (map, filter, cast), and the experimental limitations (no aggregations, no joins) are acceptable. Use default (no trigger) only for dedicated always-on streaming clusters where maximum throughput is the sole objective.
summary: Decision: processingTime for cadence/cost control, availableNow for scheduled catch-up (replaces Trigger.Once), continuous for sub-second stateless pipelines

label: When to reset vs retain the streaming checkpoint
content: Retain the checkpoint when: restarting after a transient failure, deploying a code fix that does not alter the query plan, or updating Spark/Databricks runtime without changing source or output schema — Spark validates plan compatibility on restart and resumes from the last commit. Reset (delete) the checkpoint when: the query output schema changes incompatibly, the source changes (different Kafka topic, different Auto Loader path), stateful operators are added or removed, or a full reprocess from earliest offsets is required. To reset: dbutils.fs.rm(checkpointLocation, recurse=True) before restarting the stream. If resetting a Delta append-mode sink, also truncate or recreate the target table to avoid duplicates. Streams using foreachBatch with a merge/upsert pattern are safe to reset and replay without duplicating output records.
summary: Decision: retain checkpoint for transient restarts and minor fixes; reset when query plan, source, or schema changes incompatibly

label: Choose watermark threshold based on lateness distribution and output mode
content: Set delayThreshold to the P99 observed lateness of the event source, not the average — using average causes ~1% of events to be silently dropped, producing incomplete aggregates. Too tight a threshold (e.g., 5 minutes when P99 lateness is 45 minutes) silently drops late events; too loose (e.g., 24 hours) causes excessive state retention and memory spill. For Kafka sources, measure lateness as processing_time - event_timestamp across a representative window; for IoT/edge sources with intermittent connectivity, use 30–60 minute thresholds. Choose Append output mode when downstream requires append-only semantics and complete window results are acceptable after a delay equal to the watermark; choose Update mode when partial intermediate results are needed and the sink is idempotent (Delta merge). Validate the lateness distribution before setting the production threshold — use a batch query on historical bronze data to compute percentiles.
summary: Decision: set threshold at P99 lateness; Append mode for complete results with delay, Update mode for partial results with idempotent sink

label: Decide when to use streaming-to-streaming vs batch silver transforms
content: Use streaming-to-streaming (Delta bronze → Delta silver via readStream) when: end-to-end latency SLA requires silver to be updated within minutes of bronze landing, bronze is written continuously or at high frequency (>1 micro-batch/minute), and the silver transform is stateless or uses bounded state with withWatermark. Use scheduled batch silver transforms when: silver logic requires full-scan joins across multiple bronze tables, ML model scoring, or complex multi-step aggregations that are impractical in streaming; upstream bronze is written infrequently (hourly/daily); or snapshot isolation across multiple input tables is required. Mixing streaming bronze with batch silver is architecturally valid and common — trigger the silver batch Workflow on a time schedule or via a Delta table-change trigger after bronze commits. Do not use streaming-to-streaming if the silver transformation cannot tolerate the exactly-once-per-micro-batch execution model.
summary: Decision: streaming-to-streaming for low-latency stateless/bounded-state transforms; batch silver for complex joins, ML scoring, or infrequent bronze writes

label: Kafka readStream code pattern with broker config, topic subscription, and Delta sink
content:
# Kafka Structured Streaming → Delta bronze
df = (spark.readStream
  .format('kafka')
  .option('kafka.bootstrap.servers', 'broker1:9092,broker2:9092')
  .option('subscribe', 'orders')             # or subscribePattern='ops_.*' / assign='{"orders":[0,1]}'
  .option('startingOffsets', 'latest')       # 'earliest' for initial backfill
  .option('maxOffsetsPerTrigger', 10000)     # throughput cap per micro-batch
  .option('failOnDataLoss', 'false')         # tolerate offset gaps (set true in prod)
  .load()
  .selectExpr(
      'CAST(key AS STRING) AS key',
      'CAST(value AS STRING) AS raw_value',
      'topic', 'partition', 'offset', 'timestamp AS kafka_timestamp'
  ))

# Parse JSON payload
from pyspark.sql.functions import from_json, col
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StringType, LongType
order_schema = StructType().add('order_id', LongType()).add('status', StringType()).add('amount', StringType())

parsed = (df
  .select(from_json(col('raw_value'), order_schema).alias('d'), 'kafka_timestamp')
  .select('d.*', 'kafka_timestamp'))

(parsed.writeStream
  .format('delta')
  .option('checkpointLocation', '/mnt/checkpoints/bronze/orders_kafka')
  .trigger(processingTime='30 seconds')
  .toTable('bronze.orders_kafka'))
summary: Output: Kafka readStream with subscribe, broker config, startingOffsets, JSON parsing, and Delta bronze write

label: Trigger mode code reference for processingTime, availableNow, and continuous
content:
# processingTime — fixed-interval micro-batch (most common production pattern)
(df.writeStream
  .format('delta')
  .option('checkpointLocation', '/mnt/checkpoints/silver/orders')
  .trigger(processingTime='5 minutes')
  .toTable('silver.orders'))

# availableNow — one-shot catch-up; replaces deprecated Trigger.Once()
# Processes all pending data in multiple rate-limited micro-batches, then stops.
# Safe to use with maxFilesPerTrigger / maxOffsetsPerTrigger to cap batch size.
(df.writeStream
  .format('delta')
  .option('checkpointLocation', '/mnt/checkpoints/bronze/orders')
  .trigger(availableNow=True)
  .toTable('bronze.orders'))
# DO NOT USE: .trigger(once=True)  ← deprecated, may OOM on large backlogs

# continuous — experimental sub-second latency (stateless ops only)
(df.writeStream
  .format('delta')
  .option('checkpointLocation', '/mnt/checkpoints/bronze/telemetry')
  .trigger(continuous='1 second')    # async checkpoint interval
  .toTable('bronze.telemetry'))

# Default (no trigger) — maximum throughput, next batch starts immediately
(df.writeStream
  .format('delta')
  .option('checkpointLocation', '/mnt/checkpoints/bronze/events')
  .toTable('bronze.events'))
summary: Output: Side-by-side trigger mode code reference covering processingTime, availableNow (with Trigger.Once deprecation note), continuous, and default

label: Checkpoint configuration, verification, and safe reset patterns
content:
CHECKPOINT_BASE = '/mnt/checkpoints'

# Standard writeStream with checkpointLocation
(df.writeStream
  .format('delta')
  .option('checkpointLocation', f'{CHECKPOINT_BASE}/bronze/orders')
  .trigger(processingTime='5 minutes')
  .toTable('bronze.orders'))

# Verify checkpoint health — list committed batch IDs
display(dbutils.fs.ls(f'{CHECKPOINT_BASE}/bronze/orders/commits/'))

# Reset checkpoint for full reprocess (destructive — confirm before running):
# dbutils.fs.rm(f'{CHECKPOINT_BASE}/bronze/orders', recurse=True)
# If using Auto Loader, also reset schema checkpoint:
# dbutils.fs.rm(f'{CHECKPOINT_BASE}/schema/orders', recurse=True)

# foreachBatch upsert — idempotent; safe to replay after checkpoint reset
from delta.tables import DeltaTable

def upsert_to_silver(batch_df, batch_id):
    (DeltaTable.forName(spark, 'silver.orders')
      .alias('t')
      .merge(batch_df.alias('s'), 't.order_id = s.order_id')
      .whenMatchedUpdateAll()
      .whenNotMatchedInsertAll()
      .execute())

(df.writeStream
  .foreachBatch(upsert_to_silver)
  .option('checkpointLocation', f'{CHECKPOINT_BASE}/silver/orders_upsert')
  .trigger(availableNow=True)
  .start())
summary: Output: checkpointLocation configuration, commit verification, checkpoint reset commands, and idempotent foreachBatch upsert pattern

label: withWatermark windowed aggregation and stream-stream join patterns
content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import window, count, col, expr

# Windowed aggregation with watermark — Append output mode
