windowed = (parsed
  .withWatermark('event_timestamp', '30 minutes')       # drop events >30 min late
  .groupBy(
      window(col('event_timestamp'), '10 minutes', '5 minutes'),  # 10-min window, 5-min slide
      col('device_id')
  )
  .agg(count('*').alias('event_count')))

(windowed.writeStream
  .format('delta')
  .outputMode('append')                                 # emit only complete windows
  .option('checkpointLocation', '/mnt/checkpoints/silver/device_agg')
  .trigger(processingTime='5 minutes')
  .toTable('silver.device_events_agg'))

# Use outputMode('update') to emit partial results as events arrive:
# (windowed.writeStream.outputMode('update')...)

# Stream-stream join — both sides require withWatermark
clicks = (spark.readStream.format('delta').table('bronze.clicks')
  .withWatermark('click_time', '1 hour'))
impressions = (spark.readStream.format('delta').table('bronze.impressions')
  .withWatermark('impression_time', '2 hours'))

joined = clicks.join(impressions,
    expr("click_id = imp_id AND click_time BETWEEN impression_time AND impression_time + INTERVAL 1 HOUR"))

(joined.writeStream
  .format('delta')
  .outputMode('append')
  .option('checkpointLocation', '/mnt/checkpoints/silver/click_attribution')
  .toTable('silver.click_attribution'))
summary: Output: withWatermark windowed aggregation in Append and Update modes, plus stream-stream join with dual-sided watermarks

label: Multi-hop streaming code pattern: Kafka→bronze and bronze→silver Delta streams
content:
# --- Hop 1: Kafka → Bronze (upstream stream) ---
kafka_df = (spark.readStream
  .format('kafka')
  .option('kafka.bootstrap.servers', 'broker:9092')
  .option('subscribe', 'raw_events')
  .option('startingOffsets', 'latest')
  .load()
  .selectExpr('CAST(value AS STRING) AS raw', 'timestamp AS ingest_time'))

(kafka_df.writeStream
  .format('delta')
  .option('checkpointLocation', '/mnt/checkpoints/bronze/raw_events')
  .trigger(processingTime='1 minute')
  .toTable('bronze.raw_events'))

# --- Hop 2: Bronze → Silver (downstream Delta readStream) ---
bronze_df = (spark.readStream
  .format('delta')
  .option('ignoreChanges', 'true')     # tolerate OPTIMIZE/ZORDER rewrites on bronze
  .table('bronze.raw_events'))

from pyspark.sql.functions import from_json, col, current_timestamp
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StringType, LongType
event_schema = StructType().add('event_id', LongType()).add('type', StringType()).add('payload', StringType())

silver_df = (bronze_df
  .select(from_json('raw', event_schema).alias('e'), 'ingest_time')
  .select('e.*', 'ingest_time', current_timestamp().alias('processed_time'))
  .filter(col('e.event_id').isNotNull()))   # silver quality filter

(silver_df.writeStream
  .format('delta')
  .option('checkpointLocation', '/mnt/checkpoints/silver/events')
  .trigger(processingTime='5 minutes')      # silver can lag bronze by design
  .toTable('silver.events'))
# Note: each hop runs as a separate Workflow job or always-on stream cluster
summary: Output: Complete Kafka→bronze and bronze→silver streaming-to-streaming multi-hop pattern with ignoreChanges and independent checkpoints

label: Execute idempotent batch file ingestion with COPY INTO
content: COPY INTO is a SQL command that idempotently loads files from cloud storage into a Delta table. It tracks loaded files in the Delta transaction log, skipping already-processed files on subsequent runs without a separate checkpoint. Syntax: COPY INTO <table> FROM '<path>' FILEFORMAT = <format> [FORMAT_OPTIONS (...)] [COPY_OPTIONS ('force' = 'false')]. Supported formats: JSON, CSV, Parquet, Avro, ORC, text, binaryFile. Runs as a single batch Spark job — not streaming. Default COPY_OPTIONS ('force' = 'false') provides idempotency. Set force='true' to reload all matching files regardless of prior loads. Use PATTERN = '*.json' to restrict ingestion by filename glob. Schema must be compatible at load time — COPY INTO does not support mergeSchema; add columns with ALTER TABLE before loading new columns.
summary: System: COPY INTO idempotent file ingestion — mechanics, syntax, format support, and idempotency guarantees

label: Choose COPY INTO configuration: FORMAT_OPTIONS, FILES filter, and force mode
content: Use FILES = ('<file1>', '<file2>') for targeted backfill of specific known files. Use FORMAT_OPTIONS for source-specific parsing: ('delimiter' = ',', 'header' = 'true') for CSV; ('multiLine' = 'true') for pretty-printed JSON. Set COPY_OPTIONS ('force' = 'true') only for intentional reloads (correcting bad data, schema fix) — this bypasses idempotency tracking and reprocesses all matching files; never use in production scheduled jobs. Use PATTERN to restrict by glob when a landing zone contains mixed file types. Prefer COPY INTO over Auto Loader when: schedule is hourly/daily, total distinct file count stays below ~10k, no schema evolution is needed, and a simple SQL-only pipeline is preferred over notebook/streaming infrastructure.
summary: Decision: Configure COPY INTO FORMAT_OPTIONS, FILES subset, PATTERN filter, and when force=true is appropriate

label: COPY INTO SQL syntax reference for idempotent batch ingestion
content:
-- JSON landing zone, idempotent (default force=false)
COPY INTO bronze.orders
FROM 'abfss://landing@<account>.dfs.core.windows.net/orders/'
FILEFORMAT = JSON
FORMAT_OPTIONS ('multiLine' = 'false', 'inferSchema' = 'true')
COPY_OPTIONS ('force' = 'false', 'mergeSchema' = 'true');

-- CSV with header and custom delimiter
COPY INTO bronze.sales
FROM 'abfss://landing@<account>.dfs.core.windows.net/sales/'
FILEFORMAT = CSV
FORMAT_OPTIONS ('header' = 'true', 'delimiter' = ',', 'inferSchema' = 'true')
COPY_OPTIONS ('force' = 'false');

-- Targeted backfill: specific files only
COPY INTO bronze.orders
FROM (SELECT *, _metadata.file_path AS source_file
      FROM 'abfss://landing@<account>.dfs.core.windows.net/orders/')
FILEFORMAT = JSON
FORMAT_OPTIONS ('inferSchema' = 'true');

-- Glob filter: only .json files in mixed landing zone
COPY INTO bronze.events
FROM 'abfss://landing@<account>.dfs.core.windows.net/events/'
FILEFORMAT = JSON
PATTERN = '*.json'
COPY_OPTIONS ('force' = 'false');

-- Audit: check previously loaded files
-- SELECT operation, operationParameters, timestamp
-- FROM (DESCRIBE HISTORY bronze.orders)
-- WHERE operation = 'COPY INTO'
-- ORDER BY timestamp DESC;
summary: Output: COPY INTO SQL patterns for JSON, CSV, targeted backfill, glob filter, and load history audit

label: Read from JDBC sources with spark.read.format('jdbc') and parallelism options
content: Spark reads relational databases via spark.read.format('jdbc') with required options: url (JDBC connection string), dbtable (table name or subquery alias), user, password, driver (fully-qualified JDBC driver class). The driver JAR must be installed on the cluster via Maven coordinates or library upload. By default, all data is fetched through a single JDBC connection in one Spark partition — a serial bottleneck for large tables. To parallelize, provide: partitionColumn (numeric or date/timestamp column), lowerBound, upperBound, and numPartitions; Spark creates numPartitions tasks each issuing a bounded WHERE clause. Alternatively, use the predicates option (array of WHERE clause strings) for full control over partition splits. Use a subquery as dbtable to push down filters/aggregations before transfer: dbtable='(SELECT id, amount FROM orders WHERE status = \'active\') AS t'. Common drivers: PostgreSQL=org.postgresql.Driver, SQL Server=com.microsoft.sqlserver.jdbc.SQLServerDriver, MySQL=com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver, Oracle=oracle.jdbc.OracleDriver.
summary: System: JDBC source ingestion — connection options, driver requirements, single vs parallel partition read mechanics

label: Choose JDBC partitioning strategy: partitionColumn vs predicates vs single partition
content: Use partitionColumn + lowerBound + upperBound + numPartitions when: the table has a numeric or monotonically increasing primary key with roughly uniform distribution and the database can handle parallel connections. Set numPartitions to 2–4× executor count. Warning: lowerBound/upperBound define stride only — rows outside the range are not excluded, so skewed distributions cause uneven partition sizes and stragglers; verify key distribution before using. Use predicates (array of WHERE strings) when: the partition key is non-numeric (e.g., date ranges, region codes), the data is heavily skewed and requires custom split boundaries, or you need deterministic partition mapping. Use single-partition (no partitioning options) when: table is small (<500k rows), the source database cannot handle parallel connections, or the query already aggregates to a small result. Always use subquery pushdown to filter rows and select only needed columns before transfer — minimizes data movement across the JDBC connection.
summary: Decision: JDBC partition strategy — partitionColumn for uniform numeric keys, predicates for custom splits, single-partition for small tables

label: JDBC read code patterns with partitionColumn and predicates parallelism
content:
# Basic JDBC read — single partition (small tables only)
df = (spark.read.format('jdbc')
  .option('url', 'jdbc:postgresql://host:5432/mydb')
  .option('dbtable', 'public.orders')
  .option('user', dbutils.secrets.get('kv', 'pg-user'))
  .option('password', dbutils.secrets.get('kv', 'pg-password'))
  .option('driver', 'org.postgresql.Driver')
  .load())

# Parallel read with partitionColumn (numeric key, uniform distribution)
df = (spark.read.format('jdbc')
  .option('url', 'jdbc:postgresql://host:5432/mydb')
  .option('dbtable', 'public.orders')
  .option('user', dbutils.secrets.get('kv', 'pg-user'))
  .option('password', dbutils.secrets.get('kv', 'pg-password'))
  .option('driver', 'org.postgresql.Driver')
  .option('partitionColumn', 'order_id')
  .option('lowerBound', '1')
  .option('upperBound', '10000000')
  .option('numPartitions', '16')
  .load())

# Parallel read with custom predicates (date-based, handles skew)
predicates = [
    "created_date < '2024-01-01'",
    "created_date >= '2024-01-01' AND created_date < '2024-07-01'",
    "created_date >= '2024-07-01'"
]
df = (spark.read.format('jdbc')
  .option('url', 'jdbc:sqlserver://host:1433;databaseName=mydb')
  .option('dbtable', "(SELECT id, amount, status, created_date FROM dbo.orders WHERE status = 'active') AS t")
  .option('user', dbutils.secrets.get('kv', 'sql-user'))
  .option('password', dbutils.secrets.get('kv', 'sql-password'))
  .option('driver', 'com.microsoft.sqlserver.jdbc.SQLServerDriver')
  .load(predicates=predicates))

df.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').option('overwriteSchema', 'true').saveAsTable('bronze.orders_jdbc')
summary: Output: JDBC read patterns — single partition, partitionColumn parallelism, and custom predicates with Delta bronze write

label: Extract data from REST APIs using requests library or pandas UDF fan-out
content: Databricks has no native REST API source connector. Four extraction patterns: (1) Notebook sequential loop — use Python requests library to paginate through API responses on the driver, accumulate records, convert to Spark DataFrame when complete; suitable for small-to-medium APIs (<1M records total). (2) Pandas UDF fan-out — distribute API calls across executors by applying a pandas UDF to a DataFrame of entity IDs or URL parameters; each executor makes parallel HTTP requests; suitable for per-entity enrichment at scale. (3) foreachPartition — call the API inside a partition iterator function; useful when the API requires session state (OAuth token refresh, connection pooling) or complex retry logic. (4) Spark connector libraries (e.g., spark-xml, custom MicroBatchReader) for structured streaming ingestion. Always implement retry with exponential backoff using the tenacity library for rate-limited APIs. Store raw JSON responses in bronze before parsing — separates ingestion from transformation.
summary: System: REST API ingestion patterns — sequential notebook loop, pandas UDF fan-out, foreachPartition, and retry strategy

label: Choose REST API extraction pattern based on volume, concurrency, and session requirements
content: Use notebook sequential extraction when: total API response fits in driver memory (<500MB), the API enforces low concurrency limits, or this is a one-time historical load. Use pandas UDF fan-out when: you have a large list of entity IDs requiring per-entity GET calls (e.g., GET /entity/{id}), the API allows high concurrency (>10 parallel connections), and the entity list exceeds ~10k entries. Use foreachPartition when: the API requires session-level authentication (OAuth token obtained once per partition), retry logic is stateful, or per-executor connection pooling is needed. Always: use tenacity with exponential backoff + jitter for rate limit handling; store API credentials in Databricks Secrets; write raw JSON strings to bronze before parsing (never parse in the ingestion function); add _ingested_at timestamp and source_url columns for lineage. Do not hardcode pagination assumptions — always read total_pages or next_cursor from the response.
summary: Decision: REST API extraction pattern selection — sequential for small/rate-limited, pandas UDF for entity fan-out, foreachPartition for session-auth APIs

label: REST API ingestion patterns: notebook loop with retry and pandas UDF fan-out
content:
import requests, json
from tenacity import retry, stop_after_attempt, wait_exponential
from pyspark.sql.functions import pandas_udf, current_timestamp, lit
from pyspark.sql.types import StringType
import pandas as pd

API_BASE = 'https://api.example.com/v1'
headers = {'Authorization': f'Bearer {dbutils.secrets.get("kv", "api-token")}'}

# --- Pattern 1: Sequential pagination loop (driver-side, small-medium APIs) ---
@retry(stop=stop_after_attempt(3), wait=wait_exponential(multiplier=1, min=2, max=30))
def fetch_page(page):
    r = requests.get(f'{API_BASE}/orders',
                     params={'page': page, 'limit': 200},
                     headers=headers, timeout=30)
    r.raise_for_status()
    return r.json()

records, page = [], 1
while True:
    data = fetch_page(page)
    if not data.get('items'): break
    records.extend(data['items'])
    if not data.get('next_cursor'): break
    page += 1

raw_df = (spark
  .createDataFrame([{'raw_json': json.dumps(r)} for r in records])
  .withColumn('ingested_at', current_timestamp())
  .withColumn('source_url', lit(f'{API_BASE}/orders')))
raw_df.write.format('delta').mode('append').saveAsTable('bronze.orders_api')

# --- Pattern 2: Pandas UDF fan-out for per-entity enrichment ---
entity_ids = spark.range(1, 100001).toDF('entity_id')

@pandas_udf(StringType())
def fetch_entity(ids: pd.Series) -> pd.Series:
    results = []
    for eid in ids:
        try:
            r = requests.get(f'{API_BASE}/entity/{eid}',
                             headers=headers, timeout=10)
            results.append(r.text if r.ok else None)
        except Exception:
            results.append(None)
    return pd.Series(results)

(entity_ids
  .withColumn('raw_response', fetch_entity('entity_id'))
  .withColumn('ingested_at', current_timestamp())
  .write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').saveAsTable('bronze.entities_api'))
summary: Output: REST API ingestion — paginated sequential loop with tenacity retry and pandas UDF fan-out for per-entity calls

label: Understand Parquet vs Delta vs JSON vs CSV performance and ingestion characteristics
content: Parquet: columnar binary format, splittable, excellent read performance with predicate pushdown and column pruning, Snappy/ZSTD compression; no ACID, no transaction log, no schema enforcement. Delta: Parquet files plus transaction log — adds ACID transactions, schema enforcement, time travel, Z-ordering, and streaming readStream support on top of Parquet; the standard lakehouse storage format. JSON: row-oriented text, no built-in compression, no type information, not column-prunable — must parse full rows; appropriate as landing zone raw format for API payloads and event streams. CSV: row-oriented text, no type information, no nesting, delimiter-separated; header row optional; highest human readability but lowest performance and schema reliability; appropriate for legacy system exports and small reference tables. Read performance ranking: Delta ≈ Parquet >> Avro > JSON >> CSV. Landing zone typically receives JSON/CSV; bronze layer converts to Delta for all downstream consumers.
summary: System: File format characteristics — Parquet, Delta, JSON, CSV storage model, performance tradeoffs, and appropriate use per layer

label: Choose landing zone input format and bronze storage format
content: Land as JSON when: upstream produces API payloads, event streams, or nested/semi-structured data — JSON is the universal ingestion format for these sources. Land as CSV when: upstream is a legacy relational export, flat-file feed, or the source cannot produce binary formats — accept the parsing overhead. Land as Parquet when: upstream is another Spark/Databricks job, Kafka Connect with Parquet serializer, or a modern ETL tool — eliminates parsing overhead and preserves types. Always store bronze, silver, and gold as Delta: ACID prevents partial writes, the transaction log enables incremental readStream downstream, and time travel enables debugging and reprocessing. Never leave JSON or CSV files as the durable bronze layer — parsing costs compound across every downstream consumer. For lookup/reference tables under 10MB loaded infrequently, CSV in source control is acceptable for transparency. Avoid deeply nested JSON structures in Delta bronze tables — flatten to columns during bronze→silver transformation using from_json and explode.
summary: Decision: Accept JSON/CSV/Parquet in landing zone; always convert to Delta for bronze — rationale and format-specific guidance

label: File format comparison table and landing zone to Delta bronze conversion patterns
content:
# Format characteristics reference:
# | Format  | Type       | Splittable | Schema | ACID | Compression  | Analytical perf |
# |---------|------------|------------|--------|------|--------------|-----------------|
# | Delta   | Columnar   | Yes        | Yes    | Yes  | Snappy/ZSTD  | Excellent       |
# | Parquet | Columnar   | Yes        | Yes    | No   | Snappy/ZSTD  | Excellent       |
# | Avro    | Row-binary | Yes        | Yes    | No   | Deflate      | Good            |
# | JSON    | Row-text   | Lines only | No     | No   | None         | Poor            |
# | CSV     | Row-text   | No         | No     | No   | None         | Poor            |

from pyspark.sql.functions import current_timestamp

# JSON landing zone → Delta bronze
(spark.read
  .format('json')
  .option('inferSchema', 'true')
  .option('multiLine', 'false')          # true for pretty-printed per-file JSON
  .load('abfss://landing@<acct>.dfs.core.windows.net/orders/')
  .withColumn('_ingested_at', current_timestamp())
  .write
  .format('delta')
  .mode('append')
  .option('mergeSchema', 'true')
  .saveAsTable('bronze.orders'))

# CSV landing zone → Delta bronze
(spark.read
  .format('csv')
  .option('header', 'true')
  .option('inferSchema', 'true')
  .option('escape', '"')
  .load('abfss://landing@<acct>.dfs.core.windows.net/sales/*.csv')
  .withColumn('_ingested_at', current_timestamp())
  .write
  .format('delta')
  .mode('append')
  .saveAsTable('bronze.sales'))

# Parquet → Delta bronze (no format conversion cost, preserve types)
(spark.read
  .format('parquet')
  .load('abfss://raw@<acct>.dfs.core.windows.net/events/')
  .withColumn('_ingested_at', current_timestamp())
  .write
  .format('delta')
  .mode('append')
  .saveAsTable('bronze.events'))
summary: Output: Format comparison table and batch conversion patterns for JSON, CSV, and Parquet sources to Delta bronze

label: Access ingestion metadata via _metadata struct in file-based sources
content: Spark exposes a hidden _metadata struct column on all file-based data sources (JSON, CSV, Parquet, Avro, text, binaryFile, cloudFiles). Fields available: _metadata.file_path (STRING — full URI of the source file), _metadata.file_modification_time (TIMESTAMP — last-modified time of the file on cloud storage), _metadata.file_size (LONG — file size in bytes), _metadata.file_block_start (LONG — byte offset of block within file), _metadata.file_block_length (LONG — length of block). The _metadata column is hidden — it is NOT included in SELECT * and must be explicitly selected. It is available in Auto Loader (cloudFiles), spark.read.format('json/csv/parquet'), and COPY INTO via subquery syntax. When writing to Delta, _metadata fields can be persisted as regular named columns for audit trail and lineage. _metadata.file_path returns the same value as input_file_name() but is available at read time as a column reference without requiring a UDF call after the read.
summary: System: _metadata struct — available fields (file_path, file_modification_time, file_size), access pattern, and source compatibility

label: Decide when to persist _metadata columns for audit trail and incremental filtering
content: Persist _metadata.file_path when: multiple upstream systems write to the same landing zone and source attribution is needed for debugging or compliance, or regulatory data lineage requirements mandate file-level provenance. Persist _metadata.file_modification_time when: implementing incremental batch loads that filter to files modified after the last run timestamp, or monitoring for stale/reprocessed files by comparing modification time to expected delivery windows. Do not propagate _metadata columns beyond bronze to silver/gold unless they serve a business purpose — they are operational metadata. Do not use _metadata for deduplication in silver/gold — use business keys (order_id, event_id) instead, as the same business record may arrive in multiple files. _metadata.file_path is preferable to input_file_name() UDF because it is resolved at the scan plan level and is available in streaming sources without requiring a withColumn step after load.
summary: Decision: Persist _metadata.file_path for source attribution; _metadata.file_modification_time for incremental load tracking; do not propagate beyond bronze

label: Capture _metadata columns during Auto Loader and batch ingestion for audit trail
content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, current_timestamp

# --- Auto Loader with _metadata capture (streaming) ---
df = (spark.readStream
  .format('cloudFiles')
  .option('cloudFiles.format', 'json')
  .option('cloudFiles.schemaLocation', '/mnt/checkpoints/schema/orders')
  .load('/mnt/landing/orders/'))

enriched = (df.select(
    '*',                                                      # all data columns
    col('_metadata.file_path').alias('source_file'),
    col('_metadata.file_modification_time').alias('file_modified_at'),
    col('_metadata.file_size').alias('file_size_bytes'),
    current_timestamp().alias('ingested_at')
))

(enriched.writeStream
  .format('delta')
  .option('checkpointLocation', '/mnt/checkpoints/bronze/orders')
  .trigger(availableNow=True)
  .toTable('bronze.orders'))

# --- Batch read with _metadata for incremental load ---
batch_df = (spark.read
  .format('parquet')
  .load('/mnt/landing/events/')
  .select(
      '*',
      col('_metadata.file_path').alias('source_file'),
      col('_metadata.file_modification_time').alias('file_modified_at'),
      current_timestamp().alias('ingested_at')
  ))

# Incremental filter: only files modified since last run
last_run_ts = spark.sql('SELECT MAX(file_modified_at) FROM bronze.events').collect()[0][0]
if last_run_ts:
    new_records = batch_df.filter(col('file_modified_at') > last_run_ts)
else:
    new_records = batch_df  # first run: load all

new_records.write.format('delta').mode('append').saveAsTable('bronze.events')

# --- COPY INTO with _metadata via subquery ---
# COPY INTO bronze.orders
# FROM (SELECT *, _metadata.file_path AS source_file,
#              _metadata.file_modification_time AS file_modified_at
#       FROM 'abfss://landing@<acct>.dfs.core.windows.net/orders/')
# FILEFORMAT = JSON;
summary: Output: _metadata capture patterns for Auto Loader streaming, batch read incremental filtering, and COPY INTO subquery syntax

label: Deduplicate records with dropDuplicates and window row_number
content: PySpark offers two primary deduplication approaches. dropDuplicates(['key_col1','key_col2']) removes rows with identical values across the specified subset, keeping the first occurrence in partition order — non-deterministic on tie-breaking. Window-based deduplication uses row_number() over a Window partitioned by the business key and ordered by a recency/priority column (e.g., updated_at DESC), then filters to rn == 1 — this preserves a specific, deterministic survivor record. dropDuplicates is a single-stage distinct; window row_number requires a full shuffle sort (equivalent to repartition + sort). A combined pattern applies dropDuplicates on (key, updated_at) first to reduce data volume before the window sort.
summary: System: Remove duplicate rows using dropDuplicates (any survivor) or window row_number (deterministic latest/priority survivor)

label: Choose dropDuplicates vs window row_number for deduplication
content: Use dropDuplicates when: all copies of a record are truly equivalent (no preferred survivor), no reliable ordering column exists, or throughput and simplicity are the primary constraints — it avoids the shuffle sort overhead. Always specify a subset of business key columns; calling dropDuplicates() with no arguments compares all columns including ingestion timestamps and will fail to deduplicate records with trivial field differences. Use window row_number when: a specific record must be kept (e.g., latest by updated_at, highest quality_score), deduplication logic differs per key group, or downstream auditing requires knowing which records were eliminated. Add a pre-dedup dropDuplicates(key_cols + [ordering_col]) before the window to reduce shuffle size on high-volume tables.
summary: Decision: dropDuplicates for any-survivor deduplication; window row_number for deterministic latest/priority survivor

label: Deduplication code patterns: dropDuplicates and window row_number
content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import row_number, col
from pyspark.sql.window import Window

# dropDuplicates on business key subset — fast, non-deterministic survivor
deduped = df.dropDuplicates(['order_id', 'source_system'])

# Window row_number — keeps latest record per business key
window_spec = Window.partitionBy('order_id').orderBy(col('updated_at').desc())
deduped = (df
  .withColumn('rn', row_number().over(window_spec))
  .filter(col('rn') == 1)
  .drop('rn'))

# Combined pattern: pre-dedup to reduce shuffle, then window for determinism
pre_deduped = df.dropDuplicates(['order_id', 'updated_at'])  # reduce data volume
window_spec = Window.partitionBy('order_id').orderBy(col('updated_at').desc())
final = (pre_deduped
  .withColumn('rn', row_number().over(window_spec))
  .filter(col('rn') == 1)
  .drop('rn'))

# Multi-key dedup with priority tiebreaker (e.g., source_rank ASC = lower rank wins)
window_multi = Window.partitionBy('order_id', 'line_item_id').orderBy(
    col('updated_at').desc(), col('source_rank').asc())
deduped_multi = (df
  .withColumn('rn', row_number().over(window_multi))
  .filter(col('rn') == 1)
  .drop('rn'))
summary: Output: dropDuplicates and window row_number deduplication patterns with pre-dedup optimization

label: Cleanse data with null handling, type casting, and value standardization
content: Data cleansing transforms raw bronze records into silver-quality data by addressing nulls, type mismatches, and non-standard values. Null handling: coalesce(col1, col2, lit('default')) returns the first non-null value across a prioritized column list; na.fill({'amount': 0, 'status': 'unknown'}) applies typed constant defaults across multiple columns; na.drop(subset=['order_id']) removes rows missing required keys. Type casting: col('amount_str').cast(DecimalType(18,2)) produces null on parse failure (no exception); to_date(col('date_str'), 'yyyy-MM-dd') parses strings to dates. String standardization: trim() removes whitespace, lower() normalizes case, regexp_replace() applies regex substitution. Conditional replacement: when(col.isNull(), fallback_expr).otherwise(col) for fill logic that depends on other columns.
summary: System: Handle nulls with coalesce/na.fill, cast types safely, and standardize strings with trim/lower/regexp_replace

label: Choose null handling strategy: coalesce, na.fill, na.drop, or when/otherwise
content: Use coalesce() when filling a column from a prioritized list of fallback columns (e.g., preferred_email → contact_email → account_email) — reads left-to-right and short-circuits on the first non-null. Use na.fill() when applying constant typed defaults to multiple columns in a single pass — cleaner than chaining withColumn calls. Use na.drop(subset=[...]) only for required primary/foreign key columns; never drop rows for null values in optional analytical columns. Use when(col.isNull(), expr).otherwise(col) when the fill value depends on another column or requires conditional logic beyond a constant default. Always validate cast success rate before treating null-on-fail as acceptable — add a COUNT(FILTER(amount IS NULL)) / COUNT(*) quality metric after casting to detect high failure rates indicating upstream format changes.
summary: Decision: coalesce for prioritized column fallback, na.fill for bulk constant defaults, na.drop for required keys, when/otherwise for conditional fills

label: Data cleansing code patterns: coalesce, na.fill, cast, and standardization
content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, coalesce, lit, trim, lower, regexp_replace, when, to_date, current_timestamp
from pyspark.sql.types import DecimalType, IntegerType

# Null handling
cleansed = (df
  # coalesce: prioritized fallback across columns
  .withColumn('email', coalesce(col('preferred_email'), col('contact_email'), lit('unknown@placeholder.com')))
  # na.fill: bulk constant defaults by column name
  .na.fill({'quantity': 0, 'discount_pct': 0.0, 'status': 'pending'})
  # drop rows missing required business keys
  .na.drop(subset=['order_id', 'customer_id']))

# Type casting — null on parse failure (no exception thrown)
cleansed = (cleansed
  .withColumn('amount', col('amount_str').cast(DecimalType(18, 2)))
  .withColumn('order_date', to_date(col('order_date_str'), 'yyyy-MM-dd'))
  .withColumn('quantity', col('quantity_str').cast(IntegerType())))

# Cast quality check — alert if >1% parse failures
failure_rate = cleansed.filter(col('amount').isNull()).count() / df.count()
if failure_rate > 0.01:
    raise ValueError(f'amount cast failure rate {failure_rate:.2%} exceeds threshold')

# String standardization
cleansed = (cleansed
  .withColumn('status', lower(trim(col('status'))))            # 'Active ' → 'active'
  .withColumn('country_code', regexp_replace(trim(col('country')), r'\s+', '_'))
  .withColumn('phone', regexp_replace(col('phone'), r'[^\d]', '')))

# Conditional fill — depends on another column
cleansed = cleansed.withColumn('region',
    when(col('region').isNull() & (col('country_code') == 'us'), lit('north_america'))
    .when(col('region').isNull() & (col('country_code') == 'gb'), lit('europe'))
    .otherwise(col('region')))
summary: Output: Null handling, type casting with quality check, and string standardization code patterns

label: Flatten nested JSON using explode, posexplode, inline, and schema_of_json
content: PySpark provides four functions for handling nested structures. explode(array_col) converts each array element into a separate row; the source row is dropped if the array is null or empty (use explode_outer() to preserve nulls). posexplode(array_col) adds a zero-based positional index column alongside each exploded element. inline(array_of_structs_col) explodes an array of StructType elements directly into named top-level columns without a separate .select('alias.*') step. schema_of_json(json_sample_str) returns a DDL schema string inferred from a sample JSON string; used with from_json(col, schema) to parse embedded JSON strings into StructType before exploding. get_json_object(col, '$.field') extracts a single scalar field from a JSON string without full schema inference.
summary: System: Flatten arrays with explode/posexplode/inline; parse embedded JSON strings with schema_of_json + from_json

label: Choose explode vs posexplode vs inline vs schema_of_json for nested flattening
content: Use explode() for the general case — array elements to rows when row position is irrelevant; covers scalar arrays and struct arrays (follow with .select('alias.*') to promote fields). Use posexplode() when the array index must be preserved as a data column (e.g., line item sequence number, event position within a batch). Use inline() when the array contains structs and you want each struct field promoted to a top-level column in a single step without an intermediate alias and select — cleaner syntax but less flexible for selecting a subset of fields. Use schema_of_json() + from_json() when the nested data arrives as a JSON string in a column (common in Kafka raw_value, API raw_response bronze columns) rather than as a native Spark StructType. In production, define the schema explicitly with StructType rather than using schema_of_json() — schema inference samples one row and is unstable across batches. Use explode_outer() instead of explode() when null or empty arrays must produce one null row rather than being dropped.
summary: Decision: explode for general array-to-rows; posexplode to preserve index; inline for struct arrays to columns; schema_of_json+from_json for embedded JSON strings

label: Nested JSON flattening code patterns: explode, posexplode, inline, from_json
content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import explode, explode_outer, posexplode, inline, col, from_json, schema_of_json, get_json_object
from pyspark.sql.types import ArrayType, StructType, StringType, LongType, DoubleType

# explode — array of structs → one row per element
items_df = (df
  .select('order_id', explode('line_items').alias('item'))
  .select('order_id', 'item.*'))          # promote struct fields to top-level columns

# explode_outer — preserve rows with null/empty arrays (produces one null row)
df_outer = (df
  .select('order_id', explode_outer('line_items').alias('item'))
  .select('order_id', 'item.*'))

# posexplode — preserve array index alongside element
items_with_pos = (df
  .select('order_id', posexplode('line_items').alias('pos', 'item'))
  .select('order_id', col('pos').alias('line_seq'), 'item.*'))

# inline — array of structs → named columns in one step
items_inline = df.select('order_id', inline('line_items'))
# equivalent to explode + select('alias.*') but in a single operation

# from_json with explicit schema — production-safe (no sampling instability)
payload_schema = StructType()\
  .add('event_type', StringType())\
  .add('tags', ArrayType(StringType()))\
  .add('metrics', StructType()\
    .add('latency_ms', LongType())\
    .add('status_code', LongType()))

parsed_df = (df
  .withColumn('p', from_json(col('raw_payload'), payload_schema))
  .select('order_id', 'p.event_type', 'p.metrics.*', explode('p.tags').alias('tag')))

# schema_of_json — exploration/dev only; infer from representative sample
sample = df.select('raw_payload').filter(col('raw_payload').isNotNull()).first()[0]
ddl_schema = schema_of_json(sample)
parsed_explore = df.withColumn('p', from_json(col('raw_payload'), ddl_schema))

# get_json_object — extract single scalar without full parse
df_scalar = df.withColumn('event_type', get_json_object(col('raw_payload'), '$.event_type'))
summary: Output: explode, posexplode, inline, and from_json flattening code patterns with explicit schema definition

label: Pivot and unpivot (stack) tabular data in PySpark and Spark SQL
content: Pivoting reshapes row values into columns using PySpark's .groupBy().pivot(col, values).agg() pattern: group by one or more key columns, pivot on a category column (one output column per distinct category value), and aggregate the metric column. Specify pivot values explicitly to avoid a full-scan pre-aggregation to discover distinct values. Unpivoting (stack) is the inverse: wide columns folded back into key-value pairs using the stack(n, 'name1', col1, 'name2', col2, ...) SQL function, or the unpivot() DataFrame method introduced in Spark 3.4. Both operations are common in silver-to-gold transformations to reshape data for BI tools (pivot) or normalize wide legacy exports (stack).
summary: System: Reshape row values into columns with pivot(); fold wide columns into rows with stack() or unpivot()

label: When to use pivot vs stack/unpivot for data reshaping
content: Use pivot when: downstream BI tools or analytical consumers require a crosstab/wide format (one column per category), the number of distinct pivot values is known and bounded (practical limit: <100 distinct values — beyond that, column explosion degrades Parquet read performance and query plans). Always specify pivot values explicitly (.pivot('category', ['A', 'B', 'C'])) to reduce the operation to a single pass; omitting values triggers a full pre-scan to discover distinct values, adding an extra shuffle. Use stack/unpivot when: ingesting wide flat files with one column per date, per metric, or per entity into a normalized long-format silver table; preparing data for ML feature pipelines that expect long format; or undoing a prior pivot to restore normalization. Spark 3.4+ unpivot() is preferred over stack() for readability and type safety — both produce identical plans. Avoid pivoting on high-cardinality columns; use conditional aggregations (SUM(CASE WHEN category='A' THEN value END)) as a SQL alternative when the category set is small and static.
summary: Decision: pivot for known bounded categories into wide format; stack/unpivot to normalize wide columns into long key-value rows

label: Pivot and stack/unpivot code patterns in PySpark and Spark SQL
content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, sum as spark_sum, expr, coalesce, lit

# PIVOT — explicit values (single pass, recommended)
pivot_df = (df
  .groupBy('customer_id', 'year')
  .pivot('product_category', ['electronics', 'apparel', 'home'])  # explicit = no pre-scan
  .agg(spark_sum('revenue').alias('revenue')))
# Result columns: customer_id | year | electronics | apparel | home
# Null-fill pivot result (categories with no data produce null)
pivot_filled = pivot_df.na.fill(0, subset=['electronics', 'apparel', 'home'])

# PIVOT — dynamic (discovers distinct values; exploration only — triggers extra scan)
pivot_dynamic = (df
  .groupBy('customer_id')
  .pivot('quarter')
  .agg(spark_sum('revenue')))

# STACK (unpivot) — wide columns → key-value rows, Spark SQL expr
wide_df = spark.table('gold.metrics_wide')  # cols: device_id, cpu_pct, mem_pct, disk_pct

unpivoted = wide_df.select(
    'device_id',
    expr("stack(3, 'cpu_pct', cpu_pct, 'mem_pct', mem_pct, 'disk_pct', disk_pct) AS (metric_name, metric_value)")
)
# Result: device_id | metric_name | metric_value

# UNPIVOT (Spark 3.4+) — preferred syntax
unpivoted_34 = wide_df.unpivot(
    ids=['device_id'],
    values=['cpu_pct', 'mem_pct', 'disk_pct'],
    variableColumnName='metric_name',
    valueColumnName='metric_value'
)

# SQL alternative: conditional aggregation for small static category set
spark.sql("""
  SELECT customer_id, year,
    SUM(CASE WHEN product_category = 'electronics' THEN revenue END) AS electronics,
    SUM(CASE WHEN product_category = 'apparel'     THEN revenue END) AS apparel,
    SUM(CASE WHEN product_category = 'home'        THEN revenue END) AS home
  FROM silver.sales
  GROUP BY customer_id, year
""")
summary: Output: Explicit and dynamic pivot, stack() unpivot, Spark 3.4 unpivot(), and SQL conditional aggregation patterns

label: Apply broadcast, skew join hints, and SortMerge strategies for complex joins
content: Spark supports three primary join strategies. Broadcast join replicates the smaller table to all executor memory, eliminating the sort-merge shuffle — triggered automatically when the smaller table is below spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold (default 10MB; commonly raised to 100MB–1GB) or forced with broadcast() hint or SQL hint /*+ BROADCAST(t) */. SortMergeJoin (default for large-large joins) sorts both inputs on the join key and merges via a full shuffle — scales to arbitrarily large tables but requires an inter-node sort-merge shuffle. Skew join handling: AQE (Adaptive Query Execution) detects and splits skewed partitions at runtime when spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled=true; manual salting appends a random integer suffix to the join key and replicates the non-skewed side across the salt range — used as a fallback when AQE is insufficient.
summary: System: Broadcast hint eliminates shuffle for small tables; SortMerge is the default large-large strategy; AQE skew join and manual salting address key skew

label: Choose broadcast vs SortMerge vs skew join based on table size and key distribution
content: Use broadcast join when: the smaller table fits in executor memory (raise autoBroadcastJoinThreshold to 512MB–1GB for modern clusters with ≥16GB executor RAM); the join is a fact-to-dimension lookup; or the optimizer underestimates the small table's size due to stale statistics — apply explicit broadcast() hint to override. Do not broadcast tables that might grow — set a memory-safe size ceiling and monitor. Use SortMerge as the default for all large-large joins (fact-to-fact) — prefer it over any forced strategy; ensure shuffle.partitions is tuned (target 100–500MB per partition: total_data_size_GB × 1000 / target_partition_MB). Enable AQE (spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true, skewJoin.enabled=true) for all production jobs — it handles residual skew after the optimizer plans the join without code changes. Use manual salting only when AQE is unavailable or the skew is extreme, consistent, and known in advance (e.g., one customer_id contains 40% of all rows). Set autoBroadcastJoinThreshold=-1 during plan debugging to force SortMerge and isolate performance issues from broadcast decisions.
summary: Decision: broadcast for small dimension tables; SortMerge + AQE for large-large joins; manual salting as a last resort for extreme consistent skew

label: Complex join code patterns: broadcast hint, AQE skew join, and manual salting
content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast, col, lit, concat, rand, explode, array
import pyspark.sql.functions as F

fact_df = spark.table('silver.orders')
dim_df  = spark.table('gold.dim_customer')   # small dimension table

# Broadcast join — Python API (type-safe, preferred)
joined = fact_df.join(broadcast(dim_df), on='customer_id', how='left')

# Broadcast join — SQL hint
joined_sql = spark.sql("""
  SELECT /*+ BROADCAST(d) */ f.order_id, f.amount, d.customer_name, d.region
  FROM silver.orders f
  LEFT JOIN gold.dim_customer d ON f.customer_id = d.customer_id
""")

# Raise broadcast threshold at session level (tune per cluster memory)
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold', str(512 * 1024 * 1024))  # 512 MB

# AQE skew join — enable; no code change required in the join itself
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.enabled', 'true')
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled', 'true')
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionFactor', '5')      # default 5×
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionThresholdInBytes', str(256 * 1024 * 1024))  # 256 MB

skewed_join = (fact_df
  .join(spark.table('silver.shipments'), on='order_id', how='inner'))  # AQE splits skewed partitions at runtime

# Explicit SKEW hint (SQL) — when AQE is off or hint is needed for a specific side
skew_sql = spark.sql("""
  SELECT /*+ SKEW('orders') */ o.order_id, s.shipment_date
  FROM silver.orders o
  JOIN silver.shipments s ON o.order_id = s.order_id
""")

# Manual salting — fallback for extreme consistent skew when AQE is unavailable
SALT_FACTOR = 10

# Salt the large (skewed) side with a random bucket
salted_fact = (fact_df
  .withColumn('salt', (rand() * SALT_FACTOR).cast('int'))
  .withColumn('salted_key', concat(col('customer_id').cast('string'), lit('_'), col('salt').cast('string'))))

# Replicate the small (non-skewed) side across all salt buckets
salted_dim = (dim_df
  .withColumn('salt', explode(array([lit(i) for i in range(SALT_FACTOR)])))
  .withColumn('salted_key', concat(col('customer_id').cast('string'), lit('_'), col('salt').cast('string'))))

salted_joined = salted_fact.join(broadcast(salted_dim), on='salted_key', how='inner').drop('salt', 'salted_key')

# SortMerge tuning — scale shuffle partitions for large joins
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions', '400')   # target 128–512 MB per partition
large_join = fact_df.join(spark.table('silver.returns'), on='order_id', how='left')
summary: Output: Broadcast hint (Python and SQL), AQE skew join configuration, SQL SKEW hint, manual salting, and SortMerge shuffle tuning patterns

label: Apply window functions for ranking and offset calculations in PySpark
content: Window functions in PySpark operate over a WindowSpec created with Window.partitionBy(*cols).orderBy(*cols). Ranking functions — row_number(), rank(), dense_rank(), ntile(n) — require orderBy and assign a value to each row without collapsing the DataFrame. Offset functions — lead(col, offset, default) and lag(col, offset, default) — access values from rows ahead of or behind the current row within the partition. All window functions are imported from pyspark.sql.functions; Window is imported from pyspark.sql.window. A window spec without partitionBy applies the function over the entire DataFrame as one partition — expensive on large datasets.
summary: System: Use Window.partitionBy().orderBy() with ranking (row_number, rank, dense_rank, ntile) and offset (lead, lag) functions for per-partition row-level computations

label: Choose row_number vs rank vs dense_rank vs ntile vs lead/lag for the ranking use case
content: Use row_number() when each row must have a unique sequential integer — no ties possible; standard choice for deduplication (keep rn==1) and offset-based pagination. Use rank() when tied rows share a rank and subsequent ranks are skipped (e.g., competition results where two players tied at 2nd place means no 3rd place). Use dense_rank() when tied rows share a rank and no ranks are skipped (leaderboard bucketing, percentile bands). Use ntile(n) to divide a partition into n equal-sized buckets — returns 1..n; useful for quartile/decile analysis and workload distribution. Use lag(col, offset, default) to reference a prior row value within the partition (detect session gaps, compute deltas); use lead(col, offset, default) to reference a future row value (look-ahead flags, next-event enrichment). All ranking functions require orderBy in the WindowSpec; lead/lag produce null beyond partition boundaries unless a default is provided.
summary: Decision: row_number for unique rank/dedup; rank/dense_rank for tie-aware ranking; ntile for bucket assignment; lead/lag for prior/next row value access

label: Window function code patterns: row_number, rank, dense_rank, lead, lag, ntile
content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import row_number, rank, dense_rank, ntile, lead, lag, col
from pyspark.sql.window import Window

# Signatures:
# row_number()                          -> Column  (no args; requires orderBy)
# rank()                                -> Column
# dense_rank()                          -> Column
# ntile(n: int)                         -> Column
# lead(col, offset=1, default=None)     -> Column
# lag(col, offset=1, default=None)      -> Column

# Base window spec
w = Window.partitionBy('customer_id').orderBy(col('order_date').desc())

# row_number — unique sequential rank; used for deduplication
df = df.withColumn('rn', row_number().over(w))
latest_orders = df.filter(col('rn') == 1).drop('rn')

# rank / dense_rank — tie-aware ranking
w_score = Window.partitionBy('region').orderBy(col('revenue').desc())
df = (df
  .withColumn('rank',       rank().over(w_score))
  .withColumn('dense_rank', dense_rank().over(w_score)))

# ntile — quartile bucketing
w_ntile = Window.partitionBy('product_category').orderBy('revenue')
df = df.withColumn('quartile', ntile(4).over(w_ntile))   # returns 1, 2, 3, or 4

# lead / lag — prior and next order amounts
w_ordered = Window.partitionBy('customer_id').orderBy('order_date')
df = (df
  .withColumn('prev_amount', lag('amount',  1, 0.0).over(w_ordered))  # prior row; 0.0 at boundary
  .withColumn('next_amount', lead('amount', 1, 0.0).over(w_ordered))) # next row

# Session gap detection with lag
from pyspark.sql.functions import unix_timestamp, datediff
df = df.withColumn('prev_order_date', lag('order_date', 1).over(w_ordered))
df = df.withColumn('days_since_last', datediff(col('order_date'), col('prev_order_date')))
# days_since_last IS NULL for each customer's first order
summary: Output: Window function signatures and usage examples for row_number, rank, dense_rank, ntile, lead, and lag with partitionBy/orderBy WindowSpec

label: Author multi-dimensional aggregations with groupBy, cube, rollup, and grouping_sets
content: PySpark supports four aggregation scopes beyond simple groupBy. groupBy(*cols).agg(*exprs) computes aggregates over explicitly listed grouping columns — one output row per unique group. rollup(*cols).agg(*exprs) adds hierarchical subtotals by progressively dropping the rightmost grouping column, ending with a grand total row (null in all grouping cols). cube(*cols).agg(*exprs) computes aggregates for all 2^n combinations of grouping columns, producing subtotals for every possible subset including the grand total. grouping_sets() is Spark SQL syntax specifying exact grouping combinations without computing the full cube. Use grouping(col) or grouping_id(*cols) alongside rollup/cube to identify which grouping columns were suppressed (i.e., which rows are subtotals vs leaf-level detail).
summary: System: groupBy for flat aggregation; rollup for hierarchical subtotals; cube for full cross-dimensional aggregation; grouping_sets for selective combinations

label: Choose groupBy vs rollup vs cube vs grouping_sets for aggregation scope
content: Use groupBy().agg() for all standard flat aggregations where no subtotals are needed — most silver and gold aggregations fall here. Use rollup() for hierarchical reporting where grouping columns have a natural parent-child order (e.g., year → quarter → month revenue subtotals, region → country → city sales summaries) — produces N+1 subtotal rows per leaf group. Use cube() for full cross-dimensional BI analysis requiring every combination of dimensions (region × product × channel) — output grows as 2^n per key cardinality; avoid on high-cardinality dimension sets (>4–5 dimensions) as output can exceed input size. Use grouping_sets() in Spark SQL when only specific grouping combinations are required (e.g., (year, region), (year,), () for grand total) without computing the full cube — more compute-efficient than cube() for selective reporting. Always add a grouping_id() column when using rollup or cube to allow downstream consumers to distinguish subtotal rows from leaf rows using a bitmask.
summary: Decision: groupBy for flat aggregation; rollup for ordered hierarchical subtotals; cube for full cross-product combinations; grouping_sets for selective multi-dimensional combinations

label: Aggregation code patterns: groupBy+agg, cube, rollup, grouping_sets with grouping_id
content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import sum as spark_sum, avg, count, col, grouping_id, coalesce, lit

# groupBy + agg — standard flat aggregation
result = (df
  .groupBy('region', 'product_category', 'year')
  .agg(
      spark_sum('revenue').alias('total_revenue'),
      avg('revenue').alias('avg_revenue'),
      count('*').alias('order_count')
  ))

# rollup — hierarchical subtotals: (region, product, year) → (region, product) → (region,) → ()
rollup_result = (df
  .rollup('region', 'product_category', 'year')
  .agg(spark_sum('revenue').alias('total_revenue'), grouping_id().alias('gid'))
  # gid==0: leaf detail; gid==1: year subtotal; gid==3: product subtotal; gid==7: grand total
  .withColumn('region',           coalesce(col('region'),           lit('ALL')))
  .withColumn('product_category', coalesce(col('product_category'), lit('ALL')))
  .withColumn('year',             coalesce(col('year').cast('string'), lit('ALL')))
  )

# cube — all 2^3=8 combinations of (region, product_category, year)
cube_result = (df
  .cube('region', 'product_category', 'year')
  .agg(spark_sum('revenue').alias('total_revenue'), grouping_id().alias('gid')))

# grouping_sets — Spark SQL; selective combinations only
spark.sql("""
  SELECT
    region,
    product_category,
    year,
    SUM(revenue)   AS total_revenue,
    GROUPING_ID()  AS gid
  FROM silver.sales
  GROUP BY GROUPING SETS (
    (region, product_category, year),   -- leaf level
    (region, year),                      -- region-year subtotal
    (year),                              -- year-only subtotal
    ()                                   -- grand total
  )
""")
summary: Output: groupBy+agg, rollup, cube, and grouping_sets code patterns with grouping_id for subtotal row identification

label: Apply string functions for extraction, replacement, splitting, and concatenation in PySpark
content: PySpark string functions operate on StringType columns and return null when the input is null. regexp_extract(col, pattern, groupIndex) extracts a captured group from a Java regex match — returns an empty string (not null) when the pattern does not match. regexp_replace(col, pattern, replacement) substitutes all regex matches with a replacement string. split(col, pattern, limit=-1) splits by a regex delimiter and returns an ArrayType(StringType) — limit caps the number of splits. concat_ws(sep, *cols) concatenates multiple columns with a separator, skipping null values. trim(col)/ltrim(col)/rtrim(col) strip leading/trailing whitespace; trim(trimStr, col) strips a specific character. All functions are available in pyspark.sql.functions and execute in the JVM/Photon engine without Python serialization overhead.
summary: System: regexp_extract for capture group extraction, regexp_replace for pattern substitution, split for array tokenization, concat_ws for null-safe concatenation, trim for whitespace removal

label: Choose string function based on extraction, replacement, splitting, or concatenation requirement
content: Use regexp_extract() when a specific capture group must be pulled from a pattern match (e.g., extracting a numeric order ID from a log string like 'order_id=12345;'). Be aware it returns empty string, not null, on no match — use nullif(regexp_extract(...), '') to convert non-matches to null. Use regexp_replace() for in-place substitution of matching substrings (masking PII digits, normalizing delimiters, stripping control characters). Use split() when a column contains delimited lists (e.g., comma-separated tags) that need to be converted to arrays for downstream explode or higher-order function processing. Use concat_ws() instead of concat() when joining nullable columns — concat_ws() skips nulls transparently while concat() propagates a single null to null output; also preferred for building composite keys with delimiters. Always apply trim() as a first-pass baseline cleansing on all string columns ingested from CSV or API sources before any further transformation. Never use Python UDFs for transformations that built-in string functions can handle — built-in functions avoid Python serialization and run in Photon.
summary: Decision: regexp_extract for capture-group pulls (mind empty-string on no match); regexp_replace for pattern substitution; split for array tokenization; concat_ws for null-safe joining; trim as baseline cleansing

label: String function code patterns: regexp_extract, regexp_replace, split, concat_ws, trim
content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import (
    regexp_extract, regexp_replace, split, concat_ws,
    trim, ltrim, rtrim, col, lower, nullif, lit
)

# Signatures:
# regexp_extract(str, pattern, idx)     -> Column (returns '' on no match, not null)
# regexp_replace(str, pattern, replace) -> Column
# split(str, pattern, limit=-1)         -> Column (ArrayType(StringType))
# concat_ws(sep, *cols)                 -> Column (skips nulls)
# trim(col) / ltrim(col) / rtrim(col)  -> Column
# trim(trimStr, col)                    -> Column (strip specific char)

# regexp_extract — pull capture group from pattern
# Example: extract numeric order ID from 'ORDER-12345-2024'
df = df.withColumn('order_num',
    regexp_extract(col('order_ref'), r'ORDER-(\d+)-\d{4}', 1))
# Convert empty-string non-match to null:
df = df.withColumn('order_num',
    nullif(regexp_extract(col('order_ref'), r'ORDER-(\d+)', 1), lit('')))

# regexp_replace — mask last 4 digits of phone number
df = df.withColumn('phone_masked',
    regexp_replace(col('phone'), r'\d{4}$', 'XXXX'))

# regexp_replace — strip all non-alphanumeric characters
df = df.withColumn('sku_clean',
    regexp_replace(col('sku_raw'), r'[^A-Za-z0-9]', ''))

# split — tokenize comma-separated tags into array
df = df.withColumn('tag_array', split(col('tags'), r',\s*'))
# Limit to first 3 tokens:
df = df.withColumn('tag_array_3', split(col('tags'), r',\s*', 3))

# concat_ws — build composite key; skips nulls automatically
df = df.withColumn('composite_key',
    concat_ws('|', col('region'), col('product_id'), col('year').cast('string')))

# trim — baseline string cleansing pipeline
df = (df
  .withColumn('status',   lower(trim(col('status'))))
  .withColumn('email',    lower(trim(col('email'))))
  .withColumn('country',  trim(col('country')))
  .withColumn('currency', trim(lit('$'), col('amount_str'))))  # strip leading '$'
summary: Output: regexp_extract, regexp_replace, split, concat_ws, and trim signatures with usage examples for common silver-layer string cleansing patterns

label: Apply date and time functions for truncation, differencing, and epoch conversion in PySpark
content: PySpark date/time functions operate on DateType and TimestampType columns. date_trunc(unit, timestamp) truncates a TimestampType to a specified unit ('year', 'quarter', 'month', 'week', 'day', 'hour', 'minute', 'second') and returns a TimestampType — used for time-series bucketing. datediff(end, start) returns the integer number of days between two DateType or TimestampType columns (end minus start). months_between(date1, date2, roundOff=True) returns a Double representing fractional months between two dates; negative if date1 is before date2. from_unixtime(epoch_seconds, format='yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss') converts a LongType Unix epoch column to a formatted StringType timestamp. to_timestamp(col, format) parses a StringType column into a TimestampType using the specified Java date format pattern; returns null on parse failure. to_date(col, format) is the DateType equivalent of to_timestamp.
summary: System: date_trunc for time bucketing, datediff for day-count differences, months_between for fractional month tenure, from_unixtime for epoch conversion, to_timestamp for string parsing

label: Choose date/time function based on truncation, differencing, or conversion requirement
content: Use date_trunc() for time-series aggregation bucketing (grouping events by hour, day, week, month) — preserves TimestampType for downstream comparisons and sorts, unlike date_format() which returns a string. Use datediff() for integer day-count differences (SLA breach detection, age-in-days, day-based cohort assignment) — simpler and faster than computing epoch arithmetic manually. Use months_between() when fractional month precision is required (subscription tenure, loan age, employee tenure calculations) — wrap with round() for whole-month reporting or floor() for conservative rounding. Use from_unixtime() when ingesting Unix epoch integers from Kafka messages, IoT sensor payloads, or Unix log files — chain with .cast('timestamp') to produce a native TimestampType column rather than a string. Use to_timestamp() when parsing non-ISO date strings from CSV or JSON bronze sources — always specify the format pattern explicitly (e.g., 'MM/dd/yyyy HH:mm:ss') to prevent locale-dependent parsing failures; returns null on parse failure rather than raising an exception, so add a null-count quality check after parsing.
summary: Decision: date_trunc for TimestampType bucketing; datediff for integer day differences; months_between for fractional tenure; from_unixtime for epoch→timestamp conversion; to_timestamp for string→timestamp parsing with explicit format

label: Date/time function code patterns: date_trunc, datediff, months_between, from_unixtime, to_timestamp
content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import (
    date_trunc, datediff, months_between, from_unixtime,
    to_timestamp, to_date, col, round as spark_round, current_date, current_timestamp, lit
)

# Signatures:
# date_trunc(unit: str, ts: Column)          -> Column (TimestampType)
# datediff(end: Column, start: Column)       -> Column (IntegerType)
# months_between(d1, d2, roundOff=True)      -> Column (DoubleType)
# from_unixtime(epoch: Column, fmt: str)     -> Column (StringType)
# to_timestamp(col: Column, format: str)     -> Column (TimestampType; null on failure)
# to_date(col: Column, format: str)          -> Column (DateType; null on failure)

# date_trunc — bucket events by hour and by month for time-series aggregation
df = (df
  .withColumn('event_hour',  date_trunc('hour',  col('event_timestamp')))
  .withColumn('event_month', date_trunc('month', col('event_timestamp')))
  .withColumn('event_week',  date_trunc('week',  col('event_timestamp'))))

# Group by month bucket
hourly_counts = (df
  .groupBy('event_hour')
  .count()
  .orderBy('event_hour'))

# datediff — SLA breach detection (order age in days)
df = df.withColumn('order_age_days', datediff(current_date(), col('order_date')))
overdue = df.filter(col('order_age_days') > 30)

# months_between — customer tenure in whole months
df = df.withColumn('tenure_months',
    spark_round(months_between(current_date().cast('timestamp'), col('signup_date')), 0).cast('int'))

# from_unixtime — convert Kafka epoch millis → timestamp
# (divide ms→s first, then cast to TimestampType)
df = (df
  .withColumn('event_ts_str', from_unixtime(col('epoch_ms') / 1000, 'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss'))
  .withColumn('event_ts',     col('event_ts_str').cast('timestamp')))
# Or in one step using cast:
df = df.withColumn('event_ts', (col('epoch_ms') / 1000).cast('timestamp'))

# to_timestamp — parse non-ISO strings from CSV bronze layer
df = df.withColumn('order_ts', to_timestamp(col('order_date_str'), 'MM/dd/yyyy HH:mm:ss'))
df = df.withColumn('ship_date', to_date(col('ship_date_str'),     'dd-MMM-yyyy'))

# Quality check — detect parse failures
failure_rate = df.filter(col('order_ts').isNull()).count() / df.count()
if failure_rate > 0.01:
    raise ValueError(f'to_timestamp failure rate {failure_rate:.2%} — check format pattern')
summary: Output: date_trunc, datediff, months_between, from_unixtime, and to_timestamp signatures with usage examples for time-series bucketing, tenure calculation, epoch conversion, and string parsing

label: Apply higher-order functions to transform and filter ArrayType columns without explode
content: Spark higher-order functions operate directly on ArrayType columns using lambda expressions, avoiding the shuffle and row-multiplication cost of explode/collect_list. transform(array, element -> expr) maps a function over each array element and returns a new array of the same length. filter(array, element -> condition) returns a new array containing only elements that satisfy the condition (shorter or empty array). aggregate(array, zero, (acc, element) -> merge_expr, finish_expr) performs a fold over array elements using an accumulator, returning a scalar or struct. exists(array, element -> condition) returns a BooleanType indicating whether any element satisfies the condition. forall(array, element -> condition) returns true if all elements satisfy the condition. All higher-order functions use lambda syntax and are available via pyspark.sql.functions.transform/filter/aggregate/exists or via Spark SQL's expr() string expressions.
summary: System: transform for per-element mapping, filter for in-array subsetting, aggregate for fold/reduce to scalar, exists for any-match boolean — all operate on ArrayType without explode

label: Choose higher-order function vs explode/collect for array column operations
content: Use transform() when applying a per-element scalar transformation to an array column (upper-casing string elements, multiplying price list by a factor, applying a lookup to each tag) — avoids the explode+withColumn+collect_list shuffle that would expand and re-collapse rows. Use filter() when subsetting array elements by a condition (keeping only active items, removing null elements, filtering line items above a threshold) — produces a shorter array in-place with no row count change. Use aggregate() when computing a scalar summary of an array (summing line item amounts, concatenating tag strings, finding max within an array column) — equivalent to a per-row reduce without exploding to rows; supports a finalize expression for post-accumulation transformation. Use exists() in DataFrame.filter() or .withColumn() to check if any array element meets a condition (orders containing a specific product category) — cleaner than size(filter(array, cond)) > 0. Use forall() when all elements must satisfy a condition (validate that all items are in-stock). Prefer higher-order functions over explode when: (1) the result must remain an array, (2) no cross-row aggregation is required, or (3) the input arrays are large and the explode shuffle cost is prohibitive.
summary: Decision: transform for in-array element mapping; filter for in-array subsetting; aggregate for fold-to-scalar; exists/forall for boolean array membership checks — prefer over explode when output must remain an array

label: Higher-order function code patterns: transform, filter, aggregate, exists on ArrayType columns
content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import (
    transform, filter as array_filter, aggregate, exists, forall,
    col, lit, upper, size, expr
)

# Signatures:
# transform(array, function: Column -> Column)                         -> Column (ArrayType)
# filter(array, function: Column -> BooleanColumn)                    -> Column (ArrayType)
# aggregate(array, zero, merge_func, finish_func=None)                -> Column (scalar or struct)
# exists(array, function: Column -> BooleanColumn)                    -> Column (BooleanType)
# forall(array, function: Column -> BooleanColumn)                    -> Column (BooleanType)

# transform — upper-case each string element in an array column
df = df.withColumn('tags_upper', transform(col('tags'), lambda t: upper(t)))

# transform — multiply each price in a prices array by a discount factor
df = df.withColumn('discounted_prices',
    transform(col('prices'), lambda p: p * lit(0.90)))

# filter — keep only tags that start with 'prod_'
df = df.withColumn('prod_tags',
    array_filter(col('tags'), lambda t: t.startswith('prod_')))

# filter — remove null elements from array
df = df.withColumn('clean_tags',
    array_filter(col('tags'), lambda t: t.isNotNull()))

# filter — keep line items above $50
df = df.withColumn('high_value_items',
    array_filter(col('line_item_prices'), lambda p: p > lit(50.0)))

# aggregate — sum all line item amounts in an array (fold with accumulator)
df = df.withColumn('total_amount',
    aggregate(
        col('line_item_amounts'),
        lit(0.0),                          # zero accumulator
        lambda acc, item: acc + item       # merge: add each item to accumulator
    ))

# aggregate — concatenate tags with separator
df = df.withColumn('tag_string',
    aggregate(
        col('tags'),
        lit(''),
        lambda acc, tag: expr("CASE WHEN acc = '' THEN tag ELSE concat(acc, ',', tag) END")
    ))

# exists — flag orders containing a 'priority' tag
df = df.withColumn('has_priority_tag',
    exists(col('tags'), lambda t: t == lit('priority')))

# exists in filter — select only rows where any line item exceeds $1000
high_value_orders = df.filter(
    exists(col('line_item_prices'), lambda p: p > lit(1000.0)))

# forall — ensure all items are in-stock (quantity > 0)
df = df.withColumn('fully_available',
    forall(col('item_quantities'), lambda q: q > lit(0)))

# SQL equivalent via expr() — same engine, useful in SQL-heavy notebooks
df = df.withColumn('tags_upper_sql',
    expr("transform(tags, t -> upper(t))"))
df = df.withColumn('total_sql',
    expr("aggregate(line_item_amounts, 0D, (acc, x) -> acc + x)"))
summary: Output: transform, filter, aggregate, exists, and forall signatures with usage examples for in-array mapping, subsetting, folding, and membership checks on ArrayType columns

label: Compare materialized views and gold Delta tables for the serving layer
content: Delta Lake does not natively support incrementally-refreshed materialized views in the traditional sense. Two gold serving patterns exist: (1) Gold Delta tables populated by scheduled batch jobs or DLT pipelines — full ACID, any cluster type, Z-ordering and custom partitioning, complete pipeline observability. (2) Databricks SQL materialized views (CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW … SCHEDULE CRON …, Serverless SQL warehouses only) — auto-refresh with incremental computation, no pipeline code, but limited to SQL constructs and Serverless SQL. Gold Delta tables are the standard for production lakehouse architectures; Databricks SQL materialized views suit simple SQL aggregations on Serverless infrastructure where pipeline overhead is undesirable.
summary: System: Gold serving layer options — scheduled gold Delta tables vs Databricks SQL materialized views with incremental refresh

label: Choose gold Delta table vs Databricks SQL materialized view
content: Use gold Delta tables when: transforms require PySpark (window functions, ML scoring, multi-hop joins), any cluster type must read the output, ACID guarantees and time travel are needed, or custom partitioning and Z-ordering are required for BI query performance. Use Databricks SQL materialized views when: the entire transform is expressible in plain SQL, the serving infrastructure is exclusively Serverless SQL, and eliminating Workflow/DLT orchestration overhead is a priority. Prefer gold Delta tables for production — they are portable, debuggable, and performant at any scale. Materialized views are appropriate for self-service analyst use cases where low pipeline complexity matters more than advanced optimization.
summary: Decision: Gold Delta table for complex/portable pipelines; Databricks SQL materialized view for simple SQL on Serverless SQL warehouses

label: Gold Delta table INSERT OVERWRITE refresh and materialized view DDL patterns
content:
-- Gold Delta table: idempotent partition refresh (overwrite last 2 days for late arrivals)
INSERT OVERWRITE gold.sales_daily
PARTITION (report_date)
SELECT
  CAST(date_trunc('day', order_ts) AS DATE) AS report_date,
  region, product_category,
  SUM(amount)              AS total_revenue,
  COUNT(*)                 AS order_count,
  COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS unique_customers
FROM silver.orders
WHERE order_ts >= current_date() - INTERVAL 2 DAYS
GROUP BY 1, 2, 3;

-- MERGE-based refresh (handles corrections/refunds):
MERGE INTO gold.sales_daily AS t
USING (
  SELECT CAST(date_trunc('day', order_ts) AS DATE) AS report_date,
         region, product_category,
         SUM(amount) AS total_revenue, COUNT(*) AS order_count
  FROM silver.orders
  WHERE order_ts >= current_date() - INTERVAL 2 DAYS
  GROUP BY 1, 2, 3
) AS s
ON t.report_date = s.report_date AND t.region = s.region AND t.product_category = s.product_category
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET *
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT *;

-- Databricks SQL Materialized View (Serverless SQL only):
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW IF NOT EXISTS gold.sales_daily_mv
SCHEDULE CRON '0 2 * * *'  -- refresh daily at 02:00 UTC
AS
SELECT
  CAST(date_trunc('day', order_ts) AS DATE) AS report_date,
  region,
  SUM(amount) AS total_revenue,
  COUNT(*)    AS order_count
FROM silver.orders
GROUP BY 1, 2;
summary: Output: Gold Delta table INSERT OVERWRITE partition refresh and MERGE patterns vs Databricks SQL materialized view DDL

label: Design star schema fact and dimension tables in Delta Lake
content: Star schema in Delta Lake uses Delta tables for both fact tables (business events: orders, transactions, shipments) and dimension tables (descriptive entities: customer, product, date). Fact tables hold foreign keys referencing dimension surrogate keys and measure columns. Dimension tables hold a surrogate key (BIGINT IDENTITY or SHA-256-derived), a natural/business key, descriptive attributes, and SCD columns (effective_date, expiry_date, is_current) for Type 2 history tracking. Surrogate keys via IDENTITY (Delta Lake 3.0+/Databricks) auto-increment without pipeline coordination. Hash-based keys (xxhash64 or SHA-256 cast to BIGINT) are deterministic and safe for parallel multi-pipeline writes. Dimension tables are typically small enough to broadcast in fact-to-dimension joins.
summary: System: Build star schema fact and dimension Delta tables with surrogate keys, natural keys, and SCD Type 2 columns for historical tracking

label: Choose surrogate key generation strategy and SCD type for dimension tables
content: Use IDENTITY columns when: a single pipeline loads the dimension in append-only mode, monotonically increasing integers are required for BI tool compatibility, and key stability across full reloads is not needed. Use hash-based surrogate keys (xxhash64 of business key cast to BIGINT) when: multiple pipelines or parallel jobs populate the dimension, key values must be deterministic and reproducible without coordination, or the dimension must be fully recomputable from source data. Use SCD Type 1 (UPDATE in place) when: historical attribute changes have no analytical value and overwrites are acceptable — simpler pipeline, no row accumulation. Use SCD Type 2 (new row per change with effective_date/expiry_date/is_current) when: downstream fact joins must be resolved to the attribute values valid at the time of the event.
summary: Decision: IDENTITY for single-pipeline append dims; hash keys for deterministic/parallel dims; SCD Type 1 for non-historical; SCD Type 2 for point-in-time correctness

label: Star schema DDL and SCD Type 2 merge pattern for Delta dimension and fact tables
content:
-- Dimension table: IDENTITY surrogate key + SCD Type 2 columns
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS gold.dim_customer (
  customer_sk    BIGINT GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY,  -- surrogate key
  customer_id    STRING NOT NULL,                       -- natural/business key
  customer_name  STRING,
  region         STRING,
  segment        STRING,
  effective_date DATE   NOT NULL,
  expiry_date    DATE,             -- NULL = currently active row
  is_current     BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE
)
USING DELTA
TBLPROPERTIES ('delta.enableChangeDataFeed' = 'true');

-- Fact table: foreign keys to dimension surrogate keys
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS gold.fact_orders (
  order_id      STRING        NOT NULL,
  customer_sk   BIGINT        NOT NULL,  -- FK → gold.dim_customer.customer_sk
  product_sk    BIGINT        NOT NULL,
  date_sk       INT           NOT NULL,  -- FK → gold.dim_date.date_sk (YYYYMMDD INT)
  amount        DECIMAL(18,2),
  quantity      INT,
  report_date   DATE                     -- partition column
)
USING DELTA
PARTITIONED BY (report_date);

-- SCD Type 2 MERGE: expire changed rows, insert new version
MERGE INTO gold.dim_customer AS t
USING (
  SELECT customer_id, customer_name, region, segment, current_date() AS eff
  FROM silver.customers_current
) AS s
ON t.customer_id = s.customer_id AND t.is_current = TRUE
WHEN MATCHED AND (t.region <> s.region OR t.segment <> s.segment) THEN
  UPDATE SET expiry_date = current_date() - INTERVAL 1 DAY, is_current = FALSE
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN
  INSERT (customer_id, customer_name, region, segment, effective_date, expiry_date, is_current)
  VALUES (s.customer_id, s.customer_name, s.region, s.segment, s.eff, NULL, TRUE);

-- Insert new active version for rows just expired above
INSERT INTO gold.dim_customer (customer_id, customer_name, region, segment, effective_date, expiry_date, is_current)
SELECT s.customer_id, s.customer_name, s.region, s.segment, current_date(), NULL, TRUE
FROM silver.customers_current s
INNER JOIN gold.dim_customer t
  ON s.customer_id = t.customer_id
 WHERE t.is_current = FALSE
   AND t.expiry_date = current_date() - INTERVAL 1 DAY;
summary: Output: Star schema DDL for dimension/fact Delta tables with IDENTITY surrogate key and SCD Type 2 MERGE pattern

label: Build pre-aggregated daily/weekly/monthly rollup tables for dashboards
content: Pre-aggregated summary tables store the result of expensive GROUP BY aggregations at daily, weekly, and monthly granularities so BI tools query pre-computed results without scanning silver fact tables on every dashboard load. Standard pattern: (1) define one Delta table per grain (gold.sales_daily, gold.sales_weekly, gold.sales_monthly), each partitioned by its date grain column; (2) populate with idempotent INSERT OVERWRITE PARTITION jobs that reprocess a rolling window (3–7 days for daily, 4 weeks for weekly, 3 months for monthly) to absorb late-arriving silver data; (3) schedule via Databricks Workflows to run after silver pipeline completion, using a table-sensor or Delta change trigger as the upstream dependency.
summary: System: Pre-compute dashboard aggregations into separate daily/weekly/monthly gold Delta tables with partitioned idempotent refresh and rolling reprocess windows

label: Choose rollup grain, refresh cadence, reprocessing window, and write mode
content: Define one table per grain rather than mixing grains in a single table — mixed-grain tables require NULL-filled grouping columns that confuse BI tools. Reprocessing window: daily grain → reprocess last 3–7 days; weekly → last 4 weeks; monthly → last 3 months. Window size should match the P99 silver lateness observed for the source domain. Use INSERT OVERWRITE PARTITION for immutable historical metrics (revenue, shipments) — idempotent, no duplicate risk. Use MERGE for correctable metrics (refunds, adjustments, cancellations that can update already-closed periods). Only materialize dimension combinations that appear in >80% of dashboard filters — avoid cross-joining all dimensions into a cube, which explodes row counts. Refresh cadence: daily grain → T+1 (after midnight silver close), weekly → Monday AM, monthly → 1st of month after EOD silver close.
summary: Decision: One table per grain; INSERT OVERWRITE for immutable metrics; MERGE for correctable metrics; reprocess 3-7 day window; only materialize high-frequency dimension combinations

label: Daily/weekly/monthly rollup table DDL and INSERT OVERWRITE refresh code patterns
content:
-- DDL: daily rollup table (run once)
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS gold.sales_daily (
  region           STRING,
  product_category STRING,
  channel          STRING,
  total_revenue    DECIMAL(18,2),
  total_units      BIGINT,
  order_count      BIGINT,
  unique_customers BIGINT,
  report_date      DATE
)
USING DELTA
PARTITIONED BY (report_date)
