TBLPROPERTIES ('delta.autoOptimize.optimizeWrite' = 'true');

-- Refresh: daily grain, reprocess last 7 days for late silver data
INSERT OVERWRITE gold.sales_daily PARTITION (report_date)
SELECT
  region, product_category, channel,
  SUM(amount)              AS total_revenue,
  SUM(quantity)            AS total_units,
  COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS order_count,
  COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS unique_customers,
  CAST(date_trunc('day', order_ts) AS DATE) AS report_date
FROM silver.orders
WHERE order_ts >= current_date() - INTERVAL 7 DAYS
GROUP BY region, product_category, channel, CAST(date_trunc('day', order_ts) AS DATE);

-- Refresh: weekly grain (week_start = Monday), reprocess last 4 weeks
INSERT OVERWRITE gold.sales_weekly PARTITION (week_start)
SELECT
  region, product_category,
  SUM(amount)              AS total_revenue,
  COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS order_count,
  CAST(date_trunc('week', order_ts) AS DATE) AS week_start
FROM silver.orders
WHERE order_ts >= current_date() - INTERVAL 4 WEEKS
GROUP BY region, product_category, CAST(date_trunc('week', order_ts) AS DATE);

-- Refresh: monthly grain, reprocess last 3 months
INSERT OVERWRITE gold.sales_monthly PARTITION (month_start)
SELECT
  region, product_category,
  SUM(amount)              AS total_revenue,
  COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS order_count,
  COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS unique_customers,
  CAST(date_trunc('month', order_ts) AS DATE) AS month_start
FROM silver.orders
WHERE order_ts >= add_months(current_date(), -3)
GROUP BY region, product_category, CAST(date_trunc('month', order_ts) AS DATE);
summary: Output: Daily/weekly/monthly rollup table DDL and INSERT OVERWRITE partition refresh patterns with rolling reprocess windows

label: Apply partitioning, Z-ordering, and liquid clustering to gold Delta tables for downstream consumers
content: Gold Delta tables serving BI tools should be optimized so that common dashboard queries scan the minimum data possible. Three complementary techniques: (1) Partitioning (PARTITIONED BY) — places data into directory-level buckets on the partition column; effective for date-grain filters that appear in nearly every query; eliminates whole partitions from the scan via partition pruning. (2) Z-ordering (ZORDER BY within OPTIMIZE) — co-locates related values for 2–4 high-selectivity non-partition columns within Parquet files, enabling file-level data skipping via Delta statistics; effective for region, product_category, channel. (3) Liquid clustering (CLUSTER BY, Delta 3.1+/Databricks) — an incremental, mutation-tolerant alternative to static partitioning + Z-ordering that re-clusters only affected files on each OPTIMIZE run; suited for mutable gold tables with evolving query patterns. Optimize file sizes to 128MB–1GB using OPTIMIZE before serving BI tools.
summary: System: Partition gold tables by date grain; Z-order on 2-4 secondary filter columns; OPTIMIZE before BI exposure; use liquid clustering for mutable/dynamic-query tables

label: Choose partition column, Z-order columns, and optimization cadence for gold tables
content: Partition by a date column at the grain matching the dominant query filter: daily (report_date DATE) for day-range queries, monthly (month_start DATE) for month/YTD queries. Never partition on high-cardinality non-date columns (customer_id, product_id) — creates millions of small files, degrading listing performance and read latency. Apply ZORDER BY on 2–4 columns that frequently appear in WHERE or JOIN predicates but are not the partition column (e.g., region, product_category, channel). Run OPTIMIZE + ZORDER after the daily load, scoped with WHERE report_date >= … to avoid full table rewrites. Downstream streaming readStreams on the gold table must set ignoreChanges=true to tolerate file rewrites from OPTIMIZE. Switch to liquid clustering (CLUSTER BY) when: query patterns change frequently, gold tables receive high-frequency incremental writes, or OPTIMIZE + ZORDER rewrites are causing excessive downstream checkpoint invalidation.
summary: Decision: Partition by date grain; ZORDER on 2-4 secondary columns after daily load; liquid clustering for mutable/dynamic-query gold tables; set ignoreChanges on downstream readStreams

label: Gold table partition, OPTIMIZE + ZORDER, and liquid clustering code patterns
content:
-- Standard gold table: date partition + Z-order on secondary filter columns
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS gold.orders_gold (
  order_id         STRING        NOT NULL,
  customer_id      STRING,
  region           STRING,
  product_category STRING,
  channel          STRING,
  amount           DECIMAL(18,2),
  order_ts         TIMESTAMP,
  report_date      DATE  -- partition column
)
USING DELTA
PARTITIONED BY (report_date)
TBLPROPERTIES (
  'delta.autoOptimize.optimizeWrite' = 'true',
  'delta.targetFileSize'             = '134217728'  -- 128 MB
);

-- Run after daily load: OPTIMIZE + ZORDER on last 7 days only
OPTIMIZE gold.orders_gold
WHERE report_date >= current_date() - INTERVAL 7 DAYS
ZORDER BY (region, product_category, channel);

-- Downstream streaming reader: must set ignoreChanges to tolerate OPTIMIZE rewrites
-- spark.readStream.format('delta').option('ignoreChanges','true').table('gold.orders_gold')

-- Check file size distribution after OPTIMIZE
SELECT report_date,
       count(*)       AS file_count,
       sum(size)/1e6  AS total_mb,
       avg(size)/1e6  AS avg_file_mb,
       min(size)/1e6  AS min_file_mb
FROM (SELECT explode(files) AS f, * FROM (DESCRIBE DETAIL gold.orders_gold))
SELECT report_date,
       COUNT(*)           AS file_count
FROM gold.orders_gold
GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1 DESC LIMIT 30;
-- (Use DESCRIBE DETAIL + delta history for partition-level file stats)

-- Liquid clustering (Databricks / Delta 3.1+): no static PARTITION BY required
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS gold.orders_clustered (
  order_id         STRING,
  region           STRING,
  product_category STRING,
  channel          STRING,
  report_date      DATE,
  amount           DECIMAL(18,2)
)
USING DELTA
CLUSTER BY (report_date, region, product_category)  -- replaces PARTITION BY + ZORDER BY
TBLPROPERTIES ('delta.enableDeletionVectors' = 'true');

-- Incremental clustering (fast; rewrites only unclustered files)
OPTIMIZE gold.orders_clustered;
summary: Output: Gold table DDL with PARTITIONED BY and OPTIMIZE + ZORDER, plus liquid CLUSTER BY pattern with file size tuning

label: Create Unity Catalog views and grant BI tool access to gold layer tables
content: Unity Catalog secures gold layer access through catalog.schema.table three-part naming, privilege grants, row/column-level security, and audit logging. BI tools (Power BI, Tableau, Looker) connect via Databricks SQL warehouses using JDBC/ODBC or native connectors and query gold tables or Unity Catalog SQL views. Views decouple BI consumers from underlying table structure — renaming columns to business names, applying row filters, and masking sensitive columns without touching the physical table. Grants are issued to service principals (for BI tool service accounts) or to Azure AD/Okta groups (for user access) — never directly to individual users. Grant USE CATALOG, USE SCHEMA, and SELECT on views (not on underlying gold tables) to enforce access through the view layer and enable future view logic changes without re-granting.
summary: System: Expose gold Delta tables to BI tools via Unity Catalog SQL views with row/column security; grant USE + SELECT to AD groups and service principals on views only

label: Choose Unity Catalog access model: direct table grants vs view grants vs row filter functions
content: Grant SELECT directly on gold Delta tables only when all consumers require full row/column access and no schema aliasing is needed — simplest model but exposes consumers to future table renames or column additions. Create SQL views and grant SELECT on views (not underlying tables) when: a stable query API must survive table restructuring, columns need business-friendly renaming, or row-level filtering by user/group is required using SESSION_CONTEXT() or IS_ACCOUNT_GROUP_MEMBER(). Use Unity Catalog row filter functions (CREATE ROW ACCESS POLICY) and column masks when: fine-grained row/column security must be enforced transparently across multiple consumers without view proliferation, or the access rule is dynamic (membership in a table-driven allow-list). Never grant SELECT on silver or bronze tables to BI service principals. Audit grants regularly with SHOW GRANTS ON VIEW / TABLE.
summary: Decision: Table grants for full-access internal consumers; view grants for stable BI API with row filtering; row filter functions for transparent fine-grained access without view sprawl

label: Unity Catalog view DDL, GRANT patterns, and row-level security for BI access
summary: Output: Unity Catalog view DDL, GRANT statements for BI service principals and AD groups, SESSION_CONTEXT row filter view, row filter function, and column mask patterns

label: Run OPTIMIZE and ZORDER to compact small files and co-locate data by filter columns
content: OPTIMIZE rewrites small Parquet files in a Delta table into larger target-sized files (~1 GB) using bin-packing, reducing file-open overhead and improving data skipping. OPTIMIZE is non-blocking — concurrent reads and writes continue during the operation. ZORDER BY (col1, col2, ...) applies a Z-order (Morton-curve) algorithm to co-locate rows with similar values in the same Parquet files; Delta min/max statistics on ZORDER columns enable file-level skipping when those columns appear in WHERE predicates. ZORDER accepts up to ~4 columns — effectiveness diminishes beyond that. OPTIMIZE without ZORDER performs bin-packing only. OPTIMIZE with ZORDER rewrites all files in scope regardless of current file size. Scoping with a WHERE clause on partition columns (e.g., WHERE report_date >= ...) limits rewrites to recent partitions, reducing write amplification. DESCRIBE DETAIL returns numFiles and sizeInBytes to verify compaction results.
summary: System: Compact Delta small files via OPTIMIZE bin-packing; co-locate filter-column rows within files via ZORDER for improved min/max data skipping

label: Choose when to run OPTIMIZE and which columns to ZORDER by
content: Run OPTIMIZE after any write pattern that generates many small files: streaming micro-batch sinks, frequent MERGE operations, or multi-partition INSERT OVERWRITE jobs. Schedule OPTIMIZE as a separate Databricks Workflow job after each significant load batch or daily; do not run it inline with streaming queries. Scope to recently written partitions (WHERE report_date >= current_date() - INTERVAL 7 DAYS) to avoid full table rewrites. Choose ZORDER columns by: (1) columns appearing most frequently in WHERE or JOIN predicates, (2) high-cardinality, high-selectivity columns (customer_id, product_id, region), (3) columns that are NOT the partition key — partition pruning already handles those, and ZORDER on a partition column wastes I/O. Limit to 2–4 ZORDER columns — adding more increases rewrite cost without improving skipping proportionally. Use DESCRIBE DETAIL before and after to confirm numFiles decreased and avgFileSizeBytes increased. Do not run OPTIMIZE on DLT-managed tables — DLT manages compaction internally.
summary: Decision: Schedule OPTIMIZE after batch/streaming writes; ZORDER on 2–4 high-selectivity non-partition filter columns; scope to recent partitions to limit write amplification

label: OPTIMIZE and ZORDER SQL patterns with partition scope and file health checks
content:
-- Full table OPTIMIZE + ZORDER (small/medium tables)
OPTIMIZE delta.`/mnt/silver/orders`
ZORDER BY (customer_id, region, product_category);

-- Partition-scoped OPTIMIZE (preferred for large partitioned tables)
OPTIMIZE gold.orders_gold
WHERE report_date >= current_date() - INTERVAL 7 DAYS
ZORDER BY (region, product_category, channel);

-- Bin-packing only: compact files without Z-ordering
OPTIMIZE silver.events
WHERE event_date >= current_date() - INTERVAL 3 DAYS;

-- Check file health before and after
DESCRIBE DETAIL gold.orders_gold;
-- Key fields: numFiles, sizeInBytes (derive avg = sizeInBytes / numFiles)

-- Python: run OPTIMIZE from notebook or Workflow
spark.sql("""
  OPTIMIZE gold.sales_daily
  WHERE report_date >= current_date() - INTERVAL 7 DAYS
  ZORDER BY (region, product_category)
""")

-- Verify data skipping is active (examine query plan)
spark.sql("EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM gold.orders_gold WHERE region = 'APAC' AND product_category = 'electronics'")
-- Look for 'numFiles' reduction in physical plan metrics after ZORDER
summary: Output: OPTIMIZE bin-packing-only and ZORDER SQL patterns with partition scoping, DESCRIBE DETAIL file health checks, and Python invocation

label: Remove obsolete Delta files with VACUUM to reclaim storage
content: VACUUM permanently deletes Parquet data files that are no longer referenced by the Delta transaction log AND are older than the retention threshold. Default retention: 7 days (168 hours), configurable via the delta.deletedFileRetentionDuration table property (e.g., 'interval 30 days') or the VACUUM ... RETAIN N HOURS syntax. VACUUM does not delete transaction log files (_delta_log/). Files newer than the retention threshold are never deleted, even if unreferenced, protecting concurrent readers and in-flight time travel queries. After VACUUM, time travel queries targeting versions older than the retention window will fail because the underlying Parquet files no longer exist. VACUUM supports a DRY RUN mode that returns a list of files that would be deleted without actually removing them. Databricks Runtime enforces a minimum 7-day check by default; overriding requires setting spark.databricks.delta.retentionDurationCheck.enabled=false (dev/test only).
summary: System: VACUUM deletes unreferenced Delta Parquet files older than the retention threshold (default 7 days); reduces storage cost but limits time travel to the retained window

label: Configure VACUUM retention period and schedule to balance storage cost and time travel window
content: Never reduce retention below 7 days in production — Databricks enforces this by default to protect concurrent readers. Extend retention (e.g., 30 days) on tables where auditors or downstream consumers regularly query historical snapshots; set via ALTER TABLE SET TBLPROPERTIES. Schedule VACUUM weekly or monthly on most tables — daily VACUUM on append-only streaming tables provides little benefit since new files are continuously added and none will be older than 7 days. Always run VACUUM DRY RUN first to estimate reclaimed space and confirm the file list before committing. Do not run VACUUM on Delta Live Tables-managed tables — DLT manages its own file retention. Avoid running VACUUM while an active streaming query is reading the table; checkpoint offsets may reference files that VACUUM would delete in edge cases. For tables with extended retention (30+ days) verify storage cost vs. time travel business value — each day of additional retention retains all incremental change files.
summary: Decision: Keep 7-day default in production; extend for audited tables; schedule weekly; always DRY RUN first; avoid on DLT-managed and actively-streaming tables

label: VACUUM SQL patterns with DRY RUN, retention configuration, and table property management
summary: Output: VACUUM DRY RUN, explicit RETAIN hours, ALTER TABLE retention property, and safety override reference (dev/test only)

label: Query historical Delta snapshots with time travel and restore tables to prior versions
content: Delta Lake's transaction log enables time travel — reading table data as it existed at a prior commit version or timestamp. VERSION AS OF <n> returns the table snapshot at commit version n. TIMESTAMP AS OF '<ts>' returns the snapshot at the latest version on or before the given timestamp. Both are supported in SQL (SELECT ... FROM table VERSION AS OF n) and Python (spark.read.format('delta').option('versionAsOf', n) or .option('timestampAsOf', '...')). DESCRIBE HISTORY <table> returns the full audit log of all Delta operations: version number, timestamp, userName, operation (WRITE, MERGE, OPTIMIZE, VACUUM, DELETE, etc.), operationParameters, and operationMetrics. RESTORE TABLE <table> TO VERSION AS OF <n> (or TIMESTAMP AS OF) rewinds the table logical state to a prior version by adding a new transaction log entry that points to the prior snapshot's files — it does not immediately delete newer files. Restored data is fully queryable; the restore itself is recorded as a new history entry. Time travel is limited by the VACUUM retention window — querying versions older than the retained window will fail if the underlying files have been vacuumed.
summary: System: VERSION AS OF and TIMESTAMP AS OF query historical snapshots; DESCRIBE HISTORY shows full operation audit log; RESTORE TABLE rewinds table state with a new transaction log entry

label: Choose VERSION AS OF vs TIMESTAMP AS OF vs RESTORE TABLE for historical data access
content: Use VERSION AS OF when the exact commit version is known (retrieved from DESCRIBE HISTORY) — most precise and deterministic for auditing, compliance queries, and reproducible pipeline debugging. Use TIMESTAMP AS OF when the version is unknown but a business timestamp is known (e.g., 'as of midnight last Sunday') — Delta selects the latest version at or before the given timestamp; use ISO 8601 format for unambiguous parsing. Always run DESCRIBE HISTORY before any time travel query to identify the target version, confirm its operation type (avoid querying mid-OPTIMIZE versions), and verify the version is within the VACUUM retention window. Use RESTORE TABLE when: an accidental DELETE, TRUNCATE, bad MERGE, or schema change has corrupted production data and rolling back is faster than re-running the pipeline from source — RESTORE is an O(1) metadata operation. Do not use RESTORE as a substitute for proper SCD or merge logic — it discards all valid commits after the target version. After a RESTORE, trigger downstream silver/gold pipeline reruns to propagate the corrected state. Time travel queries are read-only — they do not modify table state.
summary: Decision: VERSION AS OF for exact audit queries; TIMESTAMP AS OF for business-time snapshots; RESTORE TABLE for emergency rollback after data corruption — always check DESCRIBE HISTORY first

label: Time travel SQL and Python patterns with DESCRIBE HISTORY, VERSION AS OF, TIMESTAMP AS OF, and RESTORE TABLE
content:
-- Full audit log for a table
DESCRIBE HISTORY silver.orders;
-- Columns: version, timestamp, userName, operation, operationParameters, operationMetrics

-- Limit to recent history
DESCRIBE HISTORY silver.orders LIMIT 10;

-- Query specific version
SELECT * FROM silver.orders VERSION AS OF 42;

-- Query at a business timestamp
SELECT * FROM silver.orders TIMESTAMP AS OF '2024-06-15 00:00:00';

-- Compare row counts to detect data loss
SELECT 'current' AS snapshot, COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM silver.orders
UNION ALL
SELECT 'v42',    COUNT(*) FROM silver.orders VERSION AS OF 42;

-- Python: read historical version
historical = (spark.read
  .format('delta')
  .option('versionAsOf', 42)
  .table('silver.orders'))

# Or by timestamp
historical = (spark.read
  .format('delta')
  .option('timestampAsOf', '2024-06-15T00:00:00.000Z')
  .table('silver.orders'))

-- Restore table to prior version (new commit added to log; O(1) metadata op)
RESTORE TABLE silver.orders TO VERSION AS OF 42;
-- Or by timestamp:
RESTORE TABLE silver.orders TO TIMESTAMP AS OF '2024-06-15 00:00:00';

-- Confirm restore succeeded (latest entry should show operation = 'RESTORE')
DESCRIBE HISTORY silver.orders LIMIT 3;
summary: Output: DESCRIBE HISTORY, VERSION AS OF, TIMESTAMP AS OF, and RESTORE TABLE SQL/Python patterns with pre-restore audit and post-restore verification

label: Automate file compaction with delta.autoOptimize.optimizeWrite and autoCompact table properties
content: Two Delta table properties automate file management without manual OPTIMIZE runs. delta.autoOptimize.optimizeWrite (Databricks: spark.databricks.delta.optimizeWrite.enabled) instructs the Delta writer to coalesce shuffle output partitions into optimally-sized (~128 MB) Parquet files before committing — reduces small file accumulation at the cost of 10–30% higher write latency due to additional coalescing. Effective on streaming writeStream sinks and MERGE operations that otherwise produce one small file per task or partition. delta.autoOptimize.autoCompact (spark.databricks.delta.autoCompact.enabled) triggers a background bin-packing OPTIMIZE after each table write, compacting newly written small files asynchronously; it does not apply ZORDER and is best-effort (may not run if the cluster is under load). Both properties are set via TBLPROPERTIES on the table or spark.conf.set at session level. Neither property is a substitute for periodic OPTIMIZE + ZORDER on serving tables — autoCompact only bin-packs without co-locating data for skipping.
summary: System: optimizeWrite coalesces writer output to ~128 MB files at commit time; autoCompact triggers background bin-packing after writes — neither applies ZORDER

label: Decide when to enable optimizeWrite and autoCompact on Delta tables
content: Enable optimizeWrite on: streaming Delta sinks (reduces micro-batch small file accumulation without manual OPTIMIZE scheduling), MERGE-heavy silver tables (MERGE generates one small file per matching partition per operation), and tables where OPTIMIZE scheduling is impractical. Disable optimizeWrite when write latency is critical — sub-second streaming micro-batches should not absorb the 10–30% overhead; leave compaction to a scheduled OPTIMIZE job instead. Enable autoCompact on: tables written by multiple short-batch jobs throughout the day where manual OPTIMIZE scheduling is difficult, and tables that do not require ZORDER (autoCompact never applies Z-ordering). Do not rely on autoCompact as a replacement for scheduled OPTIMIZE + ZORDER on gold serving tables — data skipping effectiveness depends on Z-ordering, which autoCompact does not provide. Use both properties together on MERGE-heavy silver tables for best results. On Databricks, session-level spark.conf settings override table-level TBLPROPERTIES for the duration of the session.
summary: Decision: Enable optimizeWrite for streaming/MERGE sinks to prevent small files; enable autoCompact for frequent-batch tables without ZORDER needs; disable when write latency is critical

label: autoOptimize.optimizeWrite and autoCompact DDL patterns at table creation and ALTER TABLE
content:
-- Set at table creation
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS silver.events (
  event_id   STRING    NOT NULL,
  event_type STRING,
  payload    STRING,
  event_ts   TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  event_date DATE      NOT NULL
)
USING DELTA
PARTITIONED BY (event_date)
TBLPROPERTIES (
  'delta.autoOptimize.optimizeWrite' = 'true',
  'delta.autoOptimize.autoCompact'   = 'true'
);

-- Enable on an existing table
ALTER TABLE silver.events
SET TBLPROPERTIES (
  'delta.autoOptimize.optimizeWrite' = 'true',
  'delta.autoOptimize.autoCompact'   = 'true'
);

-- Disable autoCompact when switching to scheduled OPTIMIZE + ZORDER
ALTER TABLE gold.orders_gold
SET TBLPROPERTIES ('delta.autoOptimize.autoCompact' = 'false');

-- Session-level override (applies to all tables written in this session)
spark.conf.set('spark.databricks.delta.optimizeWrite.enabled', 'true')
spark.conf.set('spark.databricks.delta.autoCompact.enabled',   'true')

-- Verify current properties
SHOW TBLPROPERTIES silver.events;

-- Baseline file count before enabling (run once to see current state)
DESCRIBE DETAIL silver.events;
-- Monitor numFiles over time: should stabilize rather than grow unbounded
summary: Output: optimizeWrite and autoCompact TBLPROPERTIES at CREATE TABLE and ALTER TABLE, session-level spark.conf overrides, and verification with SHOW TBLPROPERTIES / DESCRIBE DETAIL

label: Apply liquid clustering with CLUSTER BY as an incremental alternative to static PARTITIONED BY + ZORDER
content: Liquid clustering (Delta Lake 3.1+ / Databricks Runtime 13.3+) replaces static PARTITIONED BY combined with ZORDER BY with a single CLUSTER BY clause. On each OPTIMIZE run, only newly written or unclustered files are re-clustered — not all files — dramatically reducing write amplification compared to ZORDER, which rewrites all files in the OPTIMIZE scope. Liquid clustering uses a Hilbert space-filling curve to co-locate rows with similar clustering column values within Parquet files, enabling the same min/max file-skipping that ZORDER provides. Key advantages over ZORDER: (1) incremental — OPTIMIZE re-clusters only unclustered files; (2) mutable — clustering columns can be changed with ALTER TABLE CLUSTER BY without a full table rewrite; (3) deletion-vector compatible — works with row-level deletes without full Parquet file rewrites. Liquid clustering tables must not define PARTITIONED BY. Supported on all Delta write operations (append, MERGE, INSERT OVERWRITE). Recommended to also enable delta.enableDeletionVectors=true for maximum efficiency.
summary: System: CLUSTER BY (requires Delta Lake 3.1+, Databricks Runtime 13.3+) incrementally re-clusters only unclustered files using Hilbert-curve co-location; mutable without full rewrites; use ZORDER for DBR 9.0–13.2, PARTITIONED BY only when natural partition pruning applies

label: Decide when to migrate from ZORDER to liquid clustering
content: Migrate to liquid clustering when: (1) OPTIMIZE + ZORDER runtime exceeds acceptable maintenance windows because full file rewrites on a large table are too expensive; (2) query patterns change frequently, requiring clustering column adjustments without full table rewrites; (3) the table receives high-frequency incremental writes (streaming, continuous MERGE) where ZORDER write amplification is a bottleneck; (4) the table uses deletion vectors and full file rewrites on updates/deletes are undesirable. Stay with ZORDER when: the Databricks Runtime is below 13.3 or Delta below 3.1; the table is append-only with infrequent writes and OPTIMIZE + ZORDER completes within acceptable time; or team bandwidth for migration is unavailable. Migration path: CREATE TABLE new_table CLUSTER BY (...) AS SELECT * FROM old_table (CTAS), then atomically rename — liquid clustering cannot be added to an existing PARTITIONED BY table without recreation. New tables should default to liquid clustering on Databricks Runtime 13.3+ — it is the strategic direction for Delta table optimization.
summary: Decision: Migrate to liquid clustering when ZORDER write amplification is too high, query patterns change, or deletion vectors are required; new tables on DBR 13.3+ should default to CLUSTER BY

label: Liquid clustering DDL, ALTER CLUSTER BY, incremental OPTIMIZE, and CTAS migration patterns
content:
-- Create table with liquid clustering (no PARTITIONED BY)
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS gold.orders_clustered (
  order_id         STRING,
  customer_id      STRING,
  region           STRING,
  product_category STRING,
  channel          STRING,
  amount           DECIMAL(18,2),
  order_ts         TIMESTAMP,
  report_date      DATE
)
USING DELTA
CLUSTER BY (report_date, region, product_category)  -- up to 4 clustering columns
TBLPROPERTIES (
  'delta.enableDeletionVectors' = 'true'             -- recommended for liquid clustering
);

-- Incremental OPTIMIZE: re-clusters only unclustered files (no WHERE clause needed)
OPTIMIZE gold.orders_clustered;

-- Change clustering columns without full table rewrite
ALTER TABLE gold.orders_clustered
CLUSTER BY (report_date, channel);  -- replaces prior CLUSTER BY definition

-- After ALTER CLUSTER BY: run OPTIMIZE to apply new clustering to unclustered files
OPTIMIZE gold.orders_clustered;

-- Inspect current clustering columns and file stats
DESCRIBE DETAIL gold.orders_clustered;
-- clusteringColumns field shows active CLUSTER BY columns

-- Migrate from partitioned + ZORDER to liquid clustering (CTAS + rename)
CREATE TABLE gold.orders_clustered_new
CLUSTER BY (report_date, region, product_category)
AS SELECT * FROM gold.orders_gold;   -- CTAS from old partitioned table

-- Atomic rename swap
ALTER TABLE gold.orders_gold RENAME TO gold.orders_gold_old;
ALTER TABLE gold.orders_clustered_new RENAME TO gold.orders_gold;

-- Python: verify clustering columns via DeltaTable API
from delta.tables import DeltaTable
detail = DeltaTable.forName(spark, 'gold.orders_clustered').detail()
display(detail.select('clusteringColumns', 'numFiles', 'sizeInBytes'))
summary: Output: Liquid clustering CREATE TABLE with CLUSTER BY, incremental OPTIMIZE, ALTER CLUSTER BY column change, and CTAS migration from a partitioned ZORDER table

label: Enforce data integrity with Delta CHECK constraints, NOT NULL, and informational PK/FK
content: Delta Lake supports three classes of table constraints. NOT NULL constraints are declared in CREATE TABLE column DDL (col TYPE NOT NULL) and reject any write that inserts a null in the constrained column — enforced by the Delta writer at commit time with no runtime configuration required. CHECK constraints (ALTER TABLE ADD CONSTRAINT name CHECK (boolean_expr)) are boolean predicate constraints stored in table metadata; any write producing a row that violates the predicate raises a DeltaInvariantViolationException and fails the entire write atomically. Both NOT NULL and CHECK constraints are enforced on all write operations (INSERT, UPDATE, MERGE, COPY INTO). Existing data is NOT validated when a new constraint is added — only subsequent writes are checked; pre-validate with SELECT COUNT(*) WHERE NOT (expr) before adding. Informational PRIMARY KEY and FOREIGN KEY constraints (declared in CREATE TABLE or via ALTER TABLE ADD CONSTRAINT) are stored in Unity Catalog metadata and surfaced to BI tools and the data catalog for lineage and schema documentation but are NOT enforced at write time — violations do not fail writes.
summary: System: NOT NULL and CHECK constraints are enforced write-time by Delta; informational PRIMARY KEY / FOREIGN KEY are stored in Unity Catalog metadata only and never enforced

label: Choose NOT NULL vs CHECK constraint vs informational PK/FK for data integrity requirements
content: Use NOT NULL for columns whose absence would break downstream joins, aggregations, or partition operations: primary/business keys, surrogate keys, required foreign keys, and partition columns. Use CHECK constraints for domain validation: value ranges (amount > 0), enumerated values (status IN ('active','inactive','pending')), string format patterns (regexp(email, '^[^@]+@[^@]+')), and cross-column consistency rules (end_date >= start_date). Always pre-validate with SELECT COUNT(*) WHERE NOT (expr) before adding a CHECK constraint — adding a constraint does not scan or reject existing violating rows. Avoid CHECK constraints on high-throughput streaming Delta sinks — constraint evaluation adds per-row overhead on every write micro-batch; validate at the silver cleansing layer before writing instead, and apply constraints only on lower-write-volume gold tables. Use informational PRIMARY KEY / FOREIGN KEY for Unity Catalog lineage tracking and BI tool auto-join suggestions (Power BI and Tableau read PK/FK metadata); do not rely on them for data quality enforcement. Drop constraints with ALTER TABLE DROP CONSTRAINT when schema or business rules change.
summary: Decision: NOT NULL for required keys and partition columns; CHECK for domain/range/format validation on moderate-throughput tables; informational PK/FK for Unity Catalog lineage only — never for enforcement

label: Delta constraint DDL patterns: NOT NULL, CHECK, informational PK/FK, and violation handling
summary: Output: NOT NULL at CREATE TABLE, pre-validation query, ADD/DROP CHECK CONSTRAINT, informational PK/FK DDL, SHOW TBLPROPERTIES verification, and Python violation exception handling

label: Execute MERGE INTO upsert pattern on Delta tables
content: Delta Lake's MERGE INTO command atomically upserts rows by joining a source dataset against a target Delta table on a business key. A standard upsert uses two clauses: WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET * (or explicit column assignments) to overwrite an existing row when the join key matches, and WHEN NOT MATCHED [BY TARGET] THEN INSERT * to add a new row when no match exists in the target. The merge executes as a single atomic transaction — no intermediate state is visible. Multiple WHEN MATCHED clauses are supported with additional AND conditions to apply different update logic per row type (e.g., update only if a version column is newer). For SCD Type 2 dimensional updates, use MERGE to atomically close the old dimension row (set end_date to effective_date - 1, is_current = FALSE) while inserting a new row with an incremented version number, effective_date, and is_current = TRUE — this ensures dimension history remains consistent across reclassifications. Merge reads the full target table by default unless the merge condition references a partition column, enabling partition pruning.
summary: System: Execute MERGE INTO upsert — WHEN MATCHED UPDATE + WHEN NOT MATCHED INSERT as an atomic transaction on a Delta target table

label: Choose MERGE vs INSERT OVERWRITE for upsert workloads
content: Use MERGE INTO when: the target table retains history and only a subset of rows change per run (CDC updates, SCD Type 1 overwrites, correction propagation); the source may contain both new and updated records intermixed; or row-level update semantics are required without full partition replacement. Use INSERT OVERWRITE PARTITION when: every row in the partition is replaced on each run (daily aggregate refresh, full partition reload from silver), the source always contains the complete partition dataset, and there are no partial updates — INSERT OVERWRITE is significantly cheaper than MERGE on large partitions because it avoids the join. Never use MERGE as a substitute for INSERT OVERWRITE on full-refresh aggregation tables; the join overhead is unnecessary when all rows are replaced. For streaming foreachBatch upserts, MERGE is the standard pattern because streaming sources deliver incremental deltas, not full partition replacements.
summary: Decision: MERGE for partial row-level updates and CDC deltas; INSERT OVERWRITE for full partition replacement — do not use MERGE when all rows in scope are replaced

label: MERGE INTO upsert code patterns: standard upsert and streaming foreachBatch
content:
from delta.tables import DeltaTable
from pyspark.sql.functions import current_timestamp

# --- Standard batch MERGE upsert ---
target = DeltaTable.forName(spark, 'silver.orders')

(target.alias('t')
  .merge(
      source_df.alias('s'),
      's.order_id = t.order_id'            # merge condition on business key
  )
  .whenMatchedUpdateAll()                  # update all columns when key matches
  .whenNotMatchedInsertAll()               # insert full row when no target match
  .execute())

# --- Selective column update (only update if source is newer) ---
(target.alias('t')
  .merge(
      source_df.alias('s'),
      's.order_id = t.order_id'
  )
  .whenMatchedUpdate(
      condition='s.updated_at > t.updated_at',    # only overwrite stale rows
      set={
          'status':     's.status',
          'amount':     's.amount',
          'updated_at': 's.updated_at'
      }
  )
  .whenNotMatchedInsertAll()
  .execute())

# --- Streaming foreachBatch upsert (idempotent; safe to replay) ---
def upsert_to_silver(batch_df, batch_id):
    batch_df.createOrReplaceTempView('updates')
    spark.sql("""
      MERGE INTO silver.orders AS t
      USING updates AS s
      ON t.order_id = s.order_id
      WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET *
      WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT *
    """)

(bronze_stream
  .writeStream
  .foreachBatch(upsert_to_silver)
  .option('checkpointLocation', '/mnt/checkpoints/silver/orders_merge')
  .trigger(processingTime='5 minutes')
  .start())

# SQL equivalent (batch):
# MERGE INTO silver.orders AS t
# USING (SELECT * FROM stage.orders_incremental) AS s
# ON t.order_id = s.order_id
# WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET *
# WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT *;
summary: Output: MERGE INTO upsert — batch DeltaTable API, selective column update with condition, and streaming foreachBatch upsert patterns

label: Apply WHEN MATCHED DELETE in MERGE for CDC delete propagation
content: Delta MERGE supports a WHEN MATCHED THEN DELETE clause that physically deletes the matched target row during the merge operation. This is the standard pattern for propagating hard deletes or soft-delete flags received from a CDC source (Debezium, DMS, Change Data Feed) into a Delta silver or gold table. The CDC source typically marks delete events with an op='D' or deleted=true column. The MERGE then uses a three-clause structure: (1) WHEN MATCHED AND s.op = 'D' THEN DELETE to remove rows flagged as deleted; (2) WHEN MATCHED AND s.op != 'D' THEN UPDATE SET * to update changed rows; (3) WHEN NOT MATCHED AND s.op != 'D' THEN INSERT * to insert new rows. The order of WHEN MATCHED clauses matters — Delta evaluates them top-to-bottom and stops at the first match per row. Deletion via MERGE uses deletion vectors (DBR 12.2+) when enabled, avoiding full Parquet file rewrites for each deleted row.
summary: System: WHEN MATCHED DELETE in MERGE — three-clause CDC merge pattern to propagate hard deletes and upserts from a CDC source into a Delta target

label: Decide when to add WHEN MATCHED DELETE vs pre-filter deletes from CDC MERGE source
content: Add WHEN MATCHED DELETE to the merge when: the CDC source delivers inserts, updates, and deletes in the same batch (standard Debezium/DMS full-row CDC payloads with op column); hard deletes must be physically removed from the target table (compliance, storage, downstream join correctness); or a soft-delete flag in the source (deleted=true, is_active=false) must be propagated as a physical row removal. Pre-filter deletes from the source before merging when: the delete volume is very high (>20% of the batch) and it is acceptable to handle them as a separate DELETE FROM operation — this allows the MERGE to run only on insert/update rows, reducing merge complexity. Never use WHEN MATCHED DELETE when the target implements SCD Type 2 — deletes should expire the active row (UPDATE expiry_date, is_current=false) rather than remove it. Verify deletion vectors are enabled (delta.enableDeletionVectors=true) on tables receiving MERGE deletes to avoid full file rewrites per deleted row.
summary: Decision: use WHEN MATCHED DELETE for mixed CDC batches; pre-filter for high-delete-volume batches; never hard-delete on SCD Type 2 history tables

label: MERGE with WHEN MATCHED DELETE code patterns: CDC three-clause merge and deletion vector setup
content:
from delta.tables import DeltaTable

# Enable deletion vectors on the target table (DBR 12.2+; avoids full file rewrites)
# ALTER TABLE silver.customers SET TBLPROPERTIES ('delta.enableDeletionVectors' = 'true');

target = DeltaTable.forName(spark, 'silver.customers')

# --- Three-clause CDC MERGE: delete, update, insert ---
# source_df contains CDC rows with op column: 'D'=delete, 'U'=update, 'I'=insert
(target.alias('t')
  .merge(
      source_df.alias('s'),
      's.customer_id = t.customer_id'        # business key join
  )
  .whenMatchedDelete(condition='s.op = \'D\'')           # 1. hard delete
  .whenMatchedUpdateAll(condition='s.op != \'D\'')       # 2. upsert changed rows
  .whenNotMatchedInsertAll(condition='s.op != \'D\'')
  .execute())                                              # 3. insert new rows

# SQL equivalent:
# MERGE INTO silver.customers AS t
# USING cdc_batch AS s
# ON t.customer_id = s.customer_id
# WHEN MATCHED AND s.op = 'D'  THEN DELETE
# WHEN MATCHED AND s.op <> 'D' THEN UPDATE SET *
# WHEN NOT MATCHED AND s.op <> 'D' THEN INSERT *;

# --- Soft-delete propagation (is_deleted flag → physical delete) ---
(target.alias('t')
  .merge(
      source_df.alias('s'),
      's.customer_id = t.customer_id'
  )
  .whenMatchedDelete(condition='s.is_deleted = true')
  .whenMatchedUpdateAll(condition='s.is_deleted = false')
  .whenNotMatchedInsertAll(condition='s.is_deleted = false')
  .execute())

# --- Pre-filter pattern: handle deletes separately for high-delete-volume batches ---
deletes_df  = source_df.filter('op = \'D\'')
upserts_df  = source_df.filter('op <> \'D\'')

# Step 1: apply deletes as a standalone DELETE
deletes_df.createOrReplaceTempView('delete_keys')
spark.sql('DELETE FROM silver.customers WHERE customer_id IN (SELECT customer_id FROM delete_keys)')

# Step 2: standard upsert MERGE on remaining rows
(target.alias('t')
  .merge(upserts_df.alias('s'), 's.customer_id = t.customer_id')
  .whenMatchedUpdateAll()
  .whenNotMatchedInsertAll()
  .execute())
summary: Output: three-clause CDC MERGE with WHEN MATCHED DELETE, soft-delete flag propagation, and pre-filter delete pattern for high-delete-volume batches

label: Optimize MERGE performance with partition pruning and selective merge conditions
content: MERGE performance on large Delta tables is dominated by two costs: (1) target table scan — Delta must identify which files may contain matching rows; (2) file rewrite cost — files containing matches must be rewritten. Partition pruning eliminates entire partitions from the scan when the merge condition includes the partition column (e.g., AND t.report_date = s.report_date). Without partition pruning, MERGE scans the entire table. High-selectivity merge conditions (joining on a high-cardinality indexed column like order_id) allow Delta's Z-order or liquid clustering statistics to skip non-matching files via min/max data skipping. In streaming foreachBatch merges, repartition the batch by the merge key before calling MERGE to co-locate source rows and reduce the number of target files touched per executor. MERGE on a table with deletion vectors enabled avoids rewriting full Parquet files for matched rows — only a deletion vector file is written, dramatically reducing write amplification for low-match-rate merges.
summary: System: MERGE performance — partition pruning via partition column in join condition, Z-order/liquid clustering for file skipping, foreachBatch repartition, and deletion vector write reduction

label: Structure merge conditions to enable partition pruning and minimize file rewrites
content: Include the partition column in the merge condition when the source batch corresponds to a specific partition window (e.g., AND t.report_date = s.report_date or AND t.report_date >= s.min_date AND t.report_date <= s.max_date) — this is the single highest-impact optimization for partitioned Delta tables; without it, every partition is scanned. When the source batch spans multiple partitions, derive the partition range from the source data and pass it as a literal filter: .merge(source.alias('s'), 'AND t.report_date >= \'{}\' AND t.report_date <= \'{}\'.format(min_date, max_date)). Place the high-cardinality business key join condition before secondary filter conditions in the ON clause to maximize file skipping via Delta statistics. Repartition the source DataFrame by the merge key before executing MERGE in foreachBatch — this groups source rows by key, reducing cross-shuffle data movement during the join. Monitor merge metrics via operationMetrics in DESCRIBE HISTORY: numTargetFilesRemoved / numTargetFilesAdded reveal write amplification; numTargetRowsUpdated / numSourceRows reveals match rate and whether partition pruning is effective.
summary: Decision: always include partition column in merge ON clause for partitioned tables; repartition source by merge key in streaming; monitor operationMetrics to diagnose scan and write amplification

label: Performant MERGE code patterns with partition pruning, repartition, and metrics inspection
content:
from delta.tables import DeltaTable
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, min as spark_min, max as spark_max, lit

target = DeltaTable.forName(spark, 'silver.orders')

# --- Pattern 1: Partition-scoped MERGE (partitioned by order_date) ---
# Derive date range from source batch to scope partition pruning
date_range = source_df.select(
    spark_min('order_date').alias('min_d'),
    spark_max('order_date').alias('max_d')
).first()

(target.alias('t')
  .merge(
      source_df.alias('s'),
      # partition column in merge condition → partition pruning
      's.order_id = t.order_id '
      'AND t.order_date >= \'{min_d}\' '
      'AND t.order_date <= \'{max_d}\''.format(
          min_d=date_range.min_d, max_d=date_range.max_d)
  )
  .whenMatchedUpdateAll()
  .whenNotMatchedInsertAll()
  .execute())

# --- Pattern 2: foreachBatch with repartition by merge key ---
def merge_to_silver(batch_df, batch_id):
    # Repartition by merge key before MERGE to co-locate source rows
    batch_repartitioned = batch_df.repartition(col('order_id'))

    date_range = batch_df.select(
        spark_min('order_date').alias('min_d'),
        spark_max('order_date').alias('max_d')
    ).first()

    (DeltaTable.forName(spark, 'silver.orders')
      .alias('t')
      .merge(
          batch_repartitioned.alias('s'),
          's.order_id = t.order_id '
          'AND t.order_date >= \'{min_d}\' '
          'AND t.order_date <= \'{max_d}\''.format(
              min_d=date_range.min_d, max_d=date_range.max_d)
      )
      .whenMatchedUpdateAll()
      .whenNotMatchedInsertAll()
      .execute())

# --- Inspect MERGE metrics to diagnose performance ---
import json
history = spark.sql('DESCRIBE HISTORY silver.orders LIMIT 5')
for row in history.filter('operation = \'MERGE\'').collect():
    metrics = row['operationMetrics']
    print(f"version={row['version']} "
          f"filesScanned={metrics.get('numTargetFilesScanned','?')} "
          f"filesRemoved={metrics.get('numTargetFilesRemoved','?')} "
          f"rowsUpdated={metrics.get('numTargetRowsUpdated','?')} "
          f"rowsInserted={metrics.get('numTargetRowsInserted','?')}")
# High numTargetFilesScanned → partition pruning not active
# High numTargetFilesRemoved relative to rowsUpdated → write amplification; consider deletion vectors
summary: Output: partition-scoped MERGE with date-range pruning, foreachBatch repartition-before-merge, and operationMetrics inspection for diagnosing scan and write amplification

label: Execute DELETE and UPDATE DML operations on Delta tables
content: Delta Lake supports SQL-standard DELETE FROM and UPDATE SET DML operations on Delta tables. DELETE FROM table WHERE condition removes all rows matching the predicate; without a WHERE clause, all rows are deleted (truncate semantics). UPDATE table SET col=expr [, col=expr] WHERE condition updates column values on matching rows. Both operations are atomic — readers see either the pre-op or post-op snapshot, never a partial state. On tables with deletion vectors enabled (delta.enableDeletionVectors=true, DBR 12.2+), DELETE and UPDATE write a deletion vector sidecar file marking affected rows as deleted without rewriting the Parquet data file — dramatically reducing write amplification for low-selectivity updates (few rows affected per file). Without deletion vectors, the entire Parquet file containing any matching row is rewritten. Partition pruning applies to both DELETE and UPDATE when the WHERE clause includes the partition column.
summary: System: DELETE FROM and UPDATE SET DML on Delta — atomic row removal and column update; deletion vectors avoid full file rewrites on DBR 12.2+; partition pruning applies when partition column is in WHERE

label: Choose DELETE, UPDATE, or MERGE for row-level modifications
content: Use DELETE FROM when: removing rows that meet a condition without replacement (purging expired records, GDPR right-to-erasure, removing test data, cascade deletes from a CDC stream with high delete volume). Always scope to the narrowest possible WHERE clause including the partition column to avoid full table rewrites. Use UPDATE SET when: changing specific column values on existing rows without touching other columns (status transitions, applying a correction to a pricing column, backfilling a new column for existing rows). UPDATE is cleaner than a MERGE for single-table, single-operation row modifications with no insert branch. Use MERGE when: the operation must handle both inserts and updates/deletes in a single pass from a source dataset (CDC upserts, SCD merges, batch deduplication with corrections). Do not use UPDATE or DELETE inside a streaming foreachBatch if they must run per-micro-batch at high frequency without deletion vectors — the file rewrite overhead compounds; use MERGE with deletion vectors instead. Enable deletion vectors (ALTER TABLE SET TBLPROPERTIES ('delta.enableDeletionVectors' = 'true')) on any table receiving frequent DELETE, UPDATE, or MERGE operations.
summary: Decision: DELETE for row removal without replacement; UPDATE for in-place column corrections; MERGE for combined insert+update+delete from a source; always enable deletion vectors for tables with frequent DML

label: DELETE and UPDATE DML code patterns with partition scoping and deletion vector setup
content:
-- Enable deletion vectors (run once per table; DBR 12.2+)
ALTER TABLE silver.orders
SET TBLPROPERTIES ('delta.enableDeletionVectors' = 'true');

-- DELETE: remove rows by condition (partition-scoped for pruning)
DELETE FROM silver.orders
WHERE order_date < '2023-01-01'          -- partition column scopes to relevant files
  AND status = 'cancelled';

-- DELETE: GDPR erasure by customer ID list
DELETE FROM silver.orders
WHERE customer_id IN (
  SELECT customer_id FROM gdpr_deletion_requests WHERE processed = false
);

-- UPDATE: status transition
UPDATE silver.orders
SET status = 'shipped',
    shipped_at = current_timestamp()
WHERE order_id IN ('ORD-001', 'ORD-002', 'ORD-003')
  AND status = 'pending';

-- UPDATE: backfill new column for existing rows
UPDATE silver.orders
SET region = 'APAC'
WHERE country_code IN ('AU','NZ','SG','JP')
  AND region IS NULL;

-- UPDATE: correct pricing error in a date range
UPDATE silver.orders
SET amount = amount * 0.90,            -- apply 10% correction
    correction_applied = true
WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2024-03-01' AND '2024-03-07'
  AND promo_code = 'SPRING10'
  AND correction_applied IS NULL;

# Python API equivalents:
from delta.tables import DeltaTable

target = DeltaTable.forName(spark, 'silver.orders')

# DELETE via Python
target.delete(condition="order_date < '2023-01-01' AND status = 'cancelled'")

# UPDATE via Python
target.update(
    condition="promo_code = 'SPRING10' AND order_date BETWEEN '2024-03-01' AND '2024-03-07'",
    set={"amount": "amount * 0.90", "correction_applied": "true"}
)

# Verify via DESCRIBE HISTORY
# DESCRIBE HISTORY silver.orders LIMIT 5;
# Check operationMetrics: numDeletedRows, numUpdatedRows, numRemovedFiles vs numAddedFiles
# Low numRemovedFiles with deletion vectors = efficient row-level ops
summary: Output: DELETE and UPDATE DML patterns for partition-scoped removal, GDPR erasure, status transitions, backfills, and corrections — SQL and Python DeltaTable API

label: Apply Delta schema evolution with mergeSchema, overwriteSchema, and ALTER TABLE
content: Delta Lake provides three mechanisms for evolving a table's schema after creation. mergeSchema (write option or spark.databricks.delta.schema.autoMerge.enabled session config) adds new columns present in the source DataFrame to the target table schema at write time; existing columns and their types are unchanged; removed columns in the source produce nulls in the target. overwriteSchema (write option, only with mode('overwrite') or INSERT OVERWRITE) replaces the entire table schema with the source schema — use with extreme caution as it can silently drop columns and change types. ALTER TABLE DDL provides surgical control: ADD COLUMN adds a new nullable column (existing rows read as null), RENAME COLUMN renames without rewriting data (requires delta.columnMapping.mode=name), ALTER COLUMN COMMENT updates metadata, ALTER COLUMN TYPE supports widening type promotions (INT→LONG, FLOAT→DOUBLE) but not narrowing. Column mapping (TBLPROPERTIES 'delta.columnMapping.mode'='name') must be enabled before RENAME COLUMN or DROP COLUMN is permitted.
summary: System: Delta schema evolution — mergeSchema for additive writes, overwriteSchema for full replacement, ALTER TABLE ADD/RENAME/TYPE for surgical changes; column mapping required for rename/drop

label: Choose mergeSchema vs overwriteSchema vs ALTER TABLE for schema changes
content: Use mergeSchema=true (write option) when: the source adds new optional columns that should be appended to the target schema transparently, the change is additive only, and downstream consumers can tolerate new nullable columns (typical bronze→silver pipeline). Enable spark.databricks.delta.schema.autoMerge.enabled=true at session level for streaming sinks where setting per-write options is impractical. Use overwriteSchema=true only when: the target table must be completely replaced with a new schema (column renaming without column mapping, type narrowing, column removal) and the operation is a full table overwrite — this is a destructive, non-additive change; all prior data is replaced. Never use overwriteSchema on a table with active downstream consumers; coordinate the change as a breaking schema migration. Use ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN for adding columns to a live production table without rewriting data — zero-cost, backward compatible, immediately visible. Use ALTER TABLE RENAME COLUMN (requires column mapping enabled) for renaming columns without rewriting Parquet files. Use ALTER TABLE ALTER COLUMN for widening type promotions only — narrowing casts require a full table rewrite. Pre-test all schema changes against downstream consumers before applying in production.
summary: Decision: mergeSchema for additive pipeline writes; overwriteSchema only for destructive full-replace migrations; ALTER TABLE for surgical live-table column additions, renames, and type widenings

label: Delta schema evolution code patterns: mergeSchema, overwriteSchema, ALTER TABLE ADD/RENAME/TYPE
content:
# --- mergeSchema: additive column addition at write time ---
(source_df
  .write
  .format('delta')
  .mode('append')
  .option('mergeSchema', 'true')         # adds new columns; does not remove old ones
  .saveAsTable('silver.orders'))

# Session-level autoMerge (useful for streaming sinks)
spark.conf.set('spark.databricks.delta.schema.autoMerge.enabled', 'true')

(source_stream
  .writeStream
  .format('delta')
  .option('checkpointLocation', '/mnt/checkpoints/silver/orders')
  .toTable('silver.orders'))             # autoMerge applies without per-write option

# Verify schema after merge
print(spark.table('silver.orders').schema.simpleString())

# --- overwriteSchema: full schema replacement (destructive) ---
(new_df
  .write
  .format('delta')
  .mode('overwrite')
  .option('overwriteSchema', 'true')     # replaces schema + all data
  .saveAsTable('silver.orders'))
# WARNING: all prior rows are deleted; all prior columns may be lost

# --- ALTER TABLE: surgical live-table schema changes ---

# Add a new nullable column (zero-cost; no data rewrite)
-- ALTER TABLE silver.orders ADD COLUMN discount_pct DOUBLE;
-- ALTER TABLE silver.orders ADD COLUMNS (promo_code STRING COMMENT 'Applied promotion code', referral_id STRING);

# Rename a column (requires column mapping; no data rewrite)
-- ALTER TABLE silver.orders SET TBLPROPERTIES ('delta.columnMapping.mode' = 'name', 'delta.minReaderVersion' = '2', 'delta.minWriterVersion' = '5');
-- ALTER TABLE silver.orders RENAME COLUMN order_amt TO amount;

# Drop a column (requires column mapping)
-- ALTER TABLE silver.orders DROP COLUMN legacy_field;

# Widen a type (INT → LONG; no data rewrite in Delta)
-- ALTER TABLE silver.orders ALTER COLUMN quantity TYPE BIGINT;

# Update column comment
-- ALTER TABLE silver.orders ALTER COLUMN amount COMMENT 'Order total in USD';

# Verify schema changes applied
-- DESCRIBE TABLE EXTENDED silver.orders;
-- DESCRIBE HISTORY silver.orders LIMIT 3;  -- operation = 'CHANGE COLUMN' or 'REPLACE COLUMNS'
summary: Output: mergeSchema append, session autoMerge for streaming, overwriteSchema full replacement, and ALTER TABLE ADD/RENAME/DROP/TYPE patterns for live Delta table schema management

label: Create SHALLOW CLONE and DEEP CLONE copies of Delta tables
content: Delta Lake provides two clone operations. SHALLOW CLONE copies only the Delta transaction log to a new location; the Parquet data files remain at the source location and are referenced by the clone's log — the clone is a lightweight metadata copy with no data duplication. Reads against the shallow clone access source data files directly. DEEP CLONE copies both the transaction log and all Parquet data files to the target location, producing a fully independent replica of the table. Both clone types preserve the source table's schema, partitioning, table properties (TBLPROPERTIES), and constraints. Clones support all standard Delta DML (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE) — writes to a shallow clone write new Parquet files at the clone's location; source files are never modified through the clone. Incremental clone (CREATE OR REPLACE) synchronizes a prior clone with new source commits, copying only the delta since the last clone operation.
summary: System: SHALLOW CLONE copies the transaction log only (references source Parquet files); DEEP CLONE copies log + all data files; both preserve schema, partitioning, and properties; incremental sync via CREATE OR REPLACE

label: Choose SHALLOW CLONE vs DEEP CLONE based on independence requirement and use case
content: Use SHALLOW CLONE when: creating a development or staging environment that needs to read production data without duplicating storage (dev/test exploration of a large table, schema migration dry runs, testing pipeline logic against production-sized data); the source table will not be vacuumed during the clone's lifetime — VACUUM on the source can delete data files still referenced by shallow clones, breaking reads; or a disposable temporary copy is needed for a short-lived operation. Use DEEP CLONE when: a fully independent copy is required that must survive source table deletion, vacuum, or migration (disaster recovery replica, cross-region data copy, archiving a point-in-time snapshot for regulatory compliance); the target environment has no network access to the source storage; or the clone must be writable and independently optimizable without any dependency on source files. Use incremental DEEP CLONE (CREATE OR REPLACE) to maintain a near-real-time replica of a large table without re-copying all files on each sync — only files added since the last clone are copied. Never use SHALLOW CLONE as a backup strategy — it provides no protection against source table corruption or accidental VACUUM.
summary: Decision: SHALLOW CLONE for dev/test exploration against production-sized data (no data copy, but VACUUM-dependent); DEEP CLONE for independent replicas, disaster recovery, archives, and cross-region copies

label: CLONE operation SQL and Python patterns: shallow, deep, incremental sync, and version-targeted clone
content:
-- SHALLOW CLONE: metadata copy only (no data files copied)
-- Use for dev/test; source VACUUM will break reads if shallow clone holds old file references
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS dev.orders_shallow
SHALLOW CLONE main.silver.orders
LOCATION 'abfss://dev@<account>.dfs.core.windows.net/clones/orders_shallow';

-- DEEP CLONE: full independent copy (log + all Parquet data files)
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS dr.orders_deep
DEEP CLONE main.silver.orders
LOCATION 'abfss://dr@<account>.dfs.core.windows.net/replicas/orders_deep';

-- Version-targeted clone: clone the table as of a specific version (time travel)
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS archive.orders_v100
DEEP CLONE main.silver.orders VERSION AS OF 100
LOCATION 'abfss://archive@<account>.dfs.core.windows.net/snapshots/orders_v100';

-- Timestamp-targeted clone
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS archive.orders_eoy_2024
DEEP CLONE main.silver.orders TIMESTAMP AS OF '2024-12-31 23:59:59'
LOCATION 'abfss://archive@<account>.dfs.core.windows.net/snapshots/orders_eoy2024';

-- Incremental DEEP CLONE sync (only copies files added since last clone)
-- Run on a schedule to maintain a near-real-time DR replica
CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE dr.orders_deep
DEEP CLONE main.silver.orders
LOCATION 'abfss://dr@<account>.dfs.core.windows.net/replicas/orders_deep';
-- operationMetrics will show: copiedFiles, copiedBytes (only delta since last clone)

-- Verify clone independence
DESCRIBE HISTORY dr.orders_deep LIMIT 3;
-- operation = 'CLONE', operationParameters includes sourceVersion

-- Python: SHALLOW CLONE for schema migration dry-run
spark.sql("""
  CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE dev.orders_migration_test
  SHALLOW CLONE main.silver.orders
  LOCATION 'abfss://dev@<account>.dfs.core.windows.net/clones/orders_migration_test'
""")

# Test schema migration on shallow clone — safe, no production impact
spark.sql("ALTER TABLE dev.orders_migration_test ADD COLUMN new_field STRING")
spark.sql("UPDATE dev.orders_migration_test SET new_field = 'default' WHERE new_field IS NULL")
# If successful, apply same DDL to main.silver.orders

-- Storage cost check: shallow clone has near-zero added bytes
DESCRIBE DETAIL dev.orders_shallow;
-- sizeInBytes reflects source files; no new storage consumed at clone location
summary: Output: SHALLOW CLONE for dev/test, DEEP CLONE for DR/archive, version/timestamp-targeted clones for point-in-time snapshots, incremental CREATE OR REPLACE sync, and schema migration dry-run pattern

label: Understand Unity Catalog three-level namespace: catalog.schema.table
content: Unity Catalog introduces a mandatory three-level namespace for all data assets: catalog.schema.table (also applies to views, volumes, and functions). Catalog is the top-level container representing an environment or domain boundary (e.g., prod, dev, finance). Schema is the second-level logical grouping of related objects within a catalog — equivalent to a Hive database. Table, view, volume, or function is the third level. All SQL queries must use fully-qualified three-part names (SELECT * FROM main.silver.orders) unless the session default catalog and schema are set with USE CATALOG and USE SCHEMA, which then allows two-part references. Unity Catalog prevents hive_metastore objects from being referenced alongside Unity Catalog objects in the same query. The workspace default catalog is set by the metastore admin; legacy workspaces may default to hive_metastore until explicitly migrated.
summary: System: Unity Catalog three-level catalog.schema.table namespace — structure, session USE defaults, and hive_metastore coexistence constraints

label: Managed vs external tables and volumes: storage ownership in Unity Catalog
content: Unity Catalog distinguishes two storage ownership models for both tables and volumes. Managed objects store data in the Unity Catalog managed storage location (set at metastore, catalog, or schema level via MANAGED LOCATION). When a managed table or volume is dropped, Unity Catalog deletes both the metadata AND the underlying data files — Unity Catalog owns the full lifecycle. External objects reference data at an explicit cloud storage path (LOCATION clause in CREATE TABLE/VOLUME) outside the managed location. Dropping an external table or volume removes only the metadata; data files at the LOCATION path are preserved. External tables and volumes require a registered external location in Unity Catalog covering the target path and an associated storage credential. Managed tables require a managed storage credential assigned to the catalog or schema. Both types are fully governed: access control, audit logging, and lineage apply equally regardless of object type.
summary: System: Managed vs external tables/volumes — lifecycle ownership, DROP behavior, storage credential requirements, and governance equivalence

label: Unity Catalog privilege hierarchy: catalog, schema, and object-level ACLs
content: Unity Catalog implements a cascading privilege model across four levels: metastore, catalog, schema, and individual object (table, view, volume, function). A user querying a table must have all three: USE CATALOG on the catalog + USE SCHEMA on the schema + SELECT on the table (or an ancestor grant that cascades down). Key privileges by object type — tables/views: SELECT (read), MODIFY (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/MERGE), CREATE TABLE, CREATE VIEW; schemas: USE SCHEMA, CREATE TABLE, CREATE SCHEMA; catalogs: USE CATALOG, CREATE SCHEMA, CREATE CATALOG; volumes: READ VOLUME (read files), WRITE VOLUME (create/replace/delete files); functions: EXECUTE. Privileges are granted to users, service principals, or groups (Azure AD/Okta-synced or Databricks-native). REVOKE removes a specific privilege; DENY is not supported in Unity Catalog — access is removed only via REVOKE. Granting at a higher level (e.g., SELECT on schema) cascades to all current and future objects in that container.
summary: System: Unity Catalog privilege cascade — USE CATALOG/SCHEMA/SELECT required stack, key privileges per object type, GRANT/REVOKE mechanics, no DENY support

label: Unity Catalog row filters and column masks: fine-grained security mechanics
content: Unity Catalog provides two transparent fine-grained security mechanisms. Row filter functions (CREATE FUNCTION ... RETURN <boolean_expr>) are attached to a table via ALTER TABLE SET ROW FILTER — at query time the function is evaluated per row and only rows where the function returns true are visible to the caller. Column masks (CREATE FUNCTION ... RETURN <same_type_expr>) are attached to a specific column via ALTER TABLE ALTER COLUMN SET MASK — the function transforms or redacts the column value before returning it. Both execute with the table owner's privileges (definer's rights), not the querying user's, enabling privilege escalation for security lookups. Available context functions: IS_ACCOUNT_GROUP_MEMBER('group'), CURRENT_USER(), SESSION_CONTEXT('key'). Row filters and column masks are enforced on all query paths: SQL, Spark DataFrame, BI JDBC/ODBC, Unity Catalog REST API data sampling. Multiple row filters can be attached to one table; only one mask per column. Neither can be bypassed by any non-owner principal.
summary: System: Unity Catalog row filter functions and column masks — definer rights, IS_ACCOUNT_GROUP_MEMBER predicates, enforcement on all query paths, and stack limits

label: Unity Catalog automatic data lineage: table-level and column-level tracking
content: Unity Catalog automatically captures data lineage for SQL and Spark DataFrame operations executed on Unity Catalog-enabled clusters (Single User or Shared access mode). Table-level lineage records which tables were read as sources and written as targets per operation. Column-level lineage tracks which source columns contributed to each target column through SELECT, transformations, aggregations, and joins. Lineage is captured for: CREATE TABLE AS SELECT, INSERT INTO ... SELECT, MERGE INTO, Structured Streaming reads/writes on Unity Catalog tables, and DataFrame .write/.writeStream to Unity Catalog tables. Lineage is NOT captured for: hive_metastore tables, operations on No Isolation Shared clusters, direct cloud storage reads (spark.read.load('abfss://...')), COPY INTO (table-level only, not column-level), and arbitrary Python driver-side file writes. Lineage data is queryable via the Unity Catalog REST API (GET /api/2.0/lineage-tracking/table-lineage and /column-lineage) and visible in the Databricks Data Explorer UI under each table's Lineage tab.
summary: System: Unity Catalog automatic lineage — capture scope, column-level tracking, Single User/Shared cluster requirement, REST API and Data Explorer access

label: Unity Catalog Volumes: managed vs external volumes for non-tabular file storage
content: Unity Catalog Volumes provide governed, privilege-controlled access to non-tabular files (raw files, ML models, images, scripts, archives) stored in cloud object storage. A Volume is a first-class Unity Catalog object registered as catalog.schema.volume_name. Two types: Managed volumes store files in the Unity Catalog catalog or schema managed storage location — DROP VOLUME also deletes the underlying files. External volumes reference files at a specified LOCATION path covered by a Unity Catalog external location — DROP VOLUME preserves files. Volumes are accessed via the /Volumes/ path in all Databricks interfaces: /Volumes/catalog/schema/volume_name/path/file in SQL, Python (open(), dbutils.fs), and Spark (spark.read.load('/Volumes/...')). Access is controlled via READ VOLUME (read files) and WRITE VOLUME (create, replace, delete files) privileges — independent of table SELECT/MODIFY. Volumes replace legacy /dbfs/mnt/ mount points in Unity Catalog workspaces and enforce audit logging, access control, and lineage on file operations.
summary: System: Unity Catalog Volumes — managed vs external lifecycle, /Volumes/ path access, READ/WRITE VOLUME privileges, and mount point replacement

label: Choose catalog and schema boundaries for environment, domain, and team isolation
content: Use separate catalogs for environment isolation (prod, dev, staging) — catalog-level privileges do not cascade across catalog boundaries, preventing prod data from leaking to dev pipelines. Use separate catalogs for coarse organizational domains (finance, engineering, ops) when cross-domain data sharing requires explicit governance decisions by a catalog owner. Use schemas within a single catalog for finer grouping: medallion layers (bronze, silver, gold), team domains, or data products — schemas can be self-service without metastore admin rights. Avoid over-proliferating catalogs; catalog creation requires metastore admin or CREATE CATALOG privilege. Set GRANT ... ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA for teams where any newly created table should automatically inherit access, eliminating per-table grant overhead. Never share a schema between prod and dev environments. Use a dedicated 'analytics' catalog for all BI-facing gold views to simplify BI tool permission management and isolate query traffic.
summary: Decision: Separate catalogs for environments and domains; schemas for layers/teams; FUTURE TABLES grants for schema owners; analytics catalog for BI isolation

label: Choose managed vs external table based on data ownership and lifecycle requirements
content: Use managed tables when: data is exclusively owned and accessed through Databricks/Unity Catalog, no external system reads the underlying Parquet files directly, automated storage cleanup on DROP TABLE is acceptable, and exact path management is delegated to Unity Catalog. Use external tables when: data files must be shared with non-Databricks systems (Azure Data Factory, Synapse, external Spark), data pre-existed the Unity Catalog migration, storage path organization must be controlled independently of Databricks, or regulatory retention requirements mandate file preservation after table removal. Never point external tables at paths not covered by a registered Unity Catalog external location — CREATE TABLE will fail. Never create two tables (managed and external) pointing to the same storage path — concurrent Delta transaction log management will corrupt both tables. When migrating hive_metastore managed tables to Unity Catalog, prefer CTAS into Unity Catalog managed tables over converting to external tables to benefit from managed lifecycle and simplified governance.
summary: Decision: Managed for Databricks-exclusive lifecycle; external for cross-platform shared paths — never duplicate storage ownership; CTAS preferred for hive_metastore migration

label: Choose grant scope: table-level vs schema-level vs catalog-level ACLs
content: Grant at the narrowest scope that satisfies the requirement. Grant SELECT on individual tables or views for BI consumers — prevents implicit access to future tables added to the schema. Grant MODIFY only to pipeline service principals that write to the table; never grant MODIFY to BI tool service accounts. Grant CREATE TABLE on a schema to engineers deploying pipelines to that schema. Grant ALL PRIVILEGES on a schema to data domain owners who fully control that schema. Use GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA for groups where all future tables should be accessible automatically, eliminating per-table grants. Avoid catalog-level SELECT grants — they expose all tables in all schemas, including future ones. Never grant privileges on hive_metastore objects to Unity Catalog principals — keep governance exclusively in Unity Catalog catalogs. Audit grants quarterly using SHOW GRANTS ON <object> and SHOW GRANTS TO <principal>; revoke stale access from departed contractors and retired service principals immediately.
summary: Decision: Table-level for consumers; schema FUTURE for owners; MODIFY only for pipeline SPs; no catalog-level SELECT; quarterly audit and REVOKE discipline

label: Choose row filter function vs column mask vs security view for fine-grained access control
content: Use row filter functions when: the same row-level access rule applies across multiple tables (e.g., region-based tenant isolation), enforcement must be transparent to BI tools without relying on view naming conventions, or the access logic references an external allow-list table that changes independently of the secured table's schema. Use column masks when: specific columns contain regulated data (SSN, credit card numbers, salary, health data) that must be hidden or partially redacted for non-privileged users regardless of query path. Use security views (CREATE VIEW with WHERE clauses) when: the access logic is one-off for a single table, complex multi-table joins are needed to determine access, or a named access-controlled object is needed for explicit monitoring via SHOW GRANTS. Do not combine row filter functions and security views applying the same rule on the same table — double enforcement produces unexpected empty results when filters conflict. Prefer row filter functions over views for multi-table row-level security to avoid view sprawl and maintain a single function definition.
summary: Decision: Row filters for cross-table row isolation; column masks for PII redaction; security views for one-off structural access — never combine row filter + view for the same rule

label: When to rely on automatic Unity Catalog lineage vs supplement with tags or external catalog integration
content: Rely on automatic lineage when: all pipelines run on Unity Catalog-enabled Single User or Shared clusters, all source and target tables are in Unity Catalog (not hive_metastore), and SQL/Spark DataFrame operations cover all data movement. Verify lineage capture is active by confirming cluster access mode — No Isolation Shared clusters do not capture lineage. Supplement with column tags (ALTER COLUMN SET TAGS) when compliance frameworks (GDPR, CCPA) require explicit PII classification discoverable in Unity Catalog search rather than inferred from lineage graphs alone. Add table tags (ALTER TABLE SET TAGS) for domain ownership, data product name, and sensitivity classification to enrich lineage context with business metadata. Integrate with external catalogs (Collibra, Alation, Microsoft Purview) via the Unity Catalog lineage REST API when multi-platform governance spans Databricks and other compute engines (Synapse, Spark on AKS) that require lineage stitching outside Databricks.
summary: Decision: Automatic lineage sufficient for UC-only SQL/Spark pipelines; column tags for compliance PII classification; REST API integration for multi-platform governance

label: Choose managed vs external volume based on file lifecycle and cross-system sharing requirements
content: Use managed volumes when: files are exclusively produced and consumed within Databricks, no external system reads the storage path directly, and cleanup on DROP VOLUME is acceptable (ML experiment artifacts, temporary staging files, pipeline-generated reports). Use external volumes when: files must be accessible to non-Databricks systems (Azure ML, SageMaker, Azure Data Factory, external Spark clusters), the storage path is pre-existing and independently managed, or files must survive DROP VOLUME for compliance or external SLA reasons. Always use /Volumes/ paths instead of direct ADLS/S3 paths or /dbfs/mnt/ mount points in all new code — /Volumes/ paths enforce Unity Catalog ACLs and produce audit logs; direct cloud storage paths and legacy mount points bypass governance entirely. Prefer volumes over DBFS root (/dbfs/) for all ML model artifacts and non-tabular pipeline outputs — DBFS root is workspace-global with no fine-grained access control. Volumes are the recommended storage target for MLflow model registry artifacts when using MLflow with Unity Catalog integration.
summary: Decision: Managed volumes for Databricks-exclusive lifecycle; external for cross-platform sharing; /Volumes/ over /dbfs/mnt/ always; volumes for MLflow artifacts

label: Unity Catalog namespace DDL: CREATE CATALOG/SCHEMA, USE, qualified names, and FUTURE grants
summary: Output: CREATE CATALOG/SCHEMA with managed location, USE for session defaults, three-part name patterns, FUTURE TABLES/VIEWS grants, and SHOW object discovery

label: Managed and external table DDL with storage credentials, external locations, and migration patterns
content:
-- Step 1: Register storage credential (admin, once per connector)
CREATE STORAGE CREDENTIAL adls_prod_cred
  WITH AZURE MANAGED IDENTITY
  CREDENTIAL azure_managed_identity_resource_id =
    '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.Databricks/accessConnectors/<name>';

-- Step 2: Register external location covering the ADLS path
CREATE EXTERNAL LOCATION prod_external
  URL 'abfss://external@<account>.dfs.core.windows.net/'
  WITH (STORAGE CREDENTIAL adls_prod_cred);

-- Managed table (UC owns lifecycle; DROP TABLE deletes files)
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS prod.silver.orders (
  order_id   STRING        NOT NULL,
  amount     DECIMAL(18,2),
  order_date DATE          NOT NULL
)
USING DELTA
PARTITIONED BY (order_date);
-- Data stored at catalog/schema managed location automatically

-- External table (DROP TABLE preserves files at LOCATION)
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS prod.bronze.raw_orders (
  raw_json    STRING,
  source_file STRING,
  ingested_at TIMESTAMP
)
USING DELTA
LOCATION 'abfss://external@<account>.dfs.core.windows.net/bronze/raw_orders/';
-- LOCATION must fall within a registered Unity Catalog external location

-- Verify table type
DESCRIBE EXTENDED prod.silver.orders;     -- Type: MANAGED
DESCRIBE EXTENDED prod.bronze.raw_orders; -- Type: EXTERNAL, Location: abfss://...

-- Migrate hive_metastore table to UC managed (CTAS preferred)
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS prod.silver.orders_migrated
AS SELECT * FROM hive_metastore.legacy_db.orders;

-- Convert existing Delta path to Unity Catalog external table
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS prod.bronze.events
USING DELTA
LOCATION 'abfss://external@<account>.dfs.core.windows.net/bronze/events/';
summary: Output: Storage credential and external location registration DDL, managed and external CREATE TABLE patterns, DESCRIBE EXTENDED type check, and hive_metastore CTAS migration

label: GRANT and REVOKE privilege SQL patterns for Unity Catalog objects
summary: Output: Minimum-privilege SELECT grant stack, pipeline SP write grants, FUTURE TABLES/VIEWS auto-grant, volume access, REVOKE patterns, and SHOW GRANTS audit commands
