content: Use a fixed cron schedule (quartz_cron_expression) when: bronze data arrives on a predictable cadence (hourly batch, nightly ETL), SLA allows waiting until the next schedule window, and simplicity is preferred over event-driven complexity — most production pipelines use scheduled triggers. Use a Databricks Workflows file-arrival trigger (path trigger on cloud storage) when: upstream systems drop files to a landing zone at irregular intervals and silver/gold SLA requires processing within minutes of file arrival — the trigger fires as soon as new files appear at the specified path. Use a Delta table continuous trigger (job polling a Delta table for new commits) via a taskValues short-circuit pattern when: a bronze streaming job writes continuously and the silver/gold batch job should run immediately after each bronze micro-batch completes — implement by checking the Delta last-commit timestamp or Change Data Feed at the start of the silver task. Do not poll Delta tables in a tight loop — use a minimum 5-minute processingTime interval or schedule to avoid excessive compute cost for empty runs.
summary: Decision: cron schedule for predictable batch cadences; file-arrival trigger for irregular upstream file delivery; Delta commit detection in taskValues for streaming-to-batch handoff; avoid tight polling loops

label: Bronze→silver→gold Workflow DAG with taskValues short-circuit and OPTIMIZE post-processing
summary: Output: Complete bronze→silver→gold Workflow DAG with parallel silver fan-out, taskValues short-circuit for empty runs, gold OPTIMIZE post-processing task, and AT_LEAST_ONE_FAILED error handler

label: Enforce data quality in DLT pipelines using expect, expect_or_drop, and expect_or_fail
content: DLT expectations are declarative row-level quality rules attached to table definitions via stacked Python decorators or inline SQL CONSTRAINT clauses. Three enforcement policies: @dlt.expect (warn/track) — violating rows pass through; violation counts are written to the event log at <storage>/system/events/; pipeline continues. @dlt.expect_or_drop — violating rows are silently removed before downstream tables see them; pipeline continues with only valid rows. @dlt.expect_or_fail — any violating row halts the entire pipeline update with a failure; use when the source is contract-bound and partial output is unacceptable. Multi-rule variants accept a dict: @dlt.expect_all, @dlt.expect_all_or_drop, @dlt.expect_all_or_fail. Expectations evaluate standard Spark SQL boolean expressions per row. Results (pass/fail row counts per rule per update) are visible in the DLT pipeline UI and queryable from the event log.
summary: System: DLT expectations enforce per-row quality rules with warn/drop/fail policies; results tracked in the pipeline event log

label: Validate data quality with Python assert statements and metric checks in PySpark notebooks
content: Notebook-based quality checks use Python assert statements or explicit raises to gate pipeline execution before writing to downstream tables. Standard pattern: compute a quality metric — e.g., null_rate = df.filter(col('order_id').isNull()).count() / df.count() — then assert null_rate < 0.01 or raise ValueError with a descriptive message. Common checks: row count vs expected minimum (zero-row guard), null rates on required columns, distinct value counts for low-cardinality fields (enum membership), value range violations (amount > 0), referential integrity via anti-join (records without a matching dimension key). In streaming foreachBatch, quality checks run inside the batch function before DeltaTable.merge(). Quality check failures should always raise exceptions so the Databricks Workflow task fails and retry or alert logic triggers. This pattern is appropriate for batch silver transforms where DLT is not used.
summary: System: Python assert statements and metric-based checks in PySpark notebooks gate pipeline execution before writing downstream tables

label: Integrate Great Expectations with Databricks for checkpoint-based data validation
content: Great Expectations (GE) on Databricks provides a shared expectation catalog with versioned suites and HTML DataDocs reports. Core objects: Expectation Suite — a named JSON collection of expectations (expect_column_values_to_not_be_null, expect_column_values_to_be_between, expect_table_row_count_to_be_between, etc.); Datasource — configured to read from a Spark DataFrame or Delta table; Checkpoint — links a suite to a datasource batch and executes validation; DataDocs — HTML reports published to Azure Blob Storage or S3. Setup: %pip install great-expectations; initialize a DataContext pointing to a persisted config directory (Unity Catalog Volume or ADLS path). Define expectations via a Validator object, save the suite, then run a Checkpoint. checkpoint.run() returns a CheckpointResult; result.success == False raises an exception to fail the pipeline. GE integrates with Databricks Workflows as a notebook_task or spark_python_task. GE adds ~2–5 minutes of setup overhead per run due to datasource initialization.
summary: System: Great Expectations on Databricks — Spark datasource, versioned expectation suites, checkpoint validation, and DataDocs HTML report publishing

label: Monitor pipeline health with row counts, null rates, and schema drift between runs
content: Pipeline health monitoring compares key quality metrics between successive runs to detect data anomalies before they propagate to gold. Row count monitoring: persist row counts written per pipeline run to a quality_metrics Delta table; alert when current_count < threshold × prior_7day_avg (e.g., <70% signals upstream data loss); zero-row runs almost always indicate an upstream failure. Null rate monitoring: compute null_count / total_count per required column per run; alert when null_rate increases beyond baseline (e.g., >1% on a previously clean field indicates upstream schema or process change). Schema drift detection: capture df.schema.json() after ingestion and compare field names and types to the prior run's stored schema snapshot; alert on dropped columns (high severity), type changes (critical), or unexpected additions (low severity, verify mergeSchema is set). Persist all metrics in a monitoring.quality_metrics Delta table (table_name, metric_name, metric_value, run_id, run_ts) for trend queries and Databricks SQL dashboard alerting.
summary: System: Track row counts, null rates, and schema drift between pipeline runs in a quality_metrics table; alert on anomalies before they reach gold

label: Route failed-quality records to quarantine Delta tables for investigation and reprocessing
content: Quarantine tables capture records that fail data quality rules so they are never silently dropped or propagated downstream. Batch pattern: split the incoming DataFrame into valid_df (passes all quality rules) and quarantine_df (fails at least one rule) using filter(); write valid_df to the silver table and quarantine_df to a dedicated quarantine Delta table (e.g., bronze.orders_quarantine) with additional metadata columns: failed_rule (STRING — name of the first failing rule), failed_reason (STRING — human-readable description), source_file (STRING), pipeline_run_id (STRING), quarantined_at (TIMESTAMP), quarantine_date (DATE — partition column). DLT pattern: use @dlt.expect (warn mode) so violating rows pass through with violation counts tracked; define a separate quarantine DLT streaming table that reads the same upstream table and filters for rows where the quality condition is FALSE. Quarantine tables support forensic investigation, producer feedback, and idempotent reprocessing after upstream fixes. Never use expect_or_drop as a substitute for quarantine when auditability is required.
summary: System: Split records into valid and quarantine DataFrames; write quarantine records with failed_rule metadata to a dedicated partitioned Delta table

label: Choose DLT expectation enforcement mode based on data contract and violation tolerance
content: Use @dlt.expect (warn/track) when: baselining quality for the first time without knowing violation rates, downstream consumers can filter or handle invalid records independently, or a partial output is acceptable and traceable via the event log. Deploy in warn mode first to measure violation rates before escalating. Use @dlt.expect_or_drop when: downstream silver or gold tables require clean data but the pipeline must continue for valid records, violation rates are expected to be low (<1%), and discarded records do not require investigation or reprocessing. Use @dlt.expect_or_fail when: a data contract guarantees clean input and any violation signals a critical upstream failure requiring immediate intervention, regulatory requirements prohibit processing invalid records, or the pipeline must produce complete output or nothing. Never deploy expect_or_fail on high-volume streaming tables without confirmed upstream quality controls — a single bad record halts the entire pipeline update. After deploying in warn mode, monitor the event log failure rate: >0.1% consistently → escalate to expect_or_drop; >0% on a strict contract → escalate to expect_or_fail.
summary: Decision: expect for monitoring; expect_or_drop when downstream needs clean data and low violations are tolerable; expect_or_fail for strict contracts only

label: Choose notebook asserts vs DLT expectations vs Delta constraints for quality gate placement
content: Use notebook assert checks when: the pipeline is a batch PySpark notebook without DLT, quality validation must block the write to a downstream table, or the check requires complex multi-table logic (referential integrity across two tables, row count comparison to a prior run). Use DLT expectations when: the pipeline is authored in DLT, declarative per-row SQL boolean rules are sufficient, and violation metrics should be tracked automatically in the event log without custom metric-tracking code. Use Delta CHECK constraints when: the rule is a simple boolean predicate (NOT NULL, value ranges, enum membership) that must be enforced on every write to the table regardless of which pipeline writes it — use as a write-time backstop of last resort. Combine all three in production: DLT expectations for per-row streaming quality, notebook asserts for pipeline-level metric gates in batch jobs, and Delta constraints as backstops on silver/gold tables.
summary: Decision: notebook asserts for batch multi-metric gates; DLT expectations for streaming per-row rules; Delta constraints as write-time backstops

label: Choose Great Expectations vs DLT expectations vs custom asserts for validation framework
content: Use Great Expectations when: the data team needs a shared, versioned expectation catalog across multiple pipelines and teams, HTML DataDocs reports must be published for business stakeholders or auditors, or the organization requires cross-platform validation spanning non-Databricks systems (Synapse, Redshift, dbt). Use DLT expectations when: the pipeline is fully in DLT, declarative per-row rules are sufficient, tight integration with DLT event log metrics is preferred over a separate GE backend, and setup overhead must be minimal. Use custom notebook asserts when: GE adds unacceptable overhead for a simple pipeline, the validation logic is unique to one pipeline and not shared, or the team needs multi-row metric checks (row count ratio, distribution statistics) not easily expressed as GE column-level expectations. GE is most valuable at organizational scale; for single-pipeline projects, DLT expectations or notebook asserts deliver equivalent protection with less infrastructure.
summary: Decision: Great Expectations for org-wide shared suites and DataDocs; DLT expectations for DLT-native per-row rules; custom asserts for lightweight batch pipelines

label: Set monitoring thresholds for row count, null rate, and schema drift alerting
content: Row count thresholds: compute the P10 of historical row counts over the prior 7–14 runs and use 70% of P10 as the minimum alert threshold; a zero-row run is always an immediate alert regardless of threshold. Null rate thresholds: measure the P99 null rate per column over 14 prior runs; alert if the current null rate exceeds 2× the P99 baseline or crosses an absolute threshold (e.g., >5% on a required column, >0% on a primary key). Schema drift severity levels: new columns (additive) = low severity, log warning only; dropped or renamed columns = high severity, fail the pipeline; type changes = critical, fail and page on-call. Alert routing: row count anomalies and high null rates → Slack/email to data engineer on-call; schema drift high/critical → PagerDuty. Compute baseline thresholds using a historical analysis notebook run once per quarter or when source systems change. Retain quality_metrics for at least 30 days to support trend baseline computation.
summary: Decision: alert at 70% of P10 row count; 2× P99 null rate; fail pipeline on dropped columns or type changes; low severity for additive new columns

label: Design quarantine table schema and decide when to reprocess or discard quarantined records
content: Required quarantine table columns: all original source columns (preserve the full record for reprocessing), failed_rule (STRING — name of the first failing rule), failed_reason (STRING — human-readable message), source_file (STRING — lineage to landing file), pipeline_run_id (STRING — correlation to the producing pipeline run), quarantined_at (TIMESTAMP), quarantine_date (DATE — partition column). Partition by quarantine_date for efficient time-range investigation queries. Reprocess quarantined records when: the upstream source corrects and resends the data (resend pattern), or the quality rule was overly strict and is updated — reprocessing requires a DELETE from the quarantine table after successful reprocessing to avoid duplication. Discard quarantined records when: they represent confirmed permanent source data errors with no fix path — document the decision. Set VACUUM retention to at least 30 days on quarantine tables. Never use expect_or_drop silently when downstream auditability is required — quarantine tables provide the audit trail that dropped rows cannot.
summary: Decision: quarantine schema with failed_rule/source_file/pipeline_run_id; reprocess on upstream fix; 30-day retention; never silently drop auditable records

label: DLT expectation code patterns: expect, expect_or_drop, expect_or_fail, and multi-rule variants
summary: Output: DLT expectation decorators for warn, drop, and fail enforcement modes with multi-rule expect_all_or_drop and event log monitoring query

label: Notebook data quality assertion patterns: zero-row guard, null rate, value range, enum, and referential integrity
content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, count, when, current_timestamp, lit

def assert_quality(df, table_name):
    """Run quality checks on a DataFrame; raise ValueError on failure."""
    total = df.count()

    # Zero-row guard
    if total == 0:
        raise ValueError(f'{table_name}: zero rows received — upstream data missing')

    # Null rate on required columns
    for req_col in ['order_id', 'customer_id', 'amount']:
        null_count = df.filter(col(req_col).isNull()).count()
        null_rate  = null_count / total
        assert null_rate < 0.01, (
            f'{table_name}: null rate on {req_col} is {null_rate:.2%} '
            f'(threshold 1%) — check upstream source'
        )

    # Value range check
    neg_amounts = df.filter(col('amount') <= 0).count()
    assert neg_amounts == 0, (
        f'{table_name}: {neg_amounts} rows have amount <= 0'
    )

    # Enum membership
    valid_statuses = ('pending', 'active', 'shipped', 'cancelled')
    invalid_status = df.filter(~col('status').isin(*valid_statuses)).count()
    assert invalid_status == 0, (
        f'{table_name}: {invalid_status} rows have unrecognised status values'
    )

    # Referential integrity: all customer_ids must exist in dim_customer
    unmatched = (
        df.alias('f')
        .join(spark.table('gold.dim_customer').alias('d'),
              col('f.customer_id') == col('d.customer_id'), 'left_anti')
        .count()
    )
    assert unmatched == 0, (
        f'{table_name}: {unmatched} rows reference unknown customer_id'
    )

    # Row count vs prior 7-day average
    prior_avg_row = spark.sql(f"""
        SELECT COALESCE(AVG(metric_value), 0)
        FROM monitoring.quality_metrics
        WHERE table_name = '{table_name}'
          AND metric_name = 'row_count'
          AND run_ts >= current_timestamp() - INTERVAL 7 DAYS
    """).first()[0]
    if prior_avg_row > 0:
        assert total >= 0.7 * prior_avg_row, (
            f'{table_name}: row count {total} is <70% of 7-day avg {prior_avg_row:.0f}'
        )

    return total

# Usage in batch silver notebook
silver_df = spark.table('bronze.orders').filter(col('_ingested_at') > last_run_ts)
row_count  = assert_quality(silver_df, 'silver.orders')

# Write only after all assertions pass
silver_df.write.format('delta').mode('append').saveAsTable('silver.orders')

# Persist row count metric for future baseline comparisons
spark.sql(f"""
    INSERT INTO monitoring.quality_metrics (table_name, metric_name, metric_value, run_ts)
    VALUES ('silver.orders', 'row_count', {row_count}, current_timestamp())
""")
summary: Output: Notebook assert patterns — zero-row guard, null rate, value range, enum membership, referential integrity, and row count vs prior baseline

label: Great Expectations suite definition, checkpoint run, and DataDocs publishing on Databricks
content:
# Install: %pip install great-expectations
import great_expectations as gx

# Initialize a filesystem DataContext backed by a Unity Catalog Volume
# (persists suite definitions and DataDocs config across runs)
context = gx.get_context(
    context_root_dir='/Volumes/prod/ge_context'
)

# Add or update a Spark datasource using the Fluent API (GE 0.16+)
datasource = context.sources.add_or_update_spark('orders_datasource')
asset      = datasource.add_dataframe_asset(name='orders_bronze_asset')

# Build a batch request from a Spark DataFrame
batch_request = asset.build_batch_request(
    dataframe=spark.table('bronze.orders')
)

# Define expectation suite (idempotent)
context.add_or_update_expectation_suite('orders_bronze_suite')
validator = context.get_validator(
    batch_request          = batch_request,
    expectation_suite_name = 'orders_bronze_suite'
)

validator.expect_column_values_to_not_be_null('order_id')
validator.expect_column_values_to_not_be_null('customer_id')
validator.expect_column_values_to_be_between('amount', min_value=0)
validator.expect_column_values_to_be_in_set(
    'status', ['pending', 'active', 'shipped', 'cancelled']
)
validator.expect_table_row_count_to_be_between(min_value=1)
validator.expect_column_values_to_match_regex(
    'order_id', r'^ORD-\d{6,}$'
)
validator.save_expectation_suite(discard_failed_expectations=False)

# Create or update checkpoint
checkpoint = context.add_or_update_checkpoint(
    name        = 'orders_bronze_checkpoint',
    validations = [{
        'batch_request':           batch_request,
        'expectation_suite_name':  'orders_bronze_suite'
    }]
)

# Run validation
result = checkpoint.run()

# Fail pipeline task if any expectation fails
if not result.success:
    # result.to_json_dict() contains per-expectation pass/fail detail
    raise ValueError(
        f'Great Expectations validation failed for bronze.orders. '
        f'Unsuccessful expectations: '
        f"{[r['expectation_config']['expectation_type'] for r in result.list_validation_results()[0]['results'] if not r['success']]}"
    )

# Publish DataDocs to Azure Blob (configure site in great_expectations.yml)
# context.build_data_docs(site_names=['azure_blob_site'])
# print('DataDocs URL:', context.get_docs_sites_urls()[0]['site_url'])
summary: Output: Great Expectations Spark datasource, expectation suite definition, checkpoint validation, failure exception, and DataDocs publishing on Databricks

label: Row count, null rate, and schema drift monitoring code patterns with quality_metrics and schema_snapshots tables
content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, count, when, current_timestamp, lit
import json

# --- DDL: create monitoring tables once ---
# CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS monitoring.quality_metrics (
#   table_name   STRING NOT NULL,
#   metric_name  STRING NOT NULL,
#   metric_value DOUBLE,
#   run_id       STRING,
#   run_ts       TIMESTAMP NOT NULL
# ) USING DELTA TBLPROPERTIES ('delta.deletedFileRetentionDuration'='interval 30 days');
#
# CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS monitoring.schema_snapshots (
#   table_name   STRING NOT NULL,
#   schema_json  STRING NOT NULL,
#   snapshot_ts  TIMESTAMP NOT NULL
# ) USING DELTA;

def collect_and_persist_metrics(df, table_name, run_id):
    total = df.count()
    null_exprs = [count(when(col(c).isNull(), 1)).alias(c) for c in df.columns]
    null_counts = df.select(null_exprs).first().asDict()

    rows = [(table_name, 'row_count', float(total), run_id)]
    for col_name, nc in null_counts.items():
        rate = nc / total if total > 0 else 0.0
        rows.append((table_name, f'null_rate.{col_name}', rate, run_id))

    (spark
        .createDataFrame(rows, ['table_name', 'metric_name', 'metric_value', 'run_id'])
        .withColumn('run_ts', current_timestamp())
        .write.format('delta').mode('append')
        .saveAsTable('monitoring.quality_metrics'))
    return total, {r[1]: r[2] for r in rows if r[1].startswith('null_rate.')}

def detect_and_snapshot_schema(df, table_name):
    current_json = df.schema.json()
    prior_row = spark.sql(f"""
        SELECT schema_json FROM monitoring.schema_snapshots
        WHERE table_name = '{table_name}'
        ORDER BY snapshot_ts DESC LIMIT 1
    """).first()

    if prior_row:
        prior_fields   = {f['name']: f['type'] for f in json.loads(prior_row[0])['fields']}
        current_fields = {f['name']: f['type'] for f in json.loads(current_json)['fields']}
        dropped       = set(prior_fields) - set(current_fields)
        type_changes  = {c for c in prior_fields
                         if c in current_fields and prior_fields[c] != current_fields[c]}
        added         = set(current_fields) - set(prior_fields)

        if dropped or type_changes:
            raise ValueError(
                f'Breaking schema drift on {table_name}: '
                f'dropped={dropped}, type_changes={type_changes}'
            )
        if added:
            print(f'[WARN] New columns on {table_name}: {added} — ensure mergeSchema=true')

    # Snapshot current schema
    (spark
        .createDataFrame([(table_name, current_json)], ['table_name', 'schema_json'])
        .withColumn('snapshot_ts', current_timestamp())
        .write.format('delta').mode('append')
        .saveAsTable('monitoring.schema_snapshots'))

# --- Usage in silver notebook ---
RUN_ID = dbutils.widgets.get('run_id') if 'run_id' in [w.name for w in dbutils.widgets.getAll()] else 'manual'

bronze_df = spark.table('bronze.orders')
detect_and_snapshot_schema(bronze_df, 'bronze.orders')     # fail on breaking drift
total, null_rates = collect_and_persist_metrics(bronze_df, 'bronze.orders', RUN_ID)

# Alert thresholds
if total == 0:
    raise ValueError('bronze.orders: zero rows — upstream delivery failure')
if null_rates.get('null_rate.order_id', 0) > 0.01:
    raise ValueError(f"null rate on order_id is {null_rates['null_rate.order_id']:.2%}")
summary: Output: Row count and per-column null rate collection, schema drift detection, and metric persistence to monitoring.quality_metrics and schema_snapshots Delta tables

label: Quarantine table DDL and record routing patterns for batch and DLT pipelines
content:
-- Quarantine table DDL (run once per source table)
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS bronze.orders_quarantine (
  order_id         STRING,
  customer_id      STRING,
  amount           DOUBLE,
  status           STRING,
  order_date       STRING,
  raw_json         STRING,       -- original raw record for reprocessing
  failed_rule      STRING  NOT NULL,
  failed_reason    STRING,
  source_file      STRING,
  pipeline_run_id  STRING,
  quarantined_at   TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  quarantine_date  DATE      NOT NULL  -- partition column
)
USING DELTA
PARTITIONED BY (quarantine_date)
TBLPROPERTIES ('delta.deletedFileRetentionDuration' = 'interval 30 days');

# --- Batch: split valid vs quarantine before writing silver ---
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, lit, current_timestamp, to_date, when
from functools import reduce

RUN_ID = dbutils.widgets.get('run_id') if 'run_id' in [w.name for w in dbutils.widgets.getAll()] else 'manual'

# Quality rules: (rule_name, boolean column where True = FAIL)
rules = [
    ('not_null_order_id', col('order_id').isNull()),
    ('positive_amount',   col('amount') <= 0),
    ('valid_status',      ~col('status').isin('pending', 'active', 'shipped', 'cancelled'))
]

# Tag each row with the first failing rule name
flagged = spark.table('bronze.orders')
for rule_name, cond in rules:
    flagged = flagged.withColumn(f'_fail_{rule_name}', cond)

failed_rule_col = reduce(
    lambda acc, r: when(col(f'_fail_{r[0]}'), lit(r[0])).otherwise(acc),
    reversed(rules),
    lit(None).cast('string')
)
flagged = flagged.withColumn('_failed_rule', failed_rule_col)

flag_cols  = [f'_fail_{r[0]}' for r in rules]
valid_df   = flagged.filter(col('_failed_rule').isNull()).drop(*flag_cols, '_failed_rule')
quarantine = flagged.filter(col('_failed_rule').isNotNull())

# Write valid records to silver
valid_df.write.format('delta').mode('append').saveAsTable('silver.orders')

# Write quarantine records with metadata
(quarantine
    .withColumn('failed_rule',      col('_failed_rule'))
    .withColumn('quarantined_at',   current_timestamp())
    .withColumn('quarantine_date',  to_date(current_timestamp()))
    .withColumn('pipeline_run_id',  lit(RUN_ID))
    .drop(*flag_cols, '_failed_rule')
    .write.format('delta').mode('append')
    .saveAsTable('bronze.orders_quarantine'))

print(f'Valid: {valid_df.count()}, Quarantined: {quarantine.count()}')

# --- DLT: quarantine via expect (warn) + separate quarantine table ---
# import dlt
# @dlt.expect('valid_order_id', 'order_id IS NOT NULL')  # warn — rows pass through
# @dlt.table(name='orders_silver_flagged')
# def orders_silver_flagged():
#     return dlt.read_stream('orders_bronze')
#
# @dlt.table(name='orders_quarantine_dlt', comment='Records failing quality rules')
# def orders_quarantine_dlt():
#     return (
#         dlt.read_stream('orders_bronze')
#         .filter('order_id IS NULL')   # mirror the expect condition
#         .withColumn('failed_rule', lit('not_null_order_id'))
#         .withColumn('quarantined_at', current_timestamp())
#     )

-- Investigate quarantine (daily review query)
-- SELECT failed_rule, COUNT(*) AS cnt, MIN(quarantined_at) AS first_seen
-- FROM bronze.orders_quarantine
-- WHERE quarantine_date >= current_date() - INTERVAL 7 DAYS
-- GROUP BY failed_rule
-- ORDER BY cnt DESC;
summary: Output: Quarantine table DDL and batch/DLT record routing — split valid and failing records, write quarantine with failed_rule metadata, and daily review query

label: Understand Photon engine: supported operations and activation
content: Photon is a C++ vectorized query engine in Databricks Runtime that replaces JVM Spark execution for supported operations. Accelerated operations: SQL SELECT/GROUP BY/JOIN/SORT, Delta Lake and Parquet file reads with vectorized I/O, numeric aggregations (SUM, AVG, COUNT), filter pushdown, MERGE scans, and OPTIMIZE compaction. Photon falls back to JVM Spark for unsupported operations (Python UDFs, pandas UDFs, RDD API, certain ML operators) without error. Photon is enabled per-cluster via cluster config (runtime_engine: PHOTON) or is always active on Databricks SQL warehouses. Available on Databricks Runtime 9.1+ on supported instance types (excluding GPU nodes). Photon and standard Spark share executor memory — memory sizing requirements are identical.
summary: System: Photon C++ vectorized engine — supported operations, fallback behavior, and cluster activation

label: Decide when Photon helps vs when to skip
content: Enable Photon when: workloads are dominated by SQL aggregations (SUM/COUNT/AVG over large datasets), complex multi-table joins on large fact and dimension tables, Delta Lake scan-heavy pipelines (gold serving, OPTIMIZE, Z-ordering), or mixed SQL/DataFrame gold layer serving accessed by BI tools. Photon provides the highest benefit on compute-bound queries — typically 2–5× wall-clock speedup. Skip or evaluate before committing when: more than 20% of query time is spent in Python UDFs or pandas UDFs (Photon cannot accelerate Python-serialized code — UDF rows fall back to JVM, negating gains), ML inference pipelines dominate, or the bottleneck is I/O-bound cloud storage latency rather than CPU. Photon clusters bill at a higher DBU multiplier (approximately 1.0–1.5× vs standard DBU) — verify actual query speedup before committing to Photon for cost-sensitive long-running jobs. For streaming micro-batch jobs where latency is critical, profile Photon startup overhead per batch.
summary: Decision: Enable Photon for aggregation/join/Delta-scan workloads; skip for UDF-heavy or I/O-bound jobs

label: Photon activation, plan verification, and speedup benchmarking patterns
content:
# Enable Photon on a job cluster (cluster config JSON)
# { "runtime_engine": "PHOTON", "spark_version": "15.4.x-scala2.12", ... }

# Verify Photon is active — inspect physical plan for Photon operators
df = spark.table('gold.fact_orders')
df.groupBy('region').agg({'amount': 'sum'}).explain(True)
# Photon active:   PhotonResultStage, PhotonAggregate, PhotonProject in the plan
# Photon inactive: HashAggregate, SortMergeJoin (JVM nodes)

# Session-level toggle for benchmarking (do NOT use in production)
spark.conf.set('spark.databricks.photon.enabled', 'false')  # force JVM
import time
t0 = time.time()
spark.sql('SELECT region, SUM(amount) FROM gold.fact_orders GROUP BY region').collect()
no_photon = time.time() - t0

spark.conf.set('spark.databricks.photon.enabled', 'true')   # restore Photon
t0 = time.time()
spark.sql('SELECT region, SUM(amount) FROM gold.fact_orders GROUP BY region').collect()
with_photon = time.time() - t0
print(f'Photon speedup: {no_photon / with_photon:.1f}x')

# Identify UDF bottlenecks limiting Photon effectiveness
# Spark UI > SQL > query plan: JVM nodes (HashAggregate) after a Photon stage
# indicate UDF fallback points — refactor UDFs to built-in SQL functions

# Databricks SQL warehouse: Photon always on; check query profile
# Query History > query > Photon tab: shows % of query time Photon-accelerated
# Target: >80% Photon-accelerated for cost justification on a Photon cluster
summary: Output: Photon cluster config, plan verification for PhotonAggregate nodes, and speedup benchmarking

label: Understand Adaptive Query Execution: coalescePartitions, skewJoin, localShuffleReader
content: Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) is a Spark 3.0+ runtime optimization framework that re-plans queries using statistics collected after each shuffle exchange, replacing the static pre-execution plan. Three core features: (1) coalescePartitions — collapses many small post-shuffle partitions into fewer larger ones after a shuffle, reducing task scheduling overhead for small datasets or low-cardinality groupBys; controlled by spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled and spark.sql.adaptive.advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes (target merged partition size, default 64MB). (2) skewJoin — detects shuffle partitions that are disproportionately large (by spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionFactor × median) and splits them into sub-tasks assigned across executors; controlled by spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled and spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionThresholdInBytes. (3) localShuffleReader — when a SortMergeJoin devolves into a local exchange (data is already on the correct executor), converts it to a shuffle-free local read, eliminating the network transfer stage. AQE is enabled by default in Databricks Runtime 7.3+ (spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true).
summary: System: AQE mechanics — coalescePartitions, skewJoin, localShuffleReader; configuration parameters and defaults

label: Tune AQE coalescePartitions, skewJoin, and shuffle settings for workload
content: Always enable AQE on all production clusters — it is safe, the default, and never degrades performance. Tuning coalescePartitions: increase spark.sql.adaptive.advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes from 64MB to 128–256MB on high-memory clusters (≥32 GB executor RAM) to reduce over-coalescing of large shuffle outputs into too-few partitions. Set spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.minPartitionNum to the executor count (num_workers × cores_per_worker) as a floor — coalescing below this causes under-parallelism on large datasets. Tuning skewJoin: decrease spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionFactor from 5 to 3 when moderate skew is causing stragglers visible in Spark UI task duration variance; decrease spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionThresholdInBytes from 256MB to 128MB when skewed partitions cause executor OOM. localShuffleReader: no tuning required — applied automatically by the optimizer when eligible. Set spark.sql.shuffle.partitions as a baseline (target 100–500MB per partition: total_shuffle_bytes / target_MB); AQE coalescePartitions then corrects over-provisioned partitions at runtime. Disable AQE only to isolate query plan regressions in debugging — never in production.
summary: Decision: AQE tuning — advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes 128-256MB, minPartitionNum=executor count, skewFactor 3-5

label: AQE configuration code patterns and plan verification
content:
# Enable and tune AQE (set in cluster spark_conf or notebook session)
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.enabled', 'true')

# coalescePartitions: target 128MB merged partition size
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled', 'true')
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes', str(128 * 1024 * 1024))
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.minPartitionNum', '32')  # floor = executor count

# skewJoin: detect partitions 5x median size and >256MB
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled', 'true')
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionFactor', '5')
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionThresholdInBytes', str(256 * 1024 * 1024))

# Baseline shuffle partitions (AQE coalesces down from this at runtime)
# Formula: total_shuffle_data_GB * 1024 / target_partition_MB
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions', '400')  # tune per data volume

# Verify AQE is active — look for AQEShuffleRead in the plan
spark.sql('SELECT region, SUM(amount) FROM silver.orders GROUP BY region').explain(True)
# AQE indicators: AdaptiveSparkPlan isFinalPlan=true, AQEShuffleRead, CustomShuffleReader

# Diagnose skew: Spark UI > Stages > Tasks > Duration column
# If P99 task duration >> P50, skew is present — check skewedPartitionFactor threshold
# Reduce skewedPartitionFactor to 3 if AQE is not catching moderate skew

# Cluster config JSON (set once, applies to all queries on the cluster):
# {
#   "spark.sql.adaptive.enabled": "true",
#   "spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled": "true",
#   "spark.sql.adaptive.advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes": "134217728",
#   "spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled": "true",
#   "spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionFactor": "5",
#   "spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionThresholdInBytes": "268435456",
#   "spark.sql.shuffle.partitions": "400"
# }
summary: Output: AQE spark.conf settings, coalescePartitions/skewJoin tuning, plan verification, and cluster config JSON

label: Cluster sizing: worker count, instance types, and autoscaling configuration
content: Databricks cluster sizing determines total executor cores, memory, and parallelism. Key parameters: num_workers (or autoscale min/max) — total executor cores = num_workers × vcpu_per_node; each Spark task occupies one core. Instance types: memory-optimized (Azure: Standard_E series, AWS: r5/r6 series) provide 8GB+ RAM per vCPU — suited for join-heavy and aggregation-heavy pipelines, large broadcast tables, and caching workloads. Compute-optimized (Azure: Standard_F series, AWS: c5/c6 series) provide 2–4GB RAM per vCPU — suited for CPU-bound transformations without large memory requirements. General-purpose (Standard_D/DS series, m5 series) provide 4GB per vCPU — good default for mixed workloads. Autoscaling modes: Standard autoscaling adjusts slowly (5-min intervals); Enhanced autoscaling (DLT and some job clusters) scales down faster (1-min intervals), reducing idle cost. spark.sql.shuffle.partitions should be set to 2–4× total executor cores as a baseline; AQE coalescePartitions corrects over-provisioned partitions at runtime.
summary: System: Cluster sizing parameters — worker count formula, instance type selection by workload, autoscaling modes

label: Choose worker count, instance type, and autoscaling min/max for workload
content: Worker count baseline: estimated_workers = ceil(total_data_GB × 8 / RAM_per_worker_GB) for memory-bound joins; for compute-bound transforms use ceil(total_records_per_batch / 5M_records_per_core). Minimum workers: always set min_workers ≥ 2 for fault tolerance; never set 0 for persistent streaming clusters (cluster restart latency is prohibitive). Maximum workers: set max_workers at 3–4× expected average load for burst absorption; for scheduled fixed-size batch jobs, fixed num_workers (no autoscaling) is cheaper and more predictable than autoscaling. Instance type selection: use memory-optimized (≥8GB RAM/vCPU) when: join DataFrames exceed 10GB, broadcast threshold is raised above 512MB, Delta cache must store hot partitions, or Spark UI shows GC time >10% of task time. Use compute-optimized when: workloads are pure transformations (map, filter, cast, regexp), no large joins, and Spark UI shows GC <5%. Avoid over-sizing driver — match driver to worker type; double driver RAM only for notebooks with collect() of >1GB. shuffle.partitions target: 2–4× total_cores (e.g., 8 workers × 8 cores = 64 cores → set shuffle.partitions=200).
summary: Decision: Workers=ceil(data_GB×8/RAM); memory-optimized for joins; fixed cluster for predictable batch; min≥2

label: Cluster sizing configuration reference: JSON examples and sizing formulas
summary: Output: Cluster config JSON examples for memory-optimized and compute-optimized workloads with sizing formulas

label: Spot vs on-demand instances: preemption mechanics and Spark fault tolerance
content: Cloud providers offer spot (Azure) / spot (AWS) instances at 60–90% cost reduction versus on-demand, but instances can be reclaimed with 30–120 seconds notice when spot capacity is needed by the cloud provider. Databricks supports spot workers via azure_attributes.availability=SPOT_WITH_FALLBACK_AZURE (Azure) or aws_attributes.availability=SPOT_WITH_FALLBACK (AWS). SPOT_WITH_FALLBACK mode launches spot instances when available and falls back to on-demand if spot capacity is exhausted — balances cost and availability without manual intervention. Spot eviction causes an executor loss event: Spark marks all tasks on that executor as failed and reschedules them on surviving executors (up to spark.task.maxFailures retries). Streaming checkpoints and stateful operator state stores survive executor eviction as long as the driver and checkpoint storage remain intact. Driver node eviction is catastrophic — terminates the entire job; driver should always run on-demand. Executor eviction during a MERGE or INSERT OVERWRITE write causes the transaction to fail and be retried from the start (Delta's ACID rollback).
summary: System: Spot instance preemption mechanics, SPOT_WITH_FALLBACK mode, Spark fault tolerance on executor eviction

label: Choose spot vs on-demand based on workload type and fault tolerance
content: Use spot workers for: scheduled batch jobs (bronze ingestion, silver transforms, gold rollups) where the job is idempotent (Delta MERGE, INSERT OVERWRITE) and can be retried from the beginning on eviction; DLT triggered pipelines in production mode (DLT retries automatically on executor loss); one-off historical backfills and exploration clusters where cost matters more than completion time. Use on-demand for: the driver node always — driver eviction terminates the job with no recovery; Structured Streaming always-on continuous pipelines where spot eviction during a micro-batch forces checkpoint replay and causes latency spikes in low-latency SLA scenarios (e.g., <1 minute end-to-end latency); stateful streaming jobs with large window or join state stores where eviction causes expensive state rehydration from checkpoint; jobs with external side effects (REST API calls, Kafka produces) that cannot be safely retried. Mixed mode recommendation: SPOT_WITH_FALLBACK on worker nodes + on-demand driver covers most batch workloads — set max_spot_bid_price=-1 (on-demand ceiling) to avoid bid failures. Increase spark.task.maxFailures from 4 to 8 on spot worker clusters to tolerate eviction-induced task failures.
summary: Decision: spot workers for idempotent batch/DLT; on-demand driver always; on-demand workers for continuous streaming

label: Spot instance cluster configuration patterns for batch and streaming workloads
content:
# Azure: SPOT_WITH_FALLBACK workers + on-demand driver (batch job cluster)
# {
#   "node_type_id": "Standard_DS4_v2",
#   "driver_node_type_id": "Standard_DS4_v2",
#   "autoscale": {"min_workers": 2, "max_workers": 16},
#   "azure_attributes": {
#     "availability": "SPOT_WITH_FALLBACK_AZURE",
#     "spot_bid_max_price": -1   // -1 = bid up to on-demand price (recommended)
#   },
#   "spark_conf": {
#     "spark.task.maxFailures": "8",          // tolerate more transient executor losses
#     "spark.stage.maxConsecutiveAttempts": "8"
#   }
# }

# AWS: SPOT_WITH_FALLBACK workers + 1 on-demand worker at min (batch)
# {
#   "node_type_id": "r5d.2xlarge",
#   "driver_node_type_id": "r5d.2xlarge",
#   "autoscale": {"min_workers": 2, "max_workers": 16},
#   "aws_attributes": {
#     "availability": "SPOT_WITH_FALLBACK",
#     "spot_bid_price_percent": 100,           // bid up to on-demand price
#     "first_on_demand": 1                    // keep 1 on-demand worker as fallback
#   }
# }

# Streaming: on-demand workers to prevent micro-batch disruption
# {
#   "node_type_id": "Standard_DS4_v2",
#   "num_workers": 4,
#   "azure_attributes": {"availability": "ON_DEMAND_AZURE"}
# }

# Python: programmatically check if a cluster ran on spot (from Spark UI API)
import requests
HOST  = 'https://<workspace>.azuredatabricks.net'
TOKEN = dbutils.secrets.get('kv', 'databricks-pat')
resp  = requests.get(f'{HOST}/api/2.0/clusters/get',
    headers={'Authorization': f'Bearer {TOKEN}'},
    params={'cluster_id': spark.conf.get('spark.databricks.clusterUsageTags.clusterId')})
print(resp.json().get('azure_attributes', {}).get('availability'))

# Monitor spot eviction impact:
# Spark UI > Executors > Removed > reason='ExecutorLostFailure' indicates eviction
# If eviction rate >5% of executor-hours, switch to on-demand for that job
summary: Output: Spot cluster config JSON for Azure/AWS batch and streaming, maxFailures tuning, and eviction monitoring

label: Caching layers: Delta cache (disk), Spark cache (memory), and CACHE TABLE
content: Databricks provides three caching layers. (1) Delta cache (disk cache): caches Parquet/Delta file blocks read from cloud storage (ADLS/S3/GCS) onto local NVMe SSDs of worker nodes. Transparent — no code change required; Databricks automatically caches recently accessed files. Persists across Spark sessions on the same running cluster. Enabled by default on nodes with local SSDs (Azure L-series, AWS d/i-series, GCP local SSD types). Configured via spark.databricks.io.cache.enabled (default true on supported nodes) and spark.databricks.io.cache.maxDiskUsage (max SSD space to use). (2) Spark cache (memory cache): df.cache() or df.persist() stores a deserialized or serialized DataFrame in executor JVM heap or off-heap memory. Lazy — materializes on first action. Evicted by GC pressure or when memory fills. Lost on cluster restart. (3) CACHE TABLE (SQL): caches a table or query result in Spark memory (equivalent to df.cache() on the table scan). Session-scoped in interactive notebooks; shared across sessions on a SQL warehouse for the same table.
summary: System: Three caching layers — Delta disk cache (SSD, transparent), Spark df.cache() (memory, explicit), CACHE TABLE (SQL)

label: Choose caching layer based on data size, reuse frequency, and workload type
content: Use Delta cache (automatic, no code): always prefer on clusters with local SSDs for repeated reads of the same Delta tables — dashboards, interactive exploration, ML feature preparation with multiple iterations over the same feature table. Delta cache persists across sessions and delivers near-SSD latency without consuming executor JVM heap. Verify the cluster node type supports local SSDs; if not, Delta cache is inactive. Use Spark df.cache() when: a complex transformation result (expensive join + filter) is reused 2+ times in the same pipeline run and fits within 40% of available executor memory — rule of thumb: only cache DataFrames smaller than (executor_RAM × num_workers × 0.4). Unpersist explicitly with df.unpersist() immediately after the last use to reclaim heap. Avoid df.cache() for: DataFrames larger than 50% of executor memory (OOM/GC pressure), DataFrames used only once (cache overhead outweighs savings), streaming DataFrames (cache invalidated per micro-batch). Use CACHE TABLE for: interactive sessions querying the same table 10+ times in a notebook; gold tables accessed by BI dashboards via SQL warehouses (SQL warehouses cache at the warehouse level, benefiting all sessions). Delta cache vs Spark cache: Delta cache survives session restarts and requires no heap; Spark cache is faster for in-memory (sub-millisecond) access but requires heap allocation and is lost on restart.
summary: Decision: Delta cache for repeated Delta reads (SSD, no code); df.cache() for reused transforms <40% executor memory

label: Caching code and SQL patterns: Delta cache verification, df.cache, CACHE TABLE
content:
from pyspark import StorageLevel

# --- Delta cache: verify it is active ---
print(spark.conf.get('spark.databricks.io.cache.enabled'))   # 'true' on SSD nodes
print(spark.conf.get('spark.databricks.io.cache.maxDiskUsage'))  # bytes of SSD allocated

# Delta cache accelerates repeated reads transparently — no code needed
# First read: fetches from ADLS/S3
result1 = spark.table('silver.orders').filter('region = "APAC"').count()
# Second read: served from local NVMe SSD (Delta cache hit)
result2 = spark.table('silver.orders').filter('order_date >= "2024-01-01"').count()

# --- Spark df.cache(): multi-use transform result ---
# Cache a complex base DataFrame used in 2+ downstream aggregations
base_df = (
    spark.table('silver.orders')
    .join(spark.table('gold.dim_customer'), on='customer_id', how='left')
    .filter('order_date >= current_date() - INTERVAL 90 DAYS')
    .select('order_id', 'customer_id', 'region', 'segment', 'amount', 'order_date')
)
base_df.cache()       # lazy — materializes on first action
base_df.count()       # trigger materialization

# Two downstream uses — cache avoids double recompute of the join
daily_agg    = base_df.groupBy('order_date', 'region').agg({'amount': 'sum'})
customer_agg = base_df.groupBy('customer_id').agg({'amount': 'sum', 'order_id': 'count'})
daily_agg.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').saveAsTable('gold.daily_summary')
customer_agg.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').saveAsTable('gold.customer_summary')
base_df.unpersist()   # release heap memory immediately after last use

# Storage level options:
# df.persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK)  — heap first, spill to disk (default)
# df.persist(StorageLevel.DISK_ONLY)        — no JVM heap pressure; slower access
# df.persist(StorageLevel.OFF_HEAP)         — Tungsten off-heap; avoids GC

# Check cache size in Spark UI > Storage tab
# Alternatively:
spark.sparkContext.statusTracker().getActiveJobIds()  # all active jobs

# --- CACHE TABLE: SQL-based session caching ---
spark.sql('CACHE TABLE silver.orders')        # lazy cache
spark.sql('CACHE LAZY TABLE silver.orders')   # explicit lazy (same behavior)

# Cache a filtered subset (useful for large tables with common filter)
spark.sql('''
  CACHE TABLE silver_orders_today
  SELECT * FROM silver.orders WHERE order_date = current_date()
''')

# Verify cached tables
spark.sql('SHOW CACHED TABLES').show()

# Release cache when finished
spark.sql('UNCACHE TABLE silver.orders')
summary: Output: Delta cache verification, df.cache() multi-use pattern with unpersist, and CACHE TABLE SQL patterns

label: Partition pruning, predicate pushdown, and Delta data skipping mechanics
content: Three complementary mechanisms reduce data scanned by a query on partitioned Delta tables. (1) Partition pruning (directory-level): when a query filter references a partition column (PARTITIONED BY in Delta DDL), Spark eliminates entire partition directories from the scan before opening any files — most impactful, O(1) cost. (2) Predicate pushdown (Parquet row-group level): Spark pushes WHERE predicates to the Parquet reader, which uses Parquet column statistics (min/max, null count per row group, ~128MB groups) to skip row groups that cannot match the filter. Enabled by default via spark.sql.parquet.filterPushdown=true. (3) Delta data skipping (file-level): Delta Lake maintains per-file min/max and null-count statistics for indexed columns (configurable via delta.dataSkippingNumIndexedCols, default 32). Queries with equality or range filters on indexed columns skip files whose min/max range excludes the filter value. OPTIMIZE + ZORDER BY narrows min/max ranges per file by co-locating similar values, dramatically improving skipping effectiveness. Liquid clustering provides equivalent skipping with incremental re-clustering. All three mechanisms are visible in query EXPLAIN output and Spark UI filesRead metrics.
summary: System: Partition pruning (dir-level), predicate pushdown (Parquet row-group), Delta data skipping (file-level) mechanics

label: Choose partition columns, write pruning-friendly predicates, and verify data skipping
content: Partition column selection: choose the column that appears in WHERE or JOIN predicates in >80% of queries and has low-to-medium cardinality (date at day grain is ideal — DATE or YYYYMMDD INT; avoid customer_id, product_id, or any column with >10k distinct values that would create millions of small files). One partition column per table is typical; compound partitioning (year + month) is acceptable when queries always filter both levels. Never partition on high-cardinality strings or columns never filtered. Predicate pushdown rules: write filter conditions that reference the partition column directly without wrapping expressions — WHERE order_date = '2024-01-15' enables pruning; WHERE YEAR(order_date) = 2024 does NOT (function wrapper prevents directory-level pushdown). Use BETWEEN for date ranges: WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-12-31'. For data skipping: ZORDER on 2–4 high-selectivity non-partition columns that appear frequently in WHERE clauses (customer_id, region, status) — ZORDER narrows file min/max ranges. Increase delta.dataSkippingNumIndexedCols beyond 32 only for wide tables where filter columns fall beyond position 32. Verify skipping: examine numFilesSkipped in query metrics; if 0 files skipped on a filter, the column lacks statistics (run ANALYZE TABLE or OPTIMIZE to generate them).
summary: Decision: Partition on low-cardinality date columns; reference partition col directly (no functions); ZORDER on 2-4 filter cols

label: Partition pruning verification and predicate pushdown code patterns
content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, year, lit

# Assume silver.orders PARTITIONED BY (order_date DATE)
# and ZORDER BY (region, product_category) applied

# --- CORRECT: direct partition column reference → pruning active ---
pruned = spark.table('silver.orders').filter(col('order_date') == '2024-01-15')
pruned.explain(True)
# Look for: PartitionFilters: [isnotnull(order_date#N), (order_date#N = 2024-01-15)]
# FileScan shows numFiles = 1 (only the matching partition)

# CORRECT: range filter
ranged = spark.sql('''
  SELECT * FROM silver.orders
  WHERE order_date BETWEEN \'2024-01-01\' AND \'2024-01-31\'
''')

# INCORRECT: function wrapper prevents directory-level pruning
bad = spark.sql('SELECT * FROM silver.orders WHERE YEAR(order_date) = 2024')
# Fix: replace with explicit range
good = spark.sql("""
  SELECT * FROM silver.orders
  WHERE order_date >= '2024-01-01' AND order_date < '2025-01-01'
""")

# INCORRECT: cast prevents pruning
bad2 = spark.table('silver.orders').filter(
    col('order_date').cast('string').startswith('2024')   # cast → no pruning
)

# --- Verify data skipping with EXPLAIN ---
spark.sql("""
  EXPLAIN SELECT SUM(amount) FROM silver.orders
  WHERE order_date = '2024-01-15' AND region = 'APAC'
""").show(truncate=False)
# Look for: numFiles (files scanned) vs numFilesSkipped (data skipping)
# High numFilesSkipped after ZORDER BY region = effective data skipping

# --- Verify statistics exist on indexed columns ---
spark.sql('DESCRIBE DETAIL silver.orders').select('numFiles', 'sizeInBytes').show()

# Generate column statistics (needed for data skipping without OPTIMIZE)
spark.sql('ANALYZE TABLE silver.orders COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR COLUMNS region, product_category')

# Increase indexed column count for wide tables
spark.sql("""
  ALTER TABLE silver.orders
  SET TBLPROPERTIES ('delta.dataSkippingNumIndexedCols' = '50')
""")

# MERGE: include partition column in ON clause for pruning
spark.sql("""
  MERGE INTO silver.orders AS t
  USING source_batch AS s
  ON t.order_id = s.order_id
     AND t.order_date = s.order_date   -- partition column enables partition pruning
  WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET *
  WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT *
""")

# Check partition list and file count distribution
spark.sql("""
  SELECT order_date, COUNT(*) AS files
  FROM (SELECT order_date FROM silver.orders GROUP BY order_date)
  ORDER BY order_date DESC LIMIT 30
""")
# More than ~500MB of files per partition → consider OPTIMIZE to compact
summary: Output: Partition pruning EXPLAIN verification, correct vs incorrect predicate patterns, data skipping tuning, MERGE pruning

label: Choose apply_as_deletes vs is_deleted=TRUE upsert for CDC delete events in DLT SCD2
content: apply_as_deletes / APPLY AS DELETE WHEN: DLT sets __END_AT on the matched row. No active current row remains for that key (WHERE __END_AT IS NULL returns zero rows for the key). The historical row is retained for time-travel queries. Use when: delete means 'this dimension member no longer exists' and downstream joins should find no current record. is_deleted=TRUE soft-delete upsert (PREFERRED for SCD Type 2 compliance use cases): do NOT use apply_as_deletes. Instead, in the normalized bronze or preprocessing DLT table, transform op='d' events into a regular row: populate all business-key and attribute columns from the 'before' image, and add is_deleted=TRUE. Pass this through dlt.apply_changes() without apply_as_deletes — DLT processes it as a normal upsert, creating a new SCD2 version with __END_AT=NULL and is_deleted=TRUE. A surviving active current row exists for the key (WHERE __END_AT IS NULL), and the previous version's __END_AT is set to the sequence value of the delete event. This is fully compatible with DLT-native APPLY CHANGES — foreachBatch MERGE is NOT required for soft-delete semantics. Use when: compliance or downstream consumers require a living current record per key indicating deletion state (GDPR audit, CDC fan-out, masking pipelines). Never combine both patterns on the same pipeline — apply_as_deletes and is_deleted=TRUE upsert conflict.
summary: Decision: apply_as_deletes eliminates the surviving current row for a key; is_deleted=TRUE upsert preserves a current flagged row — choose based on whether deleted keys need a surviving active record

label: Handle late-arriving CDC events with Structured Streaming watermarks in DLT
content: For late-arriving CDC events in DLT, use Structured Streaming watermarks — NOT Kafka consumer options. Apply .withWatermark('event_time_col', '2 hours') on the streaming DataFrame in the bronze or preprocessing table before stateful operations. DLT APPLY CHANGES SEQUENCE BY already handles within-batch out-of-order events: it sorts events by the SEQUENCE BY column before applying the MERGE, so events arriving late within the same micro-batch are correctly ordered. For cross-batch late arrivals, the watermark on the upstream streaming table controls how long state is retained before a window closes. maxDelayInSeconds is NOT a valid Spark Kafka connector option — it does not exist and must never be used. Valid Spark Kafka connector options related to throughput and positioning: maxOffsetsPerTrigger (batch size throttle), startingOffsets (initial position), failOnDataLoss. For 2-hour late arrival tolerance: .withWatermark('ts_ms_as_timestamp', '2 hours') on the streaming source before aggregations.
summary: Decision: use .withWatermark() on the streaming source for late-arrival tolerance; DLT SEQUENCE BY handles within-batch reordering automatically; maxDelayInSeconds is invalid and does not exist in Spark Kafka connector

label: DLT APPLY CHANGES SCD2 with is_deleted soft-delete and effective_date view aliasing
content:
import dlt
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, from_json, to_timestamp, expr
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, LongType

# Bronze: parse Debezium envelope from Kafka, apply 2-hour watermark
DEBEZIUM_SCHEMA = StructType([
    StructField('op', StringType()),
    StructField('ts_ms', LongType()),
    StructField('before', StructType([StructField('customer_id',StringType()),StructField('name',StringType()),StructField('region',StringType()),StructField('segment',StringType()),StructField('is_deleted',StringType())])),
    StructField('after',  StructType([StructField('customer_id',StringType()),StructField('name',StringType()),StructField('region',StringType()),StructField('segment',StringType()),StructField('is_deleted',StringType())]))
])

@dlt.table(name='customers_bronze')
def customers_bronze():
    return (
        spark.readStream.format('kafka')
        .option('kafka.bootstrap.servers', 'broker:9092')
        .option('subscribe', 'dbserver1.public.customers')
        .option('startingOffsets', 'latest')
        .load()
        .select(from_json(col('value').cast('string'), DEBEZIUM_SCHEMA).alias('e'))
        .select(col('e.*'))
        .withColumn('ts', to_timestamp(col('ts_ms') / 1000.0))
        .withWatermark('ts', '2 hours')  # late-arrival tolerance
    )

# Normalize: flatten envelope; for op='d', emit is_deleted=TRUE from 'before' image
@dlt.table(name='customers_cdc_normalized')
def customers_cdc_normalized():
    return (
        dlt.read_stream('customers_bronze')
        .select(
            col('op'),
            col('ts_ms'),
            expr("CASE WHEN op IN ('c','u','r') THEN after ELSE before END").alias('payload'),
            expr("op = 'd'").alias('is_deleted_flag')
        )
        .select(
            col('op'), col('ts_ms'),
            col('payload.customer_id'),
            col('payload.name'),
            col('payload.region'),
            col('payload.segment'),
            col('is_deleted_flag').alias('is_deleted')  # TRUE for delete events
        )
    )

# Silver SCD2 target
dlt.create_streaming_table(
    name='dim_customers_scd2',
    comment='SCD Type 2 customers — is_deleted soft-delete, history tracked by DLT'
)

dlt.apply_changes(
    target='dim_customers_scd2',
    source='customers_cdc_normalized',
    keys=['customer_id'],
    sequence_by='ts_ms',          # DLT handles out-of-order within and across batches
    stored_as_scd_type=2,
    # No apply_as_deletes — deletes become is_deleted=TRUE upserts, not row expirations
    except_column_list=['op'],
    track_history_column_list=['name', 'region', 'segment', 'is_deleted']
)

# View exposing conventional effective_date/end_date for BI consumers
@dlt.view(name='dim_customers')
def dim_customers():
    return spark.sql("""
        SELECT
            customer_id, name, region, segment, is_deleted,
            (__END_AT IS NULL AND NOT is_deleted) AS is_current,
            CAST(__START_AT / 1000 AS TIMESTAMP)::DATE AS effective_date,
            CASE WHEN __END_AT IS NULL THEN DATE '9999-12-31'
                 ELSE CAST(__END_AT / 1000 AS TIMESTAMP)::DATE - INTERVAL 1 DAY
            END AS end_date
        FROM LIVE.dim_customers_scd2
    """)

# Query patterns:
# Current active rows:      WHERE is_current = TRUE
# Soft-deleted records:     WHERE is_deleted = TRUE AND __END_AT IS NULL
# Point-in-time (2024-06-15): WHERE effective_date <= '2024-06-15' AND end_date >= '2024-06-15'
summary: Output: Complete DLT APPLY CHANGES SCD Type 2 pipeline for Debezium/Kafka CDC — op='d' mapped to is_deleted=TRUE upsert (no apply_as_deletes), 2-hour watermark on bronze, and a @dlt.view aliasing __START_AT/__END_AT to effective_date/end_date for BI consumers

label: Late-arriving fact SCD2 join with customer-not-in-dim fallback and self-healing MERGE
content:
-- Self-healing: re-resolve facts that previously landed with customer_sk = -1
-- CRITICAL DESIGN NOTE: The self-healing re-reads from bronze.orders_partner_feed (not from
-- fact_orders itself) so that customer_id (natural key) is available for the dim re-join.
-- Do NOT store only customer_sk in fact_orders if self-healing is required — either:
--   (a) re-read the original source batch (this pattern), OR
--   (b) store customer_id alongside customer_sk in fact_orders as a resolution aid column.
-- Mixing approach (b) with surrogate-key-only DDL causes a schema inconsistency.
WHEN MATCHED AND t.customer_sk = -1 AND s.customer_sk <> -1 THEN
  UPDATE SET customer_sk = s.customer_sk;

-- After self-healing converges, surface unresolved rows for data quality alerting:
-- SELECT order_id, order_timestamp FROM gold.fact_orders
-- WHERE customer_sk = -1 AND order_timestamp < current_timestamp() - INTERVAL 14 DAYS;
-- Dead-letter / quarantine: move persistently unresolved rows to a quarantine table.
summary: Output: Late-arriving fact MERGE with LEFT JOIN point-in-time dim lookup (TIMESTAMP-safe boundaries), customer_sk=-1 fallback, self-healing WHEN MATCHED UPDATE re-reading from bronze source (not fact_orders) to avoid needing customer_id in fact DDL, and dead-letter pattern for persistently unresolved rows

label: Decide whether to store natural key in fact table alongside surrogate key for late-arrival self-healing
content: Store natural key (e.g., customer_id) in fact_orders alongside customer_sk when: self-healing of customer_sk=-1 rows must be triggered from the fact table directly (without re-reading the original source batch), compliance requires the original identifier in the fact for audit, or the source batch is not retained beyond the ingestion window. Do NOT store natural key in fact table when: the original source batch is retained (e.g., bronze Delta table with time-travel or a partitioned landing zone) and self-healing can re-read from source — this keeps fact_orders purely surrogate-keyed and avoids DDL inconsistency. CRITICAL: choose one approach and enforce it in DDL — the schema inconsistency of referencing a natural key column in self-healing SQL that is absent from the CREATE TABLE statement is a faithfulness error that cascades into incorrect downstream joins. If natural key is stored, mark it NOT NULL and document it as a resolution aid, not a dimension join key.
summary: Decision: store customer_id in fact table for self-healing if source batch is not retained; re-read from bronze source if it is — never reference a natural key column in self-healing SQL if it is absent from the fact DDL

label: Late-arriving fact quarantine (SK=-1) and backfill MERGE code pattern
content:
-- Static unknown-member row (insert once at schema creation)
INSERT INTO gold.dim_customer (customer_sk, customer_id, name, region, segment, row_hash, effective_ts, end_ts, is_current)
VALUES (-1, 'UNKNOWN', 'Unknown Customer', 'Unknown', 'Unknown', 'UNKNOWN', TIMESTAMP '1900-01-01 00:00:00', TIMESTAMP '9999-12-31 00:00:00', TRUE);

-- Phase 1: Stage late facts, quarantine unresolvable rows with customer_sk = -1
MERGE INTO gold.fact_orders AS f
USING (
  SELECT
    s.order_id,
    s.customer_id,          -- retained for Phase 2 re-resolution
    s.order_timestamp,
    s.amount,
    s.report_date,
    COALESCE(d.customer_sk, -1) AS customer_sk   -- -1 = unknown member
  FROM stg_orders_late s
  LEFT JOIN gold.dim_customer d
    ON s.customer_id = d.customer_id
   AND s.order_timestamp >= d.effective_ts
   AND s.order_timestamp <  d.end_ts
) AS src
ON f.order_id = src.order_id
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN
  INSERT (order_id, customer_id, customer_sk, order_timestamp, amount, report_date)
  VALUES (src.order_id, src.customer_id, src.customer_sk, src.order_timestamp, src.amount, src.report_date);

-- Phase 2: Backfill MERGE — run after each dim_customer load to resolve quarantined rows
-- Prerequisite: customer_id must be stored in fact_orders (see neuron #549)
MERGE INTO gold.fact_orders AS f
USING (
  SELECT fo.order_id, d.customer_sk AS resolved_sk
  FROM gold.fact_orders fo
  JOIN gold.dim_customer d
    ON fo.customer_id = d.customer_id
   AND fo.order_timestamp >= d.effective_ts
   AND fo.order_timestamp <  d.end_ts
  WHERE fo.customer_sk = -1   -- only fix quarantined rows
    AND d.customer_sk <> -1
) AS resolved
ON f.order_id = resolved.order_id AND f.customer_sk = -1
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET f.customer_sk = resolved.resolved_sk;

-- Monitor quarantine queue
SELECT COUNT(*) AS still_unresolved FROM gold.fact_orders WHERE customer_sk = -1;
summary: Output: Two-phase late-arriving fact resolution — Phase 1 writes facts with customer_sk=-1 (unknown member) when no dim version matches; Phase 2 backfill MERGE re-resolves quarantined rows after next dim load using half-open interval join

label: Time-anchor fact joins to SCD Type 2 effective_dates for consistent YoY attribution
content: When comparing YoY metrics across dimensional reclassifications, join fact tables to dimensions at the historical effective_date that was active during the measurement period. For example, when computing current-year ROI by strategic pillar, join current-year facts to the program dimension snapshot as it existed one year prior, not the current dimension state. This ensures programs retain their original pillar assignment in YoY comparisons, preventing double-counting when programs are reclassified mid-fiscal-year. Implement via WHERE clause filtering: WHERE dimension.effective_date <= fact.measurement_date AND dimension.end_date > fact.measurement_date. This pattern decouples the analytical baseline (immutable historical classification) from current operational reality. When querying YoY comparisons, use a CTE to lock the prior-year dimension snapshot (WHERE effective_date <= DATEADD(YEAR, -1, CURRENT_DATE) AND end_date > DATEADD(YEAR, -1, CURRENT_DATE)), then join both current and prior-year facts to that same snapshot to ensure consistent attribution.
summary: System: Join facts to historical dimension snapshots at SCD Type 2 effective_date ranges to prevent double-counting when programs reclassify mid-fiscal-year; enables YoY comparisons with consistent historical attribution

label: Add classification_version FK in facts to trace measurements to SCD Type 2 reclassification versions
content: To maintain audit trails for dimensional changes, add a classification_version integer column to fact tables that logically foreign-keys to the version counter in slowly-changing dimensions. When a program reclassifies via SCD Type 2 MERGE, increment the classification_version in the new dimension row. At measurement time, capture the current classification_version in the fact row. This enables retrospective analysis: facts can be grouped by classification_version to understand how metrics evolved across different reclassification events. DESCRIBE HISTORY on the dimension table (with delta.enableChangeDataFeed=true) shows which user/timestamp/classification_reason created each version, providing full audit compliance. This pattern separates 'which version of the classification applied to this measurement' (tracked in fact via FK) from 'what was the pillar for this program right now' (tracked in current dimension row via is_current=TRUE).
summary: System: Add classification_version FK column in facts; incremented via SCD Type 2 on reclassification; enables audit trail linking facts to specific dimensional change events

label: Isolate what-if scenario analysis in separate bronze staging tables, never join to production facts
content: For exploratory what-if analyses (e.g., division heads testing alternative strategic allocations), maintain a separate bronze-layer scenario allocation table that is structurally parallel to the production dimension but is never joined directly to production fact tables. Instead, query scenarios independently against the same fact table, producing separate result sets side-by-side (SELECT ... FROM actual_dimension UNION SELECT ... FROM scenario_staging), enabling easy visual comparison without risk of speculative data leaking into production dashboards. Scenario tables include scenario_id, scenario_strategic_pillar, scenario_status (draft, approved, applied), and scenario_created_by for workflow tracking. Once an executive approves a scenario, promote it to production via a MERGE into the actual dimension table, recording the approval as a classification_reason and incrementing classification_version for audit compliance. This dual-table pattern isolates exploration from analytics, enabling risk-free what-if modeling while maintaining production data integrity.
summary: System: Maintain separate bronze scenario staging tables (never joined to facts) for what-if analyses; promote approved scenarios to production via SCD Type 2 MERGE; prevents speculative allocations from corrupting production ROI

label: Production Scheduler
content: Production Scheduler in Manufacturing & Operations. Responsible for demand forecasting, production schedule coordination, and Kanban WIP-level management. Publish rolling demand forecast 12+ weeks in advance to inform Kanban card-count adjustments and supplier procurement planning windows. Monitor actual demand variability (std. dev.) and process lead-time distribution to enable Manufacturing Engineering and Supply Chain to tune Kanban parameters and request process improvements. Coordinate demand-planning cycle with Supply Chain (supplier lead-time windows), Manufacturing Engineering (lead-time measurement and improvement priorities), and Operations (shift capacity, changeover scheduling). Execute tiered Kanban adjustments (red/yellow/green demand tiers); escalate capacity constraints (queue depth, shift starvation) to VP Operations for approval of investments or outsourcing decisions.
summary: Role: Production Scheduler in Manufacturing & Operations

label: Gauge R&R Study Execution for Self-Inspection Qualification
content: CALCULATION (per AIAG MSA 4th Ed): Repeatability (EV) = Rbar_equipment × K1 where K1 depends on number of replicates (K1=0.5908 for 2 reps, 0.5231 for 3 reps). Reproducibility (OV) = √[(Xbar_diff × K2)² - (EV²/(n×r))] where K2 depends on number of operators (K2=0.7071 for 2 ops, 0.5231 for 3 ops). Note: d2=1.693 applies ONLY to subgroup size n=2; use appropriate d2* values from AIAG tables for other sizes. %Tolerance = (5.15 × GRR) ÷ Tolerance × 100% where GRR = √(EV² + OV²). ACCEPTANCE THRESHOLDS (AIAG): <10% = ACCEPT; 10-30% = CONDITIONAL (may be acceptable depending on application, cost of measurement device, cost of rework); >30% = REJECT. Aerospace practice: <10% preferred for safety-critical dimensions; 10-20% acceptable with documented justification for non-SC features. The 5.15 multiplier represents 99% of measurement variation (±2.575σ); some standards use 6σ (5.15 vs 6.0 — verify which convention your customer requires).
summary: Step-by-step Gage R&R protocol with sample sizes, operator selection, stratification strategy, and quantitative acceptance thresholds for aerospace self-inspection qualification.

label: Operator Reproducibility Root Cause Diagnosis & Resolution
content: RETRAINING PROTOCOL (Low-Cost First): Hands-on demo using known-value part showing measurement variance; laminated station procedure card with photos of correct positioning, contact angle, and clamping force; tactile feedback training (teach consistent contact pressure). Retest Gage R&R within 24-48 hours with same study design. Expected outcome: OV improvement varies significantly by root cause — technique standardization typically yields meaningful improvement when variation stems from inconsistent procedures rather than fundamental skill gaps. If OV still exceeds target after retraining: (1) Fixture redesign to reduce operator influence on part positioning (eliminates largest OV contributor when fixturing is the issue), (2) If EV low but OV persists despite fixture improvement, evaluate whether measurement method requires less operator-dependent approach (e.g., automated contact, digital readout vs. analog). EQUIPMENT INVESTIGATION: If EV high (repeatability poor despite correct operator technique), upgrade measurement tool — verify with 5-point linearity check across tolerance range before committing to purchase.
summary: Observable diagnostic checklist for isolating operator technique variation; includes specific video observation targets, low-cost retraining approach with 24-hour success metrics, and decision thresholds for equipment vs. training investment.

label: I-MR Control Charting for Operator Self-Inspection Measurement Control
content: IMPLEMENTATION: Post Individual & Moving Range (I-MR) control chart at each operator station. Plot measurements in production sequence; operator plots immediately after measuring. CONTROL LIMIT CALCULATION: Collect 25-30 part baseline (post-operator-proficiency). Center Line (CL) = X-bar (mean of baseline individuals). MR-bar = average of consecutive 2-point moving ranges. σ_hat = MR-bar / d2 (d2=1.128 for span-2 MR). Upper Control Limit = X-bar + 2.66 × MR-bar; Lower Control Limit = X-bar - 2.66 × MR-bar. NOTE: Use X-bar (process mean), NOT nominal — nominal-based limits are valid only if process is centered on nominal. If |X-bar - Nominal| > 0.5σ, investigate centering before locking limits. OUT-OF-CONTROL SIGNALS: (1) Any point beyond UCL/LCL (3σ), (2) 2-of-3 consecutive points beyond 2σ (Western Electric Zone A), (3) Trend of 6 consecutive increasing or decreasing, (4) Run of 8 on same side of CL, (5) 4-of-5 beyond 1σ same side. INTERVENTION PROTOCOL: Signal detected → (1) Re-measure part 2× independent positioning to confirm, (2) Gage zero-check on calibrated master, (3) Fixture visual inspection (clamps, pins, wear, chips), (4) If unresolved, escalate to QA. MONTHLY RE-VALIDATION: Recalculate MR-bar from prior 30-day data; compare to baseline. If MR-bar increased >25%, investigate measurement system or process change. If process mean shifted >1σ toward spec limit, trigger preventive action. DOCUMENTATION: Control chart with operator initials, corrective action log with dates/root causes. Monthly management review per AS9100D §9.1.3.
summary: Practical I-MR charting procedure with specific control limits, out-of-control detection rules, stop-work thresholds, and root cause investigation triggers for real-time measurement control during self-inspection operation.

label: Stratified Work Sampling Study Design
content: STUDY DESIGN PROTOCOL: Conduct 10-day minimum baseline with 3+ operators per shift, all shifts represented (day, night if applicable), mixed experience levels tracked separately (<3 months newly hired; >6 months experienced). PART QUALITY STRATIFICATION: Pre-audit 50+ units for incoming dimensions; classify into 3 bins—Bin A (±25% nominal, best incoming quality), Bin B (±50% nominal, typical), Bin C (near LSL/USL, worst). Randomly assign parts within bins to operators—prevents cherry-picking bias that inflates or obscures true variation. CYCLE TIME CAPTURE (stop-watch method with element-level breakdown): Record separately—Value-Added (assembly, fastening, measurement), Setup/Unavoidable (clamp per process design), Non-Value-Added (reach >12", reposition, tool search, rework, delay). Use standardized data sheet: operator name, part quality bin, incoming dimension status, element times (seconds), fixture/technique notes. RANDOMIZATION CRITICAL: Spread observations across calendar days, not compressed into 2 days—shift fatigue, setup variation, and incoming part timing distribution affect baseline validity. SAMPLE SIZE: 5-10 stable cycles per operator-bin combination minimum; exclude first 1-2 cycles per setup (setup learning) and obvious disruptions (machine jam). STRATIFICATION OUTCOME: Enables Phase 2 decomposition to calculate 'part quality loss factor' (Bin C vs. Bin A time delta) separately from 'operator technique spread' (fastest vs. slowest operator on identical Bin A parts), proving which variation source dominates improvement opportunity.
summary: Baseline data collection protocol using stratified sampling across part quality levels (3 bins) and operator experience, with element-level cycle time capture and randomized multi-day scheduling to distinguish part-quality variation from operator-technique variation.

label: Westinghouse Performance Rating & PFD Allowance Calculation
content: OBSERVED TIME SELECTION: Use mean of stable cycles from operator at 70th percentile of experience threshold (not first 10 cycles—learning variability too high; not fastest 5% of dataset—unrepresentative of normal sustained pace). For operators <3 months, use Week-4 stabilized data only, excluding Weeks 1-3 learning ramp. WESTINGHOUSE RATING SCALE (reference = 1.0, normal pace): 0.90-0.95 (slower than normal: hesitation, careful approach, newer operators), 1.0 (normal steady rhythm, stable coordination), 1.05-1.10 (faster than normal: fluid motions, experienced, high coordination). Assign conservatively: 0.95 for operators <3 months, 1.0 for experienced >6 months unless video observation documents measurable over/under-performance. PFD ALLOWANCES (Personal-Fatigue-Delay cumulative adders): Personal 5% (breaks, hydration), Fatigue 5% (precision assembly standing 8 hrs), Delays 3% (unavoidable machine transitions, upstream waits). Total multiplier = 1.0 + (0.05 + 0.05 + 0.03) = 1.13 (+13% adder). FORMULA: Adjusted Standard Time (sec) = Observed Time × Performance Rating × PFD Multiplier. EXAMPLE: Observed 52 sec on Bin B parts, rating 1.0, multiplier 1.13 → Standard = 52 × 1.0 × 1.13 = 58.8 sec (round to 59 sec). VERIFICATION: Calculate capability index Cpk after rollout using first-month stable-operator data to confirm standard allows 95%+ of operators to meet or exceed standard, validating appropriateness (Cpk >1.33 indicates standard not inflated).
