summary: Methodology for adjusting observed cycle times to defensible standards using Westinghouse performance rating (0.90-1.10 scale) and combined PFD allowances (+13% typical), preventing inflated benchmarks by grounding standards in operator capability and operating reality.

label: Operator Learning Curve Tracking & Standard Time Validation
content: LEARNING PROGRESSION BASELINE (establish during initial work sampling using new-hire cohorts): Week 1: 85-90% of experienced operator standard, high variability (Std Dev 15-20% of mean cycle time). Week 2-3: 90-95% standard, declining variability (Std Dev 8-12%). Week 4+: 95-100% standard, stable and repeatable (Std Dev <5%). ACTION RULES: (1) Do NOT apply performance accountability to operators in Weeks 1-3; use this period for training assessment and role suitability evaluation only. Week 4 data is first formal evaluation of standard achievement. (2) Collect Week 4+ cycle time data across 1-2 weeks to confirm stability (not single-day snapshot). If operator averages <95% of standard by Week 4 completion, investigate root cause: inadequate training? role unsuitable? fixture design issue? (3) Experienced operators (>6 months): If consistently >10% above standard for 2+ weeks despite documented stability, trigger motion observation and retraining. (4) Learning curve separation prevents 'inflated benchmarks' by: (a) Never pooling Week-1-3 learning data with Week-4+ stable data in standard calculation, (b) Setting initial targets for new-hire cohorts at 90% of standard (realistic Week 2-3 expectation), (c) Advancing to 100% standard target only post-Week-4 certification. DOCUMENTATION: Record hire date, performance data by week (Week 1-4, then stable phase), certification date (end of Week 4 if standard met), trainer/cohort (identifies training batch). Use for monthly performance review—new operators evaluated only if Week-4+ data exists (minimum 20 cycles at stable pace).
summary: Protocol for tracking operator learning progression (85%-100% over Weeks 1-4) separate from stable performance, validating standards using Week-4+ data only, and avoiding inflated benchmarks by segregating learning-phase from stable-phase data.

label: Assembly Motion Study Protocol for Non-Value-Added Motion Identification
content: SETUP: Mount camera (GoPro, smartphone, or clip light at 45° angle capturing hands, reach distance, and part positioning. Record 5 complete cycles per operator at 60fps+. Use natural/clip lighting to avoid hand-position shadows. Operator consent required; frame as educational process-improvement activity, not performance evaluation. PLAYBACK & ANNOTATION: Review each video at 50% playback speed with stopwatch handy. Categorize every motion: ✓ Value-Added (assembly, fastening, measurement, joining—hands transforming part toward finished state). ○ Setup/Unavoidable (load part, position in fixture, clamp, unclamp per process design—necessary, not eliminable without redesign). ✗ Waste-Reach (operator reaches >18" from station center, search for tool/part, travel time >2 sec). ✗ Waste-Reposition (part doesn't seat first try; operator clears and re-inserts, indicates fixture alignment flaw). ✗ Waste-Rework (fastener misaligned, solder cold, part slips—quality-driven rework loss, 2-5 sec per incident). ✗ Waste-Search (operator hunts for tool location, fixture clamp lever, next part; 3-8 sec per search). ✗ Delay-External (waiting for upstream station, tool unavailable, machine cycle time; operator idle, outside control). QUANTIFICATION: Sum seconds for each category across 5 cycles. Calculate percentages: Value-Added%, Setup%, Total Waste%, Delay%. Example: 5 cycles × 50 sec = 250 sec; 180 sec value-added (72%), 35 sec unavoidable (14%), 35 sec waste (14%). WASTE PRIORITIZATION: Create frequency × duration table; highest priority = high-frequency, long-duration waste (e.g., 8 reaches/cycle × 3 sec = 24 sec = 9.6% cycle waste—high-priority redesign target). SEQUENCE INSIGHT: Identify motion batching opportunities (e.g., operator assembles after loading each part individually → could load all parts first, then assembly shift, reducing setup repetition). Document findings for Phase 4 redesign evaluation.
summary: Video-based protocol categorizing assembly motions into value-added (part transformation), setup (unavoidable), and waste (reach >18", reposition, rework, search) with timing data to quantify high-impact elimination targets.

label: Fixture Redesign Decision Framework & Cost-Benefit Analysis
content: COMMON WASTE SOURCES & REDESIGN SOLUTIONS: (1) Tool/part reach >18" (2-3 sec waste/cycle) → Angled gravity-feed tray positioning tools at elbow height. Cost $300-500, saves 3-5 sec. (2) Multi-clamp reposition (4-6 sec waste) → Add alignment pins/locators, reduce clamp count 3→2. Cost $800-1,500, saves 4-6 sec. (3) Part slips during assembly (2-3 sec rework) → Increase pneumatic clamp pressure +10% or add soft jaws. Cost $200-400, saves 2-3 sec + quality boost. (4) Fastener misalignment rework (3-4 sec/failure) → Pre-stage fasteners in gravity tray aligned to insertion. Cost $150-300, saves 2-4 sec. (5) Fixture unclamp slow—manual screw (3-4 sec wait) → Replace with pneumatic quick-release or lever clamp. Cost $500-1,200, saves 3-4 sec. ROI CALCULATION: Total Waste/Cycle (seconds) identified in Phase 4 = sum of motion wastes. Example: 3 issues × 4 sec = 12 sec/unit waste. Annual Volume = 50,000 units/year. Labor Cost = $22/hr fully-burdened = $0.00611/sec. Annual Savings = 12 sec × 50,000 ÷ 3,600 × $22 = $36,667/year. Payback Period = $2,000 redesign cost ÷ $36,667 annual savings × 52 weeks = 2.8 weeks (≈1-week payback). DECISION THRESHOLDS: Payback <8 weeks → approve (strong ROI). 8-26 weeks → approve if quality improves or operator strain decreases. >26 weeks (>6 months) → defer unless quality/safety critical or capital upgrade included. SEQUENCE: Implement shortest-payback items first to build momentum and demonstrate ROI.
summary: Framework identifying high-impact fixture improvements ($150-1,500 cost range), calculating cost-benefit and payback period, applying quantified ROI thresholds (<8 weeks approve, 8-26 weeks conditional, >26 weeks defer) to prioritize implementation.

label: Pilot Validation Design & Go/No-Go Implementation Criteria
content: PILOT SCOPE: 1 experienced operator (familiar with current manual process—isolates fixture impact from learning new base process), 1 shift (day preferred for observation access), 1 production week (5 business days). PRE-PILOT BASELINE (500 units, current process, current fixture): Collect: (a) Individual cycle times (all 500 units), mean and Std Dev. (b) Defect count and type (fastener, fit, solder, rework incidents). (c) Operator subjective strain score (1=very comfortable, 10=very difficult; asked end-of-day). (d) If available, capability index Cpk from 30-50 unit dimensional sample. PILOT PHASE (500 units, new fixture + standardized work instruction card): Train operator 4 hours hands-on (walk through new mechanics, practice clamping/releasing; laminated photo-based station card, not text-only). Run 500 production units, collect identical metrics. DATA SEGREGATION: Exclude units 1-100 from pilot analysis (operator learning curve on new fixture); analyze units 101-500 for true process performance (learning minimal by unit 150). COMPARISON ANALYSIS: Calculate means, Std Devs, defect rates, Cpk from both data sets. GO/NO-GO DECISION MATRIX: ✓ GO TO FULL ROLLOUT if ALL met: (1) Cycle time ±10% of target (e.g., target 59 sec → 53-65 sec acceptable). (2) Defect rate ≤ baseline (no quality regression). (3) Operator strain score stable or decreased. ⚠️ CONDITIONAL (extend pilot 1-2 days) if: (1) Cycle time improved but >10% above target—identify bottleneck (slow clamp? operator needs coaching? alignment difficult?). Adjust and retest 100+ units; rollout only if ±10% achieved. (2) Defects slightly elevated (up to +20%) but operator reports fixture stable—likely operator learning; if corrected by day 2-3, proceed. ✗ HOLD (revert, investigate) if: (1) Cycle time worse than baseline (fixture slower or more complex). (2) Defect rate >20% above baseline (fixture design flaw: misalignment, inadequate clamp, part damage). (3) Operator strain increased significantly (feedback: difficult clamp, frequent misalignment, tool access awkward). (4) >5% of pilot units show fixture-related rework or defects. ROLLOUT SIGN-OFF: ✓ Cycle time ±10%. ✓ Defects ≤ baseline. ✓ Operator comfort stable/improved. ✓ Laminated photo instruction ready. ✓ Trainer assigned for multi-shift onboarding.
summary: Pilot run design (1 experienced operator, 500 units, 1 week) comparing baseline vs. new fixture with pre/post metrics (cycle time, defects, operator strain) and quantified go/no-go decision criteria (±10% cycle time, defect parity, comfort stable/improved).

label: Industrial Engineer
content: Industrial engineering discipline focused on optimizing complex systems, processes, and organizations. Applies Lean Manufacturing, Six Sigma (DMAIC/DMADV), value stream mapping, process flow analysis, time and motion studies, facility layout optimization, ergonomics/human factors, statistical process control (SPC), design of experiments (DOE), and operations research. Key deliverables include process capability studies, work instructions, standard work documentation, capacity planning models, and continuous improvement (kaizen) event facilitation.
summary: Lean/Six Sigma methodology, process optimization, VSM, SPC, facility layout, ergonomics, and continuous improvement for aerospace manufacturing

label: Operator capability assessment and learning curve measurement
content: Assess baseline operator aptitude (prior manufacturing experience, tool proficiency, drawing interpretation level 1-5) and optional cognitive/motor screening for precision tasks (manual dexterity peg-and-board test <60sec, spatial reasoning, color discrimination). Record baseline cycle time from 5 production observations. Measure individual learning curves using Wright's Law power function (Ct_n = C₁ × n^(-b)) fitted to first 20 production cycles. Extract learning exponent b: b>0.28 indicates fast learner (assign Fast Track), b=0.15-0.28 normal learner (Standard Track), b<0.15 indicates slow learner or procedure clarity issue. Use learning curve analysis to separate individual aptitude from procedure design flaws—if all operators in cohort plateau early, procedure needs simplification rather than extended training. Project days-to-proficiency (target 90% of skilled-worker cycle time + zero defects in 10-cycle sample). Establish four proficiency tiers (1=supervised, 2=spot-check, 3=independent, 4=lead operator) with five-step checkpoint audits (direct observation, quality sampling, work instruction knowledge check, tooling verification, documentation review). Advance operators through tiers only upon objective evidence: Tier 1 pass = 5 consecutive error-free cycles at ≤120% standard Ct; Tier 2 pass = standard Ct ±10% + zero defects in 20-cycle sample; Tier 3 pass = Ct ±8% sustained over 10 days + Cpk ≥1.33 + ≥95% attendance.
summary: Task: Measure operator baseline aptitude and learning curves using Wright's Law; establish proficiency tiers with structured checkpoint advancement criteria

label: Design and deliver individualized training programs
content: Assign operators to three training tracks based on prior manufacturing experience and learning rate exponent (b): Fast Track (prior aerospace assembly experience + b>0.28): 1-day procedure walkthroughs + 2-cycle observation + 10-cycle supervised practice + proficiency target 3 days. Standard Track (typical learner, b=0.15-0.28): 0.5-day classroom theory + 1-day hands-on practice (20 cycles) + 1-day tandem work with lead operator (30 cycles) + 3-day independent with spot-checks (40 cycles) + proficiency target 6 days. Extended Track (no prior experience or b<0.15): 2–3-day manual dexterity and tool-use drills + 1-day detailed drawing interpretation + 2-day 1:1 coaching with lead operator + 2-day 2:1 tandem (2 trainees: 1 lead) + 1-week independent with daily coaching + detailed proficiency assessment. Deliver procedure variants matched to learning style preference (survey: visual/auditory/kinesthetic). Visual variant: laminated color photo guides per step, color-coded tools (red torque wrench for high-torque fasteners), annotated sample part showing common mistakes, 2-min silent procedure video with subtitles. Auditory variant: verbal procedure walkthrough with lead operator, procedure checklist read aloud before each step, group mini-lesson with Q&A on procedure intent, audio-narrated SOP available on shop-floor speaker. Kinesthetic variant: hands-on practice with scrap parts (10 practice cycles), lead operator guides hand placement then observes, dummy-part tolerance bands (soft constraints), simulator or mock assembly station before production work. Track whether variant assignment correlates with faster checkpoint achievement; audit quarterly.
summary: Task: Assign operators to three training tracks (Fast/Standard/Extended); deliver learning-style-matched procedure variants (visual/auditory/kinesthetic)

label: Develop job rotation and cross-training strategy
content: Categorize manufacturing processes into three job families by skill transferability and quality criticality to determine rotation frequency and cross-training prerequisites. Family A (high transferability, low critical risk): routine subassemblies, fastener insertion, label placement, cosmetics—any Tier 2+ operator eligible for rotation every 2 weeks within 3-process family to prevent repetitive strain and build flexibility. Family B (moderate transferability, critical quality): torque-critical fastening, micro-positioning, electrical continuity checks—only Tier 3 operators after 2+ weeks secondary-process qualification can rotate every 3-4 weeks; requires skill maintenance audits; rotation spreads knowledge and reduces single-operator dependency. Ppk thresholds are facility-specific and should be established from your own capability studies, not assumed from generic standards. Family C (low transferability, highest criticality): hand-soldered critical connectors, optical axis hand-alignment, specialty processes requiring NADCAP-certified operators (welding, chemical processing, NDT)—specialists remain on primary process; secondary assignment only after 6+ months and demonstrated process capability exceeding facility-established thresholds; minimal rotation preserves deep expertise and consistency on highest-risk operations. CROSS-TRAINING SCHEDULING: Integrate rotation with production calendar—use production taper phases between program ramps for intensive cross-training; maintain skill currency by rotating back to secondary process within 6 months (otherwise require refresher assessment). Limit to one new process rotation per technician at a time. If technician absent from secondary process >3 months, require return-to-work competency check before independent operation. Track rotation schedule against skill matrix monthly; alert if any Tier 2-3 technician hasn't rotated in >6 months.
summary: Task: Categorize processes into job families by transferability/criticality; assign rotation frequency from 2-week cycles (Family A) to minimal rotation (Family C)

label: Measure training program ROI and operator performance improvement
content: Measure training ROI using three primary metrics, isolated from natural improvement using quasi-experimental cohort comparison and difference-in-differences: (1) Learning curve acceleration—compare trained vs. peer-learning cohorts on days-to-proficiency (achieve 90% of skilled-worker Ct + zero defects in 10-cycle sample) via Wright's Law curve fitting; savings example: trained cohort 6 days, peer cohort 14 days, × wage × cycles/shift = $1,200/operator production cost offset against $500 training cost per operator, payback <1 month. (2) Defect rate reduction—compare defect rates post-proficiency on same process, same time period (trained 0.8% vs. peer 2.2%), calculate cost: 1.4 percentage-point improvement × 1,000 parts/week × 52 weeks × $50 rework cost per part = $36,400/year savings. (3) Cycle time consistency—measure coefficient of variation (σ/μ) post-proficiency: trained cohort 6.7% CV vs. peer 14.4% CV; lower variation indicates better procedure clarity and operator understanding; impacts process capability (Ppk/Cpk) and takt-time adherence. Use difference-in-differences when equipment or material changes confound results: training effect = (Trained_post - Trained_pre) - (Peer_post - Peer_pre); subtract out improvements occurring naturally in both groups. Calculate payback period: (annual savings - training cost)/training cost; target <6 months. Track monthly ROI dashboard: proficiency days, defect rate improvement %, CV reduction %, cumulative net benefit. Report to Plant Manager quarterly.
summary: Task: Measure learning curve acceleration, defect reduction, cycle time consistency; use quasi-experimental design to isolate training impact from external factors

label: Implement visual management and real-time coaching system
content: Create three visual management tools for supervisors to match operators to task complexity and enable real-time coaching: (1) Operator Capability Matrix (printed weekly, posted at supervisor station): rows=operators, columns=processes, cells show proficiency tier color-coded (green=Tier 3 meets Ct target ±8%, yellow=Tier 2 approaching checkpoint, red=Tier 1 or drift >12%, dashed=not trained), average cycle time from last 5-day period (e.g., 'Ct: 119s'), next checkpoint date. Used to assign primary tasks (green operators), rotation partners (yellow supervised by green), and identify escalation needs (red operators). (2) Andon Escalation Triggers: yellow cord when Ct > 120% of standard for 3 consecutive cycles (supervisor response <3 min, assess procedure/tool/fatigue, provide targeted coaching); red cord for Ct > 130% (stop line if takt-critical); quality escalation if same defect type appears 2× in a week (trigger re-training evaluation). (3) Supervisor Coaching Logs: daily entry per operator (observation time, cycle times, coaching notes with ✓/! symbols, intervention provided, next checkpoint date) for shift handoff continuity, pattern detection, and supervisor accountability. Use coaching logs to identify systemic issues: if torque-application errors appear 3× across different operators, escalate to procedure clarity review; if one operator requires extended coaching, consider specialization vs. rotation. Audit coaching logs monthly for frequency (target ≥2 entries per operator per week) and quality. Review capability matrix in daily shift handoff meeting (15 min): supervisor announces process assignments, coaching focuses, rotation partners.
summary: Task: Maintain capability matrix (weekly, color-coded tier + Ct), Andon escalation triggers, and coaching logs for real-time supervisor capability visibility and intervention

label: Tiered escalation and stoppage decision protocol for operator SPC
content: THREE-TIER ESCALATION MODEL for operator-managed control charts: TIER 1 (Operator Authority — Investigate, No Stoppage): Single out-of-control signal (1 point beyond 3σ, trend of 6, run of 8). Operator actions: re-measure part 2×, gage zero-check on master, fixture visual inspection. If resolved in <5 min and 3 re-measured parts pass, resume production and document. TIER 2 (Supervisor/QA — Conditional Stoppage): Triggered when: (a) 3+ Tier-1 signals within same shift, (b) measurement system fails zero-check, (c) MR-bar over last 10 parts exceeds 1.5× baseline. Actions: stop machine, run 5-part setup verification (100% measure, all must pass), QA performs abbreviated Gage R&R (5×3 study). Resume only with QA sign-off. TIER 3 (Quality Manager + Engineering — Mandatory Stoppage): Triggered when: (a) any part exceeds print tolerance (not just control limit), (b) 2+ nonconforming parts in same shift, (c) same Tier-2 root cause recurs within 3 shifts. Actions: quarantine last 20 parts, formal root cause analysis meeting within 4 hours, corrective action with owner and deadline. INCREASED SAMPLING PROTOCOL: After any Tier-1 resolution, increase from normal frequency to every-part measurement for next 20 parts; if all 20 pass, return to normal. Document sampling changes on control chart with date/time annotation.
summary: Three-tier escalation protocol defining operator, supervisor/QA, and engineering authority levels for SPC out-of-control signals, with specific stoppage triggers and increased sampling rules

label: Control chart sensitivity calibration against tolerance limits
content: PURPOSE: Ensure I-MR chart detects process shifts before nonconforming parts escape to downstream operations. SENSITIVITY GAP ANALYSIS: (1) Calculate distance from UCL to USL: Gap = USL - UCL = USL - (X-bar + 2.66×MR-bar). If Gap < 1σ, chart may not detect shift before defect occurs — add Pre-Control overlay. (2) Calculate ARL (Average Run Length) for shift detection: ARL₀ (false alarm) = 370 for standard 3σ limits; ARL₁ (detection of 1σ shift) ≈ 44 observations; ARL₁ (2σ shift) ≈ 6 observations. For LRHM with <50 parts/year, ARL₁=44 means a 1σ shift may persist for months. MITIGATION: (a) Add Western Electric supplementary rules (reduces ARL₁ for 1σ shift to ~12 but increases false alarms to ARL₀≈92); (b) Overlay Pre-Control zones: GREEN = Nominal ± T/4, YELLOW = T/4 to T/2 from nominal, RED = beyond T/2. Any YELLOW triggers increased sampling, any RED triggers stoppage — Pre-Control is tolerance-referenced so it catches shifts I-MR may miss when Cpk is marginal (<1.33). VALIDATION EXPERIMENT: Introduce known shift of 1.5σ on fixture, measure 15 parts, confirm detection within 5 parts with ≥80% probability. If not met, tighten control limits to 2.5σ or increase sampling frequency. HYBRID RULE: Operator triggers investigation if EITHER I-MR signal OR Pre-Control YELLOW fires — dual-chart approach reduces ARL₁ without excessive false alarms.
summary: Method to calibrate I-MR chart detection power relative to tolerance limits using gap analysis, ARL calculations, Pre-Control overlay, and experimental validation of shift detection probability

label: Sampling interval economic optimization for operator SPC
content: ECONOMIC MODEL: Total Annual Cost = (N_measured × C_measure) + (N_total × P_escape(f) × C_escape), where N_measured = parts measured per year at frequency f, C_measure = labor + equipment cost per measurement, P_escape(f) = defect escape probability as function of sampling frequency, C_escape = cost per escaped defect (rework + schedule + scrap). ESTIMATION OF P_escape(f): If process shift occurs at random time between samples, and ARL₁ = k samples to detect shift, then expected escapes before detection = (k-1) × (1/f) parts. P_escape ≈ (P_shift × ARL₁) / (f × N_total). TYPICAL VALUES (aerospace structural): C_measure = $0.50-$2.00/part (gage) or $5-$15/part (CMM); C_escape = $2,000-$50,000 depending on assembly stage discovered. FREQUENCY SELECTION: Compute total cost for f = every part, every 2nd, every 5th, every 10th; select f that minimizes total cost. STARTING POINT: Every 5th part for SC features, every 10th for CC. DYNAMIC ADJUSTMENT RULES: Tighten (double frequency) when: 3+ signals/week, operator rotation, tool change, new part number first 50 units. Relax (halve frequency) when: 8+ weeks clean, demonstrated Ppk >2.0. Document all frequency changes on control chart. CONSTRAINT: Measurement cost should not exceed 5% of expected annual escape cost savings to maintain positive ROI.
summary: Economic model for optimizing SPC sampling frequency balancing measurement cost against defect escape risk, with dynamic adjustment rules based on process stability

label: Environmental compensation for measurement systems in operator SPC
content: PROBLEM: Temperature and humidity variations affect measurement equipment accuracy — CMMs have thermal expansion coefficients, hard gages change dimensions, optical systems shift with humidity. Uncompensated environmental effects inflate apparent process variation and produce false control chart signals. QUANTIFICATION: Run 2-week MSA capturing measurements at different ambient conditions (morning cool, afternoon warm). Record ambient temperature (°F/°C) and relative humidity (%) with each measurement. Calculate correlation: if R² > 0.15 between temperature and measurement error on reference standard, compensation is needed. COMPENSATION METHODS: (1) Linear correction: X_corrected = X_observed - β₁×(T - 68°F) where β₁ = thermal coefficient from MSA regression; (2) CMM auto-compensation: most modern CMMs have temperature probes and apply CTE correction automatically — verify this is enabled and calibrated; (3) Environmental control: maintain shop floor within ±3°F and ±10% RH during measurement (preferred for SC features). IMPACT ON CONTROL LIMITS: If environmental compensation reduces measurement variation by >15%, recalculate MR-bar and control limits — tighter limits improve defect detection sensitivity. DOCUMENTATION: Record temperature/humidity on control chart or in SPC software; include environmental conditions in Gage R&R study per AIAG MSA 4th edition. Flag any measurement taken outside ±5°F of baseline temperature.
summary: Procedure for quantifying and compensating temperature/humidity effects on measurement equipment to prevent false SPC signals and inflated process variation estimates

label: Operator learning curve accommodation in SPC baseline establishment
content: PROBLEM: New operators exhibit decreasing measurement variation as they develop technique — using early (high-variation) data for control limits produces overly wide limits that miss real process shifts once operator is proficient. DETECTION: Divide first 30 baseline measurements into 3 cohorts of 10. Calculate MR-bar for each. If MR-bar decreases >20% from Cohort 1 to Cohort 3, learning curve effect is present. ACCOMMODATION: (1) Discard Cohort 1 data; use Cohort 2+3 (or Cohort 3 only) for control limit calculation; (2) Document operator proficiency sign-off date when MR-bar stabilizes (variation between consecutive 10-part cohorts <10%); (3) Lock control limits only after proficiency confirmed. ONGOING MANAGEMENT: When operators rotate to new machines or new part numbers, restart 30-part proficiency baseline at tightened sampling (every 2nd part). Track days-to-proficiency metric per operator to identify training program effectiveness. RISK: If learning curve not accounted for, control limits are 20-40% wider than necessary, reducing chart sensitivity by equivalent amount — a 1.5σ process shift that should be detected in 6 parts may take 15+ parts with inflated limits.
summary: Method to detect and accommodate operator learning curve effects when establishing I-MR control chart baselines, including proficiency sign-off criteria and sensitivity impact

label: Validate operator SPC effectiveness vs. batch inspection baseline
content: PURPOSE: Prove that operator-managed point-of-use control charting reduces defect escapes and rework compared to periodic batch inspection. BASELINE DATA COLLECTION (4 weeks minimum before SPC launch): Track at 3 points: (1) incoming inspection defect rate by feature, (2) downstream assembly rework count and root causes, (3) scrap count and cost. Calculate baseline defect escape rate = (defects found at assembly or later) / (total parts produced). POST-IMPLEMENTATION TRACKING (8+ weeks): Same 3 data points plus: (4) operator chart signal count and resolution rate, (5) defects caught by operator chart before reaching inspection. KEY METRICS: Defect escape rate reduction (target >50%), incoming inspection OOS rate reduction, rework cost avoidance, operator signal-to-noise ratio (true signals vs. false alarms, target >30% true positive). STATISTICAL VALIDATION: Use difference-in-differences when confounding factors exist (supplier changes, equipment upgrades): Treatment Effect = (Post_SPC - Pre_SPC) - (Post_control - Pre_control). For small samples, use Fisher's exact test on 2×2 table (defect/no-defect × pre/post). REPORTING: Monthly KPI dashboard to Plant Manager showing escape rate, cost avoidance, cumulative ROI. Target payback period <3 months. GO/NO-GO for expansion: Pilot must show statistically significant escape rate reduction (p<0.10) or cost avoidance >3× program cost within 8 weeks.
summary: Validation methodology comparing operator SPC to batch inspection baseline using defect escape rates, cost avoidance metrics, difference-in-differences statistical control, and expansion go/no-go criteria

label: Work Design & Measurement
summary: Work systems design, methods engineering, time study, performance ratings, work sampling, operations analysis, and line balancing.

label: Introduction to Work Systems
content: Scope of work design; role of industrial engineer in work systems; overview of productivity measurement.

label: Design System Process
content: Manual and machine work systems; methods engineering; job descriptions and specifications; process design methodology.

content: Productivity metrics; labor utilization; efficiency measures; Pareto analysis; cause-and-effect diagrams.

label: Recording & Analysis Tools
content: Process maps; operation charts; flow process charts; gang charts; worker-machine charts; activity charts.

label: Time Study
content: Stopwatch time study methods; equipment and forms; study requirements; number of cycles; elemental breakdown.

label: Performance Ratings & Allowances
content: Pace rating; Westinghouse system; objective rating; synthetic rating; PFD allowances; fatigue and delay allowances.

label: Standard Data & Pre-Determined Time Systems
content: Standard data development; MTM-1 and MTM-2; MOST; MODAPTS; application guidelines.

label: Work Sampling
content: Theory and statistical basis; observation requirements; control charts for work sampling; continuous sampling.

label: Operations Analysis
content: Machine interference and cluster analysis; assembly line balancing; mixed-model balancing; takt time.

content: Principles of motion economy; workplace layout for manual tasks; simultaneous motion patterns.

content: Hand tool design; workstation layout; lean manufacturing principles applied to workplace design.
