content: Lighting; noise; vibration; thermal conditions; air quality as they affect work performance.

label: Analytical Tools for Work Design
content: Role of technology; digital time study; video analysis; simulation for work design.

label: Design Implementation
content: Design implementation in manufacturing environments requires structured change management aligned to production maturity. KEY ELEMENTS: (1) Participative work instruction development — operators who execute procedures must co-author them; procedures written solely from engineering intent without shop-floor validation fail in practice (AS9100D Section 7.2 competence). (2) Motivation through ownership — operators given feedback authority (daily standup input, weekly issue logs, quarterly FMEA review participation) sustain improvements vs. imposed top-down procedures. (3) Staged implementation — prototype phase uses flexible design-intent WIs with operator adaptation authority; pilot phase locks process parameters with data collection; production phase enforces standardized work with zero deviation authority. (4) Sustaining improvements — monthly WI effectiveness review using defect root-cause taxonomy (setup error, operator adherence, WI ambiguity, process capability, equipment/material) identifies which WI elements actually prevent failures vs. unnecessary overhead. (5) Change resistance mitigation — visual management (color-coded WI borders, station status boards, laminated decision trees) reduces cognitive load during transitions.

label: Operations Research & Analysis
summary: Linear programming, integer programming, network flows, dynamic programming, queuing theory, simulation, metaheuristics, and decision analysis.

label: Linear Programming
content: Formulation; simplex method; duality; sensitivity analysis; transportation and assignment problems.

label: Integer & Combinatorial Programming
content: Branch and bound; cutting planes; binary and mixed-integer models; set covering/packing.

label: Network Models & Algorithms
content: Shortest path; maximum flow; minimum cost flow; minimum spanning tree; project networks (CPM/PERT).

label: Dynamic Programming
content: Bellman's principle of optimality; deterministic and stochastic DP; resource allocation; equipment replacement.

label: Nonlinear Programming
content: Unconstrained optimization; KKT conditions; convexity; gradient methods; quadratic programming.

label: Queuing Theory
content: Birth-death processes; M/M/1, M/M/c, M/G/1 models; finite queue/population; network of queues.

label: Simulation
content: Discrete-event simulation; Monte Carlo methods; random variate generation; input modeling; output analysis; verification and validation.

label: Metaheuristics
content: Genetic algorithms; simulated annealing; tabu search; ant colony optimization; particle swarm optimization.

label: Decision Analysis
content: Decision trees; utility theory; multi-attribute decision making; Bayesian inference; value of information.

label: Stochastic Models
content: Markov chains; Markov decision processes; reliability modeling; inventory models under uncertainty.

label: Engineering Economic Analysis
summary: Cost classification, cash flow analysis, depreciation, replacement analysis, break-even analysis, and risk/uncertainty in economic decisions.

label: Cost Concepts & Classification
content: COST CONCEPTS FOR AEROSPACE MANUFACTURING: (1) FIXED COSTS: Facility lease, equipment depreciation, salaried staff—do not vary with production volume. In LRIP, fixed costs per unit are high due to low volume; avoid inflating unit cost estimates by separating fixed overhead from variable production cost. (2) VARIABLE COSTS: Direct material, direct labor (touch labor), consumables—scale linearly with units produced. (3) SEMI-VARIABLE: Manufacturing engineering support, tooling maintenance, quality labor—step-function costs that increase at volume thresholds. (4) SUNK COSTS: Prototype development, initial tooling design, supplier qualification—already spent; exclude from forward-looking make-vs-buy decisions but track for program cost reporting. (5) OPPORTUNITY COSTS: Shop floor capacity consumed by LRIP that could produce mature products; quantify using Tier 0 labor rate × displaced hours. (6) INCREMENTAL COSTS: Cost of producing one additional unit beyond planned quantity—critical for contract modifications; in LRIP, incremental cost drops steeply through learning curve (Build 1 incremental may be 2-3× Build 5 incremental). COST ESTIMATION METHODS: (a) Parametric: weight-based or complexity-based cost estimating relationships (CERs) from historical data; limited utility in LRIP due to small sample. (b) Analogy: estimate from similar past programs adjusted for complexity, materials, design maturity; most common LRIP method. (c) Engineering build-up: bottom-up from process sheets, labor standards, material lists; most accurate but requires stable work instructions. (d) Hybrid: Use analogy for early estimates (pre-Build 1), transition to engineering build-up as process sheets mature (post-Build 3). KEY LRIP DISTINCTION: Separate non-recurring costs (setup, qualification, tooling) from recurring costs (steady-state labor + material) and learning costs (transitional labor that decays with repetition). Failure to separate these inflates recurring unit cost estimates and distorts full-rate production forecasts.

label: Time Value of Money & Cash Flow Analysis
content: Present/future/annual worth; interest rates; equivalence; gradient series; geometric series; continuous compounding.

label: Comparison of Alternatives
content: Present worth; annual worth; rate of return; benefit-cost ratio; incremental analysis; study period; capitalized cost.

label: Depreciation & Tax Effects
content: Straight-line; declining balance; MACRS; units-of-production; depletion; after-tax cash flow analysis.

label: Replacement & Retention Analysis
content: Economic life; defender-challenger analysis; retirement without replacement; technology obsolescence.

label: Break-Even & Sensitivity Analysis
content: Single and multi-product break-even; contribution margin; sensitivity and spider plots; scenario analysis.

label: Risk & Uncertainty in Economic Decisions
content: Expected value; decision trees; simulation for economic analysis; real options; inflation and price changes.

label: Capital Budgeting & Project Selection
content: Budget constraints; project ranking; mutually exclusive vs independent; capital rationing; portfolio selection.

label: Facilities Engineering & Energy Management
summary: Facility location, sizing, layout planning, material handling systems, warehousing, and plant/energy engineering.

label: Facility Location
content: Single and multi-facility location; center-of-gravity method; factor rating; transportation models; global considerations.

label: Facility Layout & Design
content: Systematic layout planning (SLP); relationship charting; space requirements; block layout; detailed layout; CRAFT/CORELAP algorithms. For mixed prototype/production facilities: zone segregation using production cells within shared floor space, scheduled shared-resource access (NDT, CMM, environmental chambers) with priority rules for production, flow segregation via nonconformance routing differences (prototype scrap vs. production MRB disposition), and modular fixture stations that support variant changeover. Key principle: segregate control level, not necessarily physical space—same equipment can serve both if access scheduling and documentation requirements differ by zone.

label: Material Handling Systems
content: Unit load concept; material handling equipment selection; conveyor systems; AGVs; AS/RS; handling principles.

label: Warehousing & Distribution
content: Warehouse design; storage systems; order picking strategies; cross-docking; warehouse management systems.

label: Plant Engineering & Maintenance
content: Compressed air quality: ISO 8573-1 triple-class notation (Particulate.Moisture.Oil) — Class 4.4.2 typical for general pneumatic tooling and CNC machine centers; Class 3.4.1 for precision measurement equipment and sensitive assembly processes; Class 1.2.1 is pharmaceutical/semiconductor grade, not standard for aerospace manufacturing pneumatic tooling. Specify class per equipment OEM datasheet; avoid over-specification — each moisture class upgrade requires desiccant drying adding 15–25% compressor power consumption.

label: Energy Management
content: ENERGY AUDITING FOR FACILITIES: Establish 12-month utility consumption baseline (electricity kWh, natural gas therms, chilled water MMBtu) before any improvement project. Normalize for weather using heating degree days (HDD) and cooling degree days (CDD) to isolate operational change from weather variance. ENERGY AUDIT TYPES: Level 1 (walk-through, 1–2 days) — identify obvious waste, benchmark energy intensity; Level 2 (detailed, 2–4 weeks) — quantify savings potential per system (envelope, HVAC, lighting, process equipment), calculate simple payback; Level 3 (investment-grade, 4–8 weeks) — engineering analysis sufficient for capital project authorization. ENERGY INTENSITY BENCHMARKS: General manufacturing $0.80–2.50/sq ft/year; climate-controlled manufacturing (ISO 7+ cleanroom, temperature-critical processes) often exceeds $2.00/sq ft/year due to high HVAC demand. SAVINGS ATTRIBUTION: When executing multiple improvement projects, sequence projects 4–8 weeks apart and measure interim baseline after each to attribute savings to specific improvements. If actual savings <60% of modeled, investigate control system status, repair completeness, or occupancy load changes. CONSERVATION MEASURE CATEGORIES: Envelope (infiltration sealing, insulation), HVAC (balancing, controls optimization), lighting (LED retrofit — typical 50–60% reduction, 2–4 yr payback), process equipment (VFD retrofit for motors >10 hp), behavioral (setpoint scheduling, off-shift setback). Standards: ASHRAE 90.1, ISO 50001, DOE SEP.
summary: System: Facility energy auditing — Level 1/2/3 audit types, 12-month weather-normalized utility baseline, energy intensity benchmarks ($0.80–2.50/sq ft/year), savings attribution sequencing for multi-project programs, and conservation measure categories (envelope, HVAC, lighting, process equipment, behavioral)

label: Quality & Reliability Engineering
content: Quality and reliability engineering for aerospace manufacturing. STATISTICAL PROCESS CONTROL (SPC): Control chart selection (I-MR for n=1, X-bar/R for subgroups), capability indices (Cp/Cpk require stability + n≥25; use Pp/Ppk for early production), rational subgrouping principles. In LRIP/prototype with small samples (<25 units), traditional Cpk is unreliable — use Pp/Ppk with wide confidence intervals acknowledged, or switch to defect-category trending. DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS (DOE): Full/fractional factorial designs for process parameter optimization; Taguchi methods for robust design; response surface methodology. RELIABILITY ENGINEERING: FMEA (Design and Process), reliability block diagrams, Weibull analysis for failure distribution, accelerated life testing, HALT/HASS. FAILURE ANALYSIS: 8D problem-solving, 5-Why, Ishikawa diagrams, fault tree analysis. Root-cause categorization: design tolerance, process capability, supplier quality, operator error, tooling/fixturing, measurement system. LEAN SIX SIGMA: DMAIC for process improvement, DMADV for new process design. Key tools: process mapping, value stream analysis, hypothesis testing, regression analysis, gauge R&R (MSA). AS9100D ALIGNMENT: §8.5.1 (production process controls), §8.6 (release of products), §9.1.3 (analysis and evaluation of data), §10.2 (nonconformity and corrective action).
summary: Statistical process control, control charts, DOE, Lean Six Sigma, reliability testing, failure analysis, and maintenance engineering.

label: Quality Management Systems
content: TQM; ISO 9001; quality function deployment (QFD); cost of quality; quality planning and improvement frameworks.

label: Statistical Process Control
content: CHART SELECTION BY DATA TYPE: Variables charts (I-MR, X-bar/R, X-bar/S) require continuous data with sufficient gauge resolution (resolution ≤10% of tolerance recommended; >25% of tolerance makes data near-discrete and variables charts unreliable). Attribute charts (p, np, c, u) are appropriate when data is inherently discrete, binary (pass/fail), or when gauge resolution is too coarse for meaningful variables charting. CAPABILITY INDICES: Cp/Cpk require demonstrated process stability (in-control Shewhart chart) and n≥25; Pp/Ppk use overall sample std dev and are valid for any sample size but have wide confidence intervals at n<15. Use Pp/Ppk in LRIP until sufficient data exists to demonstrate stability. RATIONAL SUBGROUPING: Subgroup should represent a single, homogeneous production condition (one setup, one operator, one material lot); variation within subgroup should reflect only common cause. In LRIP, rational subgroup is often a single part (n=1, I-MR chart). SMALL-SAMPLE LIMITATIONS: With n<25, do NOT present Cpk values as reliable — confidence intervals are extremely wide (e.g., Cpk=1.33 at n=10 has 95% CI of roughly 0.7–2.0). For prototype/LRIP with <25 units, prefer defect-category frequency tracking and trend analysis over capability indices. SPC ALTERNATIVES FOR LOW-VOLUME: When sample sizes invalidate traditional SPC, use: (1) pre-control charts (stoplight charts) requiring only 5 consecutive units in green zone, (2) short-run SPC with coded/normalized data pooling similar features across part numbers, (3) defect-type Pareto analysis trending by build sequence, (4) individual measurement plots with engineering tolerance limits (not statistical control limits) clearly distinguished.

label: Design of Experiments
content: DOE METHODS: Full factorial (2^k): Tests all factor combinations; practical limit ~5 factors (32 runs). Fractional factorial (2^(k-p)): Reduces runs by aliasing higher-order interactions; Resolution III (main effects aliased with 2FI — screening only), Resolution IV (main effects clear of 2FI), Resolution V (main effects and 2FI clear). Taguchi orthogonal arrays (L4, L8, L9, L16, L18): Standardized fractional designs emphasizing robustness; optimize signal-to-noise ratio S/N = -10*log10[(1/n)Σ(y-target)²] for nominal-is-best. Response Surface Methodology (RSM): Central Composite Design (CCD) or Box-Behnken for quadratic models; used after screening to find optimal operating window. AEROSPACE APPLICATION: For immature processes with limited parts, use definitive screening designs (DSD, Jones & Nachtsheim 2011) — require only 2k+1 runs for k factors, can estimate main effects, quadratic effects, and some 2FI. When production volume is <30 parts per configuration, prioritize screening designs over full RSM. CAUTION: DOE requires measurement system GR&R ≤30% of tolerance for response variable; otherwise factor effects are masked by measurement noise. Always run confirmation trials (n≥5) at predicted optimum before locking parameters.

label: Acceptance Sampling
content: Single, double, and sequential sampling plans; OC curves; AQL/LTPD; MIL-STD-1916; Dodge-Romig plans.

label: Lean Six Sigma
content: DMAIC methodology; value stream mapping; waste identification (8 wastes: overproduction, waiting, transport, overprocessing, inventory, motion, defects, unused talent); kaizen events; 5S workplace organization; poka-yoke (error-proofing); statistical tools integration (SPC, DOE, hypothesis testing). POKA-YOKE IN AEROSPACE ASSEMBLY: Three levels — (1) Prevention: physical design makes error impossible (asymmetric dowel pins, keyed connectors, fixture pockets accepting only correct orientation). (2) Detection: immediate feedback when error occurs (barcode scan mismatch alert, torque wrench auto-reject, go/no-go gauges). (3) Mitigation: limits error consequence (breakaway torque limiters, color-coded kits preventing cross-variant part selection). For low-volume multi-variant production, prevention-level poka-yoke (fixtures, kit design) is critical because operator repetition is insufficient to build error-resistant muscle memory. 5S APPLICATION IN LRIP: Emphasis on Sort (only current-variant parts at station) and Set-in-Order (kit positions match assembly sequence) to prevent configuration mix-ups; Standardize across variants using common fixture mounting points and kit container form factors.

label: Measurement Systems Analysis
content: MEASUREMENT SYSTEMS ANALYSIS (MSA) per AIAG MSA 4th Edition: Gauge R&R (Repeatability & Reproducibility): Crossed study design — minimum 10 parts × 3 operators × 3 trials (90 measurements). %GR&R = 100 × σ_GR&R / σ_total. Acceptance: ≤10% excellent, 10-30% conditionally acceptable (depends on application criticality and cost of improvement), >30% unacceptable — measurement system cannot distinguish part-to-part variation from measurement noise. For aerospace KCs, target ≤10%. ALTERNATIVE FOR LOW-VOLUME: When 10 unique parts unavailable, use 5-part × 3-operator × 3-trial abbreviated study (45 measurements); report with qualification that confidence intervals are wider. Number of Distinct Categories (ndc) = 1.41 × (σ_parts / σ_GR&R); minimum acceptable ndc ≥ 5. BIAS STUDY: Measure reference standard ≥25 times; bias = mean - reference value; t-test for significance. LINEARITY: Measure 5+ reference standards spanning operating range; regress bias vs. reference value; slope should not be statistically significant. STABILITY: Control chart of reference standard measurements over time (minimum 20 subgroups). ATTRIBUTE AGREEMENT: Kappa statistic for pass/fail gages; κ ≥ 0.75 acceptable. CRITICAL NOTE: MSA must precede process capability studies — if measurement system is inadequate, Ppk calculations are unreliable. Observed σ_total² = σ_process² + σ_measurement², so true process capability Ppk_true > Ppk_observed when GR&R is significant.

label: Reliability Engineering
content: Core reliability disciplines applied to aerospace production equipment. WEIBULL ANALYSIS: Fit failure time data to 2-parameter Weibull (shape β, scale η); β<1 = infant mortality (quality/installation issue), β≈1 = random failure (exponential distribution), β>1 = wear-out failure (schedule PM before η). Use Weibull to set PM intervals: replace/restore at age where survival probability drops to 85–90%. FMEA/FMECA: List failure modes per component; assign severity (S), occurrence probability (O), detection likelihood (D); RPN = S×O×D; prioritize actions where RPN >120 or severity ≥9 regardless of RPN. For aerospace production equipment, FMEA should address: bearing failure, seal/gasket degradation, control system fault, and utility (air, coolant, power) supply loss. FAULT TREE ANALYSIS (FTA): Model top-level event (e.g., 'compressed-air system failure') as AND/OR gate combinations of contributing failures; quantify top-event probability from component failure rates; identifies single-point failures (AND gate with one input). SYSTEM RELIABILITY: Series (all components must work): R_system = ∏R_i; parallel (k-of-n redundancy): R_system = 1 – ∏(1–R_i); dual-compressor system with independent controls improves availability from 98% per unit to 99.96% combined. MTBF TRACKING: Record all failures in CMMS with timestamps; calculate MTBF = total operating hours ÷ number of failures; compare to OEM design MTBF; declining MTBF trend signals emerging wear or maintenance gap.

label: Maintenance Engineering
content: Maintenance engineering disciplines for aerospace production equipment. PREVENTIVE MAINTENANCE: Time/usage-based intervals calibrated to equipment criticality — critical shared equipment at 500 hrs or 4 weeks (whichever first); single-program equipment at 300 hrs or 6 weeks. Utilization-triggered adjustments: utilization <40% for 3 consecutive weeks → extend PM 1 week; utilization >80% for 2 weeks → accelerate next PM 1 week. PREDICTIVE MAINTENANCE: Condition indicators — spindle TIR trending (>0.0015" growth rate = bearing preload degradation), thermal baseline deviation (±3°C normal, +5°C = intervention), vibration FFT analysis (bearing fault frequencies), coolant contamination ISO cleanliness code (>18/16/13 = full change). Auto-schedule PM when any predictive trigger exceeds threshold. CONDITION-BASED MAINTENANCE: Weekly spindle sound assessment, monthly thermal imaging, fortnightly coolant sampling. Substitute for scheduled PM when condition data confirms health and equipment is Tier 1/1B priority. RCM ANALYSIS: Map failure modes × consequence × detection method per SAE JA1011; classify as evident vs. hidden failure; assign maintenance task type (on-condition, scheduled restoration, scheduled discard, failure-finding, redesign). TPM: Operator-level autonomous maintenance — daily startup checks (TIR, thermal, coolant visual), cleaning, lubrication, anomaly reporting. Target: operators detect 80% of developing failures before breakdown.
summary: System: Preventive maintenance intervals calibrated by equipment criticality, predictive maintenance condition indicators, condition-based maintenance substitution logic, RCM failure mode analysis, and TPM autonomous maintenance for aerospace production equipment

label: Ergonomics & Human Factors
summary: Anthropometry, biomechanics, work physiology, thermal environment, repetitive task analysis, manual handling, and display/control design.

label: Anthropometry & Workspace Design
content: Static and functional anthropometry; percentile-based design; workspace dimensioning; adjustability principles.

label: Biomechanics of Work
content: Force and moment analysis; spine loading models; NIOSH lifting equation; push/pull force limits; hand tool biomechanics.

label: Work Physiology & Fatigue
content: Energy expenditure; cardiovascular demands; rest allowances; shift work; circadian effects; cumulative fatigue.

label: Cumulative Trauma & Repetitive Tasks
content: Risk factors for MSDs; RULA; REBA; strain index; job rotation as mitigation; ergonomic intervention evaluation.

label: Manual Material Handling
content: Lifting; carrying; pushing; pulling task analysis; NIOSH equation application; Snook tables; biomechanical modeling.

label: Environmental Ergonomics
content: Thermal comfort (WBGT, PMV/PPD); noise exposure and hearing conservation; lighting design; vibration (HAV, WBV).

label: Displays, Controls & Human-Machine Interface
content: Display design principles; control-display compatibility; alarm design; human error models; cognitive workload assessment.

label: Operations Engineering & Management
content: Operations engineering and management for low-rate aerospace manufacturing. SCOPE: (1) Forecasting — probabilistic demand weighting by program maturity (95%/70%/40% confidence tiers); do not forecast single-point estimates for variable-rate programs. (2) Production scheduling — sequence to minimize changeovers when queue depth >3 days WIP; schedule variant families consecutively to reduce setup time. (3) Inventory management — tiered safety stock: critical long-lead items (12-20 wk lead) stock 2× annual usage; medium-lead (6-12 wk) stock 1.5× annual + 2-month buffer; short-lead (<6 wk) stock consumption rate × 2-month buffer. (4) Capacity planning — utilization targets 60-75% (not traditional 85%) to absorb rework, variant changeovers, and maintenance windows. (5) MRP/ERP execution — link material availability milestones to manufacturing readiness gates; never trigger long-lead procurement before Design Baseline Release. (6) Maintenance integration — equipment PM scheduling must be visible on master production schedule; deferred PM log tracks equipment with skipped maintenance and mandatory completion dates. (7) Product lifecycle management — equipment capability must be validated against each design variant's process requirements before production authorization.
summary: Forecasting, production scheduling, inventory management, capacity planning, MRP/ERP, maintenance, and product lifecycle management.

label: Demand Forecasting
content: Demand forecasting methods and application to LRIP aerospace. CLASSICAL METHODS (limited applicability in LRIP): Time series (moving average, exponential smoothing) require 24+ periods of historical data — rarely available in low-rate production. Causal models (regression) useful only when demand drivers are quantifiable (e.g., fleet size → spare parts). LRIP-APPROPRIATE METHODS: (1) Scenario-based forecasting — build 3 scenarios per pre-contract program: Base Case (60% probability, e.g., 15 units/year), Upside (25%, 40 units), Downside (15%, 3 units sustaining only). Decision rule: if base case <50% probability, defer advance procurement. (2) Tiered confidence forecasting — Tier 1 (95% confidence, committed orders), Tier 2 (70%, pilot phase), Tier 3 (40%, prototype/exploratory). See neuron #815 for tier definitions. (3) Collaborative forecasting — monthly PM + Engineering + Supply Chain review reconciling program award status, design maturity gates, and supplier lead-time changes. FORECAST ACCURACY: Measure MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error) monthly; target <15% for Tier 1/2. If MAPE >20% + expediting >5% of spend, demand planning process requires root-cause investigation. Shift programs between tiers as contract status evolves (proposal → negotiation → signed).

label: Production Planning & Scheduling
content: Production planning and scheduling for low-rate aerospace job shop environments. AGGREGATE PLANNING: Use probabilistic demand by program tier (committed 95% confidence, pilot 70%, prototype 40%) rather than single-point forecasts; aggregate capacity requirements across tiers monthly. MASTER PRODUCTION SCHEDULING: Frozen windows proportional to commitment level—committed production 4-6 week freeze, pilot 2-4 week freeze, prototype gate-driven (no calendar freeze). SEQUENCING RULES for multi-variant job shop: (1) Priority dispatch—committed customer dates first, then pilot milestones, then prototype gates. (2) Setup-time minimization—group variants sharing fixtures/tooling consecutively. (3) Critical-ratio scheduling—ratio of time remaining to work remaining; CR<1.0 triggers expedite. THEORY OF CONSTRAINTS application: Identify facility bottleneck (often 5-axis machining or specialized test equipment); subordinate all other scheduling to bottleneck throughput; buffer the bottleneck with WIP (constraint buffer in schedule template). Do not balance capacity utilization across all resources—maximize bottleneck utilization, accept lower utilization elsewhere.
summary: System: Production planning and scheduling for low-rate aerospace job shop with probabilistic demand tiers, frozen schedule windows, critical-ratio dispatch, and Theory of Constraints bottleneck management

label: Inventory Management
content: Inventory management policies adapted for LRIP aerospace. CLASSICAL EOQ LIMITATIONS: Standard EOQ assumes stable demand and known lead times — breaks in LRIP with 10-15 units per variant and 16-26 week lead times. Use modified safety stock formula instead: SS = (Safety Factor × Supplier LT Std Dev × Daily Demand) + (Design-Change Contingency × Tier Forecast Qty). Contingency factors: 15% for Tier 2 (pilot), 30% for Tier 3 (prototype). Safety factors: 90% service level = 1.28, 95% = 1.65. REORDER POINT: ROP = (Lead-Time Demand) + Safety Stock. Dynamic adjustment: increase SS 25% if supplier lead-time std dev exceeds 2 weeks; reduce contingency from 15% to 10% when program advances to DB-3 frozen design. ABC CLASSIFICATION MODIFIED: Classify by (unit cost × design volatility) not just annual spend. High-cost volatile items = build-to-order only; low-cost stable items = build-to-stock 3-4 months supply. CENTRALIZED vs. SEGREGATED: Hybrid approach — centralized pooling for commodity materials (fasteners, raw stock), program-segregated for design-volatile materials. Centralized saves 15-25% carrying cost but requires robust Configuration Baseline Register allocation discipline. KPI TARGETS: Inventory turns 2.0-2.5, carrying cost <7% of inventory value, material availability ≥98% of scheduled production starts.

label: Capacity Management
content:
Capacity measurement; rough-cut capacity planning; CRP; capacity strategies (lead, lag, match); bottleneck management. For low-rate multi-program aerospace: (1) Tiered probabilistic capacity — weight demand by program maturity confidence (95% committed, 70% pilot, 40% prototype) with 1.33× rework multiplier for pilot builds. (2) Planning horizons — 12-week frozen for committed production, 8-week conditional for pilot, 4-week rolling for prototype. (3) Utilization targets — 60–75% (not traditional 85%) to absorb demand variability and design-change rework loops. (4) Bottleneck identification via Theory of Constraints — monitor queue depth at each process step; prototype <3 days, pilot <1 week, committed <3 days. (5) Capacity alternatives evaluation order: overtime → shift adjustment → subcontract → equipment investment (increasing commitment level). (6) Equipment sharing across programs to load-balance demand variability; position flexible equipment in shared job-shop cells serving 2–3 concurrent programs.

Standards references: AS9100D Section 7.1.1 (Resources — capacity determination); SAE AS6500 Section 5.2 (Production Planning — capacity planning); AIAG MMOG/LE Chapter 3 (Capacity planning assessment).
summary: System: Capacity management for low-rate multi-program aerospace — tiered probabilistic demand weighting, 60-75% utilization targets, queue-depth bottleneck monitoring, and capacity alternatives escalation ladder

label: MRP & ERP Systems
content: MRP logic and records; lot sizing; BOM structures; ERP modules and integration; implementation considerations.

label: Lean Production Systems
content: Pull systems; kanban; JIT; heijunka; value stream mapping; continuous flow manufacturing; lean metrics.

label: Maintenance Management
content: Maintenance management for low-rate aerospace manufacturing. PLANNING & SCHEDULING: Three-horizon PM model — Horizon 1 (committed production, 12-wk frozen): block PM 1 week before Tier 0 start, no PM during frozen window; Horizon 2 (pilot, 8-wk conditional): PM at 300 hrs or 6 wks, defer if condition monitoring healthy and backup available; Horizon 3 (prototype, 4-wk rolling): visual inspection only, corrective-acceptable during design iteration. DUAL-THRESHOLD PM TRIGGERS: For intermittent-use equipment, schedule PM at (operating hours threshold OR calendar interval), whichever comes first — prevents both over-maintenance on low-utilization equipment and idle-period degradation (corrosion, bearing preload loss, seal hardening, calibration drift) being missed on calendar-only schedules. CMMS REQUIREMENTS: Equipment log per item tracking operating hours, PM events, failures, downtime hours, parts replaced, condition data (TIR, thermal, coolant). Deferred PM log with equipment ID, program, deferral date, condition status, mandatory completion date. SPARE PARTS MANAGEMENT: Three-tier procurement — Tier A critical long-lead (12-20 wk): stock 2× annual usage, dual-source where possible, reorder on first failure signal; Tier B medium-lead (6-12 wk): stock 1.5× annual + 2-month buffer; Tier C short-lead (<6 wk): stock consumption × 2-month buffer. Safety stock formula: SS = Z × σ_demand × √LT + σ_LT × D_avg (Z=2.33 for 99% service level on Tier 0 equipment). PERFORMANCE METRICS: Equipment availability ≥95%, unplanned downtime <5% of scheduled hours, PM completion rate 100% (deferrals documented), maintenance cost per operating hour $8–12 for Tier 0 (total annual PM + corrective costs / annual operating hours), spare parts carrying cost ≤5% of equipment investment. Cost-per-operating-hour is the primary normalization metric for comparing equipment maintenance burden across programs with differing utilization rates. RCM INTEGRATION: Quarterly failure trend review — MTBF trending, root cause classification (setup-related, wear-out, fatigue, contamination), escalation if same root cause recurs >2× in 3 months.
summary: System: Three-horizon PM scheduling (committed/pilot/prototype), CMMS requirements, three-tier spare-parts management, and performance metrics for low-rate intermittent-use aerospace equipment

label: Product & Service Lifecycle Management
content: PRODUCT LIFECYCLE PHASES IN AEROSPACE: (1) Product Introduction / NPI: Design validation, process validation (IQ/OQ/PQ), trial-run capability (n≥30), FAI per AS9102. Gate: Design maturity review before LRIP release. (2) Ramp-Up / LRIP (Low-Rate Initial Production): Typically 10-50 units; purpose is to validate production processes at rate while design may still evolve. Key challenge: insufficient statistical power for traditional SPC (need ~25+ subgroups of n≥5 for reliable control limits). Use pre-control charts or individual/moving-range (I-MR) charts with provisional limits during LRIP. (3) Full-Rate Production: Formal SPC with X-bar/R or X-bar/S charts; capability indices (Cpk) replace preliminary performance indices (Ppk). (4) Sustaining: Focus shifts to supplier management, cost reduction, obsolescence management. (5) End-of-Life: Last-time buys, spare parts provisioning, archival of configuration data. CONFIGURATION MANAGEMENT per EIA-649-1: Five disciplines — CM planning, configuration identification, change control, status accounting, verification/audit. ECO process: Class I changes (affect form/fit/function) require full CCB review; Class II changes (documentation only) may use expedited review. AS9100D §8.5.2 requires identification and traceability throughout lifecycle.

label: Supply Chain Management
content: Supply chain management for LRIP aerospace manufacturing. KEY FUNCTIONS: (1) Supplier identification, qualification, and onboarding — tiered approach (Tier A full production, Tier B conditional with mitigation, Do Not Award) based on process capability, measurement system adequacy, and QMS maturity rather than certification status alone. (2) Supplier performance management — monthly scorecards tracking FTY, defect escape rate, on-time delivery, repeat NCR rate, SPC stability, lead-time variability, and cost of quality by supplier. (3) Supplier relationship management — quarterly business reviews with operations + quality managers; monthly capacity negotiation meetings for critical suppliers; escalation paths for missed commitments (1st miss = ops call, 2nd = VP meeting + contingency discussion, 3rd = formal transition). (4) Supplier development — ROI-gated capability investments targeting process gaps where annual cost avoidance exceeds development cost by >2:1. (5) Supply chain risk management — contingency supplier register, single-source dependency tracking, make-vs-buy analysis using total landed cost. (6) Requirement flow-down — design change communication with SLAs, quality agreement structuring, specification clarity. LRIP DISTINCTIONS: Supplier base is often immature (job shops without aerospace QMS); design volatility creates requalification burden; low volumes reduce supplier priority; total landed cost (not unit price) drives sourcing decisions.
summary: SCM fundamentals, logistics, reverse logistics, product flow, customer/supplier relationships, and supply chain risk management.

label: SCM Fundamentals & Strategy
content: Supply chain structure; strategy alignment; bullwhip effect; supply chain integration; performance measurement (SCOR model).

label: Logistics & Transportation
content: Transportation modes; routing and scheduling; freight economics; 3PL/4PL; global logistics; customs and trade compliance.

label: Procurement & Supplier Management
content: Procurement and supplier management for LRIP aerospace manufacturing. MOQ NEGOTIATION STRATEGIES: (1) Split-delivery MOQ — 50% deposit at T+12 weeks, balance at T+18 weeks; spreads cash flow and reduces obsolescence if ECO issued between deliveries. (2) Co-mingled MOQ across programs — single MOQ order serving Tier 1 (confirmed) + Tier 2 (pilot) programs; segregate at receipt by configuration. (3) Supplier-held inventory — negotiate supplier warehousing of excess MOQ with 0.5-1.0% monthly holding charge (vs. your 3-5% carrying cost); 4-week option window to pull or cancel post-design-freeze. (4) Split-sourced MOQ — order 50/50 from primary (12-week lead) and secondary (14-week lead) suppliers; 5-10% freight premium justified for critical-path materials. STRATEGIC SOURCING: For 16-26 week lead-time materials, initiate secondary supplier qualification 6 months before critical path need. Pre-negotiate expedite pricing: 'If primary delay >2 weeks, authorize secondary at 2.0× normal pricing.' CONTRACT MANAGEMENT: RFQ template includes split-delivery options, holding-at-supplier clauses, and restocking fee terms. Build-to-stock vs. build-to-order decision matrix: Higher unit cost ÷ higher design volatility → shorter acceptable procurement lead time → build-to-order. SUPPLIER DEVELOPMENT: Single-source exposure target <15% of total procurement spend.

label: Distribution & Warehousing
content: Distribution network design; DC location; cross-docking; last-mile delivery; omnichannel fulfillment strategies.

label: Reverse Logistics & Sustainability
content: Returns management; remanufacturing; recycling; closed-loop supply chains; circular economy principles; carbon footprint.

label: Supply Chain Risk Management
content: Supply chain risk management for LRIP aerospace programs with immature supplier bases. RISK CATEGORIES: (1) Single-Source Dependency — only one qualified supplier for component; mitigate with contingency supplier register (monthly update: primary Ppk, escape rate, backup qualification status, activation triggers). (2) Supplier Capacity Risk — supplier serves higher-volume customers who absorb capacity during surges; mitigate with contractual capacity reservation (e.g., 5 slots/month reserved), 12-week rolling forecasts with 8-week firm orders, and pre-negotiated expedite pricing. (3) Supplier Quality Risk — immature process capability or QMS gaps; mitigate with tiered qualification (Phase 1-3), 100% incoming inspection for Tier B, weekly SPC reviews. (4) Design Volatility Risk — ECOs trigger supplier requalification and tooling changes; mitigate with design stability forecasts shared with suppliers (3-month lookahead), ECO cost/lead-time impact SLAs. (5) Financial/Business Continuity Risk — small suppliers vulnerable to cash flow disruption; monitor through annual financial health check and maintain dual-source for >$100K annual spend components. CONTINGENCY ACTIVATION TRIGGERS: Ppk decline to <1.33, escape rate >1% (2-month average), capacity constraint causing >3-week slip, NCM cost >$20K in 6 months, or ≥4 major audit findings unresolved. RISK QUANTIFICATION: Estimate disruption cost = (daily production value × expected delay days) + expedite premium + schedule cascade impact. Compare against contingency qualification cost ($30-50K per supplier). TARGET: Single-source exposure <15% of total procurement spend; contingency activation rate <10% of single-source suppliers per year.
summary: Supply chain risk management for LRIP aerospace programs: single-source dependency mitigation, capacity reservation contracts, quality risk tiers, contingency activation triggers, and risk quantification methods

label: Engineering Management
summary: Leadership, strategic management, human resources, project management, organizational performance, and knowledge management systems.

label: Leadership & Organizational Behavior
content: Leadership theories; motivation; team dynamics; communication; organizational culture; change leadership.

label: Strategic Management
content: Strategic planning; competitive analysis; SWOT; balanced scorecard; technology strategy; innovation management.

label: Project Management
content: Project lifecycle; WBS; scheduling (CPM, PERT); resource management; earned value; risk management; agile/hybrid methods.

label: Human Resource Management for Engineers
content: HR management for manufacturing engineers and technical staff in aerospace. COMPENSATION STRUCTURE: Tier technical roles by certification level and specialization; aerospace technicians command 15-30% premium over general manufacturing due to quality accountability, certification requirements, and safety responsibilities. Benchmark against BLS NAICS 33641 (Aerospace Product & Parts Manufacturing) regional data. RETENTION LEVERS: (1) Career pathway clarity — define specialist track (deep process expertise), versatile operator track (cross-trained flexibility), and leadership/engineering track (transition to supervisory or manufacturing engineering roles). (2) Mentoring recognition — compensate experienced technicians who develop others (+$1-3/hr mentoring premium or quarterly bonus). (3) Certification-based advancement — automatic pay increases upon achieving higher competency tiers rather than time-based seniority alone. PERFORMANCE MANAGEMENT: Quarterly 1-on-1 reviews focused on skill development goals, career trajectory, and obstacle removal; annual compensation review benchmarked to regional market. Track technician-initiated process improvements as performance metric. PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT: Tuition reimbursement for manufacturing engineering degrees enables technician-to-engineer pipeline; internal production lead roles serve as intermediate leadership development step.

label: Financial Management for Engineers
content: Budgeting; cost accounting; financial statements; variance analysis; working capital; ROI for engineering projects.

label: Knowledge Management & Organizational Learning
content: Knowledge capture and organizational learning for manufacturing engineering. PROTOTYPE MANUFACTURING LESSONS LEARNED: Mandatory debrief post-test before LRIP cycle with Design Lead, Manufacturing Engineer, Quality Inspector, Program Manager. Report sections: (1) Build pass/fail summary, (2) Rework/scrap root cause (design tolerance vs. fixture vs. supplier vs. operator), (3) Process observations (harder/easier than expected, supplier surprises), (4) Design features contributing to rework with specific recommendations (relax tolerance, add fixturing, change supplier process), (5) Supplier performance summary. RECURRING ISSUES DATABASE: Quarterly-updated catalog mapping issue → program history → root cause → mitigation → DFM review check. Examples: bore alignment tolerance driving rework, adhesive cure voids, supplier lead-time misses, fastener torque verification labor. Integration: DFM review checklist asks 'Does this feature match any recurring issue? If yes, is mitigation the same or has design changed assumption?' FEEDBACK LOOP VALIDATION: Ask 'Has prototype manufacturing experience demonstrably changed how we design?' If answer is no, feedback loop is broken. Track production escapes linked to prototype-known issues (target: 0). Design History File (DHF) updated jointly by Design Lead + Manufacturing Manager with lessons learned rationale.
summary: Manufacturing lessons learned capture: prototype debrief reports, recurring manufacturability issues database, DFM checklist integration, and feedback loop validation metrics

label: Safety Engineering
summary: Hazard recognition, OSHA regulations, fire/electrical/mechanical hazards, safety management systems, and accident investigation.

label: Safety Management Systems
content: Safety policy; organization; planning; implementation; performance measurement; auditing; ISO 45001; management commitment.

label: Hazard Recognition & Risk Assessment
content: Hazard identification and risk assessment for manufacturing environments. JOB HAZARD ANALYSIS (JHA): For each manufacturing task, document task steps → hazards at each step (material, equipment, ergonomic, environmental) → exposure routes (inhalation, dermal, ingestion, physical) → exposure frequency and duration → existing controls → control effectiveness verification method. RISK MATRIX: Probability (1-5) × Severity (1-5) = Risk Score. Scoring: 20-25 = engineering controls mandatory; 12-16 = engineering preferred, interim PPE with commitment; 6-9 = administrative + PPE adequate; 1-4 = accept with documentation. HIERARCHY OF CONTROLS (NIOSH): Elimination > Substitution > Engineering Controls > Administrative Controls > PPE. In LRIP aerospace, engineering controls often compete with capital constraints—use two-tier approach: immediate engineering for catastrophic hazards, funded roadmap for moderate hazards with interim PPE. HAZARD COMMUNICATION: Work-cell-specific hazard placards (materials, symbols, PPE required, emergency procedures); master hazard material register linking materials to programs, exposure routes, PELs, MSDS locations; monthly update cycle for design-driven material changes. Standards: OSHA 29 CFR 1910, ANSI/ASSP Z10, ISO 45001 Section 6.1, MIL-STD-882E.
summary: System: Hazard identification techniques (JHA, risk matrices, hierarchy of controls) with scoring criteria, LRIP capital-constrained control prioritization, and hazard communication procedures

label: OSHA Regulations & Compliance
content: General industry standards (29 CFR 1910); construction standards; recordkeeping; citations; voluntary protection programs (VPP).

label: Fire & Life Safety
content: Fire prevention; detection and suppression systems; egress design; NFPA codes; emergency action plans; fire investigation.

label: Electrical & Mechanical Safety
content: Lockout/tagout (LOTO); arc flash; machine guarding; confined spaces; fall protection; PPE selection and management.

label: Accident Investigation & Prevention
content: Root cause analysis methods for aerospace incidents: 5-Why with evidence chain (SPC data, process records, material certs — not opinion), Ishikawa/fishbone for multi-factor causation, fault tree analysis (FTA) for safety-critical failures. Incident investigation must separate proximate cause (what happened) from systemic root cause (why the system allowed it). Leading indicators: near-miss reporting rate, SPC out-of-control signal frequency, supplier nonconformance trends, training compliance gaps. Lagging indicators: field failure rate per 1000 flight hours, CAR recurrence rate, mean time to root cause determination. Behavior-based safety: investigate system/process failures, not individual blame — culture must enable frontline reporting without punishment for honest mistakes. Safety culture maturity: reactive (investigate after accident) → proactive (track leading indicators) → generative (information flows freely, anomalies actively sought).
summary: System: Aerospace incident/failure investigation methods — 5-Why, Ishikawa, FTA root cause analysis; leading vs lagging safety indicators; behavior-based safety culture enabling systemic learning

label: Industrial Hygiene
content: Industrial hygiene (IH) practice for manufacturing: systematic identification, measurement, and control of occupational health hazards. EXPOSURE ASSESSMENT: breathing-zone air sampling (within 10 in. of nose/mouth) using OSHA-method sampling trains; AIHA-accredited laboratory analysis; chain-of-custody documentation required for OSHA defensibility. CRITICAL DISTINCTION: ACGIH TLVs are advisory guidelines used to guide internal action levels; OSHA PELs (29 CFR 1910.1000 Tables Z-1/Z-2/Z-3 and substance-specific standards) are legally enforceable — never substitute TLV values for OSHA PELs in compliance documentation or citations. HIERARCHY OF CONTROLS (NIOSH): Elimination > Substitution > Engineering Controls > Administrative Controls > PPE; engineering controls are OSHA's required primary method (1910.1000(c)(1)). MONITORING TRIGGERS: any process generating listed contaminants where exposure potential is uncertain requires initial sampling unless objective data documents compliance. RECORDS: retain air-sampling results, monitoring logs, and medical surveillance records per substance-specific retention requirements (minimum 30 years for carcinogens per 1910.1020).

label: Information Engineering
content: Manufacturing IT/OT data system architectures: (1) ERP systems (SAP, Oracle) — primary system of record for BOMs, routings, purchase orders, and cost accounting; integrates with MES via middleware (ISA-95/IEC 62264 interface model). (2) MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems) — real-time shop floor control layer between ERP and machine controls; manages work orders, labor tracking, WIP, and quality records; examples: Epicor Advanced MFG, Plex, Infor CloudSuite Industrial, SAP MII. (3) DNC servers — distribute NC programs to CNC machines; single source of truth for approved NC code; must version-control programs linked to part number/revision. (4) Historians (OSIsoft PI, Ignition) — time-series data collection from PLCs/SCADA; used for OEE, process parameter trending, and SPC. (5) OT network architecture: ISA-99/IEC 62443 zone model — Level 0 (field devices), Level 1 (PLCs/HMIs), Level 2 (SCADA/DCS), Level 3 (MES), Level 3.5 (DMZ), Level 4 (ERP/IT). Data flows upward through defined interfaces; CUI classification must account for CDI in NC programs, work order technical data, and acceptance records stored across these layers.
summary: Data systems, databases, system analysis/design, data analytics, cloud computing, and ERP system architectures.

label: Information Systems Fundamentals
content: IS components; systems development lifecycle (SDLC); requirements gathering; feasibility analysis; system integration.

label: Database Design & Management
content: Relational model; ER diagrams; normalization; SQL; NoSQL databases; data warehousing; ETL processes; data governance.

label: Data Analytics & Business Intelligence
content: Descriptive, predictive, and prescriptive analytics; dashboards; KPI design; statistical analysis; machine learning fundamentals.

label: Enterprise Resource Planning
content: ERP architecture; module integration; implementation methodology; master data management; ERP for manufacturing.

label: Cloud Computing & Digital Infrastructure
content: Cloud service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS); virtualization; edge computing; cybersecurity fundamentals; IT governance.

label: Industrial IoT & Smart Manufacturing
content: Sensor networks; SCADA/MES; data acquisition; digital twin concepts; Industry 4.0 data architecture.

label: Design & Manufacturing Engineering
summary: Engineering materials, casting, machining, forming, joining, additive manufacturing, manufacturing systems, and Industry 4.0.

label: Engineering Materials
content: Metals, polymers, ceramics, composites; material properties; material selection methodology; material testing.

label: Casting & Molding Processes
content: Sand casting; investment casting; die casting; injection molding; process selection; defect analysis.

label: Machining & Material Removal
content: Turning, milling, drilling, grinding; CNC programming; cutting tool materials; speeds and feeds; surface finish.

label: Forming & Shaping Processes
content: Sheet metal forming; forging; extrusion; rolling; deep drawing; process forces; formability analysis.

label: Joining & Assembly Processes
content: Welding (arc, resistance, laser); brazing; soldering; adhesive bonding; mechanical fastening; joint design.

label: Additive Manufacturing
content: FDM, SLA, SLS, DMLS processes; design for AM; post-processing; qualification; applications in prototyping and production.

label: Manufacturing Systems & Automation
content: Transfer lines; FMS; CIM; robotic systems; PLCs; manufacturing cell design; group technology.

label: Industry 4.0 & Smart Manufacturing
content: Cyber-physical systems; digital twins; AI/ML in manufacturing; predictive quality; autonomous systems; human-robot collaboration.

label: Product Design & Development
summary: Design process, feasibility analysis, DFM/DFA, design for cost, Design for Six Sigma, verification, and testing.

label: Product Design Process
content: Concept generation; concept selection (Pugh matrix); product architecture; detailed design; prototyping; design reviews.

label: Feasibility & Market Analysis
content: Technical feasibility; market research; voice of customer; competitive benchmarking; business case development.

label: Design for Manufacturability & Assembly
content: Design for Manufacturability in low-rate aerospace (10-50 units/variant). THREE-PHASE DFM REVIEW MODEL: (1) Concept DFM (Phase A-B, 8 weeks before PDR): Manufacturing-informed feasibility on design block diagram + BOM; 2-3 day review producing Green/Yellow/Red manufacturability assessment, constraint list (top 3-5 blockers), and cost/schedule risk flags. Green = proceed, Yellow = proceed with mitigation + 4-8 week reserve, Red = design simplification required or business case escalation. (2) Detailed Design DFM (Phase C, 6 weeks before CDR): Process FMEA, tolerance stack-up (Cpk/Ppk for critical fits), supplier concurrence letters, detailed cost/schedule estimates. Requires ≥80% CAD geometry + draft process flow. (3) Production DFM Gate (Phase D, 8-12 weeks before production): FAI results, Ppk ≥1.33 confirmation, production work instructions, supplier readiness. DESIGN CHANGE SEVERITY MATRIX determines formal vs. informal review: geometry impacting process, tolerance tightening on critical fits, material changes, new processes → formal review. Cosmetic geometry, tolerance relaxation, approved alternate materials, torque/clamping updates → informal sign-off. OPERATIONAL RULE: If manufacturing cannot confirm feasibility in <4 hours of analysis, escalate to formal review. DFM SCORECARD CRITERIA: Tolerance criticality (Green >±0.005"/Ppk>1.67, Yellow ±0.003-0.005"/Ppk 1.33-1.67, Red <±0.002"/Ppk<1.33), Process novelty (documented vs. new vs. development-required), Lead time risk (<6 weeks vs. 6-12 vs. >12), Assembly complexity (<5 vs. 5-10 vs. >10 fasteners per interface). MANUFACTURING SIMPLIFICATION PROPOSALS: Alternative Solutions Board where manufacturing proposes tolerance relaxations, process substitutions, or assembly simplifications; design lead approves/rejects with one-line justification; quarterly Chief Engineer review of rejection patterns.
summary: Three-phase DFM review framework (Concept/Detailed/Production) with design change severity matrix, facility capability scorecard, and manufacturing simplification proposal process for low-rate aerospace

label: Design for Cost & Value Engineering
content: Target costing; value analysis/value engineering (VA/VE); cost modeling; should-cost analysis; teardown analysis.

label: Design for Six Sigma
content: DFSS methodology (IDOV/DMADV); robust parameter design; tolerance design; reliability allocation; transfer functions.

label: Verification, Validation & Testing
content: Test planning: define V&V matrix mapping each design requirement to a method (analysis, inspection, demonstration, test); maintain traceability from requirement → test procedure → result → acceptance disposition in QMS records. Design verification methods: structural analysis (FEA), tolerance stack-up, stress margins, fatigue life, thermal/fluid analysis. Environmental testing: vibration (sinusoidal sweep + random per MIL-STD-810G Method 514); thermal cycling (Method 503); humidity, salt fog (Method 509), altitude (Method 500). Accelerated life testing: HALT for failure mode discovery beyond design limits; HASS for production screening within design limits. Regulatory compliance testing: FAA DO-160 (avionics environmental), DO-178C (software), DO-254 (hardware); MIL-PRF tests for military qualification; UL/CE for commercial. Gate discipline: no design review sign-off until V&V matrix is 100% allocated and all Severity I/II anomalies dispositioned.

label: Systems Design & Engineering
content: Systems engineering applied to manufacturing system design: (1) Requirements Analysis — translate product design requirements into manufacturing process requirements (tolerances → machine capabilities, material specs → supplier qualifications). (2) System Architecture — production system layout, material flow, workstation sequencing, automation vs. manual trade studies. (3) Verification & Validation — IQ/OQ/PQ for equipment; process capability studies verify manufacturing system meets product requirements. (4) Lifecycle Costing — total cost of ownership for production equipment, tooling amortization, recurring vs. non-recurring cost allocation. (5) Configuration Management — per EIA-649-1; track relationships between product configuration, process configuration (CNC programs, work instructions, fixture revisions), and tooling configuration. Critical in immature programs where ECOs drive cascading changes across all three configuration domains. NC CODE CONFIGURATION: Version NC programs as [PartNumber]_[ConfigIndex]_[CodeRevision]_[VariantTag].nc; maintain NC Program Register linking each serial number to code revision + ECO history; store active programs on DNC server (single source of truth); archive superseded code immediately upon new release to prevent reversion.
summary: Requirements analysis, system architecting, verification & validation, lifecycle costing, and configuration management.

label: Systems Engineering Fundamentals
content: SE process models (Vee, spiral); systems thinking; stakeholder analysis; concept of operations (ConOps); SE management plan.

label: Requirements Engineering
content: Requirements elicitation; analysis; specification; traceability; requirements management tools; validation of requirements.

label: System Architecture & Design
content: Functional decomposition; interface definition; trade studies; architecture frameworks (DoDAF, SysML); model-based SE.

label: Verification & Validation
content: V&V planning; test, analysis, inspection, demonstration; integration testing; system qualification; acceptance testing.

label: Lifecycle Costing & Affordability
content: LIFECYCLE COSTING & AFFORDABILITY IN AEROSPACE: (1) LIFECYCLE COST PHASES: Development (NRE: design, prototyping, test) → Production (recurring unit cost × quantity + production NRE) → Operations & Support (spares, maintenance, modifications) → Disposal. For LRIP programs, production NRE (tooling, supplier qualification, process development) can equal or exceed direct production labor cost—must be tracked separately. (2) COST MODELS: (a) Parametric CERs: Use weight, complexity factors, material type as independent variables; calibrate from analogous programs. Common aerospace CERs: PRICE, SEER, TruePlanning. Accuracy improves with program-specific calibration data post-Build 3. (b) Analogy: Select 2-3 similar past programs, adjust for known differences (material, complexity, design maturity, production rate). Document adjustment rationale. (c) Engineering build-up: Sum process step labor (from routing/process sheets) + material + overhead. Most accurate for mature processes; in LRIP, supplement with learning curve factors for early builds. (3) DESIGN-TO-COST (DTC): Set unit production cost target early in design; flow down cost targets to subsystems. Track cost estimates vs. targets at each design review (PDR, CDR). In LRIP, distinguish between current LRIP unit cost (includes learning) and projected full-rate unit cost (recurring only). DTC targets should reference full-rate recurring cost. (4) TOTAL OWNERSHIP COST: Production cost + spares provisioning + depot maintenance + field support + modifications over service life. For military programs, typically 60-70% of TOC is O&S, not production. (5) AFFORDABILITY ANALYSIS: Compare program cost estimate against budget profile; identify cost drivers amenable to design trade-offs. Key LRIP affordability risk: prototype-to-production cost growth typically 1.3-1.8× initial estimates due to underestimated supplier qualification, tooling, and process development costs.

label: Configuration & Change Management
content: Configuration and change management for manufacturing work instructions per EIA-649-1. KEY ELEMENTS: (1) Configuration identification — WI version tied to design baseline (DB-1 prototype, DB-2 pilot, DB-3 production); version string encodes tier: WI-[PART]-(DP) for prototype, -(TR) for trial, -(PROD) for production. (2) Change control boards — three-tier approval by impact: minor changes (photo clarity, gage ID) = Team Lead same-day approval; moderate (measurement relocation, parameter tightening) = QE + engineering peer review 2-5 days; major (new special process, design-critical changes) = formal ECO review 10 days. (3) Configuration audits — monthly WI audit reviewing 2-3 procedures for revision accuracy, operator compliance, and alignment with current design baseline. (4) Baseline management — no mid-job version changes; traveler locks WI version at job release; superseded versions removed from all stations on cutover date. (5) Version control visibility — hard-copy WIs use color-coded borders (GREEN=current, YELLOW=superseded-but-valid before cutover, RED=obsolete); digital system enforces version via traveler reference. (6) Impact assessment — ECO triggers WI revision impact analysis: which configurations affected, which serial numbers in-process, operator retraining required, effective date minimum 2 weeks for production tier.

label: Manufacturing Strategy & Planning
content:
Strategic planning for manufacturing systems in low-rate, high-mix aerospace environments. Key elements: (1) Equipment investment strategy — tiered decision framework mapping program maturity (prototype/pilot/production) to equipment class (manual/hybrid/automated) based on design volatility, volume certainty, and cycle time criticality. (2) Technology roadmap — align equipment capability with 3–5 year program pipeline; avoid over-investing in automation before design stability proven. (3) Capital justification — multi-tier ROI models that capture labor savings across all program tiers (committed + pilot + prototype absorption), not just single-program volume. (4) Make-vs-buy for equipment — COTS preferred; custom equipment only when proven on 2+ programs or in-house development fits <6-month window. (5) Facility layout — flexible cell configurations supporting concurrent prototype and production work without cross-contamination of tooling or process parameters.

Standards references: AS9100D Section 7.1 (Resources — infrastructure and equipment planning); SAE AS6500 Section 4 (Manufacturing Management — strategic planning for manufacturing capability); AS9145 Section 4.2 (APQP Planning — manufacturing feasibility and equipment strategy); AIAG APQP Section 1.13 (Management Support — capital equipment planning).
summary: Strategic planning for manufacturing systems — aligning manufacturing capability with business objectives, technology roadmaps, and capacity planning

label: Manufacturing Strategy Formulation
content:
Defining manufacturing mission and objectives; aligning mfg strategy with business strategy; functionality of manufacturing systems (cost, quality, flexibility, delivery); generic strategies and their impact on mfg structure.

Standards references: SAE AS6500 (Manufacturing Management Program — strategic manufacturing planning requirements); AS9100D Section 4.1 (Context of the Organization — understanding internal/external factors affecting manufacturing strategy); AS9100D Section 6.2 (Quality Objectives and Planning — aligning manufacturing goals with quality system); SAE J4000/J4001 (Lean Manufacturing Assessment — strategic lean maturity benchmarking for manufacturing systems).
summary: System: Manufacturing Strategy Formulation

label: Planning Process & Methodology
content:
Defining the planning process; principles of planning; factors affecting planning needs; schools of thought (formal vs. incremental); alternative end states; paradoxes, pitfalls, and implementation difficulties.

Standards references: AS9100D Section 6.1 (Actions to Address Risks and Opportunities — risk-based planning methodology); AS9145 Section 4 (APQP Planning Phase — structured planning process for product quality); SAE AS6500 Section 4 (Manufacturing Management Planning — formal planning process requirements); AS9100D Section 8.1 (Operational Planning and Control — planning for product realization).
summary: System: Planning Process & Methodology

label: Manufacturing Systems Evolution
content:
Evolution from job shop to batch to mass to flexible manufacturing; system functionality tradeoffs; mapping current-state to future-state capability; qualitative decision analysis for system architecture choices.

Standards references: SAE J4000 (Lean Manufacturing Assessment — manufacturing system maturity criteria from traditional to lean/flexible); SAE AS6500 (Manufacturing Management Program — system evolution and capability development requirements); AS9100D Section 8.5.6 (Control of Changes — managing transitions between manufacturing system states).
summary: System: Manufacturing Systems Evolution

label: Operational & Capacity Planning
content:
Operational planning for manufacturing systems in LRIP multi-program environments. Key elements: (1) Rough-cut capacity estimation linking strategic demand tiers (committed production, pilot validation, prototype engineering) to tactical shop-floor execution. (2) Material demand planning must account for supplier batch quantities (20-50 pcs) misaligned with build plans (5-15 pcs/variant) — use THREE-TIER PROBABILITY FORECASTING: Tier A (preliminary, design <75% frozen) ±30% confidence interval, capacity-planning only, no PO authority; Tier B (conditional, FAI active, design 75%+ frozen) ±15% CI, PM authority for purchase up to 1.2× mean forecast; Tier C (firm, FAI complete, Ppk ≥1.33, design frozen) ±5% CI, Production Scheduler authority. Quarterly forecast cycle: Week 1 customer schedule + design maturity review, Week 2 demand generation + supplier lead-time confirmation, Week 3 purchase decision review, Week 4 supplier communication with tier-differentiated forecasts. (3) Planning horizons: 12-week frozen window for committed production, 8-week conditional for pilot, 4-week rolling for prototype. (4) Capacity must reserve buffer for rework/retest material consumption (1.33× multiplier for pilot builds). (5) Link material availability milestones to manufacturing readiness gates (FAI completion, Ppk validation). (6) Material flow logistics: 4-stage receipt-to-workstation pipeline (Receiving/Quarantine → Engineering Verification/ECO Impact Check → Pre-Production Staging/Kitting → Workstation Delivery) with Configuration Index revision matching at each gate. Target 8-12 day receipt-to-workstation lead time; kit pull triggered 5-7 days before scheduled work-cell start. (7) Pre-production inventory management: target <7 days-on-hand for LRIP to minimize carrying cost and design-change-induced obsolescence risk; track monthly scrap cost from ECO-driven material obsolescence (escalate if >$5K/month). (8) Demand forecast governance: Engineering issues Tier A forecasts, PM authorizes Tier B purchases, Scheduler executes Tier C orders — separation prevents premature purchasing commitment during design-volatile phases.

Standards references: AS9100D Section 8.1 (Operational Planning and Control — capacity and resource planning for production); AS9145 Section 5 (Product Design and Development — capacity validation during APQP); SAE AS6500 Section 5 (Manufacturing Capability — capacity assessment and planning requirements); AIAG APQP Section 2.13 (Preliminary Process Capability Study Plan).
summary: System: Operational & Capacity Planning

label: Manufacturing Investment Analysis
summary: Economic evaluation, simulation modeling, and financial justification of manufacturing systems, equipment, and automation investments

label: Dynamic Modeling of Manufacturing Systems

Rough-cut estimation of system performance; high-resolution simulation (discrete event, Monte Carlo); modeling throughput, utilization, WIP, and bottlenecks for proposed manufacturing configurations.

Standards references: SAE AS6500 Section 4.3 (Manufacturing Risk Assessment — modeling and simulation for risk identification); AS9100D Section 6.1 (Actions to Address Risks and Opportunities — systematic risk evaluation for manufacturing investments); ISA-95 (Enterprise-Control System Integration — reference architecture for modeling manufacturing systems).

Selection of benchmark alternatives; incremental comparison of alternatives; cash flow analysis; NPV, IRR, payback period; composite cost of capital; translating operational improvements to financial metrics.

content:
Monte Carlo techniques for capturing deviation; game theory for risk; expected value calculations; decision trees for sequential investment decisions; sensitivity analysis for key assumptions.

Standards references: AS9100D Section 6.1 (Actions to Address Risks and Opportunities — risk-based decision making under uncertainty); AS9145 Section 7.3 (Risk Analysis — APQP risk assessment methods); SAE ARP4761 (Safety Assessment Process — probabilistic risk analysis techniques applicable to manufacturing investment decisions).

Building the business case for manufacturing investments; justifying automation and CIM; quantifying intangible benefits (flexibility, quality, lead time); presenting to management with risk-adjusted projections.

label: Cost Estimating & Control
summary: Manufacturing cost structures, estimating methods, learning curves, budgeting for manufacturing operations, and cost forecasting

label: Cost Structure & Recovery
content:
Types of costs (direct, indirect, fixed, variable); cost centers; components of labor/overhead rates; understanding cost allocation to manufactured products.

Standards references: FAR Part 31 (Contract Cost Principles — allowable cost definitions for government manufacturing contracts); CAS 401-420 (Cost Accounting Standards — cost allocation and accumulation requirements); SAE AS6500 Section 4.2 (Manufacturing Cost Analysis — cost structure requirements); AS9100D Section 7.1.1 (Resources — cost planning for quality system operations).

label: Manufacturing Budgeting
content:
Direct labor determination; expense and hourly rate determination; indirect manufacturing costs; budgeting for indirect labor cost centers; budgeting on automated workcenters (machine-hour vs. labor-hour).

Standards references: CAS 401 (Consistency in Estimating, Accumulating, and Reporting Costs); CAS 402 (Consistency in Allocating Costs); CAS 418 (Allocation of Direct and Indirect Costs); FAR 42.302 (Contract administration — budget monitoring and cost surveillance).

label: Cost Estimating Process
content:
Required input data for estimates; estimating material costs; estimating labor costs; the cost estimate grid; types of costing systems (job order, process, standard, activity-based); cost review process.

Standards references: FAR 15.404 (Proposal Analysis — cost estimating requirements for government contracts); CAS 401 (Consistency in Estimating — estimate methodology must match accumulation practice); SAE AS6500 Section 4.2 (Manufacturing Cost Analysis — cost estimating process requirements); AIAG APQP Section 1.11 (Cost/Timing/Quality Feasibility).

label: Learning Curves
content:
Curve parameters and types (Crawford unit, Wright cumulative average); curve construction procedure; integrating learning curves into cost estimates; realization factors; assumptions and effects of forgetting.

Standards references: MIL-HDBK-1547 (Electronic Parts and Assemblies — learning curve application guidance); FAR 15.404-1(b)(2) (Price Analysis — learning curve adjustments for production quantities); SAE AS6500 Section 5.4 (Production Readiness — learning curve integration with manufacturing maturity assessments).

label: Computer-Assisted Estimating & Forecasting
content:
Parametric cost models; feature-based estimating; computer-assisted cost estimating tools; cost forecasting methods; variance analysis and estimate-at-completion tracking.

Standards references: FAR 15.404-1(b) (Price Analysis Techniques — parametric and computer-assisted estimating methods); SAE AS6500 Section 4.2 (Manufacturing Cost Analysis — computer-aided cost tools); EIA-632 (Processes for Engineering a System — cost modeling integration with systems engineering).
summary: System: Computer-Assisted Estimating & Forecasting

label: Manufacturing Control Systems
summary: Conceptual framework for manufacturing management control — MRP, JIT, TQM, and CIM as integrated control paradigms

label: Manufacturing Control Process
content:
Manufacturing control process (plan-execute-measure-correct) at three levels: Strategic (process capability targets, supplier qualification standards, capital equipment selection), Tactical (SPC implementation, work instruction revision cycles, operator certification programs), Operational (in-process inspection, setup verification, tool wear monitoring, environmental condition checks). Control feedback loop: SPC data, first-article inspection results, nonconformance rates, and field failure data feed back into process parameter adjustment. When field failures occur, manufacturing control must correlate failure mechanisms to specific process parameters (workstation, operator shift, tool condition, material lot) using manufacturing sequence records and traveler data. Process capability assessment (Cpk) must be re-evaluated when field data reveals failures that passed in-process inspection. REAL-TIME OPERATOR FEEDBACK: For Tier 1 processes, build measurement feedback into the operation itself — display measurement results at workstation (tablet/iPad mounted on machine) with color-coded status (GREEN=in-control, YELLOW=trending toward limit at ±0.8×tolerance, RED=out-of-spec STOP). Operator measures every nth part (n=3-5 per run) and receives immediate go/no-go signal. Alert logic should detect both single-point exceedances and trending (3+ consecutive points drifting in same direction). This replaces post-facto charting reviewed days later with actionable real-time process visibility. CLOSED-LOOP FEEDBACK CADENCE: (1) Weekly shop-floor standup (15 min): NCR triage (last 7 days), SPC out-of-control signals, upcoming high-risk builds, action-item follow-up from prior week. Attendees: Production Supervisor, floor QA, lead operators. (2) Monthly Quality Review (60 min): NCR trend Pareto (by defect type, operator, equipment), Ppk trending, Gage R&R status, supplier quality, design-change impact, CAR closure verification. Attendees: QM, Mfg Eng, Chief Eng, Supplier Quality. (3) Quarterly Business Review (90 min): Scrap/rework cost vs budget, process capability summary (% features at Ppk≥1.33), field failure rate, supplier ranking, data system health KPIs. Attendees: Plant Mgr, QM, Eng Mgr, Finance. CRITICAL: Each review level must produce time-bound action items with named owners; unassigned insights decay into unused historical records.

Standards: AS9100D §8.5.1 (production control), §9.1 (monitoring/measurement/evaluation), §10.2 (nonconformity/corrective action feedback); SAE AS6500 §5.1 (process monitoring/correction).
summary: System: Manufacturing Control Process

label: Manufacturing Resource Planning (MRP/MRPII)
content:
Evolution from MRP to MRPII; closed-loop MRP; bill of materials management; capacity requirements planning; MRP system architecture and integration points. MRP SUITABILITY FOR LOW-RATE AEROSPACE: MRP works well when BOMs are frozen and demand is deterministic — applies to Tier 0 committed production. MRP breaks down when BOMs change frequently (prototype/pilot phases) because replanning cascades through dependent demand, creating nervousness. PRACTICAL GUIDANCE: (1) Use MRP for committed production with 6-week ECO freeze windows. (2) Do not run prototype work through MRP — use job shop dispatching with manual material allocation. (3) Pilot production uses hybrid: MRP for long-lead material planning, manual dispatching for shop floor sequencing. (4) BOM management discipline: maintain separate BOM revisions per tier (prototype BOM vs. production BOM); promote only via Design Release Checklist gate. (5) MRP replan should trigger supplier communication within 48 hours if delivery dates shift >1 week.

Standards references: AS9100D Section 8.5.1(d) (MRP/ERP system requirements); ISA-95 / IEC 62264 (MES/MRP integration); SAE AS6500 Section 5.2 (Production Planning and Scheduling).

label: Computer-Aided & Process-Driven DFM
content:
Tools for process-driven design; computer-aided DFM software; traditional design methodologies (DFMA, value engineering, group technology); comparison of DFM methodologies and when to apply each.

CAD/CAM INTEGRATION FOR CNC: CAM systems (NX, Mastercam, Fusion) generate NC code (G-code/M-code) from 3D models. Key decisions: (1) Parametric template programming — define variable-driven templates (pocket depth, hole count, pitch) that auto-regenerate code when parameters change; best for product families with 60-80% common geometry. (2) Discrete variant-specific programming — treat each configuration independently; required for non-parametric geometries (undercuts, complex fillets). (3) Hybrid approach — parametric for core features, discrete for outliers; recommended for LRIP with 10-20 units/variant. Post-processor translates CAM tool paths to machine-specific G-code; must validate axis velocity/acceleration limits, tool change positions, and spindle ramp times against machine capabilities.

Standards references: AS9100D Section 8.3.2 (Design and Development Planning — integration of CAD/CAM/CAPP tools); SAE AS6500 Section 3.1 (Product/Process Integration — computer-aided DFM tool requirements); ISO 10303 / STEP AP242 (Product Data Exchange — CAD/CAM data interoperability for DFM analysis); EIA-649-1 (Configuration Management — DFM change control).

label: Company Standards Programs
content:
Establishing internal manufacturing standards; process specifications; material specifications; workmanship standards; maintaining company standards library; certification of manufacturing processes and personnel. For facilities transitioning from prototype to production: capture tacit operator knowledge into formal procedures by shadowing experienced operators during builds and documenting decision points, adaptation steps, and quality checks they perform implicitly. Common pitfall: writing procedures from engineering intent rather than shop floor reality—always baseline from observed practice, then refine. Operator buy-in requires their participation in procedure authoring per AS9100D Section 7.2 competence requirements.

Standards references: AS9100D Section 7.5 (Documented Information — standards library and document control requirements); AS9100D Section 8.5.1(a) (Documented Procedures and Work Instructions — internal process specification requirements); SAE AS6500 Section 6 (Process Documentation — company manufacturing standards program); NADCAP Process Specifications (requirements for maintaining special process accreditation documentation).

label: Just-in-Time Manufacturing
summary: JIT philosophy, implementation modules, pull systems, setup reduction, and continuous improvement for manufacturing flow

label: JIT Philosophy & Themes
content:

Standards references: SAE J4000 (Identification and Measurement of Best Practice in Implementation of Lean Operation — JIT readiness criteria and 6 elements of lean assessment); SAE J4001 (Implementation of Lean Operation User Manual — detailed assessment procedures); AS9100D Section 8.5.1 (Control of Production — waste elimination in production operations); ISO 9001:2015 Section 10.3 (Continual Improvement).
