summary: Lagging indicators (rework %, design-driven rework ratio, cost variance), leading indicators (post-CDR changes, supplier risk), ROI calculation, and kill criteria for validating DFM review effectiveness

label: Manufacturing Cost Integration in Design Trades
content: Embedding manufacturing cost and schedule impact into design trade studies so design teams see manufacturability implications of performance-driven features. TRADE STUDY DFM SECTION: Each design option evaluated on Performance, Manufacturing Cost Delta, Schedule Impact, Supplier Risk, and Decision Rationale in tabular format. KEY COST METRICS TO INCLUDE: (1) Rework labor cost from prior experience ('previous alignment issue = 8 hrs/unit × 12 units = $6K'). (2) Supplier lead-time cost as schedule delay multiplier ('16-week lead vs. 12-week frozen window = $15K buffer stock or schedule slip'). (3) Tooling amortization across program tiers ('$20K fixture ÷ 50 units = $400/unit'). (4) First-article risk cost ('new supplier = 8-week FAI; if Ppk <1.33 on first run, re-tooling +$10K + 6 weeks'). DECISION THRESHOLD: If manufacturing cost delta >$3K or schedule delta >2 weeks, decision requires documented manufacturing sign-off in meeting minutes. INTEGRATION POINT: Design trade studies reviewed by program team (Design + Manufacturing + Program Manager) before design release. Manufacturing Manager provides cost/schedule estimates within 5 business days of trade study request.
summary: Template and process for including manufacturing cost deltas, schedule impacts, supplier risk, and tooling amortization in design trade studies with decision thresholds requiring manufacturing sign-off

label: Tiered hybrid scheduling architecture
content: Low-rate aerospace facilities running concurrent prototype, pilot, and committed production need a tiered scheduling architecture — not a binary choice between job shop and MRP. TIER 0 (COMMITTED PRODUCTION): MRP-driven. BOMs frozen 12+ weeks before production start. Long-lead material ordered at design baseline (20-24 weeks before first unit). Weekly replan windows; 6-week ECO freeze. Target: ≥95% on-time delivery. TIER 1 (PILOT VALIDATION): Hybrid MRP + job shop. BOMs ~80% frozen. Long-lead material ordered 16-20 weeks before build; non-critical 8-12 weeks. Biweekly replan; 1-week ECO freeze. Schedule buffer = 2-3 weeks for engineering completion variance. TIER 1B (PROTOTYPE): Pure job shop dispatching. BOMs 40-60% frozen; design evolving in real-time. Short-lead or consignment material only — no long-lead procurement. Rolling 2-week plan with daily eng/mfg standup. PROMOTION GATE: Never promote from Tier 1B to Tier 1 without Design Release Checklist (frozen BOM, locked drawing revision, configuration baseline). PRIORITY ORDER: Prototype > Pilot > Committed (by scheduling priority) because slip cascades forward — prototype delay cascades to pilot then committed production. RESOURCE ALLOCATION: Prototype gets first call on shared equipment 1-2 shifts/week; committed production gets 60-70% of remaining capacity.
summary: System: Tiered hybrid scheduling architecture (Tier 0 MRP / Tier 1 hybrid / Tier 1B job shop) matching planning rigor to design maturity, with promotion gates, priority rules, and resource allocation guidelines

label: Low-rate production batch sizing
content: Batch sizing for low-volume aerospace (10-15 units per variant) must balance three competing factors: setup cost amortization, learning curve capture, and ECO exposure. LEARNING CURVE: Aerospace assembly typically follows 80-90% learning curves — Unit 1 baseline, Unit 2 at 85-90% of Unit 1 hours, Units 3-10 plateau around 75-80%. Most learning captured in first 3-5 units. DECISION RULE: Minimum economic batch = setup hours / learning-hour benefit per unit (both at labor rate). Example: 5-hour setup at $60/hr = $300; learning benefit of 2-4 hrs/unit — minimum batch ~3-5 units. BATCH SIZING BY TIER: Pilot batch = 3-5 units (1 test article, 1 backup, 1 FAI documentation, captures initial learning). Committed production batch = 4-6 units per variant per 2-3 week window (amortizes setup, captures learning curve, limits ECO rework exposure). ECO EXPOSURE TRADE-OFF: Small batches (3 units) = lower sunk cost if mid-batch design change; large batches (10 units) = better learning amortization but higher rework risk. Sweet spot for Tier 1 production is 4-6 units. VARIANT SEQUENCING: When running multiple configuration variants through shared workstations, group same-variant units to capture learning, then change over. Sequence variants to minimize fixture/tooling changeovers. REWORK BUFFER: Reserve one batch (4-6 units) in schedule as rework buffer positioned after pilot test completion window — absorbs ECO-driven rework without impacting customer delivery dates.
summary: System: Batch sizing framework for low-rate aerospace balancing setup cost, 80-90% learning curve capture, and ECO rework exposure — pilot batches of 3-5, committed batches of 4-6, with variant sequencing and rework buffer guidance

label: Design release gates for material procurement
content: Two-gate material procurement system synchronized with design maturity to prevent premature ordering. GATE 1 — LONG-LEAD RELEASE (LLR): Triggered at ~70% BOM freeze. Identifies parts with supplier lead times >12 weeks. Engineering releases preliminary specifications sufficient for supplier quoting and raw material procurement. Supplier confirms actual lead time (not assumption) and FAI commitment. If supplier quotes >24 weeks, escalate immediately — find alternate supplier or negotiate consignment. Timing: Must occur 24-30 weeks before first production unit needed. GATE 2 — DESIGN BASELINE RELEASE (DBR): Triggered at 100% BOM freeze. Final drawing revision locked; production quantity confirmed. Triggers Tier 1 production-intent material procurement. All supplier POs reference final drawing revision. Timing: Must occur 20-24 weeks before first production unit. BETWEEN GATES: Prototype builds use stock, expedited material, or consignment only — never long-lead production-intent material. This prevents design-change-driven write-offs. SUPPLIER ENGAGEMENT: At LLR — negotiate split shipments (e.g., 20 units at Week 28 + 30 units at Week 32) to reduce inventory holding cost and ECO exposure. At DBR — supplier confirms final order and production start; flags FAI findings affecting design. TIMELINE EXAMPLE (first unit needed Week 52): Week 8 = LLR; Week 12 = prototype builds from stock; Week 20-24 = DBR; Week 24-28 = material receipt + pilot build + FAI; Week 36-44 = pilot test results trigger Tier 0 committed orders.
summary: System: Two-gate material procurement (LLR at 70% BOM freeze, DBR at 100% freeze) synchronized with design maturity to prevent premature long-lead ordering, with split-shipment negotiation and timeline template

label: Supplier Quality Agreement structuring
content: Structure Supplier Quality Agreements (SQAs) for LRIP programs with immature supply base. KEY SQA SECTIONS: (1) Documentation & traceability obligations — revision control on all manufacturing instructions, heat/lot traceability from raw material cert through finished part, AS9102 Form 1 with each shipment, monthly SPC data submission by 5th of following month, 48-hour nonconformance notification with NCR + root cause within 5 business days. (2) Cost transparency — quoted price must separate baseline production cost, quality overhead (inspection labor, SPC collection, 100% inspection during ramp-up), and qualification cost (trial-run parts, P-FMEA effort, audit participation). Design change quotes must itemize rework labor, retool cost, lead-time delta, and FAI/capability restart costs. (3) Audit & inspection access rights — announced audits every 6 months (Tier A) or quarterly (Tier B); supplier provides floor access + process documentation + SPC charts + traceability records within 48 hours of request. 100% incoming inspection first 30 production units, then transition to ANSI/ASQ Z1.4 Level II sampling. (4) Corrective action expectations — CAR response within 5 business days for major findings, 10 days for minor; effectiveness verification required before closure. (5) Design change responsiveness — cost/lead-time quote within 3 business days of ECR receipt. BALANCING SUPPLIER RESISTANCE: Frame cost transparency as mutual benefit (joint improvement opportunities); offer supply-base development incentives (training, process improvement funding); reference contractual flow-down obligations (FAR/DFARS, AS9100D).
summary: Task: Structure Supplier Quality Agreements covering documentation, cost transparency, audit rights, corrective action terms, and design change responsiveness for LRIP programs with immature suppliers

label: Design change flow to mid-production suppliers
content: Procedure for flowing engineering changes to suppliers mid-production on prototype or pilot builds. STEP 1 — Impact Assessment (Engineering + Quality, <2 business days): Determine if change affects supplier process capability, tooling, setup time, or lead-time. Classify: Minor (cosmetic, tolerance widening) = PFAI acceptable per AS9102 Rev C; Major (tolerance tightening >5%, material change, process method change) = full FAI restart + supplier trial-run restart (n=10-15 parts). STEP 2 — Supplier Cost & Lead-Time Quote (<5 business days): Supplier itemizes rework labor on in-process parts, retool cost, trial-run parts, lead-time impact. Flag if change occurs <2 weeks before scheduled delivery. Escalate if cumulative impact across >3 suppliers exceeds $50K or >30-day schedule slip. STEP 3 — Authorization Gate (PM + Quality + Engineering + Supply Chain): Prototype phase (DB-1): authorize if PFAI acceptable + lead-time <2 weeks + cost <$10K/supplier. Pilot phase (DB-2): authorize ONLY if flight-test-driven or safety-critical + lead-time <4 weeks + no Ppk capability loss. Post-DB-2 production: PROHIBITED except red safety ECOs per MIL-STD-882E. STEP 4 — Supplier Notification (<1 day): Revised drawing with delta marked, tolerance change summary, updated P-FMEA KCs. Supplier confirms capability within lead-time and identifies FAI/PFAI requirement. STEP 5 — Revalidation Sign-Off (<10 days from submission): Review AS9102 forms, confirm SPC control limits revised if process parameters changed, authorize production release. REVALIDATION TRIGGER DECISION: Changes warranting supplier process revalidation before production release include tolerance tightening, material substitution, process method change, and tooling redesign. Changes NOT warranting revalidation: cosmetic finish updates, tolerance widening, documentation-only revisions, and non-KC dimension changes.
summary: Procedure: Step-by-step process for flowing design changes to suppliers mid-production, including impact classification (minor=PFAI vs major=full FAI restart), authorization gates by program phase, and revalidation trigger criteria

label: Contingency supplier qualification strategy
content: Contingency and backup supplier qualification for single-source risk in LRIP programs. RISK CATEGORIES: Category A (Single-Source, High-Risk) — only one qualified supplier + lead-time >12 weeks OR quality excursion history; activate contingency qualification at program kickoff. Category B (Dual-Source by Design) — split first 30 units across 2 suppliers when lead-time >8 weeks AND part criticality Medium/High AND Ppk <1.67. Category C (Make vs Buy) — for ultra-critical components where no backup supplier exists and primary Ppk <1.33 after Phase 2, assess internal manufacturing feasibility. CONTINGENCY TRIGGERS: Any Ppk decline to <1.33, escape rate >1% (2-month average), capacity constraint causing >3 week lead-time slip, NCM cost >$20K in 6 months, or ≥4 major audit findings unresolved. ACTIVATION PROCEDURE: (1) Identify 2nd supplier (Tier B acceptable, even if higher cost). (2) Execute Phase 1 capability trial in parallel (4-8 weeks); cost shared 50% program/supplier development budget. (3) Maintain 2nd supplier on standby with drawing/process baseline current; quarterly process confirmation even without active orders. (4) Contingency cost: ~$30-50K per supplier qualification (labor + trial-run + travel); justified against 8-week sole-source outage cost. CONTINGENCY REGISTER: Monthly update tracking each single-source supplier's Ppk status, escape rate, lead-time, backup supplier qualification status, last qualification date, and next re-check date. TARGET: Contingency activation rate <10% of single-source suppliers per year.
summary: Procedure: Contingency supplier strategies for single-source risk including risk categories, activation triggers, parallel qualification procedures, and monthly contingency register maintenance

label: Supplier communication and QBR management
content: Supplier communication and Quarterly Business Review (QBR) management for LRIP programs. QBR STRUCTURE (90 min, quarterly): Attendees: Supplier Quality/Ops manager + PM + Quality Manager + Supply Chain Manager. Agenda: (1) Performance scorecard review (15 min) — present dashboard metrics with trend commentary; fact-based tone, recognize improvements. (2) Quality issue deep-dive (20 min) — review 2-3 significant NCRs; confirm CAR effectiveness; probe repeat NCRs using PDCA sequence. (3) Upcoming design changes & volume commitments (15 min) — inform supplier of pending ECOs, provide 6-month volume forecast, ask about capacity constraints. (4) Continuous improvement & cost reduction (15 min) — if NCM cost >5%, request process improvement proposal; set shared 6-month improvement targets; celebrate wins. (5) Audit planning & compliance (10 min) — confirm next audit date, review prior findings closure. (6) Relationship & strategic alignment (15 min) — solicit supplier feedback on our performance as customer; discuss growth plans. DIFFICULT CONVERSATION PROTOCOLS: Repeat NCR — tone: 'Help us understand what happened'; sequence: review original CAR, ask for effectiveness data, probe why defect recurred, demand new plan with verification date. Capability drift — tone: 'Trend is concerning, let's problem-solve before we hit a wall'; explore tooling wear, material variability, operator rotation; offer joint improvement support. Cost/lead-time disputes — request detailed cost breakdown; explore alternatives (defer change, narrow ECO scope, volume commitment). KEY PRINCIPLE: Position as long-term partner, not transactional buyer; builds goodwill for tough conversations. Avoid adversarial dynamics by leading with data and shared improvement goals.
summary: Task: Structure supplier communication through Quarterly Business Reviews and difficult conversation protocols. USE CASE: supplier relationship management and quality issue resolution — NOT for executive portfolio strategy or program concentration risk decisions.

label: Multi-program supplier capacity coordination
content: Coordinate supplier demands across multiple concurrent programs at different maturity levels to avoid conflicting demands exceeding supplier capacity. CONFLICT SCENARIO: When prototype (DB-1) and pilot production (DB-2) programs compete for same supplier capacity. DECISION RULES: (1) Check frozen schedule windows — production release windows are LOCKED; prototype has scheduling flexibility (2-3 week deferral acceptable). (2) Apply program tier priority: Tier 0 (mature production) > Tier 1 (pilot/trial-run) > Tier 1B (prototype). Pilot production takes priority; defer prototype orders to flex allocation. (3) If both programs in frozen windows — assess operator utilization: >85% → escalate to PM, consider contingency supplier activation; 65-85% → conditional approve with weekly monitoring; <65% → approve immediately. (4) Verify changeover time budget: if cumulative weekly changeover >20 hours, escalate to PM. (5) Heijunka capacity allocation: 40% reserved Tier 0, 30% Tier 1, 20% flex (Tier 1B + rework), 10% constraint buffer. MULTI-PROGRAM DASHBOARD: Monthly consolidated view per supplier showing each program's phase, part numbers, volumes, schedule status, Ppk, 100% inspection status, pending design changes, and capacity risk. COMMUNICATION: Transparent to both program teams — 'Program B prototype material deferred 2 weeks due to Program A production release window; no impact to DB-1 baseline.' Prevents programs from independently overloading suppliers without visibility into cross-program demands.
summary: Task: Coordinate supplier capacity across concurrent programs using tier priorities, frozen window rules, and Heijunka capacity allocation to prevent conflicting demands

label: Measurement system selection and uncertainty analysis
content: Select measurement equipment and validate measurement uncertainty for each tolerance class. EQUIPMENT SELECTION BY TOLERANCE: Ultra-critical (<±0.002"): CMM with temperature-controlled environment, U95 ≤0.0003"; Critical (±0.002"–±0.005"): CMM or precision gage, U95 ≤0.0008"; Major (±0.005"–±0.015"): CMM standard, caliper with statistical check, or vision system, U95 ≤0.002"; Functional (±0.015"–±0.050"): optical or vision gage, U95 ≤0.005"; Non-critical (>±0.050"): manual gage/caliper, U95 ≤0.010". MEASUREMENT UNCERTAINTY (per ANSI/ASME B89.7.3.1): U95 = k × √(equipment_uncertainty² + fixturing_error² + operator_variation² + environmental_effects²) where k=2 for 95% confidence. Note: This is a simplified RSS model — full uncertainty budgets per GUM (Guide to Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement) include additional contributors: thermal expansion coefficients, probe qualification error, algorithm uncertainty, and datum establishment repeatability. DISCRIMINATION RATIO: Two common standards — 4:1 ratio (U95 ≤ tolerance/4, per AIAG MSA manual, widely used in automotive/aerospace production) and 10:1 ratio (U95 ≤ tolerance/10, per traditional metrology practice for calibration labs). Select ratio based on application: 4:1 for production acceptance, 10:1 for reference/calibration measurements. If measurement uncertainty exceeds discrimination ratio, either upgrade equipment or implement 100% inspection with guardbanded acceptance limits (accept only if measured value within tolerance minus U95). SETUP TIME CONSIDERATION: CMM setup 10-40 min vs. vision 2-5 min vs. manual <1 min — balance accuracy requirement against throughput for in-process checkpoints.
summary: Task: Select measurement equipment per tolerance class, calculate measurement uncertainty per ANSI/ASME B89.7.3.1, and validate discrimination ratio (4:1 or 10:1) for each feature

label: Gage R&R study and measurement system qualification
content: Conduct Gage Repeatability and Reproducibility (Gage R&R) studies per AIAG MSA Reference Manual 4th Edition before releasing measurement procedures for production. STUDY DESIGN: Measure 10 sample parts × 3 replications × 2-3 operators (minimum). Parts should span 50-100% of expected process variation. CALCULATION: GR&R% = (√(repeatability² + reproducibility²)) / tolerance × 100. Repeatability = equipment variation (same operator, same part, multiple trials). Reproducibility = operator variation (different operators, same part). ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA: GR&R <10% = excellent, approve; 10-30% = acceptable with documentation and process control justification; >30% = unacceptable, upgrade equipment, improve fixture, or retrain operators. LRIP ADAPTATION: When 10 unique parts unavailable (common in low-rate production), use 5 parts × 4 replications or pool parts across variant family sharing same measurement method. Document reduced study limitations. CALIBRATION REQUIREMENTS: CMM — annual calibration by NIST-traceable external lab per ANSI/ASME B89.4.10; Optical/vision — 6-12 month interval using calibrated reticle + dimensional standard; Manual gages — annual calibration against certified gage blocks. Maintain calibration records with equipment ID, calibration date, next due date, and certificate reference. INITIAL EQUIPMENT ACCEPTANCE: For new CMMs, conduct Environmental Acceptance Test (EAT) measuring certified artifact across working volume to establish volumetric uncertainty baseline. Standards: ANSI/ASME B89.4.10 (CMM performance), ANSI/ASME B89.7.3.1 (decision rules for measurement uncertainty), AIAG MSA 4th Ed (Gage R&R methodology).
summary: Task: Conduct Gage R&R studies per AIAG MSA, qualify measurement systems with GR&R <10% target, manage calibration intervals, and validate equipment acceptance per ANSI/ASME B89.4.10

label: In-process inspection point determination
content: Determine in-process dimensional inspection checkpoints using gate-based model to prevent drift propagation to downstream assembly. FOUR GATES: Gate 1 (Receiving): Supplier parts — 100% visual + sample dimensional (n=min(5, lot_size)) for critical features; new suppliers 100% receiving first 25 units; established suppliers with Cpk>1.33 sample per AQL-II. Gate 2 (Post-Machining): Tolerance <±0.010" measure every 5th part or after setup change; known drift risk features measure every part or every 3rd; datum references verify after machining. Gate 3 (Post-Assembly): Tolerance stack-ups involving multiple components measure 3-5 units/batch; known assembly variability measure 1st and last per shift minimum. Gate 4 (Final Acceptance): Batch ≤8 units 100% critical features; batch 8-15 sample n=5 critical + 100% major; batch >15 sample per AQL-II. COST JUSTIFICATION: Approve in-process checkpoint if E[rework_cost] > 3× E[inspection_cost], where E[rework] = P(defect) × rework_cost + P(escape) × field_failure_cost. TIMING: Complete dimensional acceptance within 24 hours of assembly completion — prevents shipping non-compliant units. MEASUREMENT EQUIPMENT AT GATES: Tight tolerance gates use CMM; medium tolerance gates use vision/optical (faster setup); loose tolerance gates use manual gages. Balance accuracy requirement against throughput — CMM adds 10-40 min setup vs. portable gage <1 min.
summary: Task: Determine in-process inspection gates (receiving, post-machining, post-assembly, acceptance) using cost-justified checkpoint selection where rework cost exceeds 3× inspection cost

label: CMM programming strategy for multi-variant part families
content: Design modular CMM programming when configuration variants share datum references but differ in feature geometry. MASTER PROGRAM STRUCTURE: (1) Datum establishment module (shared across all variants) — measure datum A (plane, 7+ probe points), datum B (perpendicular, 5+ points), datum C (bore axis, 10+ points at multiple Z heights). (2) Common feature module — hole patterns, boss heights, parallelism shared across variants. (3) Variant-specific subroutines — called via IF/THEN logic or operator prompt; each subroutine measures variant-unique features with appropriate probe selection and point density. FIXTURE DESIGN: 3-2-1 locating principle (face + edge + hole); repeatability requirement <0.0005" repositioning error; accommodate variants via adjustable clamping or quick-change inserts; verify all critical features accessible without fixture repositioning. PROGRAM VALIDATION: Run 3 times on calibration artifact before production use; verify repeatability σ < U95/3; verify no probe crash or fixture interference; require L3 inspector or engineer sign-off. FILE MANAGEMENT: Master program + variant subfolder structure; each variant folder contains program file, fixture datum offset data, and GR&R validation report. CONFIGURATION CONTROL: CMM program revision linked to part drawing revision and ECO number; program changes require re-validation when ECO affects measured features or datum structure.
summary: Decision: Design modular CMM programs with shared datum establishment, common feature modules, and variant-specific subroutines; validate with fixture repeatability <0.0005" and program revision linked to ECO configuration control

label: Inspector training and certification program
content: Establish three-level inspector certification for dimensional verification tasks. LEVEL 1 (Production Inspector): Authority — measure non-critical features, accept/reject per clear go/no-go gages. Training — 40 hrs classroom (GD&T basics per ASME Y14.5, caliper/gage use, drawing interpretation) + 20 hrs supervised hands-on. Qualification — written exam 80% passing + practical measurement of 5 known-value standards within 0.0005" error. Audit — annual blind verification. LEVEL 2 (CMM Operator/Process Inspector): Authority — operate CMM, interpret complex GD&T (position, runout, profile), approve batches, identify measurement system issues. Training — L1 + 60 hrs advanced (CMM operation, datum establishment, tolerance stack-up, measurement uncertainty, SPC basics) + 40 hrs CMM programming. Qualification — CMM technical exam + demonstrate CMM program for assigned part family. Audit — semi-annual blind CMM verification on 2 assigned parts. LEVEL 3 (Measurement System Owner/FAI Lead): Authority — approve measurement procedures, conduct GR&R studies, design CMM programs, approve FAI/PFAI, investigate measurement escapes. Training — L2 + 80 hrs measurement science (ANSI/ASME B89, uncertainty analysis, calibration standards, advanced GD&T). Qualification — lead GR&R study + conduct FAI revalidation. Audit — quarterly GR&R study presentation to Quality Manager. CERTIFICATION: Annual renewal for all levels; each inspector authorized for specific part families and measurement equipment; sign-off by Quality Manager with valid-until date.
summary: Task: Three-level inspector certification (L1 production, L2 CMM/GD&T, L3 measurement system owner) with training hours, qualification exams, and annual renewal requirements

label: Dimensional control performance monitoring and feedback
content: Establish monthly dimensional quality dashboard and field-failure correlation feedback loop. DASHBOARD METRICS: (1) Nonconformance rate (target <1%): total dimensional rejects / total units produced. (2) FAI/PFAI completion rate and overdue items (>2 weeks). (3) In-process measurement efficiency: actual vs. budgeted measurement time per equipment type (CMM, vision, manual). (4) Measurement system validation status: current GR&R results, calibration compliance %. (5) Supplier dimensional performance: per-supplier nonconformance rate, FAI status, receiving inspection plan tier. (6) Field failure count with dimensional root cause. (7) Inspection labor allocation: actual vs. budget by activity (CMM programming, in-process, receiving, FAI documentation). (8) Design change impact: ECO count, PFAI-eligible vs. full FAI required, projected labor savings. FEEDBACK LOOP: Monthly — correlate field failures to measurement system used at acceptance; identify measurement procedure gaps (features not measured, inadequate probe strategy). Quarterly — review tolerance sensitivity vs. measurement capability; identify features where equipment discrimination ratio is inadequate. Semi-annual — full measurement system audit (GR&R revalidation, calibration compliance). Annual — inspector certification renewal, equipment investment review against variant introduction forecast. ESCALATION: When field failure traced to measurement gap → issue CMM program update + retrain inspectors + increase measurement frequency temporarily. When field failure traced to tolerance inadequacy → escalate to design change (ECO).
summary: Task: Monthly dimensional quality dashboard with nonconformance rate, FAI status, measurement efficiency, supplier performance, and field-failure correlation feedback loop for continuous measurement system improvement

label: Excess inventory disposition and obsolescence protocol
content: Excess inventory disposition for LRIP aerospace when ECOs, program cancellations, or demand shifts create surplus. THREE-TIER DISPOSITION PROTOCOL: Tier 1 Immediate (<1 week): Triggered when ECO makes >50% of on-hand incompatible or program cancelled. Decision authority: PM + Chief Engineer + Finance. Options: (a) Scrap and write off against program cost, (b) Retrofit if cost <$50/unit, (c) Reallocate to compatible program via CBR cross-reference, (d) Salvage sale (aluminum scrap ~$0.10-0.20/lb). Tier 2 Aging (4-8 week review): Triggered when material unallocated >8 weeks with no pending PO. Decision tree by quantity/cost: <50 units + <$500 keep in excess pool; 50-200 units $500-$5K flag for reallocation within 2 weeks; >200 units + >$5K escalate to VP Operations for supplier return (negotiate restocking fee) or donation. Tier 3 Program Suspension: Immediately freeze new procurement; audit inventory splitting locked-to-program materials (scrap/donate) from cross-program materials (reallocate via CBR update). MONTHLY AGED INVENTORY REPORT: CBR query for parts with 'Unallocated' status >8 weeks; include in forecast review meeting agenda. DOCUMENTATION: ECO register + Finance write-off memo + supporting photos/certificates for AS9100D audit trail. KPI: Obsolescence loss target <0.5% of material cost; >1% indicates design change process out of control.
summary: System: Three-tier excess inventory disposition protocol (immediate/aging/suspension) with decision authority, cost thresholds, and obsolescence KPI targets for LRIP aerospace

label: WIP aging and design-mismatch prevention
content: Work-in-process aging management for 8-16 week LRIP manufacturing lead times where mid-build ECOs create design-mismatch risk. WIP CONFIGURATION TRACKING: Every build order links to CBR with: serial unit → configuration index (CI) → applied ECOs → material lot allocations → build start/planned completion dates. When ECO issued, automated CBR query identifies all affected WIP orders by part number + CI baseline + serial range within 24 hours. DECISION FRAMEWORK for ECO-affected WIP: (1) If WIP <25% complete and retrofit cost >50% of unit material cost → scrap WIP, restart with updated materials. (2) If WIP 25-75% complete → halt assembly, assess retrofit feasibility (labor hours + material swap cost); proceed if retrofit <$50/unit. (3) If WIP >75% complete → complete current build, retrofit post-assembly if safety-critical; defer retrofit if cosmetic/non-critical. WIP AGING KPIs: Target 0 WIP orders >8 weeks old without approved ECO status. Flag when WIP order >6 weeks old AND design change rate >1 ECO/week. PREVENTION: Align manufacturing batch starts with design freeze windows — do not release build orders for design-volatile assemblies until DB-2 freeze confirmed. For hybrid materials, release raw material machining early but hold final assembly until configuration locked. TRACEABILITY: Build order references CI revision + material lot numbers; configuration audit at 50% completion milestone verifies no design-mismatch before irreversible assembly operations.
summary: System: WIP aging management with ECO-affected disposition framework, completion-percentage decision thresholds, design-mismatch prevention through freeze-window alignment, and mid-build configuration audit

label: LRIP inventory cost tracking and financial KPIs
content: Materials management cost tracking by program for LRIP aerospace. PROGRAM MATERIAL COST LEDGER (monthly): Direct materials (purchased parts + raw material + WIP at completion %), carrying costs (storage, insurance, obsolescence reserve — target <7% of inventory value), procurement costs (expediting charges, excess MOQ absorption, single-source contingency premiums). FINANCIAL KPIs: Material cost as % of revenue — target <45% (aerospace standard 40-50%); expediting cost ratio — target <2% of total material spend; inventory carrying cost — target <7% of total inventory value; obsolescence loss — target <0.5% of material cost (>1% = design change process issue); cash-to-cash cycle — target <90 days for LRIP. OPERATIONAL KPIs: Material availability ≥98% of scheduled production starts; on-time supplier delivery ≥95%; forecast accuracy MAPE <15% for Tier 1/2; inventory turns 2.0-2.5; days inventory outstanding 150-180 days (aligned with 16-26 week supplier lead times); design-mismatch errors <2% of WIP orders. RISK KPIs: Single-source material exposure <15% of procurement spend; WIP aging >8 weeks without ECO resolution = 0; storage capacity utilization 70-85%. RED FLAGS: Chronic shortages + weekly expedites = forecast/supplier issue; inventory turns <1.5 + 6-month aging = over-procurement; storage >90% consistently = expand or reduce advance procurement; MAPE >20% + expediting >5% = broken demand planning.
summary: System: Program-level material cost ledger structure with financial KPIs (carrying cost, expediting ratio, obsolescence), operational KPIs (availability, turns, MAPE), and red-flag diagnostic thresholds for LRIP inventory strategy assessment

label: Field Failure Investigation Board (FFIB) intake and triage procedure
content: FIELD FAILURE INVESTIGATION BOARD (FFIB): Cross-functional team (Quality, Manufacturing Engineering, Design Engineering, Supply Chain, Program Management) convenes within 24–48 hours of field failure report. INTAKE CHECKLIST: (1) Serial number, production date, operational hours at failure; (2) Failure description with photographs and measurements; (3) Environmental exposure history (temperature, humidity, vibration, maintenance records); (4) Operational context (normal use vs. documented anomalies); (5) Current inventory status — units delivered, in-build, and not-yet-started with same configuration. TRIAGE CLASSIFICATION: TIER 1 (Safety Critical) — structural/control/propulsion failure, multiple units, fleet-wide risk → engineering hold + field service bulletin evaluation within 48 hours. TIER 2 (Operational Impact) — reduced capability, mission abort, no structural risk → investigation within 1 week, interim containment (increased inspection, operational limits). TIER 3 (Maintenance/Economic) — increased maintenance cost, no flight safety impact → investigation within 4 weeks, prioritized by failure frequency trend. INTERIM ACTION: Continue production with increased inspection (e.g., 100% verification instead of sampling) until root cause determined; do not halt production unless TIER 1 confirmed.
summary: Procedure: FFIB convenes within 24-48h of field failure; intake checklist captures serial, environment, operational context; triage into Tier 1 (safety-critical, 48h), Tier 2 (operational, 1 week), Tier 3 (economic, 4 weeks)

label: Design vs. manufacturing root cause distinction procedure
content: DISTINGUISHING DESIGN FROM MANUFACTURING ROOT CAUSE — critical for determining corrective action scope. MANUFACTURING ROOT CAUSE INDICATORS: (a) SPC data shows process drift toward limit during affected production period; (b) supplier CoC shows nonconformance corrected via rework; (c) inspection method inadequate (e.g., visual instead of hardness test); (d) identical failure signature on same workstation/operator/shift; (e) design operating within nominal but manufacturing tolerance drifted causing latent defect. DESIGN INADEQUACY INDICATORS: (a) failure occurs across multiple supplier lots and manufacturing periods; (b) stress analysis shows component exceeds design margin under documented operational loads; (c) similar components from different manufacturers fail identically; (d) failure mode not captured in design verification test plan (missed environmental condition or load case); (e) manufacturing process capable and in statistical control but part still fails. PROCEDURE: (1) Retrieve SPC charts and raw measurement data from manufacturing period; (2) Perform failure analysis — fractography (SEM), material analysis (hardness, composition, metallography), dimensional analysis; (3) Compare failure mechanism to design validation test coverage; (4) If failure crosses multiple suppliers/lots → design issue; if failure clusters in specific lot/workstation/period → manufacturing issue. CRITICAL: Do not accept generic root causes ('operator error', 'material variation') — identify specific assignable cause (which operator shift, which supplier lot, which process step drifted, which design assumption was invalid).
summary: Procedure: Systematic method to distinguish manufacturing root cause (process drift, supplier lot, inspection gap) from design inadequacy (cross-supplier failures, exceeded design margin, validation gap) using SPC data, failure analysis, and supplier correlation

label: Supplier traceability and lot correlation for field failures
content: COMPONENT GENEALOGY RECORD for field failure traceability must link failed part to: (1) Supplier lot/batch number and serial range; (2) Supplier certificate of conformance (CoC) and material certifications; (3) Manufacturing workstation, operator, shift that assembled the unit; (4) In-process inspection records (FAI, setup verification, sampling data); (5) Test/verification step completion records (pressure test, vibration, thermal cycle); (6) Environmental conditions during manufacturing (temp, humidity, cleanliness); (7) Rework/repair history (was component reworked, by whom, scope). CORRELATION PROCEDURE: (a) Match failed component serial to manufacturing traveler/routing sheet; (b) Extract supplier lot, inspection records, operator initials, test completion dates; (c) If supplier lot contains multiple units, immediately quarantine identical components from same lot in inventory and in-build aircraft; (d) Retrieve SPC charts from manufacturing period — look for out-of-control signals, tool wear, material lot changes, setup changes; (e) Cross-reference supplier quality record — prior 6 months of nonconformities on same feature from that supplier. FIELD RETURN PROTOCOL: Failed component must be returned in controlled condition (sealed container, no cleaning, no additional handling) to manufacturing site or authorized failure analysis lab. Photograph as-received before any analysis.
summary: Procedure: Trace field-failed components to specific supplier lot, manufacturing workstation, operator, and process step using component genealogy records; quarantine identical components; retrieve SPC data from manufacturing period

label: Field failure trending dashboard and systemic issue detection
content: MONTHLY FIELD FAILURE DASHBOARD metrics: (1) Failure count by system/component (propulsion, fuel, hydraulic, structure, avionics) — 12-month trend; (2) Failure count by manufacturing workstation and supplier — identify if single supplier accounts for disproportionate failures; (3) Time-to-failure distribution (median operational hours before failure) — clustering suggests design validation gap; (4) Failure mode categorization (fatigue, corrosion, dimensional, contamination) — multiple unrelated failures sharing environmental exposure pattern; (5) Serial number correlation — units from same manufacturing period failing more frequently. THRESHOLD-BASED ESCALATION: ≥3 identical failure modes in same system within 12 months → trend investigation and design/manufacturing review; single supplier >30% of field failures → supplier corrective action demand, consider supplier change; failure rate increasing over time (more in year 3 than year 1) → systemic aging/fatigue issue, design life may be insufficient; failure clustering in specific serial number range → manufacturing process change or supplier lot issue at that production window. PARALLEL-PATH TRENDING: Do not wait for individual failure RCA to close before examining trends — run trend analysis concurrently. SYSTEMIC EFFECTIVENESS METRICS (annual): Recurrence rate (target <5% of closed CARs with identical reoccurrence within 12 months); time to RCA completion (target <3 weeks Tier 1/2, <8 weeks Tier 3); trend detection lag (target: identify pattern within 3 failures).
summary: Procedure: Monthly dashboard tracking failures by component/supplier/workstation/serial range with threshold-based escalation triggers; systemic effectiveness metrics including recurrence rate and trend detection lag

label: Mid-production field failure decision protocol
content: SCENARIO: Field failure discovered when units 1–N delivered, units N+1–M in-build, units M+1–P not started. THREE-PHASE DECISION PROTOCOL: PHASE A (24–48h): FFIB convenes; interim decision — continue with increased inspection (100% verification instead of sampling) until root cause determined; communicate interim action to Program Manager, Customer, affected suppliers. PHASE B (Day 3–7, root cause determined): If manufacturing root cause, limited scope → implement corrective manufacturing control, inspect in-build units, retrofit delivered units. If manufacturing root cause, broad scope → halt production, quarantine in-build units, issue rework/disposition, retrofit delivered units. If design inadequacy → halt production, implement design change (ECN), retrofit all delivered units, resume only after design change qualification. PHASE C (Retrofit & Liability): Manufacturing-confirmed root cause → manufacturing absorbs retrofit cost (supplier may share if supplier is root cause via SCAR with cost liability clause). Design root cause → design organization absorbs retrofit cost. Shared root cause (marginal design + inadequate manufacturing) → negotiate split. COST RECOVERY: Supplier root cause >$100K → engage Legal for cost recovery via contract. Document all decisions in FFIB minutes with rationale to prevent revisiting disputes. CRITICAL: Do not accept 'continue unchanged' without explicit risk acceptance sign-off from Program Manager and Quality Manager.
summary: Procedure: Three-phase decision protocol for field failures discovered mid-production — interim containment (24-48h), root-cause-driven production decision (3-7 days), and retrofit/liability cost allocation framework

label: Rework Disposition & Authorization
content: REWORK DISPOSITION SYSTEM for LRHM aerospace manufacturing where rework labor typically exceeds scrap/replacement cost. REPAIR VS. SCRAP DECISION MATRIX by defect type: (1) Dimensional out-of-spec — repair if labor < part cost + lead-time buffer AND tolerance restorable; scrap if primary structure or cumulative tolerance stack risk. (2) Material anomaly (porosity, inclusion, crack) — repair if defect <5% material loss AND non-structural location; scrap if safety-critical regardless of size. (3) Surface damage — repair if no form/fit/function impact AND cosmetic restoration feasible; scrap if affects aerodynamic or stress-concentration features. (4) Assembly error — repair if disassembly + re-inspection < replacement cost AND no secondary defect risk (e.g., cracked bushing during fastener removal → scrap). (5) Weld defect — repair only if NADCAP-approved rework procedure exists AND defect <25% weld length; scrap if >25% (efficiency threshold). BUILD-PHASE MODIFIER: Prototype builds — rework acceptance threshold +50% (learning expected); pilot — standard threshold; committed production — threshold tightens 25%, repeat defects trigger process CAPA not rework acceptance. THREE-TIER AUTHORIZATION: Tier 1 (same day) — published Standard Rework Work Instruction (SRWI) exists, defect matches scope, Quality Manager approves without engineering. Tier 2 (1-2 days) — SRWI exists but defect variant outside scope, Manufacturing Engineer + Quality Manager review. Tier 3 (3-5 days) — no SRWI exists or novel defect, Chief Engineer + Quality Manager + trial validation required before production rework authorized. CRITICAL RULE: No production rework authorized for any SRWI until First-Article Rework (FAR) validation completed — 5 trial reworks on scrap parts, all passing re-inspection + secondary defect screening.
summary: Repair vs. scrap decision matrix by defect type, build-phase modifiers, and three-tier rework authorization workflow (QA-only / Eng+QA / Chief Eng) with first-article rework validation gate

label: Rework traceability and field failure correlation
content: AS9100D §8.7 (Nonconforming Output) and §8.5.6 (Control of Changes / Configuration Management) govern rework documentation and traceability requirements. Note: §7.5.3 is 'Control of Documented Information' (document retention, access control) — not product configuration management.
summary: Rework record documentation requirements, searchable ledger database structure, field failure correlation methodology comparing reworked vs. new component reliability, and escalation path for failed rework attempts

label: Standard Rework Work Instructions (SRWI)
content: STANDARD REWORK WORK INSTRUCTIONS (SRWI) provide documented, repeatable rework procedures eliminating engineering judgment at execution. One SRWI per common defect type per part family. SRWI STRUCTURE: (1) Scope — what will/will not be reworked; defect trigger criteria; part family applicability. (2) Prerequisites — defect measurement verified by independent inspector, material cert confirmed, zero secondary defects, FAR validation completed. (3) Rework method — equipment, setup time, cycle time, material removal limits, feed/speed parameters, coolant. (4) Critical hold points — measurement after each pass, visual crack check at 50% removal under 10× magnification, final multi-point measurement. (5) Re-inspection requirements — measurement system (Gage R&R <15%), acceptance criteria (original drawing tolerance), 100% dimensional re-inspection, secondary defect screening (visual 10×, profilometer if surface-critical, metallurgical if weld). (6) Disposition if re-inspection fails — first failure escalate to engineering; second failure mandatory scrap. (7) Rework record — operator ID, start/end time, equipment used, before/after measurements, approver signature. COMMON SRWI TYPES: SRWI-001 Dimensional restoration (grinding, boring, EDM); SRWI-002 Weld repair (NADCAP-approved re-weld + X-ray + post-weld heat treat); SRWI-003 Surface damage restoration (dent removal, anodize reprocess); SRWI-004 Assembly error rework (fastener replacement, re-torque, alignment); SRWI-005 Material anomaly (defect removal via EDM/grinding, metallurgical re-cert if critical). COMPETENCY LEVELS: L1 (assembly rework only), L2 (dimensional rework under SRWI, 4-week CNC + 12 months supervised), L3 (dimensional + surface + oversee L2, 8 weeks advanced + 24 months + NADCAP familiarity), L4 (design/approve novel procedures, degree + 5 years + NADCAP auditor). Certification requires 5 trial reworks on scrap passing re-inspection + 2 supervised production reworks + 1 independent audit. Recertification every 12 months or after 6 months inactivity. DESIGN CHANGE COORDINATION: When ECO releases, review all affected SRWIs — if new tolerance invalidates prior rework acceptance criteria, SRWI must be revised and FAR re-validated before ECO effective date. Assess rework legacy: do previously reworked in-field components still meet new spec?
summary: Standardized rework procedure structure (scope, method, hold points, re-inspection, disposition), five common SRWI types by defect category, operator competency levels L1-L4 with certification requirements, and design change coordination for SRWI currency

label: Manufacturing data architecture and traceability schema
content: Relational data architecture enabling root-cause traceability from nonconformance back to all assignable causes. CORE ENTITIES: PRODUCTION_EVENT (serial number, part/rev, lot, operator_ID, equipment_ID, material_lot, process_phase, shift_date/time, event_type). MEASUREMENT_RESULT (characteristic_ID, measured_value, gage_ID, gage_uncertainty, pass/fail, CMM_program_rev). NCR_EVENT (NCR_number, defect_category, severity [scrap/rework/accept-as-is], root_cause_code [design-ambiguous/tooling-drift/operator-error/material-defect/learning-curve], corrective_action_ID). REWORK_EVENT (original_NCR_ID, SRWI_reference, rework_operator_ID, labor_hours, reinspection_pass_flag, escalation_flag). Supporting entities: OPERATOR (certification_level L1-L4, cert_date_by_operation), EQUIPMENT (machine_type, maintenance_schedule, last_calibration, spindle_hours), MATERIAL_CERT (heat_number, supplier_ID, tensile/hardness/chem from CoC), CONFIGURATION (serial→config_index→applied_ECOs→material_lots→build_phase→field_failure_flag). TIERED DATA COLLECTION: Tier 1 (mandatory, all parts): material traceability, first-piece verification, NCR events, special process certs, tool life/offset data — ~5 min operator burden/shift, mostly automated via MES. Tier 2 (targeted): safety-critical features, supplier Ppk<1.33, >5% nonconformance rate, tolerance≤0.01" — 2-5 min/measurement, 20-40 measurements/year per characteristic. Tier 3 (opportunistic, zero operator burden): CNC probe data, spindle load from PLC historian, gage R&R trending, labor routing actuals — must flow automatically to MES. IMPLEMENTATION: For <500 parts/year, lightweight MES (Airtable/structured spreadsheets with locked templates) sufficient; operator enters only yellow cells. For >2000 parts/year, enterprise MES/ERP. All measurements must link serial→operator→equipment→material_lot→design_rev for full traceability.
summary: Relational data schema design linking production events to operator, equipment, material, and configuration for root-cause traceability, with tiered data collection strategy (mandatory/targeted/opportunistic) scaled to LRHM volumes

label: Nonconformance diagnostic framework (A/B/C categories)
content: Three-category diagnostic matrix for classifying nonconformances into distinct improvement levers. Misdiagnosis wastes corrective-action effort. CATEGORY A — PROCESS CAPABILITY GAP: Symptom: I-MR chart stable (in statistical control) but measurements cluster near spec limit or Ppk<1.33. Root cause: process variation inherently exceeds design tolerance. Evidence: Ppk negative or <1.33; tight tolerance on manual/less-capable process. Response: (1) design concession (relax tolerance if functional margin permits), (2) process upgrade (CNC finishing vs manual), (3) 100% inspection acceptance (scrap/rework out-of-spec), (4) risk acceptance with customer sign-off. Success metric: Ppk≥1.33 on next pilot batch. CATEGORY B — PROCESS CONTROL EXECUTION GAP: Symptom: I-MR chart shows out-of-control signals (beyond ±3σ, trends, cycles); erratic measurements but process COULD be stable. Root cause: operator not following setup SOP, tool offset drift, fixture variation, gage misuse. Evidence: scatter >> capability baseline; assignable cause signals; operator changed between good/bad batches. Response: (1) re-train operator on setup checklist, (2) tighten tool-life rule or offset reverification frequency, (3) Gage R&R study, (4) first-piece verification gate. Success metric: zero control-chart signals in next 20 parts. CATEGORY C — DESIGN DEFINITION AMBIGUITY: Symptom: NCRs cluster at specific feature; root-cause codes inconsistent across operators/inspectors (e.g., 'radius undefined' vs 'surface finish unspecified'). Root cause: drawing annotation unclear, GD&T missing/conflicting, tolerance stack unresolved. Evidence: same feature reworked multiple times with different interpretations; inspector-operator disagreement on conformance. Response: (1) engineering design review, (2) issue EC clarifying feature, (3) PFAI on next pilot part for affected characteristic. Success metric: zero rework on clarified feature in next 10 units. MONTHLY DIAGNOSIS PROCEDURE: Pull 30-day NCR summary (top 3 defect types by count); for each, pull I-MR chart and determine A/B/C; assign corrective action owner (Engineer for A, Floor Supervisor for B, Chief Engineer for C); 30-day close-out verification with re-measured nonconformance rate.
summary: Three-category diagnostic matrix (A: process capability gap, B: process control execution gap, C: design definition ambiguity) with distinct symptoms, evidence, corrective responses, and success metrics for each category

label: Manufacturing data strategy effectiveness measurement
content: KPI framework measuring whether manufacturing data collection actually enables continuous improvement vs accumulating unused records. THREE TIERS OF MEASUREMENT: TIER 1 — DATA SYSTEM HEALTH: MES uptime ≥99% (<1 hr unplanned downtime/month); operator data-entry compliance ≥95% of parts logged; manual entry burden ≤10 min/shift per operator (survey quarterly; >15 min signals need for automation); gage R&R currency 100% of production gages annually re-qualified; data integrity ≥98% complete traceability (spot-audit 50 random parts quarterly for operator_ID, equipment_ID, timestamp, characteristic completeness). TIER 2 — ANALYTICAL RIGOR: Control chart currency (I-MR updated within 3 days of measurement); Ppk confidence interval width ≤0.5 for Tier 2 characteristics (implies n≥20; CI>0.7 = insufficient data); root-cause categorization accuracy 100% NCRs classified A/B/C with QM concurrence; corrective action closure rate ≥80% of CARs closed within target date; re-occurrence rate ≤5% of nonconformance types repeat within 6 months of corrective action. TIER 3 — BUSINESS OUTCOMES: Scrap/rework cost as % of manufacturing cost (target: reduce 30% annually from baseline); on-time delivery (target ≥95%); field failure rate (target: reduce 50% from baseline); process capability (% Tier 2 characteristics at Ppk≥1.33, target ≥85% within 2 years); supplier Tier A % (target ≥70% by year 2); data-driven decision frequency (≥90% of manufacturing decisions justified by control chart, Ppk, or NCR trend — spot-check meeting minutes). DIAGNOSTIC: If Tier 3 flat despite sound Tier 1/2 → implementation discipline problem (escalate to Plant Manager for change management). If Tier 2 fails → analytical discipline (QM + Mfg Eng training). If Tier 1 fails → system reliability (IT/MES admin). QUARTERLY DATA SYSTEM HEALTH REVIEW (90 min, QM + Mfg Eng): Pull Tier 1 metrics, spot-audit integrity, assess Tier 2 trends, compare business outcomes vs baseline, identify gap type, assign improvement actions.
summary: Three-tier KPI framework (data system health, analytical rigor, business outcomes) measuring whether manufacturing data collection enables continuous improvement, with diagnostic logic for identifying whether gaps are in collection, analysis, or implementation

label: Manufacturing knowledge preservation across programs
content: Three-layer data retention strategy preventing capability knowledge loss between production programs. LAYER 1 — HOT DATA (active production, 12-month rolling): Location: production MES with real-time dashboards, control charts, trend reports. Scope: all Tier 1/1B units with full traceability. Access: operators/supervisors (real-time), QM (weekly/monthly). LAYER 2 — WARM DATA (program history, 3-5 year retention): Location: QMS folder structure organized by program: /FAI_Packages, /Control_Plans, /Process_Capability_Studies (Ppk reports, I-MR charts, trial-run data), /Nonconformance_Trending (annual NCR summaries, Pareto charts), /Rework_Ledger, /Design_Changes (ECs + PFAI results), /Field_Failure_Analysis (warranty data linked to serial ranges). Retain full program history through EOL + 3 years. Access: QM, Mfg Eng, Chief Eng, Supplier Quality. Annual review: QM compiles lessons-learned memo for Layer 3. LAYER 3 — COLD DATA (Process Capability Library, permanent): Living document organized by PROCESS FAMILY (not program). Structure: Process Family (e.g., 5-Axis CNC Finishing) → Equipment → Program entries → Part/feature → capability baseline (Ppk, n, date) → key controls → actual performance by serial range → lessons learned (operator learning curve, tooling discipline, design margin recommendations). Updated annually with new program data; obsolete equipment removed. Open access to all manufacturing staff; used in pre-production planning for new programs. POPULATION TRIGGERS: Post-FAI (document capability baseline, archive Forms 1/2/3); post-production ramp (actual vs baseline Ppk, rework cost/root-cause summary, operator learning curve, supplier performance); post-field-failure (link failures to serial ranges, compare reworked vs new component rates). CRITICAL: Process Capability Library organized by process family enables cross-program knowledge transfer — new variant leverages capability data from prior program on same equipment/process.
summary: Three-layer data retention (hot/warm/cold) with process-family-organized Capability Library for permanent knowledge preservation across aerospace production programs

label: Cost-of-quality tracking and reporting
content: Cost-of-Quality (CoQ) tracking for LRHM/LRIP environments. CoQ TAXONOMY: (1) Prevention — engineering labor for DFM reviews, FMEA, supplier development, process planning, operator training. (2) Appraisal — inspection labor (incoming, in-process, final, FAI), test equipment depreciation, gage R&R studies, supplier audit costs. (3) Internal Failure — rework labor (at loaded rate), scrap material value + consumed labor, re-inspection after rework, NCR disposition effort, design-change-driven rework. (4) External Failure — field returns, warranty, customer-site rework (rare in LRIP but catastrophic). COST CODE STRUCTURE: Assign unique cost codes linking each quality cost event to: root cause category (supplier, design, process setup, operator), work center/station, part number, supplier ID, defect type. Example: XX-YYY-ZZZZ where XX=cost category, YYY=root cause, ZZZZ=part/workstation. LRIP-SPECIFIC CHALLENGES: With 8-15 units/variant, per-unit CoQ is volatile — track CoQ as percentage of variant-level cost baseline rather than per-unit. Threshold triggers: single supplier >40% of rework cost → supplier recovery review; total CoQ >10% of variant baseline → ECRB design maturity review. DASHBOARD KPIs (monthly): total CoQ % by category, supplier CoQ contribution ranking, process defect category Pareto, 6-month CoQ trend, CoQ by production phase (prototype vs pilot vs production). Quality Manager owns CoQ metrics; presents monthly to production leadership and program management.
summary: Task: Implement cost-of-quality tracking with prevention/appraisal/failure taxonomy, cost codes linking quality costs to root causes, and CoQ dashboards for LRIP environments

label: Manufacturing cost baseline and variance management
content: Manufacturing cost baseline and variance management for LRIP programs. REJECT FLAT PER-UNIT TARGETS: Cost targets set before process/supplier maturity = guaranteed overruns. Use phased baselines: Phase 1 (Prototype, design maturity ~60%, supplier qual ~50%) — high scrap/rework allowance (scrap 20%, rework reserve 25%, 100% inspection); Phase 2 (Pilot, design ~85%, supplier ~85%) — moderate allowance (scrap 10%, rework 12%, sampling inspection); Phase 3 (Low-Rate Production, design ~100%, supplier ~95%) — lean allowance (scrap 5%, rework 5%, selective 100% on critical features only). VARIANT-LEVEL COST STRUCTURE: Track costs per variant (not per-unit average) across categories: design engineering, process engineering, supplier development/FAI, tooling amortization, quality appraisal, direct material (with yield factor), direct labor. Marginal cost for additional units ≈ material + direct labor + scrap allowance (tooling and NRE are variant-level). ACTIVITY-BASED COSTING: Replace labor-hour overhead allocation with ABC drivers — allocate overhead by actual cost drivers (setup events, inspection hours, NCR count, supplier qualification phases) not labor hours. In LRIP with high fixed costs and variable utilization, labor-hour allocation systematically under-represents actual cost burden. MONTHLY VARIANCE ANALYSIS: Track actual-to-baseline by cost category; require root-cause narrative for any category >10% over baseline. Design change costs tracked separately — material obsolescence, manufacturing rework from ECOs, tooling modifications — to distinguish design-driven cost from manufacturing process cost. RE-BASELINE TRIGGERS: Design change affecting >3 part numbers, supplier change mid-production, process maturity assessment showing capability below baseline assumption.
summary: Task: Establish phased cost baselines aligned to design/supplier maturity, implement variant-level ABC costing, and conduct monthly cost variance analysis for LRIP programs

label: Make-vs-buy and supplier cost analysis
content: Switching costs vary significantly by subassembly complexity — do not cite a specific dollar figure in executive recommendations without a program-specific analysis. The order of magnitude (tens to hundreds of thousands) is useful for screening; the exact figure requires a site-specific cost model.
summary: Operational-level task: Conduct make-vs-buy analysis using total landed cost and categorize cost reduction opportunities for LRIP. NOTE: This is a tactical/PM tool — for executive strategic decisions involving program concentration risk or customer diversification, pair with executive strategy and legal review neurons.

label: Assembly work instruction strategy
content: Determine which assembly operations require formal detailed work instructions vs. structured guidance vs. technician discretion. TIERED APPROACH: Tier A (Formal Detailed WI, ~40-50% of operations): safety/structural load path operations, tolerance stack-ups consuming >50% of tolerance band, operations with >10% historical defect rate, operations requiring in-process measurement, operations performed by new/uncertified technicians. Tier B (Structured Guidance Cards, ~30-35%): moderate complexity with 1-2 risk factors, 1-2 page procedure cards with photos and key verification points. Tier C (Reference Cards, ~15-20%): low-risk cosmetic operations performed by experienced certified technicians, brief cards with part numbers, orientation, and torque values only. DECISION TRIGGERS for upgrading tier: defect rate >10% on any operation → promote to Tier A; design change affecting form/fit/function → promote to Tier A until FAI complete; technician turnover >20%/year → promote all operations one tier. WI DEVELOPMENT STAGING for LRIP: builds 1-3 use draft procedures with mandatory deviation capture; builds 4-6 baseline formal WI from deviation data; builds 7+ operate under full change-controlled documentation per AS9100D Section 8.5.1. Prevents over-documenting unstable processes while building toward procedural maturity.
summary: Task: Tiered work instruction strategy determining formal WI vs. structured guidance vs. technician discretion for assembly operations based on criticality, defect history, and technician experience

label: Assembly workmanship standards
content: Establish facility-specific assembly workmanship standards with three-class defect framework. CLASS A (STRUCTURAL): Compromises load path, fatigue life, or safety — zero acceptance, mandatory scrap or rework to spec. Examples: cracks, missing fasteners, misaligned structural interfaces, corrosion on load-bearing surfaces. CLASS B (FUNCTIONAL): Impairs function per design intent but no safety hazard — requires documented deviation waiver with design approval + Quality sign-off, tracked by serial number for pattern analysis. Examples: fit-up gap exceeding tolerance, fastener torque outside spec ±20%, surface finish below spec on non-corrosion-critical surfaces. CLASS C (COSMETIC): No form/fit/function impact — accept as-is per AS9100D Section 7.5.3, document in assembly log. Examples: paint drips, scratches on non-functional surfaces, minor sealant excess. ACCEPTANCE BY PHASE: Pre-assembly incoming: Class A 100% visual, Class B dimensional sampling per ANSI/ASQ Z1.4 (NOT MIL-STD-105E, which is obsolete) AQL 2.5 Level II for first 25 units then reduce if Ppk>1.67. In-process: Class A 100% at checkpoints using go/no-go gages, Class B 10-15% statistical sampling with SPC trending. Pre-test audit: 100% re-check of Class A interfaces, functional verification, WI compliance audit. Reference standards: IPC-A-610 (electronics assembly), NASA-STD-8739 (soldering/crimping), AS9100D Section 8.7 (nonconforming output).
summary: Task: Three-class assembly defect framework (structural/functional/cosmetic) with acceptance criteria by assembly phase, sampling plans per ANSI/ASQ Z1.4, and reference workmanship standards

label: Assembly error-proofing and poka-yoke
content: Graduated poka-yoke strategy for LRIP assembly, prioritized by defect cost and implementation cost. LEVEL 1 (<$200/workstation): Color-coded fastener bins preventing wrong-size selection; go/no-go hole gages before fastening; high-visibility orientation marks on procedure cards; click-type torque wrenches (vs. beam wrenches prone to misreading). LEVEL 2 ($200-$1K): Fixture-drilled templates with visible empty holes after fastening (omission detection); cable routing clips at reference points locking harness path before sealing; keyed connector designs preventing backward insertion (coordinate with design engineering). LEVEL 3 ($1K-$5K): Assembly fixtures positioning components correctly and providing go/no-go verification built into fixture; drilling jigs preventing mislocated holes; component staging trays with count verification. ROI BREAKEVEN: Fixture-based poka-yoke typically justified after 3-5 units per variant in labor savings + defect reduction. VERIFICATION CHECKPOINTS preventing downstream discovery: place inspection immediately after high-risk operations and before irreversible steps (sealing, potting, structural fastening). Self-inspection model preferred (technician performs verification using fixture gages) supplemented by roving quality technician sampling 10-15% of operations daily for compliance spot-checks. CRITICAL: Poka-yoke supplements but does not replace procedure compliance — error-proofing addresses execution errors while procedures address knowledge gaps.
summary: Task: Graduated poka-yoke error-proofing strategy for assembly (3 cost levels), fixture-based defect prevention, verification checkpoint placement, and self-inspection model with roving quality spot-checks

label: Assembly technician training and competency
content: Training matrix and certification pathway for assembly operations per AS9100D Section 7.2. TIER A OPERATIONS (structural/critical): 4-6 hrs classroom (theory + procedure walkthrough) → 2-4 hrs hands-on demo on scrap unit under supervisor → supervised assembly of production unit 1 with 30-min spot-checks → independent completion of unit 2 with 100% dimensional verification by supervisor → authority grant (supervisor + Quality Manager sign-off). TIER B OPERATIONS (functional): 1-2 hrs classroom → supervised unit 1 → independent unit 2 with 20% sample verification → supervisor approval. TIER C OPERATIONS (cosmetic/experienced): 15 min procedure card review → supervised 2-3 units → supervisor approval. OJT PROTOCOL: Supervisor demonstrates first, technician observes and verbalizes understanding of each step; technician performs while supervisor observes and provides real-time correction; supervisor documents OJT checklist. COMPETENCY ASSESSMENT: Pass = procedure compliance without prompting + measurements within spec + no visible defects + completion within estimated labor hours. Fail = identify specific skill gap, retrain, reassess after 1 week. RECERTIFICATION: Annually OR immediately after ECO-driven procedure change. CROSS-TRAINING TARGET: Each Tier A operation assigned to minimum 2 certified technicians. NEW-HIRE ONBOARDING: 2-week ramp, 4-6 days to certify on 3-4 Tier B/C operations + 1 Tier A operation, paired with experienced technician for first month. DEFECT-TRIGGERED RETRAINING: Technician defect rate >5% → targeted retraining; >8% → competency reassessment and potential reassignment.
summary: Task: Assembly technician training matrix with tiered certification pathways (A/B/C), OJT protocol, competency assessment criteria, cross-training targets, and defect-triggered retraining thresholds

label: Assembly genealogy and serial traceability
content: Assembly genealogy documentation linking operations, technicians, components, and procedures to specific serial numbers per AS9100D Section 8.5.2. GENEALOGY LOG CAPTURES PER UNIT: (1) Component traceability — part number, supplier, lot/serial number, receiving date for each incorporated component. (2) Procedural baseline — WI revision number used, ECO effective serial number boundary. (3) Assembly operations — operation performed, responsible technician (identity), sign-off date/time, measurement data (torque values, dimensional results). (4) Defect/waiver history — any deviation waivers applied, defect reports generated, rework performed. (5) Pre-test audit results — dimensional re-check, functional verification, WI compliance confirmation, release authority. CONFIGURATION LINKAGE: Each unit serial number maps to configuration baseline (e.g., DB-2.1) + list of incorporated ECOs + Configuration Build Card (CBC) reference. Serial number change boundaries documented when design changes apply forward-only. CONFIGURATION BUILD CARD (CBC): Per-unit work ticket printed from configuration database, travels with unit through assembly. Contains: serial number, assigned CI revision, component part numbers per BOM position (with approved alternates flagged), modification code checklist (checkboxes), assembly hold-point sign-off fields, test configuration requirements. CBC is single-source assignment document — technician never guesses configuration; card is unique per serial number. Old serial numbers with outdated CBC flagged immediately by database. GENEALOGY QUERY USE CASE: When field failure or test escape occurs, genealogy enables <5 min root-cause scoping — identify supplier lot, affected operation, technician, configuration variant, and all other units sharing same component lot, procedure revision, or configuration variant for containment. ECO TRACEABILITY: Track which units incorporate which ECOs; maintain retrofit status for units already delivered (via service bulletin tracking). SERIAL NUMBER ASSIGNMENT: Unit-level serial at final assembly; component serials captured at incoming; sub-assembly serials recommended for critical modules where later rework or retrofit may be needed.
summary: System: Assembly genealogy documentation linking component source, technician identity, procedural version, measurement data, and configuration baseline to unit serial numbers for root-cause traceability

label: Assembly defect trending and feedback loops
content: Assembly performance metrics and closed-loop defect feedback system. KEY METRICS (weekly): Defects-Per-Unit (DPU) target <2 Class A+B, action threshold >3; Defect Rate by Operation target <5%, escalate >10%; First-Pass Yield (FPY) target >85%, critical if <75%; Rework Labor % target <10% of assembly labor, process broken if >15%. DEFECT TRENDING: Categorize by operation type (fastening, harness routing, adhesive, connector mating), by workstation, and by technician. Plot weekly DPU trending chart per variant; detect spikes requiring root-cause investigation. CLOSED-LOOP FEEDBACK PATHS: Path 1 (in-process discovery) → defect report → 24hr root cause → categorize as design ambiguity / procedure unclear / technician skill / tool failure / supplier quality → corrective action (owner + due date) → preventive action (procedure change, training, tool upgrade) → verify over next 10 units → closure. Path 2 (test failure traced to assembly) → genealogy log trace → identify operation + technician → procedure fix + retraining + add upstream verification checkpoint. Path 3 (field failure) → genealogy review → root cause → service bulletin for retrofit + process improvement. ROOT CAUSE METHODS: 5-Why for single causal chain; Ishikawa for multi-factor (Man/Machine/Method/Material/Measurement/Environment). MONTHLY REVIEW: Quality Manager + Engineering + Manufacturing review defect category trends, identify systemic patterns, assess corrective action effectiveness. AUTOMATION VS TRAINING DECISION: If operation defect rate >10% sustained and >50 units/year → evaluate automation ROI; if <50 units/year → procedure/training improvement preferred (typical payback <1 year vs. 5-10 years for automation in LRIP).
summary: Task: Assembly defect trending dashboard (DPU, FPY, rework %), three closed-loop feedback paths (in-process/test/field), root-cause methodology, and automation vs. training ROI decision framework

label: Spare Parts Procurement & Safety Stock Strategy
content: Spare parts procurement for long-lead aerospace production equipment. THREE-TIER STRATEGY: TIER A CRITICAL (12-20 wk OEM lead): Spindle assemblies, servo motors, CNC control modules, hydraulic pump cartridges. Stock 2× estimated annual consumption; dual-source where possible; reorder immediately on consumption (do not wait for reorder point). Safety stock formula: SS = Z × σ_demand × √LT + σ_LT × D_avg where Z=2.33 (99% service level for Tier 0 equipment), σ_demand = std dev monthly usage (±50% typical in low-rate), LT = lead time in months. Procurement triggers: condition monitoring shows imminent failure → order immediately expecting 16-week lead; inventory drops to 1 unit → reorder if next program <12 weeks; OEM announces EOL/price increase → opportunistic 2-year forward buy. TIER B MEDIUM-LEAD (6-12 wk): Servo amplifiers, ballscrew assemblies, coolant pumps. Stock 1.5× annual consumption + 2-month buffer. Link to MRP: auto-trigger PO if committed production includes equipment with <6-week on-hand stock. TIER C SHORT-LEAD (<6 wk): Bearings, seals, filters, wear items. Stock consumption rate × 2-month buffer; monthly automated reorder. MINIMUM ORDER QUANTITY PROBLEM: When MOQ exceeds annual need (e.g., MOQ=5 but need 2/year), negotiate with supplier for reduced MOQ at premium (+10-15%), or consortium buy with other small facilities, or accept 2-3 year forward stock if holding cost < stockout risk ($27K per failure event). CARRYING COST TARGET: ≤5% of total equipment investment value annually.
summary: System: Three-tier spare parts procurement strategy with safety stock formulas, lead-time-based stocking policies, MOQ mitigation tactics, and carrying cost targets for low-rate aerospace equipment

label: Equipment Contingency & Backup Protocols
content: Contingency protocols when critical shared equipment fails in multi-program facility. BACKUP EQUIPMENT TIERS: Tier 0 Dedicated Redundancy — backup unit pre-staged and validated (e.g., refurbished spindle in climate-controlled cabinet, annual 20-hr operability check); quick-change interface enabling swap in 2-4 hours. Tier 1 Partial Redundancy — rental contract on standby with 1-week response (e.g., portable CMM at $800/day for loose-tolerance verification); acceptable only for Tier 1 pilot builds not requiring tight precision. Tier 1B Graceful Degradation — absorb prototype equipment failures into design iteration windows (4-week cycles); extend design review 1 week, shift build to next iteration. RAPID REPAIR CONTRACTS: Negotiate OEM emergency on-site support with 72-hour guarantee; typical cost $8K-15K/incident including technician travel + parts + verification. Justification: expected annual contract cost (failure probability × incident cost) vs. production halt cost ($30K-50K/day for multi-program facility). PRODUCTION WORKAROUNDS: Transfer production to older backup equipment (accept +15% cycle time); subcontract to qualified supplier (2-week turnaround if pre-qualified); manual production mode for 2-3 days if feasible. ACTIVATION PROTOCOL: Equipment failure detected → assess repair timeline (≤4 hrs = repair in place; 4-48 hrs = activate backup equipment; >48 hrs = activate rental/OEM emergency contract + notify affected program managers). ANNUAL VALIDATION: Test backup equipment operability, verify rental contract terms current, confirm OEM emergency contact information.
summary: System: Tiered equipment backup strategy (dedicated redundancy, rental contracts, graceful degradation) with rapid repair contracts, production workaround procedures, and activation protocols

label: Equipment Lifecycle Cost & Replacement Timing
content: Equipment replacement decision based on lifecycle cost analysis, not just age. THREE-PHASE COST MODEL: Phase 1 High Reliability (Years 1-2): MTBF at design spec, annual maintenance $3-5K, downtime <10 hrs/yr, cost/operating-hr = (amortized capex + maint) / annual hrs. Phase 2 Degradation (Years 3-5): MTBF declining 10-25% annually, maintenance cost rising 30-50%, unplanned downtime 30-50 hrs/yr, cost/hr increases 1.5-2×. Phase 3 Failure Cascade (Year 6+): MTBF <50% of design, maintenance $10K+/yr, unplanned downtime 80-120 hrs/yr, spare parts scarce, cost/hr 2.5-3× new equipment. REPLACEMENT DECISION THRESHOLD: Replace when annual lifecycle cost (maintenance + downtime + obsolescence reserve) exceeds annualized cost of new equipment (purchase / useful life + Year 1 maintenance). Typical payback for replacement at Phase 3: 1-2 years. EQUIPMENT AGING RISK SCORECARD (quarterly): Score 1-10 based on age, MTBF trend, maintenance cost trend, OEM support status, spare parts availability. Score ≥7 = replacement proposal to VP Operations within 30 days. Score ≥9 = emergency replacement authorization. OBSOLESCENCE TIERS per neuron 842: 0-5 yr low risk; 5-10 yr stock critical spares before EOL; 10-15 yr begin transition planning; >15 yr replace within 12 months. COST ACCOUNTING: Track capital cost (purchase + install + training + initial spares), maintenance cost (PM parts + labor + emergency repairs), and downtime cost (lost production + schedule penalties + rework). Equipment cost per operating hour = total annual cost / annual operating hours; allocate to product cost across all programs using equipment proportional to usage hours.
summary: Decision: Equipment replacement timing based on three-phase lifecycle cost model (reliability → degradation → failure cascade), aging risk scorecard, and cost-per-operating-hour analysis

label: Schedule recovery and contingency sequencing protocols
content: When schedule variance exceeds tier thresholds, activate tiered recovery response. VARIANCE CLASSIFICATION: (1) Root-cause attribution—categorize each slip as design-change-driven, supplier-delay-driven, manufacturing-yield-driven, or resource-constraint-driven before selecting recovery action. Different root causes require different responses. RECOVERY PROTOCOLS BY ROOT CAUSE: Design change delay—evaluate: continue on current revision with post-delivery retrofit (if non-safety, customer agrees, retrofit labor < threshold) vs. pause and resequence (if safety-critical or retrofit impractical). Supplier delivery slip—expedite current supplier (authorize premium cost within budget threshold) or activate secondary source; shift manufacturing to parallel work on unaffected variants. Manufacturing nonconformance—stop affected process, conduct root cause analysis, validate corrective action with process trial before restart; do not resume production hoping yield improves. Resource constraint—reallocate from lower-priority tier; authorize limited overtime (monitor fatigue-induced rework). CONTINGENCY SEQUENCING: Maintain pre-planned alternate production sequences for top 3 risk scenarios (e.g., 'if Variant A material delayed, pull Variant B forward'). Update contingency sequences monthly. ESCALATION: Local recovery authority for green variance; program manager for yellow; PMO + customer notification for red.
summary: System: Tiered schedule recovery protocols with root-cause-specific responses (design change, supplier delay, yield, resource constraint) and pre-planned contingency sequencing

label: Labor allocation across concurrent programs
content: Manage labor as shared resource pools with skill-based allocation across concurrent programs. LABOR POOL STRUCTURE: Group technicians by skill certification (e.g., 5-axis machining, composite layup, electrical assembly, inspection); track certification level (expert/competent/apprentice) per variant. ALLOCATION RULES: (1) Assign labor to committed production first; pilot and prototype use remaining capacity.
