content: Decision framework for fixture modifications when ECOs occur mid-production. CHANGE CLASSIFICATION: (1) ECO affects part locating datum or critical fixture-interfacing geometry → fixture modification required; assess insert modification vs. complete redesign. (2) ECO affects non-fixture-interfacing features (cosmetic, secondary dimensions) → no fixture impact; continue production unchanged. (3) ECO affects assembly sequence or accessibility but not part geometry → evaluate whether fixture clamp positions or access openings need adjustment. MODIFICATION OPTIONS: Option A (Insert modification) — minor geometry changes accommodated by modifying existing insert (re-machining, adding features); shorter lead time; requires re-validation of insert-to-frame datum stack and functional trial on modified parts. Option B (New insert) — significant geometry change beyond modification scope; design and fabricate new insert for master frame; existing insert archived with ECO revision tag. Option C (Fixture redesign) — fundamental datum change or part envelope change exceeding master frame accommodation range; full fixture redesign required; longest lead time; triggers schedule impact assessment. DECISION GATE FOR MID-PRODUCTION ECO: If units are in-build when ECO is approved: Can current fixture complete in-build units to pre-ECO configuration? If yes, complete current units, implement fixture change for next lot start. If no (safety ECO requiring immediate implementation), halt production, quarantine in-build units, implement fixture change, disposition affected WIP. BACKWARD COMPATIBILITY: When possible, design fixture modifications to be backward-compatible — new insert accommodates both pre-ECO and post-ECO part geometry via adjustable features or dual-position locating. This avoids maintaining separate fixture configurations for pre/post-ECO serial number ranges. CONFIGURATION TRACKING: All fixture modifications logged with ECO number, modification date, affected serial number range, and re-validation results. Fixture drawing revision updated to match incorporated ECO.
summary: Decision: Classify ECO fixture impact, select modification approach (insert mod/new insert/redesign), manage mid-production implementation timing, and maintain backward compatibility where possible

label: Fixture cost allocation and lifecycle economics
content: Fixture cost allocation and lifecycle-cost analysis for low-rate multi-program aerospace production. COST ALLOCATION TIERS: Tier 1 (Program-specific) — variant inserts, part-specific clamping components, and fixtures with no reuse forecast → charge to specific program cost account; amortize over that program's production volume. Tier 2 (Multi-program reusable) — master frames, standard adjustable fixtures, and tooling used across 2+ programs → allocate as facility overhead; spread across facility burden rate. Tier 3 (Capital equipment) — CNC-controlled fixture platforms, automated pallet systems → capitalize as fixed assets; depreciate per accounting policy (typically 5-7 years); allocate via facility burden rate. LIFECYCLE-COST ANALYSIS FRAMEWORK: For each fixture investment decision, compare total cost of ownership across options (dedicated, modular, flexible): (1) Capital cost — design + fabrication + acceptance validation. (2) Operating cost — assembly labor hours × burdened rate × production volume. (3) Rework cost — fixture-induced rework rate × rework hours × burdened rate × volume. (4) Maintenance cost — wear items + PM labor + periodic revalidation. (5) Changeover cost — setup/changeover time × burdened rate × number of variant changes. (6) Reuse credit — if fixture serves additional variants, allocate proportional capex reduction. BREAKEVEN ANALYSIS: Calculate units-to-payback for higher-investment options: payback units = (incremental capex) ÷ (per-unit labor + rework savings). If payback exceeds planned production volume and no reuse is forecast, lower-investment option preferred. IMPORTANT: Use facility-specific labor rates, actual prototype build rework data, and vendor quotes for fixture costs — do not rely on generic industry estimates for investment decisions. COST RECOVERY FROM DESIGN CHANGES: If supplier or customer-driven ECO triggers fixture redesign, cost responsibility follows ECO root-cause attribution per design change cost allocation procedures.
summary: System: Three-tier fixture cost allocation (program-specific, multi-program overhead, capital), lifecycle-cost comparison framework, breakeven analysis methodology, and cost recovery rules for ECO-driven fixture changes

label: Configuration-variant segregation and assembly error prevention
content: Procedure for preventing wrong-variant assembly when multiple configuration variants are concurrent on the production floor. SEGREGATION CONTROLS: (1) Spatial separation — dedicated assembly station per configuration variant with variant-labeled signage; components for different variants never co-located at same station. (2) Work order separation — each configuration variant gets unique work order number; technician's work order pulls only components assigned to that variant's Configuration Build Card. (3) Component kit segregation — color-coded hardware bags per variant (e.g., Variant A = blue bags, Variant B = yellow bags); mixed-variant bags = immediate scrap + root cause investigation. (4) Assembly procedure access control — only current-variant procedure available at station; obsolete or alternate-variant procedures physically removed; digital tablet systems display only work-order-matched procedure. (5) Configuration Technician role — dedicated person (not assembly technician) who prepares Configuration Build Cards, verifies component kits match CBC assignments, and performs independent hold-point verification at configuration-critical assembly steps. HOLD-POINT GATES: At each configuration-critical step, assembly technician performs step, then Configuration Technician independently verifies: checks CBC assignment, visually inspects component P/N marking, confirms modification executed (if required). Both sign hold-point card. Any discrepancy = unit held + escalation. CLEAN CUT-OVER PREFERENCE: When transitioning between configuration variants, prefer completing all units of old variant before starting new variant (eliminates mixed-variant risk). If mixed variants unavoidable (schedule pressure), segregation controls above are mandatory. DOCUMENT VERSION CONTROL AT POINT OF USE: Every printed procedure has CI revision + date on header; technician verifies before use. When CI revision changes, old printed procedures collected and destroyed within 24 hours. Digital systems: procedure database locks old revisions upon new release; technician cannot access superseded version.
summary: Procedure: Configuration-variant segregation controls including spatial separation, color-coded kits, dedicated Configuration Technician role, hold-point verification gates, and document version control to prevent wrong-variant assembly errors

label: Retrofit authorization decision framework
content: Decision framework for determining whether design changes warrant retrofit of already-assembled or delivered units, with cost allocation logic. RETROFIT DECISION TREE: (1) Safety/flight-critical change? → YES = mandatory retrofit; issue Service Bulletin (SB); program absorbs cost (design immaturity risk). (2) Customer-requested contractual change? → YES = customer authorizes retrofit cost + labor; ECO includes customer approval + retrofit labor estimate; retrofit kit shipped with delivery documents. (3) Supplier-caused design incompatibility? → YES = supplier absorbs cost (rework at supplier plant OR prepaid retrofit kit); ECO documents root cause. (4) Cost-justified? → Compare retrofit cost vs. (scrap cost + warranty risk exposure); if retrofit cheaper = retrofit; if not = scrap or accept-as-delivered with customer notification. RETROFIT PROCEDURE REQUIREMENTS: Identification (which serial numbers affected per Configuration Build Card CI revisions), disassembly sequence (access without damaging other assemblies), component swap or modification specification, post-retrofit test verification (measurement + functional test confirming retrofit success), labor estimate (hours × rate = cost per unit), retrofit kit assembly (if multi-component, assemble kit in controlled environment before shipment). RETROFIT COST ALLOCATION: Customer-requested changes = customer. Supplier design incompatibility = supplier. Design immaturity or configuration-control failure = program. Mixed responsibility = negotiate per contract risk-sharing clause; document in ECO financial narrative before retrofit authorization. FIELD RETROFIT (delivered units): Develop retrofit kit + retrofit procedure + customer execution instructions; ship kit with Service Bulletin; customer confirms installation; post-retrofit inspection closure tracked in configuration database. Retrofit effectiveness: monitor whether retrofitted units experience follow-on failures at higher rate than new-build units; if retrofit failure rate >2× new-build rate, retrofit procedure inadequate — revise or discontinue. RETROFIT TRACKING: Link SB to ECO in configuration database; track: kit shipment date, customer receipt confirmation, installation date, post-retrofit inspection results, closure date. Quarterly audit: any open SBs >90 days = escalation to PM.
summary: Decision: Retrofit authorization decision tree (safety → customer → supplier → cost-justified) with cost allocation logic, retrofit procedure requirements, field retrofit kit development, and retrofit effectiveness monitoring

label: Configuration metrics and error tracking
content: Configuration management effectiveness metrics for LRIP programs. CONFIGURATION ACCURACY METRICS: (1) Configuration verification defect rate = (units with configuration errors at verification / total units verified) × 100; target <1%; root cause categories: assembly error, ambiguous procedure, component-kit contamination, Configuration Build Card error. (2) Retrofit surprise rate = (ECOs requiring retrofit of already-delivered units / total delivered units) × 100; target <0.5%; high rate indicates design freeze discipline failure or inadequate pre-delivery configuration verification. (3) Configuration error escape rate = (configuration errors found at test or customer acceptance / total units delivered) × 100; target 0%; any escape triggers immediate CAPA. CHANGE VELOCITY METRICS: (4) Design-change rate by program phase: DB-1 expect 2-3 ECOs/month, DB-2 expect <2 ECOs/month (stability indicator), DB-3 expect <0.2 ECOs/month. Rising rate during DB-2/DB-3 = red flag for premature production commitment. (5) Configuration variant count: total approved variants per CI revision; if >4 major variants, assess whether variant rationalization is needed to reduce configuration management burden. COST METRICS: (6) Retrofit labor cost (actual hours × rate) by root cause: design incompatibility, customer change, design immaturity. (7) Material scrap cost from configuration ECOs by program phase. (8) Configuration-related warranty cost: field failures traced to configuration errors or retrofit inadequacy. SCHEDULE METRICS: (9) Configuration-driven production delays: % of lots delayed due to configuration changes affecting procurement or supplier lead-time. (10) Retrofit delivery impact: days delayed beyond planned delivery due to retrofit labor or kit availability. QUARTERLY EFFECTIVENESS REVIEW: Trend all metrics month-over-month. Investigate concentrations: are errors in one assembly line (procedural), one supplier (component clarity), or one design area (design ambiguity)? RED FLAGS: >1% configuration errors at verification, >$50K retrofit cost per 100 delivered units, rising design-change rate during DB-2/DB-3, or repeat configuration errors on same variant position.
summary: System: Configuration management effectiveness metrics — verification defect rate, retrofit surprise rate, change velocity, variant count, retrofit/scrap/warranty costs, and quarterly trend review with red-flag thresholds

label: Assembly procedure configuration branching and BOM variant management
content: Procedure for structuring assembly documentation with configuration-dependent instructions and variant-aware BOMs. BOM VARIANT STRUCTURE: Hierarchical BOM with top-level assembly serial number entry point. Configuration variant calls at component level reference Configuration Index: 'Select per CI DB-2.1.' Use 1-2 character option codes (A, B, A1, B2) for mutually exclusive components. Example: 'Connector J1: Install ABC-123-001 (Option A) OR XYZ-123-002 (Option B); see Configuration Build Card for unit-specific selection.' Color-code printed BOM by option (Option A = blue highlight, Option B = yellow). Link modification codes to options: 'After J1 installation, execute MOD-A3 per Assembly Procedure Section 3.4.2 (applies to Option A only).' ASSEMBLY PROCEDURE BRANCHING: Linear assembly sequence with inline configuration decision points. At variant decision point, technician checks Configuration Build Card for part number assignment, then follows option-specific sub-procedure. Each option sub-procedure contains complete step-by-step instructions (not references to other documents). Hold points inserted after configuration-critical steps requiring Configuration Technician verification signature. FORMAT EXAMPLE: 'STEP 3.4 — Install Connector J1 / CHECK Configuration Build Card for Part Number Assignment / If assigned ABC-123-001 → Proceed STEP 3.4.1 (Option A) / If assigned XYZ-123-002 → Proceed STEP 3.4.2 (Option B).' Each sub-step includes alignment instructions, torque/force specs, verification criteria specific to that component variant. CONFIGURATION-DEPENDENT TEST REQUIREMENTS: Assembly procedure must flag when component variant selection affects downstream test plan. Example: 'If assembled with J1 Option B, skip thermal cycling test (qualified per QR-2024-015).' This information also appears on Configuration Build Card test configuration section. PROCEDURE REVISION CONTROL: Assembly procedure revision linked to CI revision; when CI changes, assembly procedures updated within 5 business days; old procedure versions removed from all assembly stations.
summary: Procedure: Structuring assembly procedures with configuration decision points, option-coded BOMs with color coding, variant-specific sub-procedures, hold-point gates, and configuration-dependent test flagging

label: NCR disposition for configuration errors vs incompleteness vs incompatibility
content: Decision framework for dispositioning nonconforming material when the root cause is configuration-related. THREE CONFIGURATION NCR CATEGORIES: (1) CONFIGURATION ERROR (wrong variant assembled): Unit assembled with component from wrong configuration variant (e.g., Option A component installed where Option B was assigned). Root cause: assembly procedure ambiguity, kit contamination, or Configuration Build Card error. Disposition: If wrong-variant component is functionally equivalent AND both variants qualified → rework authorized (swap to correct component, re-verify, release). If only assigned variant qualified → retrofit required or scrap; assess retrofit cost vs. scrap cost. (2) CONFIGURATION INCOMPLETENESS (missing modifications): Unit missing one or more configuration-specific modifications (e.g., MOD-A3 shunt resistor not installed). Root cause: modification checklist not followed, Configuration Build Card unclear, or modification step skipped. Disposition: Complete missing modification per original procedure + re-verify at hold point. If modification requires disassembly of subsequent steps, estimate rework hours; if rework >8 hrs or risks damage to other assemblies, escalate to Engineering for alternative modification path. (3) DESIGN INCOMPATIBILITY (variant combinations creating functional problems): Configuration-variant component combinations that create interface, fit, or functional problems not anticipated during design. Root cause: design verification testing did not cover this variant combination, or design change created unanticipated interaction. Disposition: Engineering root cause analysis required; may result in ECO to resolve incompatibility, Configuration Index update to prohibit specific variant combination, or retrofit of all units with same combination. Cost allocation: typically program (design immaturity) or supplier (if supplier component out of spec). ESCALATION THRESHOLDS: Rework cost >$5K or schedule slip >1 week → PM escalation. Repeat configuration NCR (same root cause on >2 units) → mandatory CAPA with procedure revision. Configuration error escape to customer → warranty impact assessment + customer notification within 48 hours.
summary: Decision: NCR disposition framework distinguishing configuration errors (wrong variant), configuration incompleteness (missing modifications), and design incompatibilities (variant conflicts) with disposition paths, cost thresholds, and escalation criteria

label: Quality metrics for design-volatile prototype manufacturing
content: PROBLEM: Traditional quality metrics (FPY, Cpk, SPC control charts) assume stable processes with sufficient sample sizes. In prototype/LRIP manufacturing with concurrent design changes, these assumptions fail: design changes shift baselines (invalidating trend data), rework inflates apparent yield (masking first-pass failures), small samples (n<25) produce unreliable capability indices, and technician variation confounds root-cause assignment. ADAPTED METRIC FRAMEWORK: (1) Replace aggregate FPY with defect-category frequency tracking — catalog each defect by failure mechanism (design-tolerance, fixturing, supplier, operator, process-procedure) and track category recurrence across builds. This is statistically valid at n=1 per build because it tracks pattern presence, not statistical distribution. (2) Phase-matched targets: prototype phase allows higher non-conformance rates (focus on learning); pilot phase tracks repeat-defect elimination; LRIP tracks first-pass yield only on stable (non-reworked) operations. (3) After each design change (ECO), reset quality baselines for affected features — track pre-ECO vs post-ECO defect rates separately to validate ECO effectiveness. (4) Rework transparency: track rework hours as percentage of total production hours (target <15%); track rework pass rate (rework that fails re-inspection indicates procedure inadequacy). (5) Use defect-by-build-sequence plots instead of control charts — plot defect count by build number with ECO effective dates marked, enabling visual identification of design-change impact. IMPORTANT: Facility-specific composite indices (e.g., weighted risk scores combining defect frequency and field consequence) are useful internal tools but should be clearly labeled as facility-defined metrics, not presented as industry standards. Calibrate thresholds from your own baseline data over first 2-3 production lots.
summary: Adapted quality metric framework for prototype/LRIP environments where design volatility, small samples, and rework invalidate traditional SPC/FPY — uses defect-category tracking, phase-matched targets, ECO-reset baselines, and build-sequence trending

label: Field-failure feedback loop and risk forecasting
content: FIELD-FAILURE FEEDBACK LOOP for prototype/LRIP manufacturing where field failures arrive after production decisions. THREE-STEP PROCESS: (1) DEFECT-TO-FIELD CORRELATION: Maintain manufacturing defect library with serial-level traceability. When field failure occurs, trace failed unit's serial number back to manufacturing defect history — was this failure mode seen during production? Was it caught and reworked, or was it a production escape? If reworked, did rework reliably restore function? Track correlation rate: % of field failures that match known manufacturing defect categories. (2) RISK WEIGHT ASSIGNMENT: For each manufacturing defect category, assign field-risk weight based on evidence: defect categories with confirmed field failures get high weight; categories with zero field correlation after sufficient fleet hours get reduced weight. Update weights quarterly as field data accumulates. Initial weights before field data available should be based on DFMEA severity ratings. (3) PREDICTIVE ESCALATION: Combine defect frequency (% of builds affected) with field-risk weight to prioritize design changes. Defects that are both frequent in manufacturing AND confirmed in field failures are highest priority for ECO. IMPORTANT CAVEATS: Risk scoring is a prioritization tool, not a validated prediction model — do not present facility-specific composite scores as having statistical predictive validity without validation data. Validation requires comparing predicted risk rankings against actual field-failure rates over 12+ months. PHASE-GATE INTEGRATION: Use risk evidence (not just scores) in design-phase gate reviews — require that high-consequence defect categories have either been eliminated by ECO or accepted with documented rationale before advancing to next production phase.
summary: Three-step field-failure feedback system: defect-to-field correlation via serial traceability, evidence-based risk weight assignment, and predictive escalation for ECO prioritization — with caveats on presenting composite scores as validated predictions

label: Cross-domain root-cause investigation coordination
content: ROOT-CAUSE INVESTIGATION across design, manufacturing, and supplier domains in prototype/LRIP environments where defect ownership is ambiguous. CHALLENGE: Same defect symptom (e.g., dimensional non-conformance) can originate from design tolerance inadequacy, fixture/tooling wear, operator technique variation, or supplier material variation — and often involves interaction between multiple domains. STRUCTURED RCA PROTOCOL: (1) INITIAL TRIAGE (Quality Inspector, <2 hrs): Document defect with measurement data, assign preliminary failure category, check defect registry for prior occurrences of same type. (2) DOMAIN ASSIGNMENT (Manufacturing Engineer, <24 hrs): Using defect registry queries — if defect appears across multiple operators/fixtures → likely design or supplier issue; if defect correlates with specific operator → technique issue; if defect correlates with specific fixture → tooling issue; if defect correlates with specific material lot → supplier issue. (3) CROSS-DOMAIN RCA (within 1 week for Tier 1, 4 hrs for Tier 0): Convene affected domain owners. Design Lead assesses tolerance adequacy and stack-up. Manufacturing Engineer assesses process capability on that feature. Supplier quality reviews incoming inspection data for that material lot. (4) OWNERSHIP RESOLUTION: Assign single primary root-cause domain with documented rationale. If multi-factor, assign primary owner + contributing factors with separate corrective actions. (5) CORRECTIVE ACTION VERIFICATION: Track whether corrective action in assigned domain actually reduces defect recurrence in subsequent builds. If not, reassess domain assignment. TECHNICIAN VARIATION HANDLING: Do not blame operators without evidence. Compare defect rates across operators on same operation with same fixture/material. If variation is statistically meaningful (>2x difference in defect rate), investigate whether work instructions are ambiguous (procedure fix) before concluding operator skill gap (training fix).
summary: Structured cross-domain RCA protocol for prototype environments: triage, domain assignment via defect registry correlation queries, multi-domain investigation, ownership resolution, and corrective action verification — with guidance on fair technician variation analysis

label: Quality-evidence design phase gate criteria
content: DESIGN PHASE GATE DECISIONS using quality evidence to determine production-phase advancement. GATE PURPOSE: Prevent advancing designs to higher-commitment production phases when quality evidence shows unresolved risk. PROTOTYPE → PILOT GATE: Required evidence — defect-category trends stabilizing or improving across last 3 builds; no Tier-0 (field-critical) defect categories without corrective action plan; >80% of open RCAs have assigned owners and target dates; all high-consequence defect categories have DFMEA severity ratings assigned. No-go criteria — new defect categories still appearing in most recent build; Tier-0 defects recurring without ECO; RCA backlog growing. PILOT → LRIP GATE: Required evidence — repeat-defect rate declining (same defect type appearing in fewer builds); ECOs for high-consequence defects implemented with measurable defect-rate improvement in post-ECO builds; supplier defect rates below agreed thresholds; rework rate trending downward. No-go criteria — any Tier-0 defect appearing post-ECO (ECO did not fix the problem); rework rate >15% of production hours; supplier quality issues unresolved. LRIP → PRODUCTION GATE: Required evidence — FPY >90% on non-rework operations over 3+ consecutive builds; all high-consequence defect categories either eliminated or accepted with documented field-risk rationale; technician certification complete for all production operations; supplier OTD >95%. No-go criteria — FPY declining; new defect categories emerging; field failures correlated to manufacturing defects without corrective action. NOTE: Specific numerical thresholds (FPY targets, rework rate limits) should be calibrated to facility baseline data — the values above are starting points, not universal standards.
summary: Design phase gate decision criteria using quality evidence: prototype→pilot, pilot→LRIP, and LRIP→production gates with required evidence, no-go criteria, and caveat that numerical thresholds are facility-specific starting points

label: Design-change material disposition and obsolescence management
content: When design change (ECO) affects components already procured or in inventory, execute material disposition decision BEFORE ECRB approves the change. DISPOSITION MATRIX: (1) Cosmetic/label change → Accept inventory as-is; consume in sequence; Engineering-only authority. (2) Tolerance relaxation (spec loosened) → Existing stricter inventory exceeds new spec; accept or reassign to lower-tier use; PM+Quality authority. (3) Tolerance tightening (spec tightened) → Measure and sort existing inventory; retrofit-rework accepted units; scrap non-conforming; PM authority (<100 units), Plant Manager (>100). (4) Material substitution (e.g., alloy change) → Existing inventory incompatible; scrap/salvage; expedite new material; PM authority, escalate if cost >5% program margin. (5) Dimension change → Retrofit rework if single secondary operation feasible; otherwise scrap and reorder. GOVERNANCE: Engineering proposes change + preliminary material impact estimate → Manufacturing + Quality confirm impact (measure existing inventory, quantify rework cost/hours, assess schedule impact) → ECRB approves ONLY IF material disposition is funded (absorbed into contingency or deferred to next lot boundary) → PM documents disposition in ECO package. DESIGN-FREEZE THRESHOLDS for advance purchase authorization: Prototype phase (no freeze, Tier A forecast only, no PO authority) → Pilot transition (interim freeze at 80% design maturity, Tier B PO authorized) → Pilot production (production baseline freeze, FAI complete, Tier C execution POs) → LRIP mature (<1 ECO/6 months, standing orders authorized). KEY METRICS: material write-off $ per ECO, retrofit rework hours per ECO, time from ECO approval to material disposition execution, design-change rate trending by program maturity level.
summary: Decision: Evaluate design-change impact on procured material inventory — disposition matrix (accept/retrofit/scrap), ECRB governance requiring funded material impact before change approval, design-freeze thresholds for purchase authorization

label: Long-lead PO strategy balancing economics vs. obsolescence risk
content: Purchase order quantity and timing decisions for long-lead components (16-26 weeks) in LRIP where design changes create obsolescence exposure. DECISION BY LEAD-TIME CLASS: Short-lead (≤8 weeks): Tier C only, Scheduler places execution PO 6 weeks before need, quantity = demand ±5% + 2-week buffer. Medium-lead (8-16 weeks): Tier B conditional, PM authorizes when design ≥75% frozen, quantity = mean forecast × 1.15 (3-5% scrap buffer), approve 12-14 weeks before production. Long-lead (16-26 weeks): Tier B + design-freeze gate required, negotiate MOQ vs. expedite vs. rework cost tradeoff. QUANTITY DECISION FACTORS: (1) MOQ economics — compare bulk discount savings against additional carrying cost + obsolescence risk; carrying cost rate typically 20-30%/year in aerospace LRIP (obsolescence 8% + holding 12% + insurance 2%). (2) Working capital — average inventory value × carrying rate = monthly cost; compare purchase scenarios. (3) Design stability — if design-freeze gate NOT met, purchase minimum (accept premium pricing) to limit obsolescence exposure; if gate met, leverage quantity discounts. (4) Supplier capacity — if single-source with capacity constraint, purchase maximum of supplier-reserved capacity or forecast × 1.2, whichever is lower. (5) Perishable materials — limit purchase to consumption within shelf-life window; consume oldest first (FIFO). LOW-RATE PO TERMS NEGOTIATION: MOQ 75-150 units (not 500-1000 high-volume terms), delivery windows ±2 weeks (not fixed dates), design-change flexibility via BoM lock date with cost-sharing after lock, payment Net 45 tied to receipt confirmation. INVENTORY OPTIMIZATION TARGETS: <7 days-on-hand for LRIP; inventory turns ≥4/year; excess carrying cost variance within ±5% of budget.
summary: Decision: Determine PO quantity, timing, and terms for long-lead LRIP components — balancing MOQ economics, working capital consumption, obsolescence risk from design changes, and supplier capacity constraints

label: Material shortage response and expedite protocol
content: When inventory drops below 30-day forecast need, execute shortage response protocol. STEP 1 ROOT-CAUSE ANALYSIS (Manufacturing + Supply Chain, 30 min): Identify driver — design-change-driven consumption increase, yield loss/scrap increase, supplier delivery miss, demand forecast inaccuracy, or normal consumption variance (±10%, no action). STEP 2 MITIGATION BY LEAD TIME: Short-lead (<8 weeks) → expedite new PO, cost $500-$2K expedite fee, minimal production impact. Medium-lead (8-16 weeks) → options: (a) expedite on secondary supplier at premium, (b) negotiate split shipment from primary (25% early, 75% on schedule), (c) defer some production demand to next month (PM approval required). Long-lead (16+ weeks) → escalate to PM immediately; options: negotiate customer schedule slip OR authorize emergency supplier activation (expensive). COST DECISION RULE: Compare expedite cost against production delay cost — if Tier 0 schedule slip threatens customer delivery (CPARS impact, potential penalty), expedite cost is almost always justified. STEP 3 CORRECTIVE ACTION: Design-change cause → update forecast methodology, track write-off in CoQ. Yield loss → supplier quality corrective action, update scrap buffer in forecast. Forecast error → review forecast accuracy metrics, improve estimation process. Supplier delivery miss → schedule supplier SBR, assess backup supplier activation. KEY METRICS: material shortage-driven production delay days/year (target <5), schedule recovery cost $/year, PO expedite frequency (target <2% of orders).
summary: Decision: Material shortage escalation protocol — root-cause analysis, mitigation options by lead-time class, cost-based expedite decision rules, corrective action by shortage driver

label: Variant-specific material staging and kit-assembly segregation
content: Material staging and kit-assembly procedures for configuration-variant programs preventing cross-variant component substitution errors. BOM STRUCTURE: Design product structure with explicit variant segregation — common hardware (all variants) separated from variant-specific components at BoM Tier 2+. Each variant maintains independent BoM with unique part numbers for configuration-specific items (even when components appear similar, assign distinct P/Ns to prevent substitution). PHYSICAL SEGREGATION: Variant-specific components stored in physically separated staging areas with visual controls — color-coded bins (e.g., Variant A=blue, B=red, C=green), BoM card attached to each kit, staging location labels showing component P/N + BoM version + variant + supplier + lot number. KIT-ASSEMBLY PROCEDURE: (1) Production Scheduler issues material kit request referencing specific variant + BoM version + serial number range. (2) Material handler pulls components per kit pick list (variant-specific). (3) Kit verified against BoM pick list (count + P/N match) before release to assembly. (4) Assembly work instruction references variant explicitly; operator confirms variant match at first assembly step. (5) Quality holdpoint at assembly completion verifies configuration index matches serial number assignment. ANTI-ERROR CONTROLS: If mixed-variant production is concurrent on floor, implement: separate work orders with configuration-specific routing, assembly-sequence-dependent inspection holdpoints, dedicated configuration-control technician verifying serial-to-baseline mapping. PREFERRED: Clean cut-over (complete all units of one variant before switching) over mixed-variant production to reduce assembly-error risk. MATERIAL TRACEABILITY: Link material lot/serial numbers to production builds in ERP — enables root-cause analysis when material defects correlate to specific supplier lots. Record consumption (ERP inventory deduction) at kit release, not at assembly completion.
summary: System: Variant-specific material staging, kit-assembly, and segregation procedures preventing cross-variant component substitution — BoM structure, physical segregation controls, kit verification, and traceability linkage

label: Multi-program supplier capacity allocation and priority matrix
content: When multiple concurrent programs compete for limited supplier capacity on critical components, establish priority allocation rules preventing schedule conflicts. PRIORITY MATRIX: P0 (Tier 0 committed production, FAI complete, critical path) → supplier capacity reserved first, guaranteed lead time + premium scheduling slot, no deferral permitted. P1 (Tier 1 pilot, FAI active, schedule margin ≥2 weeks) → secondary scheduling slot, lead time = supplier standard + 1 week buffer. P2 (Tier 1B prototype, design not frozen, schedule margin ≥4 weeks) → shared remaining capacity, expedite POs incur $500-$2K charge, accept 4-week longer lead time vs. standard. QUARTERLY SUPPLIER CAPACITY REVIEW: Present aggregated demand across all programs to key suppliers; confirm reserved capacity slots; identify capacity constraints before they become delivery misses; agree on capacity expansion triggers (overtime, secondary machine, second shift). ALLOCATION ESCALATION: If P0 demand increases → supplier increases capacity (overtime/secondary) or P1/P2 defers. If P1 demand increases → escalate to PM for expedite cost approval or accept 1-week schedule slip. If P2 demand increases → first deferral candidate when supplier capacity constrained. SUPPLIER CAPACITY SCORECARD: Track supplier utilization % vs. committed capacity, on-time delivery by priority tier, capacity flex responsiveness (time to approve/deliver expedite orders). Target supplier utilization 70% of stated capacity to maintain 30% flex buffer for expedites and demand spikes. SINGLE-SOURCE RISK: If supplier is single-source for ≥2 concurrent programs, escalate supplier redundancy qualification to HIGH priority — schedule dependency across programs magnifies disruption impact.
summary: System: Multi-program supplier capacity allocation priority matrix (P0/P1/P2), quarterly capacity review process, escalation rules, and single-source cross-program risk management

label: AFP process qualification standards and NADCAP re-accreditation delta
content: AFP QUALIFICATION GOVERNING STANDARDS: (1) AS9100D Section 8.5.1(f) — validation of special processes (AFP is a special process; output cannot be fully verified by subsequent inspection alone). (2) AS9145 Section 7 — process and product validation requirements including PQ trial design. (3) CMH-17 (Composite Materials Handbook) Chapter 8 — statistical basis for equivalency demonstration between hand-layup baseline and AFP; defines B-basis/A-basis allowables methodology and equivalency testing protocols. (4) NADCAP AC7118 — composites fabrication accreditation; AFP requires supplemental accreditation extension, not full re-accreditation, if the facility already holds AC7118 for hand layup on the same material system at the same site. NADCAP AUDIT SLOT LEAD TIME: NADCAP supplemental audit slots are scheduled through the Performance Review Institute (PRI) and typically require 6–9 months lead time from notification to audit execution. Failure to notify the accreditation body at program kick-off (IQ gate) is a common scheduling risk that has caused FAI deadline slips. Notify PRI at program start, not after PQ data is available. MEASUREMENT SYSTEM PREREQUISITE FOR CPK CLAIMS: MSA/Gage R&R on all KC measurement systems must be completed and validated (target: gauge R&R <10% of tolerance for critical KCs, <30% maximum) before PQ capability data is collected. A Cpk ≥ 1.67 result from an unvalidated measurement system has no contractual standing. WHAT ASTM D6775 IS NOT: ASTM D6775 is a tensile test standard for woven textile ribbon/tape — it is NOT an AFP machine capability or process qualification standard. Do not cite it in AFP qualification documentation.
summary: Decision: AFP qualification governed by AS9100D 8.5.1(f) + AS9145 + CMH-17 Ch. 8 for equivalency stats; NADCAP supplemental (not full) re-accreditation; audit slot lead time 6-9 months; MSA/GR&R required before Cpk claims; ASTM D6775 is a textile tensile standard and must not be cited for AFP

label: Program lifecycle and phase gate management
content: Oversee program lifecycle from formulation through closeout, ensuring phase gate criteria are met at each Key Decision Point (KDP). Coordinate life-cycle reviews, Standing Review Boards, and phase transition readiness assessments per NPR 7120.5.
summary: Manage program progression through lifecycle phases and key decision points

label: Phase gate criteria and KDP preparation
content: Prepare Key Decision Point (KDP) packages including technical maturity evidence, cost/schedule status, risk posture, and staffing adequacy. Ensure all phase gate criteria are satisfied before requesting Decision Authority approval. Phases: Pre-Phase A (Concept Studies), Phase A (Concept & Technology Development), Phase B (Preliminary Design), Phase C (Final Design & Fabrication), Phase D (Assembly, Integration & Test), Phase E (Operations), Phase F (Closeout).
summary: Define and verify entry/exit criteria for each lifecycle phase gate

label: Life-cycle review planning and execution
content: Coordinate life-cycle reviews (LCRs) including Mission Concept Review (MCR), System Requirements Review (SRR), System Definition Review (SDR), Preliminary Design Review (PDR), Critical Design Review (CDR), System Integration Review (SIR), Operational Readiness Review (ORR), Flight Readiness Review (FRR), Mission Readiness Review (MRR), Post-Launch Assessment Review (PLAR), Decommissioning Review (DR), and Disposal Readiness Review (DRR). Ensure review packages address all six assessment criteria.
summary: Plan and conduct mandatory life-cycle reviews at key program milestones

label: Standing Review Board coordination
content: Coordinate Standing Review Board (SRB) establishment, terms of reference, membership selection, and independence requirements. SRBs provide independent expert assessment at each life-cycle review. Ensure SRB findings are documented, tracked, and resolved. Manage Convening Authority responsibilities and reporting.
summary: Manage independent Standing Review Board composition and activities

label: Phase transition readiness assessment
content: Conduct readiness assessments using product maturity matrices, technical and management product checklists, and the six assessment criteria: (1) strategic alignment, (2) management approach adequacy, (3) technical approach adequacy, (4) cost/schedule estimate adequacy, (5) non-budget resource availability, (6) risk management adequacy. Compile Decision Memorandum for Decision Authority signature.
summary: Evaluate program readiness to advance between lifecycle phases

label: Requirements tailoring for program category and complexity
content: Apply tailoring process per NPR 7120.5 to adapt requirements to program category (Cat 1: >$2B or human spaceflight, Cat 2: $365M-$2B, Cat 3: <$365M) and complexity. Document tailoring rationale, obtain approval from appropriate authority. Accommodate innovative acquisition approaches while maintaining safety and mission assurance standards.
summary: Tailor governance requirements based on program size, category, and risk

label: Cost and schedule baseline management
content: Develop and maintain credible cost and schedule estimates, establish baselines at key decision points, track performance via Earned Value Management, and manage reserves. Ensure compliance with JCL requirements and federal budgeting processes.
summary: Establish, maintain, and defend program cost and schedule baselines

label: Joint Cost and Schedule Confidence Level analysis
content: Conduct Joint Cost and Schedule Confidence Level (JCL) analysis for programs/projects with LCC >$250M. Achieve minimum 70% JCL at KDP B/C, minimum 50% JCL funding at KDP C. Update JCL at CDR and KDP D for projects ≥$1B. Model cost and schedule risks probabilistically, identify key risk drivers, and inform management reserve and Unallocated Future Expenses (UFE) allocation.
summary: Perform probabilistic cost/schedule analysis coupling risks to estimates

label: Earned Value Management and variance analysis
content: Implement Earned Value Management (EVM) system per ANSI/EIA-748 and DCMA-MAN 3101. Track CPI, SPI, BAC, EAC, VAC, and To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI). TCPI = (BAC − EV) / (BAC − AC) to complete to original budget; TCPI = (BAC − EV) / (EAC − AC) to complete to EAC. TCPI >1.10 signals an unrealistic recovery requirement and undermines OTB credibility. OTB variance preservation rule: cumulative cost variance at the time of rebaselining must be isolated in a separate over-target budget bucket and cannot be absorbed into the new performance measurement baseline — this preserves variance history for oversight. Analyze cost and schedule variances, identify trends, and develop corrective action plans. Conduct monthly EVM reporting with cumulative and current-period metrics.
summary: Track cost and schedule performance using EVM metrics

label: Integrated Baseline Review execution
content: Conduct Integrated Baseline Reviews (IBR) to verify the Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB) is credible, comprehensive, and executable. Evaluate work scope, schedule, budget, and risk at the control account level. Assess adequacy of management processes, earned value techniques, and variance analysis. Document findings and track corrective actions.
summary: Verify performance measurement baseline through structured reviews

label: Basis of Estimate development and maintenance
content: Develop Basis of Estimate (BoE) documentation including ground rules, assumptions, estimating methodology (analogy, parametric, engineering build-up), data sources, model inputs, and rationale. Maintain BoE currency as program matures. Ensure traceability between BoE, WBS, and Integrated Master Schedule (IMS). Support CADRe (Cost Analysis Data Requirement) submissions.
summary: Document assumptions, methods, and rationale for cost/schedule estimates

label: Management reserve and UFE allocation
content: Allocate and manage Management Reserve (MR) for known risks within the Performance Measurement Baseline and Unallocated Future Expenses (UFE) for unknown unknowns outside the PMB. Track reserve burn-down rates, establish replenishment triggers, and report reserve status at program reviews. Ensure total budget (PMB + MR + UFE) aligns with Agency Baseline Commitment (ABC).
summary: Manage budget reserves for known and unknown risks

label: Federal budget formulation and PPBE alignment
content: Navigate NASA's Planning, Programming, Budgeting, and Execution (PPBE) process per NPR 9420.1 (formulation) and NPR 9470.1 (execution). Develop and defend budget submissions through mission directorate and agency review cycles. Translate program needs into budget line items, phased funding profiles, and out-year projections. Manage between authorized and appropriated funding levels.
summary: Align program budgets with NASA's Planning, Programming, Budgeting, and Execution process

label: Acquisition strategy and contract management
content: Plan and execute acquisition strategies including make/buy decisions, contract type selection, partnership structures, and procurement compliance. Manage contract performance, deliverables, and modifications throughout program lifecycle.
summary: Develop acquisition strategies and manage contract execution

label: Make-buy and competed-directed analysis
content: Conduct make-vs-buy analysis considering technical capability, cost, schedule, risk, and industrial base health. Evaluate competed vs. directed procurement approaches. Assess partnership and contribution opportunities. Document analysis in acquisition strategy with rationale for selected approach. Consider small business set-aside requirements and socioeconomic goals.
summary: Analyze sourcing options and competition strategies for program elements

label: Acquisition Strategy Meeting preparation
content: Prepare Acquisition Strategy Meeting (ASM) package during program formulation. Present acquisition approach, contract strategy, source selection plan, partnership arrangements, and procurement timeline to Decision Authority. Address industrial base assessment, supply chain risk, and competition considerations. Obtain approval before issuing solicitations.
summary: Prepare and present acquisition strategy for approval

label: Contract type selection and structuring
content: Select contract types based on program maturity, risk allocation, and requirements definition: Firm-Fixed-Price (FFP) for well-defined requirements, Cost-Plus-Fixed-Fee (CPFF) for R&D with uncertain scope, Cost-Plus-Award-Fee (CPAF) for incentivizing performance, Cost-Plus-Incentive-Fee (CPIF) for cost control incentives, Time-and-Materials (T&M) for level-of-effort work. Structure award fee criteria, incentive fee targets, and contract data requirements.
summary: Select appropriate contract types based on risk allocation and program maturity

label: Partnership and agreement structures
content: Develop and manage partnership structures including Space Act Agreements (SAA) — reimbursable and non-reimbursable, Memoranda of Understanding (MOU), Interagency Agreements (IAA), and international cooperative agreements. Define roles, responsibilities, interfaces, data rights, and intellectual property provisions. Manage partner contributions, milestones, and deliverables.
summary: Establish international, interagency, and industry partnership agreements

label: FAR-DFARS compliance for defense and government programs
content: Ensure acquisition activities comply with Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR), Defense Federal Acquisition Regulation Supplement (DFARS), and NASA FAR Supplement (NFS). Address key provisions: cost accounting standards (CAS), Truth in Negotiations Act (TINA), contractor business system requirements, organizational conflict of interest, intellectual property and data rights, and contract audit requirements.
summary: Ensure procurement compliance with Federal Acquisition Regulation

label: Requirements management and traceability
content: Establish and maintain requirements hierarchy from program-level through subsystem-level, ensure bidirectional traceability, manage requirements changes through formal change control, and coordinate verification and validation planning.
summary: Manage requirements flow-down, traceability, and change control

label: Requirements flow-down and decomposition
content: Manage requirements flow-down from programmatic requirements (stakeholder needs, mission objectives) through technical requirements (system, subsystem, component). Categorize requirements as: Programmatic (cost, schedule, performance), Institutional (safety, security, environmental), Allocated (derived from parent to child), Derived (implementation choices), and Technical Authority (engineering, S&MA, health). Ensure each requirement is necessary, verifiable, achievable, and traceable.
summary: Decompose top-level requirements through the system hierarchy

label: Verification and validation planning
content: Develop Verification and Validation (V&V) plan defining methods (test, analysis, inspection, demonstration) for each requirement. Establish V&V success criteria, test procedures, and acceptance thresholds. Coordinate V&V across system levels — component, subsystem, system, and mission. Track V&V completion status through requirements verification matrix. Ensure environmental and qualification testing coverage.
summary: Plan V&V approach for all requirements across program lifecycle

label: Requirements change control and impact assessment
content: Operate requirements change control process: capture change requests, assess technical/cost/schedule impacts, evaluate ripple effects through traceability matrix, obtain Configuration Control Board (CCB) approval, update requirements baseline, and flow changes to affected work packages. Maintain change history and rationale. Distinguish between class I (baseline) and class II (non-baseline) changes.
summary: Manage requirements changes through formal change control process

label: Traceability matrix development and maintenance
content: Develop and maintain Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) linking stakeholder needs → program requirements → system requirements → subsystem requirements → verification evidence. Ensure bidirectional traceability (parent-to-child and child-to-parent). Identify orphan requirements (no parent) and gold-plating (no child trace). Use traceability to assess change impacts and verify completeness.
summary: Maintain bidirectional traceability from stakeholder needs to verification evidence

label: Program governance and authority management
content: Operate within organizational governance frameworks, coordinate with Technical Authorities, manage formal decision processes, and prepare briefings for management councils. Ensure clear separation of programmatic and institutional responsibilities.
summary: Navigate governance structures, authorities, and decision-making processes

label: Programmatic and institutional authority coordination
content: Manage the interface between Programmatic Authority (mission directorates, programs, projects — responsible for cost, schedule, technical performance) and Institutional Authority (Mission Support Directorate, Center Directors — responsible for workforce, infrastructure, institutional capabilities). Ensure program needs are met through institutional services while respecting authority boundaries. Coordinate center resource allocation and facility usage.
summary: Navigate the boundary between programmatic and institutional responsibilities

label: Technical Authority coordination
content: Coordinate with three Technical Authority (TA) disciplines independently funded from program budgets: Engineering Technical Authority (Office of the Chief Engineer — OCE), Safety and Mission Assurance Technical Authority (Office of Safety and Mission Assurance — OSMA), and Health and Medical Technical Authority (Office of the Chief Health and Medical Officer — OCHMO). Manage TA requirements, waivers, and dissent resolution while maintaining TA independence.
summary: Interface with independent Technical Authority disciplines

label: Formal dissent process management
content: Administer the formal dissent process for documenting and escalating technical or programmatic disagreements that cannot be resolved at working level. Ensure dissenting opinions are captured, documented, and elevated to appropriate Decision Authority. Track dissent resolution through management chain. Protect dissenter rights while maintaining program progress. Document resolution rationale and any accepted risks.
summary: Manage technical and programmatic disagreement resolution

label: Management council briefing preparation
content: Prepare briefing packages for management councils: Agency Program Management Council (APMC, chaired by Associate Administrator), Mission Directorate PMC (DPMC, chaired by MDAA), and Center Management Council (CMC). Address program status, performance against baselines, risk posture, resource needs, and decision requests. Tailor content to council level and decision authority. Support Decision Memorandum development and signature.
summary: Prepare program briefings for Agency, Directorate, and Center management councils

label: Decision memorandum development
content: Develop Decision Memoranda documenting Key Decision Point outcomes, authorized cost/schedule/content parameters, conditions and actions required for next phase, and concurrence signatures. Capture Decision Authority direction, resource commitments, and any tailoring approvals. Ensure decision memoranda align with Agency Baseline Commitment (ABC) and Management Agreement frameworks.
summary: Draft and coordinate formal decision documents for program milestones

label: Stakeholder communication and external reporting
content: Coordinate program communications across internal and external stakeholders, manage congressional and oversight body reporting requirements, maintain partnership relationships, and implement knowledge management practices.
summary: Manage stakeholder communications, external reporting, and partnership coordination

label: Congressional and oversight body reporting
content: Manage external reporting requirements for major programs (LCC >$250M): prepare quarterly and annual reports to Congress and OMB, respond to Government Accountability Office (GAO) reviews, support Inspector General audits. Report significant cost/schedule breaches per Nunn-McCurdy (defense) or NASA Authorization Act thresholds. Develop corrective action plans for breached baselines.
summary: Prepare and submit required reports to Congress, OMB, and oversight bodies

label: Program status communication and reporting cadence
content: Define and execute program communication plan: monthly program status reports with EVM dashboards, weekly management summaries, quarterly program reviews, and ad-hoc status updates for significant events. Tailor reporting content and frequency to stakeholder tier — sponsors, customers, partners, team leads. Maintain single source of truth for program metrics and status.
summary: Establish and maintain program status reporting across stakeholder tiers

label: International and interagency partnership management
content: Manage day-to-day partnership coordination: joint schedule integration, interface control document maintenance, data exchange, technology transfer controls (ITAR/EAR compliance), partner milestone tracking, and contribution verification. Conduct joint program reviews. Manage cultural and time zone challenges for international partnerships. Escalate and resolve interface issues.
summary: Manage ongoing relationships with international and government partners

label: Knowledge management and lessons learned
content: Implement knowledge management approach: capture lessons learned at program reviews and phase gates, maintain searchable lessons learned database, conduct knowledge-sharing sessions, document institutional knowledge before personnel transitions. Apply relevant lessons from predecessor programs during formulation. Develop Knowledge Management Plan and conduct post-project knowledge capture at closeout (Phase F).
summary: Capture, share, and apply program knowledge and lessons learned

label: Work breakdown structure and financial control
content: Establish product-oriented Work Breakdown Structure as the framework for planning, budgeting, scheduling, cost accounting, and performance reporting. Ensure WBS integrates with organizational structure and financial systems.
summary: Develop WBS framework and align financial tracking with work scope

label: WBS development and dictionary maintenance
content: Develop Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) per NASA standard (NPR 7120.5) or MIL-STD-881 for defense programs. Use product-oriented decomposition — each element represents a deliverable product, service, or data item. Maintain WBS dictionary defining scope, deliverables, responsible organization, and success criteria for each element. Extend WBS to control account level for EVM integration.
summary: Develop product-oriented WBS and maintain work package definitions

label: Financial tracking and accounting alignment
content: Ensure WBS aligns with agency financial system and cost accounting structure. Map WBS elements to charge numbers, cost accounts, and budget line items. Track actual costs against budgeted costs at control account and work package level. Reconcile financial system data with EVM data. Support CADRe (Cost Analysis Data Requirement) submissions with accurate historical cost data.
summary: Align WBS with accounting systems for accurate cost collection and reporting

label: Performance Measurement Baseline structure
content: Structure Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB) integrating authorized work scope, time-phased budget, and schedule at the control account level. Ensure PMB = sum of all control account budgets. Establish Budgeted Cost of Work Scheduled (BCWS) time-phased profile. Define earned value measurement techniques for each control account (milestones, percent complete, level of effort, apportioned effort). Maintain PMB integrity through formal change control.
summary: Establish PMB integrating scope, schedule, and budget at control account level

label: Program formulation and strategic planning
content: Lead program formulation activities from concept studies through preliminary design, developing the technical approach, acquisition strategy, cost and schedule estimates, and organizational structure. Produce key formulation products: Formulation Agreement, Program/Project Plan, and Program Commitment Agreement.
summary: Lead program formulation including concept development, trade studies, and planning

label: Concept studies and Design Reference Mission analysis
content: Lead Pre-Phase A concept studies: develop Design Reference Missions (DRMs) defining mission objectives, concept of operations, and system architecture options. Conduct feasibility studies, technology surveys, and preliminary risk assessments. Evaluate multiple mission concepts against science/mission objectives, cost, schedule, and risk criteria. Support Mission Concept Review (MCR) preparation.
summary: Conduct and manage concept studies and mission architecture analysis

label: Alternatives analysis and trade studies
content: Conduct Analysis of Alternatives (AoA) evaluating competing approaches for mission architecture, technology selection, and implementation strategy. Define trade study criteria (performance, cost, schedule, risk, operability). Apply decision analysis methods (weighted scoring, Pugh matrix, multi-attribute utility theory). Document trade rationale and sensitivity analysis. Feed results into acquisition strategy and requirements baseline.
summary: Evaluate alternative approaches and conduct trade studies for key decisions

label: Technology Readiness Level assessment integration
content: Integrate Technology Readiness Level (TRL) assessments into program planning: assess TRL of critical technologies at each phase gate, develop Technology Development Plans for technologies below TRL 6, identify technology maturation risks and mitigation strategies. Coordinate with technology development programs. Ensure TRL ≥ 6 for critical technologies before KDP C (entry to Final Design). Track technology maturation progress.
summary: Integrate TRL assessments into program planning and risk management

label: Program and project plan development
content: Develop Program Plan (or Project Plan) as the governing management document: define mission objectives, technical approach, organizational structure, roles and responsibilities, management processes, schedule, cost estimate, risk management approach, acquisition strategy, and key milestones. Incorporate 28 control plans by reference. Obtain Decision Authority approval. Update plan at each major phase transition.
summary: Develop comprehensive program/project plans for stakeholder approval

label: Formulation Agreement and Program Commitment Agreement
content: Develop Formulation Agreement (FA) — agreement between Mission Directorate and program/project authorizing formulation activities, defining scope, resources, and schedule for formulation phase. Develop Program Commitment Agreement (PCA) — agreement between MDAA and AA authorizing transition to implementation, documenting cost/schedule/technical baselines, safety factors, and stakeholder commitments. These agreements establish the Agency Baseline Commitment (ABC).
summary: Develop formal agreements authorizing program formulation and implementation

label: Safety and mission assurance integration
content: Coordinate Safety and Mission Assurance (S&MA) activities across the program, ensuring compliance with NASA safety standards, environmental regulations, and quality requirements. Interface with S&MA Technical Authority while maintaining program schedule and budget.
summary: Integrate safety and mission assurance requirements into program execution

label: Safety and mission assurance plan coordination
content: Coordinate development of Safety and Mission Assurance Plan with OSMA Technical Authority. Define S&MA requirements, processes, and deliverables for each program phase. Address: reliability analysis, failure modes and effects analysis (FMEA), probabilistic risk assessment (PRA), parts and materials selection, contamination control, software assurance, and quality management. Ensure S&MA activities are funded, scheduled, and staffed.
summary: Develop and coordinate S&MA plan with Technical Authority

label: Human-rating certification coordination
content: Coordinate human-rating certification per NPR 8705.2 for crewed spacecraft and launch vehicles. Manage Human-Rating Certification Package development, track certification requirements compliance, coordinate human factors engineering, crew safety analysis, abort system verification, and life support qualification. Interface with Crew Office, Astronaut Health, and independent safety panels. Applicable to human spaceflight programs.
summary: Manage human-rating certification process for crewed systems

label: Environmental compliance and NEPA coordination
content: Manage National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA) compliance documentation: Environmental Impact Statements (EIS), Environmental Assessments (EA), or Categorical Exclusions as appropriate. Coordinate with environmental offices for launch site environmental reviews, orbital debris compliance per NASA-STD-8719.14, nuclear launch safety per NPR 8715.3 (for RTG/RHU missions), and hazardous materials management. Obtain environmental approvals before irreversible commitments.
summary: Ensure environmental regulatory compliance including NEPA documentation

label: Quality assurance and surveillance planning
content: Develop Quality Assurance Surveillance Plan (QASP) defining government oversight of contractor quality systems. Establish acceptance criteria, inspection points, and surveillance methods. Coordinate quality requirements flow-down to subcontractors. Monitor contractor quality metrics: nonconformance rates, corrective action effectiveness, process audit results. Ensure AS9100 or equivalent QMS compliance for aerospace contractors.
summary: Establish quality assurance approach for program deliverables

label: Leading indicators and program health assessment
content: Establish and monitor leading indicators for early detection of program issues. Track program health metrics across cost, schedule, technical, and risk dimensions. Analyze trends and develop proactive corrective actions before problems become critical.
summary: Monitor program health using leading indicators and trend analysis

label: Required and recommended leading indicators
content: Implement NASA-required leading indicators per NPR 7120.5: cost and schedule growth trends, reserve consumption rates, risk closure rates, requirements volatility, technical performance measures, and staffing metrics. Develop program-specific indicators tailored to mission type and risk profile. Establish indicator thresholds (green/yellow/red) and escalation triggers. Report leading indicators at monthly program reviews and management council briefings.
summary: Implement NASA-required and program-specific leading indicators

label: Program health dashboard and metrics framework
content: Develop and maintain program health dashboard integrating: EVM metrics (CPI, SPI, EAC), risk burn-down charts, requirements closure status, review action item tracking, technology maturation progress, workforce loading, and major milestone status. Provide drill-down capability from summary to detail level. Automate data collection where possible. Present dashboard at monthly and quarterly reviews.
summary: Maintain comprehensive program health dashboard for management visibility

label: Trend analysis and early warning systems
content: Perform trend analysis on key program metrics: EVM cumulative CPI/SPI trends, risk identification rate vs. closure rate, reserve burn-down slope, requirements change rate, action item aging, and contractor performance trends. Establish early warning thresholds and automatic alerts. Develop root cause analysis capability for adverse trends. Brief trend analysis findings at program reviews with recommended corrective actions.
summary: Analyze performance trends to identify emerging issues before they become critical

label: Design stability and maturity assessment
content: Track design stability metrics: drawing release rate vs. plan, engineering change request rate, interface control document maturity, test-as-you-fly compliance, hardware/software integration status. Assess Technical Readiness Level (TRL) and Manufacturing Readiness Level (MRL) progression against plan. Flag design churn and instability as leading indicators of cost/schedule growth. Coordinate with Systems Engineering for maturity gate assessments.
summary: Assess design stability and technical maturity throughout development

label: Manufacturing Readiness Level progression tracking
content: Monitor Manufacturing Readiness Level (MRL) progression from MRL 1 (basic manufacturing implications identified) through MRL 10 (full rate production demonstrated). Conduct MRL assessments at key milestones: MRL 4-5 at PDR, MRL 6 at CDR, MRL 8 at production readiness. Track producibility risks, manufacturing process maturation, tooling development, and supply chain readiness. Flag MRL shortfalls as program risks requiring mitigation plans.
summary: Track MRL advancement through development and production phases

label: Contracts Manager
content: Senior contracts role responsible for acquisition strategy development, contract type selection and structuring, partnership and agreement frameworks, and regulatory compliance oversight. Works with Program Managers to align contract vehicles with program needs and risk posture.
summary: Manage acquisition strategy, contract negotiation, and partnership agreements

label: Program Control Analyst
content: Specialist role responsible for establishing and maintaining program cost and schedule baselines, Earned Value Management implementation, Work Breakdown Structure development, Integrated Baseline Reviews, JCL analysis, and budget formulation alignment. Partners with Financial Analysts for reporting and with Program Managers for decision support.
summary: Manage cost/schedule baselines, EVM, WBS, and financial control for programs

label: Program financial planning and budget formulation
content: Plan and execute program financial management including budget formulation, phasing, obligation and expenditure tracking, and alignment with federal Planning, Programming, Budgeting, and Execution (PPBE) processes.
summary: Develop and manage program budgets through federal budgeting processes

label: PPBE process alignment and budget submission
content: Align program financial plans with the Planning, Programming, Budgeting, and Execution (PPBE) process. Develop budget submissions through mission directorate and agency review cycles per NPR 9420.1 (formulation) and NPR 9470.1 (execution). Translate program needs into budget line items with out-year projections. Manage between authorized and appropriated funding levels. Address four fiscal laws: annual authorization, Antideficiency Act, Misappropriation Act, and Bona Fide Need rule.
summary: Navigate DoD/NASA budget formulation and execution cycles

label: Budget phasing and obligation-expenditure tracking
content: Develop time-phased budget profiles aligning planned obligations and expenditures with program milestones. Track obligation rates against appropriation availability periods. Monitor expenditure curves against planned disbursement schedules. Apply spreading functions (Uniform, Trapezoid, Beta, Rayleigh, Weibull) for budget phasing. Reconcile obligations vs. expenditures vs. accruals for financial reporting.
summary: Track obligations and expenditures against phased budget profiles

label: Funding profile development and appropriation management
content: Develop funding profiles across appropriation categories: RDT&E (Research, Development, Test & Evaluation), Procurement, O&M (Operations & Maintenance), and MILCON (Military Construction). Manage appropriation color-of-money constraints and fiscal year availability. Build Spruill Charts (program funding and quantities over time). Align funding profiles with contract types and payment schedules.
summary: Develop multi-year funding profiles across appropriation categories

label: Program financial baseline and management reserve tracking
content: Establish program financial baselines including Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB), Management Reserve (MR), and Unallocated Future Expenses (UFE). Track reserve burn-down rates and consumption trends. Establish replenishment triggers and escalation criteria. Report financial baseline status at program reviews. Ensure total budget (PMB + MR + UFE) aligns with Agency Baseline Commitment (ABC).
summary: Establish and maintain program financial baselines with reserve management

label: Contract funding and modification financial processing
content: Process contract funding actions including incremental funding, definitization of undefinitized contract actions (UCAs), and contract modifications. Track obligation authority against contract ceiling. Manage progress payment and performance-based payment processing. Monitor contract funding status reports and remaining unfunded requirements. Coordinate de-obligation of excess funds at contract closeout.
summary: Process contract funding actions and financial modifications

label: Variance analysis and Estimate at Completion
content: Perform detailed variance analysis using Earned Value Management data, develop Estimates at Completion using multiple projection methods, and manage over-target baseline processes when program performance degrades beyond recovery.
summary: Analyze cost and schedule variances and project final program costs

label: CPI and SPI trend analysis and interpretation
content: Track Cost Performance Index (CPI) and Schedule Performance Index (SPI) at cumulative and current-period levels. Analyze CPI/SPI trends for early warning of cost/schedule growth. Apply the CPI stability phenomenon (cumulative CPI stabilizes by 20% completion). Compare CPI/SPI at control account, WBS element, and total program levels. Develop CPI*SPI composite index for overall health assessment. Flag adverse trends for management action.
summary: Analyze cost and schedule performance indices for trend detection

label: Estimate at Completion projection methods
content: Develop Estimate at Completion (EAC) using multiple methods: (1) EAC = BAC/CPI (performance-based), (2) EAC = AC + (BAC-EV)/CPI (cumulative efficiency), (3) EAC = AC + (BAC-EV)/(CPI*SPI) (composite), (4) EAC = AC + independent ETC (bottom-up re-estimate). Compare methods against contractor EAC. Apply regression-based EAC for trend projection. Document EAC methodology selection rationale. Track Variance at Completion (VAC = BAC - EAC) and To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI).
summary: Develop EAC using multiple statistical and analytical methods

label: Over-target baseline and schedule management
content: Execute Over-Target Baseline (OTB) and Over-Target Schedule (OTS) processes when program performance degrades beyond recovery to current baseline. OTB is a contract management and EVM administrative action governed by ANSI/EIA-748 and DCMA-MAN 3101—it is legally and procedurally distinct from the Nunn-McCurdy statutory notification process and runs on a parallel track; OTB approval does not satisfy or replace congressional notification obligations, and vice versa. Evaluate need for OTB/OTS vs. continued management within existing baseline. Develop revised PMB reflecting realistic cost-to-go. Maintain traceability from original baseline through OTB adjustments; isolate cumulative cost variance in an over-target budget bucket (variance preservation rule—cannot be absorbed into new PMB). Obtain government approval for OTB implementation. Document rationale and revised management approach per ANSI/EIA-748 guidelines.
summary: Manage OTB/OTS processes when program exceeds baseline

label: Earned value data validation and surveillance
content: Validate contractor EVM system compliance with ANSI/EIA-748 32 criteria. Conduct Integrated Baseline Reviews (IBR) to verify Performance Measurement Baseline credibility at control account level. Perform ongoing EVM surveillance: verify data accuracy, assess management processes, evaluate corrective action effectiveness. Review Contract Performance Reports (CPR/IPMR Formats 1-5). Identify and investigate anomalies: retroactive changes, negative management reserve, schedule visibility issues.
summary: Validate contractor EVM data and conduct system surveillance

label: Financial reporting and audit preparation
content: Manage financial reporting requirements including cost data deliverables, audit preparation and response, and Nunn-McCurdy breach analysis for programs exceeding cost growth thresholds.
summary: Prepare financial reports and support DCAA/GAO audit activities

label: DCAA audit preparation and response
content: Prepare for DCAA audits of incurred costs, forward pricing rates, accounting system adequacy, and cost accounting standards compliance. Maintain audit-ready documentation: timekeeping records, cost pool reconciliations, rate calculations, and allocation methodology evidence. Respond to audit findings and negotiate resolution. Support pre-award accounting system surveys. Prepare incurred cost submissions within 6 months of fiscal year end per FAR 52.216-7.
summary: Prepare for and support Defense Contract Audit Agency audits

label: GAO audit response and best practice compliance
content: Support Government Accountability Office (GAO) reviews of program cost estimates. Map cost estimating practices against GAO's 18 best practices across four characteristics: Comprehensive, Well-Documented, Accurate, and Credible. Prepare responses to GAO findings. Maintain documentation sufficient for independent estimate reconstruction. Address GAO cost estimating assessment questions covering: estimate documentation, program definition, ground rules, data validation, estimating methods, inflation adjustment, sensitivity analysis, risk/uncertainty analysis, and crosschecks.
summary: Support GAO reviews using the 18 best practices framework

label: Nunn-McCurdy breach analysis and reporting
content: Monitor program cost growth against Nunn-McCurdy breach thresholds for MDAPs per 10 USC §4376. SIGNIFICANT BREACH (15% current or 30% original PAUC/APUC growth): USD(A&S) must notify Congress; CAPE must produce an Independent Cost Estimate (ICE); SAR and APB must be revised to reflect the new baseline; program must submit corrective action plan. CRITICAL BREACH (25% current or 50% original PAUC/APUC growth): triggers presumed termination review; USD(A&S) must certify to Congress that: (1) program is essential to national security; (2) no alternatives provide equal military capability at lower cost; (3) new Acquisition Program Baseline is reasonable; (4) management structure is adequate to control cost and schedule. Note: when evaluating a significant breach, always check whether cumulative growth against the ORIGINAL APB baseline may separately be approaching the critical threshold—the two breach tier calculations are independent. Support root cause analysis using Framing Assumptions methodology (PARCA/ADA). Develop corrective action plans for breached programs.
summary: Monitor and report cost growth against Nunn-McCurdy thresholds; distinguish significant breach (notification + ICE + APB revision) from critical breach (presumed termination + four USD(A&S) certifications)

label: Cost and software data reporting deliverables
content: Manage Cost and Software Data Reporting (CSDR) deliverables: FlexFile (detailed cost/hours data), Quantity Data Report, Software Resources Data Report (SRDR), and Contractor Business Data report. Submit to Cost Assessment Data Enterprise (CADE) repository. Ensure data quality and completeness per CSDR plan. Support CADRe (Cost Analysis Data Requirement) submissions at project milestones (SRR, PDR, CDR, SIR, Launch). Maintain traceability between financial system actuals and CSDR submissions.
summary: Manage CSDR and financial data deliverables to government repositories

label: Affordability analysis and cost-as-independent-variable
content: Perform affordability analyses throughout program lifecycle and apply Cost As an Independent Variable (CAIV) methodology to balance performance requirements against cost constraints.
summary: Conduct affordability assessments and cost-constrained trade studies

label: Affordability assessment across program lifecycle
content: Conduct affordability assessments comparing projected program costs against available budget toplines across the Future Years Defense Program (FYDP) or equivalent planning horizon. Evaluate affordability at each Key Decision Point and major program review. Assess affordability impacts of requirements changes, schedule delays, and technical risks. Consider operations and sustainment costs as major affordability drivers, especially for long-duration programs. Present affordability analysis results to decision authorities.
summary: Evaluate program affordability at each decision point and budget cycle

label: Cost-as-independent-variable trade studies
