content: Apply Cost As an Independent Variable (CAIV) methodology: establish cost targets as design constraints, challenge performance requirements that drive cost disproportionately, evaluate cost-performance trade spaces to find optimal balance. Support design-to-cost decisions by quantifying cost impacts of performance increments. Coordinate with systems engineering on requirements prioritization (must-have vs. nice-to-have). Document trade study rationale including rejected alternatives.
summary: Apply CAIV to converge design on cost targets rather than performance maximums

label: Life-cycle cost projection and sustainment affordability
content: Develop Life-Cycle Cost Estimates (LCCE) spanning development, production, operations & maintenance, and disposal phases. Apply appropriate estimating methods for each phase: parametric for early development, build-up for production, analogy for sustainment. Project operating and support costs using historical O&S data from VAMOSC/AFTOC/OSMIS databases. Assess sustainment affordability against projected out-year budgets. Track total ownership cost (TOC) metrics.
summary: Project total life-cycle costs including operations and disposal

label: Indirect rate structure and forward pricing
content: Manage the development, negotiation, and maintenance of indirect cost rate structures, forward pricing rate agreements, and incurred cost submissions for government contract compliance.
summary: Develop and maintain indirect rate structures and forward pricing agreements

label: Rate base development and indirect cost pool structure
content: Design indirect cost pool structure: overhead pools (engineering, manufacturing, material handling, G&A), service center allocations, and B&P/IR&D pools. Select appropriate allocation bases (direct labor dollars, direct labor hours, total cost input, value-added). Ensure rate structure reflects actual cost causation. Maintain consistency between rate structure and Disclosure Statement. Develop provisional billing rates for contract invoicing. Reconcile actual indirect costs to applied amounts.
summary: Design and maintain indirect cost pool and allocation base structures

label: Disclosure Statement development and maintenance
content: Develop and maintain CAS Disclosure Statement (CASB DS-1 and DS-2 forms) accurately describing cost accounting practices. File amendments when practices change, within required timelines. Ensure consistency between disclosed practices and actual practices — noncompliance exposure for discrepancies. Address: direct/indirect cost definitions, home office allocations, IR&D/B&P treatment, cost of money computation, pension costs, deferred compensation. Support DCAA review of Disclosure Statement adequacy.
summary: Maintain CAS Disclosure Statement reflecting actual accounting practices

label: Incurred cost submission and final rate negotiation
content: Prepare annual incurred cost submissions within 6 months of fiscal year end per FAR 52.216-7. Include: Schedule H (home office allocation), Schedule I (corporate/segment allocations), Schedule J (cost pool composition), supporting schedules for rate calculations, and Certificate of Final Indirect Costs. Support DCAA audit of incurred costs. Negotiate final indirect rates with Administrative Contracting Officer (ACO). Process retroactive rate adjustments to affected contracts.
summary: Prepare annual incurred cost submissions and negotiate final indirect rates

label: Forward Pricing Rate Agreement negotiation
content: Develop forward pricing rate proposals supported by rate buildup documentation, trend analysis, and projection methodology. Negotiate Forward Pricing Rate Agreements (FPRAs) or Forward Pricing Rate Recommendations (FPRRs) with DCAA/ACO. Address: projected direct and indirect rates, escalation factors, labor rate projections, overhead absorption forecasts. Maintain FPRA currency — update when significant changes occur. Use FPRAs to streamline proposal pricing and reduce negotiation cycle time.
summary: Negotiate and maintain forward pricing rate agreements with government

label: Cost Accounting Standards compliance
content: Manage compliance with Cost Accounting Standards (CAS) including applicability determinations, cost impact analysis for accounting changes, and accounting system adequacy for government contract cost accumulation.
summary: Ensure compliance with CAS requirements for government contracts

label: CAS applicability determination and clause analysis
content: Determine CAS applicability: full coverage (CAS-covered contracts >$50M and $7.5M threshold), modified coverage (CAS 401/402 only for contracts $2M-$50M), and exempt categories. Analyze applicability of individual standards: CAS 401 (consistency), 402 (cost allocation), 403 (home office allocation), 404 (capitalization), 405 (unallowable costs), 406 (cost accounting periods), 407 (standard costs), 408 (compensated absence), 409 (depreciation), 410 (G&A allocation), 411 (material acquisition costs), 412/413 (pension costs), 414 (cost of money), 415 (deferred compensation), 416 (insurance), 418 (direct/indirect allocation), 420 (IR&D/B&P).
summary: Determine CAS applicability and manage clause requirements

label: Cost impact statement preparation for accounting changes
content: Prepare cost impact statements when changing cost accounting practices (voluntary or required). Quantify impact on government contracts: increased costs to government, decreased costs, and cost-neutral shifts. Follow CAS Administration process: submit Disclosure Statement amendment, prepare cost impact proposal, negotiate equitable adjustment with ACO. Distinguish between required changes (CAS noncompliance correction), desirable changes (improved cost causation), and unilateral changes. Maintain records supporting impact calculations.
summary: Analyze and report cost impacts of accounting practice changes

label: Accounting system adequacy and DFARS compliance
content: Maintain accounting system meeting DFARS 252.242-7006 adequacy criteria: proper segregation of direct/indirect costs, accumulation at contract/task level, exclusion of unallowable costs per FAR Part 31, timely recording, consistent allocation methodology, and audit trail capability. Support DCAA accounting system audits and pre-award surveys. Address deficiency findings and develop corrective action plans. Ensure system integrates timekeeping, purchasing, accounts payable, and general ledger functions for government contract compliance.
summary: Maintain accounting system adequacy for government contract cost accumulation

label: Unallowable cost identification and exclusion
content: Implement processes to identify and exclude costs unallowable under FAR 31.205: entertainment, alcoholic beverages, bad debts, contributions/donations, fines/penalties, goodwill, interest (with exceptions), lobbying, organizational costs, patent costs (in some cases), and expressly unallowable costs. Maintain unallowable cost screening in accounting system. Apply directly associated cost provisions (FAR 31.201-6). Ensure penalties avoidance for claiming expressly unallowable costs per FAR 42.709. Train personnel on allowability determination.
summary: Identify and exclude unallowable costs per FAR Part 31

label: Contract pricing and profit/fee analysis
content: Support contract pricing activities including cost element development for proposals, weighted guidelines profit/fee analysis, and should-cost analysis for government cost evaluations.
summary: Support contract pricing development and profit/fee negotiations

label: Weighted guidelines profit-fee analysis
content: Apply DoD Weighted Guidelines (WGL) method per DFARS 215.404-71 for profit/fee negotiation: assign values across profit factors — technical risk (contractor effort: material, labor, overhead, subcontract type/complexity), contract type risk (cost risk by contract type), facilities capital employed, and cost efficiency factor. Calculate total profit objective as weighted composite. Compare against historical profit rates for similar contract types. Support profit/fee negotiation positions with documented rationale. Address performance-based fee structures for CPAF/CPIF contracts.
summary: Apply DoD weighted guidelines method for profit/fee determination

label: Price-to-cost reconciliation and analysis
content: Reconcile contractor proposed prices to government cost estimates for negotiation support. Decompose contractor pricing: direct labor rates × hours by category, material costs (BOM, raw material, purchased parts), subcontract costs, other direct costs, indirect rates applied, profit/fee. Identify and analyze pricing discrepancies. Evaluate labor rate reasonableness against Bureau of Labor Statistics data and historical actuals. Assess material pricing against market data. Support Truth in Negotiations Act (TINA/10 USC 3702) defective pricing analysis when applicable.
summary: Reconcile contractor proposed prices to estimated costs for negotiations

label: Should-cost analysis and cost reduction initiatives
content: Conduct should-cost analysis to establish what a product or service should cost under efficient production conditions. Analyze contractor cost structure for inefficiencies: excess overhead rates, uncompetitive subcontracting, underutilized capacity, inefficient manufacturing processes. Develop cost reduction recommendations and negotiate savings into contract pricing. Support government should-cost teams. Apply should-cost techniques to major subcontracts and material purchases. Track should-cost savings realization against negotiated targets.
summary: Conduct should-cost analysis to identify cost reduction opportunities

label: Cost Estimator
content: Specialist role responsible for developing life-cycle cost estimates using analogy, parametric, and engineering build-up methods. Manages the full cost estimating process from program definition through documentation, including risk/uncertainty analysis via Monte Carlo simulation, JCL analysis, and economic trade studies. Operates within DoD CAPE, NASA CAD, and GAO frameworks.
summary: Develop credible, defensible cost estimates for defense and aerospace programs

label: Cost estimating process management
content: Execute the 12-step cost estimating process aligned across DoD (CAPE 9-step), GAO (12-step), and NASA (12-task) frameworks. Manage cost estimate development from customer request through final documentation and presentation.
summary: Manage the 12-step cost estimating process from initiation to documentation

label: Cost estimate plan development
content: Develop Cost Estimate Plan defining: policy basis, purpose and scope, estimate structure (WBS/CES alignment), process and approach by cost element, team assignments and responsibilities, data collection plan, travel requirements, review schedule, and deliverables timeline. Identify stakeholders and coordination requirements. Establish estimate classification: Independent Cost Estimate (ICE), Component Cost Estimate (CCE), Program Office Estimate (POE), Service Cost Estimate (SCE), or Independent Government Cost Estimate (IGCE).
summary: Develop the cost estimate plan defining scope, approach, team, and schedule

label: Ground rules and assumptions development
content: Develop three categories of estimate basis: (1) Ground Rules — program manager's domain constraints: production quantities, schedule, base year, recurring/nonrecurring segregation, government-furnished equipment. (2) Assumptions — analyst-developed bridges for unknowns: software reuse percentages, technology maturity, inflation rates, FMS impacts, phase overlaps. (3) Framing Assumptions (DoD) — 3-5 critical foundational assumptions from PARCA/ADA root cause analysis of Nunn-McCurdy breaches. Document all assumptions with rationale and sensitivity indicators.
summary: Establish ground rules, assumptions, and framing assumptions for the estimate

label: Cost Analysis Requirements Description utilization
content: Utilize the Cost Analysis Requirements Description (CARD) as the primary source for program definition in DoD estimates. Extract: system description, performance characteristics, program schedule, acquisition strategy, production quantities, logistics concept, and manpower requirements. For NASA, use CADRe (Cost Analysis Data Requirement) capturing programmatic/technical/cost data at milestones. Supplement with contractor proposals, technical specifications, and subject matter expert interviews. Develop kickoff meeting questions per DoD CEG Sample Questions (Appendix D).
summary: Extract program definition from CARD and technical baseline documents

label: Estimate documentation and basis of estimate
content: Document cost estimates following standard organization: executive summary, program description, estimate structure dictionary, ground rules and assumptions, data sources, methodology by cost element, cost model description, results summary, risk analysis results, crosschecks, and recommendations. Develop Basis of Estimate (BoE) with sufficient detail for independent reconstruction. Apply GAO documentation best practices: traceable, defensible, repeatable. Support Air Force documentation checklist (ACAT I/II/III formats). Archive supporting data and models.
summary: Document cost estimates with full traceability and defensibility

label: Cost estimate presentation and defense
content: Prepare cost estimate briefings using standard visualization tools: Sand Charts (cost by year and phase), Pareto Charts (rank-ordered cost contributors), Tornado Charts (sensitivity/what-if), Cost Element Charts (cross-estimate comparison), Spruill Charts (funding and quantities), and S-Curves (probability vs. cost). Defend estimate methodology, data choices, and risk analysis results before Cost Review Boards (CRB), program decision authorities, and congressional staffers. Address challenges to assumptions, methods, and results with documented rationale.
summary: Present and defend cost estimates to decision authorities and review boards

label: Cost estimating methodologies
content: Select and apply appropriate cost estimating methods based on program maturity, data availability, and estimate purpose. Master four core methods plus supporting techniques.
summary: Apply analogy, parametric, build-up, and extrapolation methods for cost estimation

label: Analogy method application
content: Apply analogy method: identify comparable historical system, quantify technical and programmatic differences, develop adjustment factors for complexity, size, performance, and technology. Use single-system analogy for early-phase estimates when limited data exists. Document comparability rationale and adjustment factor derivation. Best suited for Pre-Phase A/Phase A estimates, ROM development, and crosscheck validation. Limitations: subjectivity of adjustment factors, sensitivity to system selection, difficulty with novel technologies.
summary: Estimate costs by adjusting historical costs of comparable systems

label: Parametric method and CER development
content: Develop Cost Estimating Relationships (CERs) and Schedule Estimating Relationships (SERs) using regression analysis. 7-step process: form hypothesis, collect data, evaluate data, perform regression, test relationship, select equation, validate. Apply linear (Cost = a + b*X) and log-linear (Cost = a * X^b) forms. Evaluate fit statistics: t-statistic, F-statistic, R-squared, standard error, confidence/prediction intervals, MAD. Address multicollinearity, outliers, and heteroscedasticity. Apply CERs from established models: NAFCOM, PRICE TruePlanning, SEER-H, SEER-SEM.
summary: Develop and apply Cost Estimating Relationships using regression analysis

label: Engineering build-up method
content: Apply engineering build-up (grassroots) method: decompose to lowest WBS level, estimate labor hours by skill category, apply labor rates, estimate material quantities and unit costs, add other direct costs (travel, test support, facilities), apply indirect rates (overhead, G&A, FCCM). Use Delphi method for expert elicitation when engineering data is limited. Best suited for mature programs (Phase C/D), production estimates, and proposal development. Advantages: detailed audit trail, direct mapping to work scope. Limitations: time-intensive, may miss cross-element integration costs.
summary: Develop bottom-up estimates from labor, material, and overhead components

label: Extrapolation from actuals and EVM-based projection
content: Apply extrapolation from actuals for in-progress programs: use actual cost data (ACWP) plus Estimate to Complete (ETC) based on CPI/SPI performance trends. Apply EVM-based methods: ETC = (BAC-BCWP)/CPI, ETC = (BAC-BCWP)/(CPI*SPI), or independent bottom-up ETC. Evaluate performance stability before selecting projection method. Use CSDR FlexFile actual cost data for completed activities. Compare against contractor EAC and independent projections. Best suited for programs past 20% completion with stable performance metrics.
summary: Project costs using actual expenditure data and EVM performance trends

label: Learning curve analysis and cost improvement modeling
content: Apply learning curve (cost improvement curve) theory: Unit Cost = T1 * X^b, where T1 is first unit cost, X is unit number, b = ln(slope)/ln(2). Determine appropriate slope from historical data — typical aerospace manufacturing slopes: 75-95% depending on labor content, complexity, and production breaks. Distinguish unit theory vs. cumulative average theory. Adjust for: lot buys, production rate changes, production breaks, design changes, and multi-contract learning. Apply learning to labor hours; material learning typically less steep. Validate slopes against CADE/CSDR historical data.
summary: Model production cost reduction using learning curve theory. USE CASE: should-cost estimation and LRIP lot pricing — NOT for executive strategic decisions about program diversification or customer concentration risk. Pair with cost estimator role only.

label: Data collection and normalization
content: Manage cost data acquisition from government repositories, contractor reports, and historical databases. Validate data quality, normalize for inflation, accounting differences, and quantity effects.
summary: Collect, validate, and normalize cost data from government and industry sources

label: CADE and CSDR data systems utilization
content: Utilize Cost Assessment Data Enterprise (CADE) as the primary DoD cost data repository. Access datasets: CSDR (Cost and Software Data Reporting) for contractor-reported cost/schedule data, EVM data for performance trends, O&S data for sustainment costs, SRDR (Software Resources Data Report) for software-specific metrics. Access component-level repositories: VAMOSC (Navy), AFTOC (Air Force), OSMIS (Army) for operations and support data. Use DAMIR for acquisition reporting and FPDS for contract information. For NASA, use ONCE (One NASA Cost Engineering) database of completed CADRe submissions.
summary: Access and utilize DoD cost data repositories for estimating

label: Data validation and quality assessment
content: Validate cost data across four dimensions: completeness (are all costs captured?), accuracy (do reported costs match actuals?), applicability (is the data relevant to the program being estimated?), and timeliness (how current is the data?). Cross-reference contractor-reported data against financial system actuals. Identify and investigate outliers. Assess data bias — optimistic vs. pessimistic reporting. Verify WBS mapping consistency across data sources. Document data limitations and impact on estimate confidence.
summary: Validate data completeness, accuracy, and applicability for estimating

label: Inflation and escalation adjustment
content: Normalize historical cost data for inflation using appropriate indices: OSD Comptroller Deflators (DoD), NASA New Start Inflation Index (NNSI), Bureau of Labor Statistics indices (BLS), or program-specific escalation rates. Convert between dollar-year bases: Base Year (BY$), Constant Year (CY$), Current/Then-Year (TY$). Apply compound inflation for multi-year conversions. Distinguish between inflation (general price level changes) and escalation (specific commodity/labor rate changes). Follow CAPE Inflation and Escalation Best Practices (2021).
summary: Normalize historical costs for inflation and convert between dollar-year bases

label: Technical baseline description development
content: Develop technical baseline description defining cost-driving characteristics: system weight, size, performance parameters, technology readiness levels, software size (SLOC/function points), complexity factors, integration requirements, and test program scope. Map technical parameters to WBS elements for CER application. Identify analogous systems and quantify technical differences. Coordinate with systems engineering for requirements decomposition. Track requirements changes that impact cost estimates.
summary: Define system technical characteristics driving cost estimates

label: Cost risk and uncertainty analysis
content: Apply probabilistic analysis methods to quantify cost estimate uncertainty, generate confidence level distributions, and support risk-informed decision making.
summary: Quantify cost risk and uncertainty using probabilistic methods

label: Monte Carlo simulation for cost estimates
content: Execute Monte Carlo simulation to quantify total estimate uncertainty: define input distributions for each cost element (triangular, beta, lognormal, uniform), specify correlations between cost elements, run 10,000+ iterations to generate output distribution. Interpret results: point estimate percentile, confidence intervals, key risk drivers. Use tools: Crystal Ball, @RISK, ACEIT Risk, Python (scipy/numpy). Apply both inputs-based method (vary CER inputs) and outputs-based method (vary cost element totals). Document simulation setup, assumptions, and limitations.
summary: Execute Monte Carlo simulation to generate probabilistic cost distributions

label: S-curve development and confidence level reporting
content: Generate S-curves (cumulative distribution functions) showing probability of achieving various cost levels. Report key percentiles: 20th (optimistic), 50th (most likely), 70th (program baseline), 80th (budget level). Calculate Coefficient of Variation (CV = standard deviation / mean) as estimate uncertainty metric. Compare S-curves across estimate versions to show uncertainty evolution. Present S-curves to decision authorities with clear explanation of confidence levels. Apply Method of Moments as alternative to full Monte Carlo when simulation is impractical.
summary: Generate cumulative probability distributions for cost estimates

label: Sensitivity analysis execution
content: Execute sensitivity analysis: vary each uncertain parameter one-at-a-time across its plausible range while holding others at baseline. Rank results in Tornado Chart format showing which parameters have greatest cost impact. Identify top cost drivers for management attention and risk mitigation. Limitations: does not capture interaction effects between parameters, may miss compounding risks. Use as input to risk analysis — top sensitivity drivers should have well-justified probability distributions. Document parameter ranges and rationale.
summary: Identify cost drivers through systematic parameter variation

label: Probability distribution selection and fitting
content: Select probability distributions for cost elements based on data availability and risk characteristics: Triangular (most common — min/most likely/max), Beta (flexible shape for bounded ranges), Lognormal (right-skewed for cost growth), Uniform (equal probability across range), Normal (symmetric uncertainty). Fit distributions to historical data using goodness-of-fit tests (Kolmogorov-Smirnov, Chi-squared, Anderson-Darling). Address correlation specification: positive correlations between cost elements increase total variance. Avoid common errors: symmetric distributions for inherently skewed costs, zero correlation assumption, over-narrow ranges.
summary: Select appropriate probability distributions for cost risk analysis inputs

label: Risk event modeling and discrete risk integration
content: Model discrete risk events from program risk register: assign probability of occurrence and cost/schedule impact if realized. Integrate discrete risks with continuous cost uncertainty in simulation — discrete risks fire probabilistically each iteration, adding impact to base cost. Avoid double-counting between discrete risks and continuous uncertainty ranges. Distinguish risk (identifiable events) from uncertainty (general estimating imprecision). Map risk events to WBS elements. Use risk-adjusted cost as basis for management reserve and UFE allocation.
summary: Model discrete risk events and integrate with continuous uncertainty

label: Joint Cost and Schedule Confidence Level analysis
content: Execute Joint Cost and Schedule Confidence Level (JCL) analysis to provide integrated probabilistic assessment of program cost and schedule, required for DoD and NASA programs above cost thresholds. DoD approval standard per WSARA/DoDI 5000.73: 80% JCL confidence is required at milestone decisions. Results below 80% indicate insufficient budget or schedule margin and typically require program adjustments before milestone approval or OTB authorization. When evaluating an OTB rebaseline, validate the contractor's proposed baseline achieves 80% JCL confidence; a result below 80% means the rebaseline is underfunded or unscheduled.
summary: Perform JCL analysis coupling cost and schedule risks probabilistically

label: JCL 6-step process execution
content: Execute JCL 6-step process: (0) Identify goals — define analysis scope, schedule network, cost elements, and risk register. (1) Develop summary analysis schedule — logic-linked network capturing critical path and key milestones. (2) Load cost onto schedule activities — classify as Time-Dependent (TD, varies with duration) or Time-Independent (TI, fixed regardless of duration). (3) Incorporate risk list — map discrete risks to schedule activities with probability and impact. (4) Conduct uncertainty analysis — apply distributions to cost and duration elements. (5) Calculate and view results — run Monte Carlo, generate scatterplots and frontier lines.
summary: Execute the standard 6-step JCL analysis process

label: Cost-loaded schedule development
content: Develop cost-loaded schedule integrating WBS cost elements with Integrated Master Schedule (IMS) activities. Classify cost elements as Time-Dependent (labor, facilities — cost scales with duration) or Time-Independent (hardware, materials — fixed cost regardless of schedule). Map cost estimates to schedule activities maintaining traceability to WBS. Validate schedule logic: network completeness, realistic durations, proper dependency types (FS, FF, SS, SF). Ensure critical path is identified and resource-constrained.
summary: Build resource-loaded schedules linking cost elements to schedule activities

label: Discrete risk and uncertainty integration in JCL
content: Integrate discrete risks from program risk register into JCL model: each risk has probability of occurrence and impact on cost/schedule activities if realized. Apply continuous uncertainty distributions (triangular: low/most-likely/high) to cost elements and activity durations separately from discrete risks. Avoid double-counting: discrete risks represent specific identified threats, uncertainty represents general estimating imprecision. Run Monte Carlo generating cost-schedule scatterplot showing joint probability distribution.
summary: Integrate discrete risks and continuous uncertainty for JCL simulation

label: JCL scatterplot interpretation and confidence reporting
content: Interpret JCL scatterplot: each point represents one simulation iteration (cost, completion date). Draw frontier lines at 50% and 70% confidence levels. Four quadrants: both cost and schedule within target (lower-left, favorable), cost overrun only, schedule delay only, both exceeded (upper-right, worst case). Report joint probability of meeting both cost and schedule targets simultaneously. NASA policy: 70% JCL for Agency Baseline Commitment, 50% minimum for Management Agreement at KDP C. DoD: JCL required for programs >$250M LCC. Present results with sensitivity to key assumptions.
summary: Interpret JCL results and report confidence levels to decision authorities

label: Cost models and tools
content: Design, build, and maintain cost models using spreadsheet and specialized cost estimating tools. Apply standard cost metrics, phasing methods, and WBS/CES alignment frameworks.
summary: Build, maintain, and apply cost models using standard tools and frameworks

label: Cost model design and best practices
content: Design cost models following best practices: color-coding convention (blue=input, black=formula, green=reference), named ranges for traceability, conditional formatting for validation, modular structure separating inputs/calculations/outputs. Avoid embedded macros and complex nested formulas that impair auditability. Maintain version control and change log. Link model structure to WBS/CES. Support multiple estimate scenarios (optimistic/baseline/pessimistic). Enable sensitivity analysis through parameterized inputs. Document all formulas and data sources within the model.
summary: Design cost models with proper structure, documentation, and audit trail

label: Phasing and spreading functions
content: Apply cost phasing methods to distribute point estimates across fiscal years: Uniform (equal distribution), Trapezoid (ramp-up, steady-state, ramp-down), Beta distribution (flexible shape via alpha/beta parameters), Rayleigh curve (front-loaded for development activities), Weibull distribution (flexible skewness). Apply Cost Informed by Schedule Method (CISM) linking cost phasing to schedule logic. Use dynamic phasing adjusting to schedule changes. Convert between obligation and expenditure phasing. Maintain consistency between phased estimate and IMS milestones.
summary: Apply time-phasing methods to distribute costs across fiscal years

label: Cost metrics framework and standard definitions
content: Apply standard cost metric hierarchy: Flyaway/Sailaway/Rollaway cost (unit recurring production), Weapon System Cost (+ nonrecurring production), Procurement Cost (+ initial spares and support), Program Acquisition Cost (+ RDT&E), Life-Cycle Cost (+ O&S), Total Ownership Cost (+ infrastructure and indirect support). Report unit metrics: Average Unit Procurement Cost (APUC), Program Acquisition Unit Cost (PAUC), Average Unit Manufacturing Cost (AUMC). Track metrics against Nunn-McCurdy thresholds and Selected Acquisition Report baselines.
summary: Apply DoD/NASA standard cost metrics for program cost reporting

label: WBS and Cost Element Structure alignment
content: Align Program WBS (per MIL-STD-881E) with Cost Element Structure (CES) for consistent cost accumulation and reporting. Map WBS product elements to CES cost categories: direct labor, direct material, subcontract, other direct, overhead, G&A. Develop WBS dictionary defining scope boundaries for each element. Extend WBS to contract level (CWBS) for contractor cost reporting. Apply NASA standard WBS for space flight projects. Ensure WBS supports EVM control account structure, CSDR reporting, and financial system cost accumulation. Reference DoD CEG WBS/CES examples (Appendix E) for aircraft, ground vehicle, missile, ship, electronics, and space systems.
summary: Align Work Breakdown Structure with Cost Element Structure for estimating

label: Economic analysis and trade studies
content: Apply economic analysis methods for investment decisions including net present value analysis, make-vs-buy determinations, and cost-based trade studies supporting acquisition strategy.
summary: Conduct economic analyses and cost-based trade studies for program decisions

label: Net present value and discounted cash flow analysis
content: Apply Net Present Value (NPV) analysis for comparing investment alternatives: discount future costs and benefits to present value using OMB Circular A-94 real discount rates. Calculate NPV = sum of [Cash Flow_t / (1+r)^t] for each year. Use Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC) for comparing alternatives with different lifespans. Apply Savings-to-Investment Ratio (SIR) as secondary metric. Distinguish between real and nominal discount rates. Address sunk cost exclusion, opportunity cost inclusion, and terminal value estimation. Present results with sensitivity to discount rate assumptions.
summary: Apply NPV and DCF methods for investment decision evaluation

label: Make-vs-buy and lease-vs-buy analysis
content: Conduct make-vs-buy analysis comparing in-house development/production costs against procurement: include all relevant costs (direct, indirect, facilities, opportunity costs) for both options. Assess non-cost factors: schedule risk, technical control, workforce availability, industrial base health, intellectual property. Conduct lease-vs-buy analysis per OMB Circular A-94: compare NPV of lease payments against purchase price plus maintenance minus residual value. Document decision rationale including quantitative and qualitative factors.
summary: Conduct sourcing and acquisition mode trade studies

label: What-if analysis and scenario-based cost assessment
content: Conduct what-if analysis varying multiple parameters simultaneously to evaluate alternative program scenarios: quantity changes, schedule acceleration/deceleration, technology insertion, requirements relaxation, contractor base changes. Develop scenario matrix with cost results for each combination. Compare against baseline estimate to quantify decision impacts. Support Analysis of Alternatives (AoA) with cost estimates for each alternative. Present scenario results using Cost Element Charts comparing alternatives across WBS elements. Document scenario assumptions and limitations.
summary: Evaluate cost impacts of alternative program scenarios

label: OMB Circular A-94 compliance for economic analysis
content: Apply OMB Circular A-94 requirements for benefit-cost and cost-effectiveness analysis: use real discount rates published annually by OMB for public investment and regulatory analysis. Distinguish between cost-effectiveness analysis (comparing alternatives achieving same objective) and benefit-cost analysis (monetizing benefits for comparison against costs). Apply 7% real rate for internal federal investments, 3% for public benefit analysis (or current OMB-published rates). Address time-value-of-money, inflation adjustment, risk-adjusted returns, and distributional analysis. Document compliance with OMB and agency-specific economic analysis guidance.
summary: Apply federal economic analysis requirements per OMB guidance

label: Executive governance council operations
content: Three-council governance structure for enterprise decision-making. Senior Management Council (SMC): chaired by CEO, includes all C-suite and VP-level leaders; reviews strategic planning, enterprise resource allocation, program portfolio, and institutional management; meets quarterly minimum; makes enterprise-level decisions on programs, budgets, and workforce direction. Program Management Council (PMC): reviews and approves program/project commitments at formal Key Decision Points (KDPs — concept, preliminary design, critical design, production readiness); ensures programs align with strategic goals, stay within cost/schedule bounds, and manage technical risk; issues Program Decision Memoranda (PDMs) at each gate. Capital Investment Council (CIC): oversees major capital investments including equipment (>$50K), facilities (>$25K modifications), and IT systems (>$100K lifecycle cost); requires business case with life-cycle cost analysis, alternatives comparison, and ROI justification before approval. Governance principle: decisions escalate to the lowest council with sufficient authority — operational issues stay in ops reviews, program issues go to PMC, enterprise issues go to SMC.
summary: Task: Three-council governance structure (SMC, PMC, CIC) for strategic, program, and capital investment decision-making

label: Enterprise risk governance across three tiers
content: Three-tier risk governance framework adapted from NASA NPG 1000.2. Tier 1 — Enterprise (strategic): risks managed at CEO/SMC level — market shifts, regulatory changes, major customer loss, reputational risk, program termination risk; reviewed quarterly in SMC; reported to board. Tier 2 — Program/project (execution): risks managed at VP/PM level — cost overruns, schedule slip, technical failures, supplier defaults, workforce gaps on active programs; reviewed monthly at PMC and in program reviews; escalated to Tier 1 when P×I score exceeds threshold (≥15 on 5×5 matrix). Tier 3 — Operational (day-to-day): risks managed at department/shop floor level — equipment failures, quality escapes, single-shift personnel gaps, immediate safety hazards; reviewed weekly in ops review; escalated to Tier 2 when unresolved for >2 weeks or when Tier 0 program impact is likely. Risk ownership is explicit at each tier; CEO owns Tier 1 register, VPs own Tier 2 program risk registers, department managers own Tier 3. Cross-tier risk rollup: top 5 Tier 2 risks reported upward to Tier 1 register quarterly.
summary: Task: Three-tier risk governance (enterprise/program/operational) with escalation thresholds, ownership, and review cadence

label: Leader development continuum and succession planning (DoDI 1430.16)
content: DoDI 1430.16 leader development continuum applied enterprise-wide across all personnel levels. Framework: five Fundamental Competencies serve as baseline for every employee; four progressive tiers (Lead Self → Lead Teams/Projects → Lead Organizations/Programs → Lead the Institution) add competencies commensurate with scope of authority. See child neurons for tier-specific competency standards and formal program structures. Development methods: rotational cross-functional assignments (min 90 days), senior leader shadowing, action learning projects on real business problems, external DoD formal programs (DCELP, ELDP, DSLDP, WHLDP) for eligible civilian equivalents, executive coaching. Succession planning: maintain documented backfill candidate with readiness rating (ready now / ready in 12 months / developmental) for all VP+ roles; review annually at SMC. Development candidates must demonstrate cross-functional experience, evidence of developing others, and strategic thinking beyond current role scope.
summary: Task: DoDI 1430.16 four-tier competency continuum (Lead Self → Lead Institution) with formal development programs (DCELP/ELDP/DSLDP/WHLDP) and succession planning for defense manufacturing

label: Defense customer and stakeholder relationship management
content: Executive-level stakeholder engagement for defense/aerospace manufacturing context. Key stakeholder tiers and engagement cadence: (1) Government program offices and contracting officers — annual strategic alignment meetings, quarterly formal program reviews (per CDRL schedule), monthly operational touchpoints with PMs; CEO/VP-level attendance signals commitment on critical programs. (2) Prime contractors and teaming partners — strategic relationship management covering workshare agreements, IP boundaries, integration requirements, and long-term pipeline; VP BD leads day-to-day, CEO engages at strategic inflection points (new contract awards, major disputes, teaming pivots). (3) Board of directors — quarterly financial and strategic performance reporting; annual strategic plan update; immediate notification for material adverse events per governance charter. (4) Regulatory and oversight bodies — DCAA (annual audit cooperation), DCMA (quality system surveillance, CPSR), FAA/EASA (if applicable), AS9100 registrar (annual surveillance audit), cybersecurity assessors (CMMC if applicable). (5) Congressional and legislative liaison — relevant for companies pursuing SBIR/STTR, Other Transaction Authority contracts, or legislative program protection. Stakeholder risk: loss of senior relationship at customer program office is a Tier 2 risk requiring documented relationship continuity plan.
summary: Task: Executive stakeholder engagement across government customers, primes, board, and regulatory bodies with cadence and escalation protocols

label: Enterprise performance management and balanced scorecard
content: Balanced scorecard performance management system adapted from NASA NPG 1000.2 for defense/aerospace manufacturing. Four scorecard perspectives: (1) Financial — revenue growth, operating margin, contract award rate, indirect cost rate vs. target, cash flow; (2) Customer — on-time delivery (OTD), first-article acceptance rate, customer satisfaction scores, repeat award rate; (3) Internal process — manufacturing cycle efficiency, first-pass yield, schedule performance index (SPI), cost performance index (CPI) across programs; (4) Learning and growth — employee retention rate, cross-training completion, cleared personnel pipeline, succession readiness. Leading indicators (predictive): EVM schedule variance trends, quality escape rate, backlog coverage, proposal win rate. Lagging indicators (results): revenue recognized, OTD actuals, audit findings, contract modifications. Review cadence: weekly ops review (Tier 3 operational indicators), monthly program reviews (Tier 2 program CPI/SPI), quarterly SMC review (Tier 1 strategic scorecard). Threshold triggers: any strategic KPI in red for two consecutive quarters requires formal corrective action plan presented at SMC. COO owns scorecard maintenance and dashboard integrity.
summary: Task: Balanced scorecard with leading/lagging indicators across financial, customer, process, and growth perspectives with tiered review cadence

label: Capital investment governance and life-cycle stewardship
content: Capital Investment Council (CIC) process for major asset decisions. Approval thresholds: equipment purchases >$50K, facility modifications >$25K, IT/software systems with multi-year lifecycle cost >$100K require formal CIC business case. Business case required elements: (1) Life-cycle cost analysis (acquisition, installation, operating, maintenance, disposal/salvage); (2) Alternatives analysis — minimum two alternatives to preferred option with cost/benefit comparison; (3) ROI calculation with payback period (target ≤4 years for non-strategic investments); (4) Strategic alignment statement — how investment supports current program portfolio or positions for future opportunities; (5) Risk assessment — probability of cost overrun, technology obsolescence risk, vendor dependency risk. Post-approval tracking: CFO tracks actual vs. projected ROI annually for 3 years post-investment; investments not achieving 80% of projected ROI trigger review and lessons-learned documentation. Emergency capital authority: Plant Manager may approve up to $10K for critical equipment repair to prevent Tier 0 production stoppage; must ratify through CIC within 30 days. Annual capital budget reserve: retain 20-30% of capital budget unallocated through Q2 for contingency and mid-year strategic opportunities.
summary: Task: CIC-equivalent capital investment governance with business case requirements, approval thresholds, and post-investment ROI tracking

label: Defense acquisition lifecycle alignment and joint warfighter perspective
content: VP Engineering responsibility for aligning internal engineering practices with DoD acquisition lifecycle milestones and joint warfighter operational requirements. Key alignment points: (1) Pre-Milestone A (concept refinement) — participate in customer requirements development, draft concept of operations (CONOPS) input, assess technical feasibility; (2) Milestone B (development start) — ensure internal KDP-B (PDR) aligns with government developmental testing schedule; (3) Milestone C (production decision) — internal KDP-D (PRR) must precede government LRIP decision; provide production readiness evidence. Warfighter perspective requirements for defense manufacturing: understand operational use environment (temperature, vibration, humidity, EMI), maintenance concept (field vs. depot repair), reliability targets (MTBF, mission availability), and interoperability requirements (MIL-STD-1553, MIL-STD-461). Technical data package (TDP) management: maintain TDP at level of detail sufficient for competitive re-procurement; identify and protect proprietary data markings per DFARS 252.227. Systems engineering integration: maintain bidirectional traceability from customer requirements through design to test (per MIL-STD-31000A); conduct formal interface control document (ICD) reviews at each KDP.
summary: Task: Aligning internal engineering KDPs with DoD acquisition milestones, maintaining warfighter operational perspective, and managing technical data packages

label: Strategic planning cascade and hierarchy
content: Hierarchical strategic planning cascade adapted from NASA NPG 1000.2 for defense/aerospace manufacturing. Level 1 — Company Strategic Plan (3-5 year): CEO/SMC sets enterprise vision, mission, strategic goals, and priority market segments; updated annually; approved by board. Level 2 — Division/Program Directorate Strategies (2-3 year): each VP translates strategic goals into division-specific plans covering program portfolio targets, workforce development, capital investment priorities, and technology roadmap contributions; must explicitly trace to Level 1 goals. Level 3 — Functional Implementation Plans (1-2 year): department managers develop functional plans addressing their contribution to divisional strategies — engineering capability builds, operational efficiency targets, financial performance commitments; reviewed semiannually. Level 4 — Program/Project Plans (contract period): program managers develop integrated master plans (IMPs) and integrated master schedules (IMSs) implementing functional commitments at program level. Cascade integrity: each lower-level plan must cite the parent-level strategic objective it supports; gaps identified in cascade reviews are escalated as planning deficiencies. Annual planning cycle: strategic planning kickoff (Q3) → division plan submissions (Q4) → SMC review and approval (Q1) → cascade to functional/program plans (Q2).
summary: System: Four-level strategic planning cascade (company → division → functional → program) with traceability requirements and annual cycle

label: Enterprise resource stewardship framework
content: Cross-cutting enterprise resource management standards ensuring consistent stewardship across all departments. Human capital stewardship: CEO/SMC approves headcount changes >5% of total workforce; VP-level approves individual backfill and new hires within approved headcount ceiling; workforce planning reviewed quarterly against program demand forecast. Financial stewardship: budget authority matrix — department managers control within approved budget, VPs approve transfers up to 10% between line items, CFO approves transfers >10%, CEO/SMC approves reallocation across divisions. Facility and equipment stewardship: VP Operations is responsible officer for all production assets; annual physical inventory reconciliation; assets >$5K tracked in fixed asset register with assigned custodian. IT and data stewardship: CTO governs enterprise IT investments, cybersecurity posture, and data classification; ITAR-controlled data requires access control lists approved by Export Compliance Officer. Indirect cost stewardship: CFO reviews indirect cost pool spending monthly; pools deviating >5% from plan trigger mandatory explanation and corrective action. Enterprise consistency: all departments follow same resource request, approval, and tracking procedures — stewardship standards are not optional for individual program expediency.
summary: System: Enterprise resource stewardship standards covering human capital, financial, facility, IT, and indirect cost governance with authority matrix

label: KDP program gate review system
content: Formal Key Decision Point (KDP) gate review process for program lifecycle milestones. Gate sequence: KDP-A (Mission Need / Contract Award): COO/CEO confirm capacity, workforce, and facility availability before contractual commitment; no program starts without resource confirmation. KDP-B (Preliminary Design Review equivalent): PMC reviews requirements baseline, top-level design approach, and preliminary cost/schedule estimate; approval criterion — cost estimate confidence ±30%, schedule has ≥15% margin. KDP-C (Critical Design Review equivalent): PMC reviews design maturity (drawings released ≥90%), manufacturing readiness (MRL ≥6), and updated cost-to-complete; approval criterion — TRL ≥6, no open safety waivers, supplier qualifications confirmed. KDP-D (Production Readiness Review equivalent): CEO/PMC confirms LRIP readiness — first article inspection (FAI) complete, Ppk ≥1.33 on critical characteristics, workforce trained, supply chain certified. Decision options at each gate: Approve (proceed), Conditional Approval (proceed with mandatory actions, re-review within 30 days), Redirect (return to prior phase with documented deficiency list), or Termination (CEO authority only). All gate decisions documented in Program Decision Memorandum (PDM) with rationale and action items.
summary: System: KDP gate review process at four program lifecycle milestones with approval criteria, decision options, and Program Decision Memoranda

label: Cross-organizational collaboration and integration framework
content: Structured collaboration patterns for cross-functional, cross-organizational, and interagency coordination in defense/aerospace manufacturing. Internal cross-functional integration: Integrated Product Teams (IPTs) formed at program start — include engineering, manufacturing, quality, supply chain, and contracts representatives; IPT lead reports to PM; COO chairs IPT of IPTs for multi-program resource conflicts. Prime contractor integration: establish joint working groups with prime for technical interface management (ICD reviews, change notification, data exchange); schedule prime-sub program management reviews monthly; escalate unresolved interface issues to VP level within 5 business days. Government customer integration: designate a single point of contact (SPOC) for each government program office; all formal government correspondence routes through contracts; VP/CEO engagement for Cure Notices, Show Cause letters, or contract modification disputes. Interagency and teaming partner coordination: for multi-company teams (JVs, CRADAs, consortiums), establish master teaming agreement with workshare, IP, and dispute resolution clauses before program start; VP BD owns teaming partner relationships. Conflict resolution escalation: cross-functional → COO (72 hr); cross-organizational → CEO (1 week); contractual dispute → Legal/CEO (per dispute resolution clause). Documentation: decisions from cross-org coordination meetings logged in action item tracker; open items >30 days escalate to next management level.
summary: System: Cross-functional IPT structure, prime-sub integration protocols, government customer coordination, and conflict escalation procedures

label: Leading Change — DoDI 1430.16 Core Qualification 1
content:
DoD Civilian Leader Competency: Leading Change (DoDI 1430.16 CQ1). Applied to defense aerospace manufacturing.

INNOVATION: Champion experimentation in manufacturing processes — authorize pilot runs of additive manufacturing, automated inspection, or digital twin integration before full production insertion. Require calculated risk assessments (cost of failure vs. competitive advantage) before IRAD investment. Reward technical staff who surface process improvements even when pilots fail. Avoid punishing well-reasoned experiments with negative outcomes.

STRATEGIC THINKING: Formulate 3-5 year objectives that account for DoD budget cycles (FYDP), evolving platform requirements (next-gen platforms replacing legacy), and emerging threats driving new capability needs. Continuously evaluate internal resources (cleared workforce, special process certifications, facility constraints) against market opportunities. Manage risks of strategic pivots — entering new NAICS codes, building MMIC or RF capability — with explicit go/no-go criteria before committing capital.

VISION: Articulate a long-term competitive position (e.g., becoming a trusted partner in multi-domain systems integration or affordable autonomy for Group III/IV UAS). Translate vision into observable near-term milestones so workforce understands how today's prototype work connects to the company's 10-year trajectory. Inspire commitment by connecting individual roles to warfighter impact and national security mission.
summary: DoDI 1430.16 CQ1: Leading Change — innovation, strategic thinking, and vision applied to defense aerospace manufacturing; covering IRAD investment discipline, FYDP-aligned strategic planning, and workforce-inspiring mission connection

label: Leading People — DoDI 1430.16 Core Qualification 2
content:
DoD Civilian Leader Competency: Leading People (DoDI 1430.16 CQ2). Applied to defense aerospace manufacturing with multi-sector workforce (civil service, military liaisons, cleared contractors, subcontractors).

MANAGING CONFLICT: Resolve discord arising from competing program priorities — engineering vs. manufacturing schedule pressure, cleared vs. uncleared personnel access restrictions, cost-type vs. fixed-price contract incentive misalignment. Facilitate dialogue early before conflict escalates to customer-visible schedule impact. Distinguish personality conflicts from structural resource scarcity issues; address root cause.

DEVELOPING OTHERS: Establish mentoring relationships for high-potential engineers pursuing systems engineering or program management career tracks. Coach direct reports on DCAA/DCMA interaction, customer relationship management, and government source selection dynamics — skills not taught in academic settings. Create rotational assignments across engineering, operations, and contracts to build enterprise perspective in future leaders.

TEAM BUILDING: Build trust and unity across functional silos (engineering, manufacturing, quality, supply chain, contracts) that naturally compete for resources on multi-program LRIP floors. Establish shared accountability for program outcomes rather than functional performance — IPT structure with shared metrics. Recognize collaborative behaviors that sacrifice functional optimization for program success.

COMMUNICATION: Articulate company strategy through multiple channels — all-hands briefings, program-level town halls, written strategic direction memos (see L5 output), and informal walkthroughs on the production floor. Tailor message to audience: warfighter impact for technicians, financial performance for board, technical risk posture for government PMs. Use storytelling to make abstract strategy concrete.

INTERPERSONAL SKILLS: Apply emotional intelligence in high-pressure customer interactions (Cure Notices, DCMA surveillance findings, source selection debriefs). Demonstrate tact when delivering program performance bad news to government program offices. Build empathy with cleared workforce facing security-driven constraints (SCIF access, foreign travel reporting, cleared hiring delays).
summary: DoDI 1430.16 CQ2: Leading People — conflict management, mentoring, IPT team building, multi-audience communication, and emotional intelligence for multi-sector defense/aerospace workforce

label: Results Driven — DoDI 1430.16 Core Qualification 3
content:
DoD Civilian Leader Competency: Results Driven (DoDI 1430.16 CQ3). Applied to defense aerospace manufacturing with government contracting accountability requirements.

ACCOUNTABILITY: Hold self and leadership team accountable for cost, schedule, and technical performance commitments made in contract baseline and program plans. Public trust dimension is real — cost-plus contracts spend taxpayer funds; DCAA audit findings and DCMA quality escapes damage institutional reputation and can trigger increased government surveillance or contract termination. Establish internal accountability through EVM (CPI/SPI trending) and balanced scorecard with visible dashboards, not just quarterly financial reviews.

DECISIVENESS: Make timely, data-driven decisions at program KDPs — approve or redirect programs based on actual performance data, not optimism bias. Apply sound judgment on risk/impact tradeoffs: when to absorb a cost overrun internally vs. submit a REA, when to subcontract vs. invest in internal capability, when to recommend sole-source vs. competitive procurement to the government. Avoid analysis paralysis — decisions delayed past KDP windows have compounding schedule cost. Document decision rationale in PDMs for auditability.

PROBLEM SOLVING: Apply critical thinking to distinguish symptom from root cause — a schedule slip may reflect a procurement lead-time problem, a design maturity problem, or a workforce availability problem; the fix differs for each. Evaluate alternatives before committing: for schedule recovery, compare overtime authorization vs. subcontract vs. scope reduction vs. contract modification. Assess unintended consequences — authorizing schedule crunch overtime may resolve near-term slip while causing quality escapes or attrition that create larger downstream problems.
summary: DoDI 1430.16 CQ3: Results Driven — public trust accountability on government contracts, data-driven KDP decisiveness, and structured problem solving with root-cause and consequence analysis

label: Business Acumen — DoDI 1430.16 Core Qualification 4
content:
DoD Civilian Leader Competency: Business Acumen (DoDI 1430.16 CQ4). Applied to defense aerospace manufacturing with government cost accounting and cleared workforce constraints.

FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT: Allocate and monitor resources across multiple cost-accounting structures — direct program charges (labor, materials, ODCs) and indirect pools (overhead, G&A, fringe) must align with CAS-compliant cost accounting practices. Maintain fiduciary responsibility through internal controls: segregation of duties, purchase order approval thresholds, and timekeeping system integrity (unallowable cost classification). Monitor cash flow against milestone billing schedule — LRIP programs with irregular delivery cadence create cash flow valleys that require line-of-credit management.

HUMAN CAPITAL MANAGEMENT: Manage workforce planning complexity driven by security clearance pipeline delays (6-24 months), ITAR nationality restrictions, and cleared personnel attrition risk. Maintain succession readiness ratings (ready-now / 12-month / developmental) for all VP+ roles and critical cleared positions. Develop multi-sector workforce strategy that optimizes mix of organic staff, cleared contractors, and subcontractors against cost structure, program surge flexibility, and knowledge retention requirements.

DIGITAL FLUENCY: Leverage ERP (financial/program cost), MES (production actuals), and EVM systems to generate data-driven decisions — not intuition. Anticipate technology implications: digital thread from design to manufacturing eliminates costly drawing-change rework; CMMC compliance for CUI handling may gate future contract eligibility; model-based systems engineering (MBSE) adoption affects engineering headcount and tool investment requirements. Evaluate digital investment proposals against concrete operational impact, not technology novelty.
summary: DoDI 1430.16 CQ4: Business Acumen — CAS/FAR-compliant financial stewardship, cleared workforce succession planning, and data-driven digital fluency for defense/aerospace manufacturing decisions

label: Building Coalitions — DoDI 1430.16 Core Qualification 5
content:
DoD Civilian Leader Competency: Building Coalitions (DoDI 1430.16 CQ5). Applied to internal/external coalition building in the defense industrial base.

POLITICAL SAVVY: Assess the political landscape of government acquisition programs — understand which Congressional delegations champion relevant programs, which PEOs are expanding vs. contracting, and how continuing resolution budgetary uncertainty affects program start authority. Navigate internal politics of prime-sub relationships (workshare disputes, IP boundaries, competitive sensitivities). Promote trust with government customers by being transparent about program problems early rather than surprises late — contracting officers and PMs reward honest partners.

INFLUENCING: Persuade and negotiate in high-stakes environments: source selection debriefs where government evaluators must be influenced to reconsider scoring, teaming negotiations where workshare percentages shape long-term competitive position, and internal budget deliberations where program advocates compete for finite capital. Manage conflicting interests — a teaming partner's desire to maximize their workshare may conflict with your company's capability development strategy; resolve through negotiated agreements that create mutual incentive alignment.

PARTNERING: Build strategic alliances across the defense industrial base — with prime contractors for sub-tier positions, with peer small businesses for complementary capability teaming on large IDIQ vehicles, with universities and FFRDCs for technology development partnerships, and with DCMA/DCAA for cooperative compliance relationships rather than adversarial surveillance dynamics. Pursue formal mechanisms: CRADAs, OTAs, teaming agreements, mentor-protégé arrangements (SBA or DoD programs). Strategic relationships should be built before they are needed — not initiated after a requirement is identified.
summary: DoDI 1430.16 CQ5: Building Coalitions — political savvy with government acquisition stakeholders, principled negotiation in defense contracting, and proactive strategic partnering across the defense industrial base

label: Enterprise-Wide Perspective — DoDI 1430.16 Core Qualification 6
content:
DoD Civilian Leader Competency: Enterprise-Wide Perspective (DoDI 1430.16 CQ6). Applied to synthesizing broad views with mission and National Security priorities in defense aerospace manufacturing.

EXTERNAL AWARENESS: Monitor defense acquisition environment continuously — DoD budget requests, FYDP program shifts, National Defense Strategy updates, NDAA provisions affecting small business or ITAR regulations, and geopolitical events driving urgent capability requirements. Understand cultural and international considerations: allied nation requirements for FMS programs, ITAR/EAR export licensing implications for international partners, and foreign ownership/control/influence (FOCI) risks from foreign investment in the domestic supply chain. Track emerging threats (hypersonics, autonomous systems, directed energy) that will reshape platform development priorities and thus contract opportunities.

SYSTEMS THINKING: Understand how the company's work fits within the broader defense acquisition enterprise — from National Security Strategy to NDS to Service acquisition strategies to specific program requirements to sub-tier component manufacturing. Recognize that internal operational decisions (make-buy, IRAD investment, workforce skill mix) create second-order effects across the enterprise: a decision to exit a special process capability may eliminate eligibility for an entire class of future contracts. Identify emerging patterns in the defense industrial base — consolidation trends, supply chain fragility in critical materials (rare earths, specialty alloys), and workforce pipeline gaps in cleared engineers — before they become acute constraints. Drive integration across internal functions (engineering, manufacturing, quality, supply chain) so the enterprise operates as a coherent whole rather than optimized silos producing locally rational but globally suboptimal outcomes.
summary: DoDI 1430.16 CQ6: Enterprise-Wide Perspective — external awareness of NDS/FYDP/ITAR environment, systems thinking across the defense acquisition enterprise, and integration of internal functions toward national security mission outcomes

label: Fundamental Leadership Competencies — DoDI 1430.16 Baseline
content: Five competencies required of all personnel at every organizational level per DoDI 1430.16. These underpin all four progressive tiers and are assessed in hiring, performance reviews, and promotion decisions. (1) Agility: adapt effectively to changing priorities, ambiguous requirements, and evolving defense program demands; remain productive under uncertainty; adjust approach when initial plans prove unworkable — critical in prototype and LRIP environments where engineering changes and schedule rebaselines are routine. (2) Resilience: maintain composure and effectiveness under pressure (concurrent program demands, audit stress, delivery crises); recover from setbacks without loss of commitment; extract lessons from failures rather than assigning blame. (3) Ethical Behavior: demonstrate honesty, fairness, and integrity in all actions; comply with government contracting ethics requirements (FAR 3.1, business ethics clauses); actively cultivate an organizational culture where ethical concerns are raised without fear of reprisal — essential for DCAA audit integrity and government customer trust. (4) Lifelong Learning: pursue continuous self-development beyond formal training; stay current on regulatory changes (FAR/DFARS, AS9100, CMMC), technical advances, and defense acquisition policy; expand competencies beyond current role requirements. (5) Service Motivated: serve customers, teammates, and mission with honor; align personal actions with customer and public needs; create trust and value through reliable, honest performance — foundational for long-term defense contracting relationships.
summary: Five DoDI 1430.16 fundamental competencies (Agility, Resilience, Ethical Behavior, Lifelong Learning, Service Motivated) required of all personnel as baseline leadership expectations

label: Lead Self — Tier 1 Competency Standards (All Personnel)
content: Competencies expected of all individual contributors and frontline technical leads per DoDI 1430.16 Lead Self tier. Builds on all five Fundamental Competencies. Additional Tier 1 competencies: Interpersonal Skills — build productive working relationships across engineering, manufacturing, quality, and contracts functions; manage professional relationships with government customer personnel and prime contractor contacts; navigate cultural and hierarchical differences. Problem Solving — identify root causes rather than symptoms; generate actionable solutions with tradeoff analysis; make sound judgments with incomplete information (common in prototype environments). Digital Fluency — leverage ERP, MES, PLM, and project management tools proficiently; maintain data literacy sufficient to interpret performance dashboards; understand cybersecurity hygiene requirements relevant to ITAR/CUI data handling. Communication — communicate clearly in written and verbal forms; listen actively; tailor communication to audience (technician, engineer, government customer); produce accurate work instructions, trip reports, and status updates. Assessment: annual performance review includes self-assessment against Tier 1 competencies; supervisor rates proficiency (developing / proficient / exemplary); development gaps trigger individual development plan (IDP) action items.
summary: Lead Self tier competencies for all individual contributors: Fundamental Competencies plus Interpersonal Skills, Problem Solving, Digital Fluency, and Communication

label: Lead Teams/Projects — Tier 2 Competency Standards (Supervisors)
content: Competencies required of all supervisors and project leads per DoDI 1430.16 Lead Teams/Projects tier. Includes all Tier 1 (Lead Self) competencies plus: Team Building — foster team cohesion across multi-disciplinary and multi-clearance-level groups; leverage diverse technical strengths; build psychological safety so quality escapes and near-misses are reported honestly; coordinate across program cells sharing equipment. Accountability — hold self and direct reports to schedule, quality, and contractual commitments; take ownership of team outcomes without deflecting to supply chain or engineering; document and close corrective actions rather than allowing recurrence. Decisiveness — make timely decisions at the point of knowledge with available data; avoid decision paralysis on routine program calls; escalate to VP level only issues genuinely requiring senior authority; accept responsibility for decisions made. Influencing — persuade and motivate technicians, engineers, and peers without relying solely on positional authority; build credibility through technical competence and follow-through; align team behavior with AS9100 quality culture and ethics requirements. Defense manufacturing context: supervisors must be capable of briefing government DCMA/DCAA representatives during surveillance visits and leading first-article inspection (FAI) teams without VP presence.
summary: Lead Teams tier competencies for supervisors: all Tier 1 plus Team Building, Accountability, Decisiveness, and Influencing

label: Lead Organizations/Programs — Tier 3 Competency Standards (Directors/VPs)
content: Competencies required of program managers, directors, and VPs per DoDI 1430.16 Lead Organizations/Programs tier. Includes all Tier 2 (Lead Teams) competencies plus: Financial Management — steward program and departmental budgets; understand government cost accounting requirements (FAR 31.2, CAS); manage earned value (CPI/SPI trends); provide credible cost-to-complete estimates to customers and CFO. Innovation — create organizational conditions for process and product improvement; champion lean manufacturing, digital tooling adoption, and engineering efficiency initiatives; balance innovation investment against program execution risk. Partnering — build sustained coalitions with external organizations: prime contractors, government program offices, DCMA/DCAA, teaming partners, and industry associations; represent company credibly in joint IPT settings and source selection environments. Human Capital Management — own workforce planning for area of responsibility; manage recruiting, retention, and succession for key technical and leadership roles; ensure cleared personnel pipeline meets program requirements 12–18 months forward. Managing Conflict — diagnose and resolve organizational conflicts before they become schedule impacts; mediate cross-functional disputes between engineering, quality, and manufacturing; escalate to COO/CEO only unresolvable structural conflicts. Developing Others — invest in subordinate professional growth; assign stretch assignments; provide candid performance feedback; build bench strength to ensure organizational resilience against attrition.
summary: Lead Organizations tier competencies for directors/VPs: all Tier 2 plus Financial Management, Innovation, Partnering, Human Capital Management, Managing Conflict, and Developing Others

label: Lead the Institution — Tier 4 Competency Standards (Senior Executives/C-Suite)
content: Competencies required of C-suite executives and senior leaders per DoDI 1430.16 Lead the Institution tier. Includes all Tier 3 (Lead Organizations) competencies plus: Vision — articulate a compelling, credible future state for the enterprise; translate national defense priorities and market trends into a company strategy that motivates the workforce and differentiates in competition; sustain vision through program volatility and budget cycles. External Awareness — continuously monitor regulatory, competitive, geopolitical, and technological environment; translate emerging changes (CMMC, ITAR reform, NDAA provisions, supply chain disruptions, autonomous systems trends) into proactive strategic adjustments before they become crises. Strategic Thinking — integrate information from financial, technical, program, and market sources into coherent long-range plans; identify second-order effects of decisions on programs, workforce, and customer relationships; sequence investments and capability builds against 3–5 year horizon. Political Savvy — navigate complex stakeholder environments including congressional staffers, OSD policy offices, prime contractor executives, and board members; understand informal power dynamics in defense acquisition; build alliances that protect program interests through budget cycles and administration transitions. Systems Thinking — view the enterprise as an interconnected system; recognize how changes in engineering processes, quality systems, supply chain health, or workforce composition propagate across programs and financial outcomes; avoid suboptimization decisions that solve one program's problem at enterprise expense. C-suite development requirement: maintain at least one active external engagement (advisory board, industry association, professional military education) to sustain external awareness and institutional perspective.
summary: Lead the Institution tier competencies for C-suite: all Tier 3 plus Vision, External Awareness, Strategic Thinking, Political Savvy, and Systems Thinking

label: DoD Formal Leader Development Programs — DCELP / ELDP / DSLDP / WHLDP
content: Four formal DoD civilian leader development programs applicable to defense manufacturing organizations with GS-equivalent civilian workforce or analogous private-sector equivalents. DCELP (DoD Civilian Emerging Leader Program) — GS-7 to GS-12 equivalent (early career supervisors, project leads, junior engineers): four 1-week seminars spaced over ~12 months covering self-awareness, communication, team building, and project management; develops Lead Self and Lead Teams competencies. Defense manufacturing adaptation: supplement with sessions on AS9100 quality management and FAR/DFARS government contracting fundamentals. ELDP (Enterprise Leader Development Program) — GS-12 to GS-14 equivalent (program managers, technical leads, department managers): 10-month program combining military installation visits (builds warfighter operational perspective), senior leader engagement (develops upward communication and executive presence), and experiential learning projects on real business problems; targets Lead Organizations competencies. Defense manufacturing adaptation: rotational assignments across engineering, quality, contracts, and production before nomination. DSLDP (Defense Senior Leader Development Program) — GS-14 to GS-15 equivalent (VPs, senior directors): 28-month journey including defense leadership seminars on enterprise-wide challenges, professional military education (senior service school) to build joint military perspective, and enterprise-wide perspective projects developing Systems Thinking and Strategic Thinking; targets Lead the Institution competencies. Defense manufacturing adaptation: VP-level candidates should complete a rotation with a prime contractor or DoD program office (even 60-day temporary duty) before or concurrent with DSLDP. WHLDP (White House Leadership Development Program) — GS-15 equivalent (senior executives): 1-year fellowship on multi-agency, high-impact national-level challenges; builds Political Savvy, External Awareness, and interagency collaboration. Private-sector analog for non-GS organizations: executive fellow programs at DoD labs (MIT Lincoln Laboratory, DARPA, DIU) or Council on Competitiveness / NDIA board service. Program nomination authority: CEO nominates for DSLDP/WHLDP; VP-level nominates for DCELP/ELDP with COO concurrence. Annual development investment target: one DCELP, one ELDP, and one DSLDP cohort slot per fiscal year where workforce size supports it.
summary: Four formal DoD leader development programs (DCELP GS-7–12, ELDP GS-12–14, DSLDP GS-14–15, WHLDP GS-15) with duration, focus, competency tier alignment, and defense manufacturing adaptations

label: Legal and contractual review before pursuing competitor prime
content: Before pursuing a prime's competitor as an alternate customer, conduct mandatory legal review: (1) SUBCONTRACT AGREEMENT: Review for exclusivity clauses, most-favored-customer provisions, or non-compete language that restricts selling to competitor primes. (2) OCI (ORGANIZATIONAL CONFLICT OF INTEREST): Assess whether access to proprietary design data, cost structure, or performance data from Prime A creates an unfair competitive advantage for Prime B's source selection — this can disqualify your bid or expose you to protest. (3) DATA RIGHTS: Identify government-furnished data, customer-furnished tooling, or design packages with restrictive markings; these cannot be used to support a competitor prime without authorization. (4) TEAMING AGREEMENTS: Check any existing teaming or strategic partnership agreements for restrictions on competing programs or customers. (5) ITAR/EAR: Confirm no technology transfer restrictions apply to sharing technical data with a new prime. Legal review is a go/no-go gate — complete before committing BD resources or making customer contact.
summary: Mandatory legal gate before pursuing a competitor prime: review subcontract exclusivity, OCI risk from data access, data rights restrictions, teaming agreement constraints, and ITAR/EAR applicability.

label: Executive framework for defense program concentration risk
content: WALK-AWAY CONDITIONS: Define before investing using relative, not absolute, thresholds. For cost reduction: 'If we cannot achieve target price with sustainable (not one-time) savings and a written volume commitment from the prime, we stop.' For legal review: walk-away threshold should be expressed as a function of recoverable revenue (e.g., 'legal complexity exceeds expected 3-year revenue at risk'), not a fixed dollar amount — the user must supply their own figures. Do not invent specific monetary thresholds. Without a written commitment, cost investment subsidizes the competitor.
summary: Executive decision framework for program concentration risk: portfolio logic across retention/diversification/new customer options, walk-away condition discipline, governance gates (legal review, board notification, capital thresholds), and decision sequencing before capital commitment.

label: Dual-sourcing competitive response: cost-reduction business case
content: When a prime dual-sources a sole-source subassembly, the cost-reduction business case has three required inputs before capital commitment: (1) CONFIRMED VOLUME — what share does the prime intend to award the incumbent if cost parity is achieved? (70/30 split vs. 50/50 dramatically changes ROI.) (2) PRICE TARGET — the prime's target unit price is the cost ceiling; if they haven't stated it, request it explicitly before any internal analysis. (3) WRITTEN COMMITMENT — verbal volume assurances are not bankable; require a memorandum of understanding or letter of intent before investing in cost reduction. Without all three inputs, cost-reduction investment risk is unquantifiable. COST GAP FRAMING: Express the required reduction as a percentage of current cost (e.g., 'we need a 12% cost reduction'), not as a dollar figure, until should-cost analysis is complete. Present to the board as a range with best/base/worst case, sourced from the should-cost model.
