summary: Structures the cost-reduction business case for a dual-sourcing competitive response: required inputs (confirmed volume, target price, written commitment) and framing guidance for executive and board presentation.

label: Strategic Problem Structuring (MECE / Issue Trees)
content: Break complex business problems into Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive (MECE) branches before analysis begins. Process: (1) Define the problem statement precisely — avoid conflating symptoms with root causes. (2) Build an issue tree decomposing the problem into independent, non-overlapping branches covering the full problem space. (3) Form hypotheses at each branch — structure drives hypothesis generation, not the reverse. (4) Assign workstreams by branch to prevent analytical redundancy across the team. (5) Prioritize branches by expected impact and resolvability before deep-diving. Application in defense/aerospace: structure program profitability problems (cost overrun root causes), bid/no-bid decisions (capability, past performance, competitive position, pricing), and organizational efficiency analyses as issue trees before executive briefings. All analyses presented to SMC or CEO should include a one-page issue tree framing the problem structure. Common failure mode: skipping structuring and jumping to data collection — produces redundant analysis and missed dimensions.
summary: Task: Structure complex executive problems using MECE issue trees — decompose into non-overlapping branches, form hypotheses, assign workstreams, and prevent analytical redundancy before deep analysis begins

label: Competitive Landscape Analysis (Porter Five Forces)
content: Assess industry structure and profit pressure origins using Porter's Five Forces framework. Five forces to evaluate: (1) Competitive Rivalry — number of competitors, product differentiation, switching costs, and growth rate of the defense/aerospace market segment; high rivalry erodes margin on fixed-price work. (2) Supplier Power — concentration of key suppliers (specialty alloys, MMIC foundries, radiation-hardened components), sole-source dependencies, and switching costs; high supplier power raises input costs and schedule risk. (3) Buyer Power — concentration of government customers, multi-award IDIQ vs. sole-source structures, and customer price sensitivity; DoD consolidation increases buyer leverage. (4) Threat of New Entrants — capital requirements, regulatory barriers (AS9100, ITAR, facility security), clearance requirements, and customer qualification cycles create high barriers but non-traditional defense entrants (commercial space, tech primes) are lowering them. (5) Threat of Substitutes — alternative technology approaches (additive manufacturing replacing traditional machining, software-defined capabilities replacing hardware), autonomous systems reducing platform unit counts. Strategic responses: differentiation (unique certifications, cleared workforce, IP), vertical integration (bring critical supplier capabilities in-house), or cost leadership (lean manufacturing, indirect rate discipline). Apply specifically to prime-sub dynamics: assess which primes are consolidating sub-tier work in-house vs. expanding workshare partnerships.
summary: Task: Apply Porter Five Forces to assess competitive pressure across rivalry, supplier power, buyer power, new entrants, and substitutes — identify profit pressure sources and recommend positioning responses for defense/aerospace market

label: Organizational Alignment Assessment (McKinsey 7S)
content: Evaluate internal organizational alignment using the McKinsey 7S framework — seven interdependent elements that must be coherent for strategy execution to succeed. Elements: (1) Strategy — articulated direction and competitive positioning. (2) Structure — reporting relationships, spans of control, and decision authority. (3) Systems — processes, ERP/MES/EVM tools, and operating procedures (QMS, AS9100). (4) Skills — workforce competencies, clearance levels, and specialized process certifications (NADCAP). (5) Staff — headcount composition, cleared personnel pipeline, and succession readiness. (6) Style — leadership culture, tone from the top, and management behavior patterns. (7) Shared Values — mission orientation, quality culture, and ethical standards. Diagnosis process: map current state of all seven elements; identify misalignments (e.g., Strategy targets LRIP production capability but Structure lacks a dedicated production engineering function; Systems lack MES integration while Strategy requires digital thread). Execution gaps are typically misalignment artifacts, not strategy failures. Use cases: reorganizations, M&A integration, culture change initiatives, or any time strategy execution is lagging despite adequate resources. Deliver findings as a 7S alignment heat map with red/yellow/green ratings and prioritized corrective actions.
summary: Task: Assess organizational alignment across McKinsey 7S elements (Strategy, Structure, Systems, Skills, Staff, Style, Shared Values) — diagnose execution gaps caused by misalignment during reorganizations, M&A integration, or culture change

label: Portfolio Strategy and Resource Allocation (BCG Matrix + GE-McKinsey 9-Box)
content: Classify and prioritize programs, products, and business units to drive investment, harvest, and divestment decisions. BCG Matrix (primary screen): plot each program/product on two axes — market growth rate (industry attractiveness) and relative market share (competitive strength). Stars (high growth, high share): invest to maintain leadership; likely next-gen platforms or emerging capability areas. Cash Cows (low growth, high share): harvest efficiently; fund Stars and Question Marks. Question Marks (high growth, low share): assess selectively — invest to win or exit; resource-intensive with uncertain return. Dogs (low growth, low share): divest or allow natural sunset; avoid resource commitment beyond contractual obligations. GE-McKinsey 9-Box (nuanced secondary evaluation): replaces single-variable axes with composite scores — Industry Attractiveness (market size, growth, competitive intensity, margin potential, technology complexity) and Competitive Strength (market share, program win rate, certifications, customer relationships, cost position). Nine cells map to Invest/Grow, Selective Investment, or Harvest/Divest strategies. Defense application: apply to program portfolio at annual strategic planning cycle — classify each active program and pipeline opportunity. Drive capital budget and BD investment allocation through portfolio analysis rather than reactive customer requests. Avoid portfolio imbalance: too many Question Marks with no Cash Cows creates cash flow risk; all Cash Cows with no Stars signals strategic decay.
summary: Task: Classify programs/products using BCG Matrix and GE-McKinsey 9-Box to drive portfolio investment, harvest, and divestment decisions — balance portfolio health across Stars, Cash Cows, Question Marks, and Dogs

label: Growth Strategy Development (Ansoff Matrix)
content: Map and evaluate four strategic growth pathways using the Ansoff Matrix, assessing risk-return tradeoffs for each. Four quadrants: (1) Market Penetration (existing products, existing markets) — lowest risk; increase share in current defense segments through competitive pricing, capture excellence, or expanded vehicle coverage on existing IDIQs; primary near-term growth lever. (2) Product Development (new products, existing markets) — moderate risk; develop adjacent capabilities (e.g., adding RF/EW subsystems to an EO/IR product line) for current defense customers; requires IRAD investment and TRL maturation. (3) Market Development (existing products, new markets) — moderate risk; enter new market segments with current capabilities (e.g., domestic defense → FMS/international, airborne → maritime platform applications, DoD → DHS or civil agency); requires new customer relationships and regulatory knowledge. (4) Diversification (new products, new markets) — highest risk; enter commercial aerospace, space, or dual-use markets with new capability development; requires capital, time, and tolerance for extended payback periods. Risk-return calibration: penetration strategies show return in 12-24 months; diversification requires 3-7 year horizon. Defense adjacencies guidance: move to adjacent markets only when penetration and selective product development are sufficiently funded; diversification requires CEO/board-level strategic commitment. Document Ansoff analysis in annual strategic plan with explicit go/no-go criteria and resource commitments for each chosen pathway.
summary: Task: Apply Ansoff Matrix to map four growth pathways (market penetration, product development, market development, diversification) — assess risk-return tradeoffs and guide defense adjacency and commercial diversification decisions

label: Macro-Environment Scanning (PESTEL Analysis)
content: Monitor and stress-test strategy against six macro-environmental dimensions using PESTEL. Dimensions and defense/aerospace-specific indicators: (1) Political — Congressional program champions, administration defense priorities, continuing resolution risks, NDAA provisions affecting small business set-asides or ITAR regulations, foreign policy drivers of allied FMS demand. (2) Economic — defense budget trajectory (FYDP submissions vs. enacted), inflation impact on fixed-price contract margins, interest rate effects on capital investment cost, industrial base consolidation trends. (3) Social — STEM workforce pipeline health, cleared personnel availability by region, veteran hiring programs, public support for defense spending. (4) Technological — autonomous systems maturation (Group III/IV UAS, LAWS), directed energy weapons programs, digital engineering mandates (MBSE, digital thread), additive manufacturing qualification standards, AI/ML integration in defense systems, CMMC cybersecurity requirements. (5) Environmental — DoD sustainability mandates, hazardous material regulatory tightening (chromate alternatives, solvent substitution), climate-driven operational environment changes affecting platform requirements. (6) Legal — DFARS cybersecurity clauses (CMMC 2.0 implementation timeline), ITAR/EAR reform, False Claims Act exposure, employment law changes affecting cleared workforce. Scanning cadence: quarterly PESTEL refresh for VP Strategy; annual comprehensive scenario analysis stress-testing 3-5 year strategic plan against pessimistic PESTEL combinations (e.g., defense budget sequestration + CMMC disqualification + key supplier exit). Findings feed CEO strategic planning inputs and board risk matrix.
summary: Task: Monitor Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, and Legal macro-environment factors — stress-test strategy under scenarios tracking defense budget trends, regulatory shifts, technology disruptions, and geopolitical risks

label: VP Strategy
content: VP Strategy in Executive Leadership. Responsible for enterprise strategic planning, competitive positioning, market analysis, and long-range growth investment prioritization. Partners with CEO/SMC to translate external market signals, defense budget trends, and internal capabilities into coherent multi-year strategy. Owns corporate strategy frameworks: Porter's Value Chain, Blue Ocean/ERRC, McKinsey Three Horizons, SWOT-to-Action, Balanced Scorecard, Three Cs (Company-Customer-Competitor), and data-driven causal analytics. Drives strategy cascade from enterprise vision to divisional plans. Interfaces with VP BD on market entry, CFO on investment thesis, and CTO on technology horizon planning.
summary: Role: VP Strategy in Executive Leadership — enterprise strategic planning, competitive positioning, and long-range investment prioritization for defense/aerospace manufacturing

label: Value Chain Analysis
content: Porter's Value Chain analysis adapted for defense/aerospace manufacturing to identify where margin is created or destroyed. Primary activities: (1) Inbound Logistics — supplier qualification, receiving inspection, material handling; margin risk from sole-source dependency, long lead items, and material price volatility. (2) Operations — machining, composite/metal assembly, NADCAP special processes (heat treat, NDT, chemical processing); margin made on proprietary process yields and lost on rework/scrap rates. (3) Outbound Logistics — government property handling, delivery packaging, export control compliance; margin impact from expediting cost and CDD compliance burden. (4) Marketing/Sales — proposal development, B&P spend, source selection; assess bid-to-win ratio and cost per award dollar. (5) After-sales Service — field support, warranty, sustainment contracts; identify margin exposure from open-ended performance guarantees. Support activities: Procurement (supplier LTAs, sole-source risk mitigation), Technology Development (IRAD ROI, digital tooling, automation), HR Management (cleared workforce pipeline, retention cost), Firm Infrastructure (indirect cost pools, DCAA-compliant accounting, ERP). Value diagnostics: high-margin nodes include proprietary NADCAP certifications, sole-source positions, and highly engineered subassemblies; margin leaks include high rework rates, B&P exceeding win rate returns, and over-specified procurement. Outsourcing candidates: commodity machining without special certification, non-ITAR subassemblies, routine testing lacking customer-required special process. Defense value chain map: raw material → supplier qualification → receiving inspection → special processes → machining/fabrication → assembly/integration → inspection/test → packaging/shipping → government acceptance → delivery.
summary: Task: Porter's Value Chain analysis for defense/aerospace manufacturing — mapping primary and support activities, identifying margin nodes, efficiency opportunities, and outsourcing candidates

label: Conduct value chain mapping workshop and margin gap analysis
content: Structured process for executing value chain analysis in defense manufacturing. Step 1 — Data Collection: pull direct labor cost by work center from ERP; collect scrap/rework rates by process from MES/quality system; compile B&P spend vs. awards from BD system; gather supplier spend data from procurement; calculate indirect cost burden by cost pool from accounting. Step 2 — Primary Activity Mapping: for each value chain stage, calculate cost incurred, value delivered to customer, and margin contribution; flag stages with cost above industry benchmark or yield below target. Typical defense sub-tier manufacturing benchmarks: direct labor efficiency >75%, first-pass yield >95% for machined parts, B&P spend <3% of annual revenue. Step 3 — Support Activity Assessment: evaluate contribution of procurement, HR, technology, and infrastructure to primary activity efficiency; identify support activities that are genuine capability enablers vs. internal overhead that could be outsourced or reduced. Step 4 — Margin Gap Identification: compare company margin per product line vs. defense industry benchmarks (typical sub-contractor gross margin 12–18%); trace margin gaps to specific value chain nodes using contribution margin analysis. Step 5 — Outsourcing Trade Analysis: for outsourcing candidates, compare fully-loaded internal cost vs. external market rate; include transaction cost (oversight burden, IP risk, quality assurance, regulatory compliance) in comparison; apply make-vs-buy framework per FAR 15.407-2. Step 6 — Output: value chain swimlane diagram with margin annotations, top-5 efficiency improvement priorities with cost/benefit estimate, outsourcing recommendation memo for CIC review, and annual re-assessment schedule.
summary: System: Value chain mapping workshop — data collection from ERP/MES/quality systems, margin gap analysis by activity, benchmarking, outsourcing trade study, and executive output

label: Blue Ocean Strategy and Market Creation
content: Blue Ocean Strategy application for defense/aerospace manufacturing to identify uncontested market space rather than competing in saturated prime-sub tiers. Framework — Strategy Canvas: map current industry value curves by plotting competitive factors (cost, delivery speed, qualification breadth, security clearance depth, specialized process certifications, engineering support, digital integration, customer co-investment) against offering level for company and top competitors; identify where the market converges (red ocean) and where divergence is possible. ERRC Grid (Eliminate-Reduce-Raise-Create): Eliminate — bureaucratic compliance overhead that doesn't add customer value; Reduce — cost in over-served areas (e.g., engineering documentation depth for rapid-prototype customers who want speed over formality); Raise — under-served attributes (e.g., speed from requirements to first article, digital data delivery, design-for-manufacturability co-engineering support); Create — new demand factors competitors don't offer (e.g., integrated IRAD co-investment arrangements, vertically integrated special processes under one roof, cleared rapid-prototyping with embedded security). Defense/aerospace application: identify uncontested niches — small UAS component manufacturing for non-traditional defense customers, digital MRO services for legacy platforms with no established supply chain, FMS integration support, hardware-software co-development for contested domains, or hypersonic test article manufacturing for emerging programs. Niche validation criteria: addressable contract value >$10M over 5 years, internal capability match >70%, no more than two established incumbents, reachable through existing customer relationships or SBIR/OTA pathway.
summary: Task: Blue Ocean Strategy and ERRC grid to identify uncontested defense/aerospace market niches — Strategy Canvas analysis, demand creation, and niche validation criteria

label: Facilitate Strategy Canvas and ERRC Grid workshop
content: Facilitation guide for Blue Ocean Strategy Canvas and ERRC Grid session applied to defense manufacturing. Preparation (1–2 weeks before): identify 6–10 competitive factors relevant to target defense market segment; gather competitive intelligence from BD pipeline win/loss debriefs, industry reports (NDIA, AIA, Avascent), FPDS-NG award data, and customer debrief feedback. Strategy Canvas Session (half-day): plot company and top 3 competitors on canvas against each factor (1–5 offering level scale); validate ratings with BD and customer-facing staff who have direct competitive insight; identify factors where all competitors converge (red ocean signals) and where divergence is feasible. ERRC Grid Session (half-day): for each factor, classify — Eliminate (factor adds cost but no customer value in target segment), Reduce (industry over-invests; scale back to cut cost), Raise (industry under-invests; invest to differentiate), Create (new factor competitors don't offer; adds value, creates new demand). Niche identification: from ERRC output, draft 2–3 candidate market creation hypotheses; validate each against (1) TAM estimate from FPDS-NG and FYDP, (2) internal capability readiness score, (3) time and cost to build competitive barrier. Output: annotated Strategy Canvas diagram, completed ERRC grid with rationale, and 1-page Blue Ocean Opportunity Brief per validated niche for VP BD and CEO review. Cadence: full Blue Ocean workshop annually; Strategy Canvas update semi-annually as competitive landscape shifts.
summary: System: Strategy Canvas and ERRC Grid workshop facilitation — preparation, canvas scoring, ERRC classification, niche validation, and output brief for executive review

label: Three Horizons Growth Planning
content: McKinsey Three Horizons framework adapted for defense/aerospace manufacturing portfolio management. H1 — Core Business Optimization (0–2 years): protect and extend existing defense programs; optimize manufacturing efficiency, improve OTD, reduce rework; secure re-competes and add-on awards on incumbent programs; target 5–8% annual revenue growth from core, improve operating margin 1–2 pts/year through lean and yield improvement initiatives. H2 — Emerging Capability Scale-Up (2–5 years): scale adjacent capabilities with validated customer demand; candidates include additive manufacturing for certified flight hardware, autonomous system component integration, directed energy sub-assemblies, software-defined avionics hardware support, and space domain structures; investment thesis requires demonstrated TRL ≥5 and at least one customer letter of interest or SBIR Phase II award; target H2 revenues representing 15–25% of total by year 5. H3 — Future Bets (5–10 years): seed early-stage opportunities in advanced materials (ceramic matrix composites, metamaterials), space domain manufacturing (hypersonic thermal protection, satellite structures), directed energy weapons subsystems, and AI-enabled autonomy hardware; H3 investment primarily via IRAD, SBIR Phase I/II, university CRADAs, and OTA exploratory agreements; maintain 3–5 active H3 bets at any time with annual portfolio review to graduate to H2 or terminate. Portfolio balance governance: present H1/H2/H3 investment split to board annually; healthy balance approximates 70/20/10 of innovation investment; overloading H1 at the expense of H2/H3 signals short-term optimization with no long-term competitive renewal — flag this as strategic risk to CEO and board.
summary: Task: McKinsey Three Horizons growth planning — H1 core optimization (0–2yr), H2 emerging capability scale-up (2–5yr), H3 future bets (5–10yr) with investment targets and board visibility for defense manufacturing

label: Maintain Three Horizons portfolio dashboard and annual board review
content: Operational system for managing Three Horizons growth portfolio in defense manufacturing. Portfolio register: maintain a live register of all active programs, capabilities, and investments classified by horizon (H1/H2/H3); each entry includes investment level ($), expected revenue contribution at year 3 and year 7, strategic rationale, milestone trigger for horizon graduation, and accountable VP owner. Horizon classification criteria: H1 — existing contract revenue or re-compete, established customer relationship, MRL ≥8, production-ready process; H2 — customer letter of interest, SBIR award, or OTA study, TRL 4–7, internal capability investment underway, revenue expected within 5 years; H3 — IRAD or academic partnership, TRL 1–4, no committed customer, requires 5+ year investment horizon before revenue. Annual Three Horizons board review: present investment split (target 70/20/10 by dollar of innovation/IRAD budget), active bets count by horizon, H2→H1 graduates in past year, H3→H2 graduates, terminated bets with lessons learned, and projected horizon mix at year 5. Mid-year check: VP Strategy reviews horizon register with CEO and CFO; adjust IRAD and B&P budget allocation if horizon balance is skewed. H2 investment gate: requires VP Strategy horizon classification approval, CIC approval for investments >$250K, and board notification if cumulative H2+H3 investment exceeds 30% of IRAD budget. Horizon graduation triggers: H3→H2 requires at least one external funding signal (SBIR Phase II award, customer-funded study, OTA award, or definitive teaming agreement); H2→H1 requires signed contract or fully executed teaming agreement with prime.
summary: System: Three Horizons portfolio dashboard — classification criteria, board review process, IRAD investment gates, and horizon graduation triggers for defense manufacturing

label: SWOT-to-Action Translation
content: Structured SWOT analysis with mandatory action intersection matrix for defense/aerospace manufacturing — move beyond listing to actionable strategy generation. SWOT Assessment (evidence-required; no unsubstantiated items): Strengths — confirmed internal capabilities: NADCAP certifications (specific scopes), cleared workforce depth by level, sole-source positions by program, proprietary special processes, EVM-certified program management, unique TDP ownership. Weaknesses — confirmed internal gaps: single-point-of-failure clearances with no backup, absence of additive manufacturing certification for flight hardware, high indirect cost rate vs. small-business competitors, limited BD bandwidth for large IDIQ capture. Opportunities — external favorable conditions: FYDP growth in specific platform domains, SBIR solicitations aligned to internal IRAD, prime contractor reshoring of sub-tier work creating new supplier slots, CMMC-naive competitors being disqualified from CUI-bearing contracts. Threats — external risks: continuing resolution budgets delaying program start authority, prime consolidation reducing sub-tier workshare opportunities, foreign material infiltration regulations adding supply chain compliance cost, commercial aerospace recruiting competing for cleared engineering talent. Four-Quadrant Action Intersection Matrix: SO Strategies (leverage strengths to capture opportunities) — e.g., use NADCAP certifications to win reshoring sub-tier awards from primes consolidating approved supplier lists. WO Strategies (address weaknesses to unlock opportunities) — e.g., pursue SBIR Phase II funding to certify additive manufacturing capability and unlock H2 pipeline. ST Strategies (use strengths to mitigate threats) — e.g., sole-source positions and proprietary TDP ownership provide insulation from prime consolidation threats. WT Strategies (minimize weaknesses, avoid threats) — e.g., reduce indirect cost rate to maintain competitiveness if budget constraints compress award values. Each strategy assigned owner (VP), timeline, and success metric; reviewed quarterly at SMC.
summary: Task: SWOT-to-Action Translation — evidence-based SWOT assessment with SO/WO/ST/WT four-quadrant action intersection matrix, ownership assignment, and quarterly review cadence for defense/aerospace manufacturing

label: Facilitate SWOT workshop and generate action intersection matrix
content: (4) 60 min — score strategies on three dimensions: Impact (1–5, revenue or risk-reduction value) × Feasibility (1–5, internal capability match) × Urgency (1–5, time-sensitivity of action window); composite score max 125; apply threshold rules from SWOT-to-Initiative Prioritization Scoring framework; select initiatives above threshold for execution;
summary: System: SWOT-to-Action workshop facilitation — evidence-gated pre-work, ranked SWOT, four-quadrant intersection matrix, strategy scoring, owner assignment, and integration with Three Horizons and Three Cs

label: Strategic KPI Design and Balanced Scorecard
content: Balanced Scorecard design for VP Strategy translating enterprise vision into measurable performance across four perspectives. Financial Perspective: revenue growth rate (target: 10–15% YoY in growth phase), operating margin (target: 12–18% for defense sub-tier manufacturing), contract backlog coverage (target: 18–24 months forward), B&P efficiency (contract awards per $1M B&P spend), indirect cost rate trend vs. prior year. Customer Perspective: on-time delivery (OTD) rate (target ≥95%), first-article acceptance rate (target ≥90% on first submission), competitive win rate (target ≥35%), sole-source win rate (target ≥75%), CPARS ratings (target: Exceptional/Very Good on >80% of evaluated ratings), repeat award rate from existing customers. Internal Process Perspective: manufacturing cycle efficiency (value-added time ÷ total lead time; target >50%), first-pass yield by process (machining target >97%, assembly >95%), schedule performance index (SPI target: 0.95–1.05), cost performance index (CPI target: 0.95–1.05), NCR closure rate within 30 days (target >90%). Learning and Growth Perspective: cleared workforce pipeline coverage (backfill candidate ready within 12 months for all critical positions), NADCAP/AS9100 certification breadth trend, IRAD investment as % of revenue (target: 2–4%), key talent retention rate (target: >90% for cleared and critical-skill personnel), cross-training completion rate. Leading indicators (predict future outcomes): proposal pipeline value and bid-to-plan ratio, EVM CPI/SPI trend at program midpoint, quality escape rate trend, employee engagement score. Lagging indicators (confirm results): revenue recognized, OTD actuals, award fee scores, DCAA audit finding count. Each KPI requires: data source, calculation method, accountable owner, target with rationale, and escalation threshold.
summary: Task: Balanced Scorecard KPI design across Financial, Customer, Internal Process, and Learning/Growth perspectives with leading/lagging indicators and ownership for defense/aerospace manufacturing

label: Build and maintain strategic balanced scorecard dashboard
content: Operational system for building, populating, and maintaining the VP Strategy balanced scorecard. Dashboard architecture: four-perspective scorecard with traffic-light status (Green: on/above target; Yellow: within 5% of threshold; Red: below threshold); each KPI displays current period actual, 3-period trend arrow, and target. Data sourcing: Financial KPIs from ERP/accounting (monthly pull); Customer KPIs from EVM reports, delivery records, and CPARS portal; Internal Process KPIs from MES production data and quality system NCR log; Learning/Growth KPIs from HR system and training completion records. Leading indicator monitoring: proposal pipeline value updated weekly from CRM; EVM CPI/SPI trend generated from monthly program reports; quality escape rate from quality system event log. KPI design gate: each new KPI must have (1) clear data source confirmed as accessible, (2) defined calculation method documented, (3) named VP accountable for result, (4) target with documented rationale, (5) defined escalation threshold. Scorecard review cadence: weekly CEO staff meeting — top 5 red KPIs reviewed; monthly VP review — all four perspectives with trend analysis; quarterly SMC/board — strategic scorecard summary with year-over-year comparison. Escalation rule: any strategic KPI in red for two consecutive quarters requires formal corrective action plan presented at next SMC meeting. KPI lifecycle: VP Strategy proposes new KPIs; CEO approves; 2-quarter baseline established before target is set. KPIs retired after CEO concurrence when no longer strategically relevant; change log maintained.
summary: System: Balanced scorecard dashboard operations — data sourcing by perspective, traffic-light status, leading indicator monitoring, tiered review cadence, and KPI lifecycle governance

label: Three Cs Strategic Positioning
content: Three Cs framework (Company–Customer–Competitor) for grounding all VP Strategy recommendations in competitive reality for defense/aerospace manufacturing. Company Analysis: audit internal capabilities against criteria that matter in defense procurement — NADCAP certifications (scope, customer-approved), security clearance depth (Secret/TS/SCI billet count), quality system maturity (AS9100 Rev D scope, CMMC level), EVM system certification, special process approvals (customer-specific QPLs), IP portfolio and TDP ownership, financial stability (DCAA audit status, Dun & Bradstreet rating), and facility capacity. Score company strength in each capability area. Customer Analysis: for each target program office or prime contractor, identify unmet needs — what capability gaps are unfilled by current supply chain? What acquisition vehicles are preferred (SBIR, OTA, IDIQ, FFP)? What are the key evaluation criteria in likely source selections (past performance, technical approach, cost realism, small business status)? Interview BD, PM, and engineering staff with direct government customer contact; supplement with FPDS-NG award history, published acquisition strategies, and BAA/SBIR solicitation analysis for capability signals. Competitor Analysis: map known competitors' certifications, customer relationships, FPDS-NG win history (contract value, NAICS codes), set-aside status, pricing posture (from similar awards), and financial health. Identify gaps in competitor coverage of specific technical domains or geographic regions. Sustainable Advantage Identification: intersection of high company strength + high unmet customer need + low competitor capability = sustainable competitive advantage zone. Defense manufacturing advantage sources: sole-source positions (no substitute available), proprietary process know-how (tacit knowledge barriers), certifications competitors lack (specific NADCAP scopes, customer QPL approvals), co-development relationships (customer-funded IRAD creating barrier), and cleared workforce depth (hard to replicate quickly due to clearance timelines). Grounding requirement: every strategic recommendation — new market entry, bid decision, IRAD investment — must explicitly state how it leverages a company strength to serve an unmet customer need in a space where competitors are weak.
summary: Task: Three Cs (Company-Customer-Competitor) strategic positioning — capability audit, unmet need identification, competitor gap mapping, and sustainable competitive advantage definition for defense/aerospace manufacturing

label: Conduct Three Cs competitive positioning analysis
content: Structured workflow for Three Cs positioning analysis in defense manufacturing. Company audit (internal): compile capability register — NADCAP certifications (scope, expiration, approved customers), security clearances by level (Secret/TS/SCI, number of billets), AS9100 scope, quality metrics (NCR rate, DCMA surveillance findings), EVM compliance, and unique technical capabilities (special alloys, proprietary coatings, tight-tolerance machining, frequency of design change absorption). Score company strength in each capability area on 1–5 scale using evidence, not self-assessment. Customer analysis (external intelligence): for each target program office or prime: pull FPDS-NG award history (what they buy, at what value, from whom, and under what contract vehicle); review published acquisition strategies and draft solicitations; analyze BAA/SBIR solicitations for capability emphasis signals; debrief BD and PM staff with direct government customer contact; align with FYDP budget line data (RDT&E vs. procurement account growth signals). Competitor mapping: for each significant competitor, build a profile using FPDS-NG (contract history by NAICS, award values, program associations), SAM.gov (certifications, set-aside eligibility, size status), public financial data, DCMA supplier risk assessments where available, and BD win/loss intelligence. Three-axis intersection matrix: plot Company Strength (high/low) × Customer Need Intensity (high/low) × Competitor Gap (present/absent); target pursuit quadrant is high company strength + high customer need + competitor gap. Output: Three Cs positioning map, 1-paragraph competitive advantage statement per target market segment, and ranked strategic priority list for CEO/SMC review. Refresh cadence: full Three Cs analysis annually (Q3 strategic planning cycle); incremental competitor update quarterly via FPDS-NG data pull.
summary: System: Three Cs positioning analysis workflow — capability audit, FPDS-NG competitor mapping, customer intelligence gathering, three-axis intersection matrix, and annual refresh cadence

label: Data-Driven Strategy and Causal Analysis
content: Analytics infrastructure and causal reasoning framework for VP Strategy decisions in defense manufacturing — move beyond correlation and descriptive reporting to causal inference. Decision use cases requiring causal analysis: (1) Make-vs-Buy: does insourcing a capability actually reduce cost and improve schedule, or do overhead absorption and learning curve effects negate savings? Build causal model controlling for volume, program maturity, and workforce stability as confounders. (2) Pricing strategy: what drives bid win rate — price alone, or the interaction of price, past performance rating, and technical proposal quality score? Causal inference via regression analysis on FPDS-NG historical award data. (3) Capacity investment: does adding a production shift actually improve OTD, or is OTD driven primarily by engineering change frequency (a different causal root)? Separate confounders from true levers via causal graph. (4) IRAD ROI: do IRAD investments in specific technology areas actually convert to contract awards, or are wins driven by incumbent relationships regardless of technical differentiation? Bayesian network analysis to estimate contribution of each factor. Tooling: data pipelines using Kedro (node-based Python pipeline orchestration) connecting ERP, MES, EVM, and BD CRM data into a unified analytics layer; causal inference via CausalNex (Bayesian network structure learning from data) or DoWhy (causal graph estimation and counterfactual queries); scenario decision trees for investment sensitivity analysis. Statistical discipline requirement: VP Strategy must document the causal assumptions underlying every major investment recommendation; assumptions are reviewed by CTO data team; sensitivity analysis required before SMC presentation. Distinguish association (useful for monitoring) from causation (required for resource commitment decisions).
summary: Task: Data-driven strategy using Kedro pipelines and CausalNex/Bayesian networks — causal analysis for make-vs-buy, pricing, capacity investment, and IRAD ROI decisions beyond correlation in defense manufacturing

label: Build strategic analytics pipeline and causal decision models
content: Technical system design for VP Strategy analytics infrastructure in defense manufacturing. Data pipeline architecture (Kedro-based): Node 1 — Data Ingestion: pull ERP (cost actuals by cost pool, indirect rates, milestone billing), MES (cycle time, OTD, yield, shift utilization), EVM system (CPI/SPI by program, variance analysis), BD CRM (pipeline value, win/loss events, B&P spend per opportunity), HR (headcount by clearance level, attrition events, training completion), quality system (NCR rate, DCMA findings, customer escapes), and FPDS-NG (competitor award data, market share trends); standardize into parquet format in on-prem data lake with ITAR-compliant access controls (no cloud storage of CUI without appropriate FedRAMP authorization). Node 2 — Feature Engineering: calculate derived metrics (bid-to-win ratio by NAICS and contract type, cost trend per program phase, OTD correlation with engineering change order rate, IRAD spend vs. subsequent award within 3 years); maintain feature catalog with lineage documentation and data quality flags. Node 3 — Causal Model Layer: use CausalNex to specify Bayesian network structure from domain knowledge (VP Strategy and engineering SMEs define causal graph edges); learn edge weights from historical data via maximum likelihood estimation; validate model against holdout period data; generate counterfactual queries (e.g., 'What would OTD be if ECO rate reduced 30%?', 'What win rate improvement results from 1-pt CPARS improvement?'). Node 4 — Decision Model Interface: for each recurring strategic decision type (make-vs-buy, capacity investment, pricing bid, IRAD allocation), maintain a pre-built decision model template with documented input parameters, causal assumptions, sensitivity ranges, and recommended decision thresholds; VP Strategy enters scenario parameters, model returns probability distribution of outcomes and confidence interval. Output: monthly strategic analytics brief (top 5 causal insights, 1-page), on-demand decision model runs for SMC presentations. Governance: CTO co-owns analytics infrastructure; VP Strategy owns decision model specifications and causal assumptions; all causal models reviewed by an independent statistician or data scientist annually for assumption validity and model drift.
summary: System: Kedro data pipeline with CausalNex Bayesian networks for strategic decision modeling — ITAR-compliant data ingestion, feature engineering, causal graph construction, counterfactual queries, and scenario decision models for VP Strategy

label: Hypothesis-driven problem decomposition process
content: Structured process for forming and validating hypotheses within MECE issue tree branches. Steps: (1) Anchor the central hypothesis to the problem statement — state the expected answer before analysis begins. (2) Decompose into sub-hypotheses by issue tree branch — each branch produces a falsifiable hypothesis (e.g., 'Cost overrun is driven by material price variance, not labor efficiency'). (3) Assign a confidence level (high/medium/low) and a validation data source to each sub-hypothesis before fieldwork begins. (4) Conduct parallel validation — teams test branches simultaneously to compress analysis cycle time. (5) Update tree structure as evidence invalidates branches or reveals uncovered sub-issues. (6) Converge: if all branch hypotheses are confirmed, synthesize the finding; if contradicted, revise the central hypothesis and iterate. Quality gate: VP Strategy reviews issue tree and full hypothesis set at analysis kickoff before workstreams are assigned — prevents teams pursuing a dead branch for weeks. Common failure mode: forming a hypothesis after seeing data, which reverse-engineers a conclusion rather than testing one.
summary: System: Hypothesis-driven problem decomposition — anchoring central hypothesis, assigning branch-level falsifiable sub-hypotheses, parallel validation, and convergence protocol for MECE issue tree analysis

label: Workstream assignment protocol from issue tree branches
content: Protocol for converting MECE issue tree branches into assigned, non-overlapping workstreams. Steps: (1) Map each Level-2 branch to one workstream lead — single owner per branch, no shared ownership. (2) Define workstream scope explicitly: data to collect, hypothesis to test, deliverable format, due date, and dependencies on other workstreams. (3) Publish a workstream charter document for each team: owner, branch hypothesis, data sources, analysis method, output format, and inter-workstream dependencies. (4) Designate VP Strategy as integration lead owning cross-workstream synthesis and MECE compliance throughout execution. (5) Mid-point integration checkpoint: all workstream leads review preliminary findings together — identify if branch coverage remains MECE as evidence develops; adjust scope before final synthesis. (6) Final synthesis: integration lead assembles findings from all branches into pyramid-structure executive brief following SCR format. MECE quality check at assignment stage: (A) no two workstreams test the same hypothesis on the same dataset; (B) combined workstreams cover 100% of the identified problem space per the issue tree.
summary: System: Workstream assignment protocol — single owner per issue tree branch, workstream charters, MECE quality check, mid-point integration checkpoint, and synthesis handoff to executive brief

label: Pyramid Principle communication structure (Situation-Complication-Resolution)
content: Minto Pyramid Principle framework for structuring all VP Strategy executive communications. SCR structure: (1) Situation — establish shared factual context the audience already accepts; describe current state briefly; do not bury the recommendation here. (2) Complication — introduce the tension, problem, or disruption requiring action; must be provocative enough to demand resolution. (3) Resolution — state the recommendation or answer first, then support with grouped evidence; never bury the answer at the end. Supporting argument structure: group supporting points by type (deductive: premise → conclusion; or inductive: parallel evidence → synthesis); each supporting level must answer the question provoked by the level above (SCQA — Situation, Complication, Question, Answer). Slide discipline: executive summary slide follows SCR in three bullets; appendix carries methodology. Defense manufacturing application: every SMC briefing, board update, and CEO strategy memo from VP Strategy follows SCR/SCQA — opens with the recommendation, not with methodology. Common failure mode: analysts present data in discovery order (bottom-up) rather than structured for executive consumption (top-down); VP Strategy enforces pyramid review before any briefing is delivered to C-suite.
summary: System: Pyramid Principle (SCR/SCQA) communication structure — situation-complication-resolution framing, top-down answer-first delivery, inductive/deductive grouping, and enforcement before SMC briefings

label: Supplier power assessment — defense primes and raw material vendors
content: Structured assessment of supplier bargaining power in the defense/aerospace sub-tier context. Assessment dimensions: (1) Supplier concentration: count of qualified domestic sources for critical inputs — specialty alloys (titanium, Inconel, beryllium), radiation-hardened components, MMIC foundries, EEE parts; single or dual sourcing signals high supplier power. (2) Switching cost: re-qualification cycles required to change supplier (NADCAP re-approval, customer QPL re-entry, first-article inspection) typically span 6–18 months — creates high switching cost and high power. (3) Forward integration threat: prime contractors that also produce sub-tier components hold a dual role as customer and potential competitor; monitor prime insourcing trends via FPDS-NG data and earnings calls. (4) Input price volatility: track commodity indices for titanium, rare earths, specialty composites, and specialty electronics; >15% annual price volatility signals elevated supplier power requiring LTA price protection. (5) Strategic reserve risk: long-lead suppliers (castings, forgings, specialty alloys >26-week lead time) require LTA coverage or strategic safety stock. Scoring: assign each critical supplier a power rating (1–5) on concentration, switching cost, and forward-integration risk dimensions; aggregate to portfolio-level supplier power score. Mitigation: multi-source qualification investment, LTA price protection, and vertical integration feasibility study for highest-power sole-source inputs.
summary: System: Supplier power assessment for defense primes and raw material vendors — concentration scoring, switching cost analysis, forward-integration monitoring, price volatility tracking, and mitigation protocols

label: Buyer power analysis — government procurement (FAR/DFARS leverage)
content: Assessment of buyer bargaining power concentrated in DoD program offices and prime contractors. Power sources: (1) Customer concentration: DoD and 3–5 major primes may represent >80% of revenue — extreme buyer concentration drives high power; track revenue dependency per customer quarterly and flag if any single customer exceeds 40% of revenue. (2) Contract structure leverage: competitive multi-award IDIQs compress margins; government should-cost studies challenge cost structures directly; DFARS 215.404 weighted profit/fee guidelines cap returns on cost-plus work; price-based profit objectives on FFP re-competes create downward price pressure. (3) Set-aside status: small business set-asides restrict competition to a preferred pool but simultaneously reduce revenue ceiling per award; government can exercise market research to compete away from set-aside — understand which NAICS codes attract mandatory set-aside thresholds. (4) CPARS leverage: government Contractor Performance Assessment Reporting System ratings carry 3-year source selection weight — poor CPARS scores reduce win probability for multiple award cycles; government holds reputational leverage that prevents exit from under-performing programs without relationship consequence. (5) Data rights leverage: DFARS 252.227 unlimited or government purpose rights assertions on TDPs and software eliminate proprietary data barriers and reduce long-term competitive advantage. Mitigation: sole-source positions and proprietary IP reduce buyer leverage; diversify revenue across program offices and agencies; build CPARS history proactively through exceptional delivery on smaller programs.
summary: System: Buyer power analysis for government procurement — customer concentration, contract structure leverage (FAR/DFARS), set-aside dynamics, CPARS reputational leverage, and data rights exposure with mitigation strategies

label: Substitute threat monitoring — additive manufacturing, autonomy, and AI
content: Monitoring system for substitute technology threats to traditional defense/aerospace sub-tier manufacturing revenue streams. Substitution categories: (1) Additive manufacturing (AM): metal AM (L-PBF, DED) increasingly qualified for flight-critical structural components; monitor AMS7000-series qualification standards, USAF AM qualification roadmap milestones, and AFRL LEAP program progress; track which programs are authorizing AM-produced flight hardware — signals substitution acceleration for traditional machined parts. (2) Autonomy replacing hardware volume: autonomous platforms (Group III/IV UAS, LAWS, unmanned maritime) shift platform functions to software, reducing per-platform hardware unit counts; track FYDP shifts from manned to unmanned programs — lower unit production counts reduce traditional sub-tier component revenue proportionally. (3) Software-defined / AI-enabled capabilities: programmable RF (software-defined radar, EW, comms) reduces discrete hardware variants; AI processing on COTS chips (Nvidia Jetson, Intel Movidius) substitutes for custom mil-spec electronics; monitor DARPA/AFRL BAA investment signals in these areas. Monitoring cadence: quarterly scan of DARPA/AFRL BAAs, SBIR solicitations, and prime contractor earnings calls for AM qualification milestones and autonomy investment signals; update Five Forces substitute threat score semi-annually. Strategic response options: invest in AM qualification to capture substitute market rather than be displaced, develop software-hardware co-development competency, or reposition portfolio toward sub-systems where hardware complexity remains high and no substitute is viable.
summary: System: Substitute threat monitoring — quarterly tracking of additive manufacturing qualification, autonomy platform unit-count shifts, and AI/software-defined capability displacement signals with semi-annual Five Forces score update

label: New entrant barriers analysis — ITAR, facility clearance, and qualification costs
content: Assessment and monitoring of barriers protecting existing defense sub-tier manufacturers from new competition. Barrier categories and strength ratings: (1) ITAR/EAR regulatory compliance: DDTC registration, export license management, and personnel vetting represent a 6–12 month ramp for new entrants with $50–150K annual compliance overhead and ongoing debarment risk; rates as strong barrier for most commercial entrants. (2) Facility security clearance (FCL): DCSA investigation, insider threat program, and KMP personal clearances require 12–24 months and significant management overhead before facility-cleared contract awards are accessible; strong barrier for commercial-origin entrants. (3) Customer qualification cycle: AS9100 Rev D certification (6–12 months), NADCAP approval per process scope (9–18 months each), customer first-article inspection, and QPL entry aggregate to 18–36 months before first revenue — strong barrier for new manufacturing entrants. (4) Capital intensity: precision aerospace manufacturing requires $500K–$5M+ in equipment and facility investment for credible sub-tier capability; moderate barrier that venture-backed entrants can overcome. (5) Non-traditional entrant risk: commercial space firms, tech primes, and AM shops are lowering selected barriers via OTA alternative approvals, government-sponsored qualification programs, and DIU/AFWERX partnerships; monitor new entrant activity via SAM.gov new registrations in target NAICS codes, SBIR new awardees, and OTA consortium memberships. Barrier erosion signals requiring response: any non-traditional entrant achieving AS9100 certification or NADCAP approval in a core capability area triggers a Three Cs competitor profile update within 30 days.
summary: System: New entrant barriers analysis — ITAR/FCL/qualification cost barrier strength ratings, non-traditional entrant monitoring via SAM.gov and OTA consortia, and barrier erosion escalation triggers

label: Competitive rivalry mapping — defense tier 2-3 suppliers
content: Systematic mapping and intensity assessment of competitive rivalry among defense tier 2-3 sub-contractors. Rivalry intensity drivers: (1) Competitor count per capability: in specialty defense sub-sectors, typically 3–8 qualified firms compete — oligopolistic structure where any one competitor's price or capacity move has outsized market impact; pull FPDS-NG awardees by NAICS code and program office quarterly to maintain current competitor list. (2) Differentiation level: commoditized machined structural parts → high rivalry and margin pressure; highly engineered assemblies with proprietary IP, NADCAP certifications, or customer sole-source positions → materially lower rivalry intensity. (3) Fixed-cost intensity and capacity utilization: high-overhead aerospace facilities create incentive to bid at marginal pricing when under-utilized; monitor whether competitors are capacity-constrained (limits aggressive pricing) or capacity-surplus (enables predatory pricing on re-competes). (4) Exit barriers: once qualified on a program, exit requires customer notification and replacement qualification — creates sticky incumbencies that protect existing players but also lock in rivals; use to assess longevity of competitor positions on programs you are targeting. (5) Market growth rate effect: slow-growth programs intensify competition for fixed contract dollars; growth programs (hypersonics, space, autonomy) temporarily reduce rivalry intensity by expanding the addressable market. Rivalry map deliverable: annual competitor capability matrix by NAICS, certifications, clearance depth, primary program associations, and pricing posture estimate; quarterly FPDS-NG award share update by target program office.
summary: System: Competitive rivalry mapping for defense tier 2-3 — FPDS-NG competitor count by NAICS, differentiation level, capacity utilization signals, exit barrier stickiness, and annual capability matrix with quarterly award share refresh

label: Market entry/exit decisions based on Five Forces scoring
content: Decision framework for entering new defense market segments or exiting existing ones based on Five Forces aggregate assessment. Entry decision criteria: (1) Five Forces composite score ≤15 (rate each force 1–5 where 1=favorable; entry threshold requires attractive-to-neutral industry structure across all five dimensions). (2) Three Cs capability match ≥70% against required certifications, clearances, and technical competencies for the target segment. (3) Addressable contract value ≥$10M over 5 years from FPDS-NG historical awards and FYDP projections. (4) Investment recovery within 3 years under base-case revenue assumption. Exit decision criteria: (1) Five Forces composite score ≥20 sustained for two consecutive semi-annual assessments with no improvement path. (2) Segment operating margin <8% for two consecutive years with no viable corrective action. (3) No defensible differentiation — commodity position with no IP, certification, or relationship barrier worth defending. (4) Strategic opportunity cost: capital and management attention locked in exit candidate demonstrably exceeds return from redeployment to a higher-scored segment (Five Forces composite ≤15). Process: VP Strategy produces market entry/exit recommendation memo with Five Forces scoring evidence, Three Cs capability match, financial model, and strategic rationale; CEO/SMC approves; CFO confirms financial modeling; VP BD confirms pipeline impact assessment.
summary: Decision: Market entry/exit triggered by Five Forces composite scoring (entry ≤15, exit ≥20 sustained), with capability match, TAM floor, and investment recovery criteria; VP Strategy recommends, CEO/SMC approves

label: Make-vs-buy decisions using value chain and Five Forces
content: Integrated decision framework combining Porter's Value Chain and Five Forces supplier market analysis for make-vs-buy (insource vs. outsource) decisions in defense manufacturing. Decision trigger: any capability where internal fully-loaded cost exceeds external market rate by >15%, OR sole-source supplier power score ≥4, OR internal process yield <90%. Value Chain lens: capabilities in high-margin primary activity nodes with proprietary differentiation → make candidate; support/commodity activities with no customer-facing value advantage → buy candidate; assess whether capability creates a competitive barrier (make) or is a cost center (buy). Five Forces lens for outsource candidates: evaluate the supplier market — concentrated external supply with high supplier power (score ≥4) → make risk; commodity supply with many qualified vendors and low supplier power (score ≤2) → buy is structurally safe. Financial analysis: fully-loaded internal cost (direct labor + overhead absorption + facilities burden + capital depreciation) vs. external price including transaction costs (QA surveillance, incoming inspection, IP risk premium, schedule flexibility cost). Buy threshold: external total cost ≤85% of fully-loaded internal cost AND supplier power ≤3 AND no strategic differentiation argument from value chain analysis. Make threshold: capability is sole-source risk if outsourced, OR provides proprietary barrier, OR fully-loaded cost is favorable. Decision documentation: VP Strategy produces make-vs-buy recommendation memo for CIC review per FAR 15.407-2, including Five Forces supplier market assessment, value chain position analysis, and NPV comparison across make/buy/partnership alternatives.
summary: Decision: Make-vs-buy framework integrating value chain position (differentiation vs. commodity) and Five Forces supplier power scoring — buy threshold at ≤85% cost + supplier power ≤3; documented via CIC memo per FAR 15.407-2

label: Competitive response playbooks
content: Pre-defined decision playbooks for responding to competitive moves by rival defense tier 2-3 suppliers. Trigger and prescribed response: (1) Incumbent displacement attempt — competitor bids below sustainable cost threshold on a re-compete: conduct should-cost analysis of competitor's bid; if economically unsustainable, maintain price discipline and document value differentiators (CPARS history, zero-defect delivery record, proprietary IP) in proposal; alert contracting officer to unrealistically low offer risk per FAR 3.103. (2) Competitor new certification acquisition — rival achieves NADCAP scope or QPL entry covering a previously differentiated capability: accelerate next certification scope on internal roadmap; identify an adjacent capability barrier competitors cannot easily replicate; update Three Cs positioning analysis within 30 days. (3) Prime-sub teaming realignment — prime announces insourcing or workshare shift to a rival: escalate to VP BD for relationship engagement with prime program office; assess counter-teaming availability; evaluate whether the threatened capability is a make candidate per value chain analysis. (4) New entrant underbidding on commercial-equivalent components: evaluate lean investment to close cost gap; assess whether the component class is a buy candidate (exit gracefully) or a core capability worth defending. (5) Competitor financial distress signals — rival DCAA finding, debarment, or M&A: immediately assess whether customer programs are at risk of gap in supply; position proactively with customer as stable alternative source. Response timelines: VP Strategy issues playbook recommendation within 5 business days of trigger event; CEO/VP BD approve response action within 10 business days.
summary: Decision: Five-scenario competitive response playbooks covering displacement bids, rival certification gains, prime teaming shifts, new entrant underbidding, and competitor distress — with 5/10-day response timelines and authority

label: Executive strategy brief template
content: Standard template for VP Strategy briefings delivered to CEO/SMC, enforcing Pyramid Principle (SCR/SCQA) structure throughout. Template sections: (1) Cover slide: problem statement, analysis date, VP Strategy author, classification, distribution list. (2) Executive Summary (1 slide): Situation (1–2 sentences of shared factual context), Complication (the strategic tension requiring action), Resolution (recommendation stated first with 3 bullet supporting points). (3) Issue Tree (1 slide): visual MECE decomposition showing the full problem space, branches analyzed vs. deferred, and branch-level hypotheses. (4) Findings by Branch (1–3 slides): for each major branch — hypothesis stated, evidence presented, conclusion reached; flag branches where evidence was insufficient or hypothesis remains open. (5) Strategic Recommendation (1 slide): specific action, accountable VP owner, timeline, resource requirement (dollar and headcount), and success metric with target. (6) Risk and Sensitivity (1 slide): top 3 assumptions underlying the recommendation; sensitivity of conclusion to each assumption; downside scenario outcome. (7) Appendix: data sources with pull dates, analytical methodology, FPDS-NG data tables, financial models, competitor profiles. Format standards: core deck ≤7 slides; appendix unrestricted; no methodology before the answer; no data without a 'so what' statement. Review protocol: VP Strategy circulates draft to relevant VPs 48 hours before SMC presentation for factual accuracy; no changes to recommendation framing without VP Strategy concurrence.
summary: Output: Executive strategy brief template — 7-slide maximum following SCR/SCQA structure with cover, executive summary, issue tree, findings-by-branch, recommendation, risk/sensitivity, and appendix; enforces answer-first discipline

label: Competitive intelligence dashboard specification
content: Specification for the standing competitive intelligence (CI) dashboard maintained by VP Strategy for CEO and VP BD consumption. Dashboard panels: (1) FPDS-NG Market Share Panel: quarterly-refreshed table of top 10 direct competitors by NAICS code — contract award value, award count, primary customer programs, and YoY market share trend arrow. (2) Five Forces Scorecard: semi-annual rating of all five forces (1–5 per force) with trend arrows vs. prior period; composite industry attractiveness score; key driver narrative per force (2–3 sentences). (3) Competitor Profile Cards (top 5 direct rivals): certifications held, clearance depth, last 4 FPDS-NG contract wins, pricing posture estimate (derived from similar-scope awards), revenue estimate, and BD intelligence notes from win/loss debriefs. (4) New Entrant Watch List: SAM.gov registrations in target NAICS codes within past 12 months; OTA consortium new members; SBIR Phase II new awardees in capability areas of interest. (5) Substitute Technology Tracker: AM qualification TRL progress by material/process, autonomy platform unit count vs. prior year (FYDP comparison), software-defined capability BAA investment signals. Data sources: FPDS-NG automated quarterly pull, SAM.gov, GovWin/Deltek, NDIA/AIA industry reports, BD win/loss debrief records, earnings call monitoring. Refresh cadence: full dashboard quarterly; FPDS-NG competitor panel monthly. Distribution: CEO, VP BD, VP Strategy; ITAR-sensitive competitor sections marked and restricted to cleared staff.
summary: Output: Competitive intelligence dashboard — five panels (FPDS-NG share, Five Forces scorecard, competitor profiles, new entrant watch, substitute tracker) with quarterly/monthly refresh cadence and ITAR-controlled distribution

label: Quarterly strategic review presentation format
content: Standard format for VP Strategy-led quarterly strategic review delivered to SMC (CEO and all VP/C-suite). Presentation structure and time allocation: (1) Strategic Scorecard Review (10 min, 3 slides): balanced scorecard traffic-light across all four perspectives; trend vs. prior quarter; red KPIs with root cause identified and corrective action plan with owner and 30-day milestone. (2) Competitive Intelligence Update (10 min, 3 slides): Five Forces score update — any force changing ≥1 point from prior quarter gets a narrative explanation; FPDS-NG competitor win/loss events since last review; new entrant watch list status. (3) PESTEL Macro-Environment Scan (10 min, 2 slides): top 3 macro-environment changes since last quarter with strategic implication assessment; FYDP budget signal updates; regulatory developments (CMMC, ITAR, DFARS changes). (4) Portfolio Health Review (10 min, 3 slides): Three Horizons H1/H2/H3 investment balance vs. 70/20/10 target; horizon graduation events or terminations since last review; BCG/9-Box portfolio position update for top 10 programs. (5) Strategic Initiative Status (10 min, 2 slides): progress update on SWOT-to-Action top-10 strategies — milestone achieved/missed, owner accountability, resource adequacy rating. (6) Decision Items (10 min, 2 slides): issues requiring SMC decision this quarter — market entry/exit recommendations, make-vs-buy approvals, competitive response playbook activations. Format: 15-slide maximum for main deck; appendix unrestricted. Pre-work: VP Strategy distributes draft to SMC 5 business days before review; receives written comments 48 hours before meeting; decision items must include a recommendation, not just a status update.
summary: Output: Quarterly strategic review format — six sections (scorecard, CI update, PESTEL, portfolio health, initiative status, decision items) in 60 minutes with 15-slide maximum and 5-day pre-distribution requirement

label: Board strategy update package
content: Annual board-level strategy update package prepared by VP Strategy for CEO delivery. Package components and slide allocation: (1) Strategic Position Summary (2 slides): Three Cs competitive position — company strengths vs. customer priorities vs. competitor gaps; sustainable competitive advantage statement with supporting evidence; changes vs. prior year with explanation. (2) Market Environment Update (2 slides): PESTEL highlights affecting the 3–5 year strategic plan; Five Forces composite score by major market segment with YoY trend; top threats and opportunities with probability and strategic implication. (3) Portfolio Performance vs. Strategy (2 slides): BCG/9-Box portfolio map of active programs and pipeline; H1/H2/H3 investment balance vs. 70/20/10 target; Three Horizons projected revenue mix at year 5 vs. plan target. (4) Strategic Plan Progress (2 slides): scorecard on annual strategic objectives from the enterprise strategic plan — on-track/off-track with brief explanation; SWOT-to-Action top-10 strategy completion rate with accountable owner status. (5) Three-Year Growth Forecast (2 slides): revenue forecast by market segment under base/optimistic/pessimistic scenario (PESTEL stress-tested); key assumptions and risk factors; capital investment requirements to achieve growth targets. (6) Strategic Risks (1 slide): top 5 Tier 1 enterprise strategic risks from CEO risk register with probability, impact, and mitigation status. (7) Board Discussion Items (1 slide): 2–3 strategic questions requiring board-level guidance or approval (e.g., new market entry, major partnership, capital structure for growth). Total: 12 slides. Timing: delivered annually at Q1 board meeting; draft to CEO 30 days in advance; board package distributed 7 days before meeting per governance charter.
summary: Output: Annual board strategy update package — 12 slides covering Three Cs position, PESTEL/Five Forces environment, portfolio health, strategic plan progress, 3-year growth forecast, and board discussion items; 30-day CEO pre-review required

label: Strategy-structure alignment audit process
content: Structured audit comparing documented strategic priorities against organizational structure. Steps: (1) Extract 3–5 strategic priorities from annual strategic plan. (2) Map each priority to required organizational capabilities and decision authorities. (3) Audit current org chart: identify structural gaps (missing functions, unclear ownership, excessive spans of control in capability-critical areas). (4) Test structural inhibitors: does decision authority rest at appropriate levels? Are cross-functional interdependencies formalized through IPT charters or matrix authority? (5) Produce alignment gap report: rank structural gaps by strategy inhibition severity. (6) Recommend adjustments: new reporting lines, IPT charters, new VP-level function creation, or delegated authority changes. Defense context: common misalignments include Strategy targeting LRIP production capability with no dedicated Production Engineering function; digital thread strategy with no Chief Digital/Data role; M&A integration with no integration management office. Output: 7S alignment heat map (red/yellow/green) per element, top-5 structural inhibitors ranked by strategic impact, and corrective action recommendations for CEO/SMC review. Cadence: annual during Q3 strategic planning; triggered additionally by major reorganizations, M&A events, or strategy pivots.
summary: System: Annual audit comparing strategic priorities to organizational structure — identifies decision authority gaps, missing functions, and structural inhibitors to strategy execution with corrective action recommendations

label: Culture assessment methodology — Style + Shared Values diagnostic
content: Diagnostic approach for the Style (leadership behavior patterns) and Shared Values (mission/ethical culture) elements of McKinsey 7S. Style assessment: structured 360-degree observation of leadership behaviors — meeting facilitation, escalation response, risk tolerance signals, candor in performance reviews, and follow-through on commitments; compare observed behaviors to stated cultural values; identify leadership behaviors that contradict or reinforce strategy. Shared Values diagnostic: (1) Document articulated values from mission statement, quality policy, ethics policy, and strategic plan. (2) Conduct anonymous employee pulse survey (15–20 items) measuring observed vs. stated values alignment. (3) Interview 5–8 frontline personnel, 3–5 managers, and 2–3 customers to triangulate lived culture. (4) Analyze quality escape trends, near-miss reporting rates, and ethics hotline usage as behavioral culture proxies — these reveal whether quality culture is real or performative. (5) Score alignment 1–5 per stated value; identify gaps between articulated and lived values. Defense context: critical culture signals include whether quality escapes are surfaced immediately or concealed to avoid schedule impact, whether audit preparation is honest or adversarial, and whether schedule pressure routinely overrides quality holds. Output: culture alignment heat map with scores per value, top 3 cultural risks, and leadership behavior change recommendations assigned to CEO/VP owners.
summary: System: Style and Shared Values diagnostic using 360-degree observation, employee survey, behavioral proxies, and evidence-based culture scoring — identifies gaps between stated and lived organizational values

label: Skills gap analysis framework linking to HR capability building
content: Structured skills assessment connecting the McKinsey 7S Skills element to HR development and recruiting plans. Process: (1) Derive required skill profile from strategy — what capabilities must the workforce possess to execute the 3–5 year strategic plan? Examples: MBSE expertise for digital engineering strategy, additive manufacturing process knowledge for H2 capability bets, cleared program management for classified program pipeline. (2) Inventory current skills: compile competency data from performance reviews, training records, certification registers (NADCAP scopes, AS9100 auditor, EVM), and clearance census by level. (3) Gap analysis: compare required profile vs. current inventory; quantify gaps by function, level, and criticality (mission-critical vs. supporting). (4) Gap categorization using 4B framework — Build (internal training/development), Buy (external recruiting), Borrow (subcontractors/consultants), Bridge (university/FFRDC partnerships). (5) Develop HR capability building plan: for each critical gap, assign 4B strategy with timeline, resource requirement (FTE, cost), accountable VP, and measurable closure metric. (6) Monitor gap closure quarterly at SMC. Defense context: clearance pipeline gaps require 12–24 month lead time — plan Build/Buy actions accordingly. NADCAP special process skill gaps must be resolved before contract qualification milestones. Output: skills gap matrix by function, 4B capability building roadmap with budget estimate, integration with annual HR operating plan and succession readiness review.
summary: System: Strategy-derived skills gap analysis using 4B framework (Build/Buy/Borrow/Bridge) — connects McKinsey 7S Skills element to HR capability building plan with quarterly closure tracking

label: Systems maturity evaluation — ERP, PLM, MES, EVM alignment with strategy
content: Assessment of maturity and strategic alignment for four core enterprise systems: ERP, PLM, MES, and EVM/reporting. Evaluation per system: (1) Current state documentation — system name, version, scope of use, integration points, user count, and annual lifecycle cost. (2) Functional adequacy — assess whether system capabilities meet current operational requirements; identify workarounds (manual Excel processes, system bypasses) as maturity deficiency signals. (3) Strategic alignment — evaluate whether system supports strategy's information requirements: digital thread strategy requires bidirectional PLM-MES integration; CMMC compliance requires CUI-classified data access controls in ERP; real-time production visibility requires MES-ERP integration for EVM actuals. (4) Maturity scoring (1–5 per dimension): system stability, data quality, integration depth, user adoption rate, and compliance support capability. (5) Gap remediation roadmap: for each maturity gap, identify investment options (configuration/upgrade vs. replacement vs. integration module); estimate cost and schedule; submit to CIC for capital approval. Defense-specific requirements: ERP must support DCAA audit trail (unallowable cost tagging, direct/indirect cost segregation per CAS); PLM must maintain TDP configuration management with ITAR data access controls; MES must feed EVM actuals for CPI/SPI calculation; all systems must address CMMC 2.0 CUI handling. Output: systems maturity heat map across four systems and five dimensions, strategic alignment gap table, prioritized investment recommendations for CIC submission. Cadence: full evaluation every 2 years; annual update at strategic planning cycle.
summary: System: Maturity and strategic alignment evaluation for ERP, PLM, MES, and EVM systems — scores five dimensions per system, identifies integration gaps vs. strategy requirements, and produces CIC investment recommendations

label: Program portfolio scoring model — growth rate, win probability, margin, strategic fit
content: Quantitative scoring model for classifying programs and pipeline opportunities on the GE-McKinsey 9-box. Industry Attractiveness composite (0–100): FYDP market growth rate for platform/capability domain (25%); addressable contract value over 5 years (20%); competitive intensity — number of qualified competitors in this segment (15%); margin potential based on typical contract type and pricing structure (20%); strategic priority in NDS/budget documents (20%). Competitive Strength composite (0–100): program win rate / market share in segment from FPDS-NG (25%); customer relationship strength — CPARS ratings, program office access depth (20%); certifications and qualifications in place vs. required — NADCAP scopes, clearances, QPL listings (20%); cost position vs. competitors — indirect rate, direct labor efficiency benchmarked against FPDS-NG award prices (20%); technical differentiation — IP ownership, sole-source position, proprietary process (15%). Scoring thresholds: High ≥67, Medium 34–66, Low ≤33. Nine-box placement: High/High = Invest/Grow; High/Medium or Medium/High = Selective Investment; Low/Low = Harvest/Divest. Data sources: BD CRM (win rate, pipeline stage), CPARS portal (ratings), FPDS-NG (market share, competitor history), ERP (cost position), contracts system (program type, margin), FYDP budget documents (market growth). Scoring cadence: all active programs and top-10 pipeline opportunities scored annually; mid-year update triggered by significant win/loss events. VP Strategy owns model; VP BD validates market intelligence inputs; CEO/SMC review placement results.
summary: System: GE-McKinsey 9-box quantitative scoring model with five-factor Industry Attractiveness and five-factor Competitive Strength composites — scores active programs and pipeline opportunities annually to drive portfolio investment decisions

label: Investment committee decision criteria for portfolio allocation
content: Decision criteria used by the Capital Investment Council (CIC) and Senior Management Council (SMC) for portfolio investment decisions informed by BCG/GE 9-box placement. CIC investment criteria (all must pass for Invest/Grow designation): (1) Portfolio balance test — investment must not result in >3 active programs in the Question Mark quadrant simultaneously; excess requires CEO explicit approval with documented risk acceptance. (2) Payback period standard — Invest/Grow programs: ≤5 years; Selective Investment: ≤3 years; Cash Cow sustaining investments: ≤2 years. (3) Strategic fit score — candidate must score ≥70/100 on Three Cs alignment (company strength × customer need × competitor gap). (4) Resource availability — investment must not require >15% of annual B&P or IRAD budget without SMC approval. (5) Pipeline viability — program must have ≥$5M in near-term contract opportunities (within 18 months) to qualify for Invest/Grow. SMC override authority: CEO/SMC may approve below-threshold investments with documented rationale (strategic relationship preservation, critical national security program, market access enabling future opportunity set). Decision record requirements: scoring inputs, criteria met/not met per line item, override rationale if applicable, approval signature, and post-investment review date. All decisions logged in CIC investment decision register maintained by VP Strategy and CFO.
summary: System: CIC/SMC investment decision criteria with five mandatory gates (portfolio balance, payback, strategic fit, resource availability, pipeline viability) and CEO override protocol for BCG/9-box informed capital allocation

label: Harvest and divest trigger thresholds for underperforming programs
content: Quantitative and qualitative triggers for transitioning programs from active investment to harvest or divestment mode. Harvest triggers (reduce investment, extract remaining contractual value): GE-McKinsey placement in Harvest zone for 2+ consecutive annual scoring cycles; operating margin below 8% for 2 consecutive quarters with no corrective trend; program backlog coverage <6 months with no viable re-compete pathway; program consuming >20% of annual B&P budget with trailing 12-month win rate <15%.
