Divest/exit triggers (discontinue investment, wind down or transfer): GE-McKinsey Low/Low with negative margin (cost overrun with no recovery path); government program cancellation or funding reduction >50%; customer CPARS rating below Satisfactory for 2 consecutive evaluations with no accepted remediation plan; sole-source position lost to competitor; program requiring capability investment >$500K with <25% follow-on contract probability. Harvest/exit decision process: VP Strategy presents trigger evidence to CEO/SMC; decision options: (A) Harvest — meet contractual obligations, zero new B&P investment, backfill workforce to growing programs; (B) Selective Exit — negotiate contract closeout or novation to another contractor; (C) Hold with conditions — 90-day performance improvement plan with defined re-evaluation gate. Documentation: Harvest/Divest Decision Record capturing trigger evidence, financial impact analysis (sunk cost, exit cost, opportunity cost of capital redeployment), CEO approval. Resource redeployment plan required within 30 days of decision.
summary: System: Quantitative harvest and divest trigger thresholds with five harvest indicators and five exit indicators — defines decision process, three response options, and documentation requirements for underperforming portfolio programs

label: Market penetration tactics — contract expansion, IDIQ task orders, ECP proposals
content: Tactical playbook for growing share within existing defense customer programs — lowest-risk Ansoff quadrant. Three primary penetration mechanisms: (1) Contract Expansion — identify out-of-scope work currently performed by customer or competitors on active programs; develop unsolicited white paper or technical proposal to absorb that work; target areas where internal capability is proven and scope expansion cost is low. Trigger: any program with CPARS Excellent or Very Good rating and ≥12 months remaining is a contract expansion candidate. (2) IDIQ Task Order Capture — review all IDIQ vehicles where company is an awardee (or sub-awardee); map uncaptured task orders against internal capability; build capture plan for each opportunity ≥$500K estimated value; maintain monthly task order pipeline tracker. Key tactic: position for next task order at program review meetings by demonstrating delivery excellence on current task orders — performance visibility is the differentiator on IDIQ vehicles. (3) Engineering Change Proposal (ECP) Strategy — proactively identify design deficiencies, obsolescence issues, or performance improvement opportunities within current deliverables; propose ECPs to government customer; ECPs generate engineering revenue and deepen customer relationship and dependency. ECP pipeline: maintain running log of potential ECPs per program with estimated value and customer benefit rationale; review monthly with VP Engineering. Penetration goal: existing customer programs should grow 5–10% annually through expansion, task order capture, and ECP revenue before committing resources to new customer development. Metrics: contract expansion rate, task order capture rate, ECP acceptance rate. Reporting: VP BD reviews penetration metrics monthly; VP Strategy integrates into balanced scorecard.
summary: System: Three-mechanism market penetration playbook (contract expansion, IDIQ task order capture, ECP proposals) for growing existing defense customer revenue 5–10% annually as primary near-term growth lever

label: Product development pipeline for adjacent defense capabilities
content: Stage-gated pipeline management for H2 adjacent capability development targeting existing defense customers with new products/services. Pipeline stages: (1) Idea Identification — mine SBIR solicitations, customer roadmap briefs, BAA postings, and BD conversations for unmet capability needs addressable with 2–3 year IRAD investment; target adjacencies where ≥60% of required capability exists internally. (2) Feasibility Assessment — for each candidate: TRL (current vs. required at customer transition), IRAD cost estimate to TRL-7, time-to-first-article, addressable market (minimum ≥$10M), customer pull signal (at least informal interest), and competitive landscape. (3) IRAD Investment Decision — VP Strategy scores feasibility; CEO/CIC approves funding: minimum $250K to reach TRL-5 for hardware adjacencies, $100K for software/services. (4) Development Milestones — stage-gated progression: concept (TRL 2–3), laboratory demonstration (TRL 4–5), prototype demonstration (TRL 6–7), customer demonstration (TRL 7–8); each gate reviewed by CTO and VP Strategy with continue/redirect/terminate decision. (5) Customer Transition — TRL ≥7 required before customer-funded transition; pursue SBIR Phase II or OTA prototype agreement to co-fund customer demonstration phase. Pipeline metrics: active adjacency count, investment by TRL stage, expected 3-year revenue contribution, conversion rate from idea to funded IRAD. Defense adjacency candidates: additive manufacturing for certified flight hardware, RF/EW subsystem integration, directed energy component manufacturing, autonomous system sensor payload integration, space-qualified structural manufacturing.
summary: System: Stage-gated H2 adjacent capability development pipeline with five stages (idea → feasibility → IRAD investment → milestone development → customer transition), TRL gates, and minimum $10M market size threshold

label: Market development assessment — FMS and direct commercial sale channels
content: Assessment framework for Ansoff market development into international defense markets via Foreign Military Sales (FMS) and Direct Commercial Sale (DCS) channels. FMS channel assessment: (1) Identify which current programs have allied nation FMS counterparts or equivalents per DSCA FMS case data and FYDP security cooperation accounts; (2) Determine ITAR license requirement and approval probability — Technology Control Plan complexity, third-country national restrictions, and end-use certificate requirements; (3) Evaluate whether prime contractor is pursuing allied nation FMS requiring sub-tier components — if yes, market development is prime-facilitated and lower cost-to-enter; (4) Assess DoD security cooperation priorities by region (Indo-Pacific, NATO, Middle East) against internal capability alignment. DCS channel assessment: (1) Identify allied nation defense procurement agencies with requirements matching current capabilities (UK DE&S, Australia CASG, Canada PSPC, Japan ATLA); (2) Evaluate export license complexity — ITAR/EAR classification, Technology Control Plan, DSP-5 filing timeline; (3) Assess in-country industrial cooperation requirements (offset obligations, workshare minimums); (4) Estimate market entry cost: BD travel, ITAR compliance infrastructure, in-country legal/agent costs, and prime qualification timeline. Go/no-go criteria: estimated addressable contract value ≥$5M/year for 5 years; ITAR license approval probability ≥80%; prime contractor endorsement (FMS) or confirmed allied nation customer contact (DCS); no FOCI disqualification risk; export compliance burden <3% of estimated contract value. Regulatory prerequisites: ITAR Part 122 registration; DSP-5 license prior to controlled technical data exchange; Export Compliance Officer approval for all international market development activities.
summary: System: International defense market development assessment covering FMS and DCS channels — evaluates ITAR license probability, prime alignment, market size, export compliance burden, and go/no-go criteria for allied nation entry

label: Diversification risk scoring for commercial aerospace entry
content: Risk scoring model for evaluating Ansoff diversification into commercial aerospace — highest-risk quadrant requiring structured assessment before capital commitment. Six risk dimensions scored 1–5 (higher = greater risk): (1) Capability gap — distance from current defense capabilities to commercial requirements (different certification regime: FAA/EASA Parts 21/25 vs. AS9100/MIL-STDs; different design standards; new DPD/model-based definition requirements); score 1 (minor adaptation) to 5 (fundamental requalification). (2) Market access difficulty — commercial Tier 1 prime qualification process 18–36 months; established supplier relationships required; Boeing/Airbus preferred supplier list penetration barriers; score by qualification timeline. (3) Capital requirement — tooling, facilities, certifications, quality system expansion, and working capital for first commercial article; score by investment as % of annual revenue (≤5%=1, >25%=5). (4) Margin timing risk — commercial pricing is more disciplined than defense (fixed-price, no CPFF fallback; production learning curve required for positive margin); score by time to first profitable production unit. (5) Customer concentration risk — commercial aerospace duopoly (Boeing/Airbus) creates dependency; score by achievable customer diversity in years 1–5. (6) Regulatory complexity — dual certification burden (FAA + AS9100 + EASA if applicable); ITAR still applies if military-origin technology embedded; score by number of new regulatory regimes required. Aggregate risk score: sum of six dimensions (6–30 scale); CIC thresholds: ≤18 = moderate risk, standard CIC approval; 19–24 = independent risk assessment required before approval; ≥25 = CEO + board-level approval with explicit risk acceptance statement. Minimum viability criteria: internal capability match ≥70%, commercial prime partner willing to co-invest in qualification, addressable market ≥$50M/year, and 3-year path to cash flow positive.
summary: System: Six-dimension risk scoring model (1–30 scale) for commercial aerospace diversification — capability gap, market access, capital, margin timing, customer concentration, and regulatory complexity — with CIC approval thresholds by score band

label: Annual portfolio rebalancing decision framework
content: Decision framework for annual portfolio rebalancing using BCG/GE 9-box scoring outputs. Decision sequence: (1) Review updated portfolio scoring for all active programs and top-10 pipeline opportunities. (2) Assess portfolio balance against target mix: ≥1 Star/Invest program, ≥2 Cash Cows sustaining operations, ≤3 Question Marks receiving active investment, 0 Dogs receiving new capital. (3) Identify rebalancing actions: B&P budget reallocation from Dogs to Stars, IRAD shift from saturated H1 to H2 capabilities, harvest decision on aging Cash Cows approaching program end. (4) Model rebalancing scenario: project revenue, margin, and backlog coverage at 1-year and 3-year horizon under current vs. rebalanced allocation; present side-by-side comparison. (5) Approve rebalancing directives: VP Strategy presents portfolio rebalancing memo to CEO/SMC with recommendations, financial model, and risk assessment; SMC votes on adoption. (6) Cascade directives: VP BD adjusts capture priority list, CFO reallocates B&P and IRAD budget lines, CTO adjusts technology investment priorities. Rebalancing cadence: annual in Q4 aligned with strategic planning cycle; mid-year portfolio health check in Q2. Off-cycle rebalancing triggers: major contract win/loss reclassifying ≥2 programs; government budget action affecting ≥20% of program backlog; competitor M&A changing competitive strength assessments for an entire market segment.
summary: Decision: Annual portfolio rebalancing using BCG/9-box outputs — target portfolio mix criteria, rebalancing scenario modeling, SMC approval, and cascade directives to VP BD, CFO, and CTO

label: Go/no-go criteria for new market entry
content: Structured go/no-go decision framework for Ansoff market development and diversification moves. Mandatory go criteria (all must be met): (1) Addressable contract value ≥$10M within 5 years (sourced from FYDP, FPDS-NG historical analysis, or independent market assessment); (2) Internal capability match score ≥65/100 on Three Cs company audit; (3) Identified customer champion — government PM, prime BD director, or commercial procurement contact — willing to provide letter of interest or informal endorsement; (4) No FOCI, ITAR, or regulatory disqualifier identified by Export Compliance and Legal review; (5) Required IRAD/B&P investment for market entry <15% of annual revenue (or CEO/board approval obtained for higher). Discriminating factors scored and weighted: strategic fit with Three Horizons plan (25%), competitive position in new market (25%), time-to-first-revenue ≤3 years for H2 entries (20%), risk-adjusted NPV positive at 5 years (20%), leadership bandwidth available for market entry execution (10%). Go decision threshold: all mandatory criteria met + discriminating score ≥70/100. No-go triggers: any mandatory criterion fails; diversification risk score ≥25 (per scoring model); market entry requires abandoning existing competitive advantage. Conditional go: mandatory criteria met but discriminating score 55–69 — proceed with 90-day validation study and revised go/no-go re-evaluation before committing capital. Decision authority: VP Strategy recommends; CEO approves investments ≤10% of annual revenue; CEO + board approve >10%.
summary: Decision: Five mandatory go criteria plus weighted discriminating score (≥70/100 threshold) for new market entry — covers market size, capability match, customer champion, regulatory clearance, and investment ceiling with conditional go protocol

label: Technology investment prioritization — Three Horizons and Ansoff intersection
content: Decision framework combining Three Horizons portfolio balance with Ansoff risk quadrant to prioritize technology investments. Intersection logic: H1 investments map to Ansoff Market Penetration (existing capabilities for existing customers) — optimize yield, lean, and quality; do not over-invest in new technology; H2 investments map to Ansoff Product Development (adjacent capabilities for existing customers) and Market Development (existing capabilities entering new markets — invest in customer qualification, not new capability build); H3 investments map to Ansoff Diversification (new capabilities for new markets — highest risk, require explicit strategic conviction and board visibility). Prioritization scoring per investment candidate: Horizon classification alignment (H1/H2/H3 per Three Horizons plan, 1–5), Ansoff risk calibration (1=penetration, 5=diversification — higher risk requires proportionally higher expected NPV), strategic leverage (does investment open multiple Ansoff pathways?), time-to-revenue (H1: ≤2yr, H2: 2–5yr, H3: 5–10yr), capital efficiency (rank by NPV per $1M invested given IRAD budget constraint). Decision authority by tier: H1 technology investments approved by VP Operations/CTO within existing capital budget; H2 investments require VP Strategy Three Horizons classification approval + CIC business case; H3 investments require CEO/SMC approval with annual board notification. Governance flag: H1 budget funding H3 bets signals strategy execution risk; H3 spending >15% of total IRAD budget without board sanction triggers mandatory SMC review. Annual deliverable: VP Strategy presents technology investment priority stack-ranked list to CEO/SMC with Three Horizons × Ansoff heat map.
summary: Decision: Technology investment prioritization using Three Horizons × Ansoff intersection matrix — maps H1/H2/H3 to Ansoff quadrants, scores by horizon alignment and capital efficiency, and sets approval authority tiers by investment classification

label: Strategic plan document template
content: Standard template structure for the annual enterprise strategic plan. Section 1 — Executive Summary (1 page): mission statement, 3-year strategic vision, top 3 strategic priorities, and key investment decisions for the plan year. Section 2 — Environmental Context (2–3 pages): PESTEL summary (top 3 issues per dimension), defense market outlook (FYDP budget trends, platform priorities), Porter Five Forces competitive pressure summary. Section 3 — Internal Assessment (2–3 pages): Three Cs company capability profile, balanced scorecard prior-year performance vs. targets, SWOT assessment with evidence citations. Section 4 — Portfolio Analysis (2–3 pages): BCG/GE 9-box program portfolio map with scoring summary, Three Horizons investment balance (H1/H2/H3 split vs. 70/20/10 target), Ansoff growth pathway selections with risk-return rationale and resource commitments. Section 5 — Strategic Objectives and Initiatives (3–4 pages): 3–5 strategic objectives with measurable 12-month goals, initiative list with VP owner, budget, and key milestones, cascade to division-level plans. Section 6 — Resource Plan (2 pages): CIC-reviewed capital investment priorities, IRAD and B&P budget allocation by program/pursuit, headcount plan by function. Section 7 — Risk Register (1–2 pages): top 10 enterprise strategic risks with likelihood, impact, mitigation owner, and current status. Appendices: full PESTEL analysis, complete 9-box scoring model, Ansoff analysis, Three Cs competitive positioning map. Distribution: CEO, all VPs, Board of Directors; classified per data sensitivity. Annual revision cycle: Q3 draft, Q1 final after SMC review and approval.
summary: Output: Seven-section annual strategic plan template covering environmental context, internal assessment, portfolio analysis, strategic objectives, resource plan, and risk register — standard format for CEO/SMC/board distribution

label: Annual strategy offsite facilitation guide
content: Facilitation guide for the 2-day annual executive strategy offsite driving strategic plan development. Pre-work (3–4 weeks before): VP Strategy distributes pre-read package — prior year balanced scorecard results, updated PESTEL brief, competitive intelligence summary, and BCG/9-box portfolio update; each VP completes a 2-page division strategic assessment. Day 1 Morning (4 hrs) — Environmental Scan: CEO opens with enterprise context; VP Strategy facilitates PESTEL review and competitive landscape discussion using Porter Five Forces; structured debate to identify top 3 external forces shaping 3-year strategy. Day 1 Afternoon (4 hrs) — Internal Assessment: SWOT-to-Action workshop (see facilitation guide #1392); Three Cs competitive positioning validation with VP BD input. Day 2 Morning (4 hrs) — Portfolio and Growth Strategy: BCG/GE 9-box portfolio scoring review with harvest/divest trigger evaluation; Ansoff growth pathway selection with go/no-go criteria application; Three Horizons investment rebalancing with H1/H2/H3 budget discussion. Day 2 Afternoon (4 hrs) — Strategic Objectives and Resource Allocation: develop or refine 3–5 strategic objectives; assign VP owners and resource requirements; identify cross-functional dependencies and sequencing conflicts; produce draft strategic priorities list. Post-offsite (2–4 weeks): VP Strategy drafts strategic plan document (see template); CEO/SMC review in two rounds; cascade to division plans upon finalization. Facilitation principles: enforce evidence-based arguments (no unsupported assertions); capture decisions and minority views in real-time; end each day with confirmed owner assignments. Output: strategic plan draft within 4 weeks of offsite completion.
summary: Output: 2-day strategy offsite facilitation guide with pre-work, day-by-day agenda (PESTEL → SWOT → portfolio → objectives), post-offsite drafting process, and facilitation principles for evidence-based executive deliberation

label: Market opportunity assessment one-pager
content: Standard one-page format for evaluating new pursuit candidates against VP Strategy analytical frameworks. Sections: (1) Opportunity Identification: program name, contract vehicle, estimated total and annual value, period of performance, NAICS code, customer (program office and/or prime). (2) Market Attractiveness: FYDP growth trend for this capability area (positive/flat/declining), number of qualified competitors, typical contract type, and historic margin range. (3) Competitive Position: Three Cs strength score (1–5) with key differentiators vs. known competitors; CPARS ratings for relevant past performance; certifications/qualifications in place vs. required. (4) Ansoff Quadrant: circle one — Market Penetration / Product Development / Market Development / Diversification; risk-return summary sentence. (5) Portfolio Alignment: Three Horizons classification (H1/H2/H3), BCG/9-box quadrant, and SWOT action strategy linkage (SO/WO/ST/WT). (6) Investment Required: estimated B&P cost, IRAD pre-investment needed, partnership or teaming required and availability. (7) Go/No-Go Assessment: mandatory criteria met? (Y/N per criterion listed), discriminating score (0–100), recommendation (Go/Conditional Go/No-Go) with rationale. (8) Next Steps: if Go — opportunity owner, target bid date, and 30-day action items. Format: single page, bullet format; completed by VP Strategy in collaboration with VP BD within 5 business days of opportunity identification. Reviewed at monthly pipeline review; scored results feed BCG/9-box portfolio update.
summary: Output: Single-page market opportunity assessment template integrating Ansoff quadrant, Three Cs score, Three Horizons classification, BCG/9-box placement, and go/no-go criteria — completed within 5 days of opportunity identification

label: Investment thesis memo format
content: Standard format for investment thesis memos supporting CIC and SMC capital allocation decisions. Sections: (1) Executive Summary (half page): investment request, total cost, strategic rationale in 2–3 sentences, and recommendation. (2) Strategic Context: BCG/GE 9-box placement of program or market being invested in; Three Horizons classification; Ansoff quadrant; alignment to named strategic objectives from current annual plan. (3) Investment Description: what specifically is being funded (IRAD project, B&P campaign, capital equipment, facility, capability build); use of funds by category; deployment timeline. (4) Financial Analysis: NPV (base case + bear/bull scenarios), IRR, payback period, revenue contribution at years 3 and 7, break-even analysis; sensitivity table at ±20% revenue and ±15% cost. (5) Strategic Value Beyond NPV: capability barrier created (competitor replication difficulty), customer relationship value (access to future programs), option value (additional future pathways opened), and risk reduction value (supply chain, clearance, or schedule dependency reduced). (6) Risk Assessment: top 3 risks with probability, impact, and mitigation plan; downside scenario analysis. (7) Alternatives Considered: minimum 2 alternatives with cost-benefit comparison and rejection rationale. (8) Decision Request: specific approval sought, authority level (CIC vs. SMC vs. CEO+Board), approval deadline, and conditions if conditional. Routing: VP Strategy drafts; CFO validates financial analysis; CEO/SMC approves per authority matrix. Retained in investment decision register for 5 years per record retention policy.
summary: Output: Eight-section investment thesis memo format with BCG/9-box context, NPV/IRR financial analysis, strategic value beyond NPV, risk assessment, alternatives comparison, and CIC/SMC approval routing — standard format for all major portfolio investment decisions

label: Defense Budget Cycle Tracking (PPBE / NDAA / CR)
content: Track DoD Planning, Programming, Budgeting, and Execution (PPBE) cycle to anticipate program funding timing. PPBE phases: Planning (NDS/NMS-driven force structure analysis, Feb–May) → Programming (POM development, FYDP submission, May–Sep) → Budgeting (OSD/OMB scrub, President's Budget release Feb) → Execution (Congressional appropriations and apportionment). Key monitoring signals: internal POM release (spring), President's Budget Request (February), NDAA enactment (December), and appropriations acts. Continuing Resolution (CR) impacts: funds government at prior-year rate; no new program starts authorized; multi-year procurement and LRIP quantities may be delayed or reduced; monitor customer programs for stop-work risk and maintain 90-day operating reserve. NDAA provisions to track annually: Section 8(a)/small business threshold changes, domestic content requirement phase-ins, ITAR reform provisions, CMMC implementation timeline mandates, and OTA authority expansions or restrictions. Monitoring cadence: quarterly PPBE signal brief to VP Strategy; CEO briefed on FYDP impacts to top-10 pipeline programs within 30 days of President's Budget release.
summary: System: Track PPBE cycle phases, President's Budget release, NDAA provisions, and CR impacts on defense program funding and new-start authority for pipeline program risk assessment

label: Regulatory Horizon Scanning (CMMC / ITAR / Buy American)
content: Track regulatory changes affecting contract eligibility, compliance burden, and competitive positioning. Key regulatory domains: (1) CMMC 2.0 — final rule implementation timeline; C3PAO assessment requirements for Level 2 contracts handling CUI; self-attestation thresholds for Level 1; track implementation deadline milestones and assess internal readiness gap against NIST SP 800-171 controls; companies not achieving required CMMC level lose eligibility for CUI-bearing contracts. (2) ITAR/EAR reform — monitor USML/CCL category revisions affecting product classification; State Dept rule changes for license exception eligibility with allied nation customers (Five Eyes, NATO); export compliance automation requirements; DSP-5/DSP-73 license pipeline monitoring. (3) Buy American Act and DFARS domestic content — track domestic content thresholds (currently 60%, phased to 75% by 2029 per FAR 52.225-1); DFARS specialty metal compliance (252.225-7009); implications for foreign-sourced supply chain components. (4) DOJ Civil Cyber-Fraud Initiative — False Claims Act liability for misrepresenting CMMC or cybersecurity compliance; monitor DOJ enforcement actions for compliance posture benchmarking. Scanning process: subscribe to Federal Register alerts for FAR/DFARS cases; monitor DAU acquisition resources and NDIA regulatory advocacy; quarterly regulatory horizon brief to VP Strategy; material changes briefed to CEO and General Counsel immediately.
summary: System: Monitor CMMC implementation timeline, ITAR/EAR category revisions, Buy American domestic content thresholds, and FCA cybersecurity enforcement to assess compliance burden and contract eligibility impacts

label: Technology Disruption Radar (AI/ML / Digital Twin / Autonomous / Directed Energy)
content: Systematic monitoring of technology disruptions affecting defense manufacturing competitiveness and customer platform requirements. Four disruption domains: (1) AI/ML — DoD AI Strategy implementation via CDAO; AI-enabled autonomy and target recognition driving edge compute and ruggedized AI accelerator hardware demand; track DARPA AI programs (AIM, SAIL-ON), CDAO data and AI governance policies, and prime contractor AI integration roadmaps for platform subsystem signal. (2) Digital Twin and Digital Engineering — DoD digital engineering mandate (MIL-HDBK-31032); prime contractor requirements for digital thread delivery from design through manufacturing; impact on TDP format and engineering data deliverable specifications; digital twin for sustainment reducing legacy MRO demand for some platforms. (3) Autonomous Systems — Group III/IV SUAS, MUMT, and autonomous logistics platforms driving demand for flight computers, sensor fusion units, PNT-denied navigation hardware, and AI inference hardware; track PEO programs (PEO Aviation, PEO GCS) for autonomous platform procurement signals. (4) Directed Energy — HELIOS, DRACO, and integrated directed energy programs; thermal management components, high-power electronics, and precision beam control subsystems creating emerging sub-tier supply chain demand; track AFRL and ARL directed energy program activity. Radar output: quarterly technology disruption brief with threat/opportunity classification per domain, internal IRAD alignment assessment, and recommended H2/H3 portfolio adjustments.
summary: System: Quarterly monitoring of AI/ML, digital engineering, autonomous systems, and directed energy technology disruptions — assess threat to existing capabilities and opportunity for IRAD/H2 portfolio alignment

label: Geopolitical Risk Assessment for Supply Chain (Rare Earth / Allied Sourcing)
content: Assess geopolitical risks to critical material supply chains affecting production continuity and program eligibility. Critical dependency domains: (1) Rare Earth Elements (REEs) — China controls ~60% of REE mining and ~85% of processing; REEs essential for permanent magnets (motors, actuators), EO/IR sensors, and radar systems; Section 232 and NDAA restrictions on REE sourcing; track DoD Critical Minerals strategy and domestic mining investment (MP Materials, Energy Fuels, Lynas); assess 90-day stockpile sufficiency for each REE-dependent product line. (2) Specialty alloys and metals — titanium (Ukraine/Russia supply disruption risk), beryllium (Materion near-monopoly risk), tungsten (China concentration); monitor DFARS specialty metal compliance (252.225-7009) and qualify domestic or allied-nation alternates proactively. (3) Semiconductor supply chain — fab concentration in Taiwan (TSMC); CHIPS Act domestic fab investment timeline; lead times for radiation-hardened and military-grade components; track DLA Critical Items list for component availability signals. (4) Allied nation sourcing — Five Eyes and NATO partners eligible for ITAR waivers and domestic content substitution; FMS supply chain integration creating allied-nation supplier qualification opportunities; monitor FOCI regulations affecting foreign-owned domestic suppliers. Risk mitigation: maintain approved domestic/allied-nation alternate supplier for each critical material; assess stockpile requirements for 90-day production continuity; track DoD DPA Title III investments in domestic industrial base capacity. Quarterly geopolitical supply chain risk brief with red/yellow/green status by material category; escalate supply chain disruption risk immediately to VP Operations and CEO.
summary: System: Quarterly assessment of rare earth dependency, specialty alloy concentration, semiconductor supply risk, and allied nation sourcing eligibility — with 90-day stockpile protocol and alternate supplier qualification tracking

label: Defense Manufacturing Value Chain Map (Design → Sustainment)
content: End-to-end defense manufacturing value chain framework mapping cost, margin, and risk profile at each stage. Stages: (1) Design/Requirements — systems engineering, TDP development, CONOPS translation; primarily NRE labor; margin potential from IP ownership and proprietary design data rights. (2) Prototype/Development — first article builds, test articles, qualification units; high unit cost, high ECO rate; cost-reimbursable contracts typical; margin risk from underestimated complexity and design churn. (3) Qualification — DT&E/OT&E support, configuration baseline lock, MRL assessment, NADCAP process qualification; one-time qualification cost amortized over production quantity; sole-source position solidified at this stage. (4) LRIP (Low Rate Initial Production) — initial production units, learning curve descent, tooling and fixture investment, supply chain qualification; fixed-price contracts typical; margin risk from learning curve shortfall (target 85–90% curve rate) and supplier qualification delays. (5) FRP (Full Rate Production) — stable processes, mature supply chain, maximum efficiency; highest sustained margin stage when yield is capable (Ppk ≥1.33); margin optimized through indirect rate discipline and supplier LTA cost reduction. (6) Sustainment — spares, repairs, upgrades, obsolescence management; long-duration predictable revenue; margin ranges 20–35% on spares, 12–22% on depot repairs. Map to company program portfolio: plot each active program against its current stage quarterly; identify programs transitioning stages in next 12 months; adjust resource allocation and margin expectations accordingly. Value chain map maintained as a living exhibit updated at each annual strategic planning cycle.
summary: System: Program-level value chain map from design through sustainment — cost/margin profile by stage, stage transition tracking, and portfolio-level value chain position visualization

label: Margin Analysis by Value Chain Stage
content: Quantitative margin analysis process for each defense manufacturing value chain stage. Margin benchmarks by stage (defense sub-tier manufacturing): Design/NRE — target 8–12% if IP owned; risk of erosion from scope creep without contract modification. Prototype/Development — cost-reimbursable; target fee 8–12%; actual margin driven by BOE accuracy; track actuals vs. basis of estimate monthly. Qualification — NRE-intensive; margin depends on qualification cost recovery; watch for under-recovery on government-directed test changes not covered by contract mod. LRIP — fixed-price pressure; target gross margin 15–20%; first-unit cost typically 30–50% above FRP estimate; track learning curve rate (target 85–90% for aerospace manufacturing assemblies). FRP — target gross margin 18–25% for high-complexity defense sub-assemblies; margin maximized through yield improvement, indirect rate discipline, and supplier LTA renegotiation. Sustainment/Spares — target gross margin 20–35% (higher due to low-volume premium and urgency); depot/repair 12–22% depending on repair complexity and competition. Margin diagnostic process: quarterly margin waterfall by program and value chain stage; trace margin gaps to specific activities (yield loss, rework rate, B&P over-spend, indirect rate creep); year-over-year margin trend per stage; flag any stage delivering <10% gross margin for two consecutive quarters as a restructuring trigger. Report to VP Strategy, CFO, and CEO at quarterly SMC review.
summary: System: Quarterly margin analysis by value chain stage — benchmark comparisons, waterfall gap identification by activity, learning curve tracking, and escalation triggers for underperforming stages

label: Outsource vs. Insource Decision Matrix per Value Chain Activity
content: Structured make-vs-buy decision matrix applied per value chain activity. Decision criteria scored 1–5: Retain (insource) factors — (1) Strategic importance: is this activity a source of competitive differentiation (proprietary process, sole-source position, unique NADCAP scope)? (2) Core competency alignment: does the activity leverage the company's primary skill base? (3) IP and data sensitivity: does the activity involve proprietary designs or controlled technical data creating IP risk if outsourced? Outsource factors — (4) Cost differential: is external market rate ≥15% below fully-loaded internal cost including overhead burden? (5) Utilization efficiency: is internal utilization <50% for this activity, making fixed-cost absorption inefficient? Constraint check — (6) Regulatory restriction: DFARS specialty metal, ITAR, or customer-approved supplier requirements may restrict or prohibit outsourcing regardless of economics. Decision rule: activities scoring ≥12 on Retain criteria (1–3) AND no regulatory restriction → insource; activities scoring <9 on Retain AND ≥10 on Outsource with no restriction → outsource candidate; mixed scores → evaluate hybrid (outsource non-CUI/non-IP portions). Mandatory compliance: apply FAR 15.407-2 make-or-buy program requirements for contracts >$15M. Defense-specific caveat: never outsource activities creating single-point-of-failure dependencies on critical program paths. Decision register maintained by VP Strategy and VP Operations; reviewed annually; major changes require CIC business case and CEO approval.
summary: System: Scored make-vs-buy decision matrix per value chain activity — six-criterion scoring (strategic importance, core competency, IP risk, cost differential, utilization, regulatory constraint) with FAR 15.407-2 compliance and annual register review

label: Strategy Canvas Value Curve Mapping (Current vs. Desired)
content: Quantitative value curve mapping tool for current-state and target-state Blue Ocean positioning. Process: (1) Define 6–10 competitive factors for the target defense segment — e.g., price competitiveness, delivery speed, qualification breadth, cleared facility depth, NADCAP process range, engineering co-development support, digital data delivery, design-for-manufacturability responsiveness, co-investment appetite, CPARS past performance ratings. (2) Score each factor on a 1–5 offering level scale for: company (current), top 2–3 competitors (from BD win/loss data and FPDS-NG), and ideal target customer need (from BD customer interviews and RFI/BAA signals). (3) Plot value curves on canvas with factors on x-axis, offering level on y-axis. (4) Identify convergence zones — factors where all competitors score similarly signal red ocean competition; divergence zones signal differentiation opportunity. (5) Map current curve vs. desired target curve: target curve reflects ERRC grid output — Raised and Created factors increase on target curve, Eliminated and Reduced factors decrease. (6) Validate target curve against customer need data: target offering level for each factor must align with customer-stated priority, not internal assumptions. Output: annotated canvas diagram with current company curve, competitor curves, and proposed target future curve with ERRC action labels. Update semi-annually to track competitive convergence or divergence trends.
summary: System: Semi-annual Strategy Canvas scoring exercise — plot company and competitor value curves across 6–10 defense market factors, identify red ocean convergence zones, and map desired future curve from ERRC grid output

label: Uncontested Defense Market Space Identification (MRO / Digital Services / Training Systems)
content: Framework for identifying and validating blue ocean market spaces in defense with low incumbent competition. Three prioritized uncontested space categories for defense manufacturing: (1) MRO for Legacy Platforms — aging aircraft, ship systems, and ground vehicles where OEM has exited the spares supply chain; no competitive supplier exists; government paying sole-source premium for critical spares; entry strategy: obtain legacy TDPs via government-furnished data or FOIA, qualify as new approved source, establish FFP spare parts contract through DLA or direct program office. (2) Digital Services for Physical Products — defense hardware manufacturers that do not offer digital twin data delivery, predictive maintenance analytics, or lifecycle data management as a service; DoD digital engineering mandate creates demand for digital deliverables bundled with hardware; differentiate by offering PLM data packages, configuration status accounting, and digital thread services as an add-on tier; entry strategy: invest in PLM/MBE tooling, market digital data delivery as a contract line item on next proposal cycle. (3) Training System Components — hardware simulators, sub-scale replicas, instrumented targets, and emulator hardware for live-fire and virtual training programs; DoD training systems market ~$12B/year; prime training system integrators dominate full systems but sub-tier manufacturing for training hardware is fragmented; entry via SBIR, OTA, or existing customer introduction. Niche validation criteria: TAM >$50M addressable within 5 years (from FPDS-NG and FYDP analysis), ≤2 established incumbents, internal capability match ≥60%, accessible via SBIR, OTA, or existing customer relationship. Assess and update annually.
summary: System: Annual identification and validation of uncontested defense market niches — MRO for legacy platforms, digital services bundled with hardware, and training system components — with TAM, capability match, and competitive density criteria

label: H1 Core Program Optimization Reviews
content: Structured quarterly reviews for Horizon 1 core business — protecting and extending existing program revenue and margin. Review cadence: quarterly, led by VP Strategy with VP Operations, CFO, and program PMs. Review elements: (1) Program financial health — CPI/SPI by program, cost-to-complete vs. remaining funding, gross margin actuals vs. plan; flag any program with CPI <0.95 for formal corrective action. (2) Re-compete pipeline — identify programs with base period of performance expiring in 12–18 months; assess re-compete risk rating (incumbent advantage, customer satisfaction score, competitive threat intelligence); initiate capture planning 18 months before re-compete expiry. (3) Add-on opportunity tracking — identify in-scope technical growth, engineering services, spares orders, or sustainment tasks on existing contracts; VP BD initiates scope growth discussions at quarterly review. (4) Manufacturing efficiency improvement — track yield improvement and rework reduction progress per program; validate that lean and Six Sigma initiatives are generating planned margin improvement; report OTD actuals vs. 95% target. (5) H1 growth metrics — measure 5–8% annual core revenue growth target; identify programs at risk of program cut, reduced quantities, or competitive re-procurement. Output: H1 health scorecard (green/yellow/red by program), top-5 optimization actions with owners and 90-day milestones, re-compete risk register update, and quarterly H1 report distributed to CEO and CFO.
summary: System: Quarterly H1 program health reviews — CPI/SPI tracking, re-compete pipeline risk assessment, add-on opportunity identification, manufacturing efficiency improvement tracking, and 5–8% core revenue growth monitoring

label: H2 Emerging Capability Investment Tracking (IRAD / CRADA / OTA)
content: Tracking system for Horizon 2 emerging capability investments across IRAD, CRADAs, and OTA mechanisms. Investment register: live register of all H2 investments with — capability area, investment mechanism (IRAD internal funding, CRADA partner agreement, OTA study contract, SBIR Phase II), current TRL, target TRL at period end, capital committed ($), projected revenue opportunity at years 3 and 5 ($M), accountable VP owner, and next milestone date. IRAD tracking: quarterly IRAD review with CTO — assess TRL progress, validate customer interest signals (LOI, RFI response, BAA submission), adjust investment rate based on milestone achievement; IRAD portfolio target 2–4% of revenue with H2 share at ~20% of IRAD budget. CRADA tracking: monitor cooperative research agreement milestones with university or FFRDC partners; assess IP ownership provisions at initiation (not retroactively); track transition pathway to H2→H1 graduation. OTA tracking: monitor Other Transaction Authority study and prototype contract milestones (10 U.S.C. 4022); assess follow-on production OTA eligibility criteria; track cost-share commitments and government matching investment. Investment gate reviews: semi-annual review of all H2 investments with VP Strategy and CEO; investments with no customer interest signal after 24 months are nominated for termination or H2→H3 regression; investments achieving TRL ≥7 plus signed customer LOI are nominated for H2→H1 graduation. Target: 15–25% of projected year-5 revenue attributable to active H2 pipeline.
summary: System: Live H2 investment register across IRAD, CRADA, and OTA mechanisms — quarterly TRL and customer signal reviews, semi-annual investment gates, and H2→H1 graduation nomination process with 15–25% year-5 revenue target

label: H3 Future Bet Portfolio with Stage-Gate Funding
content: Stage-gate funding model for Horizon 3 exploratory bets. Portfolio structure: maintain 3–5 active H3 bets; each bet is a long-horizon capability or market opportunity requiring 5–10 year investment before revenue. Funding: H3 funded primarily through IRAD (internal R&D); supplemented by SBIR Phase I/II, university CRADAs, and DARPA/AFWERX/DIU exploratory agreements for external validation and non-dilutive funding. Stage-gate model: Gate 0 (Bet Initiation) — CEO/VP Strategy approve new H3 bet; criteria: aligns with NDS priority area, TAM ≥$100M at year 10, seed capability exists (TRL 1–3), annual cost <$200K for first 2 years. Gate 1 (Concept Validation, year 1–2) — TRL 2–3 demonstrated, at least one external engagement (SBIR Phase I award, DARPA BAA submission, customer meeting with documented interest); failure to pass Gate 1 → terminate and reallocate capital. Gate 2 (Technology Demonstration, year 2–4) — TRL 4–5, external funding obtained (SBIR Phase II, OTA study, or customer-funded study); achievement promotes bet to H2 classification. Gate 3 (Capability Maturation, H2 phase) — TRL 6–7, signed LOI or definitive teaming agreement; promotes to H1. Portfolio governance: annual review at board strategy session; terminate underperforming bets without delay; reallocate capital to bets showing external validation. Every active H3 bet must have a named VP owner and defined annual investment — no unfunded wish list items in the H3 register.
summary: System: Stage-gate funding model for 3–5 active H3 future bets — four gates (initiation, concept validation, technology demonstration, H2 promotion) with TRL and external funding criteria, annual board review, and mandatory VP ownership

label: PESTEL Scenario Planning Trigger Decisions
content: Decision framework for when PESTEL signals trigger formal strategy review or plan adjustment. Trigger thresholds requiring VP Strategy decision and CEO notification: (1) Defense budget cut >5% vs. prior year enacted (sequestration or continuing resolution extended >6 months) — trigger full strategic plan stress test; activate contingency plan for affected pipeline programs; brief CEO within 30 days of budget confirmation. (2) New NDAA provision materially affecting contract eligibility (CMMC mandate acceleration, domestic content threshold increase, set-aside threshold change) — trigger compliance readiness assessment; estimate cost and timeline; determine if non-compliance disqualifies pipeline opportunities valued >$5M; CEO decision on investment to comply. (3) Technology disruption in a PESTEL-T monitored domain reaching TRL 7+ where company has active H2 investment — trigger acceleration decision: increase IRAD, pursue OTA prototype, or escalate SBIR Phase II; if disruption makes an existing capability obsolete, trigger H2 pivot or wind-down. (4) Geopolitical event triggering supply chain disruption risk for a critical material on active programs — trigger alternate supplier qualification sprint; activate 90-day stockpile protocol for affected materials. Decision authority: VP Strategy recommends action classification (monitor / prepare / execute); CEO approves plan-adjustment decisions and budget reallocations. All trigger events and decisions documented in quarterly PESTEL brief action log with resolution status.
summary: Decision: Thresholds for PESTEL signals triggering formal strategy review — defense budget cut >5%, material NDAA eligibility changes, TRL-7 technology disruptions, and supply chain geopolitical events — with VP Strategy recommendation and CEO approval authority

label: Value Chain Restructuring Decisions
content: Decision criteria for restructuring value chain activities — insourcing, outsourcing, automating, or exiting a stage. Decision trigger conditions: (1) Gross margin at a value chain stage below threshold (15% for LRIP, 18% for FRP) for two consecutive quarters — investigate root cause; options: process improvement via lean/Six Sigma, automation capital investment (CIC business case required), outsource the activity, or exit if strategically non-essential; VP Strategy and VP Operations jointly recommend; CEO approves. (2) Outsource/insource decision matrix identifies >15% cost savings after fully-loaded transaction costs — initiate formal make-vs-buy analysis per FAR 15.407-2 for applicable contracts; VP Operations and VP Strategy jointly recommend; CEO approves restructuring; legal/contracts review for subcontracting plan and consent-to-subcontract requirements. (3) New program award requiring a value chain capability not currently in-house — insourcing decision: assess build/buy/partner options; CIC business case required for equipment >$50K; subcontract bridge acceptable while internal capability matures. (4) Qualification scope change affecting a value chain stage (NADCAP scope addition/removal, customer QPL change) — assess strategic impact on contract eligibility for pipeline programs valued >$2M annually; treat as a strategic decision requiring VP Strategy sign-off, not a compliance administrative action. Restructuring decision requirements: value chain impact assessment (upstream/downstream effects), workforce impact, AS9100 change notification requirements, and 3-year cost-benefit analysis with payback calculation. VP Strategy documents decision rationale in value chain restructuring log; reviewed at annual strategic planning cycle.
summary: Decision: Triggers and authority matrix for value chain restructuring — margin-below-threshold, outsource cost savings >15%, new capability insourcing, and qualification scope changes — with VP Strategy/VP Operations joint recommendation and CEO approval

label: Blue Ocean Pivot-or-Persist Decisions
content: Decision framework for evaluating whether to pivot, persist, or exit a Blue Ocean market creation initiative. Decision cadence: annual review of each active Blue Ocean initiative at 12-month and 24-month milestones. Persist criteria (continue investment): (1) at least one qualified customer has expressed formal interest (LOI, SBIR topic identification, RFI response solicited, OTA study engagement); (2) competitive response has not closed the differentiation gap within 18 months — competitors have not replicated Raised/Created factors on the Strategy Canvas; (3) revenue opportunity estimate remains ≥$10M over 5 years per updated FPDS-NG and BD analysis; (4) internal capability build is on track vs. plan (TRL advancing on schedule, workforce skills developing, required certifications progressing). Pivot criteria (change approach): (1) no qualified customer interest signal after 12 months of active market engagement; (2) competitors have replicated key differentiators, Strategy Canvas curves have converged, eliminating the uncontested space; (3) addressable market estimate revised below $10M threshold; (4) internal capability gap requires >3× original capital estimate to close. Exit criteria: pivot reformulation finds no viable alternative positioning; investment required exceeds board-approved H2 allocation; VP Strategy and CEO joint exit decision with lessons-learned documentation. Decision documentation: pivot/persist/exit brief per initiative at annual strategy session; persists continue with updated milestones; pivots require revised Strategy Canvas showing new positioning hypothesis and updated TAM estimate.
summary: Decision: Annual persist/pivot/exit framework for Blue Ocean initiatives — four persist criteria (customer interest, differentiation durability, TAM threshold, capability progress) and four pivot criteria, with VP Strategy and CEO joint authority on exits

label: Horizon Rebalancing Investment Decisions
content: Decision framework for rebalancing Three Horizons investment portfolio when horizon mix drifts from strategic targets. Target portfolio balance: 70% of innovation/IRAD budget to H1 optimization, 20% to H2 emerging capabilities, 10% to H3 future bets. Rebalancing triggers requiring VP Strategy recommendation and CEO decision: (1) H1 >80% of IRAD budget for two consecutive years — over-investment in sustaining existing business with insufficient renewal; VP Strategy identifies 1–2 H2 bets to activate or accelerate; reallocate 5–10% of IRAD from H1 to H2; brief board at next strategy session. (2) H2 <10% of IRAD budget — insufficient emerging capability pipeline; risk of strategic decay as H1 programs mature; initiate H2 opportunity identification sprint (Strategy Canvas + ERRC workshop within 60 days). (3) H3 = 0 active bets — no long-horizon renewal options; board notification required; CEO decision to initiate H3 portfolio seeding with 2 new bets within one planning cycle. (4) H2 bet achieves graduation criteria (customer LOI + TRL ≥7) — promote to H1; backfill H2 slot with the highest-scored H3 candidate; trigger CIC investment review for H2→H1 production capability build (equipment, tooling, workforce). Decision authority: VP Strategy recommends rebalancing action; CEO approves annual rebalancing; CFO approves IRAD budget reallocations; board notified when H2+H3 total exceeds 30% or falls below 15% of IRAD budget. Annual horizon balance review at board strategy session; mid-year checkpoint at SMC.
summary: Decision: Rebalancing triggers and authority matrix for Three Horizons investment portfolio — H1 >80% or H2 <10% of IRAD triggers reallocations; H3 = 0 triggers board notification; H2 graduation triggers H1 investment and H3 backfill

label: PESTEL Quarterly Brief
content: Quarterly PESTEL intelligence brief prepared by VP Strategy for CEO and executive team distribution. Format: Executive Summary (1 page) — top 3 PESTEL signals with strategic implications and recommended actions; open items from prior quarter with resolution status. Six-section body (1–2 pages each): Political — Congressional program status for top-10 pipeline programs, NDAA enactment summary, CR risk assessment and program stop-work exposure; Economic — FYDP enacted vs. submitted comparison, inflation impact on fixed-price contract margins, industrial base consolidation and supply chain developments; Social — cleared workforce availability trend by region, STEM pipeline health, veteran hiring program utilization; Technological — technology disruption radar status per domain (AI/ML, digital twin, autonomous systems, directed energy) with TRL milestone updates and IRAD alignment implications; Environmental — DoD sustainability mandate compliance status, hazardous material regulatory changes; Legal — CMMC implementation milestone update, ITAR/EAR category revisions, FCA enforcement monitoring. Appendix: PESTEL scenario matrix (2×2 grid of most likely vs. most impactful macro combinations) with strategic risk and opportunity ratings. Preparation sources: defense budget monitoring system, Federal Register alerts for FAR/DFARS cases, BD win/loss intelligence, Avascent and Deltek GovWin industry reports. Distribution: CEO, CFO, VP BD, CTO, COO; provided to board at quarterly SMC meeting. Preparation deadline: 10 business days after quarter close.
summary: Output: Quarterly 10–15 page PESTEL intelligence brief with 6-section macro analysis, top-3 strategic signals, action log, and scenario matrix — distributed to CEO and executive team within 10 days of quarter close

label: Value Chain Optimization Report
content: Annual value chain optimization report prepared by VP Strategy with input from VP Operations and CFO. Report sections: (1) Value Chain Map Update — revised swimlane diagram showing company position in each stage for each major program line; highlight stage transitions anticipated in next 12 months (e.g., LRIP→FRP, development→qualification). (2) Margin Analysis by Stage — waterfall chart showing gross margin contribution by value chain activity; year-over-year comparison; identify stages where margin improved or eroded vs. plan and root cause. (3) Outsource vs. Insource Recommendations — updated make-buy register with 1–3 outsourcing or insourcing recommendations for the coming year, each with supporting make-vs-buy analysis (cost differential, strategic rationale, regulatory compliance check, workforce and customer notification implications). (4) Value Chain Investment Priorities — top-3 investment recommendations for value chain capability improvement (process automation, certification scope expansion, digital tooling, supplier LTA renegotiation) with ROI estimates and payback period; routed to CIC for capital approval. (5) Competitive Value Chain Benchmark — company value chain efficiency metrics vs. defense industry benchmarks (direct labor efficiency, first-pass yield, B&P/revenue ratio, indirect cost rate); identify performance gaps requiring corrective investment. Format: 15–20 page report with 1-page executive summary; presented at annual strategic planning session (Q3); reviewed by CEO and CFO; operational recommendations assigned to VP Operations with 90-day milestones.
summary: Output: Annual 15–20 page value chain optimization report — updated stage map, margin waterfall, outsource/insource recommendations, investment priorities for CIC review, and competitive benchmark analysis

label: Blue Ocean Opportunity Assessment
content: Annual Blue Ocean Opportunity Assessment brief prepared by VP Strategy for CEO and VP BD review. Content sections: (1) Strategy Canvas Update — current value curves for company and top 3 competitors across all competitive factors; highlight changes from prior year canvas showing competitive convergence or divergence trends. (2) ERRC Grid Summary — completed ERRC grid for primary target segment; proposed actions per category with implementation cost/benefit estimates and accountable owner. (3) Active Initiative Assessments — for each active Blue Ocean initiative: market entry progress vs. plan, revenue realized or under contract, competitive response monitoring (has the uncontested space been entered by competitors?), and pivot/persist/exit recommendation with criteria assessment. (4) New Niche Candidates — 2–3 newly identified uncontested spaces from annual analysis (MRO, digital services, training systems, or other emerging gaps); preliminary TAM estimate, internal capability readiness score, and recommended next step (SBIR submission, OTA inquiry, customer engagement, or IRAD seed investment). (5) Blue Ocean Portfolio Health — investment summary across all active initiatives ($, IRAD % of budget); horizon classification (H2 or H3 per initiative); ROI tracking vs. initial investment thesis at 12-month intervals. Format: 10–15 page brief with annotated Strategy Canvas diagram and completed ERRC grid as exhibits; presented at annual strategy session; provided to board if active initiatives represent >10% of H2 investment. Distribution: CEO, VP BD, VP Strategy; CTO for technology-intensive niche candidates.
summary: Output: Annual 10–15 page Blue Ocean Opportunity Assessment — Strategy Canvas update, ERRC grid, active initiative persist/pivot/exit assessments, new niche candidates with TAM validation, and portfolio health summary

label: Three Horizons Board Presentation
content: Annual Three Horizons board presentation prepared by VP Strategy for board strategy session. Presentation structure: (1) Portfolio Overview (3 slides) — current H1/H2/H3 investment split ($ and % of IRAD budget) vs. 70/20/10 target; portfolio health trend vs. prior year; summary assessment: balanced, H1-heavy, or H2/H3-deficient. (2) H1 Performance Review (4 slides) — core business revenue and operating margin vs. target; re-compete pipeline status with risk ratings; top-3 H1 optimization achievements and shortfalls; projected H1 revenue at year 3. (3) H2 Emerging Capability Update (4 slides) — active H2 initiatives with investment level, TRL progress, customer validation signals, and projected year-5 revenue contribution per initiative; H2 graduation queue listing initiatives approaching H1 threshold (TRL ≥7, LOI in hand). (4) H3 Future Bet Status (3 slides) — active H3 bets with stage-gate position, external funding obtained (SBIR/OTA/CRADA value), and graduation timeline; terminated bets in prior year with lessons learned and capital recovered. (5) Rebalancing Recommendation (2 slides) — VP Strategy recommendation on investment allocation adjustments for next FY; specific H2 bets to initiate, accelerate, or terminate; H3 bets to seed or graduate. (6) 5-Year Horizon Projection (2 slides) — projected revenue and margin contribution by horizon at year 5; scenario comparison: recommended rebalancing vs. status quo continuation. Appendix: detailed IRAD investment ledger by initiative; H2/H3 investment register with milestone status. Format: 20–25 slide deck per board presentation standards with 2-page pre-read executive summary. Cadence: annual board strategy session; mid-year horizon health check presented at SMC.
summary: Output: Annual 20–25 slide Three Horizons board presentation — portfolio balance assessment, H1/H2/H3 performance review, rebalancing recommendation, 5-year horizon projection, and IRAD investment ledger appendix

label: Facilitated SWOT Workshop Methodology
content:
Structured methodology for facilitating SWOT-to-Action workshops in defense manufacturing. Pre-work surveys (2 weeks prior): distribute structured questionnaire to all invited participants covering four prompted sections — perceived strengths by capability area (certifications, past performance, workforce depth), observed weaknesses by functional area (gap analysis, audit findings, competitor comparisons), opportunity signals by market/customer/regulatory dimension (SAM.gov, SBIR, FYDP trends), and threat indicators (competitor actions, budget risk, regulatory shifts). Require at least one evidence citation per item; responses without citations are flagged for evidence gate enforcement at session start. Pre-work survey response-rate gate: workshop proceeds only if ≥70% of invited participants submit surveys.

Cross-functional participant design: Core team (required) — VP Strategy (facilitator), VP BD (opportunity/threat intelligence), VP Engineering (technical strength/weakness calibration), VP Operations (operational weakness ground-truth), CFO (financial strength/risk data). Subject matter experts (invited) — senior PM with current government customer contact (opportunity/threat validation), Quality Manager (performance data for strengths/weaknesses), Contracts Lead (competitive intelligence for threats). Maximum 10 participants; larger groups fragment into breakout pairs.

Time-boxed session design: 30-min kickoff (problem statement framing, ground rules, evidence gate enforcement — any item without cited data source is immediately discarded); 90-min SWOT generation (facilitated round-robin by quadrant, facilitator enforces evidence gate and consolidates duplicates); 30-min break + facilitator consolidation of raw items into top candidates; 60-min priority ranking (dot voting + facilitated discussion, select top 5 per quadrant by strategic magnitude); 90-min action intersection matrix construction (see SO/WO/ST/WT matrix neuron); 30-min output review and owner assignment. Total: one full day including breaks.
summary: System: Facilitated SWOT workshop methodology — pre-work surveys with evidence gates, cross-functional participant design, and time-boxed session structure for defense/aerospace manufacturing

label: SO/WO/ST/WT Action Matrix Generation Process
content:
Systematic process for constructing the four-quadrant SWOT action intersection matrix from ranked SWOT items. Input: top-5 priority-ranked items per quadrant from workshop voting phase.

Matrix construction rules by quadrant: (1) SO Strategies (Strengths × Opportunities) — for each top-3 Strength × top-3 Opportunity combination (9 intersections), ask 'How can this strength be deployed to capture this opportunity?'; record if a concrete mechanism exists, discard if no logical link. SO strategies become growth and market entry initiatives. (2) WO Strategies (Weaknesses × Opportunities) — for each top-3 Weakness × top-3 Opportunity pair, ask 'What investment or change in this weakness would unlock this opportunity?'; these typically map to H2/H3 portfolio investments requiring capital or capability build. (3) ST Strategies (Strengths × Threats) — for each top-3 Strength × top-3 Threat pair, ask 'How does this strength hedge, mitigate, or neutralize this threat?'; ST strategies become defensive positioning and risk-buffering actions. (4) WT Strategies (Weaknesses × Threats) — for each top-3 Weakness × top-3 Threat pair, ask 'What minimum action prevents this weakness from amplifying this threat?'; WT strategies are risk mitigation priorities with defensive urgency.

Facilitation rules: each intersection gets max 5 minutes in session; facilitator captures strategy hypothesis in ≤2 sentences on a sticky note or matrix cell; ambiguous intersections marked for offline follow-up rather than debated live. Identical or overlapping action items from multiple intersections are consolidated into one strategy entry. Final matrix output: up to 36 candidate strategy hypotheses reduced to ≤20 distinct strategies entering the scoring and prioritization step. Matrix is documented as a 4×3 grid (4 quadrant types × top-3 items per axis) with strategy hypotheses in each cell.
summary: System: SO/WO/ST/WT action intersection matrix construction — combinatorial pairing rules, facilitation protocol, and consolidation logic for generating ≤20 distinct strategic hypotheses from ranked SWOT items

label: SWOT-to-Initiative Prioritization Scoring
content:
Three-dimension scoring model for prioritizing SWOT-derived strategies into executable initiative register. Scoring dimensions:

(1) Impact (1–5): estimated strategic value — revenue growth or risk reduction. 5 = >$5M revenue potential or critical Tier 1 threat neutralized; 4 = $2–5M or significant risk reduction; 3 = $500K–$2M or moderate risk addressed; 2 = <$500K or minor benefit; 1 = minimal measurable impact. Assess using TAM/SAM market size data, program pipeline values, or quantified threat cost exposure.

(2) Feasibility (1–5): internal capability match and resource availability. 5 = existing capability, no new investment required, can execute within 30 days; 4 = minor gap, <$250K investment or <90 days to close; 3 = moderate gap, $250K–$1M or 6–12 months; 2 = significant gap, >$1M or 12–18 months; 1 = fundamental capability missing, multi-year commitment required.

(3) Urgency (1–5): time-sensitivity of action window — how fast does the opportunity close or threat materialize? 5 = window closes within 90 days (imminent RFP drop, competitor entering market now); 4 = 91–180 days; 3 = 6–12 months; 2 = 12–24 months; 1 = >24 months, no near-term deadline or trigger.

Composite score: Impact × Feasibility × Urgency (max 125). Threshold rules: ≥75 → include in next strategic cycle initiative register with assigned VP owner; 40–74 → secondary initiative pending resource availability; <40 → park in watch list, revisit at next annual SWOT refresh. Tie-breaking: equal composite scores → higher Urgency rank takes priority (first-mover advantage in defense procurement). Scoring is performed by VP Strategy using workshop inputs; VP owners may challenge scores with supporting evidence within 5 business days of workshop close.
summary: System: SWOT-to-initiative prioritization scoring using Impact × Feasibility × Urgency (max 125) — threshold rules, tie-breaking logic, and initiative register classification for defense/aerospace strategic planning

label: Financial Perspective KPI Design
content:
Financial KPI specifications for Balanced Scorecard — defense/aerospace sub-tier manufacturing.

Revenue Growth Rate: (current period revenue − prior period) ÷ prior period revenue; target 10–15% YoY in growth phase, ≥5% in steady state; data source: ERP revenue recognition module; owner: CFO. Red threshold: <5% for two consecutive quarters.

EBITDA Margin: EBITDA ÷ total revenue; target 15–20% for defense sub-tier manufacturing; measures operational profitability before non-cash items; data source: monthly P&L from ERP; owner: CFO. Red threshold: <12% for two consecutive months.

Backlog Coverage Ratio: contracted backlog value ÷ trailing 12-month revenue; target 1.5–2.0× (18–24 months forward coverage); signals revenue sustainability and business development health; data source: ERP contract module + BD pipeline for probable options; owner: VP BD/CFO. Red threshold: <1.2× triggers BD escalation.

Book-to-Bill Ratio: contract award value in period ÷ revenue recognized in same period; target >1.0 for growth phase, ≥0.9 for steady state; <0.8 for two consecutive quarters triggers strategic alarm and SMC review; data source: BD tracking system + ERP; owner: VP BD/CFO.

B&P Efficiency: contract awards won per $1M B&P spend; track rolling 8-quarter trend; deteriorating ratio signals competitive positioning problem, not just investment level; data source: BD system + accounting; owner: VP BD.

Indirect Cost Rate Trend: actual vs. forward-pricing indirect rates (fringe, overhead, G&A); deviation >5% from budget triggers CFO/VP Operations review; DCAA-compliant calculation per applicable CAS standards; owner: CFO. Each financial KPI requires documented data source, calculation method, accountable owner, and escalation threshold before inclusion in scorecard.
summary: System: Financial perspective KPI design — revenue growth, EBITDA margin, backlog coverage ratio, book-to-bill, B&P efficiency, and indirect cost rate with targets, data sources, and red thresholds for defense sub-tier manufacturing scorecard

label: Customer Perspective KPI Design
content:
Customer KPI specifications for Balanced Scorecard — defense/aerospace sub-tier manufacturing.

On-Time Delivery (OTD): units delivered on or before contract delivery date ÷ total units delivered in period; target ≥95%; data source: ERP delivery records vs. CDRL schedule; owner: VP Operations. Red threshold: <90% for any rolling 30-day window.

Quality Escapes: count of non-conformances reaching the customer (field escapes + delivery-level rejections); target 0 field escapes per quarter; any single field escape triggers formal 8D corrective action and customer notification within 24 hours; data source: quality system event log + customer rejection notices; owner: VP Quality/Operations.

NPS/CSAT Score: net promoter or customer satisfaction rating from annual government customer relationship survey; defense proxy: CPARS ratings — target Exceptional or Very Good on ≥80% of evaluated contract performance ratings; data source: CPARS portal (pull quarterly); owner: VP BD. Red threshold: any Marginal or Unsatisfactory rating triggers immediate CEO notification and relationship recovery plan.

Proposal Win Rate: competitive proposals won ÷ competitive proposals submitted in period; target ≥35% overall, ≥75% on sole-source re-competes; analyze by contract type (FFP vs. cost-plus), NAICS code, and program office to identify positional strengths and weaknesses; data source: BD pipeline tracker; owner: VP BD. Red threshold: <25% over rolling 4 quarters triggers win strategy review.

Repeat Award Rate: contracts re-awarded to company from existing customers ÷ contracts eligible for re-competition in period; target ≥70%; measures customer loyalty beyond formal scoring criteria; owner: VP BD/CEO.
summary: System: Customer perspective KPI design — OTD, quality escapes, NPS/CSAT via CPARS, proposal win rate, and repeat award rate with targets, data sources, and escalation thresholds for defense scorecard

label: Internal Process KPI Design
content:
Internal process KPI specifications for Balanced Scorecard — defense/aerospace manufacturing.

Manufacturing Cycle Efficiency (MCE): value-added processing time ÷ total lead time from work order open to delivery; target >50% (world-class benchmark >75%); low MCE reveals excessive queue wait, batch delays, or rework loops; data source: MES work order timestamps; owner: VP Operations. Red threshold: <35% for any work center with Tier 0 programs.

Engineering Change Velocity: (a) ECO volume — count of engineering change orders initiated per month; (b) ECO processing time — average calendar days from ECO initiation to production release; target processing time <10 business days; high ECO volume or slow processing indicates design immaturity driving production disruption; data source: PLM/engineering change management system; owner: VP Engineering. Red threshold: ECO processing >15 days average or volume >20/month on a single program.

First-Pass Yield (FPY): units passing all inspections without rework ÷ total units inspected, reported by process area; targets — machining FPY >97%, assembly FPY >95%, final test FPY >98%; data source: quality inspection records in MES; owner: VP Quality. Red threshold: any process area below target for two consecutive periods triggers root-cause analysis.

Schedule Performance Index (SPI): earned value ÷ planned value (EVM); target 0.95–1.05 across portfolio; SPI <0.90 for two consecutive months triggers formal program corrective action; data source: EVM system; owner: VP Operations/PM.

Cost Performance Index (CPI): earned value ÷ actual cost (EVM); target 0.95–1.05; CPI <0.90 triggers program financial review with CFO; owner: CFO/PM.

NCR Closure Rate: NCRs closed within 30 days ÷ total NCRs opened in period; target >90%; aging NCRs signal chronic unresolved process deficiencies; owner: VP Quality.
summary: System: Internal process KPI design — MCE, engineering change velocity, first-pass yield by process, SPI/CPI, and NCR closure rate with targets and red thresholds for defense manufacturing scorecard

label: Learning and Growth KPI Design
content:
Learning and growth KPI specifications for Balanced Scorecard — defense manufacturing.

Voluntary Turnover Rate: voluntary separations ÷ average headcount (annualized); target <10% overall, <5% for cleared and critical-skill personnel; high turnover for cleared staff is acutely damaging due to 6–24 month clearance pipeline replacement timeline; data source: HR system monthly export; owner: COO/CHRO. Red threshold: cleared personnel voluntary turnover >8% annualized triggers immediate retention intervention and Tier 2 risk register entry.

Training Hours Per Employee: total training hours delivered ÷ total headcount (average); target ≥40 hours/employee/year per AS9100 competence requirements; track separately — technical training, compliance training (FAR/DFARS, ethics, cybersecurity/CMMC), and leadership development hours; data source: LMS training completion records; owner: COO/HR.

Succession Readiness: percentage of VP+ and critical cleared positions with a documented backfill candidate rated 'ready now' or 'ready in 12 months' in the succession planning register; target 100% coverage for VP+ roles, ≥80% for critical cleared positions; data source: succession register reviewed annually at SMC; owner: CEO. Red threshold: any VP+ role with no rated backfill candidate triggers immediate development action.

Cleared Workforce Pipeline Ratio: count of personnel with active clearance ÷ count of cleared billets required by active programs; target ≥1.2× (20% buffer above requirement); track separately by clearance level (Secret, TS, SCI); ratio <1.0 on any clearance level is a Tier 2 enterprise risk requiring immediate mitigation plan; data source: FSO clearance tracking system; owner: COO/FSO.

NADCAP/AS9100 Certification Breadth: active certifications maintained in good standing vs. target certification portfolio; loss of any certification is immediately flagged as red KPI regardless of other scorecard status; owner: VP Quality.
summary: System: Learning and growth KPI design — voluntary turnover (cleared vs. overall), training hours, succession readiness, cleared workforce pipeline ratio, and certification breadth with thresholds for defense scorecard

label: Scorecard Cascade from Corporate to Department to Program
content:
Three-tier Balanced Scorecard cascade methodology for defense manufacturing ensuring strategic alignment from enterprise vision to program execution.

Tier 1 — Corporate Scorecard (CEO/SMC): enterprise-wide KPIs across all four perspectives; rolling 4-quarter trend view; reviewed quarterly at SMC; provides strategic performance baseline for board reporting and annual planning inputs. Corporate KPIs are aggregate outcomes (total revenue growth rate, overall OTD, enterprise EBITDA margin, total voluntary turnover).

Tier 2 — Department Scorecard (VP-level): each VP translates corporate KPIs into department-specific leading indicators that are within their direct control. Example cascade: Corporate OTD target 95% → VP Operations department scorecard tracks work-center queue depth, ECO throughput time, and shift schedule adherence as leading predictors of OTD outcome. Each department scorecard includes 4–6 KPIs per perspective traceable to a corporate parent KPI; department scorecards reviewed monthly in VP staff meetings and quarterly in SMC as input to corporate scorecard health assessment.

Tier 3 — Program Scorecard (PM-level): program managers maintain program-specific EVM-based scorecard (CPI, SPI, technical milestone compliance, quality metrics, OTD to program schedule) traceable to the parent department scorecard. Program scorecards reviewed weekly in program status meetings and monthly at PMC.

Cascade integrity requirements: VP Strategy owns the cascade map document linking each Tier 3 KPI to its Tier 2 parent and each Tier 2 KPI to its Tier 1 parent; reviewed annually for continued relevance and updated when strategic priorities change. Gaps in cascade (Tier 1 objective with no Tier 2 KPI driving it) are flagged as planning deficiencies.

Escalation flow: Tier 3 red for two consecutive periods → Tier 2 VP action plan required within 30 days; Tier 2 red for two consecutive quarters → Tier 1 formal corrective action plan at SMC; corporate red for two consecutive quarters → CEO-directed corrective action with board notification.
summary: System: Balanced Scorecard cascade methodology — three-tier structure (corporate → department → program) with cascade map, traceability requirements, and escalation flow for defense manufacturing

label: Company Capability Audit Methodology
content:
Structured internal capability audit for the Company dimension of Three Cs analysis in defense manufacturing.

Audit dimensions and evidence sources:
(1) Quality Certifications: AS9100 Rev D scope (note exclusions and limitations); NADCAP approval scopes by process (heat treat, NDT, chemical processing, welding, composites) and customer-approved list per each approval certificate; most-recent audit finding history; DCMA supplier risk assessment rating (High/Moderate/Low). Evidence source: quality management system certification register.

(2) Security Clearance Depth: active clearances by level (Secret, TS, SCI) from FSO personnel security roster; cleared billet count vs. current and projected program requirements (12 months forward); clearance pipeline count (personnel in active investigation); foreign national restrictions in workforce. Evidence source: FSO personnel security register.

(3) Technical Capabilities: customer-specific QPL approvals; tight-tolerance machining capability documented by Cpk data on critical features (±0.001" and tighter); proprietary coating, surface treatment, or special process knowledge; unique test capability (vibration, thermal cycling, RF/EMC, HALT/HASS). Evidence source: equipment capability register, process qualification records.

(4) Program Management Systems: EVM compliance documentation (ANSI/EIA-748); ERP/MES integration for real-time cost tracking; bidirectional requirements traceability in PLM. Evidence source: DCAA/DCMA compliance records, system audit outputs.

(5) Financial Health Indicators: DCAA audit status (no material weakness); Dun & Bradstreet rating; current ratio and working capital sufficiency for bid bond requirements. Evidence source: most recent DCAA audit report, financial statements.
