Scoring protocol: rate each dimension 1–5 (1=absent or non-compliant; 3=meets baseline standard; 5=differentiating/best-in-class vs. competitors); weight dimensions by importance to target market segment; compute weighted company strength index. Refresh cadence: full audit annually (Q3, aligned to strategic planning cycle); triggered spot audit when any certification lapses, key personnel depart, or major program completes.
summary: System: Company capability audit methodology for Three Cs — five dimensions (quality certifications, clearance depth, technical capabilities, PM systems, financial health) with scoring protocol and evidence sources

label: Defense Customer Needs Segmentation
content:
Defense customer needs segmentation by supply chain tier and acquisition role for the Customer dimension of Three Cs analysis. Four-tier segmentation model:

(1) Platform-Level Programs (DoD program offices, prime system integrators — customer tier 0): customer needs — cost-plus development capacity, systems engineering co-development support, MIL-STD and CDRL data deliverables, government source inspection (GSI) acceptance, long-term program partnership continuity, performance-based logistics (PBL) readiness. Acquisition vehicles: MDAPs, ACAT I/II programs, multi-year procurement contracts, major IDIQs. Value proposition emphasis: past performance, program management maturity, technical depth, clearance infrastructure.

(2) Subsystem-Level Programs (prime contractors, large Tier 1 sub-primes): needs — qualified manufacturing sources with NADCAP certifications on approved supplier lists, EVM system compliance, AS9100 quality system, schedule reliability, design-for-manufacturability (DFM) co-engineering support, IP protection discipline, ITAR compliance. Acquisition vehicles: subcontract flow-downs, directed buys, blanket purchase agreements. Value proposition emphasis: certification breadth, on-time delivery history, CPARS ratings.

(3) Component-Level Programs (Tier 2 suppliers, small integrators): needs — competitive unit pricing, rapid-turn prototype capability, ITAR compliance with minimal customer quality burden, flexible minimum order quantities. Acquisition vehicles: FFP purchase orders, catalog items with mil-spec traceability, spot-buy procurements. Value proposition emphasis: price, responsiveness, delivery speed.

(4) Sustainment/MRO Customers (depots, MRO service providers, DLA): needs — legacy part reverse-engineering from obsolete drawings, repair station qualification, low-volume/low-frequency order execution, rapid response to depot surge, authorized maintenance center or repair facility certification. Acquisition vehicles: depot augmentation contracts, DLA supply chain gap fills, PBL support contracts. Value proposition emphasis: legacy expertise, rapid mobilization, certification depth.

Application: assign each pipeline opportunity to a tier; tailor win strategy and value proposition to tier-specific need profile; allocate BD investment weighted toward highest-value tier concentration in the 5-year FYDP pipeline.
summary: System: Defense customer needs segmentation — four-tier model (platform, subsystem, component, sustainment/MRO) with distinct need profiles, acquisition vehicles, and value proposition emphasis for Three Cs customer analysis

label: Competitor Intelligence Gathering and Benchmarking
content:
Systematic competitor intelligence collection and benchmarking process for the Competitor dimension of Three Cs analysis in defense manufacturing.

Intelligence sources: (1) FPDS-NG (USA Spending / USASpending.gov): pull competitor award history by NAICS code, agency, program name, contract type, dollar value, and period of performance; calculate competitor market share by segment; identify program relationships and prime contractor associations; run quarterly by VP Strategy. (2) SAM.gov profiles: competitor certifications, set-aside eligibility (SB, SDVOSB, 8(a), HUBZone), cage code history, active registrations, and exclusion status. (3) BD win/loss debriefs: after every competitive loss, VP BD debriefs relevant staff; capture competitor pricing posture signals (were we within 5%? 15%?), past performance differentiators cited, and technical approach discriminators from source selection feedback letters. Maintain rolling win/loss intelligence log. (4) Public financial data and press releases: revenue growth signals, facility investments, M&A announcements indicating capability acquisition; for public companies, quarterly earnings call transcripts for strategic commentary on defense markets. (5) Industry events: NDIA, AIA, AUSA conference attendance; capture competitor product/service announcements, customer endorsements, and new capability launches.

Benchmarking dimensions: price-per-unit from comparable FPDS-NG awards (adjust for contract type and quantity); delivery performance inferred from CPARS if obtainable via FOIA; certification breadth from SAM.gov + company website; market share trend by segment over rolling 8 quarters.

Competitor profile template: maintain one-page profile per significant competitor including FPDS-NG market share, primary NAICS codes, certifications, set-aside status, pricing posture, known customer relationships, facility footprint, and strategic trajectory assessment. Update quarterly.

Output: competitive profiles feed directly into the Three Cs positioning matrix, SWOT threat quadrant, and competitive intelligence brief (see L5 output).
summary: System: Competitor intelligence gathering and benchmarking — FPDS-NG data pulls, SAM.gov profiling, win/loss debrief protocol, benchmarking dimensions, and one-page competitor profile template with quarterly refresh cadence

label: Strategic Data Pipeline Architecture
content:
Data pipeline architecture for VP Strategy analytics infrastructure in defense manufacturing — connecting operational systems to a unified analytics warehouse.

Source systems and ingestion schedule: (1) ERP (cost actuals by cost pool, indirect rates, milestone billing, revenue recognized) — nightly incremental pull; (2) MES (work order cycle times, OTD actuals, yield by process, shift utilization) — nightly incremental pull; (3) EVM system (CPI/SPI by program, variance analysis, EAC updates) — weekly full refresh post-monthly program review; (4) BD CRM (pipeline value, win/loss events, B&P spend per opportunity, proposal status) — daily incremental pull; (5) HR system (headcount by clearance level, attrition events, training completion, succession register) — weekly full refresh; (6) Quality system (NCR counts, DCMA surveillance findings, customer escape events) — daily incremental pull; (7) FPDS-NG external feed (competitor award data, market share trends) — monthly batch pull.

Data warehouse architecture: on-premises data lake (parquet format) with ITAR-compliant access control (role-based access; no CUI data in commercial cloud without FedRAMP High authorization); staging layer for raw ingested data; curated layer for cleaned, standardized metrics with documented lineage; feature layer for derived analytics metrics (bid-to-win ratio, IRAD-to-award conversion, OTD correlation with ECO rate).

Data quality controls: automated row-count and null-value checks at ingestion; data quality flags surfaced in monthly analytics report; failed ingestion jobs alert CTO data team within 2 hours. Feature catalog: all derived metrics documented with calculation logic, source tables, lineage, and data quality SLA. ITAR compliance: data classification labels applied at ingestion; access logs audited quarterly by Export Compliance Officer.
summary: System: Strategic data pipeline architecture — source system ingestion schedule (ERP, MES, EVM, CRM, HR, quality, FPDS-NG), ITAR-compliant on-prem data warehouse design, feature catalog, and data quality controls

label: Bayesian Network Models for Make-vs-Buy and Pricing Decisions
content:
Operational guide for running and interpreting Bayesian network decision models for two core VP Strategy recurring decisions in defense manufacturing.

Make-vs-Buy Model: causal nodes — volume forecast (low/medium/high distribution from program plan), workforce availability (current headcount vs. required), overhead absorption impact (change in indirect rate from adding/removing direct labor base), learning curve phase (initial/plateau/mature), and schedule risk (probability of delay from internal vs. external production). Output: probability distribution over 3-year cost differential (make minus buy) and schedule impact. Positive cost differential exceeding +10% favors buy; negative beyond −10% favors make; intermediate range requires qualitative tie-breaking on IP retention and schedule control. Causal structure validated jointly by CFO and VP Operations using historical make-buy outcomes.

Pricing Decision Model: causal nodes — contract type (FFP/CPFF/T&M), CPARS rating tier (Exceptional/Very Good/Satisfactory), competitive field size (sole-source/2–3/4+ offerors), customer budget visibility (known/estimated/unknown), technical differentiation score (1–5 from VP Strategy), and indirect rate competitiveness vs. estimated field. Output: probability of win at three price points (10% below PTW, at PTW, 10% above PTW); also outputs probability of award at each price conditional on CPARS tier. Causal structure validated by VP BD using 3-year win/loss history; minimum 30 historical cases required for reliable edge weights.

Model operation cycle: VP Strategy inputs scenario parameters through a documented parameter input sheet → model returns probability distribution → sensitivity analysis run on top-3 uncertain nodes → 1-page decision brief produced for SMC. Back-testing cadence: quarterly comparison of predictions vs. outcomes; model structure updated if prediction accuracy drops below 65% on 10+ recent cases.
summary: System: Bayesian network model operations for make-vs-buy and pricing decisions — causal node definitions, output distributions, decision thresholds, and quarterly back-testing cadence for defense manufacturing

label: Monte Carlo Simulation for Bid/No-Bid Risk Quantification
content:
Monte Carlo simulation methodology for probabilistic bid/no-bid risk quantification in defense manufacturing — replacing single-point cost estimates with probability distributions.

Model structure and input distributions: (1) Direct labor cost uncertainty: triangular distribution per cost element (optimistic/most-likely/pessimistic from engineering bottoms-up estimate); standard deviation of ±15% on total direct labor estimate for early-phase proposals. (2) Material cost uncertainty: normal distribution around should-cost estimate; ±15% standard deviation for complex BOMs with ≥10 line items; ±8% for well-defined commodity bills. (3) Subcontract risk premium: uniform distribution 0–20% above subcontractor proposal price (accounts for scope creep, supplier performance, change order frequency). (4) Learning curve uncertainty: ±10% sensitivity on slope assumption for LRIP unit cost projection; relevant for production quantities ≥5 units. (5) Schedule risk: define probability distributions for top-3 schedule risk events (probability of engineering rework cycle, supplier delay, GFE late delivery); schedule delay converts to cost impact at program monthly burn rate. (6) Revenue risk: price-to-win range as triangular distribution from BD competitive intelligence (pessimistic PTW, most-likely PTW, optimistic PTW). (7) Contract type risk adjustment: FFP adds downside floor at cost floor; cost-plus limits downside to fee risk.

Simulation execution: minimum 10,000 iterations using @Risk (Excel), Python scipy.stats, or equivalent Monte Carlo engine; outputs: probability distribution of program contribution margin, P(positive contribution margin), P(margin < −5%). Decision thresholds: recommend bid if P(positive CM) ≥70% AND P(margin <−5%) ≤10%; recommend no-bid if P(margin <−5%) ≥25%; refer to SMC for borderline cases. Required for all capture decision briefs ≥$500K total contract value estimate. Annual calibration: compare simulation predictions vs. actual program outcomes to refine distribution assumptions.
summary: System: Monte Carlo simulation for bid/no-bid — probability distributions for labor, material, schedule, and pricing risk; 10,000-iteration execution; decision thresholds P(positive CM) ≥70% and P(margin <−5%) ≤10%; annual calibration

label: Market Sizing Using TAM/SAM/SOM Framework
content:
TAM/SAM/SOM market sizing methodology for VP Strategy growth planning and Three Horizons investment calibration in defense manufacturing.

Total Addressable Market (TAM): total DoD and allied defense spending in the targeted capability area regardless of competitive, geographic, or qualification constraints. Primary sources: FYDP budget line items by Program Element (RDT&E + procurement accounts, from President's Budget submission and Congressional Budget Justification Books); FMS demand data (DSCA case values by commodity area); allied nation defense budgets for relevant capability domains (Jane's, SIPRI data). Calculation: sum FYDP years 1–5 for relevant program elements; add FMS demand estimate if international markets are in scope. TAM updated semi-annually (aligned to FYDP publication cycle).

Serviceable Addressable Market (SAM): TAM filtered by the company's actual competitive eligibility — programs where certifications, clearances, and NAICS codes are met; contract vehicles with company access (IDIQ vehicle coverage, SBIR eligibility, set-aside status match); geographic or facility constraints (SCIF requirements, CONUS-only restrictions, facility security agreement requirements). Calculation: apply eligibility filter to TAM line items; reduce to programs company could legally and practically compete. SAM updated quarterly as certifications, contract vehicles, and set-aside status change.

Serviceable Obtainable Market (SOM): SAM × estimated win rate per segment × B&P investment capacity. SOM is the forward revenue plan sanity check — if SOM is less than revenue target, either win rate must improve, B&P investment must increase, or SAM must be expanded through new capabilities or vehicles. SOM updated monthly as pipeline win/loss events shift probability-weighted award values.

Output: market sizing table by segment (platform systems, subsystems, components, sustainment services) with TAM/SAM/SOM values and year-over-year trends. Feeds Ansoff Matrix growth pathway analysis, Three Horizons investment portfolio decisions, and BD budget justification.
summary: System: Market sizing using TAM/SAM/SOM — FYDP-sourced TAM by program element, eligibility-filtered SAM, win-rate-adjusted SOM with update cadence and segment breakdown for defense manufacturing growth planning

label: SWOT-Driven Strategic Initiative Selection
content:
Decision process for selecting the final slate of strategic initiatives from the scored SWOT action intersection matrix for execution in the upcoming planning cycle.

Decision inputs: scored initiative register (Impact × Feasibility × Urgency composite scores), available strategic investment capacity (VP-hours and budget for discretionary initiatives above run-rate operations), and current Three Horizons portfolio balance.

Decision criteria applied in sequence: (1) Score threshold gate: initiatives scoring <40 are automatically parked in the watch list; candidates must score ≥40 to advance. (2) Portfolio balance check: selected slate must include representation across all four SWOT quadrants (SO, WO, ST, WT); minimum 1 WT strategy required regardless of score (risk discipline). (3) Resource constraint filter: total estimated VP-hours and budget for selected initiatives must not exceed 100% of available strategic investment capacity; drop lowest-scoring initiatives until within capacity. (4) Strategic alignment gate: each selected initiative must map to at least one Level 1 or Level 2 strategic plan objective; non-traceable initiatives are deferred regardless of score. (5) Three Horizons balance check: no more than 60% of selected initiatives classified H1; over-indexing on H1 signals neglect of strategic renewal.

Decision output: signed Initiative Selection Record documenting selected initiatives with composite scores, quadrant origin, rationale, VP owner, 90-day milestone, success KPI, estimated budget, and H1/H2/H3 classification; presented at next SMC meeting for formal adoption. Review gate: initiative list re-scored at 90-day checkpoint; initiatives with no measurable progress are formally cancelled or rescheduled at next quarterly review.
summary: Decision: SWOT-driven strategic initiative selection — five sequential decision criteria (score gate, portfolio balance, resource constraint, strategic alignment, Horizons balance) producing a signed Initiative Selection Record for SMC adoption

label: Scorecard Red-Flag Escalation Decision
content:
Decision protocol for VP Strategy and accountable VP owners when Balanced Scorecard KPIs enter and persist in red status.

Red-flag trigger definition: KPI below threshold for 1 consecutive reporting period = Yellow alert (accountable VP notified, informal monitoring); below threshold for 2 consecutive periods = Red-flag requiring formal escalation.

Escalation decision tree: (1) Single KPI red, limited cross-functional impact — accountable VP presents corrective action plan (CAP) at next monthly VP review; 30-day resolution timeline; VP Strategy monitors trend; no SMC action required. (2) Multiple KPIs red in same scorecard perspective (e.g., 2+ Financial or 2+ Internal Process KPIs simultaneously) — COO or CFO convenes cross-functional working group within 5 business days; formal CAP with root cause analysis presented to SMC within 30 days; CEO notified same day. (3) Any Customer KPI in red (OTD <90% or any quality field escape) — immediate notification to VP BD and CEO within 24 hours; customer relationship impact assessment within 48 hours; external communication plan prepared; event entered as Tier 2 enterprise risk. (4) Any Learning/Growth KPI in red (cleared pipeline ratio <1.0 on any level or succession readiness gap at VP+ role) — VP Strategy flags as strategic vulnerability; 30-day mitigation plan due; cleared pipeline ratio below 1.0 immediately triggers Tier 2 risk register entry.

Decision record: each escalation documented in scorecard action log with date, KPI, root cause hypothesis, assigned owner, and target resolution date. Closed only when KPI returns to green for 2 consecutive reporting periods.
summary: Decision: Scorecard red-flag escalation — four-tier protocol (single KPI, perspective-wide, customer KPI, learning/growth KPI) with decision tree, escalation timelines, and Tier 2 risk register triggers

label: Competitive Response Triggers
content:
Decision framework for VP Strategy to determine when and how to respond to competitor actions in defense manufacturing markets.

Trigger categories and response decision protocols:
(1) Competitor wins major re-compete (>$2M) in company's target segment — trigger intelligence update within 5 business days (FPDS-NG award details, BD debrief from any available source selection feedback); VP Strategy assesses cause of loss: pricing problem → indirect rate analysis + pricing strategy review; past performance problem → quality/delivery improvement plan; capability gap → IRAD or certification investment decision. Response decision documented within 30 days.

(2) Competitor acquires new certification (NADCAP scope, clearance facility upgrade, new AS9100 site) — VP Strategy reassesses Three Cs competitive position within 30 days; decision: pursue defensive parity certification (if customer-required) or invest in a different differentiator to avoid head-to-head competition. Decision brief to CEO within 60 days.

(3) Competitor M&A event — immediate intelligence collection; within 2 weeks assess: workshare implications (acquired company's work may shift away from existing prime relationships), capability threat (acquirer gains new certifications or customer access), and teaming opportunity (primes losing supply chain coverage need new qualified sources). Strategic response options: deepen existing customer relationships, target former customers of acquired company, or counter-teem with affected primes. Response brief to CEO within 2 weeks of confirmed M&A announcement.

(4) New non-traditional entrant in target market segment — assess barrier-to-entry implications; determine if entrant threatens core business (invest in differentiation) or creates teaming opportunity (partner with entrant for complementary capability); decision timeline 60 days.

All response decisions documented in competitive response log; reviewed quarterly at Three Cs refresh cycle.
summary: Decision: Competitive response triggers — four trigger categories (re-compete loss, new certification, M&A event, non-traditional entrant) with decision protocols, response timelines, and competitive response log maintenance

label: Data Model Confidence Thresholds for Go/No-Go
content:
Decision authority matrix and confidence threshold standards governing when analytics model outputs may drive strategic go/no-go recommendations in defense manufacturing.

Three-tier confidence classification: (1) High-confidence model: prediction error ≤10% on holdout data, ≥50 historical training cases, causal structure validated by domain SMEs (VP Strategy + relevant functional VP). Decision authority: VP Strategy may use high-confidence model output as the primary decision input; recommendation documented with model version, confidence metrics, and key sensitivity ranges. (2) Medium-confidence model: prediction error 10–20% on holdout or 20–50 training cases or causal structure partially validated (one SME sign-off). Decision authority: model output is a supporting input only; VP Strategy must supplement with qualitative judgment and consultation of at least two independent SMEs before recommendation to SMC; SME concurrence documented. (3) Low-confidence model: fewer than 20 training cases, prediction error >20%, or unvalidated causal structure. Decision authority: model output treated as directional signal only; VP Strategy must explicitly disclose model limitations in SMC presentation; decision reverts to structured qualitative framework (issue tree + expert panel); no go/no-go recommendation may be based solely on low-confidence model output.

Model confidence maintenance: CTO analytics team maintains and publishes confidence metrics for all active decision models monthly; VP Strategy reviews metrics before each model use. Annual model audit: independent data scientist reviews all models; models below medium-confidence threshold for >2 consecutive quarters are suspended pending respecification. CEO override: CEO may override a model-recommended go/no-go with documented rationale — preserves judgment authority for novel situations outside model training distribution (new market, new contract type, unprecedented scenario).
summary: Decision: Data model confidence thresholds — three-tier classification (high/medium/low) with decision authority levels, SME concurrence requirements, disclosure obligations, and CEO override authority for strategic go/no-go decisions

label: SWOT Action Plan Deliverable
content:
Standard deliverable format for the SWOT-to-Action output package from VP Strategy to CEO and SMC, produced annually in the Q3 strategic planning cycle.

Document structure: (1) Executive Summary (1 page): top-3 strategic insights; top-5 selected initiatives with composite scores and H1/H2/H3 classification; total resource requirement; key risks from WT quadrant requiring immediate attention. (2) SWOT Matrix (1 page): four-quadrant grid with top-5 items per quadrant, each with evidence citation and strategic magnitude rating (High/Medium/Low); items color-coded by source type (internal data vs. market intelligence). (3) Action Intersection Matrix (2 pages): full SO/WO/ST/WT matrix with strategy hypotheses at each intersection; highlighted top-10 strategies; composite scores displayed. (4) Initiative Register (1 page): ranked table with columns — initiative name, SWOT quadrant origin, composite score (I×F×U), VP owner, 90-day milestone, success KPI, estimated budget, H1/H2/H3 classification. (5) Evidence Appendix: data citations for each SWOT item (FPDS-NG data, CPARS ratings, market research sources, internal performance data).

Format: 8–12 pages main body + appendix; PowerPoint for SMC briefing; Word/PDF for distribution record. Version control: VP Strategy owns document; prior-year version archived for year-over-year strategic evolution comparison. Distribution: CEO, all VPs, and board strategy committee if applicable. Interim update produced within 30 days of any major market event (material contract win/loss, competitor M&A, significant regulatory change).
summary: Output: SWOT action plan deliverable — five-section document (executive summary, SWOT matrix, intersection matrix, initiative register, evidence appendix) with format standards, version control, and distribution requirements

label: Executive Scorecard Dashboard
content:
Executive Balanced Scorecard dashboard format and delivery standards for CEO and board strategic performance review.

Dashboard structure: (1) Single-page executive summary: 16–20 KPIs arranged by four perspectives (Financial, Customer, Internal Process, Learning/Growth), each with traffic-light status (Green/Yellow/Red), current period actual, target, and 3-period trend arrow; total red and yellow counts prominently displayed; any KPI in red for two consecutive periods flagged with asterisk. (2) Financial perspective detail (1 page): revenue growth rate trend (8-quarter bar chart), EBITDA margin trend line, backlog coverage ratio with 6-quarter history, book-to-bill ratio trend, B&P efficiency index. (3) Customer perspective detail (1 page): OTD trend by program tier (Tier 0/1/2), quality escape log with 8D closure status, proposal win rate by contract type and customer segment, CPARS ratings distribution. (4) Internal process detail (1 page): MCE trend by work center, FPY by process with control chart, SPI/CPI distribution heat map across active programs, NCR age profile. (5) Learning/growth detail (1 page): voluntary turnover trend (cleared vs. non-cleared), training hours completion by department, succession readiness status by VP+ role, cleared pipeline ratio by clearance level. (6) Corrective action register (1 page): all active red-flag escalations with KPI name, owner, root cause hypothesis, action status, and target resolution date.

Delivery cadence: monthly full dashboard to VP staff and CEO within 5 business days of period close; quarterly executive summary to board strategy committee. Data ownership: Financial KPIs — CFO; Customer KPIs — VP BD/VP Operations; Internal Process — VP Operations/VP Quality; Learning/Growth — COO/CHRO. VP Strategy owns dashboard architecture, version control, and distribution; prior-quarter PDF archived for longitudinal trend comparison.
summary: Output: Executive scorecard dashboard — six-section format (summary, four-perspective detail pages, corrective action register) with traffic-light status, trend arrows, data ownership matrix, and monthly delivery cadence

label: Competitive Intelligence Brief
content:
Standard format for the quarterly competitive intelligence brief produced by VP Strategy for CEO and SMC strategic decision-making.

Brief structure: (1) Executive Summary (½ page): top-3 competitive insights this period; any trigger events (competitor M&A, major award, new certification); recommended strategic response priority and urgency rating. (2) Market Share Update (1 page): FPDS-NG data summary — competitor award values and counts by NAICS code in target segments for current quarter vs. prior 4 quarters; company market share trend; new entrants or notable exits from target segments. (3) Competitor Profiles Update (1–2 pages): refreshed one-page profiles for top-3 competitors with FPDS-NG award history, SAM.gov certification status changes, pricing posture signals from recent awards, and strategic trajectory assessment. New competitor profiles added when any competitor reaches $500K+ in company's target NAICS segment. (4) Win/Loss Analysis (½ page): proposal wins and losses this period; root cause themes from BD debriefs; win rate trend by segment and contract type. (5) Competitive Response Log (½ page): status of all open competitive response decisions from prior periods; new triggers identified this period with recommended response decision timeline. (6) Strategic Implications (½ page): VP Strategy net competitive position assessment; recommended adjustments to Three Cs positioning, SWOT threat quadrant updates, or Ansoff strategy recalibration.

Frequency: quarterly production aligned to Three Cs refresh cycle; interim brief within 2 weeks of any Tier 1 trigger event (competitor M&A, material competitive loss, new non-traditional market entrant). Distribution: CEO, VP BD, and all VP-level leaders for awareness. Handle per ITAR/export control requirements if competitive intelligence includes controlled technical data.
summary: Output: Competitive intelligence brief — six-section format (executive summary, market share, competitor profiles, win/loss analysis, response log, strategic implications) with quarterly production cadence and interim trigger-based issuance

label: Strategic Analytics Report Template
content:
Standard template for the monthly strategic analytics report from VP Strategy to CEO and SMC, delivered within 5 business days of month close.

Report structure: (1) Strategic Signals Summary (1 page): top-5 data-driven insights from analytics pipeline this month; each insight stated in plain language (1–2 sentences) with source data reference; flag any insight that contradicts a prior strategic assumption — these require immediate VP Strategy and CEO attention. (2) Causal Model Findings (1–2 pages): results from all decision model runs during the period (make-vs-buy analyses, pricing analyses, bid/no-bid Monte Carlo assessments); for each model run — decision context, input parameters used, probability distribution output (including P10/P50/P90), model confidence tier (High/Medium/Low per threshold framework), and VP Strategy recommendation; low-confidence model outputs include explicit limitation disclosure. (3) KPI Trend Analysis (1 page): leading indicator monitoring — proposal pipeline value week-over-week trend, EVM CPI/SPI distribution across portfolio, quality escape rate trend, cleared workforce pipeline ratio; KPIs trending toward threshold highlighted for preemptive VP attention before red-flag escalation is triggered. (4) Data Quality Log (½ page): data pipeline health — sources successfully ingested vs. failed ingestion counts; data quality flags raised this period; incomplete data affecting model accuracy and which decisions it may impact. (5) Model Validation Status (½ page): prediction accuracy metrics for active decision models vs. recent historical actuals; models flagged by CTO analytics team for confidence review. (6) Assumptions Audit (½ page): key causal assumptions underlying active decision models; any assumption challenged by new data this period; required model updates and owner.

Ownership: VP Strategy produces; CTO co-reviews for data integrity; CEO approves distribution list. Prior months archived for longitudinal reference.
summary: Output: Strategic analytics report template — six-section monthly report (signals summary, model findings with confidence tiers, KPI trends, data quality log, model validation, assumptions audit) with 5-day delivery target

label: Senior Management Council (SMC) meeting operations
content: Operational procedures for running SMC quarterly meetings. Agenda preparation: VP Strategy distributes agenda and pre-read package 10 business days prior; agenda items classified as Decision (requires vote), Discussion (deliberative, no immediate decision), or Information (awareness only); CEO approves final agenda 5 days prior. Quorum: CEO plus majority of C-suite and VP-level members. Meeting conduct: CEO chairs; COO or Chief of Staff serves as secretary and action item recorder; decisions documented in real-time; Program Decision Memoranda issued within 5 business days for program decisions; resource directives within 10 days for budget/headcount actions. Standing agenda elements: strategic scorecard review (VP Strategy), top enterprise risk register review (CEO), program portfolio health (COO), financial performance (CFO), and open decision items. Action item tracking: all SMC actions entered into enterprise action tracker with owner, due date, and priority tier; reviewed at subsequent SMC for closure. Off-cycle convening authority: CEO may convene emergency SMC with 48-hour notice for Tier 1 risk materialization — customer Cure Notice, program termination, VP-level or higher personnel loss, or major regulatory action.
summary: System: SMC meeting operations — agenda preparation, quorum rules, decision documentation, action item tracking, and emergency convening authority for CEO-chaired enterprise governance

label: PMC gate review package preparation and facilitation
content: Operational process for preparing and conducting Program Management Council (PMC) gate reviews at each KDP milestone. Package preparation (PM responsibility, due 15 business days before KDP): (1) Program Status Report — CPI, EAC, ETC, SPI, critical path, upcoming milestones; (2) Risk register update — all open risks with mitigation status and unmitigated P×I scores; (3) KDP-specific artifacts — KDP-A: resource confirmation and contract baseline; KDP-B: PDR package with cost estimate confidence ±30% and schedule margin ≥15%; KDP-C: CDR completion evidence, TRL ≥6, supplier qualification status; KDP-D: FAI results, Ppk data on critical characteristics, workforce readiness. PMC composition: COO chairs; includes CEO, CTO, CFO, VP Engineering, VP Operations, VP Quality, and applicable PM; quorum requires COO plus at least three VP-level members. Review conduct: PM presents program status; independent technical reviewer (designated by CTO) presents separate risk assessment; PMC deliberates; CEO holds tiebreaker authority. PDM issuance: COO issues Program Decision Memorandum within 5 business days; Conditional Approval mandatory actions logged with re-review date ≤30 days. Failed gates: Redirect requires documented deficiency list signed by COO and PM closure plan within 15 days; Termination requires CEO concurrence and board notification if program value ≥$5M.
summary: System: PMC gate review package preparation — KDP artifact requirements, PMC composition, review conduct, PDM issuance, and failed-gate protocols for program lifecycle governance

label: CIC business case submission and evaluation process
content: Operational process for submitting, evaluating, and approving Capital Investment Council (CIC) business cases. Submission triggers: equipment >$50K, facility modifications >$25K, IT/software lifecycle cost >$100K. Required package elements: (1) Request summary — item description, requestor, department, program justification, urgency (routine vs. time-sensitive); (2) Life-cycle cost analysis — acquisition, installation, operating, maintenance, and disposal costs over asset useful life; (3) Alternatives analysis — minimum two alternatives with quantitative cost-benefit comparison; (4) ROI calculation — payback period (target ≤4 years for non-strategic investments), NPV, and IRR at 5% discount rate; (5) Strategic alignment — explicit link to at least one annual strategic plan objective or active program requirement; (6) Risk assessment — cost overrun probability, technology obsolescence risk, vendor dependency risk. CIC composition: CFO chairs; CEO, COO, CTO, and VP Operations as standing members; requesting VP presents case. Evaluation scoring: ROI/payback (40%), strategic alignment (30%), risk rating (20%), urgency (10%); proposals scoring ≥70/100 recommended for approval. Decision authority: CFO approves ≤$100K routine investments with favorable scoring; CEO approves $100K–$500K; CEO plus board approval for >$500K. Post-approval: CFO tracks actual vs. projected ROI annually for 3 years; investments achieving <80% of projected ROI trigger CIC lessons-learned review. Emergency authority: Plant Manager approves up to $10K for critical equipment repair preventing Tier 0 production stoppage; must ratify through CIC within 30 days.
summary: System: CIC business case submission and evaluation — submission triggers, required package elements, CIC composition, scoring criteria, decision authority tiers, and post-approval ROI tracking

label: Operations strategy and enterprise planning
content: COO responsibility for translating enterprise strategy into operational plans, resource allocation frameworks, and multi-year capacity roadmaps. Owns the annual operating plan (AOP) development cycle, aligns divisional operating plans with CEO/SMC strategic direction, and ensures operational execution capability matches program portfolio commitments. Coordinates with CFO on operating budget, VP Operations on capacity, and VP Engineering on resource demand. Reviews operating plan progress quarterly via balanced scorecard; escalates execution gaps to SMC. Bridges gap between strategic planning (VP Strategy/CEO) and day-to-day operations execution.
summary: Task: COO ownership of operations strategy — annual operating plan development, capacity roadmap alignment, and bridge between strategic direction and operational execution

label: Develop and publish annual operating plan
content: Annual operating plan (AOP) development process owned by COO. Cadence: Q3 kickoff aligned to strategic planning cycle; Q4 submission; Q1 SMC approval. Process steps: (1) Receive enterprise strategic objectives and financial targets from CEO/CFO; (2) Collect divisional operating plan inputs from VP Operations, VP Engineering, and department managers covering headcount, capital requirements, program demand forecast, and capacity utilization projections; (3) Consolidate into integrated AOP — revenue targets by program tier, headcount plan by function, capital budget request, indirect rate forecast, and risk-adjusted operating margin target; (4) Gap analysis — identify where aggregate divisional plans exceed available budget or fall short of strategic targets; (5) Reconciliation — COO facilitates cross-functional trade-offs (overtime vs. hiring, subcontract vs. internal capacity, deferred investment vs. schedule risk); (6) SMC review and approval; (7) Cascade finalized AOP to VP-level owners as operating budgets. AOP includes: quarterly milestone targets, headcount ceiling by function, capital expenditure phasing, and capacity utilization targets (60–75% for multi-program floors). Tracked monthly via balanced scorecard; formal mid-year replan if actuals deviate >10% from plan on revenue or headcount.
summary: System: COO-led annual operating plan development — divisional input collection, consolidated resource plan, gap reconciliation, SMC approval, and VP-level cascade with quarterly tracking

label: Approve capacity allocation across programs and functions
content: COO decision authority for allocating constrained capacity (labor, equipment, facility) across competing program demands during the AOP cycle and at mid-year replan. Decision triggers: (1) Total program demand exceeds available capacity in any labor category or equipment work center; (2) New program award requiring capacity reallocation; (3) Program delay or cancellation freeing capacity for redeployment; (4) Utilization exceeds 85% threshold triggering expansion decision. Allocation framework: Tier 0 critical-path programs receive first priority; Tier 1 programs at 80% demand confidence; Tier 1B prototype/engineering at 60% confidence — consistent with tiered capacity model. Decision inputs: VP Operations capacity model (utilization actuals and forecast), VP Engineering headcount demand by skill type, CFO operating budget ceiling, PMC program priority register. Decision options: (A) Reallocate existing capacity with schedule deconfliction; (B) Authorize overtime or second shift for constrained resources; (C) Approve subcontracting for lower-priority work to free internal capacity; (D) Invest in capacity expansion (escalated to CIC if >$50K). Decisions documented in AOP revision memo distributed to all VP owners. Major reallocation decisions (affecting Tier 0 programs or requiring >$50K investment) require CEO notification within 48 hours.
summary: Decision: COO authority to allocate constrained capacity across programs — tiered priority framework, four response options (reallocation/overtime/subcontract/invest), and escalation thresholds to CEO and CIC

label: Pipeline metrics and BD velocity dashboard
content: BD pipeline health metrics framework for VP BD operational management and CEO/CFO forecasting. Pipeline stage PWIN defaults: Identified (10%) → Qualified (15–25%) → Capture (25–45%) → Proposal (40–75%) → Award/Lost. Core pipeline metrics: (1) Total pipeline value by stage — sum of estimated contract values; Qualified+ pipeline target ≥3× annual revenue goal; (2) PWIN-weighted revenue forecast — sum of (estimated value × PWIN) across all stages; primary BD forecast input to CFO; target ≥1.5× annual revenue plan; (3) Pipeline coverage ratio — total pipeline value ÷ annual revenue target; target ≥3.0×; red threshold <2.0× triggers BD resourcing review at VP level; (4) Stage velocity — average calendar days in each stage; Identify→Qualify target <10 days; Qualify→Capture target <30 days for competed opportunities; slow velocity signals customer access gap or BD bandwidth constraint; (5) B&P efficiency — contract award value per $1M B&P invested (trailing 8-quarter rolling); red threshold <$8M awarded per $1M B&P; sustained deterioration signals competitive positioning problem, not just spend level; (6) Win rate breakdown — by contract type (FFP vs. CPFF), set-aside status, NAICS code, customer segment, and proposal size band; disaggregated analysis reveals positional strengths and gaps better than blended win rate; (7) Book-to-bill ratio — new award value ÷ revenue recognized in period; target ≥1.0 growth, ≥0.9 steady state; two consecutive quarters <0.8 triggers SMC-level BD strategy review; (8) Re-compete win rate — programs retained on re-competition ÷ total eligible re-competitions; target ≥70%; each loss triggers BD debrief within 30 days. Reporting cadence: weekly BD velocity report to VP BD (stage changes, B&P actions past due); monthly pipeline health scorecard to CEO and CFO; quarterly B&P budget reallocation review against pipeline-to-strategy alignment.
summary: System: Pipeline metrics — tiered PWIN model, PWIN-weighted forecast, stage velocity, B&P efficiency, win rate breakdown, and book-to-bill dashboard for VP BD operational management

label: Opportunity qualification and PWIN scoring
content: Two-stage qualification process governing B&P resource commitment for VP BD. Stage 1 — Go/No-Go Screen (complete within 5 business days of identification): mandatory criteria — (1) NAICS code match to held certifications or an achievable qualification plan within the bid schedule; (2) Estimated contract value ≥$250K total (below threshold, BD cost-to-bid exceeds viable B&P return); (3) Relevant past performance citation available — same/similar scope CPARS or equivalent; (4) No disqualifying barrier — no unresolvable FOCI, ITAR, clearance, or customer-directed sole-source restriction to a competitor; (5) Strategic fit — opportunity aligns to at least one H1 or H2 pipeline program area per Three Horizons register. Fail any mandatory criterion → dismiss; log rationale in CRM. Stage 2 — PWIN Scoring (applied at Qualified→Capture gate): seven factors scored 1–5: (1) Customer relationship — direct working-level access to government PM and CO; (2) Past performance alignment — directly relevant CPARS in same domain, contract type, and scope; (3) Technical capability match — certifications, cleared workforce, demonstrable expertise vs. anticipated RFP requirements; (4) Pricing competitiveness — estimated fully-loaded price vs. FPDS-NG comparable award prices and known incumbent cost posture; (5) Teaming strategy — prime/sub position quality, partner past performance depth, LOI or teaming agreement in place; (6) Competitive intelligence depth — knowledge of incumbent, competitor field size, estimated evaluation criteria weights; (7) B&P investment capacity — available B&P budget vs. estimated proposal development effort. PWIN conversion: 28–35 → 50–75% (strong compete); 20–27 → 25–49% (competitive); 12–19 → 10–24% (stretch); <12 → <10% (no-bid recommended). Must-win designation: score ≥28 on a strategic program or re-compete → must-win flag; dedicated capture manager assigned, premium B&P allocated, VP BD engages directly with government customer. B&P approval authority: VP BD approves B&P commitment for opportunities >$500K estimated value at Stage 2 gate; CEO approves single-opportunity B&P spend >$150K; B&P tracker reconciled monthly against BD budget.
summary: System: Opportunity qualification — mandatory five-criterion go/no-go screen and seven-factor PWIN scoring model with must-win designation and B&P approval authority thresholds

label: IDIQ vehicle management and task order positioning
content: VP BD responsibility for strategic management of IDIQ (Indefinite Delivery/Indefinite Quantity) contract vehicles — the primary access mechanism for defense program revenue across most customer segments. Vehicle inventory register: maintain a live register of all IDIQ vehicles held as prime or sub-awardee, including vehicle name and contract number, ordering agency and program office, period of performance and expiry date, ceiling value and remaining capacity, vehicle type (MAIDIQ/GWAC/SeaPort-NxG/program-specific/single-award), active awardee count, and key customer contacts at each ordering office. Update register quarterly; flag vehicles expiring within 18 months for re-compete capture planning. Vehicle acquisition strategy: evaluate new IDIQ on-ramp opportunities annually — prioritize by (1) alignment to strategic program targets in BD pipeline; (2) favorable ceiling-to-awardee ratio (higher ratio = better per-awardee revenue potential); (3) ordering activity in agencies not covered by existing vehicles; (4) favorable task order competition rules (best value down-select preferred over LPTA). Apply standard opportunity qualification go/no-go criteria before committing proposal resources to an IDIQ on-ramp bid. Task order capture tactics: (1) Pre-RFP positioning — engage government requiring activity before task order solicitation; provide capability briefings, draft white papers, and market survey responses to shape requirements toward company strengths; no pre-solicitation contact = competing blind; (2) CPARS citation sequencing — pursue initial task orders strategically to build directly relevant CPARS citations for larger follow-on orders on the same vehicle; sequence small-to-large deliberately; (3) Pricing discipline — maintain a floor price per vehicle derived from fully-loaded cost model; use FPDS-NG to monitor competitor award prices on the same vehicle quarterly; never bid below floor regardless of volume pressure; (4) Teaming for large task orders — for orders >$2M, evaluate whether adding a complementary sub-contractor improves technical evaluation score without compressing margin below floor; analyze on a case-by-case basis, not as a default; (5) Task order sub-pipeline — maintain a dedicated sub-pipeline in CRM per vehicle tracking anticipated task orders, estimated values, PWIN, required response dates, and positioning actions outstanding. IDIQ vehicle re-compete: treat any vehicle re-compete as must-win if the vehicle supports >10% of annual revenue; initiate capture planning 18 months before expiry; brief CEO at 12-month mark with competitive assessment. Vehicle access via teaming: maintain a register of IDIQ vehicles held by active and potential teaming partners to extend pipeline reach onto vehicles where direct prime position is not held; coordinate with VP Strategy on teaming agreements structured to maximize IDIQ coverage across strategic market segments.
summary: Task: IDIQ vehicle management — vehicle inventory register, acquisition strategy, task order capture tactics, pricing discipline, CPARS citation sequencing, and vehicle re-compete planning for VP BD pipeline access

label: ERM framework — NIST SP 800-39 three-tier model, risk appetite, and 5x5 risk register
content: Enterprise Risk Management (ERM) framework implementing NIST SP 800-39 three-tier model across the defense manufacturing enterprise. Tier 1 — Organization (strategic): CEO/SMC owns enterprise risk appetite statement — written, board-approved, reviewed annually; defines tolerance bands: accept (P×I ≤9 on 5×5), treat/mitigate (10–14), escalate/transfer/terminate (≥15); enterprise risk register maintained by CEO with quarterly SMC review and annual board presentation. Tier 2 — Mission/Business Process: VP-level risk owners maintain program and functional risk registers using standardized 5×5 likelihood-impact matrix (Likelihood 1=rare/5=near-certain; Impact 1=negligible/5=catastrophic; composite P×I scores drive response tier); register entries include: risk ID, description, likelihood score, impact score, P×I composite, risk response (accept/avoid/transfer/mitigate), mitigation plan with milestones, residual risk score, owner, and next review date. Tier 3 — Operational: department managers maintain daily/weekly operational risk registers; items unresolved >2 weeks or threatening Tier 0 program critical path escalate automatically to Tier 2. Cross-tier rollup: top-5 Tier 2 risks reported to Tier 1 register quarterly; CEO presents enterprise risk summary to board annually and within 5 business days of any Tier 1 risk materialization. Risk appetite statement required elements: explicit tolerance statements per category (financial, program performance, safety/environmental, regulatory/compliance, reputational, cybersecurity, supply chain); approved by board; cascaded to all VP owners as the authority baseline for Tier 2 risk response decisions.
summary: System: NIST SP 800-39 three-tier ERM framework — risk appetite statement, 5×5 likelihood-impact register, cross-tier rollup, and board reporting cadence for CEO enterprise risk governance

label: Supply chain risk management — NIST 800-161r1, DFARS 252.204-7012, ITAR sole-source dependency tracking
content: Supply chain risk management (SCRM) program implementing NIST SP 800-161r1 and DFARS 252.204-7012 obligations across the prime/sub/supplier tiers. SCRM Plan: documented per NIST 800-161r1 Task C-SCRM-1; identifies supply chain threat actors (foreign adversary infiltration, counterfeit parts, malicious insiders), attack vectors, and countermeasures at each supply chain tier; reviewed and updated annually and within 30 days of any significant supply chain disruption event. Supplier tiered risk register: Tier A — critical/sole-source suppliers whose failure directly causes Tier 0 program impact; Tier B — significant suppliers with at least one qualified alternate; Tier C — commodity suppliers with multiple sources. DFARS 252.204-7012 compliance assessment required for all Tier A and Tier B suppliers handling Covered Defense Information (CDI): assess adequate security per NIST SP 800-171, verify 72-hour cyber incident reporting capability, confirm media preservation and submission process for malicious code. ITAR-controlled component sole-source dependency tracking: maintain a single-source dependency register for all ITAR USML-controlled parts and materials with no qualified domestic alternate; each entry includes: part number, USML category, single domestic supplier name, lead time, 90-day strategic stockpile status (adequate/marginal/deficient), alternate qualification effort status, and estimated time-to-alternate-qualification; CEO briefed on any entry with lead time >120 days and no active alternate qualification program — elevated to Tier 1 risk register. Counterfeit prevention: AS5553B (electronic parts) and AS6174 (non-electronic parts) counterfeit avoidance requirements in subcontracts; independent testing required per DFARS 252.246-7008 for suspect counterfeit components; anti-counterfeit plan documented in QMS and AS9100D Section 8.4. Annual SCRM review at SMC; immediate CEO notification for supply disruptions affecting Tier 0 program critical path.
summary: System: SCRM program per NIST 800-161r1 and DFARS 252.204-7012 — supplier risk tiering, ITAR sole-source dependency register with 90-day stockpile tracking, CDI compliance assessment, and counterfeit prevention

label: Cybersecurity risk governance — CMMC 2.0 Level 2 readiness, NIST CSF 2.0 Govern, POAM tracking
content: Cybersecurity risk governance system integrating CMMC 2.0 Level 2 assessment readiness, NIST CSF 2.0 Govern function, and Plan of Action and Milestones (POAM) management for defense manufacturing with CUI-handling obligations. CMMC 2.0 Level 2 compliance pathway: implement all 110 security practices from NIST SP 800-171 Rev 2 for contracts involving CUI; determine assessment level (self-assessment with annual affirmation for Level 2 basic programs; C3PAO third-party assessment for Level 2 advanced programs per DFARS 252.204-7019/7020/7021 clause set); submit SPRS score to Supplier Performance Risk System within 30 days of assessment completion; CEO signs annual CMMC compliance affirmation — False Claims Act liability attaches to materially false certifications per DOJ Civil Cyber-Fraud Initiative enforcement guidance. NIST CSF 2.0 Govern function implementation: establish cybersecurity governance roles — appoint Information System Security Officer (ISSO) reporting to CEO via CISO or CTO; document cybersecurity risk appetite statement consistent with Tier 1 ERM; publish cybersecurity policy suite covering NIST SP 800-171 practice families (access control, audit, configuration management, identification and authentication, incident response, maintenance, media protection, personnel security, physical protection, risk assessment, system and communications protection, system and information integrity); integrate cybersecurity risk register into enterprise Tier 1 ERM register with CEO ownership of critical cybersecurity risks. POAM management: each NIST SP 800-171 deficiency documented in POAM with: practice ID, deficiency description, remediation owner, resources required (cost and labor), scheduled completion date, milestones at 30/60/90 days, and residual risk rating until closure; CEO reviews POAM status quarterly; POAMs not closed by scheduled date escalate to Tier 2 risk register and trigger CEO corrective action mandate; all POAM items must reach closure or board-approved residual risk acceptance before C3PAO assessment submission. Incident response: 72-hour breach notification to prime contractor and DoD Cyber Crime Center (DC3) per DFARS 252.204-7012; CEO notified of any confirmed breach of CUI systems within 4 hours; preserve and isolate affected media per reporting requirements.
summary: System: Cybersecurity risk governance covering CMMC 2.0 Level 2 C3PAO readiness, NIST CSF 2.0 Govern function, CEO-reviewed POAM tracking, SPRS reporting, and 72-hour breach notification protocol

label: Executive succession pipeline — 9-box talent grid, critical position identification, emergency succession protocol
content: Executive succession pipeline management system for VP+ and critical leadership positions in defense manufacturing. 9-box talent grid: plot all VP+ candidates and high-potential managers annually on a 3×3 grid — y-axis: Performance rating (1=below expectations, 2=meets expectations, 3=exceeds expectations based on objective results and 360-degree feedback); x-axis: Leadership Potential rating (1=limited, 2=growth, 3=high based on CEO/SMC qualitative assessment and competency scores). Placements: Box 9 (High/High) — immediate succession candidate, consider for stretch assignments; Box 8 (High Performance/Medium Potential) — strong contributor, targeted functional development; Box 7 (Medium Performance/High Potential) — high-potential, requires performance coaching and stretch assignment; lower boxes assessed individually for retention, development plan, or managed transition. Grid reviewed annually at SMC; CEO chairs calibration to ensure consistent standards across VP population. Critical position identification: define critical positions as any VP+ role where a 90-day unplanned vacancy would materially impact enterprise operations, program commitments, regulatory compliance, or customer relationships; critical position list reviewed and updated annually at SMC; every critical position must have at least one documented succession candidate rated 'ready now' or 'ready in 12 months' — gap triggers immediate development action plan. Readiness ratings: Ready Now (can assume role within 30 days with minimal transition support), Ready in 12 Months (targeted development underway, stretch assignment assigned), Developmental (2–3 years to readiness, on formal development program). Emergency succession protocol (unplanned vacancy — death, incapacitation, abrupt departure): Step 1 (within 24 hours) — CEO activates pre-authorized interim designee for the affected VP role from a pre-published interim designation list updated semi-annually; Step 2 (within 48 hours) — board notification for C-suite vacancies; customer notification assessed by CEO and Legal for any vacancy with immediate contract performance implications; Step 3 (within 30 days) — CEO decision whether interim is permanent selection or formal search is initiated; if search, VP BD/HR activates search plan from succession register.
summary: System: 9-box talent grid for VP+ succession candidates, critical position identification with readiness ratings, and 3-step emergency succession protocol for unplanned vacancies

label: Leadership competency assessment — DoDI 1430.16 CQ alignment and 360-degree feedback process
content: Leadership competency assessment framework aligned to DoDI 1430.16 six Core Qualifications (CQs) applied annually to all VP+ personnel and succession candidates in defense manufacturing. Assessment dimensions — six CQs scored 1–5 (1=does not demonstrate, 3=proficient, 5=exemplary): CQ1 Leading Change (vision, strategic thinking, external awareness, innovation); CQ2 Leading People (conflict management, developing others, team building, communication, interpersonal skills); CQ3 Results Driven (accountability, customer service, decisiveness, entrepreneurship, problem solving); CQ4 Business Acumen (financial management, human capital management, technology management/digital fluency); CQ5 Building Coalitions (political savvy, influencing/negotiating, partnering); CQ6 Enterprise-Wide Perspective (external awareness, systems thinking, organizational perspective). 360-degree feedback process: for all VP+ roles, conducted annually by CEO; process — individual self-assessment; supervisor rating (CEO rates all direct VP reports; COO may rate operational VPs with CEO concurrence); 4–6 peer ratings (cross-functional VPs selected by CEO, not self-selected — prevents gaming); 3–5 direct-report ratings (anonymous to encourage candor); ratings consolidated by HR using standardized aggregation; external facilitator recommended for CEO and C-suite feedback to ensure psychological safety. Scoring and development planning: compute CQ composite score per individual; identify CQ gaps (any score <3 in two or more sub-competencies within a CQ); generate Individual Development Plan (IDP) addressing top-2 CQ gap areas with specific actions (formal program, rotational assignment, stretch project, coaching); IDP reviewed at 6-month check-in with supervisor; competency score trend tracked year-over-year in succession register. Calibration: CEO and COO conduct annual competency calibration session reviewing all VP ratings for consistency; forced ranking not required but relative standing within VP cohort discussed to ensure equitable standards. Assessment records: results are personnel records restricted to individual and CEO; succession register captures competency profile summary (CQ tier ratings) without raw scores for broader distribution.
summary: System: Annual 360-degree leadership competency assessment aligned to DoDI 1430.16 six CQs — structured rater selection, 1–5 CQ scoring, IDP generation, and CEO calibration process for VP+ succession population

label: Mentorship and executive coaching program — cleared coach requirements for SAP/SCI personnel
content: Structured mentorship and executive coaching program for defense manufacturing leadership development pipeline. Program tiers: (1) Internal mentorship — senior executive (VP+) mentors high-potential managers identified through 9-box grid (Box 7 and Box 9 priority); formal mentorship agreement with 6-month goals, monthly 1-hour sessions, and mid-point milestone review; CEO nominates and approves internal mentor-mentee pairs annually; mentor pairs span functional boundaries (e.g., engineering mentor for a supply chain high-potential) to build enterprise perspective; Internal mentor receives 360-degree feedback on mentoring effectiveness at year-end. (2) External executive coaching — retained executive coaches for VP+ and Board-designated senior leaders, particularly targeting CQ gaps identified in annual 360-degree assessment; structured 6–12 month engagements with defined coaching objectives (mapped to specific CQ sub-competencies), bi-weekly 60-minute sessions, and CEO/sponsor milestone reviews at 90 days and 6 months; coaching objectives documented in a coaching charter countersigned by coach, coachee, and CEO. Cleared coach requirements for SAP/SCI personnel: executive coaches supporting personnel with Special Access Program (SAP) briefings or SCI access must satisfy one of two cleared coach protocols — Protocol A: coach holds equivalent clearance level and is approved by Security Officer and program Security Manager before any engagement; Protocol B (preferred when clearance is impractical to obtain): coaching charter includes a classified-context limitation agreement — coach explicitly not briefed on program specifics; all sessions restricted to leadership behavior, interpersonal dynamics, and career development; no classified program content discussed; coachee acknowledges limitation in writing; FSO reviews and approves the protocol selection before engagement commencement. Approved cleared coaching vendor list: Export Compliance Officer and FSO maintain a list of coaching vendors with known cleared-personnel rosters or documented Protocol B agreements; only listed vendors used for SAP/SCI personnel. Program governance: CEO owns program; COO administers enrollment; all coaches sign NDAs and ITAR data handling agreements; coaching expenses charged to allowable G&A indirect pool per FAR 31.205-44 (employee morale, health, and welfare costs); coaching ROI assessed annually through CQ score improvement trend across participants.
summary: System: Two-tier mentorship and executive coaching program with cleared coach Protocol A (clearance required) and Protocol B (classified-context limitation) for SAP/SCI personnel, structured coaching charter, and FSO approval workflow

label: DoD customer engagement cadence — PEO executive reviews, DCMA ACO coordination, Congressional liaison
content: Structured DoD customer engagement cadence for CEO and VP-level stakeholder management in defense manufacturing. Engagement tiers and cadence: (1) Program Executive Officers (PEOs) and acquisition executive level — annual strategic alignment meeting; CEO attendance mandatory for programs with contract value >$10M or where company is sole-source; agenda: program performance posture (CPI/SPI summary), technology insertion opportunities, future platform roadmap alignment, and industrial base investment signals; coordinated with prime contractor BD leads where applicable; relationship at PEO level is a Tier 2 risk factor — loss of senior relationship requires documented continuity plan identifying alternate engagement pathway. (2) DCMA Administrative Contracting Officers (ACOs) — quarterly coordination meeting covering business system surveillance status (MMAS, EVMS, CMRS, property management, compensation, purchasing systems); VP Operations or VP Quality leads routine quarterly session; CEO participates when: DCMA has issued a Corrective Action Request (CAR), a business system is rated inadequate, or a CPSR (Contractor Purchasing System Review) is initiated; maintain cooperative engagement posture — adversarial DCMA relationships result in elevated surveillance frequency and increased audit burden; DCMA findings acknowledged in writing within 5 business days even if contested. (3) Contracting Officers (KO) and Specialists — monthly contractual touchpoints managed at Contracts/PM level; CEO engaged directly on: Cure Notices (FAR 49.607), Show Cause letters, unilateral contract modifications disputed by company, or contractor claims under the Contract Disputes Act; proactive written notification to KO required for any material program performance concern before it becomes a government-discovered deficiency. (4) Congressional and legislative liaison — applicable for companies pursuing SBIR/STTR, OTA, or programs with Congressional program champions (line-item appropriations, NDAA mandates); annual participation in industry days and NDIA/AIA forums providing Congressional staff access; all contact with Congressional staff routes through government affairs counsel or per DFARS ethics guidance; avoid ex parte communications that could compromise source selection integrity under FAR 3.104. Executive engagement log: CEO Chief of Staff maintains a stakeholder relationship map and engagement log per customer; updated after each VP+ level touchpoint; reviewed quarterly as part of enterprise stakeholder risk assessment.
summary: System: DoD customer engagement cadence — annual PEO strategic alignment, quarterly DCMA ACO coordination, KO-level contractual touchpoints, and Congressional liaison protocol with engagement log and relationship risk tracking

label: Prime contractor teaming and subcontract governance — OCI management per FAR 9.5, teaming agreement lifecycle
content: Prime contractor teaming and subcontract relationship governance system covering OCI management, teaming agreement lifecycle, and workshare integrity for defense manufacturing. OCI management per FAR 9.5: three OCI categories applicable — (1) Unequal access to information: access to a competitor's proprietary data or source selection information through a prime teaming arrangement creates an unfair advantage; mitigate via firewall provisions and excluded personnel lists in teaming agreement. (2) Impaired objectivity: a role providing advisory, evaluation, or systems engineering support to a government customer while also competing for or performing a related contract; avoid dual-role arrangements; disclose to government CO immediately if discovered. (3) Biased ground rules: participating in specification development or requirements definition that advantages own products or services; disclose and recuse relevant personnel from requirements-setting activities. OCI identification and mitigation: CEO and Export Compliance Officer/Legal review every new teaming arrangement for OCI exposure before teaming agreement execution; mitigation plan documented in teaming agreement (firewall provisions, certified personnel exclusion list, periodic OCI compliance certifications); proactively disclose known or potential OCI to contracting officer — concealed OCI is grounds for contract rescission, suspension, and False Claims Act liability. Teaming agreement lifecycle: Pre-solicitation (execute NDA within 10 days of any proprietary information exchange — before RFP release and before technical discussions begin); Pre-RFP (execute teaming agreement ≥60 days before RFP release or before any proposals submitted to prime; required elements: workshare allocation by task/CLIN, exclusivity terms, IP ownership and license scope, data rights protection provisions, protest cooperation clause, dispute resolution procedure, agreement termination conditions if prime or sub is not awarded); Post-award (execute subcontract within 90 days of prime contract award per prime's flow-down obligation under FAR 52.244-2; workshare deviation from teaming agreement of >10% requires written prime approval and CEO signature). Workshare governance: VP BD maintains workshare register per prime partner and program; actual workshare received vs. teaming commitment tracked quarterly; chronic shortfalls escalated to CEO for prime relationship engagement; persistent workshare violations are grounds for teaming agreement termination and are documented for future teaming partner qualification assessments.
summary: System: OCI management (FAR 9.5 three-category framework), teaming agreement lifecycle (NDA → teaming agreement → subcontract), workshare governance register, and CEO/Legal OCI review gate for all prime teaming arrangements

label: International customer relationship management — FMS/DCS pipeline, DSP-5/DSP-73 coordination with DDTC
content: International customer relationship management system for Foreign Military Sales (FMS) and Direct Commercial Sale (DCS) channels, covering pipeline tracking, export license lifecycle, and DDTC coordination. FMS/DCS pipeline register: maintained in CRM by VP BD; each entry includes — allied nation program name, procurement agency and country, applicable FMS case number (DSCA) or DCS contract vehicle, ITAR USML or EAR ECCN classification of all deliverables, active license number and type (DSP-5/DSP-73/TAA), license expiration date, program total and annual value, current stage, prime contractor intermediary (if FMS prime-facilitated), CEO/VP relationship sponsor; reviewed semi-annually by CEO and Export Compliance Officer; entries with license expiring within 90 days auto-flagged for renewal action. DSP-5 (Permanent Export License) management: required for permanent export of defense articles or technical data on the USML to foreign persons or countries; Export Compliance Officer initiates application via DECCS (State Dept system); standard processing 30–60 days; expedited (RUSH) processing requested for national security urgency with written justification; CEO signs DSP-5 as Empowered Official — legal certification of accuracy; any material misstatement is a criminal ITAR violation. DSP-73 (Temporary Import/Export License) management: for USML articles exported temporarily (demo hardware, GFE for foreign testing, loan items); strict re-import tracking required — export must be returned within license validity period; Export Compliance Officer maintains custody log for all DSP-73 shipments; customs broker briefed on re-import requirements before each shipment. Retransfer and re-export controls: all licenses specify approved end-users and end-use; retransfer or re-export to any third party not named on the license requires DDTC prior approval (Congressional notification for items above certain thresholds per AECA Section 36(b)); subcontract and teaming agreements include mandatory ITAR flow-down clause prohibiting unauthorized retransfer. Foreign person access control: any foreign national (including domestic U.S.-based employees or contractors) who may access ITAR-controlled technical data must be authorized via TAA (ITAR Part 124), license exception per ITAR 126.18 (approved allied nations for eligible articles), or individual DSP-5 — FSO and Export Compliance Officer approve access lists before program kickoff; no foreign national access to controlled data without written authorization on file. Denied parties screening: all international customers, end-users, prime intermediaries, and foreign sub-tier suppliers screened against OFAC SDN list, State Dept Debarred Parties List (ITAR Part 127), and BIS Denied Parties List before contract execution and at each license renewal; screening results documented with date and screener identity.
summary: System: International customer CRM register with FMS/DCS pipeline tracking, DSP-5/DSP-73 license lifecycle management via DDTC, retransfer controls, foreign person access authorization, and denied-parties screening protocol

label: Capture management process (Shipley BD Lifecycle)
content: VP BD task for executing structured capture management across the Shipley BD Lifecycle. Phase activities: (1) Identify/Qualify (18+ months out) — identify opportunity via SAM.gov, GovWin, customer engagement; apply five-criterion go/no-bid screen (see #1482); create opportunity record in CRM with estimated value, NAICS, POP, and customer contacts. (2) Capture (12–18 months out) — appoint capture manager for PWIN ≥35% or strategic opportunities; develop capture plan covering: customer hot buttons and evaluation criteria priorities, incumbent analysis via FPDS-NG, competitive landscape with known competitors and pricing posture, preliminary win themes and discriminators, teaming strategy, and B&P budget request. (3) Pre-RFP Shaping (6–12 months out) — engage government through RFI responses, industry days, and direct capability briefings; shape requirements toward company strengths where ethical and permitted; draft technical white papers; execute teaming agreements ≥6 months before RFP. (4) Proposal Development (RFP drop to submittal) — activate proposal manager and volume leads; initiate color team cycle (Pink→Red→Gold); ensure win themes flow consistently through all volumes; comply exactly with Section L requirements. (5) Post-Submittal — prepare oral presentation if required; request debrief within 5 business days of award announcement; log win/loss lessons in CRM within 10 days. Capture plan required for all competitive opportunities ≥$1M, all re-competes, and all must-win designations. Capture manager appointed for all opportunities ≥$2M. Capture plan reviewed by VP BD at 12-month, 6-month, and RFP-drop checkpoints.
summary: Task: Shipley BD Lifecycle capture management — five-phase process (identify/qualify, capture, pre-RFP shaping, proposal, post-submittal) with capture plan requirements, capture manager thresholds, and checkpoint cadence

label: Win theme development and discriminator strategy
content: VP BD task for developing win themes aligned to government evaluation criteria and source selection factors. Win themes defined as benefit-oriented statements that answer 'why us over competitors' from the government evaluator's perspective; each theme must be: (a) traceable to a specific evaluation criterion in Section M, (b) discriminating — competitor cannot make the same claim credibly, and (c) provable — supported by CPARS ratings, certifications, analogous program data, or demonstrable capability. Development process: (1) Review Section M (Evaluation Criteria) and Section L (Instructions); map factors to relative importance weighting (stated or inferable); build evaluation criteria matrix. (2) For each significant criterion, identify company discriminators from Three Cs company audit — NADCAP certifications competitors lack, CPARS Exceptional ratings on analogous work, cleared workforce depth, proprietary manufacturing process, co-development history with the customer. (3) Develop 3–5 master win themes spanning all volumes; assign a theme champion per theme enforcing consistent expression across sections. (4) Validate at Pink Team: are themes in executive summary and section narratives? (5) Stress-test at Red Team: are themes differentiated from known competitor claims? Discriminator test: if a competitor could copy the statement with equal credibility, it is not a discriminator — replace with specific, evidence-backed language. Common defense manufacturing discriminators: zero-defect CPARS history on analogous programs, unique NADCAP scope not held by competitors, cleared workforce depth exceeding minimum RFP requirements, sole-source TDP ownership enabling competitive delivery schedule, DFM co-engineering history with the prime. Win theme register: maintain per active capture listing themes, supporting evidence, proposed volume placement, and Red Team validation status.
summary: Task: Win theme development and discriminator strategy — evaluation criteria tracing, discriminator identification, theme champion assignment, and win theme register for VP BD capture management

label: Customer relationship management — DoD, primes, and international
content: VP BD task managing structured relationships across three primary defense customer segments. DoD Program Offices: maintain a named relationship map per program (Program Manager, Deputy PM, Contracting Officer, COR, key technical leads) with relationship strength rating (1–5) and last meaningful engagement date; engage through quarterly capability updates, industry day attendance, RFI responses, and program review participation where invited; deliver bad news early (schedule/cost risk) to build trust before source selections; request CPARS debriefs after each evaluation period to understand scoring trends; treat any government key contact departure as a Tier 2 relationship continuity risk requiring immediate backup contact development. Prime Contractors: maintain named relationships with workshare sponsor at each prime (Program Director, Supply Chain Manager, Technical Lead); track Approved Supplier List (ASL) qualification status and renewal dates; attend prime supplier conferences; monitor prime M&A events and new program wins for sub-tier opportunities; deliver on-time and on-spec performance as the prime relationship currency. International Customers (FMS/DCS): coordinate all international technical engagement through Export Compliance Officer — required before sharing ITAR-controlled technical content; identify FMS case triggers via DSCA and prime foreign military BD teams; DCS engagement requires DSP-5 export license pre-approval; invest in in-country relationships for key FMS nations (UK, Australia, Japan, Israel, Saudi Arabia per DSCA volume data); understand offset/industrial participation requirements per allied nation procurement rules; apply ITAR/EAR classification controls at initiation of any international customer engagement. CRM tool discipline: log all customer interactions within 24 hours; VP BD reviews CRM completeness monthly; flag single-point-of-failure relationships (one person = sole contact) in Tier 2 risk register.
summary: Task: VP BD customer relationship management across DoD program offices (CPARS/engagement cadence), prime contractors (ASL qualification/supply chain), and international FMS/DCS customers (ITAR controls/offset requirements)

label: Strategic teaming and teammate selection criteria
content: VP BD task for developing and executing teaming strategies that maximize proposal competitiveness and long-term market coverage. Teaming decision criteria — evaluate partners on five dimensions: (1) Capability complement: does the teammate provide capability (technical, NADCAP certifications, NAICS codes, clearance levels) that addresses an evaluation criterion gap the company has? (2) Customer relationship value: does the teammate bring direct working relationships with target program office or prime customer that improve access or trust?
