(3) IDIQ vehicle access: does the teammate hold a vehicle enabling proposal submission otherwise unavailable to the company (see vehicle register in #1483)? (4) Set-aside and small business status: does teammate status improve small business subcontracting plan compliance or overall team eligibility? (5) Workshare sustainability: are proposed workshare percentages economically viable for both parties without compressing total team margin below floor? Teammate selection process: identify capability/relationship gaps vs. anticipated evaluation criteria → short-list candidates using Three Cs company audit, FPDS-NG partner award history, and existing relationship inventory → assess each candidate on five criteria plus financial stability (D&B rating), DCAA audit status, AS9100 compliance, clearance facility status, and absence of conflict of interest → initiate teaming discussions ≥12 months before RFP on strategic pursuits → execute teaming agreement (TA) before RFP release covering workshare percentages, IP ownership and protection, exclusivity scope and duration, proposal cost-sharing split, and dispute resolution mechanism. Avoid: exclusive TAs that prevent pivoting to a stronger partner; TAs with financially distressed companies creating program risk; workshare allocations that compress total team margin below sustainable floor. Mentor-protégé programs: DoD Mentor-Protégé Program (10 U.S.C. § 4902) and SBA 8(a) provide competitive differentiation on set-aside-adjacent programs; evaluate mentor role eligibility based on company size, financial stability, and prime contract status.
summary: Task: Strategic teaming and teammate selection — five-dimension partner evaluation (capability, customer access, IDIQ vehicles, set-aside status, workshare), TA execution checklist, and mentor-protégé program assessment for VP BD

label: Competitive intelligence for capture — FPDS-NG and USASpending.gov
content: Operational CI system for VP BD capture activities using federal award databases. FPDS-NG capture applications: (1) Incumbent identification — query by agency, program office, and NAICS code to identify current contract awardee, contract type, period, value, and ceiling; note whether incumbent has follow-on options and their estimated exercise probability. (2) Pricing posture — extract historical award prices for analogous contracts (same NAICS, agency, similar scope); adjust for inflation using DoD CAPE indices and contract type differences (FFP vs. CPFF); compare to internal cost model to assess price-to-win (PTW) competitiveness; flag opportunities where fully-loaded cost exceeds estimated PTW by >15% — escalate pricing concern to capture plan and CFO. (3) Competitor win history — pull all awards to primary competitors in target NAICS codes and program offices over rolling 8 quarters; identify competitor program office relationships by frequency of awards to same ordering office; assess competitor expansion or contraction in the target segment. (4) IDIQ utilization analysis — for IDIQ task order opportunities, analyze historical task order frequency, average award value, and total utilization vs. ceiling to estimate realistic revenue potential. USASpending.gov: macro-level award history, OTA and grant visibility, and allied-nation FMS award data; cross-reference with FPDS-NG for validation. Capture CI deliverable: for each pursuit ≥$1M, produce a 1-page CI summary with incumbent details, known competitors, estimated pricing range, contract type risk profile, and strategic implications; update when FPDS-NG data changes materially (new award, contract modification). Tools: FPDS-NG (fpds.gov), USASpending.gov, SAM.gov, GovWin IQ (Deltek), Bloomberg Government, FOIA requests for source selection evaluation reports. This CI capability is distinct from VP Strategy CI (which serves portfolio and Five Forces analysis); VP BD CI is operationally focused on specific capture pursuits.
summary: System: VP BD competitive intelligence for capture — FPDS-NG incumbent identification, pricing posture extraction, competitor win history analysis, and IDIQ utilization assessment; produces 1-page CI summary per pursuit ≥$1M

label: Color team review process — Pink, Red, and Gold teams
content: Proposal quality gate system ensuring VP BD proposal quality at three review milestones. Pink Team (30–40% complete, ~2–3 weeks post-RFP drop): purpose — assess win theme presence and discriminating quality, solution approach alignment to stated requirements, proposal structure compliance with Section L instructions, and identification of compliance gaps early enough to correct; participants — capture manager, proposal manager, volume leads, and one independent reviewer not on the proposal team; output — annotated draft with prioritized actionable comments and compliance matrix gap list; VP BD reviews pink team findings and directs rework priorities before Red Team. Red Team (60–75% complete, 10–14 days before submittal): comprehensive evaluation as a mock government source selection board; evaluators score each section against Section M criteria rating strengths, weaknesses, and significant weaknesses; assess win theme consistency across all volumes, technical approach credibility, cost realism, and management approach; identify strengths (preserve), weaknesses (fix), and discriminators (enhance); participants — VP BD, two or more independent senior reviewers with source selection experience, and capture manager; output — written red team evaluation scorecard with section-by-section assessment and ranked rework list with owner and due date. Gold Team (90% complete, 3–5 days before submittal): executive review for overall message integrity, cover-to-cover compliance check (Section L/M cross-reference matrix), price reasonableness review (VP BD + CFO for pursuits >$2M), and executive summary final validation; participants — VP BD, CEO or COO for must-win programs, contracts lead; output — signed approval to submit or specific rework list with hard submission-day deadline. Resource thresholds: Pink + Red + Gold for all competitive opportunities ≥$2M and all must-wins; Pink + Red for $500K–$2M; BD checklist self-review only for <$500K. All color team reviews produce written reports retained in the capture file; action items tracked to closure before submission.
summary: System: Color team review process (Pink/Red/Gold) — three-gate proposal quality assurance with participant requirements, evaluation criteria scoring methodology, output deliverables, and resource thresholds by pursuit value

label: SMC strategic action item approval or tabling decision
content: Decision authority for CEO/SMC to approve or table strategic action items at SMC meetings. APPROVE when all three criteria are met: (1) strategic alignment score ≥7/10 on enterprise strategy fit assessment; (2) CFO confirms resource availability — uncommitted budget headroom sufficient for the action's stated resource requirement; (3) no unresolved Tier 1 risk dependencies that block implementation. TABLE when: alignment score <7/10; OR CFO cannot confirm resource commitment; OR one or more Tier 1 risk dependencies remain open and unmitigated. Tabled items require a documented resolution plan with named owner and resubmission deadline before returning to the SMC agenda. Partial satisfaction (e.g., alignment ≥7 but resources uncommitted) does not qualify for approval — all three criteria must clear simultaneously. CEO holds final approval authority; concurrence recorded in Program Decision Memorandum within 5 business days.
summary: Decision: Approve SMC strategic action items when alignment ≥7/10, CFO confirms resources, and no open Tier 1 risk dependencies; table when alignment <7/10 or resources uncommitted

label: PMC KDP gate approval, conditional approval, or redirect decision
content: Three-outcome decision at each KDP milestone gate governed by EVM performance, TRL attainment, and risk register status. APPROVE (proceed to next phase): CPI >0.95 AND SPI >0.90 AND TRL meets gate minimum (TRL ≥4 at KDP-B; TRL ≥6 at KDP-C; TRL ≥8 at KDP-D) AND no open critical risks (P×I ≥15) on the active risk register. CONDITIONAL APPROVAL (proceed with mandatory corrective actions): CPI in range 0.90–0.95 with PMC-accepted corrective action plan; SPI ≥0.85 with credible schedule recovery plan; all mandatory actions and re-review date (≤30 days) captured in PDM before program proceeds. REDIRECT (return program to prior phase with documented deficiency list): CPI <0.85; OR schedule slip >60 calendar days from baselined milestone date; OR TRL below gate minimum with no credible path to close before next KDP; OR open critical risk (P×I ≥15) with no accepted mitigation plan. TERMINATION: CEO authority only; required when program has no viable recovery path or strategic basis. CPI/SPI calculated from current-period EVM data submitted in gate package. COO chairs PMC; PDM issued within 5 business days of gate review.
summary: Decision: Approve KDP gate when CPI>0.95, SPI>0.90, TRL meets gate minimum, no open critical risks; conditional at CPI 0.90–0.95 with corrective plan; redirect when CPI<0.85 or schedule slip >60 days

label: CIC capital investment approval or denial decision
content: Decision logic for approving or denying capital investment business cases submitted to the Capital Investment Council. APPROVE when all four criteria are met: (1) CIC evaluation score ≥70/100 on standard rubric (ROI/payback 40%, strategic alignment 30%, risk rating 20%, urgency 10%); (2) payback period ≤4 years for non-strategic investments; (3) NPV positive at 5% discount rate calculated over asset useful life; (4) strategic alignment confirmed — explicit documented link to at least one current strategic plan objective or active program requirement. DENY when: CIC score <60/100; OR payback period >6 years without a board-approved strategic exception; OR NPV negative at 5% discount rate with no strategic override justification accepted by CEO. CONDITIONAL APPROVAL (score 60–69 with payback 4–6 years): CFO and requesting VP must address specified deficiencies within 30 days before funds are released; failure to close conditions reverts to denial. Decision authority tiers: CFO approves ≤$100K with favorable scoring; CEO approves $100K–$500K; CEO plus board required for >$500K. Emergency capital authority: Plant Manager may approve up to $10K for Tier 0 production stoppage prevention; must ratify through CIC within 30 days. CFO chairs CIC; PDM issued within 5 business days.
summary: Decision: Approve CIC capital requests when score ≥70/100, payback ≤4 years, NPV positive at 5% discount rate, and strategic alignment confirmed; deny when score <60/100 or payback >6 years

label: ERM risk accept, treat/mitigate, or escalate/transfer decision
content: Three-tier risk disposition decision based on composite P×I score on the enterprise 5×5 likelihood-impact matrix (Likelihood 1=rare to 5=near-certain; Impact 1=negligible to 5=catastrophic; composite P×I = Likelihood × Impact, max 25). ACCEPT (no active mitigation; monitor only): composite P×I ≤9; risk is within enterprise risk appetite; document in risk register with named owner and monitoring cadence; review quarterly at SMC; no mitigation budget required. TREAT/MITIGATE (active risk response required): composite P×I 10–14; risk owner develops and executes formal mitigation plan with milestones within 60 days; residual P×I score documented after mitigation; if residual P×I remains ≥10 after execution of mitigation plan, re-escalate to next tier; status reported monthly to CEO. ESCALATE TO BOARD OR TRANSFER (senior governance action required): composite P×I ≥15; CEO presents to board within 30 days of risk identification or any materialization event; board-level response options include — (a) formal board risk acceptance with documented rationale; (b) risk transfer via insurance, contract indemnification clause, or teaming workshare reallocation; (c) risk avoidance through program termination, contract re-negotiation, or market exit decision. All P×I ≥15 risks are CEO-owned Tier 1 register entries. Automatic escalation from Tier 2: any program or functional risk register entry reaching P×I ≥15 elevates to Tier 1 within 48 hours with VP owner notifying CEO directly.
summary: Decision: Accept enterprise risks with P×I ≤9; treat/mitigate when P×I 10–14 with 60-day mitigation plan; escalate to board or transfer when P×I ≥15

label: Supplier qualification or disqualification decision
content:
QUALIFY supplier when all three conditions are met: (1) SCRM assessment score >75th percentile on the supplier risk scoring rubric (tiered across financial stability, delivery performance, cybersecurity posture, counterfeit risk, and geopolitical exposure); (2) DFARS 252.204-7012 compliance verified for any supplier handling Covered Defense Information — adequate security per NIST SP 800-171 confirmed, 72-hour cyber incident reporting capability documented, and media preservation process validated; (3) no unresolved counterfeit findings — AS5553B (electronic parts) and AS6174 (non-electronic parts) screens cleared with no open suspect counterfeit investigation and no pending SDR requiring disposition.

DISQUALIFY supplier when: sole-source lead time >120 calendar days AND no active alternate supplier qualification program exists for the affected part number, material, or commodity class. Disqualification elevates the entry to the ITAR sole-source dependency register and the Tier 1 enterprise risk register; CEO notified within 5 business days per SCRM protocol. Disqualified sole-source entries require an alternate qualification sprint plan submitted to the COO within 30 days of disqualification.
summary: Decision: Qualify supplier when SCRM score >75th pct, DFARS 252.204-7012 compliance verified, and no open counterfeit findings; disqualify when sole-source lead time >120 days with no active alternate qualification program

label: Authorize CMMC C3PAO assessment scheduling
content:
AUTHORIZE C3PAO assessment scheduling when all four conditions are met: (1) POAM open items <5, with all remaining open POAMs in active remediation with documented milestones and assigned owners; (2) SPRS score >=100 (SPRS scale: maximum 110; score of 100 indicates ≤10 weighted practice-deduction points remaining, signaling near-full implementation); (3) all 110 NIST SP 800-171 Rev 2 security practices implemented and internally verified — each practice has documented implementation evidence reviewed by the ISSO; (4) internal pre-assessment completed within the prior 90 days with no critical findings — no practice rated 'not implemented' on any high-weighted control family (access control, identification and authentication, system and communications protection).

DO NOT AUTHORIZE when: POAM open items >=5; OR SPRS score <100; OR any of the 110 practices lacks documented implementation evidence; OR the internal pre-assessment surfaced critical findings still open at the time of scheduling. Re-evaluate after a targeted 60-day remediation sprint addressing all blocking conditions. CEO signs annual CMMC compliance affirmation; False Claims Act liability attaches to materially inaccurate certifications per DOJ Civil Cyber-Fraud Initiative enforcement guidance.
summary: Decision: Authorize CMMC C3PAO assessment scheduling when POAM open items <5, SPRS score >=100, all 110 NIST SP 800-171 practices implemented and verified, and no critical findings in internal pre-assessment

label: Confirm succession readiness for critical VP+ positions
content:
CONFIRM SUCCESSION READINESS for a critical VP+ position when: at least one documented candidate is rated Ready Now in the succession register AND holds a current 9-box placement in Box 7 (Medium Performance/High Potential), Box 8 (High Performance/Medium Potential), or Box 9 (High Performance/High Potential) from the most recent annual SMC talent calibration. Confirmation is documented in the succession register and reviewed at the next annual SMC cycle.

FLAG SUCCESSION GAP when: no Ready Now candidate with Box 7, 8, or 9 placement exists for any critical VP+ position at the conclusion of the annual talent review. Gap triggers the following actions within defined timelines: (1) CEO notified within 5 business days; (2) position added to the Tier 2 enterprise risk register with COO as owner; (3) formal development action plan assigned to the highest-rated developmental candidate within 30 days, including stretch assignment, coaching engagement, and 12-month readiness milestone; (4) interim succession designee named from the pre-published interim designation list within 30 days pending candidate readiness development. Gap status reviewed at 6-month SMC checkpoint; if unresolved at 12-month mark, escalates to CEO-directed external search.
summary: Decision: Confirm succession readiness when position has >=1 Ready Now candidate rated in 9-box Box 7, 8, or 9; flag gap and trigger development action plan when no Ready Now candidate exists for any critical VP+ position

label: Mandate IDP corrective action for leadership competency deficiencies
content:
MANDATE IDP CORRECTIVE ACTION when: any VP+ or succession candidate records a CQ composite score <3.0 on the same Core Qualification (CQ1–CQ6) in two consecutive annual 360-degree assessments. Mandate requires: IDP corrective action plan submitted to CEO within 30 days identifying specific development activities (formal program, rotational assignment, coaching), timeline, accountable milestone owner, and 12-month re-assessment success criteria. HR tracks IDP execution quarterly; VP Strategy flags IDP mandate status in the succession register. Failure to submit an IDP within 30 days or to demonstrate measurable improvement at the 12-month re-assessment constitutes a succession register downgrade.

ESCALATE TO CEO DIRECT INTERVENTION when: any C-suite member (CEO direct report) records a CQ composite score <2.5 on any Core Qualification in any single annual assessment. Escalation actions: (1) CEO conducts one-on-one intervention meeting within 15 business days of assessment completion; (2) joint IDP countersigned by CEO and individual within 30 days; (3) external executive coach engagement mandatory per cleared coach protocol in #1626; (4) formal re-assessment at the 6-month mark — if CQ score remains <2.5 after one full development cycle, SMC succession plan activation is mandatory, including board notification for the affected C-suite role.
summary: Decision: Mandate IDP corrective action when any CQ composite score <3.0 on two consecutive annual 360-degree assessments; escalate to CEO direct intervention when any C-suite member scores <2.5 on any CQ

label: Escalate DoD customer relationship to CEO direct engagement
content: Decision to elevate a DoD customer relationship from VP/staff-level management to CEO direct engagement. ESCALATE (CEO engages directly) when ANY of the following conditions is met: (1) DCMA issues a Corrective Action Request (CAR) on any active contract — CEO engagement within 5 business days; written response plan to DCMA within 10 business days. (2) Any business system (MMAS, EVMS, CMRS, property management, compensation, or purchasing system) is rated inadequate by DCMA — CEO notified same day; CEO attends next DCMA coordination meeting. (3) Contracting Officer issues a Cure Notice per FAR 49.607 — CEO reviews notice within 24 hours; leads internal response team; all external communications to KO signed by CEO. (4) Annual government customer satisfaction survey score drops below 3.5/5.0 — CEO initiates relationship recovery plan within 30 days; schedules direct engagement meeting with program office within 45 days. DO NOT ESCALATE (maintain VP/staff-level engagement) when: no above triggers are active AND DCMA business system ratings are all Acceptable or higher AND no open CARs AND customer satisfaction score ≥3.5/5.0. Escalation decisions documented in the executive stakeholder engagement log; CEO Chief of Staff notified simultaneously with CEO for all trigger events.
summary: Decision: Escalate DoD customer engagement to CEO when DCMA CAR issued, any business system rated inadequate, Cure Notice received, or customer satisfaction score <3.5/5.0

label: Approve or reject prime contractor teaming arrangement
content: Decision to approve or reject a proposed prime contractor teaming arrangement prior to teaming agreement execution. APPROVE when ALL three conditions are satisfied: (1) OCI review by Legal and Export Compliance Officer complete with no unmitigated category-1 OCI (unequal access to information, impaired objectivity, or biased ground rules per FAR 9.5); documented mitigation acceptable for any residual lower-category OCI; (2) workshare allocation to company is ≥10% of total program value as stated in the draft teaming agreement — workshare below 10% does not generate economically sustainable return relative to teaming overhead and BD investment; (3) IP and data rights protections are adequate per ITAR — teaming agreement includes ITAR flow-down clause, foreign person access restrictions on controlled technical data, and explicit IP ownership and license scope provisions protecting company proprietary data from unauthorized prime use or retransfer. REJECT when ANY of the following conditions exists: (a) any unmitigated category-1 OCI identified by Legal — no mitigation plan acceptable for category-1 conflicts; teaming is disqualifying; (b) proposed workshare allocation <5% of total program value — below minimum threshold for economically viable participation regardless of relationship value; (c) IP/data rights protections absent or inadequate (no ITAR flow-down, no proprietary data markings provision, or prime claims unlimited rights to company-developed data). CONDITIONAL APPROVAL zone (workshare 5–9%): CEO may approve with documented strategic rationale (IDIQ vehicle access, relationship investment, market development) and VP BD written justification; requires annual workshare performance review. Decision documented in teaming arrangement register; Legal countersigns approval.
summary: Decision: Approve teaming when OCI review clear, workshare ≥10% of program value, and IP/ITAR protections adequate; reject when any unmitigated category-1 OCI or workshare <5%

label: Approve or reject FMS/DCS international pursuit
content: Decision to approve or reject entry into an international defense pursuit via Foreign Military Sales (FMS) or Direct Commercial Sale (DCS) channel. APPROVE when ALL four conditions are met: (1) DDTC license pathway confirmed feasible by Export Compliance Officer — ITAR USML classification determined, DSP-5 or TAA license type identified, no known DDTC policy bar against the transaction, and Legal has confirmed no Congressional notification threshold (AECA Section 36(b)) would block or materially delay the license; (2) Denied-parties screening clear for ALL end-users, foreign government agencies, prime intermediaries, and known sub-tier suppliers — screened against OFAC SDN list, State Dept Debarred Parties List (ITAR Part 127), and BIS Denied Parties List; screening results documented with date and screener identity; (3) Country is not under ITAR embargo per ITAR Part 126.1 — any country listed at 22 CFR 126.1 is an automatic disqualifier with no waiver authority at company level; (4) Estimated export license processing time (DSP-5: 30–60 days standard, up to 120+ days for complex cases) is compatible with program schedule — customer solicitation response deadline, prime RFP drop, or contract award milestone must allow sufficient processing time; if incompatible, VP BD must obtain prime schedule relief or pursue expedited RUSH processing (requires national security written justification) before approval. REJECT when ANY condition fails: known DDTC policy obstacle to license approval; any denied-party match not cleared by Export Compliance Officer; country listed under ITAR Part 126.1 embargo; or license processing time incompatible with program schedule and no relief or RUSH pathway available. All approval and rejection decisions signed by CEO as Empowered Official and logged in the FMS/DCS pipeline register with the Export Compliance Officer co-signature.
summary: Decision: Approve FMS/DCS international pursuit when DDTC license pathway feasible, denied-parties screening clear, country not under ITAR Part 126.1 embargo, and license processing time compatible with program schedule

label: Pricing strategy, price-to-win analysis, and FPRA governance
content: VP BD responsibility for pricing strategy, price-to-win (PTW) analysis, and forward pricing rate governance across all pursuit types. PTW methodology: (1) FPDS-NG benchmarking — for each pursuit, pull historical award prices for analogous contracts (same NAICS, agency, scope complexity, quantity tier) from FPDS-NG; adjust for contract type, award-year inflation using DoD CAPE escalation indices, and quantity differences to derive a PTW range (floor/mid/ceiling) before bottoms-up cost buildup. (2) Bottoms-up cost model — VP BD coordinates with VP Operations (direct labor hours and rates), supply chain (material and subcontract pricing), and CFO (indirect rates, fee policy) to build a fully-loaded cost estimate; compare to PTW benchmark; if cost-at-fee exceeds PTW mid by >5%, identify and implement cost reduction actions or escalate to CEO as a below-floor submission risk requiring explicit approval. Contract type selection: FFP (Firm Fixed Price) — government-preferred for stable, well-defined scope; transfers cost risk to contractor; appropriate only when internal yield and Cpk history support confident estimate; protects margin on efficient execution, exposes company to overrun on immature scope. CPFF (Cost Plus Fixed Fee) — suited for development and prototype tasks with undefined scope; government bears cost risk; fee typically 8–12% of estimated cost per DFARS 215.404 weighted profit guidelines; lower margin ceiling, lower downside exposure. T&M (Time and Materials) — appropriate for services with indeterminate scope and duration; requires VP BD approval before commitment due to reduced pricing discipline incentive; not preferred for manufacturing-intensive deliverables. FPRA governance: Forward Pricing Rate Agreements negotiated with DCAA establish agreed labor and indirect rates for proposal use; VP BD ensures all proposals use current FPRA rates or provisional FPRRs where FPRAs are not in place; any rate deviation requires CFO and VP BD approval with disclosure to contracting officer; significant rate changes (>5%) require VP BD to recalibrate PTW on all active pursuits; CFO leads annual FPRA renegotiation cycle with DCAA. Competitive floor discipline: VP BD maintains a minimum bid floor per contract type derived from fully-loaded cost model; no proposal submitted below floor without CEO approval and documented rationale; floor is not negotiable under volume pressure alone.
summary: Task: VP BD pricing strategy — PTW analysis via FPDS-NG benchmarking, contract type selection criteria (FFP/CPFF/T&M), and FPRA governance for competitive and compliant proposal pricing

label: BD process governance — Shipley gates 0–4, BD operating rhythm, and B&P budget management
content: CEO review triggered for must-win programs and cumulative single-opportunity B&P commitments >$500K. Gate 3 (Bid Decision — Proposal Initiation) — at RFP drop, VP BD conducts final bid/no-bid review against actual RFP requirements; validates capture plan assumptions vs. solicitation; approves proposal team staffing and total B&P budget for proposal phase; CEO approval required when cumulative single-opportunity B&P exceeds $500K.
summary: Task: VP BD process governance — Shipley gates 0–4 authority and criteria, BD operating rhythm (daily/weekly/monthly/quarterly cadence), and B&P budget commitment authority with tracking discipline

label: Market diversification — defense adjacencies, commercial aerospace, and international FMS/DCS
content: VP BD responsibility for executing market diversification strategy across three vectors, coordinated with VP Strategy's Ansoff and Three Horizons frameworks. Defense adjacencies: identify adjacent DoD customer segments, domains, and platform types not in the active pipeline; adjacency selection criteria — ≥70% capability overlap with current certifications, at least one existing relationship for warm introduction, and addressable contract value ≥$5M/year per FPDS-NG; priority adjacencies in FYDP growth domains (autonomous systems, directed energy, space, hypersonics); pursue initial adjacency entry through SBIR Phase I/II solicitations (non-competitive entry), industry day attendance (relationship building), and targeted unsolicited white papers to adjacent program offices; VP BD approves B&P commitment for adjacency pursuits after VP Strategy confirms alignment to Three Horizons H2 pipeline (#1389). Commercial aerospace crossover: applicable when existing manufacturing processes (AS9100, NADCAP certifications, tight-tolerance machining) apply directly to FAA/EASA-certified production without fundamental requalification; VP BD coordinates with VP Strategy on diversification risk score (#1424) before committing BD resources — commercial pursuits proceed only when risk score ≤18 or CEO explicitly approves; initial entry via Tier 1 prime supplier qualification processes (Boeing, Airbus, Spirit AeroSystems, Collins Aerospace commercial divisions); commercial pricing discipline differs materially from cost-plus defense — capture team adjusts PTW methodology and margin floor expectations accordingly. International FMS/DCS: VP BD maintains FMS/DCS pipeline register per #1632 tracking allied nation programs, DSCA FMS case numbers, ITAR license status, and prime intermediaries; FMS market entry pursued primarily through prime contractor BD relationships as the most cost-effective pathway; DCS engagement requires Export Compliance Officer approval and DSP-5 license pre-authorization before any technical content exchange (#1629); priority target nations by DSCA sales volume — UK, Australia, Japan, Israel, Poland; international pursuits require CEO and Export Compliance Officer approval before any B&P commitment. Diversification balance: VP BD reports diversification mix (defense core vs. adjacency vs. commercial vs. international as % of Qualified+ pipeline) at quarterly SMC review; no single customer segment to exceed 60% of pipeline without CEO exception.
summary: Task: VP BD market diversification — defense adjacency entry via SBIR and white papers, commercial aerospace crossover qualification criteria, and international FMS/DCS pipeline development with ITAR controls and CEO approval thresholds

label: IR&D alignment with BD pipeline — IRAD investment supporting capture priorities
content: VP BD responsibility for ensuring IRAD investments directly support active capture priorities and near-term proposal requirements, coordinated with CTO and VP Strategy. Annual IRAD input: at fiscal year start, VP BD provides CTO with a ranked list of 5–10 capture priorities (opportunities ≥$2M, within 24 months of proposal submission) requiring technology development or demonstration to be competitive; for each, VP BD specifies the technical capability gap IRAD must close, the proposal submission date, and the required TRL at RFP drop. Quarterly IRAD portfolio review: VP BD participates in quarterly IRAD reviews with CTO and VP Strategy (#1442); evaluates pipeline-to-IRAD alignment; flags IRAD projects with no corresponding capture pipeline entry as H3 bets requiring board-level visibility rather than implicit capture support; advocates for reallocation from capture-disconnected investments to near-term proposal requirements when alignment gap is identified; IRAD investments with no pipeline linkage and no external customer signal (SBIR award, OTA study, LOI) within 24 months are nominated for termination or H3 reclassification. IRAD-to-proposal transition: when IRAD reaches TRL sufficient for proposal citation, VP BD integrates results into win themes and technical volumes; coordinates with CTO to ensure IRAD results are documented in proposal-ready form (test data, prototype performance specifications, demonstration evidence) at least 60 days before anticipated RFP drop; TRL cited in proposals must reflect actually achieved capability — misrepresentation of TRL is a material false statement. SBIR pipeline alignment: VP BD monitors SBIR solicitation releases for topics aligned to BD pipeline; identifies SBIR Phase II conversion candidates as customer-funded IRAD pathways reducing internal spend; coordinates with CTO on Phase II-to-III (sole-source contract) bridge strategy using 10 U.S.C. § 4902 authority. IRAD ROI: VP BD tracks contract awards directly attributable to IRAD capability differentiation per win theme records and source selection debrief feedback; reports IRAD-to-award conversion rate to CEO and CFO annually as evidence of IRAD effectiveness.
summary: Task: VP BD IR&D alignment — annual IRAD prioritization input to CTO, quarterly portfolio review for capture-pipeline linkage, IRAD-to-proposal transition coordination (60-day prep requirement), SBIR Phase II-to-III bridge strategy, and annual IRAD ROI reporting

label: Industry day and conference strategy for BD relationship building
content: VP BD system for structured attendance at government industry days, pre-solicitation conferences, and defense industry events. Industry day preparation: (1) Identify upcoming events via SAM.gov pre-solicitation notices and agency forecast tools 30–90 days in advance; VP BD prioritizes attendance by pipeline alignment (stage ≥Identified, NAICS match, estimated value ≥$500K); (2) Register within 24 hours of notice posting — attendance is often limited and competitive; (3) Pre-briefing preparation: review all available government documents (draft PWS, draft evaluation criteria, prior contract FPDS-NG data); prepare a one-page tailored capability summary; develop 3–5 questions that clarify government requirements or position company strengths within ethics bounds — propose alternatives from industry experience, offer risk mitigations, ask clarifying questions; avoid questions that reveal competitive pricing posture or force information from government attendees; (4) Team composition: one senior technical lead plus one BD representative; limit to two attendees unless government explicitly permits more. Industry day execution: arrive early to maximize informal networking time before formal session; capture all government attendee names and roles for CRM entry; submit written questions within government-published deadline; request one-on-one or breakout sessions when offered — highest-value relationship touchpoints on any government engagement; avoid discussing proprietary information or competitive strategy in multi-company settings (ethics and source selection integrity). Post-event actions: log all new government contacts in CRM within 24 hours; debrief BD team on requirements-shaping opportunities and any government statements creating follow-up obligations; update opportunity CRM record with intelligence gathered. Conference and association strategy: VP BD selects 3–5 tier-one annual events (NDIA, AUSA, Air Space Cyber, AFCEA, AIA) based on customer segment alignment and exhibitor cost-effectiveness; sponsor or exhibit when pipeline contact ROI (qualified contacts per $1K spend) exceeds internal benchmark; all staff briefed on conference objectives, priority government attendees to engage, and DoD ethics rules (gift limits, no entertainment creating appearance of impropriety) before each event. Conference ROI: VP BD tracks which conference-originated contacts advance to CRM opportunities within 12 months; annual conference budget justified against pipeline contribution from event-originated opportunities vs. alternatives.
summary: System: VP BD industry day preparation, execution, and post-event CRM protocol — annual conference selection criteria, ethics briefing requirements, and event ROI tracking against pipeline contribution from government contacts

label: BD workforce development and capture manager competency model
content: VP BD responsibility for developing and sustaining BD team competency across the Shipley lifecycle. Capture manager competency model — five domains assessed annually: (1) Customer intelligence: ability to develop and maintain direct working relationships with government PMs, contracting officers, and prime BD leads independent of VP BD involvement; assess by CRM contact network depth and demonstrated access to program office technical and contracting personnel. (2) Competitive analysis: proficiency with FPDS-NG and USASpending.gov for incumbent identification, competitor win history analysis, and PTW estimation; quality assessed by calibration accuracy of PTW estimates vs. actual winning prices on past pursuits managed. (3) Win strategy development: ability to develop win themes traceable to Section M evaluation criteria, differentiated from known competitor claims, and supported by verifiable evidence; assessed via Red Team evaluation scores across captures led. (4) Proposal management: skills in managing proposal team across technical, management, and cost volumes; enforcing Section L compliance; maintaining color team milestone schedule under RFP deadline pressure; assessed by Gold Team score and on-time submission rate. (5) Financial acumen: working knowledge of government cost accounting (direct vs. indirect, CAS basics), contract type risk tradeoffs, and B&P budget discipline; ability to interpret FPDS-NG award data to validate pricing assumptions in PTW analysis. Development program: new BD hires complete Shipley BD Lifecycle training within 90 days of onboarding; all capture managers complete APMP (Association of Proposal Management Professionals) Foundation certification within 12 months; senior capture managers pursue APMP Practitioner certification; VP BD sponsors at least one team member per year in external Shipley workshops or APMP national events. Performance assessment: capture managers evaluated annually on win rate for pursuits ≥$1M (target ≥35%), Pink-to-Gold proposal evaluation score improvement (target ≥30-point gain), B&P budget discipline (actuals within 10% of approved per pursuit), and CRM completeness (all contacts logged within 24 hours, opportunity records current). BD career ladder: BD Analyst → BD Coordinator → Capture Manager → Senior Capture Manager → VP BD; development assignments include observer role on must-win captures, volume lead responsibility on competitive proposals, and a 90-day cross-functional rotation to contracts or program management to build enterprise perspective. Succession: VP BD maintains a BD bench-strength register identifying staff ready to assume capture manager responsibilities for the next-tier opportunity size; reported at annual succession review with CEO.
summary: Task: VP BD workforce development — five-domain capture manager competency model, APMP certification program, annual performance assessment criteria (win rate/PTW accuracy/proposal scores), BD career ladder, and bench-strength succession register

label: Government contract cost accounting and CAS compliance
content: CAS-covered contracts (>$2M single award threshold, or contractor with >$50M covered contract aggregate) require full CAS coverage with filed CASB DS-2 Disclosure Statement. CFO ensures cost accounting practices are disclosed, consistently applied, and amended within 60 days of any material practice change per FAR 52.230-6. Annual review cycle aligns DS-2 to actual practices across all 8 parts. Indirect rate structures must be documented, consistently applied, and reconciled to provisional billing rates negotiated with DCAA/DCMA annually.
summary: Task: Government contract cost accounting and CAS compliance — disclosure statement maintenance, indirect rate structures, and cost charging consistency

label: Maintain CAS Disclosure Statement (DS-1/DS-2) with annual updates
content: Maintain CASB DS-2 Disclosure Statement reflecting actual cost accounting practices across all 8 parts: general information, direct costs, direct material, direct labor, indirect costs, depreciation, deferred compensation, and IR&D/B&P. Conduct annual practice-to-disclosure comparison before fiscal year-end. Coordinate with Contracts and Program Finance to surface any practice changes during the year. Submit FAR 52.230-6 DS amendment within 60 days of detecting a material change. Retain filed copies, amendment history, and DCAA adequacy determination letters in compliance records for 6 years.
summary: System: Maintain CASB DS-2 across all 8 parts with annual review, amendment submissions, and DCAA acknowledgment tracking

label: Operate indirect rate structure (fringe, overhead, G&A, material handling)
content: Maintain four-tier indirect rate structure: (1) Fringe benefits pool — applied to all direct and indirect labor (payroll taxes, health/dental, PTO accruals, 401k); (2) Overhead pool — applied to direct labor dollars or hours by business unit (facility costs, supervision, indirect labor); (3) G&A pool — applied to total cost input (TCI) or value-added base (executive management, finance, legal, BD, IT); (4) Material handling pool — applied to direct material purchases (purchasing, receiving, inventory control). Establish provisional billing rates annually with DCAA; reconcile actuals quarterly. Apply CAS 410 for home office allocations if applicable. Document allocation base rationale in DS-2 Part V.
summary: System: Operate four-tier indirect rate structure (fringe, overhead, G&A, material handling) with provisional rate reconciliation and DCAA coordination

label: Manage cost pool composition and allocation base selection per CAS 418
content: Per CAS 418, allocation bases must produce equitable cost distribution to cost objectives. Document rationale for each pool's base in DS-2: engineering overhead uses direct labor hours (captures labor intensity); manufacturing overhead uses direct labor dollars or machine hours; G&A uses total cost input or value-added (exclude pass-through material if >70% of revenue); material handling uses direct material cost. Review pool composition annually; document and justify any reclassification of costs between pools. Prohibited: cross-subsidization between government and commercial cost pools. Test allocation equity via cost/benefit analysis comparing alternatives when base changes are considered.
summary: System: Manage cost pool composition and equitable allocation base selection per CAS 418 with annual review and documented rationale

label: Monitor direct/indirect cost charging consistency per CAS 402
content: CAS 402 requires costs incurred for the same purpose in like circumstances be classified consistently as either direct or indirect. Maintain cost charging consistency matrix documenting treatment of labor categories, material types, subcontractor costs, and ODCs across all programs. Conduct quarterly charge analysis: identify instances where costs were charged directly on some contracts and indirectly on others for identical activities. Document and retain justification for any legitimate differences in circumstance (e.g., contract-specified direct charge requirement). Proactively disclose inconsistencies to DCAA before audit; self-disclosure significantly reduces penalty risk under FAR 52.230-7.
summary: System: Monitor direct/indirect cost charging consistency per CAS 402 with quarterly analysis and proactive DCAA disclosure

label: Trigger DS amendment when accounting practice changes exceed $500K annual impact
content: Any accounting practice change must be evaluated for materiality per FAR 52.230-6. Calculate estimated annual cost impact by comparing projected costs under new vs. old practice across all CAS-covered contracts. If cumulative impact exceeds $500K annually, prepare and submit DS-2 amendment within 60 days. Triggering changes include: depreciation method shifts, overhead pool restructuring, cost element reclassification, and allocation base changes. Coordinate with Contracts for required notifications to Cognizant Contracting Officers. Document impact analysis with pool-level detail and retain for DCAA review. Failure to timely amend triggers potential cost disallowance and CAS noncompliance findings.
summary: Decision: Trigger and submit DS-2 amendment within 60 days when accounting practice changes exceed $500K estimated annual cost impact

label: Escalate when provisional billing rates deviate >5% from actual requiring DCMA adjustment
content: Compare actual indirect rates to provisional billing rates at each quarter-end. If any cost pool deviates >5% from provisional (actual higher = under-billed; actual lower = over-billed), initiate rate adjustment request to DCMA/ACO. Under-billing risk: cash flow shortfall and contract funding gaps; submit revised provisional rate request promptly. Over-billing risk: government demand letter if not self-reported; issue contractor billing adjustment proactively. Document root cause by pool (volume changes, unexpected cost spikes, budget miss). Escalate to CFO and VP Contracts for DCMA negotiation strategy prior to submission. Track adjustment status to closure.
summary: Decision: Escalate provisional vs. actual rate deviations >5% per pool to CFO and VP Contracts for DCMA rate adjustment action

label: Updated CAS Disclosure Statement (CASB DS-2 format)
content: Produce CASB DS-2 amendment package: (1) Cover letter to Cognizant Federal Agency (CFA) identifying changed practices by DS-2 part and item number; (2) Redlined DS-2 pages showing old and new practice text side-by-side; (3) Cost impact analysis — schedule showing estimated change in cost to CAS-covered contracts with government share vs. contractor share breakdown; (4) Effective date of practice change and proposed implementation date; (5) Certificate of accurate disclosure per FAR 52.230-6(c) signed by CFO. Submit to ACO and DCAA with copies to affected program Contracting Officers. Retain DCAA adequacy determination letter confirming filing acceptance.
summary: Output: CASB DS-2 amendment package with redlined practice changes, cost impact schedule, and FAR 52.230-6(c) CFO certificate

label: Indirect rate variance analysis report (provisional vs. actual by pool)
content: Prepare quarterly rate variance report for each cost pool: (1) Provisional rate (negotiated or proposed); (2) Actual rate YTD (YTD pool costs ÷ YTD allocation base); (3) Variance in percentage points and estimated dollar impact on billed costs; (4) Projected full-year actual rate at current run rate; (5) Root cause narrative for any pool with variance >5% (volume shortfall, unplanned cost, base composition shift); (6) Recommended action: submit DCMA rate adjustment request, monitor and hold, or no action. Format: pool-by-pool summary table with Q1–Q4 trend lines. Distribute to CFO, Controller, VP Contracts, and Program Finance. Use as primary input for provisional rate renegotiation package to DCMA/ACO.
summary: Output: Quarterly indirect rate variance report by pool — provisional vs. actual rates with root cause analysis and recommended DCMA action

label: FAR Part 31 allowability determinations and cost compliance
content: FAR Part 31 establishes cost allowability, allocability, and reasonableness standards for all government contracts. CFO establishes and enforces procedures to identify, segregate, and exclude unallowable costs from billings and indirect cost pools. Key per se unallowable categories under FAR 31.205: entertainment, alcoholic beverages, lobbying and political contributions, bad debts, interest expense (except certain financing), fines and penalties, advertising unrelated to recruiting, and executive compensation exceeding NLC benchmarks. Inclusion of unallowable costs in government billings is a False Claims Act exposure. Annual unallowable cost certification required with incurred cost proposal.
summary: Task: FAR Part 31 allowability determinations and cost compliance — unallowable cost segregation, IR&D/B&P accumulation, and compensation reasonableness

label: Screen costs against FAR 31.205 unallowable cost categories
content: Implement transaction-level screening against FAR 31.205 unallowable cost subcategories. Key categories: 31.205-1 (public relations — allowable only if recruiting or contract-required); 31.205-3 (bad debts); 31.205-8 (contributions and donations — wholly unallowable); 31.205-13 (entertainment — wholly unallowable, including meals not on travel); 31.205-14 (fines and penalties); 31.205-22 (lobbying — wholly unallowable); 31.205-27 (organization costs); 31.205-41 (taxes — state income tax unallowable); 31.205-47 (costs related to legal/IR proceedings). Configure ERP cost element codes to route potential unallowable transactions to Controller for disposition review before month-end close and pool posting.
summary: System: Transaction-level screening against FAR 31.205 unallowable cost categories with ERP routing to Controller for disposition

label: Maintain unallowable cost identification and segregation procedures
content: Establish dedicated unallowable cost GL accounts excluded from all indirect cost pools and billings. Document segregation procedures in written accounting practices: (1) account code structure for each unallowable category; (2) ERP controls preventing unallowable accounts from feeding pool calculations; (3) quarterly unallowable cost ledger review by Controller; (4) annual CFO/Controller certification that unallowable costs were identified and excluded. Maintain unallowable cost log with transaction detail (date, vendor, amount, FAR citation, disposition). DCAA reconciles unallowable ledger to indirect rate calculations during Incurred Cost audit; gaps trigger findings and potential cost disallowance.
summary: System: Maintain dedicated unallowable cost accounts, ERP exclusion controls, quarterly ledger review, and annual segregation certification

label: Review IR&D and B&P cost accumulation per FAR 31.205-18
content: Per FAR 31.205-18, IR&D (Independent Research and Development) and B&P (Bid and Proposal) costs are allowable subject to reasonableness limitations. Track separately at project level: IR&D (technical effort not sponsored by contract) and B&P (proposal preparation efforts). Allocate combined IR&D/B&P to G&A pool as a visible line item; monitor to ensure combined rate does not distort overall G&A. Large defense contractors with >$100M covered contracts must report IR&D to DoD USD(R&E) and demonstrate technical benefit to national defense under 10 U.S.C. § 3762. Maintain project-level ledgers with technical descriptions, cost accumulations, and responsible engineer sign-off for each project.
summary: System: Accumulate and review IR&D and B&P costs per FAR 31.205-18 with project-level tracking and DoD reporting compliance

label: Validate compensation reasonableness per FAR 31.205-6 and NLC benchmarks
content: FAR 31.205-6 limits allowable compensation to amounts reasonable for services rendered, benchmarked against the National Compensation Matrix (NLC) for comparable positions. Annual validation: (1) obtain current NLC benchmark data for all executive and key management positions; (2) compare actual total compensation (base + bonus + deferred + benefits) to NLC median with documented reasonableness buffer (typically 10%); (3) flag any individual exceeding NLC benchmark; (4) document reasonableness determination with market comparator data; (5) calculate and exclude compensation in excess of NLC from government contract indirect costs. Coordinate with HR and Legal for Board-level compensation. DCAA tests compensation reasonableness on every incurred cost audit.
summary: System: Annual validation of executive and management compensation against NLC benchmarks per FAR 31.205-6 with documented reasonableness determinations

label: Flag costs exceeding FAR 31.205 allowability thresholds for Controller disposition
content: Configure ERP workflow to route transactions matching unallowable categories to Controller for disposition before month-end close: entertainment (any amount — 100% unallowable); lobbying or political contributions (any amount); alcohol purchases; fines and penalties; interest expense (except approved financing); advertising not tied to recruiting or contract requirement. Controller documents disposition per transaction: (1) charge to dedicated unallowable account, (2) reclassify to allowable category with written justification, or (3) hold for CFO review. Escalation to CFO threshold: single transaction >$10K or cumulative category total >$50K in a quarter. CFO reviews unallowable cost log monthly at minimum.
summary: Decision: Route and disposition costs matching FAR 31.205 unallowable categories; escalate to CFO when single transaction >$10K or quarterly category total >$50K

label: Escalate executive compensation exceeding NLC benchmarks for Board Compensation Committee review
content: When annual NLC validation identifies an executive whose total compensation exceeds the NLC median by >15%, escalate to Board Compensation Committee with: (1) NLC benchmark comparison table (position, actual total comp, NLC median, variance); (2) documented business justification (retention risk, market scarcity, unique qualifications, performance); (3) proposed allowable vs. unallowable cost split and dollar impact; (4) estimated G&A rate impact if partial compensation is excluded. Board must formally approve or adjust with documented rationale retained in Board minutes. Coordinate with Contracts on ACO notification if total unallowable compensation for any individual exceeds $100K annually.
summary: Decision: Escalate to Board Compensation Committee when executive total comp exceeds NLC median by >15%; document allowable/unallowable split for DCAA

label: FAR 31 allowability determination memo with citation and rationale
content: Issue formal allowability determination memo for any cost question exceeding $25K or involving a novel cost category not previously addressed. Memo format: (1) cost description (amount, vendor, purpose, date range); (2) FAR 31.205 subpart analysis — applicable cost principle and verbatim citation; (3) determination: allowable, partially allowable (with percentage), or unallowable; (4) allocability analysis — does cost benefit the government work specifically?; (5) reasonableness assessment; (6) disposition instructions to accounting (GL account assignment, pool treatment or exclusion); (7) CFO signature and date. File in cost compliance records retained 6 years + 3 years (statute of limitations). Use as binding precedent for recurring cost types of same character.
summary: Output: Formal FAR 31 allowability determination memo with cost principle citation, allocability/reasonableness analysis, disposition instruction, and CFO signature

label: Annual unallowable cost segregation certification
content: Prepare annual certification at fiscal year-end attesting: (1) all costs included in indirect cost pools and direct contract charges were reviewed for allowability per FAR 31.205; (2) unallowable costs were identified and segregated to dedicated GL accounts excluded from all government billings; (3) unallowable cost ledger total for year by category (entertainment, lobbying, penalties, excess compensation, other); (4) no per se unallowable costs were included in any government billing or indirect pool calculation. Signed by CFO and Controller. Submit as attachment to annual Incurred Cost Proposal per FAR 52.216-7. Retain with full transaction-level supporting detail. False certification creates False Claims Act liability — confirm accuracy before signing.
summary: Output: Annual CFO/Controller certification of unallowable cost identification, segregation, and exclusion from government billings — submitted with Incurred Cost Proposal

label: Develop forward pricing rate proposals (FPRPs) with BOEs for DCMA negotiation
content: Prepare Forward Pricing Rate Proposals (FPRPs) for DCMA submission and negotiation into Forward Pricing Rate Agreements (FPRAs). FPRP required elements: (1) projected indirect rates by pool for the proposal period (typically 2–3 fiscal years); (2) Basis of Estimate (BOE) for each pool — projected base volume, projected pool costs line-by-line, and key assumptions (headcount, program awards, escalation); (3) variance explanation comparing proposed rates to prior negotiated rates; (4) direct labor rate tables by labor category with escalation factors; (5) material escalation indices by commodity group (electronics, metals, composites); (6) sensitivity analysis showing rate impact at ±10% revenue. Submit FPRPs annually in Q1 or upon request for major proposal exceeding $25M. FPRA provides contractual cost certainty for proposal pricing and contract negotiations.
summary: System: Develop FPRPs with pool-level BOEs, labor rate tables, and sensitivity analysis for DCMA negotiation into FPRAs

label: Prepare annual operating budget aligned to contract mix (FFP, CPFF, T&M, IDIQ)
content: Develop annual operating budget structured around the contract portfolio by type: (1) Revenue forecast by contract vehicle — FFP (Firm Fixed-Price: margin risk, no cost reimbursement), CPFF (Cost Plus Fixed Fee: volume/rate risk, full cost recovery), T&M (Time and Materials: labor rate risk, no material risk), IDIQ (Indefinite Delivery/Indefinite Quantity: task order timing uncertainty, budget ±30% stress test required); (2) direct cost budgets by program — labor, material, subcontract, ODC; (3) indirect pool budgets sized to anticipated base volumes; (4) projected indirect rates at budgeted volumes and sensitivity at ±15% volume; (5) award fee and incentive fee projections by contract; (6) monthly cash flow model (FFP milestone billing vs. CPFF cost-reimbursement billing cycles differ materially). Budget approved by CFO; presented to Board with contract mix waterfall.
summary: System: Prepare annual operating budget segmented by contract type (FFP, CPFF, T&M, IDIQ) with indirect rate projections and monthly cash flow model

label: Approve FPRP submission when proposed rates differ >3% from prior negotiated FPRA rates
content: Before submitting FPRP to DCMA, CFO reviews and approves when proposed rates differ from prior FPRA rates by more than 3% on any indirect pool. Review elements: (1) validate BOE assumptions against headcount plan, overhead cost drivers, and anticipated contract awards; (2) assess impact on in-process proposals — higher rates reduce competitiveness on FFP proposals, lower rates reduce cost recovery on CPFF; (3) evaluate government negotiating posture — recent DCAA rate audit findings, any open DCMA rate disputes; (4) determine negotiation strategy: submit at full cost, negotiate to a settlement rate, or request updated provisional rates while FPRA negotiations proceed. CFO coordinates with VP Business Development on proposal pipeline rate sensitivity before submission. Document rate approval rationale in FPRP cover memorandum.
summary: Decision: CFO approval required before FPRP submission when proposed rates deviate >3% from prior FPRA on any pool; includes negotiation strategy determination

label: Forward Pricing Rate Agreement (FPRA) package with rate build-up schedules
content: Compile FPRA negotiation package for DCMA submission: (1) cover letter identifying contractor, CAGE code, fiscal year coverage, and summary of proposed rates vs. prior agreement with variance explanation; (2) rate build-up schedules — pool-by-pool cost projection with line-item detail (labor by category, fringe, occupancy, depreciation, utilities, indirect labor, management); (3) allocation base projections with supporting direct labor hour/dollar forecast and revenue plan; (4) direct labor rate tables by labor category (entry-level through principal/fellow) with applied escalation methodology; (5) historical rate comparison: last 3 fiscal years actual vs. provisional vs. negotiated by pool; (6) forward-looking assumptions package: contract award pipeline, headcount plan, facility plan, commodity escalation indices; (7) DCAA audit status summary and disposition of any open rate-related findings. Maintain executed FPRA in Contracts repository; reference FPRA agreement number in all proposals submitted during the agreement period.
summary: Output: FPRA negotiation package with pool-level rate build-up schedules, allocation base projections, 3-year historical comparison, and forward assumption documentation

label: SMC Program Decision Memorandum (PDM)
content: Formal PDM issued by CEO within 5 business days of SMC vote on strategic action items. Required sections: (1) Header — PDM number (sequential per FY), SMC meeting date, issuance date, classification, distribution list; (2) Decision Summary — action item description, SMC outcome (Approved/Tabled), alignment score achieved, CFO resource confirmation status, Tier 1 risk dependency disposition; (3) Action Items Table — for each approved action: action description, accountable VP owner, target completion date, resource requirement ($, FTE), success metric, and escalation path if milestone missed; (4) Tabling Rationale (if applicable) — specific criterion not met, resolution plan, named resolution owner, and resubmission deadline before next SMC; (5) Authorization — CEO signature, date, and Chief of Staff distribution confirmation. Document numbered sequentially per fiscal year; stored in enterprise action tracker; open action items reviewed for closure at each subsequent SMC meeting. Format: 2–3 page controlled document per records retention policy.
summary: Output: CEO-signed SMC Program Decision Memorandum with approved/tabled outcome, action items, owners, deadlines, and tabling resolution plan — issued within 5 business days of SMC vote

label: Strategic Initiative Priority Ranking Dashboard
content: Quarterly dashboard ranking all active strategic initiatives by composite priority score, refreshed within 10 business days of each SMC meeting. Dashboard panels: (1) Initiative Register Summary — all initiatives ranked by Impact × Feasibility × Urgency composite score with status indicator (on-track / at-risk / delayed), VP owner, H1/H2/H3 horizon classification, budget consumed vs. approved, and 90-day milestone status; (2) Priority Stack — top-10 initiatives sorted by composite score with change-in-rank arrows vs. prior quarter; (3) SMC PDM Action Item Tracker — all open action items from prior PDMs with owner, original due date, completion status, and days overdue if past due; (4) Resource Utilization — IRAD and B&P budget allocation across initiatives as % of plan with over/under variance; (5) Quarterly Change Log — initiatives added, composite score changes ≥10 points, initiatives completed or terminated vs. prior quarter with rationale. Owned by VP Strategy; distributed to CEO, all VPs, and SMC attendees. Serves as the live performance record between quarterly SMC sessions.
summary: Output: Quarterly strategic initiative priority ranking dashboard with composite scores, owner status, PDM action item tracker, and resource utilization — refreshed within 10 days of each SMC meeting

label: KDP Milestone Transition Authorization Memorandum
content: Formal authorization memorandum issued by COO (with CEO co-signature for KDP-D or programs >$10M contract value) within 5 business days of PMC Approve or Conditional Approval decision. Required sections: (1) Program identification — program name, contract number, KDP gate (A/B/C/D), gate review date, PM name; (2) Authorization statement — explicit declaration that the program is authorized to proceed to the named next phase per PMC vote; (3) Gate criteria confirmation — CPI and SPI actuals vs. thresholds, TRL achieved vs. gate minimum, open critical risk count (P×I ≥15); (4) Conditional actions (if Conditional Approval) — mandatory action items table with owner, resource requirement, milestone, verification method, and re-review date ≤30 calendar days from issuance; (5) Phase resource authorization — budget authority ceiling for next phase, headcount authorization, and capital expenditure limit; (6) Signatures — COO primary; CEO co-signature per thresholds above; copies to PM, CFO, VP Engineering, VP Operations, Contracting Officer, and program file. Numbered per PMC PDM sequence; constitutes the contractual-equivalent internal authority for phase transition.
summary: Output: COO/CEO-signed KDP milestone transition authorization memorandum confirming gate criteria met, authorizing next-phase resource expenditure, and documenting conditional action items — issued within 5 business days of PMC decision

label: Program Redirect Deficiency Report with Corrective Action Plan
content: Structured report issued within 10 business days of a PMC Redirect decision. Template sections: (1) Deficiency Summary — program name, KDP gate, redirect decision date, total deficiency count by category; (2) Deficiency Register — table with columns: deficiency ID, category (cost/schedule/technical/risk), description, severity (Critical/Major/Minor), root cause assessment, and gate criteria reference from KDP standards; (3) Corrective Action Plan — for each deficiency: specific corrective action, accountable owner (VP or PM level), resource requirement ($, labor hours), target completion milestone, and verification method (re-test, EVM rebaseline, risk closure); (4) Revised Gate Re-Entry Criteria — updated CPI/SPI/TRL thresholds and risk closure requirements the program must satisfy to re-enter the gate; (5) Re-Entry Timeline — projected re-review date; interim COO checkpoint milestones at 30-day intervals; (6) Authorization — COO signature; PM written acknowledgment of corrective actions; CEO concurrence for programs >$5M contract value. PM presents corrective action status at each monthly PMC session until re-entry is authorized.
summary: Output: Program redirect deficiency report identifying root-caused gate failures with a corrective action plan, revised re-entry criteria, and COO/CEO authorization — issued within 10 business days of PMC redirect decision

label: PMC Gate Review Scorecard (CPI/SPI/TRL per ANSI 748)
content: Standardized one-page scorecard summarizing program EVM and technical performance at each KDP gate, calculated per ANSI/EIA-748 EVM guidelines. Scorecard sections: (1) Program Identification — program name, contract number, KDP gate, review date, PM name, contract type (FFP/CPFF/T&M); (2) EVM Performance Summary (ANSI/EIA-748 compliant) — BAC (Budget at Completion), PV (Planned Value), EV (Earned Value), AC (Actual Cost), CV (EV−AC), SV (EV−PV), CPI (EV÷AC), SPI (EV÷PV), EAC (BAC÷CPI), VAC (BAC−EAC), TCPI (work remaining ÷ funds remaining); gate threshold status displayed as Green/Yellow/Red per KDP decision criteria from #1774; (3) TRL Assessment — subsystem-level TRL ratings vs. gate minimum; Critical Technology Items (CTIs) status; delta between achieved and required TRL per subsystem; (4) Risk Register Snapshot — total open risks by P×I tier (≤9 / 10–14 / ≥15); top-3 risks with owner, mitigation status, and residual score; (5) Gate Decision Block — PMC decision (Approved/Conditional/Redirect/Termination) with COO signature and date. Prepared by PM using current-period EVM submission; verified by CTO-designated independent technical reviewer before PMC session; issued as Exhibit A to the KDP Transition Authorization Memorandum or Deficiency Report.
summary: Output: One-page PMC gate review scorecard with ANSI/EIA-748 EVM metrics (CPI/SPI/EAC/VAC), TRL assessment per subsystem, risk register snapshot, and gate decision signature block — attached to every PMC PDM

label: Capital Investment Approval Letter with ROI Projections
content: Formal approval letter issued by CFO (≤$100K) or CEO ($100K–$500K) or CEO+Board (>$500K) within 5 business days of CIC decision. Required elements: (1) Investment identification — asset description, requesting VP/department, CIC case number, submission date, approval authority tier; (2) Approval declaration — explicit authorization to acquire the stated asset at the approved ceiling; (3) Financial authorization — total approved cost ceiling, budget source (capital budget line, cost center code, program direct charge if applicable), fiscal year phasing schedule; (4) ROI projections — documented payback period (target ≤4 years for non-strategic), NPV at 5% discount rate over asset useful life, projected year-1, year-2, and year-3 ROI from CIC business case; (5) Conditions of approval — any milestones required before funds release (e.g., vendor selection, facility modification completion, CIC-required alternatives comparison update); (6) Post-investment tracking commitment — notice that CFO will track actual vs. projected ROI annually for 3 years; investments achieving <80% of projected ROI trigger mandatory CIC lessons-learned review; (7) Signatures — CFO or CEO per authority tier; copy to requesting VP, VP Operations, CIC record register, and fixed asset register custodian. Retained per records retention policy with CIC business case as supporting documentation.
summary: Output: CFO/CEO-signed capital investment approval letter with authorized cost ceiling, ROI projections, budget source, approval conditions, and post-investment tracking notice — issued within 5 business days of CIC decision

label: Capital Investment Portfolio ROI Dashboard
content: Annual dashboard tracking actual vs. projected ROI for all CIC-approved capital investments within the prior 3-year trailing window, presented at the CIC year-end review and updated quarterly for CFO monitoring. Dashboard sections: (1) Portfolio Summary — total approved capital investments active in tracking window ($), count of investments on-track (actual ROI ≥80% of projected), at-risk (60–79%), and underperforming (<60%); aggregate portfolio actual vs. projected ROI trend; (2) Investment Tracking Table — per approved investment: CIC case number, asset description, approval date, approved cost ceiling, actual cost-to-date, projected ROI at year-1/year-2/year-3 from approval letter, actual ROI-to-date, variance (actual minus projected as %), status (on-track/at-risk/review-required); investments below 80% threshold flagged in red; (3) Underperforming Investments Sub-Report — for each investment below 80% threshold: root cause assessment, lessons-learned status, and CIC corrective recommendation (write-down, repurpose, retire); (4) Emergency Capital Ratification Register — all Plant Manager emergency approvals (≤$10K) with authorization date, ratification status, and days to CIC ratification vs. 30-day requirement; (5) Annual Capital Budget Utilization — approved vs. actual capital spend for current FY, 20–30% reserve utilization rate, and unallocated contingency balance. Owned by CFO; distributed to CIC members (CEO, COO, CTO, VP Operations) annually at year-end review.
summary: Output: Annual CIC capital investment portfolio ROI dashboard tracking actual vs. projected ROI per approved investment over a 3-year window, with underperforming investment sub-reports and emergency capital ratification register

label: Enterprise Risk Register Heat Map Dashboard (5×5 Matrix)
content: Quarterly visual dashboard displaying the Tier 1 enterprise risk register as a NIST SP 800-39 5×5 likelihood-impact heat map for CEO and SMC risk governance. Dashboard components: (1) 5×5 Heat Map — x-axis: Impact (1=Negligible to 5=Catastrophic); y-axis: Likelihood (1=Rare to 5=Near-Certain); color zones: green (P×I ≤9, Accept zone), amber (P×I 10–14, Treat/Mitigate zone), red (P×I ≥15, Escalate/Transfer zone); each active Tier 1 risk plotted as a labeled bubble with risk ID and VP owner initials; bubble outlines show prior-quarter position to visualize directional movement; (2) Risk Register Table — adjacent to heat map: risk ID, category (financial/program/regulatory/cybersecurity/supply chain/reputational), current P×I composite, residual P×I post-mitigation, owner, mitigation plan status (on-track/delayed/no plan), and next review date; (3) Trend Summary — count of risks improving, stable, or worsening vs. prior quarter; new risks added since last report highlighted; risks approaching the 10–14 threshold flagged for preemptive treatment; (4) Top-5 Risks Narrative — one paragraph per top-5 risks by P×I with likelihood/impact scoring rationale and mitigation status; (5) Tier 2 Rollup — top-5 Tier 2 risks elevated to Tier 1 watch list. Refreshed quarterly for SMC review; updated within 5 business days of any Tier 1 risk materialization event; annual version presented to board.
summary: Output: Quarterly enterprise risk register heat map dashboard with NIST SP 800-39 5×5 matrix visualization, trend indicators, top-5 risk narratives, and Tier 2 rollup — updated within 5 business days of any Tier 1 materialization

label: Risk Acceptance Memorandum Template
content: Template for documenting CEO formal acceptance of enterprise risks per the three-tier ERM disposition framework in #1776. Memorandum sections: (1) Risk Identification — risk ID from enterprise register, risk title, category, affected program(s) or function, date of assessment; (2) Risk Assessment — likelihood score (1–5) with scoring rationale and data basis, impact score (1–5) with quantified consequence estimate (financial exposure $, schedule impact in days, regulatory consequence), composite P×I score, and response tier (Accept/Treat/Escalate); (3) Residual Risk Statement — description of risk remaining after any partial mitigations already implemented; explanation of why full elimination is not feasible or cost-justified (cost-benefit comparison); (4) Acceptance Justification — explicit business rationale: cost of complete elimination exceeds expected loss value; strategic mission requires proceeding despite risk; risk is within board-approved risk appetite tolerance band; (5) Monitoring Commitment — named risk owner, monitoring frequency (weekly/monthly/quarterly per P×I tier), specific threshold event or leading indicator that would trigger re-assessment or automatic escalation; (6) Authorization — CEO signature and date for P×I ≤9 accepted risks; CEO plus board chair signature for P×I ≥15 formal board-authorized acceptances; review period expiry date (maximum 12 months before mandatory re-evaluation). Retained in enterprise risk register; flagged for re-evaluation at each SMC risk session; updated within 30 days of any change in risk probability, impact, or mitigation status.
summary: Output: CEO (and board, if P×I ≥15) signed risk acceptance memorandum with residual risk justification, monitoring commitment, and 12-month review expiry — retained in enterprise risk register and reviewed at each SMC session

label: Quarterly Board Risk Posture Briefing Deck
content: Quarterly briefing deck prepared by CEO with VP Strategy input for board-level enterprise risk governance review. Deck structure: (1) Risk Posture Executive Summary (1 slide) — board-approved risk appetite statement reference; current Tier 1 risk count by response tier (Accept/Treat/Escalate); net posture assessment vs. prior quarter (improving/stable/deteriorating) with one-sentence rationale; any Tier 1 risk materializations since last board report; (2) 5×5 Heat Map Update (1 slide) — current quarter risk positions vs. prior quarter bubble positions; new risks highlighted; risks resolved since last report removed with notation; (3) Top-5 Risk Deep Dives (3–5 slides, 1 per risk) — risk description and category, likelihood and impact scoring with evidence, mitigation plan with milestone status and owner, residual P×I, and CEO recommendation to board (continue current mitigation / accept residual per memorandum / escalate response); (4) Materialized Risks Report (1 slide) — any risks that activated during the quarter; actual impact vs. predicted impact; response effectiveness assessment; lessons learned; (5) Board-Authorized Risk Acceptance Register (1 slide) — all P×I ≥15 risks with board-signed acceptance memoranda currently on file; review expiry dates approaching within 90 days requiring re-affirmation; (6) Emerging Threats (1 slide) — risks identified since last board report not yet formally scored; preliminary likelihood/impact assessment; CEO recommendation (monitor/elevate to register/immediate board action). Format: 8–12 slide deck; CEO presents; VP Strategy authors; Legal reviews for privilege before distribution; marked Board Confidential; distributed 7 days before board meeting per governance charter.
summary: Output: Quarterly CEO-presented board risk posture briefing deck with 5×5 heat map update, top-5 risk deep dives, materialization report, board-authorized acceptance register, and emerging threats — distributed 7 days before board meeting

label: Supplier risk scorecard with SCRM assessment results and DFARS compliance status
content: Quarterly scorecard (Tier A suppliers) / semi-annual (Tier B) assessing each supplier against five SCRM dimensions scored 1–5: (1) Financial stability — D&B rating, cash flow trend, contract revenue concentration; (2) Delivery performance — OTD actuals, NCR rate, corrective action closure rate; (3) Cybersecurity posture — NIST SP 800-171 practice implementation status, SPRS score if applicable, 72-hour incident reporting capability verified; (4) Counterfeit risk — AS5553B (electronic parts) / AS6174 (non-electronic) compliance evidence, independent testing status per DFARS 252.246-7008; (5) Geopolitical exposure — country of manufacture, rare earth/specialty material dependency, FOCI indicators. Aggregate score vs. 75th-percentile qualify/disqualify threshold from #1777. DFARS 252.204-7012 compliance section: adequate security per NIST SP 800-171 (Y/N), 72-hour cyber incident reporting capability (Y/N), media preservation process validated (Y/N). Scorecard includes qualify/disqualify recommendation with CEO/COO signature block. Issued to CEO and COO; disqualification entries simultaneously populate SCRM risk register and trigger alternate qualification sprint.
summary: Output: Quarterly/semi-annual supplier risk scorecard with five-dimension SCRM scoring, DFARS 252.204-7012 CDI compliance verification, aggregate score vs. qualify/disqualify threshold, and CEO sign-off block

label: Sole-source dependency alert report for ITAR-controlled components with stockpile status
content: Monthly report generated from the ITAR sole-source dependency register maintained per #1622. Per-entry fields: part number, USML category, sole domestic supplier name, current lead time in calendar days (flag red if >120 days), 90-day strategic stockpile status (Adequate / Marginal / Deficient), active alternate supplier qualification program Y/N with estimated time-to-qualification, and CEO action required flag. Report sections: (1) Executive Alert — entries with lead time >120 days AND no active alternate qualification program; these are auto-escalated to Tier 1 enterprise risk register per #1622; (2) Marginal/Deficient Stockpile Entries — items with <90 days on-hand inventory requiring immediate procurement action or alternate sourcing; (3) Active Alternate Qualification Progress — status updates for all in-flight alternate supplier qualifications with milestone and projected qualification date; (4) Resolved This Period — entries removed from alert status with qualifying alternate approved.
