Distributed monthly to CEO, VP Operations, and Export Compliance Officer. Any new entry with lead time >120 days triggers CEO brief within 5 business days per SCRM protocol in #1622.
summary: Output: Monthly sole-source dependency alert report with ITAR USML register entries, lead time flags (>120 day threshold), 90-day stockpile adequacy ratings, alternate qualification sprint status, and CEO escalation triggers

label: CMMC readiness assessment report with SPRS score and POAM closure status
content: Pre-C3PAO readiness assessment report prepared by ISSO and reviewed by CEO before scheduling authorization per #1778. Required sections: (1) SPRS Score Summary — current calculated SPRS score (0–110 scale), deduction breakdown by control family, comparison to 100-point authorization threshold; (2) POAM Status Matrix — total open POAMs by status: closed (complete with evidence), active/in-remediation (with milestone and scheduled completion date), and overdue (past scheduled completion date, flagged red); count of open POAMs vs. <5 authorization threshold; (3) 110-Practice Implementation Status — control family heat map (green=implemented and internally verified with evidence, yellow=partially implemented, red=not implemented) across all 14 NIST SP 800-171 Rev 2 families; (4) Internal Pre-Assessment Results — assessment date, assessor name/role, critical findings count (practices rated 'not implemented' on high-weighted families: access control, IA, SC, SI), blocking conditions remaining open at report date; (5) C3PAO Authorization Eligibility Checklist — four #1778 criteria evaluated Y/N with supporting evidence reference. Report issued to CEO no later than 30 days before desired C3PAO assessment window. CEO annotates and signs authorization or denial.
summary: Output: CMMC readiness assessment report with SPRS score vs. 100-point threshold, POAM closure status vs. <5 open threshold, 110-practice heat map by control family, and pre-assessment finding summary for CEO C3PAO scheduling decision

label: CEO CMMC compliance affirmation letter for SPRS submission
content: Annual CEO-signed affirmation letter entered into the Supplier Performance Risk System (SPRS) following each CMMC assessment cycle. Required elements: (1) Contractor identification — company name, CAGE code, DUNS/UEI, assessment date, assessment type (self-assessment Level 1 / C3PAO Level 2 third-party); (2) SPRS score being reported (numeric, 0–110); (3) Affirmation statement — CEO certifies that the reported SPRS score accurately reflects the company's implementation status of all applicable NIST SP 800-171 Rev 2 security practices as of the assessment date, consistent with the POAM register and ISSO-verified evidence; (4) ISSO verification acknowledgment — CEO confirms that the ISSO has reviewed and verified implementation evidence for each practice included in the score; (5) False Claims Act acknowledgment — CEO explicitly acknowledges that materially inaccurate SPRS submissions are subject to False Claims Act liability per the DOJ Civil Cyber-Fraud Initiative enforcement guidance, and that this affirmation is made under penalty of civil and criminal law; (6) CEO wet signature and date. Retained in cybersecurity compliance file with full POAM register and internal pre-assessment report as supporting documentation. Re-submitted annually and following any C3PAO re-assessment. Draft prepared by ISSO; legal review required before CEO execution.
summary: Output: CEO-signed annual CMMC compliance affirmation letter for SPRS entry — includes CAGE code, assessed SPRS score, ISSO verification acknowledgment, and explicit False Claims Act liability acknowledgment; retained with POAM register as compliance evidence

label: 9-box talent grid summary briefing for annual SMC calibration
content: Structured briefing package facilitating CEO-chaired annual SMC talent calibration session for VP+ and succession candidate population. Package components: (1) Completed 9-box grid — each individual plotted by Performance rating (1–3 based on objective results and balanced scorecard outcomes) and Leadership Potential rating (1–3 based on CQ composite scores and CEO/SMC qualitative assessment); Box 9/8/7 placements highlighted as succession-eligible per #1624 criteria; (2) Placement evidence summary — for each individual: prior-year performance rating, current CQ composite scores by CQ1–CQ6, notable accomplishments and development evidence supporting potential rating; (3) Year-over-year movement arrows — individuals who changed box placement since prior calibration with CEO commentary on trajectory; (4) Calibration consistency check — VP-population distribution across boxes with prompt if >40% are in Boxes 8/9 (grade inflation signal) or <20% in Boxes 7/8/9 (bench-strength deficiency); (5) Succession pipeline readiness count — Ready Now and Ready in 12 Months candidate count per critical position. Pre-read distributed to SMC 5 business days before calibration session. Marked Board Confidential; personnel records restrictions apply. CEO facilitates; HR administers. Output of calibration session feeds directly into #1624 succession register update.
summary: Output: Annual 9-box talent grid summary briefing package for SMC calibration — grid with VP+ placements, evidence summaries, year-over-year movement, consistency check, and succession pipeline count; Board Confidential distribution

label: Critical position succession gap report with development action plans
content: Report issued within 30 days of annual SMC talent calibration identifying all critical VP+ positions lacking a Ready Now candidate with Box 7, 8, or 9 placement per #1779 gap-flag criteria. Per gap entry: (1) Position identification — title, incumbent name, vacancy risk horizon (immediate/12-month/3-year based on retirement eligibility, health, or strategic transition signals); (2) Highest-rated developmental candidate — name, current 9-box placement, readiness rating, current CQ composite scores vs. threshold, and estimated months to Ready Now; (3) Development Action Plan — assigned stretch assignment (title, scope, duration), external formal program nomination (DCELP/ELDP/DSLDP per tier, #1236), executive coaching engagement if warranted per #1626, and defined 12-month readiness milestone with observable evidence criteria; (4) Interim succession designee — name from pre-published roster per #1624 emergency protocol; (5) Risk register reference — Tier 2 enterprise risk register entry number per #1624 gap protocol. CEO assigns development owners within 15 days of report issuance. 6-month checkpoint produces written progress memo from HR to CEO for each open gap. Report retained in succession register file; reviewed at mid-year SMC checkpoint.
summary: Output: Post-calibration critical position succession gap report — per-position gap entry with developmental candidate assessment, assigned development action plan (stretch/program/coaching), 12-month readiness milestones, interim designee, and Tier 2 risk register reference

label: Emergency succession interim designation roster updated semi-annually
content: Pre-authorized roster of interim designees for all VP+ and critical leadership positions enabling immediate activation within 24 hours of unplanned vacancy without a new CEO decision, per Step 1 of the emergency succession protocol in #1624. Roster elements per position: (1) Role title and current incumbent; (2) Designated interim — name, current title, and scope of authority delegated (full acting authority vs. limited operational authority); (3) Activation trigger — definition of qualifying vacancy event (death, incapacitation, abrupt departure, extended medical leave >30 days); (4) Board notification requirement — C-suite roles: board notification within 48 hours; VP-level roles: notification within 5 business days per #1624; (5) Customer notification assessment flag — Y/N indicator whether any active contract has a key personnel clause requiring government notification; (6) Search initiation authority — CEO decision deadline (within 30 days) on whether interim becomes permanent or formal search is initiated. Roster reviewed, updated, and re-signed by CEO semi-annually (January and July calendar cycles); signed copies distributed to CEO, COO, and Chief of Staff only. Board Chair receives C-suite section. Interim designees acknowledge their designation annually in writing. Supersedes any prior roster version on re-issue date; prior version archived.
summary: Output: Semi-annually updated (January/July) pre-authorized emergency succession interim designation roster — per VP+ position lists interim designee, authority scope, activation trigger, board/customer notification requirements, and CEO search decision deadline; Board Confidential distribution

label: Individual Development Plan directive with CQ gap analysis and 6-month milestones
content: Formal IDP directive issued within 30 days of a CEO mandate trigger per #1780 (CQ composite score <3.0 on same CQ in two consecutive annual assessments, or <2.5 on any CQ for C-suite). Required directive sections: (1) Trigger identification — individual name and role, triggering assessment cycles (year N and year N-1), CQ identifier (CQ1–CQ6), composite score history for the failing CQ with sub-competency breakdown (each sub-competency scored 1–5 vs. 3.0 threshold); (2) CQ gap analysis — root cause hypothesis for the gap based on 360-degree feedback themes (rater patterns, self-assessment vs. supervisor discrepancy, specific behavioral evidence cited); (3) Development activities — minimum two assigned activities from: formal program enrollment (DCELP/ELDP/DSLDP per tier and CQ target, #1236), rotational cross-functional assignment (min 90 days, #1216), external executive coaching engagement per cleared coach protocol (#1626), stretch project with defined deliverable; each activity linked to specific sub-competencies being developed; (4) 6-month milestone schedule — observable evidence criteria at 30/60/90/180 days; milestone verifier named; (5) 12-month re-assessment success criteria — specific CQ composite score target, sub-competency evidence required, and consequence of non-achievement (succession register downgrade); (6) Accountability — supervisor or CEO as accountable overseer; HR tracking enrollment confirmation; (7) CEO signature (mandatory for all directives per #1780); individual acknowledgment signature. Mid-year checkpoint produces written progress memo to CEO.
summary: Output: CEO-signed IDP corrective action directive with CQ gap root cause analysis, sub-competency score breakdown, assigned development activities (formal program/rotation/coaching), 6-month milestone schedule, 12-month re-assessment criteria, and HR tracking enrollment

label: Annual leadership competency trend report across VP+ population
content: Annual population-level report tracking DoDI 1430.16 CQ competency trends across all VP+ personnel and succession candidates following completion of all 360-degree assessment cycles. Report sections: (1) Executive summary — cohort CQ averages (CQ1–CQ6) vs. prior year with statistical distribution (mean, standard deviation, % at proficient ≥3.0 per CQ); net change arrows; VP Strategy overall succession pipeline health rating (Strong/Adequate/At-Risk); (2) CQ improvement trend for IDP corrective action population — for all personnel on active corrective IDPs, delta between triggering score and current score per CQ; IDP effectiveness rate (% achieving ≥3.0 target at 12-month re-assessment); (3) Succession pipeline competency heat map — percentage of VP+ population at Proficient (≥3.0) or Exemplary (≥4.0) on each CQ; identify CQs where population-wide gap exists (e.g., if <60% of cohort is proficient on CQ5 Building Coalitions, signals systemic development need vs. individual gap); (4) Recurring CQ gap patterns by function — if VP-level leaders in a particular function consistently score below cohort average on specific CQs, flags structural development investment or organizational design issue; (5) IDP mandate register status — active mandates by CQ, closed mandates with improvement vs. closed without improvement (succession register downgrade cases), and new mandates triggered this cycle; (6) Program effectiveness assessment — CQ score trend for participants in formal programs (DCELP/ELDP/DSLDP) vs. non-participants to evaluate ROI of development investment. Prepared annually by CEO/HR following all 360-degree assessment completion; presented at SMC; informs following year's development investment priorities, succession register updates, and formal program nomination decisions.
summary: Output: Annual leadership competency trend report — cohort CQ averages (CQ1–CQ6) vs. prior year, IDP corrective action effectiveness rates, succession pipeline CQ heat map, recurring gap patterns by function, and formal program ROI assessment for VP+ population

label: CEO customer engagement action plan with stakeholder relationship map
content: Structured action plan produced within 5 business days of CEO escalation trigger per #1781. Sections: (1) Trigger Summary — escalation event (CAR, inadequate business system, Cure Notice, or customer satisfaction <3.5/5.0), date, contract number, program office, and contracting officer; (2) Stakeholder Relationship Map — named individuals at the program office and DCMA: Program Manager, Deputy PM, Contracting Officer, ACO, COR, and key technical leads; relationship strength rating (1–5) per individual and last meaningful engagement date; single-point-of-failure contacts highlighted in red; (3) CEO Engagement Actions — calendar of scheduled touchpoints (phone, in-person, formal meeting) with purpose, target outcome, and preparation brief per touchpoint; (4) Internal Response Team — named VP owners, contracts lead, program manager, and quality representative with assigned deliverable responsibilities; (5) 90-Day Recovery Milestones — measurable actions with completion dates and verification criteria linked to the triggering deficiency; (6) Escalation Conditions — additional events that would elevate response to board notification or Contract Disputes Act protocol. Format: 2–3 page controlled document plus stakeholder map exhibit. CEO Chief of Staff coordinates scheduling; VP BD updates CRM stakeholder records within 24 hours of each CEO touchpoint. Stakeholder relationship map reviewed and refreshed at each 30-day milestone.
summary: Output: CEO customer engagement action plan with named stakeholder relationship map — triggered by DCMA CAR, inadequate business system, Cure Notice, or customer satisfaction <3.5/5.0; includes CEO touchpoint calendar, internal response team roles, and 90-day recovery milestones

label: DCMA Corrective Action Response letter with 30-day remediation timeline
content: Formal written response to a DCMA Corrective Action Request (CAR) issued under CEO signature within 10 business days of CAR receipt per #1781 and #1627. Required elements: (1) Header — CAR reference number, contract number(s), DCMA ACO name and address, response date; (2) Acknowledgment — explicit acknowledgment of each finding by CAR finding number; (3) Root Cause Analysis — factual cause of each deficiency categorized as: process gap, personnel competency gap, system or tool deficiency, or oversight failure; avoid defensive framing — government expects honest root cause; (4) Corrective Actions — for each finding: specific corrective action steps, named internal owner, and completion date; all actions due within 30 calendar days unless CAR specifies otherwise; actions requiring >30 days include an interim mitigation and written ACO-accepted justification; (5) 30-Day Remediation Schedule — tabular timeline with milestone-per-finding, owner, and verification method (procedure update, training record, test result, DCMA on-site verification invitation); (6) Systemic Prevention — QMS procedure changes preventing recurrence citing AS9100 Section 10.2 or relevant procedure number; (7) CEO Signature — letter signed by CEO as authorized representative; CC to ACO, Cognizant Federal Agency, internal Contracts and Quality files. Submit via certified mail or DCMA electronic submission system; track ACO acknowledgment receipt. Retain in DCMA correspondence file for 6 years.
summary: Output: CEO-signed DCMA Corrective Action Response letter with per-finding root cause analysis, named corrective action owners, and 30-day tabular remediation milestone schedule — submitted within 10 business days of CAR receipt

label: Teaming arrangement OCI clearance memorandum signed by CEO and General Counsel
content: Formal OCI clearance memorandum issued prior to teaming agreement execution, jointly signed by CEO and General Counsel (or Export Compliance Officer as legal authority), per #1782 and #1628. Sections: (1) Arrangement Identification — prime contractor name, program or solicitation reference, estimated contract value, teaming agreement reference number, and CEO approval date; (2) OCI Category Review — three-part assessment per FAR 9.5: (a) Unequal Access to Information — list all instances where company may access competitor or government proprietary data via this prime relationship; firewall provisions and excluded personnel list appended; determination (conflict present / not present / mitigated); (b) Impaired Objectivity — whether company provides advisory, evaluation, or systems engineering support to the government on a related contract; determination and mitigation if applicable; (c) Biased Ground Rules — whether company participated in requirements or specification development advantaging its own products; determination and recusal provisions; (3) Category-1 OCI Determination — explicit declaration that no unmitigated category-1 OCI exists; basis for CEO approval per #1782; for any mitigated lower-category OCI, mitigation description and adequacy rationale; (4) Disclosure Obligations — any planned ITAR or FAR 9.504–9.506 disclosure to contracting officer required prior to or concurrent with proposal submission; (5) Clearance Authorization — joint attestation by CEO and General Counsel that OCI review is complete and teaming agreement execution is authorized; (6) Signatures — CEO, General Counsel, date. Retained in teaming arrangement register per #1628 for 3 years post-performance. Concealed OCI after this clearance constitutes False Claims Act exposure.
summary: Output: CEO and General Counsel co-signed OCI clearance memorandum assessing all three FAR 9.5 categories, declaring no unmitigated category-1 conflict, documenting any mitigation provisions, and formally authorizing teaming agreement execution

label: Workshare allocation tracking report per prime partner program
content: Quarterly workshare tracking report maintained by VP BD per #1628 and #1782 for each approved teaming arrangement. Report sections per prime partner and program: (1) Arrangement Summary — prime contractor name, program or contract number, teaming agreement date, CEO-approved workshare percentage commitment, period of performance, and total program value; (2) Committed vs. Received Workshare — cumulative task orders or contract modifications issued to company ($, % of total program value) vs. teaming agreement commitment; variance in dollar and percentage terms; (3) Quarterly Milestone Trend — committed workshare schedule from teaming agreement vs. actuals per quarter; color-coded Green (within ±2% of target), Yellow (3–9% cumulative shortfall), Red (≥10% shortfall or two consecutive Yellow quarters); (4) Contract Line Item Detail — CLINs or task orders received this period with value, scope, and period of performance; (5) Prime Engagement Log — dates of workshare performance discussions with prime program director or supply chain manager; commitments and outcomes documented; (6) Open Actions — workshare deficiencies requiring prime engagement with assigned owner, due date, and escalation level (VP BD or CEO); (7) Workshare Adequacy Flag — current trajectory vs. 10% minimum threshold per #1782 approval criteria; CEO escalation alert when any arrangement is trending below 5% minimum. Distributed monthly to VP BD; CEO notified when any arrangement reaches Yellow or Red status for two consecutive quarters. Workshare data sourced from contract management system and BD CRM.
summary: Output: Quarterly workshare allocation tracking report per prime partner program — committed vs. received workshare with traffic-light milestone trend, prime engagement log, open actions, and CEO alert triggers when trajectories fall below the 5–10% teaming agreement minimums

label: International pursuit authorization memo with DDTC license pathway and timeline
content: Formal authorization memorandum issued by CEO as Empowered Official within 5 business days of FMS/DCS approval decision per #1783, co-signed by Export Compliance Officer. Sections: (1) Pursuit Identification — allied nation, program name, procurement agency, FMS case number (DSCA) or DCS contract vehicle, estimated value, prime intermediary if applicable, and BD pipeline CRM record reference; (2) ITAR/EAR Classification — USML category or ECCN number for each controlled article or technical data deliverable; classification rationale and controlling authority reference; (3) DDTC License Pathway — license type determined (DSP-5 permanent export / DSP-73 temporary export / TAA technical assistance agreement / applicable license exception); application preparation owner (Export Compliance Officer); target submission date; standard vs. RUSH processing election with justification; estimated DDTC processing timeline (DSP-5: 30–60 days standard, up to 120+ days complex) mapped against program schedule milestones; (4) Congressional Notification Assessment — AECA Section 36(b) threshold analysis; if notification required, estimated Congressional review period and program schedule impact; (5) License Conditions Summary — anticipated end-use restrictions, authorized end-users, retransfer prohibition summary, and Technology Control Plan reference; (6) Schedule Integration — license milestone mapped to program contract response deadline or RFP drop; go/no-go re-evaluation trigger if DDTC decision is not received within defined window before commitment deadline; (7) Authorization — CEO Empowered Official signature, Export Compliance Officer co-signature, date. Entered into FMS/DCS pipeline register (#1629) within 24 hours of issuance. Subsequent technical data exchanges must be individually verified against the authorized license scope before transmission.
summary: Output: CEO Empowered Official authorization memo for approved FMS/DCS pursuit — documents ITAR/EAR classification, DDTC license type and processing timeline, Congressional notification assessment, and end-use restrictions; co-signed by Export Compliance Officer and entered into the FMS/DCS pipeline register

label: Denied-parties screening certification report for all end-users
content: Formal screening certification report produced by Export Compliance Officer at pursuit initiation and updated at each license renewal or new party addition per #1783 and #1629. Sections: (1) Screening Scope — all screened parties listed by full legal name, country, and role: foreign government procurement agency, end-user military branch or ministry, prime contractor intermediary, known sub-tier foreign suppliers, and foreign persons requiring technical data access; (2) Lists Screened — confirmation that all parties were screened against: OFAC Specially Designated Nationals (SDN) and Blocked Persons List; State Dept Debarred Parties List (ITAR Part 127); BIS Denied Parties List (EAR Part 744/Supplement 4); OFAC Consolidated Sanctions List; UN Security Council Consolidated List for international FMS cases; (3) Screening Results Table — per party: screening date, screener identity, and result (Clear / Match Pending Review / Confirmed Match); Confirmed Match is an automatic pursuit disqualifier per #1783; Match Pending Review triggers Export Compliance Officer hold within 24 hours pending legal review; (4) Certification Statement — Export Compliance Officer certifies that as of the report date no confirmed denied-party match exists for any listed party; (5) Re-Screening Schedule — required re-screening events: at license renewal, upon addition of any new party, and annually throughout program performance; (6) Signatures — Export Compliance Officer signature, date; CEO co-signature for pursuits with estimated value >$5M. Retained in FMS/DCS compliance file per #1629. Any subsequent confirmed match triggers immediate CEO notification and program activity hold pending legal disposition.
summary: Output: Export Compliance Officer-certified denied-parties screening report confirming all FMS/DCS end-users, intermediaries, and foreign personnel were screened against OFAC SDN, State Dept Debarred Parties, and BIS Denied Parties lists, with per-party results table and re-screening schedule

label: Export license status dashboard tracking DSP-5/DSP-73 applications and expirations
content: Monthly export license status dashboard maintained by Export Compliance Officer and reviewed by CEO per #1629 and #1783. Dashboard panels: (1) Active License Register — per license: license number, type (DSP-5 / DSP-73 / TAA), program name and allied nation, USML categories authorized, authorized end-users, authorized dollar value and remaining balance, effective date, expiration date, days until expiration (amber ≤90 days, red ≤30 days), and renewal action status; (2) In-Process Applications — licenses submitted to DDTC awaiting decision: application date, DDTC case number, estimated decision date, RUSH status flag, and program schedule impact if decision is delayed beyond estimated date; (3) 90-Day Expiration Alert Queue — all licenses expiring within 90 days with renewal action owner and target submission date; any license expiring within 30 days with no renewal in process escalated immediately to CEO; (4) DSP-73 Custody Log — all articles currently outside the U.S. under temporary export authorization: item description, USML category, export date, return-by date per license terms, current location, and customs broker re-import contact; overdue returns flagged red and reported to CEO within 24 hours; (5) License Utilization Summary — per active license: authorized value vs. cumulative shipments and technical data transactions recorded; licenses at >80% utilization flagged amber for renewal planning; (6) License Exceptions Log — transactions executed under ITAR or EAR license exceptions (ITAR 126.18, EAR License Exception GOV) with exception category, transaction date, parties, and article or data description; audited semi-annually by Export Compliance Officer. Distributed monthly to CEO, VP BD, and Export Compliance Officer; reviewed at semi-annual FMS/DCS pipeline review per #1629.
summary: Output: Monthly export license status dashboard tracking all active DSP-5/DSP-73/TAA licenses, in-process DDTC applications, 90-day expiration alert queue, DSP-73 custody positions, license utilization rates, and license exception log for CEO and Export Compliance Officer review

label: Regulatory
summary: Department: Regulatory

label: AS9100 Rev D
summary: Role: AS9100 Rev D in Regulatory

label: Clause 4 — Context of the Organization
content: Clause 4 requires the organization to determine external/internal issues relevant to its purpose and strategic direction, understand needs of interested parties (customers, regulatory authorities, suppliers), and define QMS scope including aerospace-specific requirements. The scope must address product safety, prevention of counterfeit parts, and applicable statutory/regulatory requirements.
summary: AS9100D Clause 4: organizational context, interested parties, QMS scope

label: 4.1 Understanding the Organization
content: SWOT analysis for QMS context. Must consider: industry-specific risks, technology changes, supply chain complexity, organizational knowledge, regulatory environment changes. Review annually at management review.
summary: Determine internal/external issues affecting QMS

label: 4.4 QMS and Its Processes
content: Define processes needed for QMS: inputs/outputs, sequence/interaction, criteria/methods for effectiveness, resources needed, responsibilities, risks/opportunities. Must address: process owners, KPIs, customer requirements flowdown, regulatory compliance verification.
summary: Process approach requirements for aerospace QMS

label: Clause 5 — Leadership
content: Clause 5 requires top management to demonstrate leadership and commitment to the QMS by ensuring quality policy and objectives are established, integrating QMS into business processes, promoting process approach and risk-based thinking, and ensuring QMS achieves intended results. Must ensure customer focus, establish quality policy communicated throughout the organization, and assign roles/responsibilities/authorities.
summary: AS9100D Clause 5: management commitment, customer focus, quality policy, org roles

label: 5.1 Leadership and Commitment
content: Top management must: ensure quality policy/objectives are compatible with strategic direction, ensure QMS integration into business processes, promote use of process approach and risk-based thinking, ensure resources available, communicate importance of effective QMS, ensure QMS achieves intended results, engage/direct/support persons, promote improvement, support other relevant management roles.
summary: Top management commitment to QMS effectiveness

label: 5.2 Quality Policy
content: Quality policy must: be appropriate to purpose/context, provide framework for quality objectives, include commitment to satisfy requirements, include commitment to continual improvement. Must be available as documented information, communicated/understood/applied within organization, available to relevant interested parties.
summary: Establishing and communicating quality policy

label: 5.3 Organizational Roles, Responsibilities and Authorities
content: Top management must assign responsibility/authority for: ensuring QMS conforms to requirements, ensuring processes deliver intended outputs, reporting QMS performance and improvement opportunities, ensuring promotion of customer focus, ensuring QMS integrity maintained during changes. Must include management representative with authority for resolving quality issues.
summary: Assigning QMS roles and authorities

label: Clause 6 — Planning
content: Clause 6 requires planning to address risks and opportunities (considering context and interested parties), establish quality objectives at relevant functions/levels/processes, and plan changes to QMS in a systematic manner. Aerospace additions include product safety risks, operational risks, and supply chain risks.
summary: AS9100D Clause 6: risk-based thinking, quality objectives, planning of changes

label: 6.1 Actions to Address Risks and Opportunities
content: Must consider issues from 4.1 and requirements from 4.2 to determine risks/opportunities that need addressing. Plan actions to address risks/opportunities, integrate into QMS processes, evaluate effectiveness. Actions proportionate to potential impact on product/service conformity. Aerospace: must address product safety, counterfeit prevention, supply chain risks.
summary: Risk-based thinking for QMS planning

label: 6.2 Quality Objectives and Planning
content: Quality objectives must: be consistent with quality policy, be measurable, take into account applicable requirements, be relevant to product/service conformity, be monitored, be communicated, be updated as appropriate. Planning must determine: what will be done, what resources required, who responsible, when completed, how results evaluated.
summary: Setting measurable quality objectives

label: Clause 7 — Support
content: Clause 7 covers support requirements: determining/providing resources (people, infrastructure, monitoring equipment, organizational knowledge), ensuring competence through education/training/experience, awareness of quality policy/objectives, internal/external communications, and controlling documented information including retention requirements.
summary: AS9100D Clause 7: resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information

label: 7.1.5 Monitoring and Measuring Resources
content: Equipment must be calibrated/verified at specified intervals against traceable standards. Calibration status must be identifiable. Records retained. If found out of calibration, assess validity of previous results. Applies to all measurement equipment affecting product conformity — including software used for inspection.
summary: Calibration and measurement system requirements

label: 7.5 Documented Information
content: Controlled documents: creation/update with proper identification, format, review/approval. Distribution, access, retrieval, storage, preservation, retention, disposition. Prevent unintended use of obsolete documents. External documents identified and controlled. Electronic signatures validated per 21 CFR Part 11 where applicable.
summary: Document control and records management for aerospace

label: 8.1 Operational Planning and Control
content: Operational planning must address configuration management throughout product lifecycle, product safety assessment and management, prevention of counterfeit parts (detection, quarantine, reporting), and work transfer activities (in-house, supplier, between facilities). Must plan, implement, and control processes needed to meet requirements for provision of products and services.
summary: AS9100D 8.1: config mgmt, product safety, counterfeit prevention, work transfer

label: Configuration Management
content: Configuration management per AS9100D §8.1 must address five disciplines: (1) CONFIGURATION IDENTIFICATION: Assign unique Configuration Index (CI) revision to each approved design baseline; link CI to BOM revision, affected serial number ranges, and applied ECOs. CI document lists approved component part numbers, approved alternates per BOM position, modification codes, and critical characteristic references. (2) CHANGE CONTROL: Class I changes (affect form/fit/function) require CCB review with customer/authority approval and impact assessment on supplier qualification, tooling, and WIP. Class II (documentation only) may use expedited review. (3) STATUS ACCOUNTING: Maintain Configuration Baseline Register linking each unit serial number to declared CI revision + applied ECOs — enables reconstruction of any unit's as-built configuration. (4) CONFIGURATION AUDIT: Verify that physical product matches declared baseline (functional configuration audit) and that documentation accurately reflects the product (physical configuration audit) — required before production lot acceptance. (5) PHYSICAL SEGREGATION: When concurrent builds have different configurations, AS9100D §8.5.2 requires controls to prevent commingling — distinct staging zones, visual identification, and pre-assembly verification holdpoints. Note: EIA-649-1 provides implementation guidance for CM disciplines complementary to AS9100D requirements.
summary: Product configuration control per AS9100D 8.1

label: Counterfeit Parts Prevention
content: AS9100D Section 8.1.4 requires documented processes to detect and prevent counterfeit or suspect counterfeit parts from entering production. STANDARD SCOPE DISTINCTIONS (critical — do not conflate): AS5553/AS5553A applies to manufacturers and contractors for electronic parts (EEE parts); AS6081 applies specifically to independent distributors of electronic/electrical parts (NOT to manufacturers or raw material sourcing broadly); AS6174 applies to non-electronic counterfeit materiel avoidance for manufacturers. For DoD contracts, the counterfeit electronic parts clause is DFARS 252.246-7007 (Counterfeit Electronic Part Detection and Avoidance System) and 252.246-7008 (Sources of Electronic Parts) — NOT FAR 52.209-2, which governs inverted domestic corporations. REQUIRED ELEMENTS: (1) Approved supplier/distributor list with franchise status verification; (2) Component authentication at receiving (visual, dimensional, composition testing for risk-tiered materials); (3) Lot-level traceability linking components to certified source and material lot numbers; (4) Employee counterfeit awareness training (receiving inspection, purchasing, quality); (5) Segregation and quarantine procedure for suspect parts; (6) Customer and government notification procedure when counterfeit confirmed (GIDEP report required under DFARS 252.246-7007(c)); (7) Flow-down to subcontractors at all tiers. COMMON PITFALL: Aggregate CoC without lot-level traceability does not satisfy counterfeit avoidance traceability requirements — require heat/lot number linkage to original mill or OEM source.
summary: AS9100D 8.1.4 counterfeit parts prevention: scope distinctions (AS5553 manufacturers, AS6081 distributors, AS6174 non-electronic), DFARS 252.246-7007/7008 for DoD contracts, lot-level traceability, GIDEP reporting requirement

label: 8.2 Requirements for Products and Services
content: Requirements determination must include customer-specified requirements (delivery, post-delivery, product safety), requirements not stated but necessary for intended use, statutory/regulatory requirements, and any additional requirements the organization considers necessary. Must review requirements before commitment to supply, resolve differences, confirm customer requirements when not documented.
summary: AS9100D 8.2: customer communication, requirement determination/review

label: 8.3 Design and Development
content: Design and development must consider: nature/duration/complexity, required stages (including reviews), verification/validation activities, responsibilities/authorities, resource needs, interface management between groups, customer/user involvement, subsequent production requirements, expected level of control. Inputs must include functional/performance requirements, applicable statutory/regulatory, information from previous similar designs, standards/codes of practice, potential consequences of failure.
summary: AS9100D 8.3: D&D planning, inputs, controls, outputs, changes

label: 8.3.4 Design and Development Controls
content: Controls must ensure: results to be achieved are defined, reviews evaluate ability to meet requirements, verification confirms outputs meet input requirements, validation confirms product meets intended use requirements. Must consider product safety, reliability, durability, maintainability, testability. Design reviews at planned stages — include representatives of functions concerned.
summary: Design verification, validation, and review controls

label: Clause 8 — Operation (Existing)
content: Clause 8 is the core operational clause covering: operational planning and control, requirements for products/services, design and development, control of externally provided processes/products/services, production and service provision, release of products, and control of nonconforming outputs. Includes critical aerospace requirements for configuration management, product safety, prevention of counterfeit parts, and first article inspection.
summary: AS9100D Clause 8: operational planning, design, production, release

label: 8.4 Control of External Providers
content: Determine controls for external providers based on: type/extent of control, defined criteria for evaluation/selection/monitoring/re-evaluation. Flowdown requirements: product/service requirements, approval of products/processes/equipment, competence/qualification of personnel, QMS requirements, interaction with provider's QMS, verification activities, right of access by organization and customer. Must maintain approved supplier list and monitor supplier performance.
summary: Supplier management and flowdown requirements

label: 8.5 Production and Service Provision
content: Controlled conditions: documented information defining product characteristics and activities, monitoring/measuring resources, process validation for special processes (per NADCAP where applicable), actions to prevent human error (poka-yoke), production process verification, FOD prevention program, tool/equipment management including first-piece verification, control of production equipment/tooling/software programs.
summary: Production controls including special processes and FOD

label: 8.7 Control of Nonconforming Outputs
content: Nonconforming outputs must be identified, controlled, and prevented from unintended use/delivery. Disposition: rework, accept with/without concession (requires customer authorization for use-as-is/repair), scrap. Retain documented information on nonconformity, actions taken, concessions, authority making disposition. Report to customer when product shipped under concession.
summary: Nonconformance disposition: use-as-is, rework, scrap

label: Clause 9 — Performance Evaluation
content: Clause 9 requires: monitoring/measuring/analyzing/evaluating QMS effectiveness, customer satisfaction measurement, internal audits, and management review. Aerospace additions include: on-time delivery performance, product safety/conformity trends, and supplier performance metrics.
summary: AS9100D Clause 9: monitoring, measurement, analysis, evaluation

label: 9.1 Monitoring and Measurement
content: Must monitor/measure: customer satisfaction, QMS process performance, product/service conformity, on-time delivery (OTD) performance. Determine methods, timing, and criteria for analysis. Use statistical techniques where appropriate. Results feed into management review inputs.
summary: KPIs for QMS performance including OTD and quality metrics

label: 9.2 Internal Audit
content: Planned audit program considering: process importance, changes affecting organization, previous audit results. Audit criteria, scope, frequency, methods defined. Auditor objectivity/impartiality ensured. Results reported to relevant management. Corrective actions taken without undue delay. Follow-up verification of actions. Audit program per AS9101 Standard for Audit requirements.
summary: Internal audit program per AS9100D and AS9101 requirements

label: Clause 10 — Improvement
content: Clause 10 covers: nonconformity and corrective action (react, evaluate, implement, review effectiveness), and continual improvement of QMS suitability/adequacy/effectiveness. Aerospace additions: timely/effective corrective action proportional to impact, problem-solving methodology (8D, Ishikawa, 5-Why), lessons learned dissemination.
summary: AS9100D Clause 10: nonconformity, corrective action, continual improvement

label: 10.2 Corrective Action
content: React to nonconformity: take action to control/correct, deal with consequences. Evaluate need for action to eliminate cause by: reviewing/analyzing nonconformity, determining causes (8D, 5-Why, Ishikawa), determining if similar nonconformities exist. Implement action, review effectiveness, update risks/opportunities, make changes to QMS if needed. Corrective actions proportional to effects of nonconformities encountered.
summary: Root cause analysis and corrective action per AS9100D

label: FAR/DFARS
summary: Role: FAR/DFARS in Regulatory

label: FAR Part 15 — Contracting by Negotiation
content: FAR Part 15 governs negotiated acquisitions: proposal evaluation factors, cost/price analysis requirements, competitive range determination, discussions/negotiations, source selection procedures. Contractors must provide certified cost or pricing data (per 15.403) when contract value exceeds threshold ($2M) unless exception applies (adequate price competition, prices set by law/regulation, commercial items).
summary: Cost/price analysis, proposal preparation, competitive range

label: 15.403 Cost or Pricing Data
content: Certified cost or pricing data required for negotiated contracts/modifications >$2M threshold (adjusted for inflation). Exceptions: adequate price competition, prices set by law/regulation, commercial items, waiver. Data must be accurate, complete, current as of agreement on price. Defective pricing clause (52.215-10) allows price reduction if data was defective.
summary: Truth in Negotiations Act (TINA) requirements

label: FAR Part 31 — Cost Principles
content: FAR Part 31 establishes cost principles for determining allowable costs under government contracts. Key tests: allowability (per contract terms and regulations), allocability (benefits the contract), reasonableness (prudent business person standard). Selected costs: 31.205-6 compensation (reasonable for services rendered), 31.205-18 independent R&D/B&P, 31.205-33 professional services, 31.205-46 travel.
summary: Allowable costs, allocability, reasonableness standards

label: 31.201 Allowability Criteria
content: A cost is allowable if it meets ALL four criteria: (1) Reasonableness — prudent business person would incur at that amount; (2) Allocability — benefits the contract or is necessary for overall business operation; (3) Complies with CAS/GAAP and contract terms; (4) Not specifically unallowable under FAR 31.205 cost principles. Burden of proof on contractor.
summary: Four tests for cost allowability

label: FAR Part 32 — Contract Financing
content: FAR Part 32 covers contract financing methods: progress payments based on costs (customary 80% for large business, 90% for small), performance-based payments (preferred method tied to milestones/events), commercial item financing, advance payments, and loan guarantees. Liquidation of progress payments upon delivery. Contractors must maintain adequate accounting systems for progress payment requests.
summary: Progress payments, performance-based payments, loan guarantees

label: FAR Part 42 — Contract Administration
content: FAR Part 42 governs contract administration: assignment of contract administration offices (CAO), contracting officer's representative (COR) duties, contract modifications (bilateral/unilateral), novation agreements (successor-in-interest), change-of-name agreements, stop-work orders, government delay of work, contract closeout procedures. ACO responsibilities include monitoring contractor performance, approving/disapproving accounting systems, and issuing determination of adequacy.
summary: COR duties, modifications, novation, change-of-name

label: FAR Part 45 — Government Property
content: FAR Part 45 covers government property in possession of contractors: government-furnished equipment (GFE), government-furnished material (GFM), contractor-acquired property. Requirements: property management system, records/tracking, physical inventories, maintenance, risk of loss, disposition (return, transfer, sale, donation, abandonment/destruction). Property in contractor's possession is government property regardless of how acquired under contract.
summary: Contractor use, management, and disposal of GFE/GFM

label: FAR Part 46 — Quality Assurance
content: FAR Part 46 covers quality assurance: types of contract quality requirements (standard inspection, higher-level, government source inspection), inspection and acceptance procedures, certificate of conformance, warranties (express and implied), correction of defects, inspection by higher-level authority. Government source inspection required for critical items, complex requirements, or when past performance warrants.
summary: Inspection levels, acceptance, warranties, government source inspection

label: FAR Part 49 — Termination of Contracts
content: FAR Part 49 covers termination procedures: termination for convenience (T4C) — government's right to end contract when in its interest, contractor entitled to settlement (costs incurred + profit on work done + settlement expenses); termination for default (T4D) — contractor fails to perform, may be liable for excess reprocurement costs. Settlement proposals: inventory basis or total cost basis. Contractor must stop work, protect government property, terminate subcontracts.
summary: T4C vs T4D, settlement proposals, continuing claims

label: FAR Part 52 — Solicitation Provisions and Contract Clauses
content: FAR Part 52 contains standard provisions and clauses prescribed throughout the FAR. Key clauses: 52.215-10 (Price Reduction for Defective Pricing), 52.222-26 (Equal Opportunity), 52.223-6 (Drug-Free Workplace), 52.227-14 (Rights in Data), 52.244-6 (Subcontractor flowdown).
summary: Standard contract clauses and flowdown requirements

label: DFARS 252.204 — Safeguarding CUI
content: DFARS 252.204-7012 requires contractors to: provide adequate security for covered defense information on covered contractor information systems, report cyber incidents within 72 hours, submit malicious software, preserve/protect images of affected systems. Requires compliance with NIST SP 800-171. Flows down to subcontractors handling CUI.
summary: Controlled Unclassified Information protection requirements

label: 252.204-7012 Safeguarding Requirements
content: Must implement NIST SP 800-171 for CUI systems. 72-hour reporting for cyber incidents to DoD via DIBNet. Preserve affected systems for 90 days. Provide access to equipment/information for forensic analysis. Subcontractor flowdown required for all tiers handling CUI. Cloud services must meet FedRAMP Moderate or DoD Cloud SRG Impact Level 2+.
summary: CUI protection and cyber incident reporting

label: DFARS 252.211 — Item Unique Identification
content: DFARS 252.211-7003 requires item unique identification (IUID) for all delivered items with unit acquisition cost ≥$5,000, regardless of dollar value if item is serially managed/mission essential/controlled inventory, and items embedded within larger items. Data matrix marking per MIL-STD-130. Data submitted to IUID Registry. Concatenated UIIs using enterprise identifier + serial number or batch/lot.
summary: IUID marking requirements for defense items

label: DFARS 252.225 — Foreign Acquisition
content: DFARS 252.225 implements restrictions on foreign acquisition: Buy American Act compliance, specialty metals restrictions (252.225-7009/7014), qualifying country sources, trade agreements. Specialty metals (titanium, zirconium, hafnium, tungsten, steel alloys) must be melted/produced in US or qualifying country for DoD contracts unless exception applies.
summary: Buy American Act, specialty metals, qualifying countries

label: DFARS 252.227 — Technical Data Rights
content: DFARS 252.227-7013/7014 establish data rights framework: unlimited rights (developed exclusively at government expense), government purpose rights (developed with mixed funding — 5-year restriction then unlimited), limited/restricted rights (developed exclusively at private expense). Contractors must assert rights via 252.227-7017 marking requirements. Pre-existing data rights preserved.
summary: Government data rights in technical data and software

label: DFARS 252.246 — Quality Assurance
content: DFARS 252.246-7001 requires first article testing (FAT) approval before production: contractor submits first article for government testing/approval, production quantities not authorized until approval. 252.246-7007 establishes higher-level contract quality requirements when standard inspection is insufficient — requires contractor QMS per AS9100 or equivalent, government source inspection, and statistical process control.
summary: First article testing and higher-level quality requirements

label: SAE AS6500 — Manufacturing Management
summary: Role: SAE AS6500 — Manufacturing Management in Regulatory

label: Process Planning and Control
content: AS6500 requires formal process planning: manufacturing plan documents, process flow diagrams, control plans, work instructions. Must address: material control, equipment maintenance, environmental controls, in-process verification, statistical process control (SPC), special process qualification. All manufacturing processes must be validated before production use.
summary: Manufacturing process planning per AS6500

label: Process Validation
content: Demonstrate process capability (Cpk ≥ 1.33 minimum, 1.67 preferred for critical characteristics). First article inspection (FAI) per AS9102 required for new parts, processes, or significant changes. Process parameters documented and monitored via SPC. Special processes require NADCAP accreditation or equivalent qualification.
summary: First article and process capability requirements

label: Production Readiness
content: Formal production readiness review (PRR) before rate production: tooling validated, processes qualified, operators trained/certified, supply chain qualified, quality plans approved, measurement systems validated (MSA), first articles completed. Risk assessment of production maturity using Manufacturing Readiness Level (MRL) framework.
summary: Production readiness review criteria per AS6500

label: Material Management
content: Material management requirements: receiving inspection and verification of material certifications, storage conditions per material specification (temperature, humidity, ESD controls), traceability from raw material to finished part (heat lot, batch, serial), shelf-life management for age-sensitive materials (adhesives, sealants, prepregs), material review board (MRB) for nonconforming material disposition.
summary: Receiving, storage, traceability, shelf life, material review

label: Tooling Management
content: Tooling management requirements: tool design validation before production use, preventive maintenance schedules, FOD prevention controls for tooling (accounting, inspection, marking), tool life tracking for perishable tooling (cutters, drills, inserts), calibration of measurement tools per AS6500/ISO 10012, first-piece verification after tool change/rework, storage and environmental protection.
summary: Design validation, maintenance, FOD prevention, tool life tracking

label: Supplier Process Control
content: Supplier process control: supplier qualification assessment before approval, ongoing monitoring via scorecards (quality, delivery, responsiveness), flowdown of process requirements including special process accreditation (NADCAP), sub-tier supplier management and visibility, right of access for customer/regulatory authority source inspection, supplier corrective action process, approved supplier list maintenance.
summary: Qualification, monitoring, flowdown, sub-tier management

label: ITAR/EAR Export Controls
content: ITAR/EAR supplier compliance flow-down for aerospace manufacturing. KEY REQUIREMENTS: (1) Technical data access restriction: limit to U.S. persons only per 22 CFR Part 120; Green Card holders qualify; H-1B/L-1 visa holders require Technology Control Plan (TCP) with DDTC authorization. (2) Foreign national access control: maintain visitor log (date, name, citizenship, purpose, escort); no unauthorized foreign national access to drawings, specs, process documents, or test data. (3) Subcontractor flow-down: certify all sub-tier suppliers as U.S.-owned, U.S.-controlled, U.S.-persons-only access; foreign subcontractors prohibited without State Department license. (4) Technical data marking: all ITAR-restricted documents marked 'ITAR Controlled—Export Restricted per 22 CFR Part 120-130'; electronic access via ITAR-compliant encrypted network only. (5) Annual ITAR training: all personnel with restricted-data access trained annually; records retained 5 years. (6) Anomaly reporting: potential ITAR breach reported within 24 hours; root-cause investigation within 5 business days. (7) Foreign content reporting: supplier certifies U.S.-origin components or documents foreign-source justification; Material Test Reports include country of origin. (8) Annual compliance audit: joint Contractor + Supplier; findings resolved within 30 days. EAR (15 CFR 730-774): applies to dual-use items on Commerce Control List (CCL); ECCN classification required; BIS-administered. CONSEQUENCES OF NON-COMPLIANCE: DDTC debarment, criminal penalties up to $1M per violation per 22 CFR Part 127, program suspension, loss of export privileges. COMMON PITFALLS: failing to screen sub-tier suppliers, allowing foreign nationals network access to drawings, not maintaining visitor logs, inadequate subcontractor NDA coverage.
summary: Role: ITAR/EAR Export Controls in Regulatory

label: ITAR — International Traffic in Arms Regulations
content: ITAR (22 CFR 120-130) controls export of defense articles on the US Munitions List (USML). Requires: State Department registration, export licenses (DSP-5 for permanent, DSP-73 for temporary), Technical Assistance Agreements (TAA) for defense services, Manufacturing License Agreements (MLA). Violations: civil penalties up to $500K per violation, criminal penalties up to $1M and 20 years imprisonment. No de minimis exception — any ITAR content makes entire item ITAR-controlled.
summary: USML-controlled defense articles and services

label: ITAR Compliance Program Elements
content: Effective ITAR compliance requires: (1) Empowered Official designation, (2) technology control plan (TCP), (3) visitor access procedures, (4) IT security for controlled data, (5) employee training and awareness, (6) voluntary disclosure procedures, (7) screening against denied parties lists (DDTC, BIS), (8) record retention (5 years), (9) regular compliance audits.
summary: Required elements of an ITAR compliance program

label: EAR — Export Administration Regulations
content: EAR (15 CFR 730-774) controls export of dual-use items on the Commerce Control List (CCL). Classification by Export Control Classification Number (ECCN). License requirements vary by ECCN, destination country, end-use, and end-user. License exceptions (e.g., TMP for temporary exports, TSR for technology/software under restriction). EAR99 items generally exportable without license except to embargoed destinations or denied parties.
summary: Dual-use items on Commerce Control List

label: USML Categories
content: Key USML categories for aerospace/defense: Category IV — Launch Vehicles, Guided Missiles, Ballistic Missiles, Rockets, Torpedoes, Bombs, Mines (includes UAV strike systems). Category VIII — Aircraft and Related Articles (military aircraft, engines, components, ground support equipment). Category XI — Military Electronics (C4ISR, EW, radar, military GPS). Category XV — Spacecraft and Related Articles (satellites, space-qualified components, ground control equipment). Jurisdiction determination critical — some items may be EAR-controlled instead.
summary: Key USML categories for aerospace: Cat IV, VIII, XI, XV

label: Deemed Exports
content: A deemed export occurs when controlled technology/source code is released to a foreign national in the US — treated as an export to the person's home country. Requires license or license exception. Fundamental research exclusion (§734.8): basic/applied research at accredited institutions, results ordinarily published, no access/dissemination restrictions. Does not apply to proprietary/export-controlled research. Employers must screen employees and visitors against ITAR/EAR requirements.
summary: Technology release to foreign nationals in US, fundamental research exclusion

label: TAA/MLA Details
content: Technical Assistance Agreements (TAA): authorize defense services (training, technical data, maintenance) to foreign persons. Manufacturing License Agreements (MLA): authorize foreign production of defense articles. Both require: provisos/limitations, Congressional notification for agreements >$50M (MLA) or $100M (TAA) or significant military equipment >$14M. Amendments required for scope changes, additional foreign parties, or extended duration. Processing time typically 30-60 days for non-Congressional cases.
summary: Agreement types, provisos, amendments, Congressional notification

label: NADCAP Special Process Accreditation
summary: Role: NADCAP Special Process Accreditation in Regulatory

label: AC7000 — Audit Criteria General
content: AC7000 establishes baseline requirements for all NADCAP accreditations: quality system documentation, personnel qualification, equipment calibration, process control documentation, nonconformance procedures, internal audit, continuous improvement. Root cause corrective action required for all findings. Merit status available after consecutive successful audits.
summary: NADCAP general audit requirements applicable to all special processes

label: AC7004 — Welding
content: Covers fusion welding processes: GTAW, GMAW, PAW, EBW, LBW, resistance welding. Requirements: qualified welding procedures (AWS D17.1 for aerospace), welder qualification/certification, welding parameter monitoring, joint preparation standards, shielding gas purity, post-weld heat treatment, NDT inspection (radiographic/ultrasonic per applicable spec). Weld quality per AWS D17.1 Class A/B/C.
summary: NADCAP welding process accreditation requirements

label: AC7101/AC7102 — Heat Treating
content: AC7101 (general heat treat) and AC7102 (specific alloy requirements). Covers: solution treatment, aging, annealing, normalizing, stress relief, hardening/tempering. Requirements: pyrometry per AMS 2750 (instrument calibration, SAT/TUS surveys), atmosphere control, quench system monitoring, hardness testing, metallographic examination, batch/lot traceability.
summary: NADCAP heat treatment accreditation for metals processing

label: AMS 2750 Pyrometry
content: AMS 2750 Rev G requirements: instrument types (1-5), SAT frequency based on furnace class/instrument type, TUS requirements by furnace class (1-6) with tolerances ±5°F to ±50°F. Thermocouple types, calibration frequency, correction factors. Load thermocouples required for Class 1/2 furnaces. Furnace survey interval extensions based on successful history.
summary: Temperature measurement and control for heat treating

label: AC7108/AC7109 — Chemical Processing
content: AC7108 (chemical processing) covers: cleaning, etching, conversion coating, anodizing, passivation, chemical milling. AC7109 (coatings) covers: electroplating, electroless plating, phosphate coating. Requirements: solution analysis/control, process parameter monitoring (temperature, time, concentration, pH), thickness measurement, adhesion testing, hydrogen embrittlement relief.
summary: NADCAP chemical processing and surface treatment accreditation

label: AC7114 — Liquid Penetrant Testing
content: AC7114 covers liquid penetrant testing accreditation: fluorescent and visible methods, sensitivity levels (½ to 4 per AMS 2644), pre-cleaning requirements, dwell times, developer application, inspection under UV-A (fluorescent) or white light (visible). Process control: penetrant material testing per ASTM E1417, UV light intensity verification (≥1000 µW/cm²), ambient light control (<2 fc for fluorescent), system performance checks (TAM/PSM panels), contamination control.
summary: NADCAP fluorescent/visible PT, sensitivity levels, process control

label: AC7113 — Ultrasonic Testing
content: AC7113 covers ultrasonic testing accreditation: pulse-echo (A-scan, B-scan, C-scan), through-transmission, phased array, immersion and contact methods. Requirements: equipment calibration (linearity, DAC curves, reference standards), transducer characterization, scan plan documentation, gate settings, surface condition requirements, coupling medium control. Acceptance criteria per customer/spec requirements. Data recording and archival for critical components.
summary: NADCAP pulse-echo, through-transmission, phased array, immersion/contact

label: AC7110/AC7111 — Radiographic/Eddy Current
content: AC7110 covers radiographic testing: film-based and digital (CR/DR) methods, source selection (X-ray/gamma), geometric unsharpness, image quality indicators (IQI/penetrameters), film density/contrast requirements, darkroom controls, film processing/storage. AC7111 covers eddy current testing: surface and subsurface detection, impedance plane analysis, frequency selection, probe/coil calibration, reference standards, conductivity measurement, liftoff compensation.
summary: Film/digital RT and ET for surface/subsurface detection

label: AC7120 — Nonmetallic Materials Testing
content: AC7120 covers testing of nonmetallic materials: mechanical testing (tensile, compression, flexural, shear, peel, lap shear per ASTM D-series), thermal analysis (DSC, TGA, DMA, TMA), environmental testing (fluid resistance, humidity, weathering), specimen preparation requirements, conditioning procedures, test fixture requirements. Critical for incoming material acceptance, process qualification, and lot acceptance of composite/adhesive/sealant materials.
summary: Polymer/composite mechanical, thermal, environmental testing

label: AC7116 — Elastomer Seals
content: Covers molding and bonding of elastomeric seals for aerospace applications. Requirements: compound certification, mold validation, cure parameter control (temperature, pressure, time), dimensional inspection, hardness testing, compression set testing, fluid compatibility, lot acceptance testing per applicable seal specifications.
summary: NADCAP elastomer seal processing accreditation

label: AC7118 — Composites
content: Covers layup, cure, bonding, and repair of composite structures. Requirements: material receiving inspection (prepreg life tracking, resin content, fiber areal weight), controlled environment (cleanroom class, temperature, humidity), layup verification (ply orientation, compaction), autoclave cure monitoring (temperature, pressure, vacuum), NDI per applicable specification (ultrasonic, thermography), coupon testing per process spec.
summary: NADCAP composite material processing accreditation

label: MIL-STD Series
summary: Role: MIL-STD Series in Regulatory

label: MIL-STD-810H — Environmental Testing
content: MIL-STD-810H provides test methods and engineering guidance for environmental considerations in design and test. Method categories: climatic (temperature, humidity, rain, sand/dust, solar radiation), dynamic (vibration, shock, acceleration, pyroshock), pressure (altitude), and chemical (salt fog, fluid contamination). Tailoring is required — use service environment data, not just standard profiles. Part One provides engineering guidance; Part Two/Three contain test methods.
summary: Environmental engineering considerations and test methods

label: Method 514.8 — Vibration
content: Covers: Category 1-24 vibration environments (aircraft, ground vehicles, shipboard, etc.). Test types: sinusoidal sweep (resonance search), random vibration (operational/functional), combined environments (vibration + temperature). Tailoring: use measured data from actual platform when available. Duration: endurance testing based on life cycle exposure hours. Fixture design per Method 514 Annex A guidelines.
summary: Vibration test methods: sinusoidal, random, combined environments

label: MIL-STD-461G — EMI/EMC Requirements
content: MIL-STD-461G establishes EMI/EMC requirements for military equipment. Emission limits: CE101/CE102 (conducted, power leads), RE101/RE102 (radiated). Susceptibility limits: CS101/CS114/CS115/CS116 (conducted), RS103 (radiated). Test setup per MIL-STD-461G: ground plane, LISN configuration, antenna positioning, ambient measurements. Applicability tailored by platform (aircraft, ship, ground, space).
summary: Electromagnetic interference and compatibility requirements

label: MIL-STD-882E — System Safety
content: MIL-STD-882E establishes system safety engineering practices: hazard severity categories (I-IV: Catastrophic to Negligible), hazard probability levels (A-F: Frequent to Eliminated), risk assessment matrix. Required analyses: Preliminary Hazard List (PHL), Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA), Subsystem Hazard Analysis (SSHA), System Hazard Analysis (SHA), Operating & Support Hazard Analysis (O&SHA). Software safety per MIL-STD-882E Task 207.
summary: System safety program requirements and hazard analysis

label: Hazard Risk Assessment Matrix
content: Severity: I=Catastrophic (death/system loss), II=Critical (severe injury/major damage), III=Marginal (minor injury/minor damage), IV=Negligible. Probability: A=Frequent, B=Probable, C=Occasional, D=Remote, E=Improbable, F=Eliminated. Risk levels: High (IA-IIC), Serious (ID-IIIB), Medium (IE-IIIC), Low (IIF-IVF). Acceptance authority: High=PEO/DASA, Serious=Program Manager, Medium=delegated, Low=delegated.
summary: Severity vs probability risk classification

label: MIL-STD-881E — Work Breakdown Structure
content: MIL-STD-881E defines standard WBS elements for defense programs by commodity type: Aircraft Systems, Electronic/Automated Software Systems, Missile Systems, Ordnance Systems, Sea Systems, Space Systems, Surface Vehicle Systems, Unmanned Maritime Systems. Level 3+ elements tailored by program. Used for cost estimating, scheduling, EVM reporting, and technical performance measurement.
summary: Standard WBS for defense materiel items

label: MIL-STD-1530 — Aircraft Structural Integrity
content: MIL-STD-1530 establishes Aircraft Structural Integrity Program (ASIP) requirements across five tasks: Task I (Design Information), Task II (Design Analyses & Development Testing), Task III (Full-Scale Testing), Task IV (Certification & Force Management Development), Task V (Force Management Execution). Covers: damage tolerance, durability, fatigue life management, corrosion prevention, loads/environment spectra.
summary: ASIP requirements for aircraft structures

label: MIL-STD-130N — Marking
content: MIL-STD-130N establishes requirements for identification marking of US military property. Unique identification (UID) via machine-readable 2D data matrix per ISO/IEC 16022. Marking methods: direct part marking (DPM — dot peen, laser etch, chemical etch, inkjet), labels (metallic, polyester). Content: enterprise identifier (CAGE/DUNS), serial number, part number, batch/lot. Marking location, size, contrast requirements. Verification per AS9132 (quality grade).
summary: UID marking, data matrix, human-readable identification

label: MIL-STD-1472H — Human Engineering
content: MIL-STD-1472H establishes human engineering design criteria for military systems/equipment. Covers: workspace design (anthropometric data, reach envelopes, clearances), controls and displays (layout, labeling, coding, feedback), visual displays (CRT/LCD requirements, symbology, lighting), auditory displays (warning signals, voice communication), environment (illumination, noise, vibration, temperature), maintainability (access, handling, test points), safety (hazard prevention, warnings).
summary: Human factors design criteria for military systems

label: MIL-STD-31000B — Technical Data Packages
content: MIL-STD-31000B defines requirements for technical data packages (TDP): content (drawings, specifications, 3D models, associated lists), delivery format (2D drawings per ASME Y14 series, 3D models per ASME Y14.41/ISO 10303 STEP), TDP types (conceptual, developmental, product, process), validation requirements, intellectual property/data rights marking per DFARS 252.227. Supports transition to model-based definition (MBD) and digital engineering.
summary: TDP content, delivery format, model-based definition

label: ISO Standards
summary: Role: ISO Standards in Regulatory

label: ISO 9001:2015 — Quality Management
content: ISO 9001:2015 establishes QMS requirements using process approach and risk-based thinking. Seven quality management principles: customer focus, leadership, engagement of people, process approach, improvement, evidence-based decision making, relationship management. AS9100 Rev D is built on ISO 9001:2015 with additional aerospace-specific requirements.
summary: Quality management system requirements (foundation for AS9100)

label: ISO 14001:2015 — Environmental Management
content: ISO 14001:2015 EMS framework: environmental policy, planning (aspects/impacts, legal requirements, objectives), support and operation (resources, communication, documented information, operational planning, emergency preparedness), performance evaluation (monitoring, internal audit, management review), improvement. Relevant for aerospace manufacturing: hazardous materials, emissions, waste management, chemical storage.
summary: Environmental management system requirements

label: ISO 45001:2018 — Occupational Health and Safety
content: ISO 45001:2018 replaces OHSAS 18001. Framework: context of organization, worker participation, hazard identification and risk assessment, legal compliance, OH&S objectives, operational planning and control, emergency preparedness, performance evaluation, incident investigation, continual improvement. Aerospace-specific hazards: chemical exposure, noise, confined spaces, fall protection, radiation, ergonomics.
summary: OH&S management system requirements

label: ISO/IEC 17025:2017 — Testing/Calibration Lab Competence
content: ISO/IEC 17025:2017 establishes competence requirements for testing and calibration laboratories. KEY CLAUSE GROUPS: (1) Impartiality (§4) — lab must be structurally independent from production pressures; personnel cannot be rewarded based on test outcome. (2) Resource Requirements (§6) — personnel competence (§6.2), facilities/environmental conditions (§6.3), equipment (§6.4), metrological traceability (§6.5), externally provided products/services (§6.6). (3) Process Requirements (§7) — method selection/validation (§7.2), sampling (§7.3), handling of test items (§7.4), technical records (§7.5), measurement uncertainty (§7.6), ensuring validity of results (§7.7), reporting (§7.8), nonconforming work (§7.10). (4) Management System (§8) — documentation, records control, internal audits, management reviews, corrective actions. AEROSPACE SCOPE: FAI Form 3 dimensional testing, material certification testing, production-acceptance sampling, and calibration services all require ISO 17025 accreditation when an in-house lab claims measurement uncertainty validity for customer acceptance. ACCREDITATION BODIES: A2LA (aerospace-preferred, DoD-recognized), ANAB, NVLAP; internationally recognized via ILAC MRA. AS9100D INTEGRATION: AS9100D §7.1.5 (monitoring/measuring resources) and §8.5.1 (production control) reference calibrated equipment and documented test procedures — unified procedures can satisfy both standards without duplicate documentation.
summary: Lab competence requirements critical for aerospace test labs

label: ISO 19011:2018 — Auditing Management Systems
content: ISO 19011:2018 provides guidance on auditing management systems including: principles of auditing (integrity, fair presentation, due professional care, confidentiality, independence, evidence-based approach), managing an audit program (objectives, risks, resources, records), conducting an audit (planning, document review, on-site activities, reporting, follow-up), competence/evaluation of auditors. Applicable to all management system audits (ISO 9001, 14001, 45001, AS9100). Supports both internal and supplier audits.
summary: Guidelines for auditing management systems

label: OSHA 29 CFR 1910 — General Industry
summary: Role: OSHA 29 CFR 1910 — General Industry in Regulatory

label: Subpart Z — Toxic and Hazardous Substances
content: 29 CFR 1910.1000: permissible exposure limits (PELs) for airborne contaminants — Tables Z-1 (adopted from 1968 ACGIH TLVs; 8-hr TWA unless ceiling noted), Z-2 (8-hr TWA with ceiling and peak), Z-3 (mineral dusts). CONTROL OBLIGATION (1910.1000(c)(1)): employer must use engineering and administrative controls to achieve PEL compliance before relying on respiratory protection; PPE as primary control is citation risk. OBJECTIVE DATA (1910.1000(e)): historical or supplier data may substitute for air sampling only if specific to the exact process, materials, and facility conditions. SUBSTANCE-SPECIFIC STANDARDS SUPERSEDE TABLE Z-1: Cr(VI) → 1910.1026 (PEL 5 µg/m³); Lead (general industry) → 1910.1025 (PEL 50 µg/m³); Formaldehyde → 1910.1048 (PEL 0.75 ppm); Crystalline silica → 1910.1153 (PEL 50 µg/m³); Cadmium → 1910.1027; Beryllium → 1910.1024. 1910.1200 Hazard Communication (HazCom): SDS, GHS container labeling, employee training, written program.
summary: Chemical exposure limits and hazard communication

label: Subpart O — Machinery and Machine Guarding
content: 29 CFR 1910.212: general machine guarding — point of operation, ingoing nip points, rotating parts, flying chips. 1910.147: Control of Hazardous Energy (Lockout/Tagout) — energy isolation procedures, lockout devices, periodic inspection, training. Applies to CNC machines, presses, lathes, mills, grinders used in aerospace manufacturing.
summary: Machine guarding and lockout/tagout requirements

label: Subpart I — Personal Protective Equipment
content: 29 CFR 1910.132: general PPE requirements — hazard assessment, selection, training. 1910.133: eye/face protection. 1910.134: respiratory protection program — medical evaluation, fit testing, maintenance. 1910.135: head protection. 1910.136: foot protection. 1910.137: electrical protective equipment. 1910.138: hand protection. Aerospace-specific: chemical-resistant gloves for processing, respirators for composite dust/painting, hearing protection for rivet/machine shop.
summary: PPE requirements for aerospace manufacturing

label: Subpart D — Walking-Working Surfaces
content: 29 CFR 1910.21-30: walking-working surfaces requirements. 1910.28: duty to have fall protection (unprotected sides/edges ≥4 ft, hoist areas, holes, dockboards, runways). 1910.29: fall protection systems criteria (guardrails, safety net, personal fall arrest). 1910.23: ladders (design, use, inspection). 1910.25-26: stairways. 1910.27: scaffolds (capacity, platform, access). Aerospace relevance: aircraft maintenance stands, assembly platforms, hangar ladders.
summary: Fall protection, ladders, scaffolds, stairways

label: Subpart H — Hazardous Materials
content: 29 CFR 1910.101-126: hazardous materials. 1910.106: flammable liquids (storage, handling, use — cabinet/room/tank requirements). 1910.101: compressed gases (inspection, storage, handling). 1910.119: Process Safety Management (PSM) for highly hazardous chemicals — process hazard analysis, operating procedures, mechanical integrity, management of change, incident investigation. Aerospace: solvents, primers, fuel handling, composite resin systems, cryogenic fluids.
summary: Flammable liquids, compressed gases, process safety management

label: Subpart S — Electrical
content: 29 CFR 1910.301-399: electrical standards. 1910.303-308: design safety (wiring, overcurrent protection, grounding). 1910.331-335: safety-related work practices (qualified/unqualified persons, approach distances, lockout/tagout for electrical). 1910.399: definitions. NFPA 70E referenced for arc flash hazard analysis, PPE selection, energized electrical work permits. Aerospace: high-power test equipment, aircraft electrical systems maintenance, avionics test benches.
summary: Electrical design, safety-related work practices, maintenance

label: Subpart L — Fire Protection
content: 29 CFR 1910.155-165: fire protection. 1910.156: fire brigades (organizational statement, training, protective equipment). 1910.157: portable fire extinguishers (selection, placement, maintenance, training). 1910.158: standpipe/hose systems. 1910.159: automatic sprinkler systems (design, maintenance, testing). 1910.160: fixed extinguishing systems. 1910.164: fire detection/alarm systems. Aerospace: hangar fire protection (NFPA 409), flammable storage areas, composite layup rooms.
summary: Fire brigades, extinguishers, detection/alarm, sprinkler systems

label: NIST/CMMC
summary: Role: NIST/CMMC in Regulatory

label: NIST SP 800-171 Rev 2
