content: NIST SP 800-171 contains 110 security requirements in 14 families for protecting CUI: Access Control (AC), Awareness and Training (AT), Audit and Accountability (AU), Configuration Management (CM), Identification and Authentication (IA), Incident Response (IR), Maintenance (MA), Media Protection (MP), Personnel Security (PS), Physical Protection (PE), Risk Assessment (RA), Security Assessment (CA), System and Communications Protection (SC), System and Information Integrity (SI).
summary: CUI protection requirements for non-federal systems

label: Key Control Families
content: Access Control (AC): 22 requirements including least privilege, session controls, remote access, wireless restrictions. System & Communications Protection (SC): 16 requirements including boundary protection, CUI encryption in transit/at rest, FIPS-validated cryptography. Audit & Accountability (AU): 9 requirements including audit events, correlation, protection of audit info. Most common assessment gaps: multi-factor authentication (3.5.3), encrypted CUI (3.13.11), audit log review (3.3.1).
summary: Critical NIST 800-171 control families for aerospace

label: CMMC 2.0 Framework
content: CMMC 2.0 has three levels: Level 1 (Foundational, 17 practices, self-assessment for FCI), Level 2 (Advanced, 110 practices = NIST 800-171, third-party assessment for CUI), Level 3 (Expert, 110+ practices from NIST 800-172, government-led assessment for critical programs). Assessment types: self-assessment (Level 1), C3PAO assessment (Level 2), DIBCAC assessment (Level 3). POA&Ms allowed for limited scope/duration.
summary: Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification levels and assessment

label: NIST SP 800-172
content: NIST SP 800-172 adds enhanced security requirements beyond 800-171 for high-value assets and critical programs: penetration-resistant architecture, cyber resiliency, security operations center (SOC), threat hunting, advanced access control (attribute-based/risk-adaptive), supply chain risk management, system recovery within defined time/point objectives. Required for CMMC Level 3.
summary: Enhanced security requirements for CUI in critical programs

label: NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5
content: NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 provides a comprehensive catalog of security and privacy controls organized in 20 families: AC, AT, AU, CA, CM, CP, IA, IR, MA, MP, PE, PL, PM, PS, PT, RA, SA, SC, SI, SR. Controls selected based on system categorization (FIPS 199 low/moderate/high). Baselines provide starting point, tailored for specific mission needs. Overlays for specialized environments (e.g., DoD, IC). Supports FedRAMP, RMF, and FISMA compliance. Broader and more granular than 800-171.
summary: Security and privacy controls for federal information systems

label: FIPS 140-3
content: FIPS 140-3 (supersedes FIPS 140-2) establishes requirements for cryptographic modules used in federal systems. Four security levels: Level 1 (basic — approved algorithms, no physical security), Level 2 (tamper evidence, role-based authentication), Level 3 (tamper resistance, identity-based authentication, physical/logical separation), Level 4 (complete envelope of protection, environmental failure protection). Validated through CMVP (NIST/CCCS). Required for CUI encryption per DFARS 252.204-7012/NIST 800-171.
summary: Cryptographic module validation requirements

label: DO-178C/DO-254/DO-160G
summary: Role: DO-178C/DO-254/DO-160G in Regulatory

label: DO-178C — Software Considerations
content: DO-178C defines software lifecycle processes for airborne systems. Design Assurance Levels (DAL): A (Catastrophic), B (Hazardous), C (Major), D (Minor), E (No Effect). Key processes: planning, development (requirements, design, coding, integration), verification (reviews, analyses, testing), configuration management, quality assurance. Structural coverage criteria vary by DAL: MC/DC (Level A), decision coverage (Level B), statement coverage (Level C). Supplements: DO-330 (tool qualification), DO-331 (model-based), DO-332 (OO), DO-333 (formal methods).
summary: Airborne software development assurance levels A-E

label: DAL A Requirements
content: Level A (Catastrophic) requires: 100% MC/DC structural coverage, independence of verification from development, independence of QA, complete requirements-based testing, robustness testing (normal + abnormal + boundary), data coupling and control coupling analysis, tool qualification at TQL-1 for verification tools. Traceability: high-level requirements ↔ low-level requirements ↔ source code ↔ test cases ↔ test results.
summary: Level A software verification requirements (catastrophic failure condition)

label: DO-254 — Hardware Considerations
content: DO-254 defines hardware lifecycle processes for airborne electronic hardware (AEH). Covers: simple/complex hardware classification, planning, requirements capture, conceptual design, detailed design, implementation, verification, production transition, configuration management. Complex hardware (FPGAs, ASICs, PLDs) requires additional verification rigor. Advanced verification methods: formal verification, elemental analysis, simulation at gate/RTL level.
summary: Airborne hardware development assurance levels

label: DO-160G — Environmental Conditions
content: DO-160G defines environmental test conditions and procedures for airborne equipment across 26 sections: temperature/altitude (Sec 4), temperature variation (Sec 5), humidity (Sec 6), shock/crash safety (Sec 7), vibration (Sec 8), explosion proofness (Sec 9), waterproofness (Sec 10), fluids susceptibility (Sec 11), sand/dust (Sec 12), fungus (Sec 13), salt spray (Sec 14), magnetic effect (Sec 15), power input (Sec 16), voltage spike (Sec 17), audio frequency conducted susceptibility (Sec 18), induced signal susceptibility (Sec 19), RF susceptibility (Sec 20), emission of RF energy (Sec 21), lightning (Sec 22-23), icing (Sec 24), ESD (Sec 25), fire/flammability (Sec 26).
summary: Environmental test standard for airborne equipment

label: DO-330 — Software Tool Qualification
content: DO-330 defines criteria for qualifying software tools used in DO-178C/DO-254 processes. Five Tool Qualification Levels (TQL-1 to TQL-5) based on tool output impact and DAL. TQL-1 (most rigorous): tools whose output is part of airborne software and could insert errors not detected by other activities. TQL-5 (least rigorous): tools that cannot introduce errors. Qualification planning, tool operational requirements, tool verification, tool configuration management. Applies to compilers, linkers, test tools, code generators, model checkers.
summary: Tool qualification considerations for airborne software tools

label: DO-326A/DO-356A — Airworthiness Security
content: DO-326A (Airworthiness Security Process Specification) and DO-356A (Airworthiness Security Methods and Considerations) address cybersecurity for aircraft systems certification. Security risk assessment: threat identification, vulnerability analysis, risk determination, security requirements derivation. Security development lifecycle integrated with DO-178C/DO-254. Addresses: unauthorized access, data integrity, denial of service, system availability. Required by FAA for new type certificates and major modifications affecting security.
summary: Airworthiness security process and methods

label: ARP4754A — Civil Aircraft/System Development
content: ARP4754A provides guidelines for civil aircraft and system development processes. Key activities: functional hazard assessment (FHA), preliminary system safety assessment (PSSA), system safety assessment (SSA), common cause analysis (CCA — zonal, particular risks, common mode). Development assurance levels (DAL A-E) assigned based on FHA. Covers: requirements validation, implementation verification, configuration management, process assurance. Interfaces with DO-178C (software) and DO-254 (hardware) for item-level development.
summary: Guidelines for development of civil aircraft and systems

label: ARP4761 — Safety Assessment Process
content: ARP4761 describes safety assessment processes and methods: Functional Hazard Assessment (FHA — identify hazards, classify severity), Preliminary System Safety Assessment (PSSA — derive safety requirements, architectural strategies), System Safety Assessment (SSA — verify safety requirements met). Analysis methods: Fault Tree Analysis (FTA), Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA), Dependence Diagram (DD/RBD), Markov Analysis (MA), Common Cause Analysis (CCA — zonal safety, particular risks, common mode). Quantitative targets: catastrophic <1E-9/flight hour, hazardous <1E-7/flight hour.
summary: FHA, PSSA, SSA, CCA safety assessment methodologies

label: ASTM Standards
summary: Role: ASTM Standards in Regulatory

label: ASTM E8/E8M — Tension Testing of Metals
content: ASTM E8/E8M defines tension test methods for metallic materials at room temperature. Specimen types: rectangular (sheet/plate/flat), round (bar/rod/wire). Key parameters: gauge length, crosshead speed (strain rate), extensometer class. Outputs: yield strength (0.2% offset), ultimate tensile strength, elongation, reduction of area. Testing machine requirements per ASTM E4. Extensometers per ASTM E83 Class B-1 minimum.
summary: Standard test methods for mechanical testing

label: ASTM E18 — Rockwell Hardness Testing
content: ASTM E18 covers Rockwell hardness testing: scales (HRA, HRB, HRC, etc.), test blocks, machine verification. ASTM E10 covers Brinell hardness. ASTM E384 covers microindentation (Knoop/Vickers). Conversion tables per ASTM E140. Testing per customer specification — common aerospace: HRC for steels, HRB for aluminum alloys, Brinell for castings/forgings.
summary: Hardness testing methods for metals

label: ASTM E1444 — Magnetic Particle Testing
content: ASTM E1444/E1444M standard practice for magnetic particle testing (MT): continuous method, residual method, wet/dry particles, fluorescent/non-fluorescent. Magnetization: circular/longitudinal, AC/DC/HWDC, required field strength (30-60 A/cm or L/D ratio method). Sensitivity verification: QQI (quantitative quality indicator), Ketos ring, test ring. Demagnetization verification: residual field ≤3 Gauss. Personnel certification per NAS 410/SNT-TC-1A.
summary: NDT method for ferromagnetic material inspection

label: ASTM E165/E165M — Liquid Penetrant Testing
content: ASTM E165/E165M defines standard practice for liquid penetrant testing (PT): Type I fluorescent / Type II visible, Method A water washable / Method B post-emulsifiable lipophilic / Method C solvent removable / Method D post-emulsifiable hydrophilic. Covers surface preparation, penetrant application, dwell time (5-60 min based on material/defect type), excess penetrant removal, developer application, inspection, post-cleaning. Reference standard: TAM panel, nickel-chrome comparator block.
summary: Standard practice for liquid penetrant examination

label: ASTM B117 — Salt Spray (Fog) Testing
content: ASTM B117 establishes procedures for salt spray (fog) testing to evaluate relative corrosion resistance of metallic materials with or without coatings. Test conditions: 5% NaCl solution, 35°C chamber temperature, continuous fog exposure. Specimen preparation, positioning (15-30° from vertical), duration (per specification — typically 24-2000+ hours). Evaluation per ASTM D1654 (scribe failure) or ASTM B537 (rating of coated specimens). Not predictive of real-world performance — comparative test only.
summary: Standard practice for salt spray corrosion testing

label: ASTM A967/A380 — Passivation of Stainless Steel
content: ASTM A967 covers passivation treatments for stainless steel parts: nitric acid (20-50% concentration, 20-60 min, 70-140°F), citric acid (4-10% concentration, various temperatures), electropolishing. Pre-cleaning per ASTM A380 (degreasing, descaling, removal of free iron). Acceptance testing: copper sulfate test (ASTM A967 Practice E), high humidity test (Practice B), salt spray test (Practice C), free iron test (ferroxyl — ASTM A380). Critical for aerospace components requiring corrosion resistance.
summary: Passivation and cleaning of stainless steel parts

label: ASTM E466 — Axial Force Controlled Fatigue
content: ASTM E466 covers constant-amplitude axial fatigue testing of metallic materials: specimen design (uniform gauge, hourglass, button-head), loading parameters (maximum force, minimum force, stress ratio R, frequency), alignment requirements (<5% bending), test termination criteria (failure or runout at defined cycle count). S-N curve generation for design allowables. Related standards: E606 (strain-controlled), E647 (fatigue crack growth rate da/dN). Critical for aerospace structural materials characterization and design allowable development.
summary: Standard practice for conducting force-controlled fatigue tests

label: ASME Y14.5 — GD&T
content: Role scope: Provides ASME Y14.5-2018 GD&T expertise for datum reference frame design, geometric tolerance specification, material condition modifier selection, tolerance stack-up analysis, and drawing compliance review. Interfaces with mechanical engineering (design intent), manufacturing engineering (process feasibility), and quality (inspection planning and CMM programming). Does NOT set manufacturing process parameters or approve production releases — those are manufacturing engineering and quality manager responsibilities respectively.
summary: Role: ASME Y14.5 — GD&T in Regulatory

label: Geometric Dimensioning and Tolerancing
content: ASME Y14.5-2018 defines GD&T symbols and rules for engineering drawings. Geometric characteristics: form (flatness, straightness, circularity, cylindricity), orientation (perpendicularity, angularity, parallelism), location (position, concentricity, symmetry), runout (circular, total), profile (surface, line). Datum reference frames: primary/secondary/tertiary datums, datum features, datum targets. Material condition modifiers: MMC (Ⓜ), LMC (Ⓛ), RFS (default). Rule #1: envelope principle for size features.
summary: GD&T per ASME Y14.5-2018 standard

label: Positional Tolerance
content: Position tolerance defines a zone within which the center/axis/center plane of a feature is permitted to vary from true position. Cylindrical tolerance zone for holes (diameter in feature control frame). Bonus tolerance with MMC: actual mating envelope departure from MMC adds to stated tolerance. Composite position: upper segment = pattern location, lower segment = feature-to-feature. Fixed/floating fastener formulas: T = H - F (fixed), 2T = H - F (floating).
summary: True position tolerancing and bonus tolerance calculations

label: Profile Tolerance
content: Profile tolerance establishes a uniform boundary along true profile. Bilateral equal (default), bilateral unequal, unilateral. Applied to: complex contours, irregular shapes, surfaces requiring form + orientation + location control. Composite profile: upper = profile location, lower = profile form. All-around symbol (circle at leader junction) applies profile to all surfaces in the view. All-over applies to entire part surface.
summary: Profile of surface and line tolerancing applications

label: Datum Reference Frames
content: Datum reference frame (DRF) establishes coordinate system for measurements. Primary datum constrains 3 DOF (rotation), secondary constrains 2 DOF (rotation + translation), tertiary constrains 1 DOF (translation). Datum features: actual part surfaces used to establish datums. Datum targets: specified points/lines/areas on datum features. Simultaneous requirements: features referencing same DRF treated as single pattern unless noted SEPARATELY. Customized datum reference frames using movable/fixed datum targets for complex geometry.
summary: Establishment, degrees of freedom, simultaneous requirements

label: Form Tolerances
content: Form tolerances control the shape of individual features independent of datums. Flatness: surface must lie between two parallel planes (applied to planar surfaces, sealing surfaces). Straightness: line element or derived median line must lie within a tolerance zone (applied to cylindrical features, edges). Circularity: cross-sectional elements must lie between two concentric circles (applied to spheres, cylinders, cones). Cylindricity: entire surface must lie between two coaxial cylinders (combines circularity, straightness, and taper control). No datum reference needed — form tolerances are self-referencing.
summary: Flatness, straightness, circularity, cylindricity — when and how to apply

label: Runout Tolerances
content: Runout tolerances control relationship of features to a datum axis. Circular runout: each circular element individually must not exceed tolerance when rotated 360° about datum axis (applies to surfaces perpendicular or at angle to axis). Total runout: entire surface simultaneously must not exceed tolerance during full rotation with indicator traversing along/across surface (controls circularity + straightness + taper + coaxiality). Applications: bearing journals, seal surfaces, gear bores, turbine shafts. Always requires datum axis reference.
summary: Circular vs total runout, application to rotating parts

label: Y14.100 — Engineering Drawing Practices
content: ASME Y14.100 establishes requirements for engineering drawing practices: drawing types (detail, assembly, installation, schematic), sheet sizes/format per Y14.1, title block content, revision history block, general notes (tolerances, material specs, finish requirements), parts lists/BOM, zone system for locating features. Revision control: letter-based (A, B, C...), revision status tracking, revision history documentation. Supersedes cancelled portions of MIL-STD-100.
summary: Drawing types, revision control, notes and formats

label: Y14.41 — Digital Product Definition / MBD
content: ASME Y14.41 establishes requirements for digital product definition using 3D model-based definition (MBD). Model must contain complete product definition: geometric shape, dimensions, tolerances (GD&T per Y14.5), annotations, supplemental geometry, material/process notes. Display requirements: saved views, annotation planes, visibility controls. Data quality: geometric accuracy, model validation, archival format (STEP AP242, QIF). Enables drawing-less manufacturing when fully implemented. Supports digital thread/digital twin concepts.
summary: Model-based definition for 3D product data

label: NAS 410 — NDT Personnel Qualification
summary: Role: NAS 410 — NDT Personnel Qualification in Regulatory

label: NDT Personnel Certification
content: NAS 410 (harmonized with EN 4179) establishes qualification and certification requirements for NDT personnel in aerospace. Three levels: Level 1 (perform tests under Level 2/3 direction), Level 2 (set up/calibrate equipment, interpret/evaluate results, prepare procedures, train Level 1), Level 3 (develop procedures/techniques, interpret codes/standards, technical authority). Methods: MT, PT, UT, RT, ET, VT, and emerging methods.
summary: NAS 410 / EN 4179 NDT operator qualification requirements

label: Training and Examination Requirements
content: Minimum training hours vary by method and level. Example (UT): Level 1 = 40 hrs classroom + 210 hrs OJT, Level 2 = 40 hrs classroom + 630 hrs OJT. General/specific/practical examinations required. Written exams: 40 questions minimum, 80% passing score. Practical exams: demonstrate proficiency on actual parts. Re-examination allowed after additional training. Annual visual acuity exam (Jaeger J1 at 12 inches, color perception). Certification valid for 5 years, renewal by exam or continued satisfactory performance.
summary: Hours and exam requirements by NDT method and level

label: Method-Specific Requirements
content: Method-specific requirements beyond general NAS 410: Radiographic Testing (RT) — radiation safety officer designation, dosimetry, source handling, darkroom/digital processing, film/image interpretation skills. Eddy Current Testing (ET) — impedance plane understanding, frequency selection, probe types, reference standard fabrication. Visual Testing (VT) — aided/unaided examination, lighting requirements (min 100 fc), magnification tools, surface condition assessment, acceptance criteria interpretation. Each method requires separate qualification and certification.
summary: RT, ET, VT specifics — equipment, technique, interpretation

label: Written Practice Requirements
content: Each employer must maintain a written practice defining their NDT certification program: scope (methods/techniques covered), responsibility assignments (Level 3 technical authority, management approval), education/training/experience requirements (minimum per NAS 410 + employer additions), examination content and administration, certification/recertification procedures, documentation/record requirements, interruption-in-service provisions (>180 days requires re-demonstration). Must be reviewed/approved by Level 3 and authorized management.
summary: Employer's written practice — scope, content, approval authority

label: Recertification
content: Recertification options: (1) Re-examination (written general + specific + practical) at 5-year intervals, or (2) performance-based (documented evidence of continued satisfactory performance + current visual acuity). Lapse provisions: certification lapses if recertification not completed by expiration date. If lapsed <180 days, may recertify by examination only. If lapsed >180 days, must complete additional training + re-examination. Employer may impose more stringent requirements. Records retained for duration of certification + one certification period.
summary: Performance-based vs exam renewal, lapse procedures

label: Cash flow management and working capital optimization
content: CFO responsibility for managing cash conversion cycles, government payment mechanisms, and liquidity across a mixed contract portfolio. Key mechanisms: (1) Progress payments per FAR 32.5 / FAR 52.232-12 — up to 80% of allowable costs (85% for small business) billed as costs are incurred; liquidation rate applied at delivery; CFO monitors unliquidated progress payment balances and liquidation schedules to avoid government demand for return. (2) Performance-based payments per FAR 32.10 / FAR 52.232-32 — event-triggered payments up to 90% of contract price; CFO works with Contracts and PM to structure payment events that front-load cash recovery while satisfying DFARS performance criteria. (3) CPFF cost-reimbursement billing — monthly WAWF invoicing; CFO tracks billed-vs-incurred positions per contract; provisional rate variances affect billing authority and must be monitored to prevent over-billing exposure. (4) FFP milestone billing — negotiate billing milestones tied to deliverable events rather than government acceptance where contract language permits; reduces working capital drag on long-duration fixed-price programs. (5) WIP inventory and working capital — high-mix LRIP programs carry significant WIP financed internally; CFO monitors inventory turns (target ≥4× annually) and days-in-WIP vs. funded contract value; coordinate with VP Operations on material pacing to match funding. (6) Rolling 13-week cash flow forecast — updated weekly; identify valleys caused by irregular LRIP delivery cadence; maintain revolver draw authority to bridge operational gaps. (7) Line of credit management — revolving facility sized to peak working capital plus 20% buffer; daily availability monitoring and covenant compliance; interest expense allowability per FAR 31.205-20 (generally unallowable on government billings).
summary: Task: CFO cash flow management and working capital optimization — progress payments (FAR 32.5/52.232-12), performance-based payments (FAR 32.10), WIP inventory financing, FFP milestone billing design, and 13-week rolling cash forecast for defense contract portfolio

label: Internal controls and Sarbanes-Oxley compliance
content: CFO responsibility for establishing and maintaining an internal control framework over financial reporting (ICFR) and compliance operations. COSO 2013 framework — five integrated components: (1) Control Environment — ethics code, board oversight, tone from the top on compliance culture; (2) Risk Assessment — financial reporting risk identification including fraud risk assessment; (3) Control Activities — authorization matrices, three-way match (PO/receipt/invoice), journal entry review, account reconciliation, and system access controls in ERP; (4) Information and Communication — reporting integrity and disclosure controls; (5) Monitoring — internal audit function, management self-assessment, and deficiency remediation tracking. SOX compliance for public companies: Section 302 — CEO and CFO certify each quarterly and annual filing for accuracy, completeness, and disclosure control effectiveness; knowing false certification carries criminal liability. Section 404(a) — annual management assessment of ICFR effectiveness; Section 404(b) — external auditor attestation for accelerated filers. Deficiency classification: Control Deficiency (gap in design or operation), Significant Deficiency (warrants attention below material weakness), Material Weakness (reasonable possibility of material misstatement — triggers SEC disclosure obligation and restatement risk). Private company application: PE sponsors, lenders, and bonding companies may require COSO-compliant ICFR as a covenant condition; adopt voluntarily per board direction. Segregation of duties (SoD) critical controls: purchasing authority ≠ invoice approval ≠ payment release; payroll setup ≠ payroll processing ≠ payroll review; journal entries require maker-checker with CFO approval above defined threshold. Defense-specific controls: timekeeping system integrity (DCAA labor charging accuracy), unallowable cost segregation (FAR 31.205), and subcontract consent per FAR 44.2 are ICFR-adjacent controls assessed by DCAA as business system adequacy under DFARS 252.242-7006.
summary: Task: CFO internal controls and SOX compliance — COSO 2013 framework (five components), segregation of duties, SOX Section 302/404 certifications, material weakness classification and remediation, and DCAA accounting business system adequacy

label: Contract profitability analysis and EAC oversight
content: CFO responsibility for monitoring contract-level profitability, estimate-at-completion (EAC) accuracy, and revenue recognition integrity across the program portfolio. EAC oversight: maintain an EAC register by contract updated monthly; EAC = Actual Costs to Date + Estimate to Complete (ETC); EAC adjustments of ±10% on any contract or total dollar impact >$250K require documented CFO and PM review before revision; upward EAC adjustments on fixed-price contracts are direct margin erosion events requiring immediate CEO notification. Revenue recognition per ASC 606 (over-time method): government manufacturing contracts typically satisfy the 'customer controls the asset as it is created' criterion — use the cost-to-cost input method (costs incurred ÷ total estimated contract costs × transaction price); EAC changes propagate immediately into recognized revenue; an EAC increase reduces the percentage of completion and reverses previously recognized revenue in the current period. Loss contract identification: when total estimated contract costs exceed the transaction price, recognize the entire estimated loss immediately per ASC 606 and ASC 450; CFO maintains a loss contract reserve updated at each EAC review; DCAA-billable cost-type work continues regardless of the loss reserve. Profit/fee recognition timing: CPFF fixed fee earned pro-rata with costs; award fee recognized at the most likely amount assessed quarterly based on contract performance; incentive fee recognized at the constrained transaction price until the amount is probable of not reversing significantly. EAC review cadence: monthly for programs with CPI <0.95; quarterly for all others; annual baseline revalidation for all programs regardless of performance. Contract profitability dashboard: CFO segments gross margin by contract type (FFP, CPFF, T&M, IDIQ) and customer; tracks EAC variance waterfall (labor efficiency, material price, subcontract, overhead absorption) to direct corrective action to VP Operations and PMs.
summary: Task: CFO contract profitability analysis and EAC oversight — monthly EAC register by contract, ASC 606 cost-to-cost over-time revenue recognition, immediate loss contract reserve recognition (ASC 450), award/incentive fee constrained recognition, and gross margin dashboard by contract type

label: Treasury and debt management
content: CFO responsibility for capital structure management, credit facilities, bonding program, and debt covenant compliance in support of government contract performance obligations. Credit facility management: revolving credit facility sized to peak working capital requirement plus 20% liquidity buffer; monitor borrowing base certificate compliance for asset-based lending facilities; minimize interest expense while maintaining liquidity coverage ratio ≥1.5×. Bonding capacity for government contracts: performance bonds and payment bonds are required on federal construction contracts >$150K per the Miller Act (40 U.S.C. § 3131); non-construction defense manufacturing contracts may require bid bonds at the government's discretion; CFO manages aggregate bonded exposure across active programs, monitors per-project and aggregate capacity limits, and maintains the surety relationship. Surety relationship management: submit annual surety questionnaire with audited financial statements; communicate proactively on any material adverse changes (loss contract recognition, material weakness, significant litigation) affecting net worth and bonding capacity; surety credit lines typically 10–15× net worth for established contractors in good standing. Debt covenant compliance: common financial maintenance covenants include maximum total leverage ratio (Total Debt ÷ EBITDA ≤3.5×), minimum fixed charge coverage ratio (EBITDA ÷ Fixed Charges ≥1.25×), and minimum liquidity floor; CFO prepares quarterly compliance certificates for lender submission within 45 days of quarter-end; annual audited financials delivered within 120 days of fiscal year-end; covenant breach triggers cure period or acceleration risk — CFO notifies CEO and Legal immediately upon identification of potential breach; proactive waiver negotiations are strongly preferred over covenant breach disclosure. Interest expense allowability: FAR 31.205-20 makes commercial interest on borrowings generally unallowable on government contract billings; CFO segregates interest expense to unallowable GL accounts. Standby letters of credit: some contracts require LC as performance security; CFO tracks LC expiration dates and coordinates renewal with the issuing bank before contract-required dates.
summary: Task: CFO treasury and debt management — revolving credit facility, Miller Act bonding (performance/payment bonds >$150K construction), surety relationship management, quarterly debt covenant compliance certificates (leverage ≤3.5×, FCCR ≥1.25×), and FAR 31.205-20 interest expense unallowability

label: Tax planning and government contractor tax considerations
content: CFO responsibility for federal and state tax planning in the government contracting context, including research credits, capitalization rules, multi-state nexus, and ITAR-constrained international tax obligations. R&D tax credit (IRC Section 41): IRAD expenditures that qualify as Qualified Research Expenses (QREs) — direct wages, supplies, and 65% of contract research costs — generate a federal credit of up to 20% of excess QREs over a base amount (regular credit) or 14% of QREs exceeding 50% of the three-year average (simplified alternative credit); defense contractors can monetize substantial credits from IRAD; document technical uncertainty and the experimental process for each project to withstand IRS examination; R&D credits interact with the G&A pool when the same IRAD wages drive both the credit and the IR&D cost pool allocation. Section 174 capitalization (TCJA, effective tax year 2022): all R&D expenditures that were previously immediately deductible must now be capitalized and amortized over 5 years (domestic) or 15 years (foreign-sourced); materially increases current tax liability and deferred tax asset balances for IRAD-intensive contractors; CFO coordinates with tax counsel on deferred tax asset recognition, AMT interaction, and cash tax modeling; Section 174 capitalization is a tax treatment and does not change FAR 31.205-18 IR&D cost accounting for government billing purposes. State tax nexus for multi-site manufacturing: physical presence (facility, payroll) creates state income and franchise tax nexus; economic nexus rules in many states apply even without physical presence; apportionment of taxable income across states using sales, payroll, and property factors; ITAR compliance constrains manufacturing location decisions, limiting the ability to optimize state tax outcomes. ITAR-related transfer pricing: intercompany transactions involving ITAR-controlled hardware, data, or services between related domestic and foreign entities must satisfy both the arm's-length standard (IRC Section 482) and ITAR licensing requirements; transfer of controlled technical data to a foreign affiliate requires an ITAR license regardless of transfer pricing; CFO coordinates with Export Compliance Officer to ensure transfer pricing documentation and license compliance are aligned.
summary: Task: CFO tax planning and government contractor considerations — IRC Section 41 R&D credit (up to 20%) on IRAD QREs, Section 174 mandatory R&D capitalization/amortization (TCJA 2022), multi-state nexus for manufacturing facilities, and ITAR-constrained intercompany transfer pricing under IRC Section 482

label: Mergers acquisitions and divestitures financial due diligence
content: CFO responsibility for financial due diligence, valuation, and transaction structuring on defense M&A — acquisitions of sub-tier suppliers or adjacent capability businesses, and divestitures of non-core programs or product lines. Valuation of defense businesses: (1) DCF analysis using contract-specific cash flow projections probability-weighted for option year exercise rates and re-compete win probability; WACC adjusted for security clearance facility, ITAR compliance, and customer concentration risk premiums. (2) Comparable company analysis: traded defense sub-tier and Tier 2 multiples (EV/EBITDA typically 6–10× for cleared sub-tier manufacturers with stable backlogs); adjust for contract type risk mix (FFP vs. CPFF), program stage (LRIP vs. FRP), and backlog concentration. (3) Comparable transaction analysis: use publicly available defense M&A transaction data and FPDS-NG award history as size/revenue signal proxies. Backlog analysis: distinguish funded backlog (definitized with appropriated, obligated funds), unfunded backlog (options not yet exercised but under contract), and pipeline-estimated backlog; assess re-compete exposure within 18 months, key personnel clause risk on program continuity, and top-3 customer concentration as % of total backlog value; verify that assumed option exercise probability is consistent with CPARS history and customer relationship strength. DCAA compliance gap assessment: review most recent DCAA incurred cost audit status — open findings, questioned costs, and compliance status of all six DFARS 252.242-7005 business systems (accounting, estimating, purchasing, property management, MMAS, EVMS); open material weakness findings or inadequate business system ratings represent post-close remediation cost liability to be reflected in purchase price or escrow. Novation agreements per FAR 42.12: when a contractor's assets or business transfers to a new legal entity, the government's consent is required via a three-party novation agreement between transferor, transferee, and government (FAR 42.1204); government has no obligation to novate and may terminate for convenience if novation is denied; plan novation timeline (typically 60–120 days post-close) into acquisition structure; small business set-aside contract assignability requires SBA re-certification for the acquirer. Representations and warranties insurance: CFO assesses R&W insurance coverage limits against estimated DCAA liability exposure from pre-close undetected cost accounting noncompliance.
summary: Task: CFO M&A and divestitures financial due diligence — DCF and EBITDA multiples valuation (6–10× for cleared sub-tier), funded/unfunded backlog analysis, DCAA six business system compliance gap assessment, and FAR 42.12 novation agreement timeline and risk

label: Investor relations and financial reporting
content: CFO responsibility for external financial communication with equity investors, debt holders, and government customers on financial performance, backlog, and strategic outlook. SEC reporting (public companies): Form 10-K — audited financial statements, MD&A with contract backlog disclosures (funded, unfunded, total contract value), risk factors covering ITAR compliance risk, DCAA audit exposure, and customer concentration; ICFR management assessment; Form 10-Q — unaudited quarterly financials with period EAC-driven contract profitability updates; Form 8-K — material event reporting (contract award or termination exceeding materiality threshold, material weakness identification, auditor change); ITAR and classified program limitations constrain public disclosure — classified program revenues disclosed only at aggregate level. Bank covenant reporting: quarterly compliance certificates to lenders within 45 days of quarter-end demonstrating adherence to financial maintenance covenants; audited annual financials delivered within 120 days of fiscal year-end; material adverse change notifications triggered by program loss reserve recognition, DCAA findings, or customer Cure Notices; CFO maintains direct relationship with lender credit officer to communicate pipeline changes affecting future compliance projections proactively before breach. Government contract backlog disclosures: defense contractors conventionally disclose funded backlog (awarded, appropriated, obligated), unfunded backlog (options and unexercised IDIQ task orders), and total estimated contract value as non-GAAP metrics; book-to-bill ratio reported as a leading revenue growth indicator; CFO coordinates with VP BD on segment definitions and option exercise probability assumptions; non-GAAP backlog metrics require reconciliation to recognized revenue if public. Quarterly earnings guidance: CFO issues revenue and operating margin guidance reflecting contract award timing uncertainty (IDIQ task order variability), EAC adjustment risk, and defense budget continuing resolution exposure; avoid disclosing individual classified program revenue; private company equivalent — monthly management accounts and quarterly board reporting packages per investor or PE sponsor agreement.
summary: Task: CFO investor relations and financial reporting — SEC 10-K/10-Q/8-K with ITAR-constrained classified program disclosures, quarterly bank covenant compliance certificates, funded/unfunded backlog non-GAAP disclosures, and earnings guidance reflecting CR and EAC risk

label: Digital engineering and model-based systems engineering
content: CTO responsibility for implementing the DoD Digital Engineering Strategy (2018) across the enterprise. Key obligations: (1) Authoritative Source of Truth (ASoT) — establish and maintain a single model-based repository serving as the authoritative product definition, replacing drawing-centric baselines; enforce MIL-HDBK-31032 digital engineering guidance across all programs. (2) MBSE tool deployment — standardize on an enterprise MBSE toolchain (e.g., Capella, Cameo Systems Modeler/MagicDraw, or Rhapsody); maintain SysML model libraries, interface definitions, and ontology standards; govern tool licensing, access control, and ITAR data handling within the MBSE environment. (3) Digital thread governance — ensure bidirectional traceability from requirements through design, manufacturing, test, and sustainment using PLM-integrated digital thread; define mandatory thread artifacts at each KDP milestone; audit thread continuity at CDR and PRR. (4) Model-Based Definition (MBD) — mandate 3D annotated model deliverables (per ASME Y14.41 / MIL-STD-31000A) in lieu of 2D drawings on new program starts; manage customer and supplier MBD acceptance. (5) Customer deliverable alignment — ensure digital engineering artifacts satisfy CDRL requirements for prime contractors and government program offices mandating digital thread delivery. (6) Workforce enablement — own MBSE training roadmap; target INCOSE CSEP certification for senior systems engineers; track tool adoption rates as a Learning/Growth KPI.
summary: Task: CTO implementation of DoD Digital Engineering Strategy — ASoT establishment, MBSE toolchain governance (Capella/Cameo/SysML), digital thread continuity across product lifecycle, MBD standards, and customer digital deliverable compliance

label: Manufacturing technology and process innovation
content: CTO responsibility for advancing manufacturing readiness and process capability in support of program transitions from development to LRIP and FRP. Key areas: (1) Manufacturing Readiness Level (MRL) assessments — conduct MRL assessments per the DoD MRL Deskbook (MRL 1–10 scale) at each KDP milestone; MRL ≥6 required at CDR equivalent (KDP-C), MRL ≥8 at PRR equivalent (KDP-D); identify and mitigate manufacturing critical items (MCIs) and key characteristics (KCs). (2) Additive manufacturing (AM) qualification — manage AM qualification roadmap against AMS7000-series standards (AMS7001 through AMS7004 for metal PBF/DED), USAF AM qualification requirements, and customer-specific design allowables; govern AM-produced flight hardware TDP markings and configuration control. (3) Automation and robotics integration — evaluate and pilot automation for high-labor, repetitive, or ergonomically hazardous operations; calculate ROI with CIC business case before capital commitment; govern integration with existing MES and quality systems. (4) Industry 4.0 adoption — assess and selectively deploy IIoT sensors, real-time process monitoring, machine learning for predictive maintenance (reducing unplanned equipment downtime), and digital twin for manufacturing process simulation; prioritize investments by constraint-relief impact. (5) Special process technology — advance NADCAP-scoped process capabilities; govern process parameter qualification data; interface with NADCAP auditor on merit-based recognition initiatives. (6) Technology transition to production — own the TRL-to-MRL bridge; ensure IRAD technology exits do not enter production before MRL ≥6; coordinate with VP Operations on production insertion timelines.
summary: Task: CTO manufacturing technology advancement — MRL assessments per DoD MRL Deskbook, additive manufacturing qualification (AMS7000-series), automation/robotics integration, Industry 4.0 adoption, and TRL-to-MRL transition governance

label: Cybersecurity technology governance
content: CTO responsibility for the enterprise cybersecurity technology architecture supporting CMMC 2.0 compliance and CUI protection obligations. Key areas: (1) NIST SP 800-171 technology implementation — own the technical implementation of all 110 NIST SP 800-171 Rev 2 security practices across IT/OT systems; maintain implementation evidence for each practice in coordination with the ISSO; track POAM remediation progress and report to CEO per CMMC governance framework. (2) CUI protection architecture — design and operate a CUI enclave (network segmentation, access control, endpoint protection) that limits CUI exposure to cleared personnel on authorized systems; integrate with PLM and ERP access controls; ensure no CUI in commercial cloud without FedRAMP High authorization. (3) STIG implementation — apply DISA Security Technical Implementation Guides (STIGs) to all applicable platforms (Windows, Linux, network devices, software applications); automate STIG compliance checks via SCAP-validated tools; track open STIG findings as POAM items. (4) Secure development environments — establish a secure software development environment for all internally developed firmware and software; implement static analysis (SAST), dynamic analysis (DAST), and software composition analysis (SCA) in the development pipeline; enforce secure coding standards (CERT C/C++, MISRA for safety-critical firmware). (5) OT/ICS security — extend cybersecurity governance to manufacturing operational technology (CNC controllers, PLCs, SCADA systems) using NIST SP 800-82 guidance; segment OT from enterprise IT; assess ICS-specific vulnerabilities via ISA/IEC 62443 framework. (6) Zero-trust architecture — align enterprise network design to DoD Zero Trust Strategy (2022) pillars: identity, device, network, application, data; prioritize zero-trust investments in the CIC business case cycle.
summary: Task: CTO cybersecurity technology governance — NIST SP 800-171 technical implementation, CUI protection architecture, DISA STIG compliance, secure development environment, OT/ICS security per NIST 800-82, and zero-trust architecture alignment

label: Intellectual property and technical data rights strategy
content: CTO responsibility for protecting and managing the company's intellectual property and technical data rights across all government contracts. Key areas: (1) DFARS data rights framework — govern the assertion and negotiation of data rights per DFARS 252.227-7013 (Rights in Technical Data — Noncommercial Items) and DFARS 252.227-7014 (Rights in Noncommercial Computer Software and Documentation); distinguish Limited Rights (government may not disclose outside government), Government Purpose Rights (GPR — government may use for any government purpose but not for competitive procurement for defined period), and Unlimited Rights (government may use for any purpose). (2) Background vs. foreground IP — maintain a background IP register of all technology developed exclusively at private expense (no government funding); apply proprietary markings per DFARS 252.227-7013(f) and FAR 52.227-14 before disclosing to government; identify foreground IP (developed with government funding) and assert appropriate rights at each contract. (3) Technical Data Package (TDP) strategy — determine TDP deliverable level (Type I through Type V per MIL-STD-31000A) at contract negotiation; resist government demands for unlimited rights to privately funded technology; identify TDP elements subject to Limited Rights assertions and document in contract. (4) SBIR data rights protection — for SBIR Phase I/II funded technology, assert SBIR data rights protection period (up to 20 years per 15 U.S.C. § 638(j) and 10 U.S.C. § 4902(c)) during which government may not disclose SBIR data outside government or use for competitive procurement; track protection period expiry per SBIR award. (5) IP due diligence — conduct IP audit before responding to any government data call or source selection requiring TDP delivery; coordinate with Legal on patent filing strategy for novel manufacturing processes and proprietary designs. (6) Licensing and commercialization — identify IP with dual-use commercial potential; manage licensing agreements; coordinate with VP BD on IP-based competitive discriminators in proposals.
summary: Task: CTO IP and technical data rights strategy — DFARS 252.227-7013/7014 data rights assertions, background vs. foreground IP register, TDP deliverable level negotiation, SBIR data rights protection (up to 20 years), and IP due diligence before government data calls

label: IRAD program management
content: CTO responsibility for governing the Internal Research and Development (IRAD) portfolio per FAR 31.205-18 allowability requirements and enterprise strategic priorities. Key areas: (1) Portfolio governance — maintain a live IRAD project register with project ID, technical objective, TRL at initiation, TRL target at project close, accountable technical lead, annual funding allocation, and cumulative spend; present portfolio to CEO/SMC quarterly and to VP BD for capture-pipeline alignment assessment. (2) FAR 31.205-18 compliance — ensure all IRAD projects constitute legitimate independent technical effort (not sponsored by a specific contract); maintain project-level technical descriptions and cost accumulations with responsible engineer sign-off; for contractors with >$100M covered contracts, report IRAD to DoD USD(R&E) per 10 U.S.C. § 3762 and demonstrate technical benefit to national defense; segregate IRAD from B&P cost tracking per FAR. (3) TRL advancement tracking — define TRL exit criteria for each project phase; conduct formal TRL assessments at each quarterly IRAD review; redirect or terminate projects failing to advance TRL on plan; target TRL ≥6 before transitioning IRAD results to proposal-ready status. (4) Technology transition planning — develop a transition plan for each IRAD project approaching TRL ≥6 identifying: target program or market application, customer qualification pathway (SBIR Phase II bridge, OTA prototype, program insertion), required additional investment to reach TRL ≥8, and lead time to contractual application. (5) Investment prioritization — score IRAD projects annually against Three Horizons classification, Ansoff growth alignment, and VP BD capture-pipeline linkage; recommend portfolio rebalancing to CEO; terminate projects with no capture pipeline linkage and no external validation signal within 24 months. (6) IRAD ROI — track IRAD-to-award conversion rates (contract awards where IRAD investment was cited as a win theme or technical discriminator); report annually to CEO and CFO.
summary: Task: CTO IRAD portfolio governance per FAR 31.205-18 — project register with TRL tracking, DoD USD(R&E) reporting for >$100M contractors, technology transition planning, capture-pipeline linkage, and annual IRAD ROI reporting

label: Software and firmware architecture governance
content: CTO responsibility for enterprise software and firmware development assurance standards and modern delivery practices. Key areas: (1) DO-178C compliance strategy — govern software development assurance per RTCA DO-178C for airborne software-based systems; establish Design Assurance Level (DAL) determination process at system level (DAL A through E based on failure condition severity per ARP4754A); maintain a software development plan, software verification plan, and configuration management plan per DO-178C objectives for each applicable program; coordinate with VP Engineering on tool qualification (DO-330) for any automated tools producing software outputs. (2) DO-254 compliance strategy — govern hardware design assurance per RTCA DO-254 for complex electronic hardware (FPGAs, ASICs, PLDs) at applicable DAL levels; maintain hardware design lifecycle and verification plans; manage hardware configuration items (HWCIs) under configuration control. (3) DevSecOps pipeline — implement a DoD DevSecOps Reference Design-aligned pipeline (based on DoD Enterprise DevSecOps Reference Design v2.0) integrating: source code management, CI/CD automation, container security scanning, SAST/DAST, artifact management, and deployment orchestration; adopt a software factory model (modeled on DoD Platform One / Kessel Run concepts) for shared pipeline infrastructure reuse across programs. (4) Continuous ATO — implement continuous Authority to Operate (cATO) principles per DoD CIO guidance; integrate automated compliance testing and real-time security monitoring into the DevSecOps pipeline to support ongoing ATO validity rather than point-in-time assessments; coordinate with ISSO on ATO artifact generation from pipeline outputs. (5) Software SBOM governance — maintain Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) per NTIA minimum elements for all delivered software products; implement automated SBOM generation in the CI/CD pipeline; assess open-source component license compliance and vulnerability status. (6) Firmware security — apply secure boot, code signing, and anti-tamper design principles to all firmware deliverables; coordinate with cybersecurity technology governance on STIGs applicable to embedded systems.
summary: Task: CTO software and firmware architecture governance — DO-178C/DO-254 DAL determination and compliance plans, DevSecOps pipeline per DoD reference design, continuous ATO strategy, SBOM governance, and firmware security controls

label: Test and evaluation technology strategy
content: CTO responsibility for enterprise T&E technology investments and digital test environment strategy supporting program developmental and operational testing. Key areas: (1) T&E infrastructure investment — maintain a T&E capability roadmap identifying internal lab, test range, and instrumentation investments required to support program DT&E milestones; submit T&E capital requirements to CIC with ROI justification tied to program revenue opportunity; assess external T&E facility partnerships (government ranges, national labs, commercial test houses) vs. internal investment using make-vs-buy framework. (2) Digital test environments — implement model-based test environments using digital twins of the system under test; use simulation-based verification to reduce physical test article count and compress test schedules; govern digital test environment fidelity standards — document validated correlation between simulation and physical test results before using digital test as test evidence. (3) Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) simulation — establish HIL simulation capability connecting physical hardware (flight computers, actuators, sensors) to simulated environment models; HIL testing is required for DO-178C software verification at higher DAL levels and for system integration testing before hardware availability; govern HIL testbed configuration management and version control. (4) Virtual and constructive testing — leverage virtual (fully simulated) and constructive (simulated entities in real environments) testing per DoD M&S standards (IEEE 1516 HLA, SISO standards) for system-of-systems integration and operational scenario testing; coordinate with government T&E agencies (TRMC, service T&E commands) on distributed simulation architectures using JMETC. (5) T&E data management — implement automated test data collection, tagging, and analysis pipelines; ensure T&E data is retained as evidence for airworthiness, qualification, and DCMA/government acceptance; apply data rights protections to proprietary test methodologies and calibration data. (6) Test efficiency metrics — track first-test-pass rate, test cycle time, and cost-per-test-point as operational efficiency KPIs; report trends at quarterly program reviews.
summary: Task: CTO T&E technology strategy — T&E infrastructure investment roadmap, digital test environment fidelity governance, HIL simulation for DO-178C verification, virtual/constructive testing per IEEE 1516 HLA, T&E data management, and test efficiency metrics

label: Technical workforce development
content: CTO responsibility for building and sustaining the technical workforce pipeline and institutional engineering competency across the enterprise. Key areas: (1) STEM pipeline — establish and maintain relationships with target universities, community colleges, and STEM programs (FIRST Robotics, Science Olympiad, co-op/internship programs) aligned to critical skill needs (systems engineering, embedded software, RF/EW, additive manufacturing); coordinate with HR on intern-to-hire conversion targets and university recruiting budgets; participate in university advisory boards to shape curriculum relevant to defense manufacturing needs. (2) Key personnel retention — identify and maintain a register of key technical personnel (cleared engineers with specialized skills, NADCAP process owners, senior systems engineers) with individual retention risk ratings; recommend retention investments (technical career ladder advancement, skill-premium pay, clearance incentives) to CEO and CHRO; track voluntary turnover for cleared and critical-skill technical staff as a CTO-owned KPI (target <5%). (3) Technical fellow and principal engineer programs — establish a technical career ladder (Engineer I through Distinguished/Technical Fellow) providing advancement to senior individual contributors without requiring management track; define Technical Fellow designation criteria: sustained technical excellence, enterprise-wide influence, external recognition, and mentorship contributions; Technical Fellows assigned to strategic IRAD and technology transition challenges. (4) Engineering competency framework — define and maintain a role-based engineering competency matrix (aligned to INCOSE systems engineering competency framework and relevant domain standards — DO-178C, MBSE, NADCAP process knowledge); use competency assessments in performance reviews and IDP development; identify enterprise-wide competency gaps for targeted investment. (5) Continuing education — fund and track professional development: INCOSE certification (ASEP/CSEP), professional engineering (PE) licensure support, relevant IEEE and AIAA technical society memberships, and conference participation; require CTO approval for external training budgets >$5K per person. (6) Knowledge capture — implement structured knowledge management for critical technical domains: document process recipes, tribal manufacturing knowledge, and proprietary engineering methods before key personnel retire or depart; integrate captured knowledge into QMS procedures and engineering handbooks.
summary: Task: CTO technical workforce development — STEM pipeline partnerships, key personnel retention register with cleared-staff turnover KPI, technical fellow/principal engineer career ladder, INCOSE-aligned engineering competency framework, continuing education governance, and structured knowledge capture

label: Technical review governance (MIL-STD-1521B)
content: VP Engineering oversight of formal technical review gates per MIL-STD-1521B. Review sequence: System Requirements Review (SRR) — validates requirements completeness and feasibility before design begins; Preliminary Design Review (PDR) — confirms preliminary design meets requirements at acceptable risk; Critical Design Review (CDR) — baselines completed design for fabrication release; Test Readiness Review (TRR) — confirms test readiness before qualification testing begins; Production Readiness Review (PRR) — confirms process maturity before LRIP commitment. Review board composition: VP Engineering chairs all internal reviews; CTO-designated independent technical reviewer provides separate risk assessment; customer representatives participate per CDRL obligation. Entrance and exit criteria: VP Engineering maintains a standard criteria checklist per review type aligned to MIL-STD-1521B Appendix requirements; programs may not exit a review without formal closure of all exit criteria or VP Engineering-approved waiver documenting residual risk acceptance. Action item tracking: all review action items logged in the engineering action tracker with owner, priority (Critical/Major/Minor), and target closure date; open Critical AIs block review exit; VP Engineering reviews open AI register weekly until all Critical and Major items are resolved.
summary: Task: Technical review governance — MIL-STD-1521B formal review gates (SRR/PDR/CDR/TRR/PRR), review board composition, entrance/exit criteria management, and action item tracking for VP Engineering

label: Engineering process and standards compliance
content: VP Engineering responsibility for maintaining design process compliance across four regulatory domains. AS9100D Section 8.3 design controls: documented design plans required for all programs covering design inputs, outputs, reviews, verification, validation, and change; VP Engineering ensures formal design review records exist and are retained in the QMS. ASME Y14.5-2018 GD&T: all engineering drawings shall conform to Y14.5-2018 dimensioning and tolerancing standards; VP Engineering approves drawing release; chief engineer certifies GD&T correctness before release to production. Configuration management per MIL-STD-973: maintain Configuration Identification (CI) baselines, Configuration Control Board (CCB) authority, Configuration Status Accounting (CSA) log, and Functional/Physical Configuration Audits (FCA/PCA) per contract requirements; functional baseline established at PDR, allocated baseline at CDR, product baseline at PRR. Drawing release procedure: design → peer review → senior engineer approval → VP Engineering signature authority → release to production via PLM; no informal or verbal design changes are authorized; all revisions processed through the ECO workflow.
summary: Task: Engineering process and standards compliance — AS9100D design controls, ASME Y14.5 GD&T, MIL-STD-973 configuration management, and drawing release procedures for VP Engineering

label: Systems engineering integration
content: VP Engineering ownership of systems engineering practices per NASA Systems Engineering Handbook SP-2016-6105. Requirements management: bidirectional Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) maintained from customer requirements through design to test; requirements changes managed through CCB with VP Engineering concurrence; RTM reviewed at each KDP gate and verified complete before CDR exit. Interface control: Interface Control Documents (ICDs) maintained for all inter-subsystem and external interfaces; ICD baseline reviewed at PDR and re-confirmed at CDR; interface changes require VP Engineering approval and customer notification per contract requirements. Verification and validation planning: VP Engineering approves the Verification and Validation Plan (VVP) defining the verification method (analysis, inspection, demonstration, or test) for each requirement; VVP baselined at CDR; deviations from VVP require VP Engineering waiver and customer acceptance per contract. Trade study governance: all major design trades (material selection, architecture, make-buy for designed components) conducted using structured methodology with documented criteria weighting per SE Handbook Chapter 6; VP Engineering approves trade study plans before execution and accepts results before design commitments are recorded in the technical baseline.
summary: Task: Systems engineering integration — requirements management (RTM), interface control (ICDs), V&V planning, and trade study governance per NASA SE Handbook SP-2016-6105 for VP Engineering

label: Engineering change management (ECN/ECO/CCB)
content: VP Engineering governance of engineering change processes across all programs. ECN (Engineering Change Notice): documents a proposed change for evaluation and impact assessment before CCB decision; ECN does not authorize production incorporation. ECO (Engineering Change Order): authorizes incorporation of a CCB-approved change into the drawing baseline, BOM, and production process; triggers drawing revision, PLM configuration update, and manufacturing change notification. Configuration Control Board (CCB): VP Engineering chairs the internal CCB; standing membership includes engineering, manufacturing, quality, and contracts; meets weekly plus ad hoc for urgent changes; customer participation required per contract for Class I changes. Change classification per MIL-STD-973: Class I — changes affecting form, fit, function, reliability, survivability, safety, electromagnetic characteristics, or contractual requirements; require government CCB approval before incorporation; Class II — all other changes; internal CCB authority. Impact assessment workflow: each proposed change assessed for schedule impact, cost impact (NRE and recurring unit cost), performance impact, and interface impact before CCB disposition; VP Engineering approves all Class I change packages before submission to the government CCB. Closure tracking: approved ECOs tracked to implementation closure in PLM; engineering change not considered complete until production verification at first affected unit or first article inspection of changed configuration.
summary: Task: Engineering change management — ECN/ECO processes, CCB governance (VP Engineering chair), Class I/II change classification per MIL-STD-973, impact assessment, and PLM closure tracking

label: Design for manufacturability and producibility
content: VP Engineering responsibility for integrating manufacturing engineering early in the design process to ensure producible, cost-effective designs. DFM/DFP integration: manufacturing engineers participate in IPTs from concept phase; VP Engineering mandates formal DFM reviews at PDR and CDR with manufacturing concurrence required before drawing release; manufacturing concurrence documented in drawing approval workflow. Producibility assessments: formal producibility assessment conducted at PDR evaluating critical manufacturing processes, special tooling requirements, critical and key characteristics, estimated process capability (Cpk ≥1.33 target for critical characteristics, ≥1.67 for safety-critical), and make-buy implications of design geometry choices; assessment results feed the program risk register. Design-to-cost targets: VP Engineering establishes unit recurring cost (URC) targets at contract award; design-to-cost reviews at each KDP gate compare actual design-driven cost estimate vs. target; designs exceeding URC targets by >10% require VP Engineering-approved cost reduction action plan before CDR exit. Critical and key characteristics: VP Engineering designates critical characteristics (CCs) and key characteristics (KCs) on engineering drawings per AS9100D Section 8.3 and customer requirements; CCs drive inspection plans, statistical process control requirements, and first article inspection scope; CC list maintained in the program configuration baseline.
summary: Task: Design for manufacturability and producibility — DFM/DFP integration in IPTs, producibility assessments at KDPs, design-to-cost target management, and critical/key characteristic designation for VP Engineering

label: Test and qualification program oversight
content: VP Engineering oversight of the verification and validation program including qualification testing, first article testing, and test laboratory governance. Qualification test planning: VP Engineering approves the Qualification Test Plan (QTP) defining test sequence, test levels, acceptance criteria, test configuration, and test article disposition before TRR; QTP baselined and customer-approved before qualification testing begins. First article inspection (FAI) per AS9102: FAI conducted on the first production unit before LRIP deliveries begin; VP Engineering approves the FAI plan and reviews the First Article Inspection Report (FAIR) for conformance; FAIR closure is a KDP-D exit criterion per #1223; FAI scope includes design documentation review, material and special process verification, dimensional inspection, and functional test. Test laboratory accreditation: test laboratories used for qualification must hold ISO/IEC 17025 accreditation for the applicable test scope or equivalent customer-approved qualification; VP Engineering maintains an approved test laboratory register; unaccredited labs require VP Engineering documented waiver with customer acceptance. Test anomaly disposition: all qualification test anomalies subject to formal Failure Review Board (FRB) chaired by VP Engineering before test continuation or result acceptance; FRB disposition (accept, retest, redesign) documented and retained in the qualification record package.
summary: Task: Test and qualification program oversight — qualification test planning, first article inspection per AS9102, ISO/IEC 17025 lab accreditation, and FRB anomaly disposition for VP Engineering

label: Technical risk management (KTPs and TPMs)
content: VP Engineering ownership of the program technical risk register, integrated with the enterprise Tier 2 ERM framework per #1215. Technical risk identification: VP Engineering conducts technical risk workshops at program initiation and each KDP gate; inputs include requirements analysis, design margin assessment, manufacturing process maturity (MRL), supplier technical capability, and test planning uncertainty. Key Technical Parameters (KTPs): VP Engineering designates 3-7 KTPs per program representing the highest-consequence technical performance requirements (weight, power, RF performance, reliability, etc.); KTPs are tracked monthly against planned achievement curves. Technical Performance Measures (TPMs): each KTP has an associated TPM chart showing planned vs. actual achievement vs. threshold; TPM trending at or below threshold for two consecutive periods triggers formal corrective action plan reviewed by VP Engineering. Risk mitigation authority: all technical risks with P×I ≥12 (5×5 matrix) require a formal mitigation plan approved by VP Engineering with bi-weekly review; residual risks P×I ≤12 accepted at VP Engineering level; risks P×I ≥15 escalated to Tier 1 enterprise risk register per #1776. Cross-program risk rollup: VP Engineering produces a monthly technical risk summary for COO PMC review listing top-5 cross-program technical risks; risks threatening Tier 0 program critical paths are escalated immediately to COO and CEO.
summary: Task: Technical risk management — KTP designation and TPM tracking, 5×5 risk register governance, mitigation authority thresholds, and cross-program technical risk rollup for VP Engineering

label: Engineering tools and infrastructure strategy
content: VP Engineering ownership of CAD/CAE/PLM tool strategy, license management, computing infrastructure, and engineering data management across the enterprise. Tool strategy: VP Engineering maintains the approved engineering tool standard with version control policy; non-standard tools require VP Engineering exception approval; tool standards reviewed annually for alignment with customer digital engineering requirements (MIL-HDBK-31032 digital engineering mandate), prime contractor interoperability requirements, and DoD model-based systems engineering (MBSE) adoption trends. License management: engineering software license pool managed by VP Engineering; utilization monitored monthly; licenses underutilized (<50% average over 6 months) eligible for reduction at next renewal; license queue depth >0.5 hr average triggers expansion justification for CIC submission per #1219 thresholds. Computing infrastructure: VP Engineering coordinates with CTO on simulation and analysis computing requirements (HPC cluster, cloud simulation); ITAR-controlled simulation data and digital models must reside on ITAR-compliant infrastructure with access controls approved by Export Compliance Officer per #1629. Engineering data management (EDM): all controlled engineering documents (drawings, specifications, models, analyses, ICDs) managed in PLM with version control and access control lists; ITAR-controlled data classified with FSO-approved personnel access lists; annual EDM audit conducted by VP Engineering and Export Compliance Officer to verify access control integrity and configuration status accounting completeness.
summary: Task: Engineering tools and infrastructure strategy — CAD/CAE/PLM tool standardization, license management, ITAR-compliant computing infrastructure, and EDM governance for VP Engineering

label: Supplier technical management
content: VP Engineering management of the technical dimension of supplier relationships, complementing VP Operations supply chain management in #47. Technical supplier evaluation: VP Engineering participates in supplier qualification reviews for all sources providing design-critical parts, performing NADCAP-required special processes, or holding delegated design authority; evaluates supplier technical capability against drawing requirements, process specifications, and quality plan adequacy. Source inspection designation: VP Engineering determines source inspection requirements (mandatory vs. discretionary) based on engineering risk assessment — first articles, safety-critical characteristics, and high-risk processes receive mandatory source inspection with VP Engineering-approved inspection plan; government source inspection (GSI) requirements flowed to supplier via purchase order with contracts coordination. Supplier Corrective Action Requests (SCARs): VP Engineering reviews and approves SCARs where the root cause involves a design, specification, or engineering definition issue (vs. process-only quality escapes); engineering root cause determination requires VP Engineering concurrence before SCAR closure. Delegated design authority: VP Engineering documents and controls any delegation of design responsibility to suppliers; delegation agreement defines design scope, interface requirements, design review participation, verification requirements, and VP Engineering approval authority over supplier design documentation; delegated design authority requires customer notification per DFARS 252.246-7000 material inspection requirements and contract data requirements.
summary: Task: Supplier technical management — technical supplier qualification, source inspection designation, SCAR engineering root cause concurrence, and delegated design authority governance for VP Engineering

label: Task order tracking and ceiling monitoring process
content: Operational system for monitoring IDIQ task order sub-pipelines and vehicle ceiling consumption. Components: (1) Task order sub-pipeline per vehicle — CRM tracks all anticipated task orders by ordering office, estimated value, anticipated issue date, incumbency status, and company position (prime or sub-awardee); BD staff updates weekly from SAM.gov solicitation notices and direct ordering office engagement; (2) Ceiling consumption monitoring — per vehicle: pull FPDS-NG quarterly for total ceiling value, cumulative awards to all awardees, remaining ceiling balance, and implied burn rate (avg annual award × remaining base years); flag vehicles where ceiling is >70% consumed with >2 years remaining POP as ceiling exhaustion risk requiring early re-compete or ceiling modification planning; (3) Competitive task order share — for each MAIDIQ vehicle, track company's share of total task order awards vs. total awards to all awardees via FPDS-NG; target ≥fair share (1÷awardee count); share below 50% of fair share triggers positioning review with VP BD; (4) Dormancy monitoring — vehicles with <$500K in task order awards in trailing 12 months may be dormant; assess whether continued compliance investment is warranted vs. voluntary withdrawal; (5) Ceiling exhaustion notification — when remaining ceiling falls below 1× estimated annual revenue contribution from that vehicle, VP BD alerted for re-compete or expansion modification planning.
summary: System: IDIQ task order sub-pipeline tracking, ceiling consumption monitoring, competitive share analysis, and dormancy assessment per vehicle

label: IDIQ recompete timeline management
content: Structured 18-month process for managing IDIQ vehicle recompetes to protect incumbent revenue. Timeline milestones: (1) 18 months before vehicle expiry — VP BD flags vehicle as must-win if it supports >10% annual revenue; assigns capture manager; initiates capture plan per #1630; requests informal debrief from government ordering office on incumbent performance and future requirements direction; (2) 15 months — pull FPDS-NG utilization and ordering concentration data; assess whether government is consolidating awardee counts (reduces competition odds) or expanding (lowers per-awardee ceiling); identify anticipated evaluation criteria changes from pre-solicitation signals; (3) 12 months — brief CEO with competitive assessment: incumbent threat level, known competitor field, anticipated pricing posture, expected scope or requirements changes, and B&P budget requirement; initiate teaming discussions if capability gaps are identified; (4) 9 months — respond to all government market research (Sources Sought, RFIs, draft RFP) emphasizing delivery performance record and CPARS history on the current vehicle; monitor pre-solicitation planning notices on SAM.gov for solicitation timeline signals; (5) 6 months before anticipated RFP — finalize teaming strategy and execute teaming agreements; confirm all required certifications and clearances are current; complete PTW analysis using current vehicle award history and competitor pricing posture; (6) RFP drop — activate full proposal process per #1630; prioritize transition risk mitigation narrative and incumbent knowledge as proposal differentiators. Recompete risk rating: assess 90 days before expiry on four factors — CPARS ratings, price competitiveness, scope changes vs. current capability, and competitive field depth; escalate High risk to CEO immediately.
summary: System: 18-month IDIQ recompete timeline — 6 milestone checkpoints from capture initiation through RFP drop with competitive assessment and CEO briefing at 12-month mark

label: On-ramp off-ramp competitive assessment
content: Evaluation framework for new IDIQ on-ramp opportunities and off-ramp risk monitoring. On-ramp assessment criteria: (1) Strategic alignment — vehicle must cover NAICS codes, program offices, or agencies not served by existing vehicles; minimum 3 pipeline opportunities ≥$500K each without alternative vehicle access; (2) Ceiling-to-awardee ratio — calculate vehicle ceiling ÷ number of awardees; prioritize vehicles with ratio >$5M per awardee as better revenue potential; (3) Task order competition rules — best-value preferred over LPTA where technical differentiation is company's competitive advantage; assess historical task order methodology via FPDS-NG on existing similar vehicles; (4) Cost-benefit — estimated proposal cost for on-ramp bid vs. expected task order revenue over vehicle life; minimum threshold 5× B&P investment return within 3 years; (5) On-ramp go/no-go — authorized when all four criteria met plus standard five-criterion go/no-bid screen from #1482. Off-ramp risk monitoring: (1) Monitor IDIQ vehicle recompete announcements — consolidation events typically reduce awardee count; any vehicle with ≤12 months remaining POP and no recompete planning triggers off-ramp risk alert to VP BD; (2) Utilization trend decline — declining ordering activity over trailing 3 years signals government transition to a replacement vehicle; identify successor vehicle and initiate on-ramp at least 18 months before current vehicle exhaustion; (3) Awardee removal risk — CPARS Marginal or Unsatisfactory on any task order may trigger government removal from vehicle access; maintain CPARS ≥ Satisfactory on all active task orders; (4) Annual vehicle portfolio review — VP BD reviews all held vehicles annually for utilization, remaining ceiling, competitive standing, and strategic value; voluntarily withdraw from dormant vehicles consuming compliance resources with no expected revenue.
summary: System: On-ramp opportunity scoring (alignment, ceiling-to-awardee ratio, competition rules, ROI) and off-ramp risk monitoring for IDIQ vehicle portfolio management

label: Gate 0-1 opportunity identification and qualification
content: Detailed process for Shipley Gates 0 and 1 within VP BD capture management.
