Gate 0 (Opportunity Identification): (1) Trigger sources — SAM.gov pre-solicitation alerts, GovWin IQ ratings, industry day attendance, direct government customer conversations, prime contractor pipeline intelligence, and FPDS-NG re-compete identification (programs expiring within 18 months); (2) CRM record creation — BD staff creates opportunity record within 1 business day of identification; required fields: opportunity name, estimated value, program office and contracting activity, anticipated RFP date, set-aside type, period of performance, NAICS code, and SAM.gov solicitation number if available; (3) Five-criterion go/no-bid screen (#1482) applied within 5 business days — NAICS match, minimum $250K value, past performance citation available, no disqualifying barrier (FOCI/ITAR/clearance), and strategic fit with H1/H2 pipeline; fail any → close with logged rationale; pass all → advance to Gate 1; (4) VP BD notification required within 24 hours for any identification ≥$1M. Gate 1 (Opportunity Qualification): (1) VP BD approves CRM advancement to Qualified status; (2) Initial B&P allocation authorized for capture research; (3) Capture manager assigned for PWIN ≥35% or must-win designations; (4) Initial seven-factor PWIN scoring from #1482 applied and documented in CRM; (5) Capture plan initiation — for Qualified opportunities ≥$1M, capture manager begins formal capture plan development covering customer intelligence profile, competitive landscape, and preliminary win strategy; (6) Qualified+ pipeline coverage check — VP BD confirms total Qualified+ pipeline coverage ratio remains ≥3× annual revenue target after adding new opportunity.
summary: System: Gate 0 opportunity identification and five-criterion go/no-bid screen; Gate 1 VP BD qualification approval, initial PWIN scoring, capture manager assignment, and B&P authorization

label: Gate 2 capture strategy and solution development
content: Gate 2 capture strategy development — the core capture phase occurring 6-18 months before anticipated RFP drop. Capture plan required elements developed or refined at Gate 2: (1) Customer intelligence profile — named government PM, CO, COR, and technical leads with relationship strength rating; customer hot buttons and evaluation priorities inferred from RFI responses, industry day Q&A, and direct engagement; customer budget environment (FYDP health, CR risk, program office priorities); incumbent performance assessment via FPDS-NG and debrief intelligence; (2) Competitive landscape — incumbent identification via FPDS-NG; known competitor field, pricing posture, and teaming tendencies; ghost team competitive analysis (#N8) initiated for pursuits ≥$2M; (3) Win strategy — 3-5 master win themes aligned to anticipated Section M evaluation criteria per discriminator workshop (#N7); each theme with supporting discriminator, proof point, and theme champion assignment; solution development addressing top customer hot buttons; (4) Teaming strategy — capability gap analysis vs. anticipated RFP requirements; short-list of teaming candidates evaluated on five dimensions from #1633; teaming discussions initiated ≥6 months before RFP; preliminary workshare allocation; (5) B&P budget request — bottom-up estimate of proposal development effort (person-hours by role and volume); VP BD review and approval required; cumulative single-opportunity B&P commitments >$500K require CEO approval; (6) PWIN refinement — update seven-factor PWIN based on new intelligence; document score changes. Gate 2 review: capture manager presents capture plan to VP BD; VP BD approves, redirects, or no-bids; CEO review for must-win programs; approved B&P documented in commitment log.
summary: System: Gate 2 capture plan development covering customer intelligence, competitive landscape, win themes, teaming strategy, and B&P budget approval — VP BD review and approval gate

label: Gate 3 proposal readiness review
content: Gate 3 process at RFP drop — final bid/no-bid decision and proposal initiation authorization. Gate 3 activities: (1) RFP receipt and triage — VP BD reviews RFP within 24 hours; confirms Section L/M requirements against capture plan assumptions; identifies material differences from expected scope, evaluation criteria, or contract terms; (2) Capture plan validation — validate win themes are still traceable to actual Section M criteria; confirm proposed solution addresses actual PWS/SOW; flag surprises (unexpected requirements, adverse FAR clauses, pricing structure changes) requiring capture team response; (3) Final bid/no-bid decision — VP BD conducts final go/no-bid review against actual RFP and current PWIN; CEO review when cumulative B&P will exceed $500K or program is must-win; decision documented in bid/no-bid decision memo (L5 output); rationale logged in CRM regardless of outcome; (4) Proposal team staffing — approve proposal manager and volume lead assignments; define capture manager transition role; confirm color team reviewer availability for Pink/Red/Gold schedule; set Pink Team date (RFP + 10-14 days), Red Team (RFP + 25-30 days), Gold Team (5 days before submission); (5) Total B&P budget confirmation — validate budget is sufficient for actual RFP complexity; VP BD approval for any upward revision; revised total >$500K triggers CEO approval; (6) Compliance matrix initiation — proposal manager begins Section L/M compliance matrix within 48 hours of RFP; VP BD reviews before Pink Team. Outcome: bid authorized with proposal team, schedule, and B&P budget — or no-bid with rationale and pipeline lessons captured in CRM.
summary: System: Gate 3 RFP drop review — capture plan validation against actual Section L/M, final bid/no-bid decision, proposal team activation, color team scheduling, and compliance matrix initiation

label: Discriminator identification workshop methodology
content: Structured workshop process for systematically identifying true discriminators from the company capability inventory. Preparation (1 week before): compile anticipated evaluation criteria from Section M signals (draft RFP, RFI, similar contracts, industry day Q&A) with estimated importance weighting; gather company evidence inventory — CPARS ratings by domain, NADCAP certification scopes, clearance levels, unique process capabilities, co-development history with the customer, and analogous program data; compile known competitor capabilities from Three Cs competitor analysis and FPDS-NG history. Workshop execution (4 hours): Phase 1 — Brainstorm (15 min): capture manager facilitates round-robin generation of all company strengths; no filtering. Phase 2 — Evidence Gate: each claimed strength requires verifiable evidence (CPARS rating with period, certification number, test data, program citation); claims without evidence removed. Phase 3 — Discriminator Test: surviving claims tested with the challenge question — 'Can a competitor make this exact statement with equal credibility?' Claims failing the test are recategorized as strengths (present but not differentiating); claims passing advance as discriminator candidates. Phase 4 — Evaluation Criteria Cross-Walk: discriminator candidates mapped to specific evaluation criteria; criteria with no discriminator identified flagged as gaps requiring solution development or teaming action. Phase 5 — Theme Construction: group related discriminators into 3-5 master win themes; each theme must address at least one high-weight evaluation criterion; assign theme champion enforcing consistent expression across all proposal volumes. Output: discriminator register per active capture with evidence citations, evaluation criteria mapping, theme champion assignment, and Red Team validation status.
summary: System: 5-phase discriminator workshop — brainstorm, evidence gate, discriminator test, evaluation criteria cross-walk, and win theme construction with evidence-backed register

label: Ghost team competitive analysis framework
content: Ghost team process for simulating competitor proposal development to identify competitive vulnerabilities and sharpen proposal positioning. Structure: (1) Ghost team composition — 2-3 personnel NOT on the proposal writing team; ideally include individuals with direct competitor intelligence (former employees, BD staff familiar with competitor captures, or cleared external advisors); ghost team lead reports to VP BD, not proposal manager; (2) Assignment timing — VP BD assigns ghost team at Gate 2 completion, minimum 4 weeks before Red Team; ghost team receives all available competitor intelligence. Process phases: Phase 1 — Competitor Profiling: develop 1-page assessment per major competitor covering likely solution approach (extrapolated from past performance and program experience), pricing posture from FPDS-NG comparable awards, probable win themes, and anticipated teaming structure. Phase 2 — Ghost Executive Summary: for top 1-2 competitors, draft a 2-3 page ghost executive summary as if written by that competitor; capture their likely discriminator claims, past performance citations, and probable price-to-win posture. Phase 3 — Vulnerability Identification: compare ghost proposals against company's draft proposal; identify evaluation criteria where competitor's simulated approach scores higher; flag company weaknesses competitor will likely highlight in their proposal. Phase 4 — Counter-Strategy Development: for each competitive vulnerability, develop a specific response — either a proposal content addition strengthening the weakness or an enhanced win theme in an area of company superiority; present findings to capture manager for integration before Red Team. Phase 5 — Red Team Integration: ghost team presents findings as independent advisory; Red Team evaluates whether actual proposal is stronger than ghost proposals on all high-weight criteria. Output: ghost team competitive assessment summary (2-3 pages) to VP BD and proposal manager.
summary: System: 5-phase ghost team framework — competitor profiling, ghost executive summary drafting, vulnerability identification, counter-strategy development, and Red Team integration advisory

label: Congressional engagement and legislative tracking
content: VP BD system for tracking Congressional program champions, NDAA provisions, and legislative developments affecting the BD pipeline. Congressional program champion tracking: (1) For each pipeline program >$5M, identify relevant authorizing committees (HASC, SASC), appropriations subcommittees (HAC-D, SAC-D), and known Congressional champions (Members with program activity in their district or with strong defense constituency interests); (2) All direct Congressional staff contact routes through government affairs counsel per FAR 3.104 to protect source selection integrity — no ex parte communications that could compromise pending source selections; (3) Permissible engagement: industry association participation (NDIA, AIA, AUSA), public comment on proposed regulations, advocacy through government affairs channels for general program support, attendance at Congressional staff defense industry forums. NDAA monitoring: track annually — Section 8(a)/SDVOSB threshold changes affecting set-aside competition; domestic content mandate phase-ins per DFARS 252.225; SBIR/STTR reauthorization provisions (set-aside percentages, Phase III sole-source transition authority); OTA authority scope changes; CMMC implementation mandates affecting contract eligibility. Appropriations tracking: monitor HAC-D and SAC-D markup cycles for program additions, reductions, and unfunded priorities lists; Congressional add-backs to pipeline programs create unsolicited proposal or rapid-response opportunities; CR impacts on program start authority and multi-year procurement timing trigger pipeline risk adjustments. SBIR legislative alignment: track Congressional investment priorities in SBIR technology areas aligned to BD pipeline; Phase III bridge funding authority enables sole-source transitions from SBIR Phase II. Cadence: quarterly NDAA/appropriations update briefed to VP BD; material legislative changes briefed to CEO within 30 days.
summary: System: Congressional champion identification, NDAA provision tracking, appropriations monitoring, and ethics-compliant engagement protocol for legislative activities affecting the BD pipeline

label: Prime-sub relationship management and teaming agreement governance
content: Structured management of prime contractor relationships and teaming agreement compliance. Prime relationship management: (1) Named relationship map per prime — Program Director, Supply Chain Manager, BD counterpart, Technical Lead, and Contracts lead; relationship strength rating (1-5) per contact and last meaningful engagement date; single-point-of-failure contacts flagged quarterly; (2) Engagement cadence — monthly operational touchpoint (delivery performance, interface issues); quarterly strategic touchpoint (upcoming solicitations, workshare pipeline); annual VP-level strategic alignment (multi-year opportunities, teaming preferences, capability gaps the prime needs filled); (3) Approved Supplier List (ASL) governance — maintain qualification status and renewal dates per prime's ASL; 90-day advance notice for any renewal action required; degraded performance threatening ASL removal escalated to VP BD and VP Operations immediately. Teaming agreement governance: (1) Active TA register — VP BD maintains register of all executed teaming agreements with prime partner name, program/solicitation, agreement date, committed workshare percentage, exclusivity scope and duration, IP provisions summary, and expiration date; review for expirations quarterly; (2) Workshare performance monitoring — per #1834, track actual workshare received vs. committed; cumulative shortfall >10% from committed percentage triggers formal prime engagement documented in CRM; persistent shortfall (two consecutive quarters >10%) escalates to CEO per #1782 approval criteria; (3) IP protection enforcement — all technical data submissions to prime reviewed for ITAR compliance and proprietary data markings per DFARS 252.227 before transmission; any prime request for unlimited rights beyond contract scope escalated to Legal and CEO before response; (4) Exclusivity scope compliance — confirm exclusivity clauses do not prevent responding to other primes on non-competing programs; ambiguous exclusivity scope reviewed by Legal before responding to a competing prime opportunity.
summary: System: Named prime contractor relationship map, engagement cadence, ASL governance, teaming agreement register, workshare performance tracking, and IP protection enforcement

label: Price-to-win model development
content: Quantitative PTW model development and maintenance for competitive proposal pricing. Model components: (1) FPDS-NG benchmark database — pull historical awards for analogous contracts by NAICS, agency, contract type, and scope complexity; adjust for inflation using DoD CAPE escalation indices, quantity differences, and contract type risk differentials (FFP vs. CPFF fee); derive PTW range (floor/mid/ceiling) per pursuit; minimum 5 analogous awards required for reliable range; <5 awards requires documented assumption expansion and VP BD acknowledgment; (2) Parametric estimating layer — develop $/labor-hour by NAICS and complexity tier, $/unit by production maturity phase, and $/drawing by engineering discipline from historical program actuals; parametric estimates cross-check bottoms-up BOEs and flag outliers requiring explanation; (3) Competitive intelligence integration — incorporate pricing signals from BD win/loss debriefs, FPDS-NG recent comparable awards, and ghost team pricing assessment; adjust PTW range for competitive field size (fewer competitors → PTW ceiling rises) and set-aside status (restricted competition raises PTW ceiling); (4) Model calibration — quarterly back-test comparing PTW mid predictions against actual winning prices on recently awarded pursuits; target accuracy ±10% for 70%+ of historical cases; models with accuracy <65% on 10+ cases suspended for recalibration; (5) PTW report format — per pursuit: FPDS-NG data sources, adjustments applied, PTW range (floor/mid/ceiling), company cost-at-fee vs. PTW mid, competitive gap if any, and VP BD-recommended price point with rationale; delivered with every Gate 2 capture plan review.
summary: System: PTW model using FPDS-NG benchmarking, parametric cross-checks, competitive intelligence integration, and quarterly calibration — produces per-pursuit floor/mid/ceiling pricing range

label: Wrap rate analysis and competitive positioning
content: Wrap rate analysis system for assessing indirect cost competitiveness and positioning proposals relative to competitor cost structures. Wrap rate calculation: (1) Fully-burdened labor rate = direct labor rate × (1 + fringe%) × (1 + overhead%) × (1 + G&A%); total wrap multiplier = fully-burdened rate ÷ direct base rate; typical defense manufacturing wrap rates 1.8×–3.2× depending on facility intensity and executive overhead structure; (2) Rate competitiveness benchmark — extract awarded T&M contract labor rates from FPDS-NG in target NAICS codes to infer competitor fully-loaded rates; wrap rate materially higher than market (>15%) signals pricing competitiveness risk on labor-intensive pursuits; SBIR Phase II public awards reveal competitor cost structures for small business competitors; (3) Rate reduction levers — VP BD and CFO identify indirect cost pool elements reducible without capability impact: occupancy cost through facility optimization, G&A through BD efficiency improvement, fringe through benefit plan benchmarking; VP BD quantifies competitive benefit of each lever before committing reduction resources; (4) Competitive positioning strategy — VP BD determines appropriate wrap rate position per pursuit: high-differentiation sole-source positions sustain premium wrap rates; competitive MAIDIQ environments require market or discount positioning; LPTA environments require cost-minimization approach; (5) Rate governance — all proposals use current FPRA or provisional FPRR rates; deviation requires CFO concurrence; rate changes >5% on any pool trigger PTW recalibration across all active pursuits per #1784. Annual review: VP BD and CFO compare wrap rates against FPDS-NG award market data annually; rate competitiveness incorporated into SWOT analysis.
summary: System: Wrap rate calculation, FPDS-NG competitive benchmarking, rate reduction lever analysis, and competitive positioning strategy by pursuit type

label: B&P budget allocation and ROI tracking
content: B&P budget management system covering annual allocation, per-opportunity commitment tracking, and ROI measurement. Budget allocation: (1) Annual B&P budget planning — VP BD develops annual request in Q4 aligned to pipeline strategy; structured by stage (Identification/Qualified/Capture/Proposal) and by must-win vs. competitive categories; must-win programs receive priority allocation; calibrated to target B&P efficiency ≥$8M awarded per $1M B&P from #1481; (2) Per-opportunity allocation — Gate 1: initial research budget ($5-25K typical); Gate 2: capture plan development budget VP BD approved; Gate 3: full proposal development budget VP BD approved; cumulative single-opportunity B&P >$500K requires CEO approval; (3) B&P commitment log — per-opportunity log with gate approval date, approved amount, actuals-to-date, and estimate-to-complete; reconciled monthly against accounting actuals by CFO; over-commitment against approved budget requires VP BD approval before additional charges incurred. ROI tracking: (1) B&P efficiency — contract awards won ÷ B&P spent, rolling 8-quarter calculation per #1481; disaggregated by contract type, NAICS, customer segment, and proposal size band to identify highest and lowest ROI pursuit categories; (2) B&P write-off log — when pursuit is lost or voluntarily no-bid after B&P investment, VP BD logs write-off reason in CRM (No-Bid Voluntary / Lost Competitive / Lost Sole-Source / Contract Not Awarded); write-off log feeds quarterly ROI analysis; (3) Reallocation process — VP BD may reallocate B&P within approved total budget as priorities shift; reallocation >$50K from one pursuit to another requires VP BD formal approval with rationale documented; (4) Quarterly B&P performance report — VP BD presents B&P actuals vs. plan, efficiency trend, write-off analysis, and next-quarter projection to CEO and CFO at monthly pipeline review.
summary: System: Annual B&P budget allocation by stage and priority, per-opportunity commitment log with gate approval, B&P efficiency ROI tracking, write-off analysis, and reallocation process

label: Bid/no-bid gate criteria — PWIN, strategic fit, resources, customer access
content: AUTHORIZE BID (VP BD approval) when ALL four criteria are met: (1) PWIN score ≥40% based on seven-factor model from #1482 — scores 30-39% require explicit VP BD override with documented strategic rationale (must-win or high-strategic-value); scores <30% are no-bid unless CEO override with documented rationale; (2) Strategic fit confirmed — opportunity aligns to at least one H1 or H2 pipeline program area per Three Horizons register or is a must-win re-compete; opportunities with no strategic alignment require CEO approval to proceed; (3) Resource availability confirmed — B&P budget has uncommitted headroom ≥ estimated proposal development cost; BD team bandwidth can support RFP deadline without sacrificing higher-priority must-win or higher-PWIN pursuits; no critical color team scheduling conflict with a higher-ranked pursuit; (4) Customer access adequate — company holds a direct working relationship with at least one named government decision-maker (PM, CO, COR, or key technical lead) OR is incumbent with established CPARS history on the program. NO-BID when: PWIN <30% with no strategic override; OR resource conflict with a must-win or higher-PWIN pursuit is unresolvable; OR no customer access and no executable pre-RFP engagement plan to remedy access gap before submission; OR B&P investment would exceed $500K with CEO approval unavailable before proposal deadline. CONDITIONAL BID (VP BD discretion): PWIN 30-39% on a strategic or must-win program — bid only if resource availability is confirmed and a targeted customer access improvement plan is executable within the proposal timeline. All bid/no-bid decisions documented in decision memo (L5) with per-criterion assessment; no-bid decisions include coded rationale for pipeline analytics.
summary: Decision: Authorize bid when PWIN ≥40%, strategic fit confirmed, resources available, and customer access adequate; no-bid when PWIN <30%; conditional bid at 30-39% for strategic programs

label: Teaming partner selection criteria — past performance, clearances, cost structure, no OCI
content: SELECT teaming partner when ALL four criteria are satisfied: (1) Past performance alignment — partner holds CPARS ratings of Very Good or Exceptional on at least two analogous programs (same domain, contract type, and scope complexity) within the prior 3-year evaluation window; no Marginal or Unsatisfactory ratings within the prior 3 years on any contract in the relevant domain; (2) Clearance and security infrastructure — partner's facility security agreement (FCL) and personnel clearances are at the level required by anticipated program classification; no unresolved FOCI concerns; if clearances are insufficient, an executable qualification plan within the proposal timeline is documented; (3) Cost structure compatibility — partner's fully-loaded rates do not compress total team margin below VP BD-approved floor; proposed workshare allocation is economically viable for both parties; partner's rates documented with FPRA or provisional FPRRs for proposal cost volume compliance; workshare allocation to partner ≥10% of total program value per #1782 approval criteria; (4) No organizational conflict of interest — Legal and Export Compliance Officer OCI review complete with no unmitigated category-1 OCI per FAR 9.5 (unequal access to information, impaired objectivity, or biased ground rules); lower-category OCI is acceptable with documented mitigation plan appended to teaming agreement. REJECT candidate when: any category-1 OCI is unmitigated; OR partner CPARS includes Marginal or Unsatisfactory in relevant domain within 3 years; OR cost structure would compress team margin below floor regardless of workshare adjustment; OR FOCI concern is unresolvable within proposal timeline. CONDITIONAL selection: workshare 5-9% of program value — CEO approval required per #1782 with VP BD strategic rationale (IDIQ vehicle access, market development value). Decision documented in teaming partner selection register with per-criterion assessment.
summary: Decision: Select teaming partner when CPARS ≥VG/Exceptional, clearances adequate, cost structure margin-safe, and no unmitigated category-1 OCI; reject on any category-1 OCI or Marginal/Unsatisfactory CPARS

label: B&P investment threshold — CEO approval required above $500K
content: CEO APPROVAL REQUIRED when cumulative B&P investment committed to a single opportunity pursuit exceeds $500K across all gates (Gates 1+2+3 combined). This threshold applies to total B&P spend per opportunity, not individual gate approvals. Authorization protocol: (1) VP BD presents CEO approval request with: opportunity name, estimated contract value, current PWIN score, capture plan executive summary (2 pages max), total B&P committed to date, projected additional B&P through proposal submission, and strategic rationale for investment level; (2) CEO evaluates: expected value ROI (estimated contract value × PWIN ÷ total projected B&P; target ≥10:1); risk-adjusted return vs. alternative BD investment options for the same budget; win strategy confidence level and competitive landscape assessment; (3) CEO approves, approves with conditions (e.g., requires Red Team milestone before incremental spend authorization), or redirects to lower B&P with modified capture strategy; decision documented in B&P commitment log and CRM opportunity record within 2 business days of request. VP BD AUTHORITY (no CEO approval required): cumulative single-opportunity B&P ≤$500K; VP BD may reallocate B&P within approved total budget. Interaction with gate approvals: CEO also reviews must-win programs and single-gate B&P requests per Gate 2 and Gate 3 governance in #1785, but those reviews do not independently satisfy the >$500K cumulative threshold — both governance layers apply when triggered. No-pursuit consequence: if CEO declines approval, VP BD must reduce B&P scope and submit revised plan within 10 business days or close the pursuit as no-bid with rationale logged.
summary: Decision: CEO approval required when cumulative single-opportunity B&P investment exceeds $500K; VP BD has full authority ≤$500K; CEO evaluates expected value ROI ≥10:1 and strategic rationale

label: Customer engagement strategy for DoD, primes, and international customers
content: Develop and execute customer engagement plans across DoD service branches (Army, Navy, Air Force, USMC, USSF), defense prime contractors (Lockheed Martin, RTX, Boeing, Northrop, L3Harris), and international customers pursuing FMS/DCS opportunities. Map key stakeholders (PEOs, PMs, contracting officers, prime BD leads) and assign relationship owners. Conduct pre-solicitation engagement: industry days, RFI responses, one-on-one meetings, and capability briefings per procurement integrity rules (41 USC 2102). Track engagement cadence and relationship health in CRM. Tailor messaging by customer segment—operational requirements for end users, teaming value proposition for primes, and export compliance posture for international customers (ITAR/EAR, DSCA FMS process).
summary: Task: Customer engagement strategy for DoD branches, prime contractors, and international FMS/DCS customers

label: Competitive intelligence and defense landscape mapping
content: Maintain strategic-level competitive landscape map of defense market segments relevant to Yggdrasil Aero. Identify top competitors by NAICS/PSC code, contract vehicle, and customer agency using FPDS-NG, USASpending.gov, and industry reports. Assess competitor financial health, M&A activity, contract wins/losses, and capability investments. Map competitor portfolio gaps and areas of potential displacement. Track industry consolidation (primes acquiring small/mid-tier firms) and emerging entrants. Produce semi-annual competitive landscape briefing for leadership covering market share by segment, competitor win rates, and strategic threats. Differentiate from bid-level competitive intel (capture_mgr scope) by focusing on macro positioning and portfolio-level strategy.
summary: Task: Strategic competitive landscape mapping and defense market intelligence at portfolio level

label: Strategic teaming and joint venture development for large defense pursuits
content: Identify and structure teaming and joint venture arrangements for large defense opportunities (>$100M IDIQ, GWAC, major program acquisitions). Evaluate JV structures (populated vs. unpopulated, LLC vs. formal JV) against opportunity requirements, SB set-aside thresholds, and performance obligations. Assess mentor-protégé program applicability (DoD Mentor-Protégé Program, SBA All Small Mentor-Protégé) to create advantaged teaming arrangements. Negotiate work share allocation, management control, and IP ownership prior to proposal submission. Coordinate with legal counsel on JV formation documents, operating agreements, and ITAR flow-down requirements. Maintain strategic teaming pipeline aligned to multi-year BD roadmap; distinguish from tactical teaming managed per-proposal by capture_mgr.
summary: Task: JV formation, mentor-protégé structures, and strategic teaming for large defense program pursuits

label: BD pipeline metrics and reporting
content: Define, track, and report BD pipeline metrics at stage-gate level: total qualified pipeline value by phase (Identify, Qualify, Capture, Proposal, Award), weighted pipeline value (pipeline value × Pwin), win rate by opportunity type (re-compete vs. new business, competitive vs. sole-source), bid-to-win ratio, and pipeline velocity (average days per stage). Maintain pipeline data in CRM with monthly refresh. Produce BD scorecard for leadership review: trailing 12-month win rate, weighted pipeline coverage ratio (target: 3-5× annual revenue goal), B&P spend efficiency, and stage conversion rates. Benchmark metrics against industry standards. Use pipeline velocity data to identify stage bottlenecks and resource constraints impeding capture maturation.
summary: Task: BD pipeline tracking, win rate reporting, and pipeline velocity metrics for leadership decision-making

label: Marketing and trade show strategy
content: Develop annual marketing and conference strategy targeting key defense industry venues: AUSA Annual Meeting (October, Washington DC), Sea-Air-Space (April, National Harbor), Paris Air Show (June, biennial, Le Bourget), DSEI (biennial, London), and agency-specific symposia (AFCEA, NDIA). Define exhibit objectives per event (capability awareness, customer meetings, teaming outreach, international market development). Coordinate pre-show customer scheduling, capability briefing materials, and exhibit booth design. Produce post-show engagement report with meeting log, pipeline opportunities identified, and ROI assessment against B&P spend. Align trade show calendar with customer buying cycles and RFP forecast to maximize pre-solicitation engagement windows. Manage marketing budget allocation across events.
summary: Task: Annual trade show and conference strategy covering AUSA, Sea-Air-Space, Paris Air Show, and other defense venues

label: Small business compliance and subcontracting plan management
content: Ensure compliance with FAR 19.7 small business subcontracting requirements for contracts exceeding applicable thresholds (>$750K for other than small business prime contractors). Develop Individual Subcontracting Plans or Master Subcontracting Plans with SB participation goals by category: Small Business (SB), Small Disadvantaged Business (SDB), Women-Owned Small Business (WOSB), HUBZone, Veteran-Owned (VOSB), and Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned (SDVOSB). Maintain subcontracting reporting in Electronic Subcontracting Reporting System (eSRS): Individual Subcontract Reports (ISR) and Summary Subcontract Reports (SSR). Coordinate with subcontracting plan administrator to track actual vs. planned SB spend. Monitor compliance with DoD SB program objectives and agency-specific SB participation requirements per solicitation instructions.
summary: Task: FAR 19.7 small business subcontracting plan development, goal-setting, and eSRS compliance reporting

label: Proposal oversight and BD-level win strategy
content: Provide BD director-level oversight and strategic direction across active proposal efforts. Establish and enforce gate review criteria: Gate 1 (Qualify/No-Bid), Gate 2 (Capture Strategy Approval), Gate 3 (Proposal Ready/B&P Authorization). Approve win strategy and discriminator framework prior to proposal kickoff. Review and approve color team findings (Pink, Red, Gold) for strategic consistency with customer engagement intelligence and corporate messaging. Arbitrate resource conflicts between concurrent proposal efforts. Ensure proposal win themes reflect BD-developed customer insight and competitive positioning—distinct from proposal_mgr execution focus. Conduct lessons-learned reviews post-award/post-loss with capture and proposal teams to improve future win rates.
summary: Task: BD director governance of proposal gate reviews, win strategy approval, and cross-proposal resource oversight

label: Federal opportunity sourcing via SAM.gov, FPDS-NG, and USASpending.gov
content: Systematically source defense contracting opportunities from federal databases: SAM.gov (contract opportunities, pre-solicitation notices, Sources Sought, RFIs, RFPs), FPDS-NG (Federal Procurement Data System—Next Generation for historical award data, incumbent contract values, and agency spending patterns), and USASpending.gov (agency budget trends, prime/sub award data, spending by NAICS/PSC). Configure SAM.gov automated opportunity alerts by NAICS code, PSC code, agency, and set-aside type. Use FPDS-NG data to identify expiring contracts (re-compete pipeline) 18-24 months in advance. Cross-reference USASpending.gov to validate agency budget trends and prioritize opportunity pursuit. Supplement with agency budget justification books (P-40, R-2, O-1 exhibits) from congressional submissions for program-level investment visibility.
summary: Task: Systematic opportunity sourcing from SAM.gov, FPDS-NG, and USASpending.gov to build and validate BD pipeline

label: Contract negotiation and definitization
content: Manage transition from undefinitized contract actions (UCAs) and letter contracts to definitive contracts per FAR 16.603 and DFARS 217.74. UCAs must be definitized within 180 days of award or before 50% of not-to-exceed price is obligated, whichever is earlier. Key activities: prepare and negotiate Price Negotiation Memoranda (PNMs) documenting basis of price, document negotiation positions in negotiation memoranda, ensure prenegotiation objectives are approved before opening discussions, and obtain DCAA audit of cost proposals where required. Track all UCAs in a definitization log with contractual deadlines. Failure to definitize on schedule requires CO notification to head of contracting activity.
summary: Task: Manage UCA and letter contract definitization — negotiation memoranda, PNMs, and definitization deadlines per FAR 16.603 and DFARS 217.74

label: Contract type management and risk allocation
content: Select and administer contract types based on risk allocation, requirements definition maturity, and regulatory constraints. FFP: maximum contractor risk, appropriate for well-defined requirements with stable design; no cost visibility obligation. CPFF: government bears cost risk; requires adequate accounting system (DFARS 242.7502) and DCAA audit access. CPIF: cost incentive shares cost risk; structure target cost, target fee, ceiling price, and share ratio. T&M: highest government risk; requires determination that no other type is suitable (FAR 16.601(d)); requires government surveillance. IDIQ: issue delivery/task orders against a base vehicle; ensure minimum guarantee is legally defensible and maximum ceiling is realistic. For each type, document risk allocation rationale in the acquisition strategy. Monitor cost-type contracts for cost growth triggers requiring contractor notification and CO intervention.
summary: Task: Select and administer FFP, CPFF, CPIF, T&M, and IDIQ contract types — risk allocation rationale, accounting system requirements, and monitoring obligations

label: Contract administration and modifications (FAR Part 43)
content: Process contract modifications per FAR Part 43. Bilateral modifications (SF 30, FAR 43.103(a)): require mutual agreement; used for scope changes, price adjustments, definitization, and settlement of claims. Unilateral modifications (FAR 43.103(b)): issued by CO without contractor signature; authorized for administrative changes, exercise of options, and changes under a Changes clause. Change orders (FAR 43.201): CO-issued directive for work within the general scope of the contract; contractor must proceed pending equitable adjustment negotiation. Supplemental agreements: bilateral instruments that change any contract term. Administrative changes: correct errors, update addresses, or change payment office — no contractor signature required. Track all modifications in a modification log with SF 30 numbers, effective dates, and cumulative contract value. Verify every modification includes correct clause updates, period of performance adjustments, and CLIN/SLIN structure.
summary: Task: Process bilateral and unilateral contract modifications, change orders, and supplemental agreements per FAR Part 43 — modification log and clause compliance

label: Contract closeout procedures
content: Execute contract closeout per FAR 4.804. Initiation: CO initiates closeout after physical completion (all deliverables accepted, period of performance expired). Required actions: (1) verify final invoice submitted and paid; (2) obtain contractor's final release of claims (FAR 52.216-7 release language); (3) disposition of government property — inventory reconciliation, transfer, or abandonment per FAR 45.6; (4) patent and royalty clearance — contractor certifies no outstanding patent, royalty, or copyright obligations; (5) subcontract closeout — verify all subcontract obligations settled; (6) DCAA final audit for cost-type contracts — resolves indirect rate settlements; (7) deobligation of excess funds; (8) complete DD Form 1594 (Contract Completion Statement). Target closeout timelines: commercial contracts within 6 months of physical completion; cost-type contracts within 36 months. Maintain closeout checklist in contract file per FAR 4.803.
summary: Task: Execute contract closeout per FAR 4.804 — final payment, property disposition, patent clearance, DD Form 1594, and file completion requirements

label: Terms and conditions review
content: Analyze contract Section I (Contract Clauses) for standard and special contract requirements. Review mandatory FAR clauses (FAR 52.2xx series) and DFARS clauses (DFARS 252.2xx series) for applicability, prescription, and compliance obligations. Flag clauses that impose internal process requirements: FAR 52.222-26 (Equal Opportunity), FAR 52.222-35 (Affirmative Action for Veterans), FAR 52.215-2 (Audit and Records — Negotiation), FAR 52.203-13 (Contractor Code of Business Ethics), DFARS 252.204-7012 (cybersecurity). Identify special contract requirements in Section H that impose unique program obligations. Prepare clause compliance matrix mapping each clause to responsible internal party. Verify that all clauses are current per FAR/DFARS as of contract date — outdated clause versions are a common audit finding. Flag clauses with flow-down obligations for subcontract terms review.
summary: Task: Review FAR/DFARS standard and special contract clauses — compliance matrix, responsible party mapping, and mandatory clause currency verification

label: Government property management (FAR 52.245)
content: Manage Government Furnished Property (GFP), Government Furnished Equipment (GFE), and Government Furnished Information (GFI) per FAR Part 45 and clause FAR 52.245-1. Key obligations: (1) establish and maintain a property control system meeting FAR 52.245-1(f) requirements — receipt, identification, records, movement, storage, reports, relief, and disposal; (2) maintain property records with required data elements (acquisition cost, national stock number, serial number, location, condition code); (3) conduct annual physical inventory and reconcile against property records; (4) report lost, damaged, or destroyed government property per FAR 52.245-1(h) — prepare Property Loss Report within required timeframe; (5) submit Contractor's Annual Inventory Schedule; (6) coordinate disposal through Plant Clearance Officer (PLCO) at contract completion — excess property screened for reutilization before disposal. DCMA/DCAA will audit property records; inadequate system is a contractor business system deficiency with payment withholding risk under DFARS 252.242-7005.
summary: Task: Administer GFP/GFE/GFI per FAR 52.245-1 — property control system, inventory reconciliation, loss reporting, and disposal coordination with PLCO

label: OCI identification and mitigation (FAR Subpart 9.5)
content: Identify and mitigate Organizational Conflicts of Interest per FAR Subpart 9.5. Three OCI categories: (1) Unequal access to information — contractor has proprietary information giving unfair competitive advantage; (2) Biased ground rules — contractor helped write the RFP or SOW it will compete on; (3) Impaired objectivity — contractor evaluates its own products or services (e.g., advisory/assistance contracts). Mitigation actions: Firewall/disclosure plan — separate organizational unit, restricted information flow, employee certifications; Exclusion — bar contractor from competing on follow-on work; Mitigation plan — documented procedures, monitoring, and government oversight. OCI mitigation plans must be submitted to CO for approval before award. Monitor throughout contract performance for new OCI triggers (acquisitions, teaming changes, new task orders). Document all OCI reviews in contract file per FAR 9.504. Failure to disclose known OCI is grounds for contract termination for default and suspension/debarment.
summary: Task: Identify and mitigate unequal access, biased ground rules, and impaired objectivity OCIs per FAR Subpart 9.5 — mitigation plan development and CO approval

label: Subcontract management and flowdown
content: Manage subcontract compliance including prime contract requirement flowdown and consent-to-subcontract obligations. Consent to subcontract: required for cost-type contracts per FAR 44.201-1 when subcontract exceeds simplified acquisition threshold; submit SF 1474 or equivalent package to CO. Mandatory flowdown clauses: at minimum, flow FAR 52.244-6 (Subcontracts for Commercial Items); also flow all clauses whose prescription requires it regardless of subcontract type (e.g., DFARS 252.204-7012, FAR 52.222-26, FAR 52.222-50). Review each prime contract clause against FAR/DFARS prescription language to determine required flowdown. Ensure subcontract SOW, deliverables, and acceptance criteria align with prime contract obligations. Maintain subcontract file documentation: consent request and approval, subcontract terms, flowdown clause list, performance surveillance records. For cost-type subcontracts, verify subcontractor has an adequate accounting system before award (DFARS 242.7502).
summary: Task: Ensure prime contract requirements flow to subcontractors — consent-to-subcontract per FAR 44.201-1, mandatory clause flowdown analysis, and subcontract file maintenance

label: Claims and disputes resolution
content: Manage contractor claims and disputes per the Contract Disputes Act (CDA), 41 U.S.C. §§ 7101-7109, and FAR Part 33. Request for Equitable Adjustment (REA): informal mechanism preceding a formal claim; contractor submits REA for government-caused cost impacts; negotiate resolution before CDA filing to avoid adversarial posture. Formal CDA claim: must be submitted in writing, state specific dollar amount, request a final decision, and be certified if over $100,000 (FAR 33.207). CO must issue Final Decision within 60 days for claims ≤$100K or notify contractor of decision date for larger claims. Appeal options: Armed Services Board of Contract Appeals (ASBCA), Civilian Board (CBCA), or U.S. Court of Federal Claims within 90 days of Final Decision. Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR): mediation and mini-trials are encouraged per FAR 33.214 to reduce litigation cost and schedule impact. Maintain REA/claim log tracking submission dates, dollar amounts, CO decision deadlines, and appeal windows. Coordinate with legal counsel before CO Final Decision issuance.
summary: Task: Manage REAs, CDA claims, CO Final Decisions, and ADR processes per FAR Part 33 — certification requirements, appeal timelines, and dispute log maintenance

label: Environmental compliance management (EPA NESHAP 40 CFR 63 Subpart GG)
content: Ensure facility compliance with EPA National Emission Standards for Hazardous Air Pollutants applicable to aerospace manufacturing operations (40 CFR Part 63 Subpart GG — National Emission Standards for Aerospace Manufacturing and Rework Facilities). Manage Title V or synthetic minor source air permits, including permit renewal timelines and condition tracking. Track HAP (hazardous air pollutant) and VOC emissions from painting, coating, adhesive bonding, and solvent degreasing operations against permit thresholds. Conduct semi-annual compliance audits and annual emission calculations. Submit required reports (semi-annual compliance certifications, annual emission inventories) to EPA and state/local air quality management districts. Coordinate with EHS manager on deviation notifications, permit modifications when production processes change, and response to agency inspections. Maintain regulatory records retention per 40 CFR Part 63 recordkeeping requirements (minimum 5 years).
summary: Task: Manage facility air permit compliance under EPA NESHAP 40 CFR Part 63 Subpart GG for aerospace manufacturing HAP/VOC emissions

label: Security and access control for classified areas (NISPOM 32 CFR Part 117)
content: Implement and maintain physical security requirements per NISPOM (32 CFR Part 117, effective 2021) for classified work areas, closed areas, and restricted areas within the facility. Manage SCIFs (Sensitive Compartmented Information Facilities) and closed areas — construction, accreditation, and periodic reinspection per ICD 705 and DCSA standards. Design and maintain access control systems (ACS), intrusion detection systems (IDS), and alarm monitoring with required response protocols. Control visitor access and escort procedures for classified areas; maintain visitor logs per NISPOM requirements. Coordinate directly with the Facility Security Officer (FSO) on DCSA inspections, vulnerability assessments, and annual self-inspections. Support DCSA facility inspections and respond to security findings within required timeframes. Maintain as-built security drawings and as-installed IDS/ACS documentation under configuration control.
summary: Task: Implement and maintain NISPOM-compliant physical security for classified areas, SCIFs, and restricted zones; coordinate with FSO on DCSA requirements

label: Fire protection and life safety (NFPA codes)
content: Manage fire protection systems and life safety compliance for aerospace manufacturing facilities. Maintain fire suppression systems (wet pipe, dry pipe, deluge, clean agent per NFPA 13) and conduct annual inspection, testing, and maintenance (ITM) per NFPA 25. Conduct fire risk assessments per NFPA 1 (Fire Code) and NFPA 101 (Life Safety Code) — including occupancy classification, occupant load, egress capacity, and travel distance verification. CODE SYSTEM DISTINCTION: IBC Chapter 3 occupancy codes (F-1, F-2, H-1 to H-5, S-1) govern building construction and fire-separation wall ratings; NFPA 101 chapters (Ch.40/41 Industrial, Ch.42/43 Storage) govern life-safety system requirements — both apply simultaneously but must not be conflated: 'H-3 per IBC §415.4' is correct; 'H-3 per NFPA 101' is incorrect. Assess combustible dust hazards per NFPA 652/654 for machining/abrasive-blasting; require explosion-relief vents or suppression on all flammable-dust ductwork. Manage hot-work operations per NFPA 51B: written permit, dedicated fire watch for ≥30 minutes post-completion, temporary non-combustible barriers within 35 ft. Maintain and rehearse emergency egress plans; conduct minimum 2x annual fire drills and document participation. Ensure code compliance for flammable liquid storage areas per NFPA 30, compressed gas storage per NFPA 55, and spray finishing operations per NFPA 33. Manage fire extinguisher inspection and hydrostatic test program. Coordinate with local Authority Having Jurisdiction (AHJ) on plan reviews, certificates of occupancy, and periodic inspections. Address fire marshal findings within required timeframes and document corrective actions.
summary: Task: Manage fire suppression systems, fire detection/alarm systems (NFPA 72), life safety compliance per NFPA 1/101/13/25, egress capacity and evacuation planning, hazardous material storage compliance, and AHJ coordination

label: HVAC and environmental controls for manufacturing
content: Design, maintain, and monitor HVAC and process ventilation systems for aerospace manufacturing environments with process-specific requirements. Maintain temperature and humidity controls for composite layup areas (typical: 65–75°F, 30–50% RH per material manufacturer specifications — verify against specific material PTDs). Manage paint booth ventilation systems for airflow velocity, exhaust rates, and make-up air per NFPA 33 and EPA permit conditions. Ensure welding fume extraction systems meet OSHA 1910.252 requirements: maintain minimum 100 fpm capture velocity at source for general welding; stainless steel and high-manganese alloys require higher capture velocities. OSHA PEL for manganese fume (1910.1000 Table Z-1) is a ceiling of 1 mg/m³ — do not conflate with ACGIH TLV-TWA of 0.2 mg/m³, which is advisory only and not an OSHA enforcement threshold. Fire extinguisher placement within 25 feet of hot-work derives from NFPA 51B; 1910.252 requires adequate suppression equipment without stating the distance directly. Provide cooling capacity for heat treat furnace areas, CNC machining coolant systems, and autoclave rooms. Calibrate and maintain building automation systems (BAS) for continuous environmental monitoring, alarm setpoints, and data logging. Validate HVAC performance after maintenance or process changes affecting heat or contamination load. Coordinate with Quality Manager when environmental excursions affect in-process material or process qualification windows.
summary: Task: Design, maintain, and monitor HVAC and environmental control systems for manufacturing facilities — covering thermal load assessment, zone design, equipment sizing and selection, process ventilation for specific manufacturing operations, IAQ compliance, part-load efficiency controls, and preventive maintenance

label: Facility expansion and modification management
content: Plan and execute facility buildouts, expansions, and modifications to support program growth and new production capabilities. Define scope, budget, and schedule for construction projects; present capital requests with ROI analysis to leadership. Manage general contractor and subcontractor selection per company procurement policy; issue RFPs, evaluate bids, and administer construction contracts. Coordinate utility upgrades (electrical service capacity, compressed air distribution, natural gas, fire suppression tie-ins, communication infrastructure) with utility providers and AHJ. Develop construction phasing plans to minimize production disruption — sequence work around production schedules, establish physical barriers, and maintain required environmental controls throughout construction. Ensure construction meets applicable building codes, OSHA 29 CFR 1926 construction safety, AHJ requirements, and DCSA physical security standards before occupancy. Maintain current as-built drawings and update facility documentation within 30 days of project closeout. Obtain all required permits and certificates of occupancy before productive use.
summary: Task: Plan and execute facility buildouts and modifications — scope, contractor management, utility upgrades, production disruption minimization, and as-built documentation

label: Utility management and energy efficiency
content: compressed air leak detection and repair surveys using ultrasonic acoustic detectors during production shutdowns; quantify system-wide leakage via bleed-down method (CAGI/Compressed Air Best Practices industry protocol — note: ISO 50001 is an organizational energy management standard unrelated to compressed air audit methods): isolate compressor, record time for pressure to decay from P1 to P2 (psig) on receiver gauge, calculate Leakage (scfm) = [V (ft³) × (P1–P2)] / [14.7 × T (min)]; target leak rate <5% of peak FAD after repair program; >10% indicates immediate repair priority
summary: Task: Monitor and optimize facility utility consumption, implement energy efficiency programs, manage backup power systems, and track utility costs by production area

label: Cleanroom and controlled environment management
content: Coordinate HEPA and ULPA filter integrity testing (aerosol photometry/DOP/PAO testing per IEST-RP-CC001) and filter replacement schedules based on pressure drop trending. NOTE on filter standards: US HEPA (MIL-STD-282) = ≥99.97% @ 0.3 µm; EN 1822 H14 = ≥99.995% @ MPPS (higher efficiency than US HEPA); ULPA (US) or U15+ (EN 1822) = ≥99.999%+ — do not equate H14 with US HEPA or label H14 as ULPA (H14 is the highest HEPA class under EN 1822; ULPA starts at U15).
summary: Task: Maintain ISO 14644 cleanroom classifications, particle count monitoring, gowning protocols, HEPA filter testing, and environmental parameter control for sensitive manufacturing processes

label: Management Review Effectiveness Criteria
content: Management review is rated effective only when ALL criteria are met: (1) ≥80% of prior review action items closed on due date with objective evidence; (2) quality objectives trend is stable or improving vs targets across ≥3 of 5 core KPIs; (3) repeat nonconformities on same clause/process reduced vs prior period; (4) customer satisfaction score stable or improved; (5) resource decisions from prior review implemented with documented outcome. Effectiveness rating scale: Effective (all criteria met), Partially Effective (3-4 criteria met — increase review frequency to quarterly), Ineffective (<3 criteria met — mandatory improvement plan within 30 days and executive escalation). Evidence package required: KPI trend charts, action item register, corrective action status, customer feedback summary. Review effectiveness determination made by Management Representative and documented in minutes.
summary: Framework for rating whether management review outputs drive measurable QMS improvement per AS9100D 9.3

label: Internal Audit Nonconformity Severity Classification
content: Classification criteria per AS9101F grading guidance: MAJOR — absence of or total breakdown of a required QMS system element; systemic failure affecting multiple occurrences or locations; direct risk to product safety or conformity; repeat minor nonconformity on same clause/process within 12 months; shipment of nonconforming product to customer. MINOR — single isolated lapse; partial or incomplete implementation not representing systemic breakdown; one instance of a documented requirement not followed with no evidence of systemic failure. OBSERVATION/OFI — practice not yet nonconforming but carrying risk if uncorrected; potential opportunity for improvement. Response timelines: Major = CAR issued within 5 business days, corrective action plan within 30 days; Minor = CAR within 10 business days, corrective action within 60 days; Observation = disposition at organization discretion. Classification authority: Lead Auditor; disputes escalated to Quality Manager. Severity drives audit follow-up interval: Major = re-audit within 6 months.
summary: Major/minor/observation classification criteria for internal audit findings per AS9100D clause 9.2 and AS9101F grading guidelines

label: Design Review Gate Approval Criteria
content: Three-gate framework per AS9100D 8.3.4: PDR (Preliminary Design Review) GO criteria — design approach selected and documented; requirements allocated to subsystems; trade studies complete with rationale; preliminary hazard/risk assessment complete; no open Severity I risks without accepted mitigation plan; customer requirements mapped to design inputs. CDR (Critical Design Review) GO criteria — detailed design complete and verified against all inputs (8.3.3); interface definitions frozen and released; stress/thermal/tolerance analyses support all critical requirements; test plans approved by customer/authority; manufacturing feasibility confirmed; DFMEA complete. QDR/TRR (Qualification/Test Readiness Review) GO criteria — first article plan approved; acceptance test procedures reviewed and approved; tooling and fixtures qualified; production processes validated or plan in place. NO-GO triggers (any one = gate hold): open Severity I finding with no accepted mitigation; ≥3 open Severity II findings without closure schedule; customer or regulatory requirements not traceable to design outputs; verification matrix <100% allocated. Conditional GO: 1-2 Severity II open items with documented owner, due date ≤30 days, and written customer concurrence.
summary: Go/no-go decision criteria for PDR, CDR, and qualification design review gates per AS9100D 8.3.4

label: Supplier Audit Frequency Determination
content: Risk-based audit frequency matrix combining criticality tier and performance score: TIER 1 CRITICAL (sole source, safety-critical parts, NADCAP-required special processes, Class I/II items) + POOR PERFORMANCE (OTD <90%, PPM >1000, or any customer quality escape in prior 12 months) = annual on-site audit. TIER 1 CRITICAL + ACCEPTABLE PERFORMANCE (OTD ≥90%, PPM ≤1000, no escapes) = biennial on-site audit. TIER 2 STANDARD (non-sole-source, non-safety-critical, standard commercial processes) + POOR PERFORMANCE = annual on-site audit. TIER 2 STANDARD + ACCEPTABLE PERFORMANCE = triennial on-site audit or annual desk audit (document review + scorecard review). Unscheduled audit triggers (any one): quality escape reaching customer; corrective action overdue >30 days; change in ownership, key personnel, processes, or facility; loss of required certification (NADCAP, AS9100D); new product/process introduction. Performance score inputs: on-time delivery (40%), incoming inspection acceptance rate (30%), customer escape rate (20%), corrective action responsiveness (10%). Acceptable performance threshold: composite score ≥80. Audit tier assignment reviewed annually at management review.
summary: Risk-based supplier audit frequency matrix considering criticality tier, delivery performance, quality escapes, and unscheduled audit triggers

label: Corrective Action Closure Verification Criteria
content: CAPA closure requires ALL of the following verified with objective evidence: (1) ROOT CAUSE VALIDATED — root cause statement supported by data (process capability data, failure analysis report, timeline analysis); if 5-Why or Ishikawa used, final cause must be verified as truly causal, not symptomatic; (2) CONTAINMENT VERIFIED — 100% inspection results or equivalent objective evidence that nonconforming product is identified and quarantined; customer notification completed if product shipped; (3) CORRECTIVE ACTION IMPLEMENTED — process change confirmed at source (updated procedure approved and in use, operator training records, equipment modification record); (4) EFFECTIVENESS CHECK PASSED — zero recurrence of same root cause during monitoring period: 30 days for Minor nonconformities, 90 days for Major or customer-impacting, 180 days for systemic/repeat issues; effectiveness check method defined upfront (sampling plan, SPC chart, audit of the corrected process); (5) LESSONS LEARNED DOCUMENTED — finding and corrective action entered into lessons learned register; related processes/PFMEA/control plans reviewed and updated if applicable; (6) QMS UPDATED — risk register, PFMEA, control plan, work instruction updated as applicable. Closure authority: Quality Engineer for minor; Quality Manager for major; Director of Quality or VP Quality for customer-escapes or safety-related. Premature closure (reopening) requires re-initiation as new CAPA with linkage to original.
summary: Criteria for verifying CAPA effectiveness and authorizing closure, including recurrence check periods and root cause validation requirements

label: Management Review Minutes Template
content: TEMPLATE SECTIONS: (1) HEADER — meeting date, location/format, attendees by name/title, next scheduled review date, minutes author; (2) INPUTS REVIEW per AS9100D 9.3.2 — each input must include quantitative trend data (rolling 12 months minimum): [a] status of actions from previous review (% complete, overdue items); [b] external/internal issue changes affecting QMS; [c] customer satisfaction and feedback (score, complaints, commendations); [d] quality objectives status (KPI vs target, trend direction); [e] process performance and product/service conformity (FPY, scrap rate, OTD); [f] nonconformities and corrective actions (volume, open aging, repeat rate); [g] monitoring/measurement results; [h] audit results (internal and external — findings, status); [i] supplier performance (OTD, incoming quality, CAPAs); [j] adequacy of resources; [k] effectiveness of actions taken on risks/opportunities; [l] opportunities for improvement; (3) DISCUSSION SUMMARY — key discussion points per input; (4) DECISIONS AND ACTIONS TABLE — columns: action description | type (QMS improvement/product improvement/resource need) | owner name/title | due date | success criteria; (5) QMS EFFECTIVENESS RATING — per effectiveness criteria decision framework; (6) NEXT REVIEW INPUTS — items to carry forward; (7) SIGNATURES — Management Representative, Top Management. Document as controlled record; retain per documented information retention schedule (minimum 3 years or contract duration).
summary: Management review minutes template covering all AS9100D 9.3.2 required inputs, effectiveness rating, decisions, and action items with owners and due dates

label: Internal Audit Report Format
content: REPORT STRUCTURE: (1) AUDIT HEADER — audit number (sequential), audit date(s), report issue date, lead auditor name/certification, audit team members, auditee organization/site, audit type (system/process/product/follow-up); (2) AUDIT SCOPE — processes audited, AS9100D clauses covered, departments/areas visited, products/contracts in scope, exclusions stated; (3) AUDIT CRITERIA — standard(s) referenced (AS9100D Rev D, internal procedures, customer requirements, regulatory requirements); (4) AUDIT SUMMARY — overall audit outcome, notable strengths, systemic observations; (5) FINDINGS TABLE — columns: finding # | clause reference | finding classification (Major/Minor/Observation) | objective evidence statement (specific record/observation/interview reference) | requirement not met (exact clause text or procedure requirement) | finding description (factual, non-prescriptive); (6) CORRECTIVE ACTION REQUIREMENTS — for each Major/Minor: CA reference number, assigned owner, required response date, verification method; (7) POSITIVE OBSERVATIONS — documented strengths and best practices; (8) AUDIT CONCLUSION — conformity statement, overall rating; (9) DISTRIBUTION LIST; (10) SIGNATURES — lead auditor, auditee representative (acknowledgment, not agreement). Objective evidence attachments: photos, copies of records reviewed (redacted as needed). Issue within 5 business days of closing meeting. Retain as quality record per 7.5.
summary: Standardized internal audit report format covering scope, criteria, classified findings with objective evidence citations, and corrective action assignments per AS9100D 9.2

label: Design Review Checklist
content: CHECKLIST STRUCTURE per AS9100D 8.3.4 and 8.3.3: Each item has: checkbox (pass/fail/N-A) | objective evidence reference | reviewer initials. SECTION A — DESIGN INPUTS VERIFIED (8.3.3): customer functional/performance requirements documented; applicable statutory/regulatory requirements identified; product safety requirements identified; interface requirements defined; design standards and codes of practice listed; lessons learned from previous designs reviewed. SECTION B — DESIGN OUTPUTS REVIEWED: drawings/models approved and at correct revision; material/process specifications called out; acceptance criteria defined for all critical characteristics; interface control documents released. SECTION C — VERIFICATION AND VALIDATION STATUS: analysis complete (stress, thermal, tolerance stack-up, fatigue as applicable); test results reviewed and acceptance criteria met; V&V matrix 100% allocated; test anomalies dispositioned. SECTION D — RISK ASSESSMENT: DFMEA/FMEA reviewed and current; Severity I/II risks dispositioned with accepted mitigation; product safety assessment updated; FHA/PSSA updated (if civil aviation); design margins documented. SECTION E — REGULATORY COMPLIANCE: applicable standards (MIL-STD, DO-178C/254, FAA TSO, etc.) verified against design; certification basis confirmed with customer/authority; export control classification reviewed. SECTION F — MANUFACTURING FEASIBILITY: producibility assessment complete; special processes identified and suppliers qualified; first article plan referenced. SECTION G — OPEN ITEMS REGISTER: item description | classification (Major Blocker/Minor/Informational) | owner | due date. Gate decision block: GO / CONDITIONAL GO (attach mitigation plan) / NO-GO. Signatures: Design Lead, Quality Representative, Customer Representative (as required).
summary: Design review checklist per AS9100D 8.3.4 covering design inputs, outputs, V&V status, risk assessment, regulatory compliance, and go/no-go gate decision

label: CAPA Log and Effectiveness Tracking Report
content: CAPA LOG FIELDS (one row per CAPA): CAPA # | date opened | source (internal audit / customer complaint / supplier issue / internal detection / management review / field failure) | nonconformity description | affected part number(s) / contract | severity (Major/Minor) | containment action | containment completion date | root cause analysis method used | verified root cause statement | corrective action description | planned implementation date | actual implementation date | effectiveness check criteria (defined at open) | effectiveness check due date | effectiveness check completion date | effectiveness check result (Pass/Fail) | recurrence detected (Y/N) | lessons learned record # | closure date | closure authority. MONTHLY TREND REPORT SECTIONS: (1) CAPA Volume — opened this period, closed this period, total open; (2) Open CAPA Aging — 0-30 days, 31-60, 61-90, >90 (>90 days = escalation required); (3) On-Time Closure Rate — actual vs ≥90% target; (4) Source Distribution — Pareto chart of CAPA sources; (5) Repeat Nonconformity Rate — CAPAs where recurrence detected ÷ total closed; target 0%; (6) Overdue Effectiveness Checks — list with owner and days overdue; (7) Top 3 Systemic Root Causes — for rolling 6-month period with frequency. Report reviewed at management review as 9.3.2(f) input. Trend data maintained for minimum 3 rolling years.
summary: CAPA log template and monthly effectiveness tracking report covering root cause, containment, corrective action implementation, recurrence verification, and trend analysis per AS9100D 10.2

label: Quality Objectives Dashboard
content: DASHBOARD LAYOUT: One KPI tile per quality objective. Each tile displays: KPI name | process owner | data source | reporting period | target | current period actual | trend indicator (improving ↑ / stable → / degrading ↓) | YTD performance | prior year baseline | color status (Green = at/above target; Yellow = within 5% below target; Red = >5% below target). STANDARD AS9100D AEROSPACE KPI SET: (1) On-Time Delivery to Customer — target ≥95%; (2) First Pass Yield (internal) — target ≥98%; (3) Customer PPM (escapes) — target ≤200 PPM; (4) Supplier On-Time Delivery — target ≥93%; (5) Supplier Incoming Acceptance Rate — target ≥99%; (6) Internal Audit Schedule Adherence — target 100%; (7) CAPA On-Time Closure Rate — target ≥90%; (8) Calibration Due Compliance — target 100%; (9) Customer Satisfaction Score — target per contract or internal benchmark; (10) Management Review Action Item Closure — target ≥80% by due date. DASHBOARD MANAGEMENT RULES: Data updated monthly, reviewed at each management review as 9.3.2(d) input; objectives reviewed for continued relevance annually; objectives that miss target for 3 consecutive periods trigger mandatory corrective action or objective revision; trend charts show minimum 12 rolling months. Dashboard owner: Quality Manager; distributed to all department heads and top management.
summary: Quality objectives dashboard with KPI tiles, targets, trend indicators, and color-coded status per AS9100D 6.2 and 9.3 management review linkage

label: Stakeholder mapping and relationship tracking
content: Map key stakeholders by account: PEOs, PMs, contracting officers, DCSA contacts, and prime BD leads. Document relationship owner, organizational role, last contact date, and relationship health score (strong/developing/gap) in CRM. Refresh quarterly and before planned engagement windows.
summary: System: Map and track key customer stakeholders with relationship health scores by account

label: Pre-solicitation engagement planning
content: Plan and schedule pre-solicitation engagement activities 12–24 months prior to anticipated RFP release: industry day attendance, Sources Sought and RFI responses, one-on-one capability briefings, and pre-proposal conference participation. Align engagement calendar to agency acquisition forecast milestones.
summary: System: Plan pre-solicitation engagement activities 12–24 months before RFP release

label: Procurement integrity compliance
content: Ensure all customer engagement activities comply with 41 USC 2102 (Procurement Integrity Act). Document all contacts with government personnel on active acquisitions in a contact log. Train BD staff on prohibited communications (source selection information, contractor bid/proposal information). Flag potential violations to legal counsel immediately.
summary: System: Enforce procurement integrity compliance per 41 USC 2102 across all customer engagement activities

label: CRM management and engagement cadence
content: Maintain CRM (Salesforce or Dynamics) with up-to-date contact database, engagement history, opportunity linkage, next-action dates, and follow-up reminders. Define tiered engagement cadence by account priority: Tier 1 accounts (monthly touchpoint), Tier 2 (quarterly), Tier 3 (semi-annual). Run weekly CRM hygiene reviews with BD team.
summary: System: Maintain CRM with tiered engagement cadence and weekly data hygiene

label: Customer meeting prioritization
content: Prioritize customer meetings based on: opportunity pipeline value, acquisition lifecycle stage (pre-RFP windows preferred), relationship gap severity, and BD/SME resource availability. Deprioritize or decline meetings on active source selections to avoid procurement integrity exposure. Approve meeting agenda and attendee mix before scheduling.
summary: Decision: Prioritize customer meetings by pipeline value, acquisition stage, and resource availability

label: Engagement resource allocation
content: Allocate BD director, capture manager, and SME time across customer accounts based on pipeline priority tier, relationship urgency, and available capacity. Balance concurrent engagement commitments against proposal and capture workload. Re-allocate resources at monthly pipeline review when new high-priority opportunities enter the pipeline.
summary: Decision: Allocate BD and SME resources across customer accounts based on pipeline priority and capacity

label: Customer engagement plan template
content: Standardized per-account engagement plan document covering: account overview and strategic importance, stakeholder map summary, relationship owner assignments, engagement objectives by stakeholder, 12-month meeting and touchpoint calendar, key messages tailored by customer segment (end user, prime, international), compliance checkpoint reminders, and success metrics (meetings held, RFI responses submitted, briefings delivered).
summary: Output: Per-account engagement plan document with stakeholder assignments, calendar, and messaging

label: Stakeholder map deliverable
content: Documented stakeholder map for each priority account: organizational chart showing decision-maker hierarchy, key influencers and gatekeepers, relationship owner assignments, relationship strength rating (strong/developing/gap), last engagement date, preferred communication channel, and coverage gap analysis identifying unstaffed or weak relationships requiring outreach.
summary: Output: Account-level stakeholder map with relationship strength ratings and coverage gap analysis

label: Competitor capability profiling
content: Build and maintain structured profiles for top 10–15 competitors by relevant NAICS/PSC codes: active contract vehicle holdings, key customer agency relationships, core technical capabilities, recent contract wins and losses (from FPDS-NG and press releases), pricing posture indicators, known teaming arrangements, and key personnel. Update profiles semi-annually and after significant competitor events (major wins, M&A, leadership changes).
summary: System: Maintain structured capability profiles for top competitors updated semi-annually

label: FPDS-NG historical award analysis
content: Query FPDS-NG systematically to extract competitor award history: total obligated value by agency, PSC, and NAICS code; contract vehicle utilization (IDIQ, BPA, GWACs); prime/subcontractor relationships; and award trend trajectories over 3–5 year windows. Identify agency spending concentration, share-of-wallet by competitor, and displacement opportunities where incumbents are losing ground.
summary: System: Query FPDS-NG to extract competitor award history, market share, and spending trends

label: Defense industry M&A tracking
content: Monitor defense industry M&A activity via SEC filings (8-K, S-4), DoD press releases, DSCA notifications, and industry reporting (Defense News, Bloomberg Government). Assess acquisition impacts: changes to competitor contract vehicle access, capability breadth, small business set-aside eligibility, and competitive positioning in segments relevant to Yggdrasil Aero. Flag material M&A events to leadership within 30 days of announcement.
summary: System: Track defense M&A activity and assess competitive impact on Yggdrasil Aero positioning

label: Competitive threat assessment and displacement prioritization
content: Determine which competitors represent strategic threats requiring active displacement campaigns versus those where coexistence or differentiation is sufficient. Criteria: degree of market overlap by agency/NAICS, relative win rate trends, customer relationship depth comparison, and M&A-driven capability shifts. Output priority tier (high/medium/low threat) for each competitor to drive BD investment allocation and capture strategy emphasis.
summary: Decision: Classify competitors as high/medium/low strategic threats to prioritize displacement campaigns

label: Semi-annual competitive landscape briefing
content: Formal semi-annual briefing to BD and executive leadership covering: market share by segment (Yggdrasil Aero vs. top competitors), competitor win/loss trend analysis (trailing 12 months), M&A activity summary and assessed impacts, emerging entrant profiles, strategic threats requiring response, and recommended BD investment priorities by market segment. Produced in June and December aligned to budget planning cycles.
summary: Output: Semi-annual leadership briefing on market share, competitor trends, M&A, and strategic threats

label: JV structure evaluation
content: Assess joint venture structural options for each large pursuit: populated JV (employees on JV payroll, CAGE code, SAM registration required) vs. unpopulated JV (pass-through arrangement, no separate workforce). Evaluate LLC formation requirements, SBA size standard applicability at JV level, performance bond obligations, and customer agency acceptance of JV prime arrangements. Document structural recommendation with legal and finance input.
summary: System: Evaluate populated vs. unpopulated JV structures for compliance, size standards, and performance obligations

label: Mentor-protege program assessment
content: Evaluate eligibility and strategic value of DoD Mentor-Protégé Program (10 USC 4902) and SBA All Small Mentor-Protégé Program for current teaming relationships. Assess developmental assistance obligations (technical, financial, management), set-aside joint venture advantages (exception to affiliation rules), and alignment to long-term BD pipeline. Coordinate with SBA district office or DoD OSBP for application requirements and approval timeline.
summary: System: Assess DoD and SBA mentor-protégé program eligibility and strategic teaming advantages

label: Teaming agreement IP and data rights negotiation
content: Negotiate IP ownership and data rights protections before any technical collaboration with teaming partners. Key provisions: background IP identification and protection, foreground IP ownership allocation, DFARS 252.227-7013/7014 data rights flow-down obligations, ITAR/EAR technology transfer restrictions and TAA/license requirements, and restrictions on partner use of shared technical data post-teaming. Coordinate all IP provisions with legal counsel before execution.
summary: System: Negotiate background/foreground IP ownership, DFARS data rights flow-down, and ITAR restrictions in teaming agreements

label: Work share allocation planning
content: Define work share percentages for each teaming partner by task area, labor category, CLIN structure, and delivery location. Align work share with: partner technical capability fit, SB subcontracting plan goals (FAR 19.7), performance risk tolerance, and cost competitiveness. Document work share matrix and obtain partner sign-off prior to proposal submission. Track actual vs. planned work share post-award for compliance and relationship management.
summary: System: Define and document partner work share percentages aligned to capability fit and SB subcontracting goals

label: JV vs. teaming agreement structure decision
content: Determine whether a large pursuit requires a formal JV entity or a prime/sub teaming agreement.
