Decision factors: set-aside type and size standard compliance requirements (SBA affiliation rules), customer preference for JV prime arrangements, performance bond and bonding capacity requirements, contract administration complexity, and duration of strategic relationship. Document rationale and obtain legal and leadership concurrence before partner discussions.
summary: Decision: Choose between JV entity formation and prime/sub teaming agreement based on set-aside, size, and performance requirements

label: Partner selection criteria application
content: Apply structured partner evaluation criteria to select teaming and JV partners: technical capability fit against opportunity requirements, past performance relevance and CPARS ratings, financial stability (Dun & Bradstreet, financial statements), security clearance posture (facility clearance level, cleared workforce size), geographic delivery presence, existing customer relationships and access, and organizational culture/BD philosophy alignment. Score candidates against weighted criteria matrix before outreach.
summary: Decision: Apply weighted partner evaluation criteria to select teaming and JV candidates

label: Teaming agreement template
content: Standard teaming agreement template with legal review incorporating: exclusivity provisions (scope and duration), work share percentage schedule, IP/background data rights protections, ITAR/EAR technology transfer restrictions, flow-down clause requirements, representations and certifications (SB status, conflicts of interest), termination for convenience and cause provisions, and dispute resolution mechanism. Reviewed and updated annually by legal counsel.
summary: Output: Standard teaming agreement template with exclusivity, work share, IP, and ITAR provisions

label: JV operating agreement framework
content: Framework JV operating agreement for defense JV formations covering: management control and governance structure (board composition, voting rights, managing member designation), profit and loss allocation methodology, capital contribution requirements and drawdown schedule, IP ownership and license-back provisions, ITAR compliance obligations and technology control plan integration, exit and dissolution provisions, and non-compete scope. Serves as starting point for legal drafting on each JV formation.
summary: Output: Framework JV operating agreement covering governance, profit allocation, IP, ITAR, and exit provisions

label: Pipeline stage-gate tracking
content: Track each active opportunity through defined pipeline stages — Identify, Qualify, Capture, Proposal, Award — with documented entry/exit criteria at each gate, responsible owner (BD director, capture manager), estimated award date, and Pwin assessment. Maintain stage-gate data in CRM with mandatory monthly refresh. Flag opportunities stalled in stage beyond velocity threshold for review at pipeline meetings.
summary: System: Track opportunities through defined pipeline stages with entry/exit criteria and monthly CRM refresh

label: Win rate analysis
content: Calculate and track win rates segmented by: opportunity type (re-compete vs. new business), competitive environment (full competition vs. sole-source), set-aside category (full and open, 8(a), SDVOSB, WOSB, HUBZone), bid value band (<$10M, $10–50M, >$50M), and customer agency. Track trailing 12-month and rolling 3-year win rates. Identify win rate trends, low-performing segments, and high-performing niches to inform future pursuit prioritization.
summary: System: Calculate segmented win rates by opportunity type, competition, set-aside, and agency with trend analysis

label: B&P spend efficiency tracking
content: Track bid and proposal (B&P) expenditures per opportunity against approved B&P budget. Calculate cost-per-bid (total B&P spend ÷ total bids submitted) and cost-per-win (total B&P spend ÷ contracts awarded) by opportunity type and value band. Monitor cumulative B&P spend vs. annual B&P budget with monthly variance reporting. Flag opportunities consuming B&P above allocated budget for BD director review and reauthorization.
summary: System: Track B&P spend per opportunity, calculate cost-per-bid and cost-per-win, and monitor budget variance

label: Pipeline coverage sufficiency decision
content: Assess at monthly pipeline review whether weighted pipeline value (pipeline value × Pwin) meets the 3–5× annual revenue coverage target. If coverage falls below 3× threshold: trigger pipeline expansion actions (qualify deferred opportunities, initiate new opportunity identification sprint, revisit deprioritized pursuits). If above 5×: assess whether B&P resources are overextended and consider raising qualification bar. Document coverage decision rationale and actions in pipeline review minutes.
summary: Decision: Determine if weighted pipeline coverage meets 3–5× revenue target and trigger expansion or tightening actions

label: No-bid recommendation trigger
content: Identify and apply criteria that trigger a no-bid recommendation for pipeline opportunities: Pwin assessment below 15–20% threshold without a clear path to improvement, B&P budget exhaustion with no reauthorization path, resource conflict with higher-priority concurrent proposals, strategic misalignment with current portfolio, or late RFP release creating insufficient proposal preparation time. Escalate no-bid recommendation to BD director gate review for final decision and documentation.
summary: Decision: Apply Pwin, resource, and strategic criteria to trigger no-bid recommendations at gate reviews

label: Monthly BD scorecard
content: Monthly dashboard report for leadership review containing: trailing 12-month win rate (overall and segmented), weighted pipeline value vs. coverage target, active pipeline count by stage, B&P spend vs. monthly and annual budget, stage conversion rates (Identify→Qualify, Qualify→Capture, Capture→Proposal, Proposal→Award), upcoming gate review schedule (next 30/60/90 days), and top 5 opportunities by weighted value. Distributed 5 business days before monthly leadership pipeline review.
summary: Output: Monthly BD scorecard with win rate, pipeline coverage, B&P spend, and stage conversion metrics

label: Pipeline coverage report
content: Quarterly pipeline coverage report providing: total pipeline value and weighted pipeline value by stage, revenue coverage ratio vs. 3–5× target with trend chart, top 10 opportunities ranked by weighted value with Pwin, responsible owner, and estimated award date, average stage dwell time (velocity analysis) vs. targets, B&P spend efficiency metrics (cost-per-bid, cost-per-win trailing 4 quarters), and resource loading assessment against concurrent proposal and capture commitments.
summary: Output: Quarterly pipeline coverage report with stage-level metrics, velocity analysis, and resource loading

label: Manage CMMS work order lifecycle for PMs and corrective work orders
content: Manage full work order lifecycle in CMMS: create and schedule preventive maintenance (PM) work orders from equipment PM plans; create corrective work orders upon failure or deficiency discovery; assign to craft technicians by skill and availability; track status through open → assigned → in-progress → completed → closed. Monitor backlog hours by craft and equipment class. Calculate MTTR from failure-reported timestamp to return-to-service timestamp. Track PM completion rate (target ≥95% on-time monthly). Flag PMs exceeding scheduled window by >7 days for supervisor escalation. Archive CMMS records to satisfy AS9100D §7.1.3 infrastructure compliance and audit traceability.
summary: System: Create, assign, track, and close PM and corrective work orders in CMMS; monitor backlog hours, PM completion rate, and MTTR by asset class

label: Capital project lifecycle management - charter through closeout
content: Manage capital improvement projects through full lifecycle: (1) Charter — define scope, objectives, ±30% order-of-magnitude budget estimate, milestone schedule, and success criteria; obtain leadership approval before spending. (2) Design — engage A/E or in-house engineering; conduct design review with facilities, production, and safety stakeholders. (3) Bid — issue RFP to qualified contractors; evaluate on price, schedule, qualifications, and safety record; award contract. (4) Execution — manage contractor on-site: daily progress tracking, RFI responses, submittal review, safety compliance. (5) Change order management — evaluate contractor change order requests for scope justification and fair pricing; log all approved changes against original budget baseline. (6) Closeout — punch list completion, commissioning/startup, as-built documentation delivered within 30 days, warranty period tracking, final cost reconciliation, and lessons learned memo filed.
summary: System: Manage capital project lifecycle from charter and design through design-bid-build execution, change order control, and formal closeout with as-built documentation

label: Prioritize capital projects using risk-weighted scoring when requests exceed annual budget
content: When capital project requests exceed available budget, score and rank using weighted risk criteria: (1) Safety/life-safety risk (30%) — imminent hazard = 10, code violation = 7, minor safety concern = 3. (2) Regulatory/compliance risk (25%) — permit violation or legal exposure = 10, agency audit finding = 6, proactive compliance = 3. (3) Production impact (25%) — single-point-of-failure on critical production equipment = 10, significant downtime risk = 6, minor inconvenience = 2. (4) Cost avoidance/ROI (20%) — payback <1 year = 10, 1–3 years = 6, >3 years = 2. Weighted score = Σ(criterion score × weight). Projects scoring ≥8.0 are mandatory regardless of budget envelope; 6.0–7.9 compete for available funds; <6.0 defer to next capital cycle. Present ranked list with scoring rationale to Operations and Finance leadership for final authorization. Document scoring basis in capital request package.
summary: Decision: Score and rank competing capital project requests on safety, regulatory, production, and ROI risk weights when total requests exceed annual capital budget

label: Determine maintenance strategy per asset - run-to-failure vs preventive vs predictive
content: Select maintenance strategy for each asset based on criticality and failure mode characteristics. RUN-TO-FAILURE (RTF): Non-critical, easily replaceable assets with low failure consequence and repair cost less than PM cost (e.g., standard light fixtures, minor HVAC components). PREVENTIVE MAINTENANCE (PM): Assets with age-related or usage-related wear failure modes; schedule based on OEM intervals adjusted by operating hours and environment (e.g., filters, belts, lubrication, rotating equipment). PREDICTIVE MAINTENANCE (PdM): Critical assets with detectable degradation signatures — apply vibration analysis for rotating machinery (pumps, motors, fans) to detect bearing wear, imbalance, and misalignment; thermography for electrical panels and connections to identify hot spots; oil analysis for gearboxes and compressors to detect wear particles and contamination. PdM justified when asset criticality is high (production stoppage risk) and inspection cost < expected avoided failure cost. Record strategy selection rationale in CMMS equipment record. Review and update strategy after any significant failure event on that asset class.
summary: Decision: Select run-to-failure, preventive, or predictive maintenance strategy per asset class based on criticality, failure mode type, and cost-benefit analysis

label: Annual facility condition assessment report with prioritized capital list and 5-year forecast
content: Produce annual Facility Condition Assessment (FCA) report documenting condition of all major facility systems: structure/envelope, HVAC, electrical distribution, plumbing, fire suppression, process utilities (compressed air, natural gas), and cranes/hoists. Rate each system on condition scale (Good/Fair/Poor/Critical) based on age, observed deficiencies, maintenance history, and remaining useful life estimate. Categorize all identified deficiencies: Immediate (safety or regulatory, fund in current year), Near-Term (significant risk within 2 years), or Planned (lifecycle replacement within 3–5 years). Apply risk-weighted scoring (see L4 decision) to rank projects within each year. Develop 5-year capital forecast with year-by-year funding requirements by system category. Include assumptions, exclusions, and escalation factors. Present to leadership with budget request justification. Distribute to Operations, Finance, and Program Management by Q4 each fiscal year.
summary: Output: Annual facility condition assessment with system-by-system condition ratings, prioritized deficiency list, and 5-year capital investment forecast

label: Monthly maintenance KPI dashboard - PM completion, backlog, MTTR, MTBF by asset class
content: Publish monthly maintenance performance dashboard covering: (1) PM Completion Rate — PMs completed on schedule ÷ total PMs scheduled (target ≥95%); (2) Corrective Work Order Backlog — open corrective WOs by craft and priority (target: zero Priority-1 backlog >48 hrs); (3) Emergency Work Percentage — unplanned/emergency WOs ÷ total WOs completed (target <10%; >20% signals PM program inadequacy); (4) MTTR by asset class — mean-time-to-repair for top 10 critical assets, tracked month-over-month; (5) MTBF by asset class — mean-time-between-failures (increasing MTBF validates PM program effectiveness); (6) Planned vs. reactive ratio (target 80:20). Flag assets with MTTR trending up or MTBF trending down as PdM candidates in dashboard notes. Deliver to Facilities Manager, Operations Manager, and Production Manager by 5th business day of the following month.
summary: Output: Monthly maintenance KPI dashboard reporting PM completion rate, backlog hours by priority, emergency work percentage, MTTR, and MTBF by asset class

label: Track HAP/VOC emissions using SDS and usage logs against permit thresholds
content: Apply source-specific transfer efficiencies per 40 CFR Part 63 Subpart GG: airless/HVLP spray 0.60–0.75; air-assist spray 0.50–0.65; electrostatic spray 0.75–0.85; hand brush/roll 0.85–0.90; dip/flow coat 0.95–0.98. Higher TE = less overspray = lower uncontrolled emissions. Verify equipment-specific TE against Subpart GG Table 3 or EPA-approved engineering data rather than using generic estimates.
summary: System: Calculate and track actual HAP/VOC emission rates by source category using SDS data and monthly material usage logs; compare against permit thresholds with rolling 12-month tracking

label: Manage air permit modification process for production changes affecting emission sources
content: When production process changes affect regulated emission sources or throughput, manage the permit modification process: (1) Identify triggering change — new coating operation, change in coating material, throughput increase, new solvent degreasing operation, or addition of emission unit. (2) Pre-modification emission analysis — calculate projected emission rate for changed operation; compare against existing permit conditions and Title V/synthetic minor source thresholds. (3) Determine modification type per L4 decision logic. (4) Prepare modification application: describe process change, provide revised emission calculations, identify applicable control requirements under 40 CFR Part 63 Subpart GG, draft proposed permit condition revisions. (5) Submit to EPA Region or state/local air district per applicable SIP procedures. (6) Implement required controls and recordkeeping before commencing modified operations, or per permit approval date if agency review required. (7) Update internal emission tracking system to reflect newly approved permit conditions. Archive application and agency correspondence per §63.755.
summary: System: Manage air permit modification application process when production changes affect regulated emission sources — impact analysis, application preparation, agency submission, and compliance implementation

label: Determine when process changes trigger Title V significant modification vs minor modification vs administrative amendment
content: Evaluate process changes against Title V permit modification thresholds under 40 CFR Part 70 and applicable state program rules. ADMINISTRATIVE AMENDMENT (self-execute, no agency approval): Typographical corrections, contact information changes, incorporation of already-approved permit changes. MINOR MODIFICATION (streamlined agency review, typically 90-day clock): Changes that do not violate applicable requirements and do not involve net significant emission increases per 40 CFR §52.21 thresholds; do not require new case-by-case MACT or BACT determination. Example: switching to a lower-HAP coating within the existing emission rate limit. SIGNIFICANT MODIFICATION (full public comment and agency review, 3–6+ months): Net emission increase ≥ significant emission rate threshold for any regulated pollutant (HAPs: 10 tpy single HAP or 25 tpy combined; criteria pollutants per applicable NSR thresholds); addition of emission unit subject to NSPS or MACT. SYNTHETIC MINOR SOURCE TRIGGER: If change would cause actual emissions to exceed major source thresholds, facility must apply for full Title V operating permit — evaluate before commencing any change that substantially increases throughput or adds regulated emission sources. Consult with environmental counsel for changes near thresholds. Document determination rationale in permit compliance file regardless of outcome.
summary: Decision: Determine whether production process changes require Title V significant modification, minor modification, or administrative amendment based on emission increase thresholds and applicable NSR/MACT requirements

label: Semi-annual NESHAP compliance certification report submitted to EPA/state agency
content: Prepare and submit semi-annual compliance certification per 40 CFR §63.753(c) for aerospace manufacturing operations subject to Subpart GG. Reporting periods: January–June (due July 31) and July–December (due January 31). REQUIRED CONTENT: (1) Facility identification, permit number, and responsible official contact. (2) Compliance status for each applicable emission limitation — coatings, adhesives, sealants, paint strippers, chemical milling maskants, and cleaning solvents — state compliant or deviation. (3) Summary of operating hours and material consumption by regulated operation category. (4) Deviations from permit conditions or applicable emission standards: description, duration, cause, and corrective action taken or planned. (5) Certification statement signed by responsible official per 40 CFR §70.5(d) attesting truth, accuracy, and completeness. Submit to EPA Regional Office and state/local air quality agency per permit-specified submission addresses. Retain copy minimum 5 years per §63.755. Coordinate with EHS Manager and legal counsel before submission.
summary: Output: Semi-annual NESHAP compliance certification per 40 CFR §63.753(c) submitted to EPA and state agency documenting compliance status, any deviations, and operating data for each permit period

label: Annual HAP/VOC emission inventory with source-level calculations and trend analysis
content: Produce annual HAP and VOC emission inventory documenting actual emissions from all regulated source categories per 40 CFR Part 63 Subpart GG and permit requirements. INVENTORY STRUCTURE: By source category (spray coating, hand-application coating, adhesive bonding, solvent degreasing, chemical milling, paint stripping) — material name, annual consumption quantity, HAP/VOC content %, transfer efficiency basis, and calculated emission (lb/yr). Aggregate to facility totals for each regulated HAP (methyl ethyl ketone, toluene, xylene, methylene chloride, etc.) and total VOC. TREND ANALYSIS: Compare current year emissions against prior 3 years by source category; identify categories with >10% year-over-year increase for root-cause review (production increase, process change, or data gap). Flag any HAP reaching 50% of major source threshold for synthetic minor source risk management. SUPPORTING DOCUMENTATION: Attach SDS references, emission factor sources, transfer efficiency basis, and monthly consumption records used in calculations. Use as basis for Title V annual emissions fee calculation. Retain per §63.755 minimum 5 years. Deliver to EHS Manager and leadership by February 28 for prior calendar year.
summary: Output: Annual HAP/VOC emission inventory with source-level calculations, 3-year trend analysis, and supporting documentation for permit compliance and Title V fee reporting

label: Manage ACS badge provisioning, access level assignments, and monthly audit trail review
content: Administer physical access control system (ACS) for classified areas, closed areas, restricted areas, and SCIFs. BADGE PROVISIONING: Process access requests with FSO verification of personnel clearance level, need-to-know, and program access authorization before granting access; document authorization in ACS and personnel security file. ACCESS LEVEL ASSIGNMENTS: Configure ACS access levels aligned to area classification tier (SCIF > closed area > restricted area > general facility); apply principle of least privilege — grant minimum access consistent with job duties. AUDIT TRAIL REVIEW: Export and review ACS access logs monthly — flag anomalies: after-hours access, ≥3 consecutive badge failures by one individual, access to unauthorized areas, or active credentials for separated employees. Revoke credentials immediately upon employee separation, clearance loss, or program reassignment. SYSTEM MAINTENANCE: Manage ACS hardware maintenance (card readers, door hardware, controllers) under service agreement; maintain redundant power for IDS/ACS per NISPOM §3.5; conduct quarterly functional test of all alarm zones. Maintain as-installed ACS/IDS documentation under configuration control; update within 30 days of any system modification.
summary: System: Administer ACS badge provisioning, access level assignments, monthly audit trail review, credential revocation, and system maintenance for classified areas per NISPOM requirements

label: Coordinate DCSA vulnerability assessment preparation and corrective action response tracking
content: Coordinate facility preparation for DCSA vulnerability assessments and periodic security inspections. PRE-ASSESSMENT PREPARATION: (1) Review prior assessment findings and confirm all corrective actions closed with supporting evidence. (2) Conduct internal pre-assessment walk-through of all classified areas — verify physical security measures (door hardware, locks, barrier integrity, IDS functionality), verify access control lists current against cleared personnel roster, verify visitor logs complete and retained. (3) Ensure as-built security drawings and ACS/IDS documentation current and available for DCSA review on day of inspection. (4) Prepare summary of program changes, facility modifications, or significant personnel changes since last assessment. (5) Brief FSO and classified area custodians on DCSA assessment scope and typical inspector interview questions. CORRECTIVE ACTION TRACKING: For each DCSA finding document: finding description, NISPOM citation, root cause, corrective action plan with owner and due date, evidence of completion. Maintain corrective action register with monthly status updates to FSO until all findings closed. Route all DCSA correspondence through FSO; Facilities Manager supports but does not independently communicate with DCSA on classified program matters.
summary: System: Coordinate pre-assessment preparation for DCSA vulnerability assessments and maintain corrective action tracking register for all security findings through closure

label: Determine closed area vs restricted area vs SCIF designation based on program classification and handling requirements
content: Determine appropriate classified area designation based on program classification level and information handling requirements per NISPOM 32 CFR Part 117 and ICD 705. RESTRICTED AREA: Appropriate where classified information is discussed or used but not stored overnight; requires physical barriers adequate to prevent inadvertent disclosure, visual access controls, and personnel access control; IDS not always required. CLOSED AREA: Required when SECRET or below classified information, material, or hardware is stored or processed; requires floor-to-true-ceiling barriers meeting NISPOM construction standards, NISPOM-compliant locking hardware, IDS with central station monitoring and documented alarm response protocol, and maintained access control list with documented need-to-know. SCIF (Sensitive Compartmented Information Facility): Required for processing, storing, or discussing SCI; requires IC Tech Spec (ICD 705) accreditation by Cognizant Security Authority (CSA) — fixed facility checklist covers construction standards (2-hour rated assemblies, acoustic and RF controls as applicable), IDS per UL 2050 contract monitoring standards, ACS, and TEMPEST requirements where applicable. Re-accreditation required after any modifications to accredited space. DECISION PROCESS: Facilities Manager and FSO jointly determine designation based on program Security Classification Guide (SCG) requirements; engage DCSA or CSA early for SCIF accreditation path. Document designation rationale in facility security plan.
summary: Decision: Determine restricted area vs. closed area vs. SCIF designation in coordination with FSO based on classification level, information handling requirements, and NISPOM/ICD 705 standards

label: Approve or deny temporary access exceptions for uncleared personnel in classified areas
content: Evaluate and approve or deny temporary access requests for uncleared personnel (maintenance vendors, contractors, inspectors, utility workers) entering classified areas, applying NISPOM escort and sanitization requirements. APPROVE when all conditions met: (1) Business necessity — documented operational requirement (equipment repair, inspection, construction) that cannot reasonably be performed outside the classified area or deferred until sanitization complete. (2) Escort availability — sufficient cleared escorts available to maintain continuous line-of-sight supervision per NISPOM §3.4 requirements (one escort per uncleared individual unless SCG specifies stricter ratio). (3) Sanitization achievable — all classified material, equipment, and media removed, covered, or secured before uncleared personnel enter; if sanitization is incomplete or impractical, DENY until achievable. (4) Duration bounded — defined start/end time; minimize time uncleared personnel are in area. DENY if: classified material cannot be fully sanitized, adequate cleared escorts unavailable, work scope requires access to classified systems or storage containers, or counterintelligence concern exists regarding the individual (coordinate with FSO). DOCUMENTATION: Log all approved temporary access — visitor name, employer, purpose, escort name(s), entry time, exit time — in area visitor log per NISPOM §3.4. Notify FSO before approving any foreign national access to classified areas (additional requirements and possible DCSA notification apply).
summary: Decision: Approve or deny temporary access for uncleared personnel to classified areas based on business necessity, escort availability, sanitization feasibility, and NISPOM compliance

label: Annual NISPOM self-inspection report with findings and corrective action plan
content: Conduct and document annual security self-inspection per NISPOM 32 CFR Part 117 §117.14(b). INSPECTION SCOPE: Physical security of all classified areas (construction integrity, lock hardware, IDS and ACS functionality and configuration, access control lists vs. cleared personnel roster); personnel security (cleared employee roster current; reinvestigation or Continuous Evaluation [CE] enrollment status — SEAD 4 [2016] replaced fixed periodic reinvestigation intervals with CE for enrolled programs; do NOT assume legacy 5-year TS / 10-year Secret intervals apply; verify current program requirements with FSO before citing specific timelines; departure debriefs completed); information security (classified material accountability, document control logs, destruction records); ACS/IDS maintenance and test records currency; visitor and escort log completeness; prior year corrective action closure verification. REPORT STRUCTURE: (1) Executive summary — overall compliance posture and significant finding count by severity. (2) Inspection methodology — areas and records inspected, inspectors' names, dates. (3) Findings — each finding with NISPOM citation, description, severity classification (critical/significant/minor), root cause, and corrective action plan with owner and target closure date. (4) Prior year finding closure confirmation with evidence references. (5) Improvement recommendations. FSO reviews and signs report; submit to DCSA if required by program. Retain minimum 3 years; provide to DCSA on request during formal assessment. Track corrective actions monthly to closure.
summary: Output: Annual NISPOM self-inspection report per §117.14(b) documenting physical, personnel, and information security compliance status with findings by severity and corrective action plan

label: Monthly security incident and access control audit report to FSO
content: Publish monthly security audit report to FSO covering: (1) ACS ACCESS AUDIT — total badge transactions by classified area; anomalies flagged (after-hours access, ≥3 consecutive failed attempts by individual, access-to-unauthorized-area attempts); active credential count vs. authorized personnel roster with identification of orphaned credentials for immediate revocation. (2) VISITOR LOG REVIEW — uncleared visitor count by area; continuous escort documentation compliance rate (target 100%); entries with incomplete documentation identified and remediated. (3) SECURITY INCIDENTS — count and description of all incidents (unauthorized access attempt, classified material mishandling, IDS false alarm, IDS alarm activation); open investigation status with estimated closure date. (4) IDS PERFORMANCE — alarm activations by zone, false alarm rate (target <3 per zone per month per UL 2050 monitoring standards), alarm response time by central station or security force. (5) CORRECTIVE ACTION STATUS — open findings from self-inspection and DCSA assessments with completion percentage and projected closure dates. Deliver to FSO by 5th business day of following month. FSO uses report as basis for monthly security posture briefing to program leadership and DCSA periodic reporting obligations.
summary: Output: Monthly security incident and access control audit report to FSO covering ACS anomalies, visitor log compliance, security incidents, IDS performance, and open corrective action status

label: Annual conference calendar planning
content: Maintain annual defense conference calendar with event dates, registration deadlines, exhibit space booking windows, and required lead times (12–18 months for major international shows). Track: AUSA Annual (October, DC), Sea-Air-Space (April, National Harbor), Paris Air Show (June, biennial, Le Bourget), DSEI (biennial, London), AFCEA TechNet (varies), and NDIA symposia. Align conference calendar to RFP forecast and customer buying cycles. Submit conference budget requests in October for the following fiscal year during annual BD planning cycle.
summary: System: Maintain and align annual defense conference calendar to BD pipeline and RFP forecast

label: Exhibit booth and capability briefing preparation
content: Manage exhibit booth design and logistics per event: booth size selection, display materials, AV equipment, demonstration hardware or scale models, and staffing plan. Develop capability briefing materials tailored to event audience—technical capability decks, program overview one-pagers, and leave-behind collateral. Align briefing themes to current pipeline priorities and key win themes. Ensure ITAR compliance review for any technical materials displayed or distributed at international venues (DSEI, Paris Air Show, IDEX) before export.
summary: System: Coordinate exhibit booth logistics and ITAR-compliant capability briefing materials per event

label: Pre-show customer meeting scheduling
content: Schedule targeted customer meetings 4–8 weeks in advance of each major show. Prioritize meetings by: pipeline value and acquisition stage, relationship gap severity, access difficulty (flag officers, SES, prime BD leads), and procurement integrity compliance (41 USC 2102—avoid scheduling with personnel on active source selections). Develop meeting agendas aligned to customer priorities and pre-solicitation engagement objectives. Assign BD and SME attendees per meeting based on account ownership and technical relevance.
summary: System: Schedule and prioritize pre-show customer meetings 4–8 weeks before each event

label: Post-show ROI assessment
content: Conduct structured ROI assessment within 30 days of each event: total meetings held vs. planned, new pipeline opportunities identified, teaming conversations initiated, relationship advancement against CRM targets, and total event cost (booth, travel, collateral, staff time). Calculate cost-per-meeting and cost-per-qualified-lead metrics. Compare against prior year event performance to inform future event prioritization. Feed ROI data into annual conference calendar planning and budget allocation decision.
summary: System: Assess post-show ROI within 30 days using cost-per-meeting and pipeline yield metrics

label: Event prioritization and budget allocation decision
content: Determine annual trade show participation portfolio and allocate B&P/marketing budget across events during annual planning cycle. Decision criteria: pipeline value associated with event audience, historical pre-show meeting scheduling success rate and cost-per-qualified-lead, alignment to BD roadmap focus areas (agency, capability domain), international market development priorities, and exhibit cost vs. expected ROI. Produce ranked event participation list with budget allocation recommendation for BD director and executive leadership approval. Document rationale for events deselected or reduced in investment.
summary: Decision: Select annual event portfolio and allocate marketing budget based on ROI and BD alignment

label: Post-show engagement report template
content: Standardized post-show engagement report template covering: event overview and total attendance summary, pre-scheduled vs. actual meetings held, meeting log (attendee names, organizations, topics discussed, action items, follow-up owner), new pipeline opportunities identified (opportunity name, agency, estimated value, next action), teaming conversations initiated, key competitive and market intelligence gathered (competitor activity observed, customer acquisition timeline signals, budget insights), total event cost summary (booth, travel, collateral, staff hours), ROI metrics (cost-per-meeting, cost-per-pipeline-lead), and recommended follow-up actions with assigned owners and due dates. Distributed to BD team within 10 business days post-event.
summary: Output: Standardized post-show engagement report with meeting log, pipeline yield, ROI metrics, and follow-up actions

label: Subcontracting plan development per FAR 19.7
content: Develop Individual Subcontracting Plans (ISP) or Master Subcontracting Plan (MSP) per FAR 19.704 for contracts >$750K (>$1.5M construction) awarded to other-than-small business primes. Required ISP elements: good-faith SB participation goals by category as dollar values and percentages, description of outreach efforts and SB source identification methods, recordkeeping and eSRS reporting procedures, subcontracting plan administrator contact, and equitable opportunity assurance. Coordinate goal-setting with contracts and procurement to ensure goals are realistic and defensible. Submit ISP with proposal or negotiate post-award per contracting officer direction.
summary: System: Develop FAR 19.704-compliant Individual and Master Subcontracting Plans with SB participation goals

label: eSRS reporting (ISR and SSR)
content: Submit required subcontracting reports in Electronic Subcontracting Reporting System (eSRS) per FAR 52.219-9: Individual Subcontract Reports (ISR) semi-annually (due October 30 and April 30) covering SB spend by category during the reporting period, and Summary Subcontract Reports (SSR) annually (due October 30) covering aggregate SB subcontracting across all applicable contracts. Coordinate with subcontracting plan administrator for accurate spend data from accounts payable. Obtain contracting officer acknowledgment in eSRS within required timelines. Retain eSRS submission records for 3 years per FAR 4.703.
summary: System: Submit semi-annual ISR and annual SSR in eSRS per FAR 52.219-9 reporting requirements

label: SB goal tracking by category (SDB, WOSB, HUBZone, VOSB, SDVOSB)
content: Track actual SB subcontracting spend vs. planned goals monthly by category on all contracts with active subcontracting plans: Small Business (SB), Small Disadvantaged Business (SDB), Women-Owned Small Business (WOSB), HUBZone Small Business, Veteran-Owned Small Business (VOSB), and Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Business (SDVOSB). Source spend data from accounts payable and purchase order systems. Maintain cumulative totals and percent-of-goal achieved per contract and in aggregate. Flag shortfalls >5% below annualized pace-to-goal for corrective action initiation.
summary: System: Monthly tracking of actual vs. planned SB spend by all six SB categories across active subcontracting plans

label: Mentor-protege SB credit tracking
content: Track developmental assistance costs and associated SB subcontracting credit under active DoD Mentor-Protégé Program (10 USC 4902) or SBA All Small Mentor-Protégé agreements. Document eligible assistance types: technical and management consulting, property or equipment transfer, employee training, financial assistance, and trade education. Calculate SB credit amounts applicable against active subcontracting plan goals per program agreement terms. Submit annual mentor-protégé progress reports to OSBP (DoD) or SBA district office. Coordinate credit calculation methodology with contracting officer and program office.
summary: System: Track mentor-protégé developmental assistance costs and calculate applicable SB subcontracting plan credit

label: SB goal shortfall corrective action decision
content: Determine corrective action when SB subcontracting goal shortfall is identified (>5% below annualized pace-to-goal in any category). Evaluate root cause: lack of capable SB sources in category, price differentials pricing out SB firms, SB subcontractor performance issues, or procurement planning failures. Select corrective action path: identify and qualify new SB subcontractors in deficient category, increase work share allocation to existing SB subcontractors, conduct targeted SB outreach (matchmaking events, SBA database searches), or adjust upcoming task order procurement strategy to direct work to SB firms. Document corrective action plan with committed actions, responsible owner, and target recovery date. Notify contracting officer if shortfall risk is material to eSRS reporting period.
summary: Decision: Identify root cause of SB goal shortfall and select corrective action path with documented commitments

label: Individual subcontracting plan template
content: Standard Individual Subcontracting Plan (ISP) template per FAR 52.219-9 covering: applicable contract identification (contract number, agency, period of performance, total estimated value, applicable threshold), SB participation goals by category (SB, SDB, WOSB, HUBZone, VOSB, SDVOSB) as estimated dollar values and percentages of total planned subcontracting, description of SB subcontracting program and outreach procedures (source lists, trade associations, SBA databases, matchmaking), subcontracting plan administrator and SB liaison officer contact information, recordkeeping and eSRS reporting procedures (ISR/SSR dates and system), equitable opportunity assurance statement, and flow-down requirements to first-tier subcontractors above applicable thresholds. Reviewed by legal and contracts annually for FAR regulatory currency.
summary: Output: FAR 52.219-9-compliant ISP template with goal tables, outreach procedures, and reporting obligations

label: SB utilization dashboard
content: Real-time SB utilization dashboard tracking across all contracts with active subcontracting plans: actual vs. planned spend by SB category (SB, SDB, WOSB, HUBZone, VOSB, SDVOSB) with percent-of-goal achieved, annualized pace-to-goal analysis, top SB subcontractors by spend (name, SB certification category, obligated amount, performance status), contracts flagged at shortfall risk (>5% below pace-to-goal), eSRS report submission status and upcoming due dates (ISR/SSR), and open corrective action items with owner and target date. Refreshed monthly from accounts payable data. Accessible to BD director, subcontracting plan administrator, and contracts team.
summary: Output: Monthly SB utilization dashboard with pace-to-goal tracking, risk flags, and eSRS compliance status

label: Gate review process management (Gate 1, 2, 3)
content: Administer formal three-gate review process for all qualified pipeline opportunities. Gate 1 (Qualify/No-Bid): assess strategic fit, initial Pwin estimate, customer relationship status, and resource availability before committing capture resources—held at Identify-to-Qualify stage transition. Gate 2 (Capture Strategy Approval): review and approve capture plan, customer intelligence quality, competitive positioning, and win strategy before proposal kickoff—held 90–120 days before RFP release. Gate 3 (Proposal Ready/B&P Authorization): confirm proposal resource allocation, B&P budget approval, and final bid/no-bid before proposal submission—held within 10 days of RFP receipt. Maintain 90-day forward-looking gate review schedule. Document all gate decisions with rationale, conditions, and responsible owner in CRM.
summary: System: Administer three-gate review schedule with defined criteria, timing, and CRM-documented decisions

label: Color team strategic review
content: Conduct BD director-level strategic review of color team outputs to ensure proposal themes and discriminators reflect BD-developed customer intelligence—distinct from proposal_mgr execution focus on compliance. Review Pink Team outputs (storyboards) for alignment to customer priorities and competitive positioning established during capture. Review Red Team draft for strategic gaps beyond compliance deficiencies: win theme strength, discriminator substantiation, and competitive differentiation. Provide BD director written strategic guidance to proposal team before Gold Team. Ensure win themes are grounded in documented customer engagement data and competitive intelligence, not generic marketing language.
summary: System: BD director strategic review of Pink/Red/Gold color team outputs for intelligence-grounded win theme alignment

label: Cross-proposal resource conflict resolution
content: Identify and resolve resource conflicts between concurrent proposal efforts: SME availability (engineers, technical leads), proposal writer and editor capacity, pricing analyst bandwidth, and capture manager assignments. Maintain cross-proposal resource loading chart updated weekly during peak proposal periods (when three or more proposals are active concurrently). Apply triage criteria when conflicts are irresolvable within BD: prioritize by opportunity strategic value (weighted pipeline value), proposal due date proximity, and Pwin differential. Escalate unresolvable conflicts to VP/EVP level with documented trade-off options and BD director recommendation.
summary: System: Weekly cross-proposal resource loading tracking with triage criteria and executive escalation path

label: Lessons learned collection
content: Conduct structured lessons-learned sessions within 30 days of each proposal award or loss decision. Solicit input from BD director, capture manager, proposal manager, pricing lead, and volume leads. Document: what worked well in capture and proposal execution, process breakdowns and root causes, intelligence gaps that affected win strategy, competitive factors influencing award, debrief findings (for losses—request formal debrief per FAR 15.506 within allowable period), and specific process improvement recommendations. File records in BD knowledge base indexed by opportunity type, agency, competition type, and outcome. Review aggregate lessons quarterly to identify recurring themes and update gate review criteria.
summary: System: Structured post-award/post-loss lessons-learned sessions with BD knowledge base filing and quarterly trend review

label: Bid/no-bid decision at Gate 1
content: Make final bid/no-bid decision at Gate 1 review based on: strategic alignment to portfolio growth objectives, Pwin estimate vs. no-bid threshold (typically <15–20% without clear improvement path), competitive landscape assessment (incumbent strength, known competitors), capture resource availability vs. concurrent pipeline workload, B&P budget availability for opportunity value band, and customer relationship status (intelligence access, engagement quality). Document Gate 1 decision with rationale and conditions in CRM. No-bid decisions require follow-on assessment: monitor for future re-compete, identify relationship development needed to improve position, or close from pipeline. BD director signature required on Gate 1 record.
summary: Decision: Final bid/no-bid determination at Gate 1 with documented Pwin, resource, and strategic rationale

label: B&P authorization decision
content: Authorize bid and proposal (B&P) budget at Gate 3 review for opportunities advancing to proposal submission. Authorization factors: validated Pwin above investment threshold, strategic priority tier, estimated proposal complexity and resource requirements (labor hours by function, subcontractor quote costs, travel for site visits or oral presentations), available B&P budget vs. concurrent proposal commitments, and anticipated award timing vs. revenue gap impact. Issue written B&P authorization memo with approved budget ceiling, responsible BD director, tracking code, and reauthorization trigger threshold (if proposal scope expands >20%). Unauthorized B&P expenditure above approved ceiling requires BD director approval prior to commitment.
summary: Decision: Authorize B&P budget ceiling at Gate 3 with written memo, ceiling amount, and reauthorization trigger

label: Gate review briefing template
content: Standardized gate review briefing template (slide deck) covering: opportunity overview (agency, program name, estimated value, set-aside type, contract vehicle, anticipated RFP date, NAICS/PSC), strategic alignment assessment (portfolio fit, growth objective supported), Pwin assessment with supporting rationale (customer access quality, incumbent position, competitive landscape, key discriminators), resource requirements and B&P budget estimate, capture activities completed and planned (Gates 2 and 3), win strategy and key discriminators (Gates 2 and 3), color team findings summary (Gate 3), B&P spend to date vs. authorized (Gate 3), and recommended gate decision with any conditions attached. Template maintained in BD shared drive; completed by capture manager and reviewed by BD director before each formal gate review meeting.
summary: Output: Standardized gate review briefing template covering Pwin, strategy, resources, and gate decision recommendation

label: Lessons learned report
content: Standardized lessons-learned report template for post-award and post-loss documentation: opportunity identification and background summary, capture approach assessment (customer access achieved, intelligence quality, win strategy effectiveness), proposal execution assessment (schedule adherence, quality, resource adequacy, color team findings vs. final submission), pricing and competitive assessment (price-to-win accuracy, competitor pricing gap if disclosed), competitive outcome analysis (debrief findings per FAR 15.506, award rationale, technical/past performance evaluation results), process improvement recommendations by function (capture, proposal, pricing, teaming) with specific corrective actions, and owner assignments with target implementation dates. Stored in BD knowledge base by agency, opportunity type, and outcome. Reviewed at quarterly BD process improvement meeting to identify recurring themes.
summary: Output: Post-award/post-loss lessons-learned report with debrief findings, root cause analysis, and process improvement actions

label: SAM.gov alert configuration and monitoring
content: Configure and maintain automated SAM.gov opportunity alerts filtered by: NAICS codes relevant to Yggdrasil Aero portfolio, PSC codes (product and service codes), primary target agencies (NAVAIR, AFLCMC, Army AMC, SOCOM, MDA, and others), set-aside types (full and open, 8(a), SDVOSB, WOSB, HUBZone), and contract vehicle types (IDIQ, GWAC, BPA). Review daily SAM.gov alert digest and triage new postings: pre-solicitation notices, Sources Sought, RFIs, draft RFPs, and final RFPs. Log all relevant opportunities in pipeline CRM tracker within 24 hours of posting with initial fit assessment (NAICS/capability alignment, set-aside eligibility, size and complexity). Assign preliminary owner for further qualification.
summary: System: Configure SAM.gov automated alerts by NAICS/PSC/agency and triage new postings within 24 hours

label: FPDS-NG re-compete pipeline identification
content: Query FPDS-NG systematically to identify expiring contracts in Yggdrasil Aero target market segments 18–24 months prior to contract end. Search by: agency, NAICS/PSC code, contract type (IDIQ, FFP, CPFF), period of performance end date, and estimated value. Extract incumbent contractor, contract number, total obligated value, performance period history, and option exercise pattern. Cross-reference with SAM.gov for posted re-compete solicitations or pre-solicitation notices. Prioritize re-compete opportunities where: incumbent shows performance vulnerabilities (indicated by CPARS ratings or news), contract is approaching ceiling, or agency budget exhibits show increased program investment in the out-years.
summary: System: Systematic FPDS-NG queries to identify expiring incumbent contracts 18–24 months forward for re-compete pipeline

label: USASpending.gov budget trend analysis
content: Analyze agency spending trends using USASpending.gov to validate pipeline opportunity sizing and identify emerging investment areas. Extract: agency obligation trends by NAICS/PSC code over a 3–5 year window, prime vs. subcontract award patterns, geographic concentration of awards, top prime contractors by agency and category, and award types (new contracts vs. modifications vs. task orders). Identify agencies increasing investment in Yggdrasil Aero capability areas (expanding addressable market) and agencies with declining budgets (deprioritize pursuit resources). Update analysis semi-annually aligned to President's Budget submission (February) and Congressional appropriation cycles.
summary: System: Semi-annual USASpending.gov analysis of agency obligation trends by NAICS/PSC to validate pipeline sizing

label: Agency P-40, R-2, O-1 budget exhibit review
content: Review DoD budget justification exhibits submitted with the President's Budget (released February each year) to identify program-level investment visibility: P-40 (Aircraft Procurement justification sheets by service), R-2 (RDT&E Program Element exhibit with project descriptions and funding profiles), and O-1 (Operation and Maintenance exhibit by program). Extract: program name, program element number (PE#), appropriation account, FY budget request and FYDP out-year funding profile, program description and acquisition milestone schedule. Cross-reference with SAM.gov and FPDS-NG data to validate pipeline opportunity sizing and anticipated RFP release timing. File exhibits in program intelligence database organized by service branch, agency, and program element.
summary: System: Annual review of P-40/R-2/O-1 budget exhibits to extract program-level funding visibility and RFP timing indicators

label: Opportunity qualification and pursuit prioritization decision
content: Apply structured qualification criteria to advance raw opportunities from Identify to Qualify stage and rank active pursuits. Criteria: strategic alignment (NAICS/PSC fit, target agency priority, technical capability match), opportunity value vs. B&P investment required (minimum deal size threshold by company revenue band), competitive landscape (ability to compete, incumbent advantage severity, Yggdrasil Aero differentiation), customer relationship status (access quality, existing intelligence), and pipeline coverage need (fill gap by stage, value band, or agency). Score each opportunity against weighted criteria matrix. Advance to Qualify only if minimum threshold score is met. Document qualification rationale in CRM with scoring results, next-action owner, and 30-day action deadline. Review and re-prioritize ranking at monthly pipeline meeting.
summary: Decision: Score and qualify raw opportunities against weighted criteria; rank active pursuits for monthly pipeline prioritization

label: Opportunity assessment report template
content: Standardized opportunity assessment report template for Gate 1 pipeline review covering: opportunity identification data (SAM.gov notice number if posted, agency, program name, NAICS/PSC codes, set-aside type, estimated value range, anticipated RFP release date), program background (mission need, acquisition history, current incumbent with contract value from FPDS-NG, relevant budget exhibit reference), strategic alignment assessment (technical capability fit, portfolio growth objective alignment), competitive landscape (incumbent CPARS indicators, estimated Pwin relative to incumbent, potential competitors with contract vehicle access), Yggdrasil Aero positioning (most relevant past performance references, technical discriminators, gaps requiring teaming), initial Pwin estimate with supporting rationale, estimated B&P investment required by phase, and recommended pursuit decision (pursue/monitor/no-bid) with conditions and next-action steps. Completed for every opportunity advancing to Gate 1; maintained in BD shared pipeline library.
summary: Output: Standardized Gate 1 opportunity assessment report with program background, competitive landscape, Pwin, and pursuit recommendation

label: Manage fire suppression system ITM program per NFPA 25
content: Schedule and track inspection, testing, and maintenance (ITM) for all fire suppression systems per NFPA 25. QUARTERLY: wet pipe system gauges and waterflow alarm devices; dry pipe low-point drum drains and air pressure; sprinkler head visual inspection. SEMI-ANNUAL: antifreeze system specific gravity testing; standpipe hose stations. ANNUAL: full internal wet pipe system inspection; obstruction investigation; fire pump flow test at churn, rated, and peak load per NFPA 25 §8.3; backflow preventer test. DEFICIENCY TRACKING: maintain deficiency log per system with location, description, severity (imminent-hazard impairment vs. non-critical), date found, corrective action due date, and closure date. Prioritize impairments requiring immediate correction vs. items correctable at next scheduled service. IMPAIRMENT NOTIFICATION: when any suppression system is taken offline for maintenance, issue formal impairment notification to: building occupants, local fire department (AHJ notification per NFPA 25 §15.5), insurance carrier, and monitoring center. Implement fire watch per NFPA 25 §15.5.2 for impairments exceeding 10 hours or overnight. Retain all ITM records for AHJ audit access and insurance compliance.
summary: System: Schedule quarterly/semi-annual/annual NFPA 25 ITM inspections, track deficiencies, and manage impairment notifications when suppression systems are taken offline

label: Conduct fire risk assessments for hazmat storage areas per NFPA 30/55/33
content: Conduct fire risk assessments for areas storing or using hazardous materials, applying applicable NFPA codes by hazard type. FLAMMABLE/COMBUSTIBLE LIQUIDS (NFPA 30): verify flammable liquid cabinet loading does not exceed 60 gallons combined Class I/II per cabinet per NFPA 30 §9.5.1.1; confirm minimum separation from ignition sources; verify proper vent configuration; assess dispensing area drainage and bonding/grounding for flammable liquid transfer; evaluate sprinkler protection adequacy against pool fire scenarios. COMPRESSED GAS CYLINDERS (NFPA 55): verify cylinder restraint within 12 inches of top per NFPA 55 §5.1.5; verify incompatible gas segregation (oxidizers from flammables by ≥20 ft or 1-hour fire-rated barrier per NFPA 55 §8.1.10); verify permitted quantities are not exceeded. SPRAY FINISHING (NFPA 33): verify spray booth exhaust velocity meets minimum 100 fpm average with no single point <25% of average per NFPA 33 §7.4; verify fire suppression coverage inside booth and over spray-wetted surfaces; verify interlock prevents spray gun operation when airflow is inadequate. Document each assessment: findings, corrective actions required, responsible party, and due date. Reassess after any layout change, new chemical introduction, or AHJ finding.
summary: Procedure: Fire risk assessments for flammable liquid cabinets (NFPA 30), compressed gas cylinder restraints (NFPA 55), and spray booth suppression adequacy (NFPA 33)

label: Determine when facility modifications trigger AHJ plan review and new certificate of occupancy vs. minor modification notification
content: Evaluate modification scope to determine required AHJ engagement level before commencing construction or system changes. NEW CERTIFICATE OF OCCUPANCY REQUIRED (full AHJ plan review and inspection): change in occupancy classification (e.g., adding hazardous occupancy per IBC Chapter 4); increase in occupant load requiring egress system modification; additions increasing building area; installation of new fire suppression or alarm systems (new coverage zones, not in-kind replacements); changes affecting means of egress (relocating exit doors, changing exit travel distance); construction of new hazardous material storage areas meeting H-occupancy thresholds. MINOR MODIFICATION (notify AHJ, streamlined review): in-kind replacement of fire suppression or alarm components at identical location and coverage; tenant improvements not affecting occupancy classification or egress; HVAC changes not affecting egress or rated assemblies. NO AHJ NOTIFICATION REQUIRED: like-for-like equipment replacement with identical specifications; routine maintenance on permitted systems. DECISION RULE: any modification touching fire suppression system coverage zones, egress paths, occupancy classification, or occupant load requires AHJ pre-submittal consultation before committing to scope. Pre-submittal meeting prevents costly redesign when AHJ interpretation differs from in-house assessment. Document AHJ determination (including verbal guidance) in project compliance file.
summary: Decision: Determine whether facility modifications require new certificate of occupancy and full AHJ plan review vs. minor modification notification vs. no AHJ action

label: Annual fire protection system inspection summary report with deficiency tracking and remediation status
content: Compile annual fire protection system inspection summary consolidating all ITM results for the calendar year. REPORT STRUCTURE: (1) System inventory — all suppression systems with type, coverage area, last full inspection date, inspection contractor, and NFPA 25 compliance status. (2) Deficiency log summary — all deficiencies identified during the year: impairment vs. non-critical, severity (immediate hazard, corrective action recommended, or observe until next inspection), system affected, date identified, corrective action taken, and closure date. (3) Impairment record — all system impairments: duration, notification documentation, fire watch records, and restoration confirmation. (4) Open deficiencies — items not yet corrected with current status and projected closure date. (5) Fire drill compliance — drill dates, participation records, and any deficiencies in drill execution. (6) AHJ correspondence — certificates of inspection, plan approvals, fire marshal findings with corrective action status. (7) Insurance-required documentation — any carrier-specified test results or certifications. DISTRIBUTION: Risk Manager, Insurance Carrier (as required), Operations Leadership, FSO (for classified area systems). Retain in facilities compliance records minimum 10 years.
summary: Output: Annual fire protection inspection summary consolidating all NFPA 25 ITM results, deficiency log, impairment records, drill compliance, and AHJ correspondence

label: Monitor and validate process-critical environmental conditions via building automation system (BAS)
content: Configure and operate building automation system (BAS) for continuous environmental monitoring in process-qualified manufacturing areas. ALARM SETPOINTS: Configure alarms at ±2°F and ±3% RH inside validated process specification limits to provide response time before spec boundary is reached; do not set alarms at the spec limit edge. DATA LOGGING: 15-minute intervals for temperature and humidity in standard process areas; 5-minute intervals for cleanroom differential pressure. ESCALATION PROTOCOL: first alarm → area supervisor notification within 5 minutes; sustained excursion >30 minutes → automatic notification to Facilities Manager and Quality Manager. COMPOSITE LAYUP AREAS: typical alarm band 68–77°F, 32–52% RH (verify against specific material PTDs — these values are not universal); validate sensors quarterly against NIST-traceable reference (tolerance ±0.5°F, ±1.5% RH). CLEANROOM: monitor differential pressure continuously; alarm at ±0.005 in. w.c. below minimum positive pressure specification. BAS VALIDATION: after any BAS modification or sensor replacement, conduct functional verification by introducing controlled environmental change and confirming alarm triggers within specified response time. Archive BAS data logs minimum 3 years; deliver monthly excursion summary to Quality Manager for process qualification records.
summary: System: Configure BAS alarm setpoints, data logging intervals, and escalation protocols for composite layup and cleanroom areas; validate sensors quarterly

label: Manage paint booth and process ventilation compliance per NFPA 33 and OSHA requirements
content: Maintain spray finishing and chemical process ventilation systems in continuous NFPA 33 and OSHA compliance. AIRFLOW VELOCITY VERIFICATION (quarterly per NFPA 33 §15.1.4): conduct face velocity traverse using calibrated anemometer across spray booth face; calculate average velocity (minimum 100 fpm per NFPA 33); identify any single-point readings below 25% of average. Document results in booth log with technician, date, instrument ID, and readings. If average velocity below minimum — stop spray operations immediately; diagnose and correct (filter loading, fan belt, damper) before resuming. FILTER CHANGEOUT: track static pressure differential across intake filters via magnehelic gauge; trigger changeout at manufacturer-specified threshold (typically 0.5–1.0 in. w.c.) regardless of calendar schedule. Maintain filter changeout log per EPA permit requirements. CAPTURE VELOCITY DOCUMENTATION (OSHA compliance): for welding fume extraction and chemical process exhaust, document design capture velocity and measured actual velocity at source quarterly; retain records per OSHA 1910.1000 documentation basis. If measured capture velocities fall below OSHA-required minimums, coordinate with EHS Manager — evaluate whether to stop operations pending repair or implement supplemental controls. File annual verification results in permit compliance records.
summary: System: Quarterly airflow velocity verification per NFPA 33, filter changeout scheduling by pressure differential, and OSHA capture velocity documentation for paint booths and process exhaust

label: Decide whether environmental excursion invalidates in-process material or requires engineering disposition
content: When BAS alarm indicates environmental excursion in a process-critical area, evaluate whether in-process materials are affected and determine appropriate response. EVALUATION CRITERIA: (1) Excursion magnitude — measure maximum deviation from specification limits; minor excursion (<2°F or <5% RH beyond alarm setpoint) vs. significant (>5°F or >10% RH beyond spec limit). (2) Duration — short (<15 min for composite layup areas with high material mass buffering) vs. sustained (>30 min or overnight). (3) Material state at time of excursion — open/uncured state (highest sensitivity), bagged but not yet cured (moderate sensitivity), already cured/processed (typically unaffected by post-cure variation). (4) Material specification — review specific material PTD for storage and process environment requirements; apply material-specific out-of-temperature or out-of-humidity criteria where defined. DECISION PATHS: Excursion entirely within spec limits → no action; document in BAS log for traceability. Brief minor excursion → Quality Manager review; document; typically continue processing with traceability note. Significant or sustained excursion → stop production in affected area; Quality Manager and Engineering joint disposition; evaluate material against PTD criteria; options: accept with documented rationale, scrap and replace, or request manufacturing deviation. Excursion during active cure cycle → Engineering required; thermal analysis may be needed to assess cure kinetics impact. Never disposition process-qualified material unilaterally — Quality Manager concurrence required on all non-trivial excursions.
summary: Decision: Evaluate excursion magnitude, duration, and material state against PTD criteria to determine accept/continue, Quality Manager review, or Engineering disposition path

label: Monthly environmental monitoring report for process-critical areas with temperature, humidity, and differential pressure trending and excursion log
content: Publish monthly environmental monitoring report for all process-critical manufacturing areas. REPORT CONTENT BY AREA: for each monitored area (composite layup, cleanroom, paint booth, other process-qualified spaces): (1) Temperature trending — monthly min/max/average with plot against specification limits; excursion events with timestamp, magnitude, duration, and disposition. (2) Humidity trending — same format as temperature. (3) Differential pressure (cleanroom) — monthly average and minimum readings; any loss-of-positive-pressure events with investigation notes. (4) BAS sensor calibration status — due dates; sensors past due flagged red. (5) Excursion log — table of all excursions: area, parameter, specification limit, actual value, duration, root cause, material disposition decision, corrective action to prevent recurrence. (6) Filter status — paint booth pressure differential readings with trend; changeout events during month. (7) Ventilation test results — if quarterly velocity test conducted during month, include results. DISTRIBUTION: Quality Manager (process qualification records), Operations Manager, EHS Manager, Program Management (for material traceability on excursion events). Retain minimum 3 years; retain longer if tied to material qualification records required by contract.
summary: Output: Monthly environmental monitoring report with temperature/humidity/differential pressure trending, BAS sensor status, and excursion log with material disposition records

label: Manage construction phasing plans to minimize production disruption
content: Develop and execute construction phasing strategy that maintains production continuity throughout facility modifications. PHASING PLAN DEVELOPMENT: before construction, map work zones against production floor plan; identify all operations within or adjacent to construction zone; sequence phases to isolate construction from production in distinct zones. PHYSICAL BARRIERS: install temporary walls and hoarding with sealed penetrations preventing dust, debris, and contaminant migration into production; barrier placement requires Quality Manager review for composite layup or cleanroom adjacency. When construction is adjacent to positive-pressure controlled areas, increase BAS differential pressure monitoring frequency to hourly during active adjacent construction. UTILITY TIE-IN COORDINATION: identify all planned utility interruptions (electrical, compressed air, HVAC, fire suppression impairments) minimum 3 weeks in advance; coordinate with Production Manager to schedule interruptions during production holds, planned maintenance windows, or off-shift periods; issue formal notification to all affected departments ≥5 business days before each interruption. SEQUENCING RULES: schedule noisiest and dustiest work (concrete cutting, demolition, major rough-in) during nights and weekends when production is offline; schedule finish work and commissioning during production hours; maintain at least one clear production access aisle through any construction area at all times. Review phasing schedule with Production Manager at weekly operations meeting throughout construction.
summary: System: Develop construction phase sequencing, establish physical barriers, coordinate utility tie-in timing with Production, and maintain environmental controls during construction

label: Contractor safety and security management during facility construction
content: Pre-qualify, onboard, and manage contractor safety and security compliance throughout construction projects. PRE-QUALIFICATION: verify 3-year OSHA 300 EMR ≤1.0; active certificates of insurance (general liability and workers' compensation at contract minimums); OSHA 10/30 certification for foreperson/superintendent. For classified area proximity: verify no employees on DCSA debarment lists; coordinate clearance requirements with FSO before contract award. SAFETY ORIENTATION (required before first day on site): facility-specific hazards (flammable storage locations, pressurized systems, crane load zones, classified area boundaries), emergency procedures (evacuation routes, muster points, spill response), hot work permit process, LOTO requirements when working near energized production systems. DAILY SITE MANAGEMENT: conduct morning safety tailgate with contractor crews (10 min); verify hot work permits issued and fire watch in place before any welding, cutting, or grinding. Monitor barrier integrity daily — stop work if dust or debris containment is compromised. HOT WORK: issue permit with pre-work inspection checklist; post fire watch during work and for 30 minutes after; fire extinguisher at point of work; prohibit hot work within 35 ft of flammable liquid storage. CLASSIFIED AREA PROXIMITY: enforce physical exclusion zones around classified areas; escort all uncleared contractor personnel continuously per NISPOM §3.4 requirements; log contractor access in classified area visitor log; coordinate with FSO for any work requiring entry into or immediately adjacent to classified areas.
summary: Procedure: Contractor pre-qualification (EMR, COI), safety orientation, daily tailgate management, hot work permit enforcement, and NISPOM escort requirements for classified area proximity

label: Decide build-new vs. renovate-existing based on total cost of ownership, production disruption risk, timeline constraints, and regulatory compliance path
content: Evaluate build-new versus renovate-existing for significant facility capability additions using a structured multi-factor analysis. (1) TOTAL COST OF OWNERSHIP: Build-new — full site development, design fees, construction; lower long-term operating cost from modern energy-efficient systems; clean regulatory baseline. Renovate — design and preconstruction assessment, construction typically 70–85% of equivalent new construction per sq ft, plus production disruption cost (disrupted days × daily production value), plus potential triggered code upgrades (ADA, seismic, energy code on existing non-complying systems). Older facility renovation frequently triggers costly compliance upgrades to existing conditions. (2) PRODUCTION DISRUPTION RISK: Renovation carries months-to-years of concurrent construction-production risk; build-new preserves production continuity until a single planned cutover event. Quantify: expected disrupted production days × daily loaded production cost. In aerospace LRIP, this factor frequently dominates the decision for moderate-scale additions. (3) TIMELINE: Build-new typically 18–36 months (design + permitting + construction); renovation may be faster or slower depending on site constraints and triggered upgrade scope. Evaluate against program delivery commitments. (4) REGULATORY COMPLIANCE: Renovation of older buildings can trigger full code compliance upgrade on existing systems regardless of renovation scope — assess triggered improvement cost before assuming renovation is less expensive. (5) SECURITY/CLASSIFICATION: For classified areas, build-new provides a clean DCSA accreditation path and avoids disruption to existing accredited spaces; renovation requiring modification of accredited spaces triggers re-accreditation. Present analysis to leadership as scored decision matrix with factor weighting documented.
summary: Decision: Multi-factor build-new vs. renovate analysis covering TCO, production disruption cost, timeline, triggered regulatory upgrades, and classified area accreditation path

label: Facility modification project closeout package — as-built drawings, commissioning records, final cost reconciliation, warranty documentation, and lessons learned
content: Compile and distribute complete project closeout package within 30 days of substantial completion. PACKAGE CONTENTS: (1) AS-BUILT DRAWINGS — contractor redline drawings showing actual installed conditions for all systems (architectural, structural, mechanical, electrical, fire suppression, security/ACS); verify completeness against contract scope before accepting from contractor; update CAD master facility drawings; file in facility document control under project number. (2) COMMISSIONING AND STARTUP RECORDS — all system startup reports: HVAC air balance per ASHRAE, electrical testing records, fire suppression acceptance test per NFPA 13/25, clean agent system test certificate, cleanroom certification if applicable; document all commissioning setpoints that differ from design basis with engineering justification. (3) PERMITS AND CERTIFICATES OF OCCUPANCY — all pulled permits with inspection sign-off, certificate of occupancy, AHJ correspondence, conditional approvals with evidence of conditions cleared. (4) FINAL COST RECONCILIATION — original budget vs. final cost by CSI division; approved change orders itemized with justification; owner-directed vs. contractor-initiated changes; variance explanation to Finance and leadership. (5) WARRANTY REGISTER — each system and major equipment item: description, warranty start and end dates, warranty scope, contractor/vendor contact, and claim procedure. Flag for 11-month warranty walkthrough before first-year expiration. (6) LESSONS LEARNED MEMO — construction schedule vs. actual, production disruption vs. planned, budget variance, quality issues encountered, contractor performance assessment, and recommendations for future similar projects. File in facilities project archive; distribute to Facilities Manager, Operations, Finance, and Program Management.
summary: Output: Project closeout package with as-built drawings, commissioning records, all permits and certificates, final cost reconciliation, warranty register, and lessons learned memo

label: Opportunity qualification and PWIN assessment
content: Conduct structured opportunity qualification using Shipley criteria: customer access and relationship quality, competitive position vs. incumbent, solution fit to stated requirements, and resource availability. Score each opportunity against a weighted qualification matrix aligned to company growth objectives. Develop probability of win (Pwin) estimate using discriminators (past performance relevance, key personnel, pricing posture, teaming strength) and detractors (incumbent advantage, customer access gaps, known competitor strengths). Document Pwin rationale with supporting evidence—not a gut-feel percentage. Update Pwin monthly as capture intelligence matures and at each gate review. Pwin below 15–20% without a credible improvement path triggers no-bid recommendation at Gate 1.
summary: Task: Structured opportunity qualification using Shipley criteria and probability of win scoring

label: Capture strategy development with Shipley gate process
content: Develop and maintain a formal capture plan progressing through Shipley gate milestones: Gate 0 (opportunity identification and initial screening), Gate 1 (qualify/no-bid decision with Pwin and resource assessment), Gate 2 (capture strategy approval—customer intelligence, competitive positioning, win strategy, teaming structure, solution concepts), Gate 3 (proposal ready—B&P authorization, resource allocation, volume assignments, final bid decision), Gate 4 (post-submittal—lessons learned, debrief planning). Capture plan document must include: opportunity overview, customer hot buttons, competitive landscape, win strategy and discriminators, teaming plan, solution approach, Pwin assessment, B&P budget estimate by phase, and milestone schedule. Update capture plan at each gate and present at formal gate review boards. Gate 2 approval required before proposal kickoff.
summary: Task: Formal capture plan development through Shipley Gate 0 to Gate 4 milestones

label: Solution architecture for proposals
content: Lead development of technical and management solution concepts during the capture phase—before RFP release—so the proposal team enters the proposal phase with a mature, customer-validated approach. Engage technical subject matter experts (SMEs) and solution architects to develop draft technical approach: systems or service delivery concept, staffing model, management structure, key personnel identification, relevant tools and methods, and performance approach. Map solution elements to anticipated evaluation criteria (technical approach, management approach, staffing, past performance). Conduct solution validation activities: customer briefings to test approach, industry day feedback incorporation, and internal technical reviews. Document solution concepts in capture plan to enable rapid proposal volume drafting upon RFP release.
summary: Task: Developing technical and management solution concepts during capture phase to enable rapid proposal response

label: Ghost teaming and competitive assessment
content: Identify likely competitor teams by analyzing: incumbent past performance data (FPDS-NG, SAM.gov, CPARS), known contract vehicle holders eligible for the opportunity, competitor teaming history on similar awards, and industry conference intelligence.
