Develop ghost team hypotheses for top 2–3 likely competitors: estimated prime, probable subcontractors, anticipated technical approach themes, pricing posture, and key personnel they may propose. For each ghost team, assess strengths and vulnerabilities. Develop counter-strategy: identify gaps in competitor past performance, cost performance concerns (CPARS indicators), key personnel risks, and areas where Yggdrasil Aero can demonstrate clear superiority. Integrate ghost team assessment into win strategy and discriminator development. Update as new intelligence is gathered.
summary: Task: Identifying likely competitor teams and developing targeted counter-strategies

label: Customer call planning and shaping
content: Structure pre-solicitation customer meetings with explicit objectives: gather intelligence on requirements priorities, validate solution concepts, shape evaluation criteria and SOW language, and advance Yggdrasil Aero competitive position. For each customer call, prepare: meeting objective and desired outcome, customer stakeholder profiles and known priorities, agenda aligned to customer interests (lead with value to customer, not sales pitch), technical briefing materials, and targeted questions to extract requirements intelligence. Debrief after each call: document new intelligence (requirements shifts, evaluation emphasis, incumbent vulnerabilities, budget constraints), update Pwin and capture strategy, and assign follow-up actions. Log all contacts with government personnel per procurement integrity requirements (41 USC 2102). Use call intelligence to influence SOW language, PWS requirements, and evaluation criteria through industry day comments and RFI responses.
summary: Task: Structuring pre-solicitation customer meetings to gather intelligence and shape requirements and evaluation criteria

label: Teaming agreement negotiation
content: Lead capture-phase teaming agreement negotiation with prospective subcontractors and partners for specific bid opportunities—distinct from BD director strategic teaming (JV formation, mentor-protégé). Activities include: identify capability gaps requiring subcontractor fill based on RFP requirements analysis, evaluate candidate subcontractors against technical fit and past performance, initiate NDA execution before technical discussions, negotiate Teaming Agreement (TA) provisions: exclusivity scope and duration, work share percentage by task area, role designation (prime/sub, key vs. non-key positions), proposal participation obligations, flow-down clause requirements (FAR, DFARS, ITAR), IP and background data rights protections, and no-hire restrictions. Coordinate TA legal review before execution. Finalize TA before proposal kickoff (Gate 3) to ensure subcontractor proposal contribution commitments are binding.
summary: Task: Negotiating and executing per-opportunity teaming agreements with subcontractors and partners during capture phase

label: Price-to-win analysis coordination
content: Coordinate price-to-win (PTW) analysis to develop an independent estimate of what the winning price will be, informing Yggdrasil Aero pricing strategy. Activities: engage PTW analyst (internal pricing team or external PTW firm) early in capture phase—ideally 6–12 months before RFP release. Provide PTW analyst with: FPDS-NG incumbent contract data (labor mix, period of performance, total value, modifications), CPARS and performance history, known competitor labor rates and overhead structures (from public rate data, GSA schedules, previous proposals), government estimate signals (budget exhibits, IGCE indicators from RFI responses). Integrate PTW output into capture plan: compare PTW estimate against Yggdrasil Aero's internal build-up cost to identify cost gap, assess feasibility of competing at or below PTW, and recommend pricing strategy (price to win vs. price to perform vs. no-bid). Update PTW as RFP draft and final are released with more detailed requirements.
summary: Task: Coordinating price-to-win analysis to inform capture and pricing strategy decisions

label: Capture review preparation and gate reviews
content: Prepare and present capture materials for formal gate review boards at each Shipley gate milestone. For each gate review: assemble capture plan documentation (opportunity overview, Pwin assessment, customer intelligence quality, competitive landscape, win strategy, teaming status, solution concept maturity, B&P budget estimate and spend to date), complete gate review briefing template (per BD shared drive standard), schedule review with BD director and required stakeholders (at least 5 business days' notice), present at gate review meeting and respond to board questions, document gate decision (approve/conditional approval/no-bid/defer) with rationale and assigned conditions in CRM within 24 hours. For conditional approvals, track condition closure actions and confirm resolution before next gate. Coordinate with BD director on gate 2 and gate 3 timing to align with RFP release forecast.
summary: Task: Preparing and presenting gate review materials at each Shipley gate for BD director review and decision

label: Win theme and discriminator development
content: Develop compelling win themes and discriminators grounded in documented customer intelligence—not generic marketing claims. Process: compile customer hot buttons from call logs, industry day feedback, RFI responses, and stakeholder mapping data. Map hot buttons to Yggdrasil Aero capabilities, past performance, and solution concepts. Draft win theme candidates: each must be customer-relevant (addresses a documented hot button), differentiating (demonstrably better than likely competitors), provable (substantiated by past performance, data, or technical approach), and compelling (meaningful to the evaluator). Validate win themes against ghost team assessment: confirm each theme exploits a known competitor vulnerability or gap. Develop parallel discriminator matrix: for each discriminator, document the customer hot button addressed, the Yggdrasil Aero proof point, and the competitor gap exploited. Present win theme framework at Gate 2 for BD director approval. Carry themes forward into proposal storyboards and section outlines to ensure consistent messaging across all volumes.
summary: Task: Developing and validating win themes and discriminators based on customer hot buttons and competitive assessment

label: Selecting primary/secondary/tertiary datum features per Y14.5
content: Systematic process for selecting primary/secondary/tertiary datum features per ASME Y14.5-2018 Section 7. Step 1: Identify assembly interface surfaces — primary datum = largest/most stable mating surface, constrains 3 DOF (one translation + two rotations for a plane). Step 2: Evaluate secondary datum — functional orientation surface perpendicular or at known angle to primary, constrains 2 DOF. Step 3: Select tertiary datum — eliminates final translational DOF, typically a hole, slot, or small surface. Evaluation criteria scored in priority order: (1) functional priority — assembly mating surface > manufacturing reference > inspection convenience; (2) manufacturing stability — large flat rigid surfaces preferred over curved or flexible; (3) measurement accessibility — CMM probe must qualify datum without repositioning; (4) inspection repeatability — setup GR&R ≤10% of the tightest tolerance referencing the DRF. Datum targets (points/lines/areas) used when full datum feature contact is impractical or nonrepeatable (e.g., castings, weldments). Document datum selection rationale in design record and drawing notes per Y14.5-2018 Section 7.3. DOWNSTREAM IMPACT: Datum selection directly determines fixture design (fixture locating surfaces must physically simulate the selected datum features) and CMM programming (CMM must qualify datums in the same precedence order using appropriate algorithms — least squares for planes, max inscribed for internal features, min circumscribed for external). Poor datum selection that ignores manufacturing or inspection constraints forces costly fixture redesigns or creates systematic measurement discrepancies between fixture-based acceptance and CMM-based verification.
summary: Systematic selection of primary/secondary/tertiary datum features per Y14.5-2018 Section 7 based on functional priority, manufacturing stability, and inspection repeatability

label: Tolerance Allocation Process
content: Method for distributing assembly functional tolerance to individual component features. Step 1: Define assembly requirement — total allowable gap, clearance, or positional error at the functional interface. Step 2: Construct tolerance loop diagram — identify every contributing dimension/tolerance from reference to reference, assign direction (+/-). Step 3: Select allocation method (worst-case, RSS, or Monte Carlo — see L4 decision). Step 4: Assign initial individual tolerances proportional to manufacturing difficulty — critical functional features tighter, non-critical looser; use equal-allocation as starting point then adjust. Step 5: Verify allocation — worst-case: sum of individual tolerances ≤ assembly requirement with ≥10% design margin; RSS: √(Σtᵢ²) × Z-factor ≤ requirement; Monte Carlo: yield at requirement ≥99.73% (3σ) or customer-specified target. Step 6: Check Cpk feasibility — each individual tolerance must support Cpk ≥1.33 at actual process capability (tolerance ≥ 8 × process σ for Cpk 1.33). Step 7: Iterate — loosen non-critical features or redesign if any contributor is not manufacturable. Document results in tolerance stack-up analysis report; link to drawing revision.
summary: Allocating assembly tolerance to component features using worst-case, RSS, or Monte Carlo with design margin and Cpk feasibility verification

label: MMC/LMC Bonus Tolerance Application Procedure
content: Procedure for applying material condition modifiers to geometric tolerances per ASME Y14.5-2018 Section 10. Step 1: Determine feature type — internal (hole, slot) or external (pin, boss) and applicable modifier (MMC, LMC, or RFS default). Step 2: Calculate virtual condition — external feature at MMC: VC = MMC size + geometric tolerance (worst-case assembly envelope); internal feature at MMC: VC = MMC size − geometric tolerance; for LMC reverse direction. Step 3: At any actual mating envelope (AME) departure from MMC, calculate bonus tolerance — bonus = |AME − MMC size|; total allowable geometric tolerance = stated tolerance + bonus. Step 4: For MMC-modified datum features of size, calculate permissible datum shift = |actual datum feature AME − datum virtual condition|; datum shift is additional freedom of movement of part relative to gage. Step 5: Define functional gage pin/hole dimensions directly from virtual condition — gage element at VC with gage tolerance taken into material (10% of part tolerance per ASME B4.4M). Step 6: Document virtual condition, bonus table or formula, and gage dimensions on drawing or in associated tolerance analysis. Step 7: Verify that datum modifier (if any) is on a feature of size — modifiers on surfaces (planes) are not permitted.
summary: Applying MMC/LMC modifiers, calculating virtual condition and bonus tolerance, and deriving functional gage dimensions per Y14.5-2018 Section 10

label: Composite Position Tolerance Interpretation
content: Process for interpreting composite feature control frames per ASME Y14.5-2018 Section 10. Step 1: Identify the two-segment composite FCF — upper segment = Pattern Locating Tolerance Zone Framework (PLTZF); lower segment = Feature Relating Tolerance Zone Framework (FRTZF). Step 2: Apply upper segment — locates the entire pattern relative to all datum references in the upper FCF; pattern must fit within larger PLTZF zones located from DRF. Step 3: Apply lower segment — controls feature-to-feature spacing and orientation within the pattern; FRTZF zones are smaller and translate as a group within PLTZF; datum references in lower segment control orientation only (not pattern location). Step 4: Determine constraint of lower segment — if lower FCF has datum references, pattern orientation to those datums is controlled; if no datum references, only feature-to-feature spacing is controlled (pattern may rotate within PLTZF). Step 5: Verify FRTZF < PLTZF in magnitude (required — drawing error if FRTZF ≥ PLTZF). Step 6: For simultaneous requirements, verify all features referenced to same DRF are gauged in a single setup. Step 7: Distinguish from two single-segment FCFs — two separate frames each fully locate pattern from DRF; composite lower segment floats in location. Apply to bolt hole patterns, pin patterns, and slot groups.
summary: Interpreting PLTZF and FRTZF composite position segments for pattern location vs feature-to-feature control per Y14.5-2018 Section 10

label: Profile Tolerance for Complex Surfaces
content: Methodology for applying profile of a surface to complex contoured parts per ASME Y14.5-2018 Section 11. Step 1: Define true profile — via CAD model annotated per Y14.41 (MBD) or basic dimension coordinates; no toleranced dimensions may define the true profile shape. Step 2: Establish DRF for profile — use 3-2-1 datum scheme simulating functional mounting or assembly condition; profile without datum references controls form only. Step 3: Specify tolerance zone type — bilateral equal (default, zone straddles true profile equally), unequal bilateral using Ⓤ modifier with explicit inward/outward values, or unilateral (Ⓤ with one value = 0). Step 4: Apply scope — all-around symbol (circle at leader junction) for all cross-sectional profile elements in one view; all-over for entire 3D surface including ends; between symbol (←A→B) to limit application to a surface segment. Step 5: Define CMM measurement strategy — probe density ≥20 scan points per nominal tolerance zone area unit for Class A surfaces; datum qualification sequence (primary first); alignment method (constrained 3-2-1 vs iterative best-fit with locked DOF); specify algorithm (least squares, min zone); measurement uncertainty ≤ T/4 (4:1 TUR). Step 6: Specify measurement reporting — surface color map for complex contours; worst-point deviation and Cpk; boundary condition pass/fail per each tolerance zone.
summary: Applying profile of a surface to complex contoured parts: true profile definition, DRF setup, tolerance zone type, scope symbols, and CMM measurement strategy per Y14.5-2018 Section 11

label: Datum Feature Selection Criteria
content: Decision matrix for ranking candidate datum features. Score each candidate on four criteria (1-3 pts each, max 12): CRITERION 1 — Functional Priority: Assembly mating surface = 3; functional reference surface not at assembly interface = 2; manufacturing or inspection convenience only = 1. CRITERION 2 — Manufacturing Stability: Large (>25% of part envelope), flat, rigid, accessible on all production parts = 3; moderate size or mild curvature = 2; small, highly curved, flexible, or intermittent contact = 1. CRITERION 3 — Measurement Accessibility: Fully accessible for CMM probe without repositioning or angle adapter = 3; accessible with angle probe ≤45° = 2; requires part flip, repositioning, or custom fixture = 1. CRITERION 4 — Inspection Repeatability: Setup repeatability (GR&R) ≤10% of tightest referencing tolerance = 3; 10–20% = 2; >20% = 1. SELECTION RULES: Score ≥10 = preferred primary datum candidate; 7–9 = secondary/tertiary candidate; ≤6 = do not use as datum without design review approval. OVERRIDE: Any functional assembly mating surface must be evaluated regardless of score. Datum features must meet minimum size/quality for CMM qualification per ISO 5459. For simultaneous-requirement DRFs, all datum features must be co-accessible in a single setup. Document selection rationale; datum changes after CDR require formal drawing change and tolerance re-analysis.
summary: Decision matrix scoring datum candidates by functional priority, manufacturing stability, measurement accessibility, and repeatability to determine datum precedence assignment

label: Tolerance Allocation Method Selection
content: Criteria for selecting worst-case (WC), RSS, or Monte Carlo (MC) tolerance allocation method. WORST-CASE: Select when — production volume <100 units/year; 100% assembly required (no sorting, no rejects); assembly failure is safety-critical or has catastrophic consequence; process Cpk data unavailable or unreliable; ≤5 contributors in tolerance loop. Characteristic: guarantees assembly for all conforming parts; typically yields individual tolerances 30–50% wider than RSS; conservative and directly verifiable. RSS (Root Sum Square): Select when — production volume ≥500 units/year; statistical conformance acceptable (≥99.73% yield target = ±3σ); all contributors have verified Cpk ≥1.33 and near-normal distribution; 5–20 contributors; assembly failure is not safety-critical. Characteristic: allows tighter individual tolerances; assumes normal distribution, zero mean shift, independent contributors. MONTE CARLO SIMULATION: Select when — non-normal or truncated distributions present (cast/forged features, controlled-bias processes); >20 contributors; correlated contributors (same setup drives multiple features); specific yield target required (e.g., 99.9%); RSS assumptions cannot be verified. Characteristic: most accurate for complex stacks; requires historical process distribution data for each contributor. DEFAULT GUIDANCE: ≤5 contributors → WC; 6–20 contributors with verified Cpk → RSS; >20 contributors or non-normal → MC. Mixed approach permitted: WC for safety-critical sub-loops, RSS for non-critical sub-loops within same analysis.
summary: Selecting worst-case vs RSS vs Monte Carlo based on production volume, safety criticality, contributor count, distribution type, and process capability data availability

label: Material Condition Modifier Selection Criteria
content: Decision criteria for applying MMC (Ⓜ), LMC (Ⓛ), or RFS (default, no symbol) to geometric tolerances and datum features. APPLY MMC (Ⓜ) when: feature is in a clearance-fit assembly (bolt/clearance hole, pin/slot); functional gaging is acceptable or preferred for production inspection; bonus tolerance on the feature is beneficial for manufacturing yield; virtual condition boundary is the critical assembly constraint; applies to datum features of size when interchangeability at virtual condition is functionally acceptable. APPLY LMC (Ⓛ) when: minimum wall thickness between a hole and part edge is critical; minimum clearance to an adjacent feature must be guaranteed; maintaining minimum material at thin cross-sections is the functional concern. Less common than MMC; not typically applied to datum features. APPLY RFS (default) when: precision location is required regardless of actual feature size (bearing journals, precision gear bores, turbine discs, optical mounts); functional gaging is not appropriate (CMM-only inspection required); position must be verified independent of fit condition; feature is a datum of size for precision reference (no datum shift permitted). DATUM MODIFIER GUIDANCE: Apply MMC/LMC modifier to datum features of size only when assembly interchangeability permits movement relative to virtual condition; omit modifier (RFS) for precision reference datums where datum shift would mask functional misalignment. PROHIBITED: Modifiers cannot be applied to surfaces (planes, irregular surfaces) — only to features of size (cylinders, slots, spheres, cones).
summary: Selecting MMC vs LMC vs RFS modifier based on fit type, inspection method, virtual condition requirements, and precision location needs

label: Position vs Profile Tolerance Selection
content: Criteria for selecting position tolerance vs profile of a surface tolerance for a given feature. USE POSITION TOLERANCE when: feature is a regular feature of size (cylindrical hole, counterbore, slot width, pin, boss); the controlled geometric element is a derived center (axis, median plane, center point); MMC/LMC modifier is required for functional gaging or bonus tolerance; composite position is needed for pattern location vs feature-to-feature control; tolerance zone shape is cylindrical (Ø prefix in FCF) or bounded parallel planes. USE PROFILE OF A SURFACE when: feature has a complex, irregular, non-circular, or swept cross-section defined by basic dimensions or CAD model; tolerance must simultaneously control form + orientation + location in a single callout; feature transitions between geometry types along its length; no single axis or center plane can represent the controlled element; surface finish and form requirements are inseparable from location. EITHER MAY BE USED FOR: Flat planar surfaces — profile controls surface location + form simultaneously (preferred for tight tolerances); position (with center plane interpretation) for symmetric features. Conical features — profile for combined form and location; angularity for axis orientation control. KEY DISTINCTION: Position controls a derived (imaginary) element; profile controls the actual surface. When both form control and location control must be held simultaneously to the same tolerance, profile is required. Composite approach permitted: profile for overall shape + position for hole center within that profile.
summary: Choosing position vs profile tolerance based on feature geometry type, controlled element (derived vs surface), modifier applicability, and simultaneous form/location control requirements

label: Surface Profile Tolerance Zone Type Selection
content: Criteria for selecting bilateral equal, bilateral unequal (Ⓤ), or unilateral profile tolerance zone per Y14.5-2018 Section 11. BILATERAL EQUAL (default, no modifier): Select when — manufacturing process centers on nominal with no systematic bias; equal material addition/removal is acceptable on both sides of true profile; tolerance zone straddles true profile symmetrically (each boundary = half total tolerance from true profile). Use for: machined contours with balanced tool deflection, symmetric formed features. BILATERAL UNEQUAL (Ⓤ modifier with two numeric values): Select when — process bias is known and consistent (casting shrinkage systematically adds/removes material on one side; electroform builds preferentially); tolerance zone must compensate for known process offset; unequal functional requirements exist on inboard vs outboard surface side. Specify as Ⓤ with total tolerance and inward offset value (e.g., 0.8 Ⓤ 0.6 = 0.6 inward / 0.2 outward from true profile). Apply when process capability data shows mean bias >20% of tolerance over sustained production. UNILATERAL (Ⓤ with one value = 0): Select when — hard functional limit exists on one side (aerodynamic surface must not exceed nominal envelope; sealing surface must not fall below nominal); part function prohibits any material on one side of true profile. Specify Ⓤ with 0 for the constrained direction. SELECTION GUIDANCE: Default to bilateral equal; collect process capability data for ≥3 production lots before applying unequal or unilateral; verify zone type supports Cpk ≥1.33 at target process capability before drawing release.
summary: Selecting bilateral equal vs unequal (Ⓤ) vs unilateral profile zone type based on process bias, functional hard limits, and manufacturing capability data

label: Documenting datum reference frame selections and rationale
content: TEMPLATE SECTIONS: (1) HEADER — part number, drawing revision, DRF identifier (label A/B/C for multiple DRFs), author, date, associated drawing zones. (2) DRF IDENTIFICATION TABLE — columns: datum letter | datum feature description | feature type (plane/cylinder/slot/cone/point) | DOF constrained | datum target IDs (if applicable) | drawing zone. (3) DEGREES OF FREEDOM MATRIX — six DOF rows (X-trans, Y-trans, Z-trans, X-rot, Y-rot, Z-rot); one column per datum; mark constrained (✓) vs free (–) per datum; total row must reach 6 for fully constrained DRF or note intentional partial constraint. (4) DATUM FEATURE REQUIREMENTS — for each datum: nominal geometry, size/extent, surface finish Rₐ requirement, minimum contact area or point count, material condition modifier (if datum of size), drawing callout reference. (5) DATUM SIMULATOR SPECIFICATION — CMM datum establishment algorithm (least squares / min zone / max inscribed / min circumscribed per Y14.5-2018 datum feature simulator type); minimum probe points required (plane ≥6, cylinder ≥8 pts × 2 cross-sections, cone ≥12); probe sphere diameter; maximum allowable datum qualification residual error. (6) FUNCTIONAL JUSTIFICATION — which assembly interface or functional reference this DRF simulates; sketch or annotated CAD view of DRF in assembly context. (7) RELATED GD&T CALLOUTS — table of all FCFs referencing this DRF with feature description and tolerance value. (8) SELECTION RATIONALE — brief statement per datum on why selected (primary = largest mating surface, etc.). (9) CHANGE HISTORY. Classify as design record; retain per drawing release retention schedule.
summary: Template documenting DRF datum letters, feature types, constrained DOF matrix, datum simulator requirements, functional justification, and related GD&T callouts

label: Sections and content for a tolerance stack-up analysis report
content: REPORT SECTIONS: (1) HEADER — part/assembly number, revision, analysis ID (sequential), engineer, peer reviewer, date, analysis method (worst-case/RSS/Monte Carlo), software tool. (2) OBJECTIVE — functional requirement statement; acceptance criterion (min gap = X, max interference = Y, clearance ≥ Z); consequence of failure. (3) TOLERANCE LOOP DIAGRAM — dimensioned sketch or CAD view showing loop path from reference surface to reference surface; each contributor labeled with sequence number and direction; loop closure equation. (4) CONTRIBUTOR TABLE — columns: seq # | feature description | nominal dimension | tolerance source (drawing callout or GD&T FCF) | tolerance value | distribution type (uniform/normal/truncated normal) | ±3σ or WC value | direction (+/-) | % contribution to total variation. (5) ANALYSIS RESULTS — WC: nominal result ± WC sum vs acceptance criterion; RSS: nominal ± (√Σtᵢ²) × Z-factor vs criterion; MC: mean, σ, histogram, % yield at criterion. (6) DESIGN MARGIN — WC margin = (allowable − WC sum); RSS Cpk = (allowable/2) / (RSS result/2); minimum required: WC margin ≥10% of allowable; RSS Cpk ≥1.33. (7) SENSITIVITY ANALYSIS — Pareto chart of each contributor's % of total variation; top contributors identified for tolerance tightening priority. (8) MANUFACTURING FEASIBILITY — each tolerance vs estimated process σ; Cpk at stated tolerance for each contributor (flag if Cpk < 1.33). (9) CONCLUSIONS — pass/fail; recommendations if failed (tighten specific contributor, change method, design change). (10) REVISION HISTORY — re-analysis triggered by drawing changes. Link report number to drawing revision in configuration management system.
summary: Tolerance stack-up analysis report covering loop diagram, contributor table, WC/RSS/Monte Carlo results, design margin, sensitivity Pareto, and manufacturing feasibility

label: Checklist items for reviewing GD&T drawing compliance
content: CHECKLIST STRUCTURE — each item: Pass / Fail / N-A | note | reviewer initials. SECTION A — STANDARDS COMPLIANCE: Drawing standard version cited in notes (ASME Y14.5-XXXX); Y14.100 title block format complete; third-angle projection symbol present; units and scale stated. SECTION B — DATUM STRUCTURE: All datum features labeled with capital letters in square frames; datum precedence unambiguously established (primary/secondary/tertiary per FCF datum reference order); datum features are surfaces or features of size (not axes, center planes, or theoretical constructs); datum targets defined per Y14.5 Section 7.6 when full-surface contact is impractical; DRF fully constrains 6 DOF or partial constraint explicitly documented. SECTION C — FEATURE CONTROL FRAMES: Geometric symbol valid per Y14.5 symbol library; tolerance value preceded by Ø for cylindrical zones; material condition modifiers applied only to features of size; datum modifiers applied only to datum features of size; composite FCF: PLTZF > FRTZF confirmed; two single-segment vs composite distinction intentional and correct. SECTION D — TOLERANCE COMPLETENESS: All features toleranced (no untoleranced dimensions); Rule #1 envelope principle applies to all size features (or explicitly overridden with note); profile tolerance or direct GD&T applied to all functional surfaces; no features relying solely on general title block tolerance for critical characteristics. SECTION E — DRAWING CLARITY: Basic dimensions (boxed) used wherever GD&T references true position/profile; no ± tolerances on basic dimensions; no redundant tolerancing of same feature; all-around/all-over symbols applied correctly per Section 11. SECTION F — SPECIAL APPLICATIONS: Ⓤ modifier values sum to stated profile tolerance; MMC/LMC modifier only where clearance fit or functional gaging confirmed (see modifier selection L4 criteria); composite position lower segment ≤ upper segment; virtual condition calculable from drawing data. Gate: all Fail items must be dispositioned before drawing release.
summary: GD&T drawing review checklist covering standards compliance, datum structure, FCF correctness, tolerance completeness, and special application verification per ASME Y14.5

label: Functional Gage Design Specification Template
content: TEMPLATE SECTIONS: (1) HEADER — gage number (sequential, linked to part drawing number), part number and revision, FCF transcribed verbatim from drawing, gage type (GO/functional/attribute), design engineer, approver, release date. (2) GAGE FUNCTION STATEMENT — assembly condition simulated; which virtual condition(s) verified; pass/fail criterion (part passes if it loads freely / seats fully / clears all gage elements). (3) VIRTUAL CONDITION CALCULATIONS TABLE — columns: feature/datum | MMC or LMC size | stated geometric tolerance | virtual condition (VC) | VC formula used; show arithmetic for each element. (4) GAGE ELEMENT DIMENSIONS TABLE — columns: gage element description | nominal dimension (from VC) | gage tolerance direction (into material for GO gage) | gage tolerance magnitude (= 10% of part tolerance per ASME B4.4M or per applicable standard) | wear limit dimension | action at wear limit (resurface / replace / retire). (5) DATUM SIMULATOR REQUIREMENTS — datum order loaded into gage; datum simulator geometry (flat plate, precision bore, locating pin); perpendicularity/parallelism of gage datum elements (typically ≤10% of datum feature tolerance). (6) GAGE MATERIAL AND FINISH — material specification (e.g., D2 tool steel, cemented carbide for high-volume); hardness (58–62 HRC for steel); surface finish on gage surfaces (Ra 0.2–0.8 µm); dimensional stability heat treatment for precision gages. (7) CALIBRATION REQUIREMENTS — calibration interval (typically 6 months or per usage-based trigger); setting master or CMM verification method; dimensional characteristics verified at calibration; acceptance criteria for continued service. (8) OPERATION INSTRUCTIONS — datum seating verification sequence; loading force limit; pass/fail indication (air, visual, probe); operator qualification requirement. (9) FIRST ARTICLE GAGE INSPECTION — inspection plan citing ASME B89 series; all gage element dimensions verified vs spec with documented measurement uncertainty. Retain as controlled design record; update at each drawing revision.
summary: Functional gage design specification covering virtual condition calculations, gage element dimensions, tolerances, material, calibration requirements, and operating instructions

label: CMM Measurement Plan for GD&T Features
content: PLAN SECTIONS: (1) HEADER — part number/revision, CMM equipment ID and software version, probe configuration (stylus length/diameter, tip material), metrology engineer, approval date, measurement plan revision. (2) MEASUREMENT SCOPE — list of GD&T callouts to measure (FCF transcribed, feature description, drawing zone); sampling plan (100% for critical characteristics; AQL-based per ISO 3951 for non-critical); production phase (FAI = 100%, rate production = per sampling plan). (3) DATUM QUALIFICATION PROCEDURE — qualification sequence (primary → secondary → tertiary); algorithm per datum type (plane: least squares for stable reference, min zone for functional simulation; cylinder: max inscribed for shaft, min circumscribed for bore per Y14.5-2018 datum feature simulator); minimum probe points (plane ≥6, cylinder ≥8 per cross-section × 2 cross-sections minimum, slot ≥4 per wall); maximum allowable datum residual error per axis. (4) PART ALIGNMENT METHOD — 3-2-1 constrained alignment per DRF (do not use unconstrained best-fit for toleranced features); list DOF locked by each datum; alignment residual check (max allowable misfit per axis). (5) FEATURE MEASUREMENT STRATEGY — for each GD&T feature: geometry type, measurement algorithm, minimum point count, scan path direction, approach/retract vectors, collision check required (Y/N); for profile surfaces: scan density (min 20 pts per cm² for Class A), scan direction (along or across dominant curvature), surface normal direction. (6) MEASUREMENT UNCERTAINTY BUDGET — CMM volumetric MPE (from calibration certificate), probe qualification uncertainty, thermal expansion correction method (part/CMM temperature recorded), fixture compliance estimate; total expanded uncertainty U (k=2); verify U ≤ T/4 (4:1 TUR) for each characteristic; document if TUR < 4:1 and obtain engineering disposition. (7) REPORTING FORMAT — actual vs nominal deviation table per FCF; color deviation map for profile surfaces (scale per tolerance zone); pass/fail per characteristic; statistical summary (mean, σ, min, max, Cpk) for sampled characteristics; nonconforming feature list with deviations. (8) DEVIATION DISPOSITION — nonconforming feature action: hold tag, MRB initiation, or accept under engineering disposition per drawing concession process. (9) REVISION HISTORY. Qualify CMM to ISO 10360-2 before use; re-qualify after probe change or thermal event.
summary: CMM measurement plan covering datum qualification algorithms, constrained alignment, feature measurement strategies, measurement uncertainty budget, and reporting for GD&T verification

label: Implement utility sub-metering and consumption tracking by production area
content: Install dedicated sub-meters for electricity, compressed air, and natural gas at production area boundaries. Integrate sub-meter data into energy management dashboard (EMS or BAS extension) with automated daily consumption logging. Assign utility cost codes per production area to enable overhead rate allocation. Configure anomaly alerts when consumption deviates >15% from 30-day rolling baseline for equivalent production schedule conditions. Calibrate sub-meters annually against utility master meter; verify sub-meter sum within ±3% of master meter total monthly. Feed area-level consumption data to Finance for loaded overhead rate calculation by the 3rd business day of each month.
summary: System: Install and integrate electricity, compressed air, and natural gas sub-meters by production area; feed data to energy management dashboard for overhead allocation and anomaly detection

label: Manage backup power infrastructure — diesel generators, UPS, and ATS
content: Maintain standby diesel generator fleet with monthly no-load exercise and quarterly load-bank testing at ≥30% rated load; conduct annual full-load transfer test per NFPA 110 Level 1 requirements. Maintain UPS systems for critical environmental controls (cleanroom, composite layup, BAS) and data systems; test UPS battery capacity annually at rated load with discharge to 80% depth. Test automatic transfer switches (ATS) monthly for proper operation and transfer-time compliance per NFPA 110. Maintain minimum 72-hour on-site diesel fuel supply; inspect fuel quarterly for water contamination and microbial growth; treat fuel annually with biocide and stabilizer. Maintain utility interconnect agreements and coordinate planned outage notifications with local utility. Archive all test records per NFPA 110 and AS9100D §7.1.3.
summary: System: Monthly and annual testing protocols for diesel generators (load-bank per NFPA 110), UPS battery capacity, automatic transfer switches, and 72-hour fuel supply management

label: Decide whether to invest in energy efficiency capital projects based on payback, incentives, and production risk
content: Evaluate energy efficiency capital projects (LED lighting conversion, variable frequency drives on HVAC/compressor motors, compressed air system optimization) using three-factor framework: (1) Simple payback period — calculate annual energy cost savings from utility sub-meter baseline; approve if payback <3 years without incentives or <5 years with utility incentive funding. (2) Utility incentive availability — query state and utility demand-side management programs annually; incentives of 20–50% of project cost are common for LED and VFD retrofits in industrial applications; apply incentive to net cost basis before payback calculation. (3) Production continuity risk during installation — if installation requires >1 production shift of disruption in a critical area, require phased installation plan scoped to scheduled maintenance windows or planned production holds; if no feasible low-disruption installation path exists, defer to next capital cycle unless payback <18 months. MANDATORY COMPLIANCE INVESTMENTS (not ROI-gated): Emergency lighting and exit sign upgrades required by NFPA 101 are code-compliance obligations — fund regardless of payback period; coordinate with Authority Having Jurisdiction (AHJ) on compliance timeline. ANSI RP-7 illumination deficiencies in manufacturing task areas are occupational health obligations — cannot defer on ROI grounds when worker safety or product quality is impacted. Document decision rationale in capital request package. Coordinate final installation schedule with Production Manager before committing contractor.
summary: Decision: Evaluate energy efficiency capital projects (LED, VFD, compressed air) on simple payback period, utility incentive availability, and production continuity risk during installation

label: Monthly utility consumption and cost report by production area with variance analysis
content: Publish monthly utility consumption and cost report by production area covering electricity (kWh), compressed air (scfm-hours), natural gas (therms or MMBtu), and water (gallons) where sub-metered. REPORT SECTIONS: (1) Consumption summary — actual vs. budget by production area and utility type; month-over-month and year-over-year comparison. (2) Cost summary — actual utility cost vs. budget by area; overhead rate allocation inputs for Finance. (3) Variance analysis — explain variances >10% from budget attributing cause: production schedule change, process change, equipment addition, or efficiency gain. (4) Energy intensity metrics — kWh per unit produced and kWh per occupied sq ft; compare against prior 12-month rolling average and available aerospace industry benchmarks; flag if intensity increases >5% month-over-month without corresponding production volume change. (5) Energy efficiency program status — open project list with completion percentage, projected annual savings, and post-implementation actual savings tracking. (6) Anomaly flags — production areas triggering >15% consumption deviation alerts with root-cause investigation notes. Distribute to Operations leadership, Finance (overhead rate update), and Facilities Manager by 5th business day of following month.
summary: Output: Monthly utility consumption and cost report by production area with budget variance analysis, energy intensity benchmarking, and energy efficiency program status

label: Manage cleanroom particle count monitoring program per ISO 14644-2
content: Configure continuous particle counters at monitoring locations defined per ISO 14644-2 Annex A spatial sampling plan for each room classification. Set alert limits at 50% of ISO classification limit and action limits at 100% of classification limit for each applicable particle size threshold (≥0.5 µm and ≥5.0 µm per class). Log all readings with date, time, location, and room classification; trend results monthly to detect gradual contamination increases before action limits are reached. ALERT LIMIT RESPONSE: notify area supervisor, increase monitoring frequency to hourly, investigate root cause (gowning compliance, personnel count, open containers, HEPA pressure drop trending). ACTION LIMIT RESPONSE: stop production in affected area, quarantine in-process materials pending Quality Manager review, perform root-cause investigation with remediation sign-off before resuming. Calibrate particle counters per ISO 21501-4 on annual schedule; verify zero-count operation daily using HEPA-filtered reference sample. CRITICAL: ISO 14644-2 requires optical airborne particle counters (ISO 21501-4 calibrated) — liquid-suspension particle counting methods (ISO 4406, ISO 11171) are NOT compliant for cleanroom classification and must not be substituted; they cause particle aggregation and sub-micron loss that invalidates the airborne contamination profile.
summary: System: Configure particle counters per ISO 14644-2 spatial sampling plan; define 50%/100% alert and action limits per classification; monthly trending with escalation protocols at each limit tier

label: Coordinate HEPA/ULPA filter integrity testing per IEST-RP-CC001
content: Schedule aerosol photometry (DOP/PAO challenge) integrity tests for all HEPA and ULPA filters annually and within 5 business days after any filter replacement or cleanroom modification affecting airflow. Contract certified filter testing services per IEST-RP-CC001 test methodology; verify technician certification and calibration records for challenge aerosol generator before each test campaign. Track filter pressure drop via differential pressure gauges or BAS at each filter bank; establish replacement threshold per filter manufacturer specification (typically 150–200% of initial resistance at rated airflow). Trend pressure drop monthly — sustained increase indicates filter loading approaching replacement; sudden pressure drop decrease may indicate filter damage or bypass, triggering immediate unscheduled integrity test regardless of annual schedule. Maintain filter test log: filter ID, location, installation date, test dates, test results (pass/fail with scan area map), pressure drop at test, and replacement history. Coordinate replacement scheduling with Production Manager to minimize cleanroom downtime; plan filter changeouts during production holds where possible.
summary: System: Annual and post-replacement aerosol photometry (DOP/PAO) integrity testing per IEST-RP-CC001; monthly filter pressure drop trending for replacement planning and damage detection

label: Determine cleanroom re-qualification scope after contamination events or facility modifications
content: Determine whether full ISO 14644-1 re-certification or targeted verification is required based on event type and severity. FULL RE-QUALIFICATION REQUIRED: new HVAC equipment installation or ductwork modification affecting supply air volume to cleanroom; HEPA filter replacement in >25% of filter banks simultaneously; partition modification changing room volume or design air change rate; particle counts exceeding action limits with root cause not isolated to a single identifiable point event; room re-designation to a tighter ISO class. TARGETED VERIFICATION SUFFICIENT: single HEPA filter replacement (re-test that filter bank and immediately downstream monitoring locations only); isolated particle count exceedance with confirmed and remediated root cause (gowning breach, isolated spill) — re-test at original monitoring locations after remediation; minor penetration seal repair with no airflow path change and no BAS differential pressure anomaly. AMBIGUOUS CASES requiring Quality Manager judgment: multiple filter replacements below 25% threshold; contamination event with incompletely characterized root cause; construction in adjacent area during which BAS recorded differential pressure anomaly. Default to full re-qualification when scope is genuinely ambiguous — re-certification cost is lower than product liability risk from under-qualified cleanroom. Document decision rationale with Quality Manager concurrence signature before resuming production in affected area.
summary: Decision: Full ISO 14644-1 re-certification vs. targeted verification — HVAC changes, multi-filter replacement, and uncharacterized contamination trigger full re-qualification; isolated events with confirmed root cause allow targeted re-test

label: Annual cleanroom qualification report per ISO 14644 with particle count data and filter integrity results
content: Compile annual cleanroom qualification report per ISO 14644-1 and ISO 14644-2 for all classified cleanroom and controlled environment areas. REPORT STRUCTURE: (1) Room inventory — each cleanroom designation, ISO class target, design and measured air change rate, filter coverage area, and current qualification status. (2) Particle count data — annual classification measurement results per ISO 14644-1 Section 5 spatial sampling plan; tabulate by monitoring location vs. ISO class limits; include statistical analysis per ISO 14644-1 Annex B for multi-location rooms. (3) Environmental parameter trending — annual min/max/average for temperature, relative humidity, and differential pressure by room; excursion events with timestamps, magnitudes, durations, and material disposition records. (4) Filter integrity test results — all DOP/PAO test records for the year with pass/fail by scan area; filters replaced during the year with triggering pressure drop values and installation dates. (5) Re-qualification records — any re-qualification events during the year: triggering event description, scope determination rationale per L4 decision, test methodology, results, and Quality Manager sign-off date. (6) Qualification status summary — each room: Qualified / Qualified with Conditions / Requires Re-qualification, with next scheduled qualification date. DISTRIBUTION: Quality Manager (QMS records per AS9100D §7.1.3 and process qualification file), Operations Manager, Program Management. Retain minimum 5 years or contract period plus 3 years, whichever is longer.
summary: Output: Annual ISO 14644 cleanroom qualification report with particle count data by monitoring location, environmental trending, filter integrity test results, re-qualification records, and room-by-room qualification status

label: DO-178C Objective Matrix by DAL Level
content: Systematic mapping of all DO-178C objectives (Annex tables A-1 through A-10) to DAL levels A/B/C/D with independence requirements and applicability. Table A-1: Software Planning Process — 7 objectives, applicable DAL A-D, independence not required. Table A-2: Software Development Process — 7 objectives, DAL A-D. Table A-3: Verification of Software Requirements Outputs — 8 objectives; independence required DAL A-B. Table A-4: Verification of Software Design Outputs — 13 objectives; independence required DAL A. Table A-5: Verification of Coding and Integration Outputs — 9 objectives; independence required DAL A. Table A-6: Testing of Integration Process Outputs — 5 objectives. Table A-7: Verification of Verification Process Results — 4 objectives; independence required DAL A-B. Table A-8: Software Configuration Management Process — 13 objectives, DAL A-D. Table A-9: Software Quality Assurance Process — 9 objectives; independence required DAL A-B. Table A-10: Certification Liaison Process — 6 objectives, DAL A-D. Summary counts: DAL A = 71 objectives total, 52 requiring independence; DAL B = 69 objectives, 14 requiring independence; DAL C = 62 objectives, 0 requiring independence; DAL D = 26 objectives, 0 requiring independence. Independence means development and verification performed by separate persons or organizations. Verification method for each objective documented in software verification plans and traced in Software Accomplishment Summary (SAS).
summary: Complete mapping of DO-178C Annex A objectives to DAL A/B/C/D with independence requirements and objective counts per table A-1 through A-10

label: Structural Coverage Analysis Process
content: Procedure for performing and documenting structural coverage analysis per DO-178C Section 6.4. Step 1: Instrument source code using coverage measurement tool; tool must be qualified per DO-330 if coverage data is credited toward DO-178C objectives. Step 2: Execute complete requirements-based test suite and collect coverage data from instrumented binary or source-level analysis. Step 3: Analyze coverage results against DAL requirement: DAL A = Modified Condition/Decision Coverage (MC/DC) — every condition in every decision independently shown to affect decision outcome, independence pairs documented; DAL B = decision coverage — every Boolean outcome (true/false) of every decision executed; DAL C = statement coverage — every statement executed at least once; DAL D = no structural coverage required. Step 4: Identify all coverage gaps — code not exercised by the test suite. Step 5: Classify each gap: (a) dead code — code that cannot be executed under any conditions; must be removed or justified with DER/ACO agreement; cannot be retained as uncovered; (b) deactivated code — code disabled by explicit mechanism (configuration parameter, compilation switch, requirement-documented deactivation); deactivation mechanism must comply with DO-178C guidance; (c) test deficiency — genuine gap in test suite coverage. Step 6: Add test cases targeting test deficiency gaps; verify new tests are traceable to requirements (requirements-based tests added, not coverage-based tests alone). Step 7: Re-execute and confirm 100% structural coverage of required type or document all justified exclusions with DER approval. Step 8: Generate structural coverage analysis report. MC/DC independence pair criterion: for condition C in decision D, identify test case pair where C changes value, all other conditions held constant, and D outcome changes — demonstrating C independently affects D.
summary: Step-by-step procedure for statement, decision, and MC/DC structural coverage analysis per DAL requirement, gap classification, and additional test case development

label: DO-254 Hardware Design Assurance Process
content: Implementation procedure for hardware design lifecycle per DO-254 Sections 4-11. Phase 1 — Planning: produce all hardware plans (PHAC, Hardware Design Plan, Hardware Validation Plan, Hardware Verification Plan, Hardware CM Plan, Hardware Process Assurance Plan); establish DAL from ARP4754A FHA; identify complex vs simple hardware classification. Phase 2 — Requirements Capture: derive hardware requirements from system requirements allocation; document in hardware requirements specification (HRS); identify all safety requirements and derived requirements; trace all requirements to parent system requirements; conduct requirements review. Phase 3 — Conceptual Design: develop block diagram and architecture; allocate requirements to hardware components; perform preliminary FMEA; document architectural decisions and trade studies. Phase 4 — Detailed Design: complete schematic capture; HDL coding for FPGAs/ASICs/CPLDs (RTL and gate-level simulation); PCB layout with signal integrity, power integrity, and EMC design rules; derating analysis per applicable standard; design constraints documented. Phase 5 — Implementation: fabrication per design package; assembly per IPC-A-610; acceptance testing per ATP. Phase 6 — Production Transition: establish documented acceptance test procedures; document any design changes from implementation; first article inspection. Phase 7 — Verification: reviews and analyses of all lifecycle phase outputs; for complex hardware at DAL A/B — elemental analysis (formal verification, exhaustive simulation at gate level, model checking) per DO-254 Section 9.4b; test coverage analysis; independence of verification from development required for DAL A/B. Phase 8 — Configuration Management: establish design baselines; change impact analysis; hardware configuration index (HCI) at each baseline. Simple hardware (no combinatorial or sequential logic): reduced verification scope, no advanced verification methods required.
summary: Complete DO-254 hardware design assurance lifecycle procedure covering planning, requirements capture, conceptual/detailed design, implementation, verification, and configuration management

label: DO-160G Test Category Selection Methodology
content: Systematic process for selecting applicable DO-160G environmental test categories based on installation and function. Step 1: Define equipment installation location — map to aircraft environmental zone (Zone A: controlled temperature/pressurized; Zone B: partially controlled; Zone C: severe temperature; Zone D: outside of controlled zone; Zones E-F: ground/water exposure zones per aircraft-specific zone map). Step 2: Identify equipment function — avionics, sensor, actuator, display; operational modes during test (operating, standby, off). Step 3: Obtain aircraft OEM equipment installation control drawing (EICD) or environmental qualification specification (EQS) defining required categories per section. Step 4: For each of the 26 DO-160G sections, determine applicability: always applicable (Sections 4, 7, 8, 16, 17, 20, 21); installation-dependent (Sections 9 explosion-proofness for fuel zones only; 10 waterproofness for exposed locations; 22/23 lightning for externally connected equipment; 24 icing for de-icing equipment); equipment-function-dependent (Section 14 salt spray for maritime/coastal operations; Section 15 magnetic effect for compass-sensitive equipment). Step 5: Select specific category letter per applicable section from DO-160G Section 3 table — categories range A through Z depending on section, with each letter representing a severity level envelope. Step 6: Document selection rationale in compliance matrix with reference to aircraft installation location, OEM specification, or analysis basis. Step 7: Obtain DER concurrence on category selections before test execution for FAA/EASA certification credit. Step 8: Update matrix if installation location or equipment configuration changes post-qualification.
summary: Systematic procedure for selecting DO-160G test category letters for each section based on aircraft installation zone, equipment function, and OEM/certification authority requirements

label: DAL Assignment Criteria
content: Decision framework for assigning Design Assurance Levels per ARP4754A Section 2.5 based on failure condition severity classification from ARP4761 FHA. Failure condition severity classifications and resulting DAL assignments: CATASTROPHIC — failure condition prevents continued safe flight and landing; results in hull loss or multiple crew/passenger fatalities; DAL A required; quantitative probability target < 1×10⁻⁹ per flight hour. HAZARDOUS — large reduction in safety margins; physical distress or workload such that crew cannot be relied upon to perform tasks accurately; serious or fatal injuries to a small number of occupants; DAL B required; target < 1×10⁻⁷ per flight hour. MAJOR — significant reduction in safety margins; significant increase in crew workload; physical discomfort to occupants possibly including injuries; DAL C required; target < 1×10⁻⁵ per flight hour. MINOR — slight reduction in safety margins; slight increase in crew workload; some physical discomfort to occupants; DAL D required; target < 1×10⁻³ per flight hour. NO SAFETY EFFECT — failure has no effect on operational capability or safety; DAL E (no development assurance required); no quantitative target. Independence credit rule (ARP4754A Section 2.5.1): if N independent items must all fail to cause the failure condition, each item may be assigned one DAL level lower than the composite condition per contributor (dual independent DAL A functions may each be assigned DAL B, subject to authority acceptance and common cause analysis). Derived requirements not traceable to higher-level system requirements must be fed back into the safety assessment. DAL assignment documented in FHA, approved by DER/ACO, and baselined before development commences.
summary: Decision framework assigning DAL A-E based on ARP4761 failure condition classification (catastrophic/hazardous/major/minor/no effect) with quantitative probability targets and independence credit rules

label: Tool Qualification Level Determination
content: Criteria for determining DO-330 Tool Qualification Level (TQL-1 through TQL-5) based on tool impact on airborne software and software DAL. Determination logic: QUESTION 1 — Does the tool output become part of the airborne software (e.g., code generator output, compiler-generated object code, data loader output)? If YES: go to Q2. If NO (development support only, no output in airborne product): no qualification needed. QUESTION 2 — Could the tool introduce an error into the airborne software that is NOT detected by any credited verification activity in the software lifecycle? If YES → TQL-1 (applies regardless of DAL): full tool operational requirements, design description, validation, and CM required. If NO (another credited verification activity provides adequate detection): go to Q3. QUESTION 3 — What is the software DAL and what does the tool automate? Tool automates a DO-178C development process (not verification) with error detection by other activities: TQL-2 (DAL A), TQL-3 (DAL B), TQL-4 (DAL C), TQL-5 (DAL D). Tool automates a DO-178C verification activity (test execution, coverage measurement, static analyzer providing credit): TQL-4 (DAL A), TQL-5 (DAL B-D); no qualification needed if tool output is only informational and not credited. TQL rigor summary: TQL-1 requires tool operational requirements + design description + test coverage analysis + full tool CM baseline; TQL-5 requires tool operational requirements + basic validation only. Qualification baseline must be established before first credited use; any tool change triggers re-evaluation per tool qualification plan. Document TQL rationale in PSAC and tool qualification plan.
summary: Decision logic for determining DO-330 TQL-1 through TQL-5 based on whether tool output enters airborne software, error detection coverage by other activities, and software DAL

label: Plan for Software Aspects of Certification (PSAC)
content: PSAC template per DO-178C Section 11.1 and FAA AC 20-115D. SECTION 1 — SYSTEM OVERVIEW: system description; safety assessment summary referencing FHA; DAL assignment for each software component with failure condition classification and ARP4754A reference. SECTION 2 — SOFTWARE OVERVIEW: table of software components — component name | DAL | language | target processor | memory constraints | estimated size (SLOC); interfaces to other software and hardware. SECTION 3 — CERTIFICATION BASIS: applicable airworthiness regulations (e.g., 14 CFR 25.1309, EASA CS-25.1309); means of compliance (DO-178C with Change XX); DO-178C compliance approach (all objectives or specific alternatives); applicable special conditions, deviation proposals (DPs), issue papers; TQL-X tool qualification options elected. SECTION 4 — SOFTWARE LIFECYCLE DESCRIPTION: planning process; requirements process; design process; coding process; integration process; verification processes (reviews, analyses, testing); configuration management process; quality assurance process; certification liaison process; lifecycle environment (OS, compiler name/version, linker, build tools, target hardware description); tool qualification plans for each qualified tool with TQL. SECTION 5 — SOFTWARE LIFECYCLE DATA (SLD) LIST: table — SLD item name | DO-178C section reference | DAL applicability | planned document ID | planned delivery date; include all items from Tables A-1 through A-10. SECTION 6 — SCHEDULE AND MILESTONES: key lifecycle milestones linked to certification schedule; SOI #1-4 audit dates. SECTION 7 — ADDITIONAL CONSIDERATIONS: previously developed software (PDS) — identification, original DAL, change assessment method (DO-178C Section 12); COTS software — supplier oversight plan; partitioning approach (ARINC 653 or other); multiple-version dissimilar software; user-modifiable software. SECTION 8 — SUPPLIER OVERSIGHT: how software developed by suppliers is overseen; supplier audit plan; supplier SLD delivery requirements. Signature block: Software Development Manager, Software QA Lead, DER. PSAC agreed with DER/ACO before lifecycle activities begin; forms basis for SOI audits 1-4.
summary: PSAC template covering system overview, certification basis, software lifecycle processes, SLD list, tool qualification plans, and PDS/COTS treatment per DO-178C Section 11.1

label: Software Accomplishment Summary (SAS)
content: SAS template per DO-178C Section 11.20. SECTION 1 — SYSTEM AND SOFTWARE IDENTIFICATION: system name and part number; software component name, part number, version identifier (as loaded in airborne system); DAL; target processor; compiler and linker versions. SECTION 2 — SOFTWARE LIFECYCLE SUMMARY: narrative summary of each lifecycle process as executed; deviations from PSAC — each deviation listed with description, impact assessment, and DER approval reference number; software lifecycle environment identification (exact tool versions and configurations used). SECTION 3 — SOFTWARE LIFECYCLE DATA INDEX: table — SLD document name | document number | revision | date | location/storage | retention status; must include every SLD item planned in PSAC. SECTION 4 — DO-178C OBJECTIVE COMPLIANCE MATRIX: one row per objective from Tables A-1 through A-10 — objective ID | objective description | DAL applicability | compliance status (Satisfied / Not Applicable / Alternative Means) | evidence reference (document number and section); alternative means reference if used. SECTION 5 — PSAC COMPLIANCE COMPARISON: item-by-item comparison of PSAC commitments vs actual execution; deviations listed with DER-approved justification. SECTION 6 — PROBLEM REPORT STATUS: total PRs opened and closed during lifecycle; count of open PRs at delivery; table of deferred open PRs — PR number | description | severity classification (No Safety Effect or Minor per DO-178C Section 11.17) | DER-accepted disposition; statement that no open PRs affect safe operation. SECTION 7 — SOFTWARE CONFIGURATION INDEX (SCI): exact configuration of all SLD items at delivery — document number, revision, date, checksum/hash for executable code; build instructions; binary or object code checksums. SECTION 8 — STATEMENT OF COMPLIANCE: statement that software was developed in accordance with DO-178C, that all applicable objectives for the stated DAL are satisfied, and that all SLD is identified in the SCI. Signature block: Software Development Manager, Software Verification Manager, Software QA Lead, DER (as applicable). Submit to ACO/DER as part of type certificate (TC), STC, or amended TC data package.
summary: SAS template documenting PSAC compliance, DO-178C objective compliance matrix, lifecycle deviations, open problem report disposition, and SCI per DO-178C Section 11.20

label: DO-160G Environmental Test Plan Template
content: DO-160G test plan template per FAA AC 21-16G and DO-160G Section 3. SECTION 1 — EQUIPMENT IDENTIFICATION: equipment name, part number, version; intended aircraft installation (aircraft type, ATA chapter, installation zone); equipment function and operational modes. SECTION 2 — TEST BASIS: DO-160G Change G (date); applicable FAA/EASA certification basis references; aircraft OEM EQS or EICD reference (if applicable). SECTION 3 — COMPLIANCE MATRIX (one row per DO-160G section): DO-160G Section # | Title | Applicable (Y/N) | N/A Justification | Category/Level Selected | Selection Basis | Test Facility | Status (planned/in-work/complete/waived). SECTION 4 — CATEGORY SELECTION RATIONALE: installation zone description; basis for each category letter selection (aircraft OEM statement, analysis, or direct specification); DER concurrence date. SECTION 5 — TEST ARTICLE CONFIGURATION: hardware part number and revision; software/firmware version; pre-test functional check procedure reference; post-test functional check procedure reference; number of test articles per section. SECTION 6 — TEST SEQUENCE: recommended test sequence to avoid sequential damage effects (standard sequence: pre-test functional → temperature/altitude → humidity → vibration → shock → conducted susceptibility → radiated susceptibility → emissions → post-test functional). SECTION 7 — PASS/FAIL CRITERIA MATRIX: table — Section | Category | Test Level | Operating Performance Standard (equipment specification reference) | Minimum Performance Level During Test | Pass Criteria | Fail Criteria. SECTION 8 — ANOMALY HANDLING: test anomaly report procedure; criteria for DER notification; re-test requirements after anomaly; deviation acceptance criteria. SECTION 9 — DOCUMENTATION DELIVERABLES: test report format; DER witness requirements per section (required for certification credit); data retention requirements. Signature block: test engineer, responsible DER. Agree with DER before test commencement.
summary: DO-160G test plan template with compliance matrix, category selection rationale, test article configuration, test sequence, and pass/fail criteria per section per AC 21-16G

label: Hardware Accomplishment Summary (HAS) per DO-254
content: HAS template per DO-254 Section 10.4 and FAA AC 20-152A. SECTION 1 — HARDWARE IDENTIFICATION: system name and part number; hardware component description (FPGA, ASIC, PCB assembly); component part number and version; DAL; manufacturer name and facility; complex vs simple hardware classification with justification. SECTION 2 — HARDWARE DESIGN LIFECYCLE SUMMARY: narrative summary of each lifecycle phase as executed (planning, requirements capture, conceptual design, detailed design, implementation, verification, production transition); compliance with PHAC commitments; deviations from plans — each listed with description and DER approval reference. SECTION 3 — DO-254 OBJECTIVE COMPLIANCE: traceability to DO-254 Figure 2-2 hardware design lifecycle; for each process stage — objectives addressed per DO-254 Section 4-11 | evidence document reference | alternative methods accepted by DER; for DAL A/B complex hardware: elemental analysis method(s) used (formal verification, gate-level exhaustive simulation, model checking) and coverage results. SECTION 4 — HARDWARE LIFECYCLE DATA (HLD) INDEX: complete list of all HLD items — document name | document number | revision | date; must include: PHAC, HDP, HVP, HCMP, HPAP, hardware requirements specification, conceptual design description, detailed design description, HDL source files (FPGA/ASIC), verification procedures, verification results, FMEA, hardware configuration index. SECTION 5 — VERIFICATION RESULTS SUMMARY: design review results; analysis results (derating, FMEA, worst-case); test results; structural coverage summary for complex hardware at DAL A/B; independence of verification confirmation. SECTION 6 — PHAC COMPLIANCE COMPARISON: PHAC section-by-section comparison vs actual execution; deviation list. SECTION 7 — OPEN PROBLEM REPORTS: count at delivery; table of deferred PRs — PR number | description | safety classification | DER-accepted disposition. SECTION 8 — HARDWARE CONFIGURATION INDEX (HCI): exact configuration of all HLD at delivery including FPGA bitfile checksum, schematic revision, PCB revision, BOM revision. SECTION 9 — STATEMENT OF COMPLIANCE: signed declaration of compliance with DO-254 for stated DAL. Signatures: Hardware Development Manager, Hardware Verification Manager, Hardware QA Representative, DER. Submit as part of TC/STC data package.
summary: HAS template documenting DO-254 lifecycle compliance, objective compliance matrix, HLD index, verification results, open PRs, and HCI per DO-254 Section 10.4 and AC 20-152A

label: Structural Coverage Analysis Report Template
content: Structural coverage analysis report template per DO-178C Section 6.4.4. SECTION 1 — HEADER: software component ID and version; DAL; required coverage type (MC/DC for DAL A, decision for DAL B, statement for DAL C); coverage measurement tool name, version, and qualification status (TQL and tool qualification plan reference); test suite version analyzed; analysis date and analyst name. SECTION 2 — COVERAGE SUMMARY TABLE: columns — Coverage Type Required | Target (100%) | Achieved (%) | Gap Count | Gap Lines of Code. SECTION 3 — MC/DC ANALYSIS DETAILS (DAL A only): table of every decision in source code — file | function | line | decision expression | number of conditions; for each condition in each decision: independence pair test case IDs (case A: condition true, case B: condition false, all others held constant); coupled outcome change confirmed (Y/N); cross-reference to test case log. SECTION 4 — COVERAGE GAPS INVENTORY: table — gap ID | source file | function | line numbers | gap type (Dead Code / Deactivated Code / Test Deficiency) | gap description. SECTION 5 — DEAD CODE DISPOSITION: for each dead code instance — code excerpt | reason code exists (defensive coding, unreachable error handler, legacy remnant) | disposition (removed at revision X / DER-accepted justification with reference number); dead code cannot remain without DER concurrence. SECTION 6 — DEACTIVATED CODE DISPOSITION: deactivation mechanism description (configuration parameter name, compilation directive, requirement reference) | DO-178C Section 6.4.4.3 compliance confirmation; deactivated code must not compromise safety of active code. SECTION 7 — ADDITIONAL TEST CASES FOR DEFICIENCY GAPS: table — new test case ID | requirements-based objective | coverage gap(s) closed | test results log reference; confirm all new test cases are requirements-based (not coverage-driven). SECTION 8 — FINAL COVERAGE STATUS: re-run summary after additional test cases — 100% coverage achieved per DAL requirement (or justified exceptions with DER approval references listed). SECTION 9 — TOOL QUALIFICATION EVIDENCE: coverage tool TQL; tool qualification plan document number and revision; tool qualification data document number and revision; qualified tool configuration used. SECTION 10 — CONCLUSION AND SIGNATURE: statement of compliance; signatures of Software Verification Lead and Software QA. Attach raw coverage tool output report as appendix.
summary: Structural coverage analysis report template covering MC/DC independence pairs, gap classification inventory, dead/deactivated code disposition, additional test case tracking, and tool qualification evidence

label: Production scheduling and capacity planning
content: Develop master production schedule (MPS) integrating customer delivery dates, material availability, tooling readiness, and labor capacity by skill. CAPACITY MANAGEMENT: Calculate available capacity per work center (planned hours × efficiency factor); compare against loaded demand; identify overloads 8-12 weeks ahead. Leveling tools: shift overtime authorization, subcontract release to job shop, demand deferrals with PM concurrence. Maintain capacity loading chart by work center updated weekly. SCHEDULE RECOVERY: When disruptions occur (design change, supplier miss, yield failure), assess critical path impact, activate pre-planned contingency sequences, communicate recovery plan with milestone dates to PM within 24 hours. Distinguish root cause before selecting recovery action — design change delay, supplier slip, and manufacturing yield each require different responses. KEY INPUTS: confirmed material on-dock dates, tooling availability, operator skill certification matrix, equipment maintenance windows, program tier priority. KEY METRICS: schedule adherence (on-time starts ≥95%), capacity utilization target 70-75% for LRIP (not 85%), expedite events per month. Coordinate with Supply Chain on 12-week material forecasts; align MPS releases only to confirmed material availability.
summary: Task: Develop and manage master production schedule with capacity loading analysis and schedule recovery protocols

label: Work order management and shop floor control
content: Create, release, and track work orders (WOs) through the manufacturing execution system (MES); manage shop floor routing, operation sequencing, and WIP tracking. WO RELEASE RULES: Release WOs only when material confirmed on-dock and tooling available — prevents WO starvation on the floor and false schedule signals. Configure shop floor routing with operation sequences, work center assignments, and estimated labor hours per step. WIP TRACKING: Maintain real-time WO status visibility at each routing step (open, active, hold, complete); flag holds immediately with hold reason (missing material, design change, quality finding, tooling unavailable). Holds must surface within the same shift they occur. MES DATA INTEGRITY: Operators clock in/out at each operation; supervisors approve completions; engineering change holds prevent WO advancement on open ECO-affected operations. Close WOs within 24 hours of completion for accurate cost capture. SHOP FLOOR CONTROL: Supervisor uses MES dispatch list to assign daily work; sequence by priority tier (committed delivery > pilot > prototype); reassign labor when holds block progress. KEY METRICS: WO on-time start rate, WO cycle time vs. standard hours, WIP age (flag WOs open >2× standard cycle time), WOs on hold by hold reason — review hold-reason Pareto weekly to identify systemic blockers.
summary: Task: Create and track work orders through MES; manage shop floor routing, WIP status, and real-time production visibility

label: Manufacturing readiness assessment (MRL per DoD MRL Deskbook)
content: Conduct Manufacturing Readiness Level (MRL) assessments per the DoD Manufacturing Readiness Level (MRL) Deskbook. MRL SCALE: 1-10 mapped to program gates — MRL 5 target by SDD, MRL 6 by CDR, MRL 7 at PDR, MRL 8-9 by LRIP start, MRL 10 at full-rate production. ASSESSMENT PROCESS: Evaluate ten sub-threads (Technology, Design, Cost/Industrial Base, Materials, Process Capability and Control, Quality Management, Manufacturing Workforce, Facilities, Manufacturing Management, Producibility); score each against Deskbook criteria; identify gaps from target MRL at each gate. MATURATION PLAN: For each below-target sub-thread, develop specific action (process qualification, workforce training, facility investment, supplier qualification), assign owner, and set milestone date. Track monthly; update assessment at each program phase gate. PROGRAM INTEGRATION: MRL assessment results feed program Integrated Master Schedule (IMS) as Manufacturing Readiness Events; below-target sub-threads reported to PMO as manufacturing risks with probability and consequence estimates. DCMA INTERFACE: Document MRL assessments in standard format; maintain evidence packages for DCMA review at applicable program milestones. In LRIP environments with multiple concurrent programs, maintain separate MRL tracking per program and consolidate manufacturing risk report monthly.
summary: Task: Conduct MRL assessments per DoD MRL Deskbook; develop maturation plans and report manufacturing risks at program phase gates

label: Production quality control (first article per AS9102, in-process inspection)
content: Coordinate first article inspection (FAI) and in-process inspection to ensure production quality gates are properly integrated into the manufacturing workflow. FAI COORDINATION (PRODUCTION ROLE): Ensure the FAI article is produced on production tooling, by production operators, using the production process — reject hand-fit or prototype-method FAI articles. Track FAI completion against production schedule; FAI schedule slip is a schedule risk to program. Provide Quality Manager with production readiness inputs (tooling qualified, operators certified, work instructions released). IN-PROCESS INSPECTION INTEGRATION: In-process quality hold points must be embedded in WO routing (not a separate workflow); no WO advancement past a hold point without QC sign-off. Coordinate with Quality Manager on hold point placement — insert gates before irreversible operations (potting, sealing, structural fastening). INSPECTION STAFFING: Ensure QC inspection resources are included in production scheduling — inspect-by dates must be realistic against QC workload; production should not be gated by inspection backlog. QUALITY RECORDS: Maintain inspection records (dimensional results, pass/fail) in WO traveler or MES for each unit; records retained per AS9100D §7.5.3. METRICS: FAI open items count by program, in-process inspection hold cycle time (target <4 hrs for standard inspections), first-pass inspection rate by operation.
summary: Task: Coordinate FAI per AS9102 and integrate in-process inspection hold points into production workflow and scheduling

label: Tooling and fixture management
content: Plan, procure, maintain, and control all production tooling and fixtures as a production scheduling constraint and quality input. TOOLING REGISTER: Maintain register with tool ID, description, associated part numbers/operations, current location, status (active/in-repair/retired/calibration-due), tool life limit, cycles used, calibration due date. TOOL LIFE TRACKING: Establish tool life limits (cutting inserts by cycle count, drill bits by hole count, gages by calibration interval); flag tools approaching life limit 20% before limit; replace proactively to prevent in-process failure and unplanned downtime. CALIBRATION SCHEDULES: All gages and precision tools on calibration schedule per measurement system requirements; overdue items removed from service immediately; coordinate with Quality Manager on calibration intervals. FIXTURE MAINTENANCE: Inspect fixtures for wear or datum degradation at each production use; conduct periodic CMM re-verification per fixture qualification plan (typically annually or after defined use count); quarantine fixtures failing verification. PROCUREMENT PLANNING: Forecast tooling replacement needs from production plan and tool life data; custom tooling lead times 8-26 weeks — include in 3-month material plan coordinated with Supply Chain. TOOLING AVAILABILITY: Track tooling availability as a hard constraint in production scheduling — do not schedule WOs to a work center when required tooling is unavailable, in-repair, or overdue for calibration.
summary: Task: Manage production tooling and fixture inventory, tool life tracking, calibration schedules, and tooling availability as a scheduling constraint

label: Production cost tracking and variance analysis
content: Track actual production costs against estimates at work order and program level; analyze variances and implement corrective actions. COST ELEMENTS: Labor (direct hours × loaded rate), material (actual consumption vs. BOM), overhead (applied rate × direct hours). Compare against engineered standard cost per unit. WO VARIANCE REPORTING: Generate variance report per closed WO — labor variance (actual vs. standard hours), material variance (actual consumption vs. BOM quantity/price); investigate variances >5% of standard cost.
