ROOT CAUSE CATEGORIZATION: Rework (excessive rework hours; flag if rework >10% of total WO labor); yield loss (scrap driving material overage); learning curve (expected early-production variance, should trend toward standard over first 20-30 units); engineering change (design change-driven rework not in standard — charge to program, not manufacturing variance). CORRECTIVE ACTIONS: Rework-driven variances → coordinate with Quality Manager on defect elimination; material overage → investigate yield loss root cause; standard cost inaccuracy → update standards after 10+ WOs of actual data. REPORTING: Weekly WO variance summary to PM; monthly program-level cost summary to Finance and PM. LRIP CONSIDERATION: In low-rate programs, track cumulative program cost (not per-unit) against total program budget and TCPI (To Complete Performance Index) per EVMS requirements if contractually applicable.
summary: Task: Track actual vs. estimated production costs by work order; analyze labor, material, and overhead variances and drive corrective actions

label: Workforce planning and skill certification management
content: Plan production workforce requirements by skill category and shift; manage operator certification records and cross-training programs per AS9100D Section 7.2. SKILL MATRIX: Maintain current skills matrix cross-referencing all production operators against certified processes, certification level (per tier), and certification expiration date; update monthly. WORKFORCE PLANNING: For each production WO, identify required skill certifications; verify sufficient certified operators available before scheduling; flag skill gaps ≥4 weeks ahead to supervisor and HR. CERTIFICATION MANAGEMENT: Track operator certifications per AS9100D §7.2; manage recertification due dates proactively (reminders at 60/30/7 days before expiry); expired certifications = operator removed from that process immediately until recertified. No exceptions — operating on expired certification is AS9100D nonconformance. CROSS-TRAINING PROGRAM: Identify critical processes with fewer than 2 certified operators (single-point-of-failure); schedule cross-training during production taper phases between program ramps; maintain rolling 12-week cross-training plan. SPECIAL PROCESS CERTIFICATIONS: Coordinate external recertification (NDT Level II/III, welding per D17.1, NADCAP-required operator quals) on program schedule — external certs have 2-6 month lead time for scheduling; late renewal creates production stoppage risk. SHIFT COVERAGE: Ensure certified coverage for all critical processes on all active production shifts; authorize overtime or shift changes when certification coverage gaps threaten schedule.
summary: Task: Plan workforce by skill and shift; manage operator certification records per AS9100D and coordinate cross-training to prevent single-point-of-failure

label: Lean manufacturing implementation (VSM, 5S, kanban, standard work)
content: Implement and sustain lean manufacturing practices to eliminate waste, improve flow, and reduce production cost per unit over program life. VALUE STREAM MAPPING (VSM): Map current-state value stream per program annually — identify value-added vs. non-value-added steps, flow time, wait time, WIP queue sizes; develop future-state target; create 90-day action plan to close gap. 5S WORKPLACE ORGANIZATION: Implement Sort/Set-In-Order/Shine/Standardize/Sustain at each workstation; monthly area 5S audits by supervisor, quarterly cross-area audits; 5S scores posted on area visual board with trend. KANBAN PULL SYSTEMS: Implement kanban for internal material movement between production stages; size kanban cards using: Cards = (D × LT / container size) + 1; review card counts monthly against current demand; do not mix kanban-controlled items with MRP-driven items in same staging area. STANDARD WORK: Document standard work (step sequence, cycle time, standard WIP) for all repetitive operations with production volume ≥5 units/year; standard work sheets posted at workstation; use as training baseline and time standard source. LEAN METRICS (monthly): OEE by work center (availability × performance × quality; target ≥85% mature processes), value-added ratio (VA time ÷ total flow time), defects per unit, 5S audit scores. Coordinate kaizen events with Engineering and Quality targeting highest-waste processes; document improvements through updated standard work and track before/after metrics.
summary: Task: Implement lean manufacturing including value stream mapping, 5S, kanban pull systems, and standard work; track and report lean metrics

label: Production data collection and MES integration
content: Implement and maintain MES data collection infrastructure to generate production metrics supporting continuous improvement, program cost reporting, and EVMS (where applicable). KEY METRICS CAPTURED: Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE = availability × performance × quality; target ≥85% for mature processes), cycle time per operation (actual vs. standard), throughput (units completed per shift/week vs. plan), first-pass yield per operation, labor hours per WO (cost variance input), scrap and rework by operation. DATA INTEGRITY REQUIREMENTS: Real-time labor clock-in/out at operations (no batch entry >same shift); material transactions at point of use; inspection results entered at point of inspection; WO status updated within same shift. Integrity target: ≥95% of parts logged with complete operator ID, timestamp, equipment ID, and characteristic data (spot-audit 50 random WOs monthly). MES INTEGRATION: Verify MES data feeds cost system for program cost reporting (labor hours → cost accounting, material transactions → inventory and WIP); data must be sufficient for DCMA EVMS reporting if contractually required. SYSTEM AVAILABILITY: Coordinate with IT on MES uptime targets (≥99%); define downtime backup procedures (paper traveler) with MES retroactive entry on restoration within 4 hours to maintain data continuity. CONTINUOUS IMPROVEMENT USE: Run weekly MES reports — OEE by work center, cycle time trend by operation, yield loss Pareto; use data to identify top 3 improvement priorities; assign owners; track before/after metrics to validate improvement effectiveness.
summary: Task: Implement and maintain MES data collection for OEE, cycle time, yield, and labor metrics; ensure data integrity for cost reporting and continuous improvement

label: Shipley qualification criteria evaluation
content: Apply Shipley seven-part qualification criteria to each opportunity: (1) need—compelling mission need documented; (2) budget—funding identified and available in FYDP or continuing resolution; (3) timeline—RFP release workable within capture resource calendar; (4) competition—credible competitive position exists; (5) customer access—existing or accessible relationships with key decision-makers; (6) solution fit—Yggdrasil Aero capabilities match stated requirements without material gaps; (7) business case—opportunity meets minimum deal size threshold and margin targets. Score each criterion using a weighted matrix (1–5 scale per criterion). Document evidence for each score with source citation. Any criterion scored zero triggers automatic no-bid consideration before advancing to Pwin scoring.
summary: System: Score each opportunity against Shipley seven-part qualification criteria with weighted matrix and evidence documentation

label: Pwin scoring methodology
content: Score Pwin using a structured discriminator/detractor model across five weighted factors: (1) customer relationship and intelligence access quality (0–25 pts); (2) competitive position vs. likely incumbent and top challengers (0–25 pts); (3) solution and technical fit to anticipated requirements (0–20 pts); (4) past performance relevance and recency (0–15 pts); (5) teaming strength and key personnel availability (0–15 pts). Total score converts to Pwin range: ≥75 pts = >50%, 60–74 = 35–50%, 45–59 = 20–35%, <45 = <20%. Each factor score must be supported by specific documented evidence—not narrative estimates. Pwin is reported as a range until Gate 2 when capture intelligence is sufficiently mature to narrow the range. Document scoring in the opportunity qualification scorecard.
summary: System: Score Pwin using five-factor weighted model with evidence-based rationale and point-to-range conversion

label: Monthly Pwin refresh process
content: Refresh Pwin estimate monthly for all active Capture-stage opportunities and mandatorily at each gate review. Trigger immediate off-cycle refresh on: new competitive intelligence (competitor teaming announcement, CPARS release, industry day intelligence), customer engagement outcome (new relationship established or access lost), draft RFP release with requirement changes materially different from Sources Sought, and major program event (budget cut, schedule slip, leadership change at PM or PEO level). For each refresh, document: trigger event, evidence source, prior Pwin range, new Pwin range, and delta rationale. Escalate Pwin drops below 20% threshold to BD director for unscheduled gate review consideration. Log all Pwin updates with date, trigger, and rationale in CRM opportunity record.
summary: System: Conduct monthly Pwin refresh with documented trigger events, delta rationale, and CRM logging; escalate drops below 20%

label: Qualify/no-bid recommendation
content: Determine pursue or no-bid recommendation based on qualification criteria score and Pwin assessment. Pursue threshold: qualification score above minimum (≥60% of maximum weighted score), Pwin above 20% with a credible improvement path identified, capture resources available to initiate activities within 60 days, and strategic portfolio alignment confirmed. No-bid triggers: qualification score below threshold on any critical criterion (budget, solution fit), Pwin below 15–20% without viable shaping or teaming improvement path, competing resource demand from higher-priority concurrent opportunities, or opportunity value below minimum deal-size threshold. Prepare written qualify/no-bid recommendation with evidence-based rationale citing qualification scorecard scores, Pwin range, and resource assessment. Present recommendation at Gate 1 for BD director final decision and CRM documentation.
summary: Decision: Determine pursue or no-bid based on qualification criteria score, Pwin threshold, and resource availability for Gate 1 presentation

label: Opportunity qualification scorecard
content: Standardized one-page qualification scorecard capturing: (1) opportunity identification header—agency, program name, NAICS/PSC, estimated value, anticipated RFP date, incumbent with contract number, set-aside type; (2) Shipley criteria scoring table—each of seven criteria with score (1–5), weighting, weighted score, and evidence notes; (3) Pwin assessment—five-factor scoring table with scores, total, Pwin range, and supporting rationale narrative; (4) resource requirement estimate—capture FTE hours by month for next 90 days and full capture phase, plus B&P dollar estimate by phase; (5) pursue/no-bid recommendation with conditions and next-action steps; (6) completion metadata—capture manager name, date completed, BD director reviewer. Completed by capture manager for every opportunity advancing to Gate 1. Filed in BD shared pipeline library per opportunity and attached to CRM opportunity record.
summary: Output: Standardized one-page scorecard with Shipley criteria scores, Pwin range, resource estimate, and pursue/no-bid recommendation for Gate 1

label: Capture plan document assembly
content: Assemble and maintain the formal capture plan document per BD standard template. Required sections: (1) opportunity overview—program background, acquisition history, incumbent data from FPDS-NG, NAICS/PSC, estimated value, PoP, set-aside type, anticipated schedule; (2) customer intelligence—stakeholder map, documented hot buttons, priorities, and shaping opportunities; (3) competitive landscape—ghost team assessments for top 2–3 competitors with strengths, vulnerabilities, and counter-strategies; (4) Yggdrasil Aero positioning—past performance alignment, capability gaps, and discriminators; (5) win strategy—win themes, discriminators, and competitive counter-strategies; (6) teaming plan—partner roles, work share percentages, TA execution status; (7) solution concept—draft technical and management approach; (8) Pwin assessment with scoring summary and range; (9) B&P budget by phase with authorization status; (10) gate milestone schedule; (11) open actions with owners and due dates. Update capture plan at each gate review. Maintain single version-controlled document in BD shared drive per opportunity.
summary: System: Assemble and maintain the ten-section formal capture plan document with mandatory content at each gate review

label: Gate milestone scheduling
content: Develop and maintain the gate milestone schedule for each active capture, working backward from the anticipated RFP release date: Gate 0 (opportunity identification and initial screening, 24+ months before RFP), Gate 1 (qualify/no-bid decision, 18–24 months before RFP), Gate 2 (capture strategy approval, 90–120 days before RFP), Gate 3 (proposal ready/B&P authorization, within 10 business days of RFP receipt), Gate 4 (post-submittal lessons learned, within 30 days of submission). Schedule formal gate review meetings in BD director calendar at least 5 business days in advance of each milestone. Update gate milestone schedule immediately when RFP release forecast changes (SAM.gov pre-solicitation notice date revisions, agency acquisition forecast shifts, program restructures). Flag gate schedule compression (Gate 2 to Gate 3 window <60 days) to BD director for resource surge planning. Track milestone schedule in CRM with automated reminder alerts at 30/14/5 days before each gate.
summary: System: Develop and maintain gate milestone schedule aligned to RFP forecast with CRM-tracked reminders and compression alerts

label: B&P budget estimation
content: Estimate B&P budget requirement by capture phase and present for authorization at each gate. Phase structure: (1) pre-Gate 2 capture activities—travel for customer calls and site visits, competitive intelligence tool access, initial solution development SME hours, teaming outreach; (2) Gate 2–Gate 3 proposal preparation—proposal manager and volume lead labor hours, SME technical writing, pricing analyst hours, graphics and desktop publishing, color team review participant time, subcontractor coordination; (3) post-submission—oral presentation preparation if applicable, debrief support. Build estimate by labor category, hours, and fully loaded rate per forward pricing rate structure. Benchmark against opportunity value band B&P guideline (typically 1–2% of opportunity estimated value for competitive bids; higher for new business vs. re-compete). Document budget assumptions, uncertainty range, and contingency. Present to BD director at Gate 1 (rough order of magnitude) and Gate 2 (refined estimate for authorization).
summary: System: Build phase-structured B&P budget estimate by labor category and hours benchmarked against opportunity value for gate authorization

label: Capture strategy pivot decision
content: Determine when capture strategy requires a material pivot versus incremental update. Pivot triggers requiring off-cycle BD director review: draft RFP requirements materially different from Sources Sought scope (>20% scope change or new evaluation factor); loss or withdrawal of primary teaming partner; major competitive development (incumbent rebound, strong competitor teaming announcement closing a key capability gap); Pwin decline of >15 percentage points within 60 days; customer access loss or primary relationship deterioration to gap status; program budget restructure, schedule extension >6 months, or scope reduction. A confirmed pivot requires: full capture plan revision, Pwin re-assessment and re-scoring, BD director gate review outside standard schedule, potential B&P reauthorization if budget impact >20%, and updated milestone schedule. Incremental adjustments (refined competitive intel, staffing model updates, minor solution refinement) do not require pivot review—capture manager updates capture plan and logs change rationale in CRM.
summary: Decision: Determine whether new intelligence triggers a material capture strategy pivot requiring off-cycle BD director review or an incremental update

label: Capture plan template
content: Standardized capture plan document template with required sections and completion guidance: cover page (opportunity name, agency, capture manager, BD director, current gate, version date, classification); opportunity overview and program background; customer stakeholder map and hot buttons summary table; competitive landscape with ghost team assessments; Yggdrasil Aero positioning and past performance alignment matrix; win strategy with win themes, discriminators, and counter-strategies; teaming plan with partner roster, work share summary, and TA execution status; solution concept overview with key technical approach elements; Pwin assessment with five-factor scoring table and range; B&P budget by phase with authorization status; gate milestone schedule (all five gates with dates and status); open actions table (action, owner, due date, status); and revision history log. Template maintained in BD shared drive with read-me guidance notes per section. One document file per opportunity, version-controlled at each gate update. Capture manager completes; BD director reviews and approves at Gate 2.
summary: Output: Standardized ten-section capture plan document template maintained per opportunity in BD shared drive with version control at each gate

label: SME engagement for technical concept
content: Identify and engage technical SMEs and solution architects during capture phase prior to RFP release to develop a mature draft technical concept. Process: (1) identify required SME disciplines against anticipated RFP technical requirements (systems engineering, software development, logistics, intelligence analysis, operations, program management—dependent on opportunity type); (2) schedule structured working sessions with SMEs to develop solution concept—minimum two sessions before Gate 2; (3) brief SMEs on customer hot buttons, anticipated evaluation factors, and competitive context; (4) develop preliminary technical concept covering systems or service delivery approach, unique methods or tools, performance approach, and preliminary technical risk identification; (5) document SME contributions and technical concept in capture plan annex; (6) obtain functional manager approval for SME B&P charge authorization before engagement. Protect SME availability for proposal phase by flagging key SMEs to BD director resource loading tracker. Carry technical concept into proposal volume drafting upon RFP release.
summary: System: Engage technical SMEs in pre-RFP working sessions to develop draft solution concept documented in capture plan annex

label: Staffing model development
content: Develop preliminary staffing model during capture phase to support proposal pricing and technical volume. Activities: (1) estimate total labor requirement by labor category (program manager, deputy PM, engineers by discipline, analysts, technical leads, administrative support) across all periods of performance; (2) identify key personnel positions likely to be evaluated (typically PM, deputy PM, principal technical leads) and initiate candidate identification and vetting—match candidate qualifications against anticipated RFP requirements; (3) assess staffing sourcing mix: organic workforce, subcontractor personnel, or new hires; (4) document staffing model matrix: position title, minimum qualifications, identified candidate (internal or sub), estimated level of effort by PoP year, and clearance requirement; (5) initiate key personnel commitment letters or letters of intent from candidates before proposal submission; (6) flag staffing gaps requiring teaming agreement work share resolution. Coordinate staffing model with pricing team for labor cost build-up input. Update model on draft and final RFP release with confirmed LCAT definitions and minimum qualifications.
summary: System: Build preliminary staffing model with key personnel identification, LOE by LCAT and PoP year, sourcing mix, and commitment letter initiation

label: Solution-to-evaluation criteria mapping
content: Map draft solution elements to anticipated evaluation criteria to identify coverage gaps before RFP release. For each anticipated evaluation factor (technical approach, management approach, staffing, past performance, transition/phase-in, relevant experience—dependent on RFP type): identify the customer hot button the factor addresses, the solution element responding to it, the Yggdrasil Aero discriminator or proof point, and any gap requiring additional solution development or teaming fill. Build solution-evaluation criteria matrix as capture plan annex: rows are evaluation factors, columns are solution element, hot button addressed, discriminator, competitor comparison, and gap/action. Update matrix on draft RFP release with Section M evaluation factors and scoring weights; perform gap assessment against confirmed criteria. Use matrix to drive proposal storyboard development at proposal kickoff, ensuring no evaluation factor is uncovered and each volume section has an assigned discriminator.
summary: System: Build solution-to-evaluation criteria matrix mapping solution elements to anticipated Section M factors with discriminators and gap actions

label: Solution approach selection
content: Select among competing solution concepts the approach to carry forward into proposal development. Decision factors weighted in priority order: (1) alignment to documented customer hot buttons and anticipated evaluation criteria—the solution that best addresses evaluator priorities, not the most technically sophisticated; (2) validated customer receptivity—feedback from customer briefings, Industry Day exchanges, and RFI response signals; (3) demonstrated past performance relevance—existence of credible past performance substantiating the approach; (4) differentiation from likely competitor approaches—assessed via ghost team analysis; (5) technical and staffing feasibility within B&P constraints; (6) internal SME consensus on approach viability. Document selected approach with rationale and rejected alternatives, noting the competitive and customer-intelligence basis for rejection. Flag any unresolved solution element gaps (teaming fill, SME hire, past performance gap) as capture plan open actions. Present at Gate 2 for BD director approval before proposal kickoff and carry into draft technical approach document.
summary: Decision: Select solution concept to carry forward based on customer hot button alignment, competitive differentiation, and past performance substantiation for Gate 2 approval

label: Draft technical approach document
content: Draft technical approach document developed during capture phase for pre-RFP solution maturation and rapid proposal volume conversion. Required sections: (1) executive summary—selected approach overview, top 3 discriminators, and key proof points; (2) systems or service delivery concept narrative with supporting diagrams (block diagrams, process flows, CONOPS illustrations); (3) technical methodology, unique tools, frameworks, or processes with differentiating explanation; (4) management structure diagram with key personnel roles and qualifications summary; (5) staffing model overview—total headcount by LCAT and PoP year, key personnel candidates identified; (6) performance approach—metrics, quality assurance methodology, and risk management framework; (7) solution-to-evaluation criteria compliance summary table cross-referencing each anticipated factor; (8) known technical risks and proposed mitigations; (9) open development items requiring resolution on RFP release with owner and trigger. Reviewed and approved at Gate 2. Converts directly to proposal technical and management volume section outlines on RFP release—reducing proposal phase ramp-up time.
summary: Output: Pre-RFP draft technical approach document with solution concept, staffing model, criteria compliance table, and open development items for Gate 2 review

label: Incumbent analysis via FPDS-NG
content: Conduct structured incumbent contract analysis using FPDS-NG data as primary source. Extract for the current incumbent on the target program: all contract awards and modifications (contract number, award date, total obligated value, modification history, NAICS/PSC, period of performance, contract type), option period exercise pattern (all options exercised on time = stable performance; unexercised or late = potential vulnerability), cost growth indicators (CPFF modifications increasing ceiling = cost overrun signal), subcontractors listed in SAM.gov and FPDS-NG records, and contract ceiling utilization rate. Supplement with: published CPARS ratings accessible through agency databases, news and press coverage of program performance issues, and any available Government Accountability Office (GAO) or DoD IG reports referencing the program. Assess overall incumbent vulnerability: low (strong ratings, no cost growth, customer relationship intact), medium (mixed indicators), high (cost growth, schedule slip, leadership changes, CPARS concerns). Document analysis in capture plan competitive section with FPDS-NG contract number citations.
summary: System: Extract and analyze FPDS-NG incumbent contract data—modification history, cost growth, option exercise pattern—to assess incumbent vulnerability

label: Competitor teaming history mapping
content: Map competitor teaming relationships on comparable past awards to project likely teams for the current opportunity. Data sources: FPDS-NG prime-subcontractor award records, SAM.gov contract registration data, publicly available press releases and contract announcements (competitor websites, PRNewswire, Defense News), FOIA requests for bid packages on comparable past awards (where applicable), and industry conference and trade show intelligence on BD conversations and teaming discussions. Build competitor-teaming history matrix: rows are top 3–5 likely competitors; columns are historical prime role, recurring subcontractor relationships by capability area, geographic delivery partners, small business team composition, and historical work share patterns. Identify subcontractors where Yggdrasil Aero has superior or competing past performance—flag as potential teaming displacement opportunities or competitive vulnerabilities. Update matrix as new intelligence is gathered throughout capture phase.
summary: System: Map competitor teaming history from FPDS-NG, SAM.gov, and FOIA data to project likely competitor team compositions for the current opportunity

label: Ghost team hypothesis development
content: Develop ghost team hypotheses for the top 2–3 likely competitors, integrating incumbent analysis and teaming history data. For each ghost team, document: (1) estimated prime contractor with confidence level and basis; (2) projected subcontractors by capability area with rationale; (3) anticipated technical approach themes based on competitor past performance patterns and known capabilities; (4) expected pricing posture—aggressive low-price, technical value premium, or cost-plus; (5) likely key personnel based on competitor contract history and public sources; (6) competitive strengths—where they will score well against Yggdrasil Aero; (7) exploitable vulnerabilities—past performance gaps, cost growth history, personnel risk, CPARS concerns, solution concept weaknesses, or teaming instability. For each vulnerability, assess exploitability: can Yggdrasil Aero credibly claim superiority and substantiate it with evidence? Document hypotheses as a living annex in the capture plan; update on receipt of new intelligence with revision date and evidence source.
summary: System: Develop documented ghost team hypotheses for top 2–3 competitors covering team composition, approach themes, pricing posture, and exploitable vulnerabilities

label: Counter-strategy prioritization
content: Prioritize counter-strategies against the top-threat competitor ghost teams based on impact and feasibility. Evaluation criteria: (1) Pwin impact—does executing this counter-strategy materially improve Yggdrasil Aero's evaluated position vs. the ghost team? (high/medium/low); (2) substantiation feasibility—can the counter-argument be proven with past performance data, technical evidence, or documented customer intelligence? (3) customer receptivity—is the customer likely to respond to this differentiator given their documented hot buttons and priorities? (4) resource cost to execute within capture and proposal phase constraints. Select 2–3 priority counter-strategies: typically one past performance exploit (superior references vs. competitor gap), one solution superiority argument (technical approach advantage grounded in customer priorities), and one risk narrative (incumbent cost growth, key personnel risk, or competitor teaming instability). Document prioritized counter-strategies in capture plan win strategy section. Carry each counter-strategy into win theme development and proposal storyboard assignments.
summary: Decision: Select 2–3 priority counter-strategies against top ghost teams based on Pwin impact, substantiation feasibility, and customer receptivity

label: Competitive assessment matrix
content: Competitive assessment matrix document integrating incumbent analysis, teaming history, and ghost team hypotheses. Structure: (1) header—opportunity name, agency, program, estimated value, set-aside, assessment date, and last update; (2) Yggdrasil Aero competitive position summary—overall strengths, weaknesses, current Pwin range; (3) competitor rows (one per top 2–3 ghost teams)—prime identity and confidence level, projected subcontractors by capability, technical approach themes, pricing posture estimate, likely key personnel, competitive strengths vs. Yggdrasil Aero, exploitable vulnerabilities with evidence, and assigned counter-strategy; (4) comparative scoring table—Yggdrasil Aero vs. each competitor across key evaluation factors (technical, past performance, staffing, management, price) with color-coded rating (green/yellow/red); (5) recommended competitive actions with priority ranking and owner. Updated at each gate review and upon receipt of material new intelligence. Presented to BD director at Gate 2 as part of capture strategy approval package.
summary: Output: Competitive assessment matrix with ghost team profiles, comparative scoring across evaluation factors, and prioritized counter-strategies for Gate 2

label: Meeting objective and agenda preparation
content: Define explicit, intelligence- or shaping-specific objectives for each pre-solicitation customer call before scheduling. Objectives must be specific—not 'relationship building' but: confirm requirements priority ranking among competing system capabilities, validate draft solution concept with program technical lead, advance evaluation criteria language favoring Yggdrasil Aero positioning, extract budget or schedule constraint signals, assess incumbent performance sentiment, or identify new stakeholders requiring engagement. Develop call agenda structured to lead with customer value (mission challenge, program support topic, technical discussion) before transitioning to Yggdrasil Aero-specific content. Prepare 3–5 targeted open-ended questions designed to extract requirements intelligence (priorities, trade-off decisions, evaluation emphasis). Coordinate agenda content with account relationship owner and BD director before finalizing. Confirm procurement integrity clearance before scheduling: verify the program is not in an active source selection period per 41 USC 2102; do not schedule meetings with source selection evaluation board members on active competitions.
summary: System: Define specific intelligence/shaping objectives and customer-first agenda for each call; confirm procurement integrity clearance before scheduling

label: Customer stakeholder profiling
content: Build and maintain individual stakeholder profiles for each government customer contact engaged during capture. Profile fields: full name, current title and organization, program role (PM, deputy PM, contracting officer, technical lead, end user, DCSA contact), decision-making authority level (primary decision maker, key influencer, gatekeeper, potential champion), documented priorities and hot buttons from prior engagements, communication style and preferred meeting format, relationship owner (Yggdrasil Aero BD or SME), relationship history summary (meetings, calls, events), relationship strength rating (strong/developing/gap), last engagement date and method, and next planned touchpoint. Cross-reference stakeholder map with program acquisition authority chain: identify coverage gaps where key decision influencers have no current Yggdrasil Aero relationship. Update profiles within 24 hours of each customer engagement. Use profiles to tailor briefing content, question strategies, and attendee selection for each upcoming meeting. Store profiles in CRM linked to the opportunity record.
summary: System: Build and maintain individual stakeholder profiles with authority level, hot buttons, relationship strength, and coverage gap assessment updated after each engagement

label: Post-call debrief and intelligence capture
content: Conduct structured debrief and intelligence documentation within 24 hours of each customer call. Capture manager completes call report covering: attendees (government and Yggdrasil Aero), meeting objectives achieved (yes/partial/no with notes), requirements intelligence (new priority signals, scope clarifications, schedule indicators, budget constraint signals, requirement changes from prior understanding), competitive intelligence (incumbent performance comments, other industry activity mentioned, source selection approach signals), shaping opportunities identified (specific SOW, PWS, or evaluation criteria language that can be influenced, recommended vehicle: RFI response, industry day comment, follow-on meeting), relationship advancement observations (new contacts met, relationship rating update, champion development signals), and open follow-up actions with responsible owner and due date. Log all government contacts in dedicated procurement integrity contact log per 41 USC 2102: contact name, title, date, method, topics discussed, and Yggdrasil Aero attendees. Update Pwin and capture strategy based on debrief findings—flag material changes to BD director. Distribute call report to BD director and capture team within 24 hours.
summary: System: Complete structured debrief within 24 hours capturing requirements/competitive intelligence, shaping opportunities, and procurement integrity log entry

label: Requirements shaping priority decision
content: Determine which requirements shaping opportunities to pursue and at what priority level based on: (1) Pwin impact—will successful shaping of this SOW requirement, evaluation criterion, or PWS metric materially improve Yggdrasil Aero's competitive position? (high/medium/low assessment); (2) feasibility—is the acquisition timeline early enough to influence (pre-draft RFP), and does customer receptivity signal openness to input from industry? (3) relationship and credibility risk—overly aggressive or transparent shaping attempts can damage customer trust and credibility; calibrate assertiveness to relationship maturity; (4) procurement integrity compliance—all shaping must occur exclusively through legitimate channels: RFI responses, industry day questions/comments, draft RFP formal comment submissions, direct meetings on non-active solicitations; no back-channel communications. Select 3–5 highest-impact shaping priorities. For each: define specific language or criteria change desired, assign responsible owner (capture manager, BD director, SME), identify target submission vehicle and deadline, and document rationale in capture plan. Track shaping action completion and customer response in capture plan open actions log.
summary: Decision: Prioritize 3–5 shaping actions by Pwin impact and feasibility; assign vehicle and owner; enforce legitimate-channel compliance

label: Customer call report template
content: Standardized customer call report template completed by capture manager after each government engagement. Template sections: (1) call header—date, location or virtual platform, program name, customer organization, government attendees (name, title, role), Yggdrasil Aero attendees, opportunity name and CRM ID; (2) call objectives—pre-call goals stated; objectives achieved (yes/partial/no); (3) meeting narrative summary—topics covered and discussion highlights in chronological order; (4) requirements intelligence—new requirements information, priority signals, scope clarifications, schedule/budget constraints (flag each item with intelligence confidence: confirmed/probable/possible); (5) competitive intelligence—incumbent signals, competitor activity mentioned, source selection approach indicators; (6) shaping opportunities—specific language or criteria to pursue, recommended vehicle, assigned owner, priority; (7) relationship development notes—new contacts, relationship rating update, champion signals; (8) open follow-up actions table (action, owner, due date); (9) procurement integrity contact log entry confirmation (yes/no, log entry reference); (10) capture plan update triggered—yes/no with summary of changes if yes. Completed and distributed to BD director and capture team within 24 hours. Filed in CRM opportunity record and BD shared drive call log.
summary: Output: Standardized ten-section customer call report completed within 24 hours with intelligence captures, shaping opportunities, follow-up actions, and procurement integrity log confirmation

label: FAR Part 15 Negotiated Procurement Process
content: Step-by-step process for competitive negotiated acquisitions. Step 1: Acquisition planning — define requirement, competition strategy, socioeconomic goals, and estimated value. Step 2: RFP development — draft SOW/SOO/PWS; define evaluation factors and subfactors (technical, past performance, cost/price) with relative importance disclosed per FAR 15.304. Step 3: RFP issuance via SAM.gov; Q&A period; amendments as needed. Step 4: Proposal evaluation — technical evaluation panel scores technical volume; cost/price analysis (cost realism for CR, price analysis for FP); past performance assessment via PPIRS/CPARS. Step 5: Competitive range determination (FAR 15.306(c)) — CO includes all offerors with reasonable chance of award; written notification to excluded offerors with opportunity to request pre-award debriefing. Step 6: Discussions — CO must discuss significant weaknesses, deficiencies, and adverse past performance; ENs (Evaluation Notices) exchanged; cannot reveal other offerors' pricing or approach. Step 7: Final Proposal Revisions (FPRs) — CO requests FPRs with common cut-off date; no additional discussions after FPRs unless reopened. Step 8: Source selection decision — SSA documents best-value tradeoff rationale; award to selected offeror. Step 9: Post-award debrief — unsuccessful offerors entitled to debrief within 5 days of request (FAR 15.506).
summary: End-to-end negotiated acquisition process: RFP development, proposal evaluation, competitive range, discussions, FPRs, and best-value award

label: FAR Part 12 Commercial Item Acquisition Process
content: Process for acquiring commercial items and commercial services under FAR Part 12. Step 1: Market research (FAR Part 10) — determine whether requirement can be met by commercial item sold in substantial quantities to general public on competitive basis; document market research methodology and findings. Step 2: Commercial item determination — document basis: catalog/market pricing, comparison to similar items sold commercially, or waiver per FAR 12.102. Step 3: Solicitation development — use SF 1449 or equivalent commercial format per FAR 12.204; include mandatory clauses 52.212-1 (Instructions), 52.212-3 (Reps and Certs), 52.212-4 (Contract Terms), 52.212-5 (Required Deviations); tailor terms per FAR 12.302 (may not increase contractor obligations beyond commercial practice). Step 4: Evaluation — best value per FAR 12.206; price reasonableness based on market research, catalog prices, or prior commercial sales; no certified cost or pricing data required for commercial items (FAR 15.403-1(c)(3)). Step 5: Contract award — preferred types: FFP or FPLOE for services (FAR 12.207); cost-reimbursement prohibited for commercial items; inspection/acceptance per commercial practices or FAR 52.212-4 terms. Step 6: Administration — government data rights limited to those customarily offered to the public; warranty terms are contractor's standard commercial warranty unless negotiated otherwise.
summary: Market research, commercial item determination, tailored solicitation using SF 1449 and 52.212 clause set, and award under simplified commercial terms

label: DFARS 252.204-7012 CUI Safeguarding Implementation Process
content: Step-by-step implementation of DFARS 252.204-7012 cybersecurity clause. Step 1: Scope determination — identify all covered defense information (CDI) received or generated under contract and all covered contractor information systems (CCIS) that process, store, or transmit CDI. Step 2: NIST SP 800-171 gap assessment — assess current security posture against all 110 security requirements across 14 families; document results in System Security Plan (SSP) with implementation status (implemented, partially implemented, planned, not applicable). Step 3: POA&M development — for each unimplemented requirement document: remediation action, responsible owner, resources required, and completion date; prioritize by risk. Step 4: Remediation implementation — deploy technical controls (MFA per 3.5.3, FIPS 140-2/3 validated encryption per 3.13.10, audit logging per 3.3.1); implement operational and management controls. Step 5: SPRS score submission — calculate summary-level score per NIST 800-171 DoD Assessment Methodology; submit score, SSP, and POA&M summary to Supplier Performance Risk System (SPRS) before contract award or as required by CO. Step 6: Cloud service compliance — if using cloud services to process CDI, verify provider holds FedRAMP Moderate authorization or meets DoD Cloud SRG IL2+ equivalency; document in SSP. Step 7: Cyber incident response procedures — designate cyber incident reporter; establish 72-hour reporting workflow to DoD via DIBNet portal; preserve and protect compromised system images and malicious software for 90 days; provide access to DoD for forensic analysis upon request. Step 8: Subcontractor flowdown — include 252.204-7012 in all subcontracts where subcontractor may handle CDI at any tier; verify subcontractor SPRS scores as part of supplier qualification. Step 9: Annual reassessment — update SSP and POA&M when system changes occur; conduct annual self-assessment; engage C3PAO if CMMC Level 2 required.
summary: NIST 800-171 gap assessment, SSP/POA&M development, SPRS score submission, FedRAMP cloud compliance, and 72-hour cyber incident reporting implementation steps

label: FAR Part 31 Cost Allowability Determination Process
content: Systematic procedure for evaluating cost allowability under FAR Part 31.2. Step 1: Identify cost element — categorize by FAR 31.205 selected cost principle (31.205-6 compensation, 31.205-18 IR&D/B&P, 31.205-33 professional services, 31.205-46 travel, etc.). Step 2: Apply reasonableness test (FAR 31.201-3) — would a prudent person in similar circumstances incur this cost at this amount? Compare to market rates, competitive benchmarks, contractor's established policies; document comparative data (salary surveys, published per diem rates, competitive bids). Step 3: Apply allocability test (FAR 31.201-4) — determine if cost is allocable: (a) incurred specifically for this contract (direct charge), (b) benefits both contract and other work (prorate via cost allocation base per CAS 418), or (c) necessary to overall business operations (pool charge). Document allocation methodology. Step 4: Verify CAS/GAAP compliance (FAR 31.201-2(a)(3)) — confirm cost treated consistently with contractor's disclosed accounting practices (CAS 401); confirm proper period recognition and classification. Step 5: Check FAR 31.205 specific cost principles — apply bright-line unallowable determinations (entertainment 31.205-14, advertising 31.205-1 except recruitment/bid solicitation, interest 31.205-20, lobbying 31.205-22, fines and penalties 31.205-15, contributions 31.205-8). Step 6: Review contract terms — apply any additional allowability restrictions specific to this contract. Step 7: Document determination — record test results, supporting data, and conclusion; retain for 3 years post-final payment per FAR 4.703.
summary: Sequential four-test procedure applying reasonableness, allocability, CAS/GAAP compliance, and specific cost principle analysis to determine cost element allowability

label: DFARS 225 Foreign Acquisition Compliance Process
content: Step-by-step process for DFARS 225 foreign acquisition restriction compliance. Step 1: Identify applicable clauses — review contract for inclusion of 252.225-7001 (BAA), 252.225-7009/7014 (specialty metals), 252.225-7021 (TAA), 252.225-7012 (Berry Amendment); note which restrictions apply. Step 2: Buy American Act analysis (252.225-7001) — for each delivered end product, document country of origin; verify domestic end product status (U.S.-made components ≥50% of component cost); identify qualifying country exceptions per DFARS 225.872-1; obtain manufacturer's country of origin certifications. Step 3: Specialty metals compliance (252.225-7009/7014) — identify all specialty metals content (titanium, zirconium, hafnium, tungsten, tantalum, steel alloys, nickel alloys) in deliverable items; trace melting and production to US or qualifying country; collect certifications from mills, processors, and raw material suppliers; document any commercial item exceptions or COTS exceptions. Step 4: Trade Agreements Act determination (252.225-7021) — verify end product qualifies as US-made or designated country; document substantial transformation analysis for assembled items; obtain country-of-origin certifications from suppliers. Step 5: Berry Amendment compliance — for clothing, food, tents, hand tools, stainless steel flatware, synthetic fabric purchased under DoD contracts: verify 100% domestic source unless exception (contingency operations, domestic non-availability determination) obtained from contracting officer. Step 6: Qualifying country verification — check DFARS 225.872-1 list; confirm bilateral agreement in force; retain documentation. Step 7: Assemble documentation package — country of origin certs, mill certs, processing records, exception approvals; make available for government audit. Step 8: Subcontract flowdown — incorporate DFARS 225 clauses at all subcontract tiers for items that become part of the deliverable.
summary: Process for BAA analysis, specialty metals traceability, TAA country determination, Berry Amendment verification, and supply chain documentation under DFARS 225

label: Contract Type Selection Criteria
content: Decision framework for selecting FFP, CR, T&M, or IDIQ contract type per FAR Part 16. FIXED-PRICE (FFP/FP-EPA): Select when requirements are definitive and stable, reliable cost estimating data exists, commercial items or mature products, performance risk is low and controllable by contractor. Contractor bears cost risk. FP-EPA appropriate when contract >1 year and significant economic uncertainties (materials, labor). COST-REIMBURSEMENT (CPFF/CPIF/CPAF): Select when requirements are developmental or unstable, cost cannot be estimated with sufficient confidence for FFP, technical risk is high, government directs performance approach. Government bears cost risk; contractor must have adequate accounting system (FAR 16.301-3). CPIF/CPAF: Add when meaningful incentives exist to motivate performance improvement. T&M/LABOR HOUR (FAR 16.601): Select only when no other type is suitable; requires determination and findings; preferred for services of uncertain duration/level of effort. Ceiling price required; government bears cost risk above ceiling. IDIQ (FAR 16.504): Select when quantity and delivery schedule cannot be predetermined; covers minimum guaranteed order and maximum ceiling; issued against schedule via delivery/task orders. Requires minimum guaranteed quantity. REGULATORY PREFERENCE ORDER: FFP > FPIF > CPIF > CPAF > CPFF > T&M. Cost-plus prohibited for commercial items. T&M requires CO determination and 12-month limitation without extension justification.
summary: Decision matrix for FFP, CPFF, CPIF, CPAF, T&M, and IDIQ selection based on requirement maturity, cost estimating confidence, technical risk, and regulatory constraints

label: Source Selection Method Determination
content: Criteria for selecting among Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA), Best Value Tradeoff, and Sole Source. LPTA (FAR 15.101-2): Use when — requirement is clearly defined with objective, measurable pass/fail criteria; commercial or non-developmental item; risk of technical failure is low; past performance risk is low; no meaningful value expected from technical capability above threshold. LPTA preferred by DFARS 215.101 for DoD when requirement is mature and performance risk is not appreciable. Award to lowest-priced technically acceptable offeror; no tradeoffs permitted. BEST VALUE TRADEOFF (FAR 15.101-1): Use when — technical capability above minimum adds meaningful value to the government; past performance significantly affects probability of mission success; cost/technical/schedule tradeoffs are warranted and can be documented. Evaluation factor weights must be disclosed with relative importance. SSA must document tradeoff rationale showing benefit of higher-rated proposal justifies cost premium (or vice versa). SOLE SOURCE (FAR 6.302): Use when — only one responsible source can satisfy requirements; unusual and compelling urgency; national security; follow-on work for competitive contract. Requires written J&A approved at threshold-based level: >$750K to $15M = Head of Contracting Activity; >$15M = Agency Senior Procurement Executive; publish to SAM.gov for 15 days (unless urgency or security). SMALL BUSINESS SET-ASIDE: Mandatory if requirement can be met by two or more small businesses at fair market price (FAR 19.502-2); use LPTA or best value within set-aside. Decision documented in acquisition plan or source selection plan.
summary: Decision criteria for LPTA vs best-value tradeoff vs sole source based on requirement definition, technical risk, performance complexity, and competition availability

label: Cost Allowability Threshold Decision Criteria
content: Specific thresholds and bright-line rules for determining allowability of individual cost elements. COMPENSATION (FAR 31.205-6): Allowable ceiling per NDAA Section 702/739 benchmark (annually adjusted by DoD); executive compensation compared to benchmark using Form 1018; reasonable for services rendered per labor market surveys (BLS, Radford, Mercer). CONSULTANT/PROFESSIONAL SERVICES (31.205-33): Compare rate to established rates for similar services; require time records and statement of work; costs for directly related business activity of legal, financial, accounting, engineering services allowable. TRAVEL (31.205-46): Domestic per diem — FTR rates (GSA published); international — JTR rates; first-class travel unallowable except: emergency, medical necessity, available coach >14-hour flight. ENTERTAINMENT (31.205-14): Zero allowable regardless of business purpose; any cost for amusement, diversion, social activity, and costs directly associated with such activities — entirely unallowable. ADVERTISING (31.205-1): Unallowable except: recruitment advertising, acquisition of scarce items, disposal of scrap/surplus, costs required by contract, and classified advertising for competitive acquisitions. IR&D/B&P (31.205-18): Allowable to extent allocable; no cost ceiling under FAR (DoD ceiling per DFARS 231.205-18 for major contractors required to report to government); costs must represent independent effort not otherwise funded. INTEREST (31.205-20): Interest on borrowings, bond discounts, and financing costs — always unallowable regardless of purpose. PRE-CONTRACT COSTS (31.109): Allowable only with written CO authorization; costs incurred before contract effective date require specific approval; CLIN coverage must be negotiated.
summary: Bright-line thresholds, benchmarks, and specific rules for executive compensation, travel, entertainment, advertising, and interest cost allowability under FAR 31.2

label: Contract Clause Matrix Template
content: TEMPLATE PURPOSE: Systematically identify and map required FAR/DFARS clauses to contract characteristics before solicitation issuance and at each modification. MATRIX COLUMNS: Clause Number | Clause Title | FAR/DFARS Citation | Applicability Basis (mandatory/threshold/conditional) | Dollar Threshold | Contract Type (FFP / CR / T&M / IDIQ — check applicable) | Commercial Item (Y/N) | Small Business Program | Subcontract Flowdown Required (Y/N) | Flowdown Threshold | Notes. CATEGORY TABS: Tab 1 — Mandatory Clauses (all contracts regardless of value): 52.202-1 Definitions, 52.203-17 Contractor Employee Whistleblower, 52.222-26 Equal Opportunity, 52.225-13 Restrictions on Certain Foreign Purchases, DFARS 252.203-7000 series as applicable. Tab 2 — Threshold-Based Clauses: 52.203-13 Code of Business Ethics (>$6M, >120 days); 52.215-2 Audit and Records (>SAT, CR types); 52.215-10/11 Price Reduction for Defective Pricing (>$2M certified cost threshold); 52.219-series (SB programs by dollar threshold); 52.230-series CAS (>CAS threshold: $2M). Tab 3 — Contract Type Specific: 52.216-7 Allowable Cost and Payment (CR only); 52.232-16 Progress Payments (FFP >SAT); 52.246-2 Inspection of Supplies (non-commercial). Tab 4 — Program/Content Specific: DFARS 252.204-7012 (if CDI present); 252.211-7003 IUID (unit acquisition ≥$5K or serially managed); 252.225-7009 specialty metals (if applicable); 252.246-7001 FAT (if first article required). Tab 5 — Subcontract Flowdown Map: for each clause in Tabs 1-4, specify flowdown obligation per 52.244-6 and clause-specific flowdown instructions; flowdown threshold; whether to subcontractor of what type/tier. REVIEW PROCEDURE: Complete matrix at solicitation stage; update at each significant modification; retain completed matrix as contract file document per FAR 4.802.
summary: Matrix template mapping applicable FAR/DFARS clauses by mandate type, dollar threshold, contract type, and flowdown obligation for pre-award clause selection

label: FAR/DFARS Pre-Award and Post-Award Compliance Checklist
content: PRE-AWARD COMPLIANCE CHECKLIST: □ Market research documented per FAR Part 10 with methodology and results; □ Commercial item determination or exclusion documented; □ Acquisition plan approved (if ≥$25M or requiring HCA approval); □ Competition strategy documented; J&A approved if other than full and open competition; □ Small business program goals assessed; set-aside determination made; subcontracting plan included if >$750K non-SB prime (>$1.5M construction); □ Evaluation factors with relative importance stated in solicitation (FAR 15.304); □ Certified cost or pricing data obtained, or exception documented (FAR 15.403) for >$2M threshold; □ Contractor responsibility determination per FAR 9.104 (financial resources, integrity, performance record, technical capability); SAM.gov active registration verified; □ DFARS 252.204-7008/7019/7021 CMMC representation verified; □ Foreign acquisition restrictions reviewed (BAA, specialty metals, TAA, Berry); □ Contract clause matrix completed and reviewed against contract type/value; □ DPAS rating assigned if defense priority item. POST-AWARD COMPLIANCE CHECKLIST: □ Accounting system adequacy — DCAA approval obtained where required (CR contracts); □ Property management system — government property receipt, records, and inventory procedures established (FAR 45); □ Progress payment surveillance — cumulative unliquidated progress payments vs. 80% (large)/90% (small) ceiling monitored; □ CUI protection — NIST 800-171 implemented, SPRS score on file, 72-hour incident reporting active; □ IUID compliance — items ≥$5K unit acquisition cost marked and submitted to DoD IUID Registry; □ Quality system — contractor QMS per applicable standard (AS9100D or FAR 46.202-4 higher-level QR) maintained; □ Subcontract flowdown — clause matrix flowed down, subcontractor compliance monitored; subcontracts reported in FSRS if ≥$30K (FFATA); □ EEO and labor standards — VETS-4212 filed annually (>$150K); certified payrolls if Davis-Bacon or SCA applicable; □ Termination readiness — stop-work order compliance procedures established; settlement proposal process documented; □ Contract closeout — final invoice/voucher submitted within 120 days of completion; final indirect rate negotiation initiated; government property disposed; DD Form 1594 executed.
summary: Pre-award and post-award compliance checklist covering major FAR/DFARS requirements from solicitation planning through contract closeout

label: Manage capacity loading analysis across work centers
content: Calculate available capacity hours per work center: shifts/week × hours/shift × planned efficiency factor (typically 0.85 for LRIP). Load planned WO demand against available capacity using standard hours per routing step. Build 8-12 week forward-looking capacity loading chart by work center. Identify overloaded work centers (demand >85% available) as bottleneck candidates — flag those on the critical path of Tier 0 committed deliveries first. Distinguish peak overloads (1-2 week spikes, absorb with overtime) from sustained overloads (3+ weeks, require structural response: subcontract release, resequencing, or demand deferral with PM concurrence). Update chart weekly as new WOs open and completions post in MES. KEY INPUTS: operator skill certification matrix (which operators are available and certified per work center), equipment PM windows (block capacity in advance), material on-dock dates (do not load demand against a work center if upstream material is not confirmed). OUTPUT: color-coded capacity loading chart — green ≤75% utilization, yellow 75-90%, red >90% — by work center and week. Sustained yellow or red >2 weeks triggers L4 recovery strategy decision.
summary: System: Calculate available hours by shift and work center, compare against WO demand, and identify capacity bottlenecks 8-12 weeks ahead

label: Decide schedule recovery strategy when production disruptions occur
content: When a disruption impacts the critical path, identify root cause first — design change delay, supplier slip, manufacturing yield failure, or resource constraint — then select recovery strategy. OVERTIME: authorize when overload duration ≤2 weeks, certified operators are available, and fatigue-induced rework risk is acceptable; limit to ≤20% above standard hours per week; Production Manager authorization sufficient. SUBCONTRACT TO JOB SHOP: appropriate when overload exceeds 2 weeks, qualified outside processor exists for the affected operations, and operations are non-safety-critical; requires Quality Manager approval for special processes; plan for 3-6 week activation lead time. RESEQUENCE: pull forward unblocked WOs from lower-priority programs to maintain labor utilization while blocked critical-path work waits; requires PM concurrence when inter-program sequence shifts; document rationale. CUSTOMER DELIVERY DATE RENEGOTIATION: last resort only when critical path slip exceeds all recovery options; requires PM + customer concurrence; assess contractual penalty and CPARS impact before committing. CRITICAL PATH RULE: apply recovery resources only to work centers on the critical path — off-critical-path overloads have schedule float and do not require recovery action. Communicate recovery plan with milestone dates to PM within 24 hours of disruption identification.
summary: Decision: Select overtime, subcontract, resequence, or delivery date renegotiation based on disruption root cause, duration, and critical path impact

label: Weekly master production schedule with capacity loading chart and schedule adherence metrics
content: Publish weekly by Thursday COB for the following week plus 8-week rolling forward view. REPORT SECTIONS: (1) MPS TABLE — WO number, program, variant, routing step sequence, planned start/complete by shift, operator assignment, material status (confirmed / at-risk / missing), tooling status, priority tier; flag AT-RISK WOs in yellow. (2) CAPACITY LOADING CHART — rows = work centers, columns = weeks; show planned hours vs. available hours and utilization percentage; color-code green/yellow/red per loading thresholds; annotate overloaded cells with mitigation action. (3) SCHEDULE ADHERENCE METRICS (prior week actuals) — WO on-time start rate (target ≥95%), WO on-time complete rate, capacity utilization by work center (target 70-75% average), expedite events count, WOs added to schedule after freeze. (4) OPEN RISKS AND MITIGATIONS — schedule threats (material at-risk, design change pending, yield concern) with owner and planned mitigation action. DISTRIBUTION: production supervisors, Quality Manager (inspection resource planning), Supply Chain Manager (material pull timing), Program Manager (delivery commitment visibility).
summary: Output: Weekly master production schedule with 8-week capacity loading chart, WO assignments, and prior-week schedule adherence metrics

label: Manage work order release gating criteria
content: Before releasing any WO to the shop floor, verify four gating criteria in MES: (1) MATERIAL AVAILABILITY — all BOM-required materials confirmed on-dock or in staging per ERP; 'in transit' or 'on order' status does not qualify; short-kit check identifies missing components; partial-kit release permitted only for Tier 0 programs with PM authorization and documented risk acceptance. (2) TOOLING READINESS — all required fixtures, jigs, cutting tools, and gages verified available in tooling register: not checked out, not in calibration, not in repair, not overdue for calibration; tooling overdue for calibration is removed from service and cannot support WO release. (3) OPERATOR CERTIFICATION — at least one certified operator confirmed available on the planned shift for each routing operation; verify against current skill certification matrix; expired certification = operator is ineligible, no exceptions (expired cert is AS9100D nonconformance). (4) WORK INSTRUCTION CURRENCY — work instructions for all routing operations are at current drawing revision; if a pending ECO affects any routing step, WO is held until ECO is dispositioned (incorporate change, formally defer, or document proceed-with-risk with QM and PM sign-off). LOG: record gating verification result and releasing supervisor identity in MES WO record at time of release for audit traceability.
summary: System: Verify material availability, tooling readiness, operator certification, and work instruction currency before releasing WOs to the shop floor

label: Decide work order priority when multiple WOs compete for the same work center
content: When available work center capacity is insufficient to run all queued WOs simultaneously, sequence using a three-factor priority hierarchy: (1) PROGRAM TIER (primary sort) — Tier 0 committed delivery > Tier 1 pilot > Tier 1B prototype; never displace Tier 0 work for lower tiers without PM authorization; within Tier 0, use customer-defined delivery priority when multiple programs are Tier 0. (2) DELIVERY COMMITMENT DATE (secondary sort within same tier) — sort by schedule float ascending, where float = (committed delivery date − today) − (remaining standard hours ÷ daily work center capacity); WOs with least float are most time-critical; using raw dates without float calculation mispriorizes long-duration WOs. (3) WIP AGE (tiebreaker within same tier and same float) — WO with oldest in-process start date takes priority to prevent chronic pre-emption; flag any WO open >2× standard cycle time as priority escalation to PM — chronic displacement indicates a systemic scheduling problem. OVERRIDE CONDITIONS: quality hold resolution WOs (clearing a production block) take immediate priority above all other factors; NCR-driven rework WOs schedule after current priority sequence unless PM declares recovery emergency. DOCUMENTATION: record priority assignment rationale in MES dispatch comment whenever non-standard priority is applied so the decision is traceable.
summary: Decision: Sequence competing WOs at shared work centers using program tier priority, delivery schedule float, and WIP age as a three-factor hierarchy

label: Daily shop floor dispatch list by work center with priority-sequenced WO assignments
content: Generate and distribute before each shift start for every active work center. DISPATCH LIST FORMAT per work center: (1) Header — work center ID, shift date, supervisor name, available capacity (hours). (2) Priority-sequenced active WO table — columns: priority rank, WO number, program/variant, part description, routing operation, standard hours, planned start time, operator assignment (from skill matrix), material status (confirmed / at-risk), special notes (inspection hold points, ECO awareness, quality alerts). (3) On-hold WO section (separate) — WOs at this work center currently on hold with hold reason, hold owner, and expected resolution date. (4) Prior shift completion summary — WOs completed vs. planned, cycle time variance, holds placed during shift. GENERATION: export from MES using L4 priority sequence logic; supervisor reviews and adjusts for shift-specific constraints (operator callouts, equipment status) before posting; post physically at work center AND in MES supervisor dashboard. REAL-TIME UPDATES: supervisor updates dispatch list when holds are placed or resolved during shift; MES hold reasons must be entered within the same shift they occur. END-OF-SHIFT: supervisor reports actuals vs. plan for schedule adherence tracking; variances feed weekly MPS schedule adherence metrics.
summary: Output: Daily dispatch list by work center with priority-ranked WO assignments, operator allocation, material status, and prior-shift completion actuals

label: Conduct MRL sub-thread assessments across 10 manufacturing dimensions
content: Assess each DoD MRL Deskbook sub-thread by collecting objective evidence against Deskbook criteria for the target MRL at the current gate. EVIDENCE TYPES BY SUB-THREAD: (1) Technology — process maturity vs. TRL; evidence: process qualification records, technology transition plan. (2) Design — design stability, DFM completion; evidence: open ECO count, DFM review records. (3) Cost and Industrial Base — should-cost model maturity, supply base risk; evidence: should-cost study, qualified supplier list coverage. (4) Materials — material and special process qualification; evidence: material certs, qualified material/process specifications. (5) Process Capability and Control — process yield and Pp/Ppk data, SPC deployment; evidence: capability studies, control plans. (6) Quality Management — QMS compliance, MRB process, inspection planning; evidence: AS9100D compliance assessment, inspection plans. (7) Manufacturing Workforce — skill availability, training completion, certification currency; evidence: skill matrix, training records, certification logs. (8) Facilities — facility and tooling adequacy for planned production rate; evidence: FCA, equipment list vs. production plan, tooling lead-time analysis. (9) Manufacturing Management — production planning, cost tracking, schedule management maturity; evidence: MPS, EVMS status, EAC trend. (10) Producibility — producibility risk identification, DFM closure status; evidence: PFMEA completion, producibility assessment report. Score 1-10 per Deskbook criteria at target MRL; document scoring rationale and evidence package reference for each sub-thread. Sub-threads below target MRL become manufacturing risk entries.
summary: System: Score all 10 MRL Deskbook sub-threads against objective evidence packages; identify gaps from target MRL at each program gate

label: Determine MRL risk disposition — formal mitigation plan vs. risk acceptance at program phase gates
content: For each below-target MRL sub-thread, determine disposition before gate review: FORMAL MITIGATION PLAN REQUIRED when: (1) gap prevents achieving contractual production rate or delivery schedule if not addressed; (2) gap involves a process with no demonstrated production-representative performance (Ppk unknown, no trial-run data, not validated on production equipment); (3) gap is a single-point-of-failure in workforce or industrial base with no identified backup; (4) gap is in a safety-critical manufacturing process (load path operations, special processes, flight hardware fabrication). RISK ACCEPTANCE appropriate when: (1) gap is in a non-critical-path sub-thread with sufficient schedule float; (2) mitigation action is already planned and funded within the IMS (risk is time-bounded with an exit criterion); (3) compensating controls reduce consequence to acceptable level (e.g., 100% incoming inspection compensates for below-target process capability in sub-thread 5); (4) PM and customer have reviewed and acknowledged risk at gate. DOCUMENTATION REQUIREMENT: every below-target sub-thread must have a disposition — either a formal maturation action plan (owner, milestones, exit criteria, funding) or a documented risk acceptance rationale signed by PM; undocumented gaps at a gate review are not acceptable. PRIORITIZATION GUIDANCE: concentrate formal mitigation on sub-threads 4 (Materials), 5 (Process Capability), and 6 (Quality Management) — these have the highest probability of driving production yield and schedule failures.
summary: Decision: Determine whether each below-target MRL sub-thread requires a formal mitigation plan or qualifies for documented risk acceptance at program phase gates

label: MRL assessment report with sub-thread scores, gap analysis, and maturation action plan for program milestone review
content: Produce for each program gate (SDD, CDR, PDR, LRIP start) and update monthly between gates. REPORT STRUCTURE: (1) EXECUTIVE SUMMARY — current MRL achieved vs. target MRL for gate; sub-thread at-target count vs. below-target count; overall manufacturing risk posture (Low/Medium/High) with one-sentence rationale. (2) SUB-THREAD SCORECARD — table: 10 sub-threads as rows; columns: target MRL, assessed MRL, gap (target − assessed), risk level, mitigation plan or risk acceptance reference; color-code green (at/above target), yellow (1 below target with mitigation underway), red (≥2 below target or no disposition). (3) SUB-THREAD EVIDENCE SUMMARIES — one paragraph per sub-thread citing objective evidence and scoring rationale. (4) GAP ANALYSIS — for each below-target sub-thread: gap description, root cause, schedule/cost consequence if unresolved, disposition (mitigation plan or risk acceptance with rationale). (5) RISK REGISTER INTEGRATION — manufacturing risks mapped to program risk register with probability and consequence ratings; linked to IMS manufacturing readiness events. (6) MATURATION ACTION PLAN — tabular summary of all open actions: owner, action description, due date, completion criteria, current status. (7) DCMA EVIDENCE PACKAGE INDEX — list of evidence documents available for DCMA review at applicable milestones. DISTRIBUTION: PM, Chief Engineer, DCMA (as applicable), program customer at milestone review; monthly update distributed to PM and Engineering.
summary: Output: MRL assessment report with 10 sub-thread scores, gap analysis, risk register linkage, and maturation action plan for program milestone review

label: Integrate in-process inspection hold points into MES work order routing
content: Coordinate with Quality Manager to define and embed inspection hold points in WO routing before WO release. HOLD POINT PLACEMENT CRITERIA: (1) Before irreversible operations — potting, sealing, encapsulation, structural fastening, heat treat, plating, painting; any operation that permanently conceals or changes the prior step. (2) At configuration decision points — before installing variant-specific components, verify correct CBC assignment and component P/N match. (3) At assembly phase boundaries — end of sub-assembly before integration, end of integration before functional test. (4) For first-article builds — inspection hold at every routing step for first unit of a new part number or first unit post-ECO affecting form/fit/function. MES IMPLEMENTATION: Add operation type = 'INSPECTION HOLD' in WO routing; MES must prevent WO step advancement without QC digital sign-off or traveler stamp; define for each hold point: inspection type (dimensional/visual/functional), responsible party (in-process QC inspector vs. operator self-check per L3 825 role boundary framework), accept/reject criteria reference (drawing callout or work instruction section). STAFFING COORDINATION: when placing hold points, confirm with Quality Manager that QC inspection staffing supports expected cycle time at planned production rate — inspection must not become the schedule bottleneck. TARGETS: standard dimensional hold cycle time <4 hours; complex CMM or functional test hold <8 hours; production held >8 hours awaiting inspection sign-off is a QC resource escalation trigger.
summary: System: Define and embed in-process inspection hold points in MES WO routing in coordination with Quality, with placement before irreversible operations and configuration decision points

label: Decide whether to proceed with production before FAI closure when schedule pressure exists
content: When FAI is open and schedule pressure exists, evaluate proceed-with-risk decision using the following framework. DO NOT PROCEED when: (1) open FAI items are on safety-critical or structural features — risk of an uninspected fleet is disproportionate to schedule benefit; stop until characteristics are measured and conforming. (2) Open items indicate a process capability problem (nonconformances found on trial articles) — additional units will compound scrap and rework cost; resolve root cause first. (3) Customer contract or program directive prohibits production before FAI closure — verify contract terms; unauthorized production may create contractual and warranty liability. PROCEED WITH COMPENSATING CONTROLS when: (1) Open FAI items are administrative only (missing certificate, incomplete Form 2 paperwork) with all measurements already conforming — proceed with enhanced inspection; close administrative items in parallel within 5 business days. (2) Open items are on non-critical, non-structural features with confirmed conforming measurements on trial articles — proceed with documented risk acceptance, PM sign-off, and 100% inspection on open features for all articles produced under risk; quarantine produced articles until FAI closes. (3) FAI is in progress (measurement not yet complete) and schedule float is zero — proceed maximum one unit at a time with QC co-witness on open characteristics; halt immediately if any nonconformance is found. AUTHORIZATION: proceed-with-risk on open FAI requires PM + Quality Manager written concurrence; document decision, risk accepted, compensating controls, and serial numbers of articles produced under risk in QMS and WO traveler.
summary: Decision: Evaluate risk of producing uninspected articles vs. schedule impact when FAI is not yet closed; proceed only on administrative gaps with PM and QM written concurrence

label: Weekly production quality metrics report — first-pass yield, inspection cycle time, open FAI status
content: Publish weekly (Monday morning for prior week) covering all active programs. REPORT SECTIONS: (1) FIRST-PASS YIELD BY OPERATION — table of routing operations with units attempted, units passing first inspection, FPY%, and 4-week trend; flag operations <85% FPY in yellow, <75% in red; FPY declining two consecutive weeks triggers root-cause escalation to Quality Manager and Manufacturing Engineering. (2) IN-PROCESS INSPECTION CYCLE TIME — average and maximum hold time at each inspection gate (target <4 hrs standard, <8 hrs complex); flag gates where production was held >8 hours awaiting QC sign-off; identify if inspection backlog is a recurring schedule constraint. (3) OPEN FAI STATUS BY PROGRAM — table: program, part number, drawing revision, FAI trigger event, open item count, days open, blocking/non-blocking classification, responsible owner, target close date; items open >30 days flagged red; items blocking production flagged with schedule impact estimate. (4) IN-PROCESS NONCONFORMANCE SUMMARY — NCR count by operation and root cause category (design, process, supplier, operator error) for the week vs. 4-week rolling average; new NCR types (not seen before) highlighted. (5) REWORK LABOR HOURS — actual rework hours as % of total production hours by program (target <10%); breakdown by root cause; >15% triggers Production Manager + Quality Manager joint review. (6) QUALITY HOLD TIME IMPACT — total production hours lost to quality holds during the week; programs and operations affected. DISTRIBUTION: Production Manager, Quality Manager, Program Manager, Manufacturing Engineering; Quality Manager owns FAI and inspection sections; Production Manager owns schedule impact section.
summary: Output: Weekly production quality metrics report covering first-pass yield by operation, in-process inspection cycle time, open FAI status by program, and rework labor percentage

label: Manage tooling register and tool life tracking in CMMS/MES
content: Maintain a tooling register in CMMS/MES covering all production tooling and fixtures. REGISTER FIELDS PER TOOL: Tool ID, description, associated part numbers and routing operations, current location (work center or storage rack), status (active/in-repair/retired/calibration-due), tool life limit (cycle count or calendar interval), cycles used to date, calibration due date, last calibration certificate reference, custodian. CYCLE COUNT TRACKING: MES records a tool usage transaction per WO operation completion; CMMS aggregates cycle counts against life limit; supervisor and tooling coordinator alerted at 80% of life limit. CALIBRATION DUE DATE TRACKING: calibration interval set per measurement system requirement; CMMS generates calibration work order 30 days before due date; overdue tools flagged for immediate removal from service and blocked from WO release gating check. LOCATION TRACKING: check-out recorded when tool leaves storage for production, check-in when returned; location disputes investigated within same shift. FIXTURE WEAR: CMM re-verification due date tracked per use count or calendar interval; fixtures due for re-verification quarantined until re-verified. OUTPUT: weekly tooling availability exception report listing all tools with status other than active and expected return date; feeds L5 monthly tooling status report.
