summary: System: Maintain tooling register in CMMS/MES tracking cycle counts, calibration due dates, current location, and status for all production tooling and fixtures

label: Decide repair-vs-replace for worn or failed tooling
content: When a tool reaches its life limit, fails in service, or is flagged as worn, decide between repair and replacement. DECISION INPUTS: (1) Remaining useful life — for pre-limit failures, assess whether failure was isolated (proceed with repair) or indicates true end-of-life (replace); for measurable wear (fixture datum degradation, gage drift), estimate remaining life from wear rate trend. (2) Repair cost estimate — obtain quote from toolroom or vendor within 48 hours; include rework labor, replacement components, and re-qualification cost (e.g., CMM re-verification, recalibration). (3) Replacement cost and lead time — use tooling register for standard replacement cost; custom tooling lead time 8-26 weeks must be factored against production need date; if replacement lead time exceeds need, repair is the default unless a spare tool is available. (4) Root cause — operator misuse or process excursion favors repair; repeated failure on the same tool favors replacement with improved design. DECISION RULE: Repair if repair cost <60% of replacement cost AND repair restores full functional life AND repair lead time meets production need. Replace if repair cost ≥60% of replacement cost, or design is inadequate, or same tool has failed twice in one production program. DOCUMENTATION: log decision rationale in CMMS tool record; initiate replacement procurement simultaneously with repair authorization when lead time risk exists — do not wait for repair to fail before ordering.
summary: Decision: Repair vs. replace worn tooling based on remaining useful life, repair cost vs. replacement cost, and replacement lead time

label: Monthly tooling status report — availability, life limits, calibration compliance, production delays
content: Publish monthly tooling status report to Production Manager and Supply Chain Manager by 5th business day of each month. REPORT SECTIONS: (1) TOOLING AVAILABILITY RATE — tools active and available ÷ total tools in register (target ≥95%); tools in repair, retired, or out for calibration counted as unavailable; month-over-month trend. (2) TOOLS APPROACHING LIFE LIMIT — all tools within 20% of life limit: Tool ID, description, associated operations, cycles used, life limit, percent consumed, projected limit date based on production plan; flag any appearing on the current 90-day production schedule. (3) CALIBRATION COMPLIANCE RATE — tools with current calibration ÷ total calibration-required tools (target 100%); any overdue tools listed with days overdue and production impact (WO blocked or proceeded under risk acceptance). (4) TOOLING-RELATED PRODUCTION DELAYS — all WO holds or delays during the month attributable to tooling unavailability, calibration overdue, or in-repair status: WO number, program, hold duration, schedule consequence. (5) OPEN PROCUREMENT ACTIONS — replacement tooling POs in process with expected delivery vs. production need date; flag lead time risk items. DISTRIBUTION: Production Manager, Supply Chain Manager; Quality Manager receives calibration compliance section for AS9100D §7.1.5 records.
summary: Output: Monthly tooling status report covering availability rate, tools approaching life limit, calibration compliance rate, and tooling-related production delays

label: Collect and reconcile actual production costs by work order from MES and ERP
content: Collect and reconcile actual WO production costs from three source systems before closing each WO. LABOR COST (from MES): extract operator clock-in/clock-out transactions by WO and operation; multiply actual hours by loaded labor rate (base wage + burden per HR-published rate table); reconcile MES total labor hours against payroll for the period — discrepancy >2% triggers MES data entry audit. MATERIAL COST (from ERP): pull material consumption transactions issued to the WO; apply purchase order price or standard cost per cost accounting policy; flag unplanned material transactions (items not on BOM) as potential yield loss or rework events requiring root cause. OVERHEAD ALLOCATION: apply overhead rate ($/direct labor hour or $/machine hour per cost accounting policy) to each WO based on actual direct labor hours; verify overhead pool absorption is consistent with monthly actual overhead expenditures. RECONCILIATION STEPS: (1) Sum labor + material + overhead per WO = actual total cost. (2) Compare to WO standard cost (engineered standard hours × rates + BOM cost at standard). (3) Calculate variance $ and %. (4) Flag WOs with variance >5% of standard for L4 root cause categorization. (5) Resolve data integrity gaps (missing labor transactions, unmatched material issues) before WO close in ERP — target close within 24 hours of build completion. DATA DEPENDENCIES: MES requires 100% labor clock-in/out compliance; ERP material transactions must post at point of use, not batch entry; overhead rates updated by Finance quarterly.
summary: System: Collect and reconcile actual WO production costs from MES labor transactions, ERP material transactions, and overhead allocation; flag variances >5% of standard for root cause categorization

label: Categorize root cause of WO cost variances exceeding 5% of standard
content: For each WO with variance >5% of standard cost, assign one root cause category before the WO is closed and rolled into monthly reporting. FOUR CATEGORIES: (1) REWORK — actual rework hours (MES transactions coded to rework operations) exceed 10% of total WO labor hours; labor cost inflated by unplanned rework not included in standard. Response: coordinate with Quality Manager on defect elimination; charge rework cost to quality variance account, not manufacturing efficiency. (2) YIELD LOSS — material consumption variance: actual material issued to WO exceeds BOM quantity by >5% in cost value; caused by scrap, damage, over-issue, or process waste. Response: investigate lot-level material yield; if supplier-caused, initiate NCR and cost recovery claim. (3) LEARNING CURVE — expected variance on early-production WOs (typically first 20-30 units per new part number or post-major design change); actual labor hours trending toward standard as operators gain experience per Wright's Law. Response: accept as forecast variance; monitor curve against projection; if curve plateaus above standard, re-examine procedure clarity rather than operator aptitude. (4) ENGINEERING CHANGE — rework or scrap driven by an ECO issued after WO standard cost was baselined; standard not yet updated to reflect design change. Response: charge to program budget, not manufacturing efficiency; document ECO number; request standard cost update from Finance. UNRESOLVED VARIANCES: if root cause cannot be assigned within 5 business days of WO close, escalate to Manufacturing Engineering; do not carry WOs as unresolved >1 week. Record category code in ERP WO record for monthly variance report aggregation.
summary: Decision: Assign root cause category — rework, yield loss, learning curve, or engineering change — to WO cost variances >5% of standard to direct appropriate corrective action

label: Monthly program-level production cost summary with variance analysis and corrective action tracking
content: Publish monthly program-level production cost summary to Finance and Program Manager by 10th business day of following month. REPORT SECTIONS: (1) PROGRAM COST SUMMARY — total actual cost for month vs. standard cost; year-to-date actual vs. budget; TCPI (To Complete Performance Index) if EVMS-applicable. (2) VARIANCE BY COST ELEMENT — table with rows = direct labor, direct material, overhead; columns = standard cost, actual cost, variance $, variance %; flag elements with variance >5%. (3) VARIANCE BY ROOT CAUSE CATEGORY — aggregate all WO variances by category (rework, yield loss, learning curve, engineering change): total $ per category, % of total production cost, month-over-month trend; rework as % of manufacturing cost flagged if >10% (escalate at >15%). (4) TOP VARIANCE WORK ORDERS — WOs with highest absolute variance (top 10 or all WOs >$2K variance): WO number, program, part number, standard cost, actual cost, variance $, root cause category, corrective action assigned. (5) CORRECTIVE ACTION TRACKING — open actions from prior months: description, owner, original due date, current status; closed actions with outcome and verified effectiveness. (6) LEARNING CURVE TREND — for programs in ramp-up, plot actual unit cost by production sequence number against Wright's Law projection; flag if actuals trend above projection for two consecutive months. DISTRIBUTION: Finance (cost accounting), Program Manager, Operations leadership; Quality Manager receives rework variance section.
summary: Output: Monthly program-level production cost summary with actual vs. standard by cost element, root cause variance breakdown, corrective action tracking, and learning curve trend

label: Maintain operator skill certification matrix cross-referencing operators against certified processes
content: Maintain the operator skill certification matrix as the authoritative scheduling and compliance source for workforce capability. MATRIX STRUCTURE: rows = all production operators; columns = all certifiable processes organized by work center or operation family; cell content = certification tier level + expiration date + certification document reference. TIER LEVELS: Tier 1 = supervised only; Tier 2 = independent with periodic oversight; Tier 3 = fully independent, authorized to supervise Tier 1/2. EXPIRATION TRACKING: each cell carries an expiration date; color-code — green = current with >60 days to expiry; yellow = expiring within 60 days; red = expired or within 30 days. Expired cells render the operator ineligible for that process immediately with no exceptions — operating on expired certification is an AS9100D §7.2 nonconformance. UPDATE CADENCE: update within 24 hours of any certification event (new cert awarded, expiry, recertification, operator departure); monthly audit to verify matrix accuracy against underlying certification records in the QMS. SPECIAL PROCESS FLAGS: NADCAP-required special process columns (NDT, welding, chemical processing) marked distinctly; external certification lead times (2-6 months) noted in column header as a scheduling risk reminder. INTEGRATION: skill matrix feeds WO release gating (L3 2223), scheduling decisions, SPOF identification (L4 under this task), and monthly workforce readiness report (L5 under this task). RETENTION: certification records retained per AS9100D §7.2; matrix itself updated as a living document, not a static snapshot.
summary: System: Maintain operator skill certification matrix cross-referencing all operators against certified processes with tier levels, expiration dates, and color-coded status for scheduling and AS9100D §7.2 compliance

label: Prioritize cross-training investments based on single-point-of-failure risk
content: Determine which cross-training assignments to schedule first from the pool of identified gaps. SPOF IDENTIFICATION: query skill certification matrix for all production processes with fewer than 2 operators certified at Tier 2 or above — these are single-points-of-failure (SPOFs) where any unplanned absence stops production on that process. PRIORITIZATION CRITERIA (apply in order): (1) PRODUCTION CRITICALITY — Tier 0 committed-delivery SPOFs rank first; Tier 1 pilot SPOFs rank second; Tier 1B prototype rank third. Within same tier, processes on the near-term critical path rank above off-critical-path. (2) ABSENCE RISK — processes where the sole qualified operator has scheduled leave, chronic absence history, or is approaching retirement are elevated above processes with reliably present operators. (3) CERTIFICATION LEAD TIME — processes certifiable in 2-4 weeks (quick wins) scheduled immediately; NADCAP or external special process certifications (2-6 months lead time) initiated in parallel as long-range actions — do not defer starting just because completion is distant. (4) OPERATOR AVAILABILITY — cross-training assigned only to operators with capacity; avoid pulling operators off Tier 0 committed production for cross-training without PM authorization. SCHEDULING RULE: limit to one active cross-training assignment per operator at a time to prevent dilution of attention. TARGET: no Tier 0 process with fewer than 2 Tier 2+ certified operators at any point on the rolling 12-week horizon. DOCUMENTATION: maintain rolling 12-week cross-training plan updated monthly; share with HR for shift scheduling coordination.
summary: Decision: Prioritize cross-training investments by identifying processes with fewer than 2 certified operators as SPOFs, ranked by production tier criticality, absence risk, and certification lead time

label: Monthly workforce readiness report — certification compliance, SPOFs, upcoming expirations, cross-training progress
content: Publish monthly workforce readiness report to Production Manager, HR, and Quality Manager by 5th business day of each month. REPORT SECTIONS: (1) CERTIFICATION COMPLIANCE RATE — active certifications that are current ÷ total active certifications in matrix (target 100%); any expired certifications listed with operator name, process, expiration date, and production schedule impact; expired certs are AS9100D nonconformances — flag to Quality Manager on the day discovered, not monthly. (2) SINGLE-POINT-OF-FAILURE PROCESSES — all processes with fewer than 2 Tier 2+ certified operators: process name, work center, programs impacted, sole qualified operator, production tier, and cross-training plan status; month-over-month SPOF count trend (target: zero SPOFs for all Tier 0 processes). (3) UPCOMING EXPIRATIONS — certifications expiring within 60 days: operator, process, expiry date, recertification action scheduled (Y/N/Date); NADCAP/external special process certs expiring within 90 days listed separately (longer scheduling lead time); flag any without a scheduled recertification action. (4) CROSS-TRAINING PLAN PROGRESS — active cross-training assignments: operator, target process, start date, expected completion date, current stage (classroom/supervised/assessment), percent complete; completions this month vs. plan; delayed assignments with reason and revised target. (5) WORKFORCE COVERAGE SUMMARY — by work center: operator count certified Tier 2+ on primary process vs. minimum threshold for active production shifts; flag work centers below minimum coverage. DISTRIBUTION: Production Manager, HR (shift scheduling), Quality Manager (AS9100D §7.2 compliance), Program Manager (awareness of Tier 0 SPOF risks).
summary: Output: Monthly workforce readiness report covering certification compliance rate, single-point-of-failure processes, upcoming expirations within 60-90 days, and cross-training plan progress

label: Conduct value stream mapping and maintain current-state and future-state VSM per production value stream
content: Conduct value stream mapping (VSM) to identify and quantify waste in production flow. VALUE STREAM DEFINITION: identify major value streams (typically one per program family or product line); map from raw material receipt through customer delivery. CURRENT-STATE MAP CONTENT: each production step (machining, sub-assembly, assembly, test, inspection) with cycle time, changeover time, uptime %, and operator count; push/pull signals between steps; WIP queue size and wait time between each step; information flow (how orders are communicated to each step); total flow time and value-added time (calculate value-added ratio = VA time ÷ total flow time). MAPPING CADENCE: annually per value stream and after any significant process change (new program launch, major equipment addition, layout change, design change affecting production routing). FUTURE-STATE MAP: identify target condition by eliminating or reducing top 3-5 waste categories (wait, transport, overproduction, inventory, motion, over-processing, defects); set quantified targets (e.g., reduce flow time from 12 to 6 days, reduce WIP from 8 to 3 units between assembly and test). 90-DAY ACTION PLAN: decompose future-state gap into specific improvement actions with owners, due dates, and measurable success criteria; review monthly; update future-state map as improvements land. DOCUMENTATION: maintain current-state and future-state maps as controlled documents (A3 format); post in production area and share with Production Manager and Engineering; store in lean program records folder.
summary: System: Conduct annual VSM and maintain current-state and future-state value stream maps per major production value stream; develop and track 90-day gap closure action plans

label: Select lean improvement projects based on waste elimination impact, difficulty, and production disruption risk
content: When VSM analysis, 5S audits, OEE data, and NCR trends surface multiple lean improvement candidates, decide which projects to activate and in what order. SCORING CRITERIA (score each 1-5, higher = more favorable): (1) WASTE ELIMINATION IMPACT — estimated reduction in flow time, WIP carrying cost, defect rate, or labor per unit; quantify from VSM data; convert to financial impact where possible (WIP reduction × carrying cost, defect reduction × rework cost per unit × annual volume). (2) IMPLEMENTATION DIFFICULTY — effort required in person-hours, capital cost, and dependency on design or engineering changes; low-capital 5S, standard work, and kanban adjustments score high; equipment moves or layout changes score low. (3) PRODUCTION DISRUPTION RISK — risk and duration of production interruption during implementation; improvements executable during planned taper phases or off-shift score high; improvements requiring production line shutdown score low; include recovery risk if implementation fails (cost and time to revert). WEIGHTED SCORE: (impact × 0.5) + (ease × 0.3) + (low disruption × 0.2); rank all candidates; activate top 3 as simultaneous kaizen projects (limiting simultaneous projects preserves team focus). OVERRIDE CONDITIONS: safety-related improvements (5S elimination of slip/trip hazards) take priority over score. Improvements directly on the delivery critical path of a Tier 0 program escalate to high priority. SEQUENCING PREFERENCE: schedule easy wins first to build momentum and demonstrate ROI before committing to high-disruption projects. RE-SCORE: refresh opportunity list monthly at lean metrics review; incorporate new opportunities from VSM updates, 5S audits, and NCR Pareto analysis.
summary: Decision: Score and select lean improvement projects by waste elimination impact, implementation difficulty, and production disruption risk; limit to top 3 active kaizen projects to maintain execution focus

label: Monthly lean metrics dashboard — OEE by work center, 5S scores, WIP vs. kanban targets, kaizen tracking
content: Publish monthly lean metrics dashboard to Production Manager and Operations leadership by 5th business day of each month. Post summary version on production floor visual board. DASHBOARD SECTIONS: (1) OEE BY WORK CENTER — Availability × Performance × Quality for each work center; target ≥85% for mature processes, ≥75% for processes in ramp-up; color-code green/yellow/red; month-over-month trend; decomposed view showing which OEE factor drives below-target performance (availability = downtime, performance = cycle time loss, quality = yield loss) to direct corrective action. (2) 5S AUDIT SCORES — monthly 5S score by area (0-100, target ≥80); Sort, Set-In-Order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain scored separately by area supervisor; quarterly cross-area audit by rotating team; scores posted in each area; any Sustain category scoring <60 triggers corrective action with due date. (3) WIP LEVELS VS. KANBAN TARGETS — for each internal kanban-controlled staging area: actual WIP count vs. target card count, percent above/below; areas consistently above target (buffer creeping) flagged for card count reduction review; areas hitting zero (stockout risk) flagged for capacity or lead time investigation. (4) KAIZEN EVENT TRACKING — active and recently completed kaizen events: event name, target process, status (planned/in-progress/complete), improvement hypothesis, before-and-after metrics (quantified); overdue active events flagged red with revised completion date. (5) VALUE STREAM SUMMARY — total flow time and value-added ratio per value stream vs. future-state VSM target; trend indicating progress toward target. DISTRIBUTION: Production Manager, Operations leadership, Engineering (OEE, kaizen); floor visual board shows OEE and 5S sections only (not full report).
summary: Output: Monthly lean metrics dashboard covering OEE by work center, 5S audit scores, WIP levels vs. kanban targets, and kaizen event completion tracking

label: Commodity Jurisdiction Determination Process
content: Step-by-step process for determining ITAR vs EAR jurisdiction. Step 1: Document item technical characteristics, design parameters, performance specifications, and intended end-use. Step 2: Compare against USML Categories I–XXI enumerated items; apply 'specially designed' analysis per 22 CFR 120.41 — catches items designed/modified for military application or providing performance parameters not found in commercial equivalents; releases items already on CCL, available commercially without restriction, or in the public domain. Step 3: If not clearly USML, classify against CCL (15 CFR 774 Supp. 1) — review ECCN technical parameters, units of measurement, and technical notes; evaluate 600-series ECCNs for items transitioned from USML via Export Control Reform. Step 4: If no CCL match, designate EAR99 — still subject to embargoed-country restrictions (15 CFR 746), denied-party prohibitions, and WMD end-use controls (15 CFR 744). Step 5: For ambiguous items, submit Commodity Jurisdiction (CJ) request to DDTC via DTRADE — include complete technical description, engineering drawings, specifications, end-use statement, and manufacturer declaration; concurrent BIS referral available for CCL/USML boundary disputes. DDTC statutory response period is 45 days. Step 6: Document determination in product classification database and export compliance records; retain CJ letter and supporting analysis ≥5 years. Critical rule: no de minimis exception under ITAR — any ITAR-controlled content in a foreign-produced item may render the entire item ITAR-controlled.
summary: CJ determination process: USML enumeration → specially designed test (22 CFR 120.41) → CCL/ECCN classification → EAR99 → CJ request via DTRADE; 5-year record retention; no de minimis rule

label: License Exception and Exemption Evaluation Process
content: Systematic process for evaluating EAR license exceptions and ITAR exemptions before initiating a license application. EAR Process — Step 1: Confirm item ECCN and reason(s) for control. Step 2: Screen destination against embargoed countries (15 CFR 746: Cuba, Iran, North Korea, Syria; Russia/Belarus comprehensive controls post-2022) — comprehensive embargoes eliminate most exceptions. Step 3: Screen all parties against Entity List, Denied Persons List, Unverified List, and OFAC SDN List; any hit eliminates exception availability. Step 4: Evaluate applicable exceptions: TMP (§740.9) — temporary export for exhibition/demo/testing, return within 12 months, no title transfer; RPL (§740.10) — 1-for-1 replacement of defective/broken parts; TSR (§740.6) — technology/software by US persons to approved country nationals; GOV (§740.11) — US and foreign government agencies, international organizations; STA (§740.20) — strategic trade authorization for NATO and close allies (significant military equipment and most 600-series items excluded); LVS (§740.2) — low-value shipments below ECCN-specific threshold. Step 5: Document exception symbol in AES/EEI filing; maintain exception justification records ≥5 years. ITAR Exemption Process — Step 1: Confirm destination not on current 22 CFR 126.1 restricted country list — NO ITAR exemption is available for 126.1 destinations under any circumstances. Step 2: Evaluate 22 CFR 125.4 technical data exemptions: (b)(1) publicly available data; (b)(2) fundamental research (results ordinarily published, no access/dissemination restrictions); (b)(4) data in furtherance of a US government contract with required flowdown; (b)(9) intra-company to US persons abroad. Step 3: Evaluate 22 CFR 123.16 hardware exemptions (limited to specific USML categories, quantities, and dollar thresholds). Step 4: Document exemption basis, items covered, parties, date, and Empowered Official authorization; exemptions are self-executing but must be contemporaneously documented; retain ≥5 years.
summary: EAR exception evaluation (TMP/RPL/TSR/GOV/STA/LVS) and ITAR exemption evaluation (22 CFR 123.16, 125.4) with 126.1 absolute bar check, denied party screening, and documentation requirements

label: TAA/MLA Agreement Drafting Process
content: Procedure for preparing and submitting Technical Assistance Agreements and Manufacturing License Agreements per 22 CFR 124. Step 1 — Scope definition: identify all USML-controlled articles, technical data, and defense services to be covered; identify all authorized foreign parties (legal entity names, addresses, countries, individual authorized employees if required). Step 2 — Agreement type: TAA for defense services (training, maintenance, technical assistance, controlled technical data transfer); MLA for foreign production/manufacturing of USML defense articles. Step 3 — Draft agreement body per 22 CFR 124 requirements: purpose clause; enumerated USML category and item descriptions; authorized parties; authorized territory; authorized end-use and end-items; agreement duration; Empowered Official identification. Step 4 — Draft required provisos: non-retransfer/non-reexport restrictions; certification requirements for foreign parties; security requirements for controlled data; Congressional notification conditions; audit and inspection rights; termination clauses. Step 5 — Congressional notification assessment per AECA Section 36(c) and 22 CFR 124.11: notification required when aggregate value exceeds $14M for major defense equipment (MDE) under MLA, $50M for non-MDE significant military equipment under MLA, or $100M for defense services under TAA; notification period is 30 days (15 days for NATO members, Australia, Israel, Japan, New Zealand, South Korea). Step 6 — Submit via DTRADE: cover letter citing 22 CFR 124; draft agreement; technical data package description; Form DSP-83 (Nontransfer and Use Certificate) if significant military equipment or classified; end-use statements from each foreign party. Step 7 — DDTC processing: non-Congressional cases typically 30–60 days; Congressional cases 60–90+ days; respond to RFIs within stated deadline. Step 8 — Amendments: any change in scope, parties, articles, territory, or duration requires amendment via DTRADE; review typically 15–30 days. Step 9 — Record retention: retain signed agreement, all amendments, and records of all activities performed thereunder ≥5 years after agreement expiration.
summary: TAA/MLA drafting: scope → agreement type → body/provisos → Congressional notification thresholds ($14M/$50M/$100M) → DTRADE submission → amendments → 5-year retention

label: Empowered Official Decision Process
content: Procedure for Empowered Official (EO) review and authorization of exports per 22 CFR 120.25. EO Qualifications: senior official of the US registrant; must be a US person; has authority to inquire into any export transaction and take corrective action; cannot authorize exports the EO knows or has reason to know violate ITAR. Step 1 — Transaction referral: export coordinator submits complete transaction package to EO — jurisdiction determination memo, all parties (consignee, end-user, intermediaries), items/quantities/values, proposed authorization basis (license number, TAA/MLA reference, or exemption citation), and end-use statement. Step 2 — EO review checklist (all six must be confirmed): (a) USML jurisdiction confirmed and current; (b) all parties screened against DDTC Debarred List (22 CFR 127.6), BIS Entity/Denied Persons/Unverified Lists, OFAC SDN/Consolidated Sanctions List — screening dated within 30 days of proposed export; (c) destination not on current 22 CFR 126.1 prohibited country list; (d) license conditions or exemption conditions fully satisfied; (e) end-use and end-user consistent with stated authorization; (f) TAA/MLA coverage confirmed active and in scope if applicable. Step 3 — EO authorization decision: Authorize — EO executes signature on license application, exemption record, or shipping authorization; Authorize with conditions — additional documentation or verification required before release; Deny — document reason; escalate to legal counsel and evaluate voluntary disclosure if violation suspected. Step 4 — Delegation of authority: EO may delegate authorization in writing, naming delegate, scope of authority, and effective dates; delegate must meet 22 CFR 120.25 qualifications; EO retains ultimate legal responsibility; delegation list reviewed annually and updated on personnel changes. Step 5 — Record-keeping: EO maintains authorization log — date, authorizing person, items, recipients, authorization basis, any conditions imposed; retained ≥5 years per 22 CFR 122.5. Step 6 — Compliance oversight: EO must have independent reporting authority to Board/CEO level; EO initiates voluntary disclosure per 22 CFR 127.12 upon discovery of potential violation; EO cannot be overridden by business units or sales personnel on compliance determinations.
summary: Empowered Official (22 CFR 120.25) procedure: transaction referral package, 6-point review checklist (jurisdiction/parties/destination/license/end-use/TAA), authorization decision, delegation documentation, 5-year log, independent reporting authority

label: Commodity Jurisdiction Determination Criteria
content: Decision matrix for USML vs CCL vs EAR99 classification. LEVEL 1 — USML ENUMERATION: Is the item specifically named or described in USML Categories I–XXI? Key aerospace/defense categories: Cat IV (missiles, rockets, guided munitions, armed UAVs, space launch vehicles); Cat VIII (military aircraft, engines, aircraft components, ground support equipment); Cat XI (military electronics — C4ISR, EW, radar, military GPS); Cat XV (spacecraft, satellites, space-qualified components, ground control equipment); Cat XIX (gas turbine engines designed/modified for military platforms). If enumerated → ITAR-controlled; analysis stops. LEVEL 2 — SPECIALLY DESIGNED TEST (22 CFR 120.41 'catch-and-release'): CATCHES — (a) designed or modified to provide military-application performance parameters not achievable by commercial equivalents (G-hardening, radiation tolerance, temperature range, EMI shielding); (b) item designed for use in a specific military platform or to a military specification without commercial equivalent. RELEASES — (a) item is a mass-market commercial product sold to general public without restriction; (b) item appears on CCL with matching ECCN and performance parameters; (c) DDTC or BIS has previously determined item is EAR-controlled; (d) item is in public domain without access or dissemination restrictions. If caught and not released → ITAR; if released → CCL analysis. LEVEL 3 — 600-SERIES CCL ITEMS: Items transitioned from USML via Export Control Reform classified in x600y ECCNs (e.g., 0A606 military vehicles/vessels, 0B606 test equipment, 1C608 military explosives). 600-series = EAR-controlled but NOT EAR99; license required for virtually all destinations (AT minimum, frequently NS, MT, RS controls); no license exception STA for significant military equipment in 600-series. LEVEL 4 — STANDARD ECCN CLASSIFICATION: Match item against CCL technical parameters by category (0–9) and product group (A–E); verify all controlled parameters against ECCN thresholds. LEVEL 5 — EAR99: No CCL match → EAR99. Still prohibited: to 15 CFR 746 embargoed countries; to denied/entity/SDN-listed parties; for WMD end-uses (15 CFR 744.2/3/4/6); military end-use rule (744.21) for China, Russia, Venezuela, Myanmar.
summary: USML vs CCL decision matrix: USML enumeration by category (I–XXI) → specially designed catch-and-release (22 CFR 120.41) → 600-series identification → standard ECCN classification → EAR99 with residual restrictions

label: License Requirement Determination Matrix
content: Decision framework integrating item classification, destination, end-use, and end-user to determine whether an export license is required. GATE 1 — ITAR ABSOLUTE BAR (22 CFR 126.1): Is the destination country on the current 22 CFR 126.1 restricted list? If yes → export absolutely prohibited regardless of item or authorization; no license can be issued; no exemption is available; consult legal counsel and evaluate voluntary disclosure if transaction was previously attempted. GATE 2 — ITAR USML ITEMS: All USML items require DDTC license (DSP-5 permanent export, DSP-73 temporary export/import) unless a specific ITAR exemption (22 CFR 123.16, 125.4, 126.4) applies and destination is not 126.1-restricted. Exemption claim → document and proceed. No exemption available → submit DSP-5/DSP-73. GATE 3 — EAR ECCN COMMERCE COUNTRY CHART (15 CFR 774 Supp. 1 & 2): Cross-reference item ECCN against destination country column headers (AT, CB, CC, CW, EI, FC, FT, MT, NP, NS, RS, SL, SS, UN, XP). If 'X' at ECCN row / country column intersection under any applicable reason for control → license required for that reason; evaluate license exception before applying. No 'X' → no license required from classification alone; proceed to Gate 4. GATE 4 — END-USE RESTRICTIONS (15 CFR 744): License required regardless of ECCN/destination if: nuclear weapons/propulsion end-use known or suspected (744.2); missile development/production end-use (744.3); chemical/biological weapons end-use (744.4/6); military end-use/end-user in China, Russia, Venezuela, or Myanmar for items meeting 744.21 parameters. GATE 5 — PARTY-BASED RESTRICTIONS: Screen all transaction parties against: BIS Entity List (15 CFR 744 Supp. 4 — license required, often policy of denial); BIS Denied Persons List (export prohibited); BIS Unverified List (STA exception unavailable); OFAC SDN/Consolidated Sanctions (export prohibited; OFAC license required); DDTC Debarred List (22 CFR 127.6 — ITAR export prohibited). Any positive hit → consult legal counsel; most cases = export prohibited. GATE 6 — OUTCOME DETERMINATION: License required → submit DSP-5/DSP-73 (ITAR) or BIS-748P (EAR); License exception/exemption available → document and proceed; Prohibited → do not export; notify EO; evaluate voluntary disclosure.
summary: 6-gate license requirement matrix: 126.1 absolute bar → ITAR USML default license → CCL/Commerce Country Chart X-reference → end-use restrictions (744.2-21) → party screening (Entity/DPL/SDN/Debarred) → outcome

label: Technology Control Plan Template
content: TCP template for ITAR/EAR compliance programs. SECTION 1 — PURPOSE AND SCOPE: applicable regulations (ITAR 22 CFR 120–130, EAR 15 CFR 730–774); controlled technology categories and USML/ECCN designations covered; facility locations in scope; Empowered Official name and contact. SECTION 2 — FACILITY PHYSICAL ACCESS CONTROLS: perimeter access controls and badge system; restricted/controlled area designations (posted signage required); visitor log requirements; identification of areas where ITAR/CUI technical data is used, stored, or displayed; foreign national access determination authority (EO approval required). SECTION 3 — VISITOR AND FOREIGN NATIONAL PROCEDURES: pre-visit screening form (purpose, citizenship, employer, position); denied party screening for all foreign visitors before access; nationality-specific restrictions (22 CFR 126.1 country nationals may not access controlled data or areas); access authorization form with EO signature; escort assignment and escort training requirements; visitor access log retained ≥5 years; debriefing on controlled data handling at visit conclusion. SECTION 4 — IT SECURITY FOR CONTROLLED TECHNICAL DATA: network segmentation — ITAR technical data stored and processed on physically or logically separated system; FIPS 140-2/3 validated encryption for CUI/ITAR data at rest and in transit; cloud service approved list (FedRAMP Moderate minimum for CUI; dedicated GovCloud for higher sensitivity); email policy — ITAR data transmitted only via encrypted channels to authorized recipients; portable media (USB/external drives) prohibited in controlled areas without written EO authorization; VPN with MFA required for remote access; access control lists limiting ITAR data folders to authorized persons only; audit logging of access to controlled data. SECTION 5 — CONTROLLED TECHNICAL DATA IDENTIFICATION AND MARKING: marking requirement for all ITAR-controlled documents, drawings, and specifications: 'ITAR CONTROLLED — 22 CFR 120–130 — Disclosure to foreign nationals without DDTC authorization is prohibited'; CUI marking per NARA 32 CFR Part 2002 for government-furnished data; classification review process for newly generated technical data before any release or transfer. SECTION 6 — EMPLOYEE TRAINING: initial training within 30 days of hire for all employees with access to controlled data (minimum 2 hours); annual refresher (minimum 1 hour); content: ITAR/EAR overview, deemed export rules (technology release to foreign nationals), export authorization procedure, marking and handling, violation reporting chain; minimum 80% passing score on knowledge assessment; training records retained ≥5 years; foreign national employee awareness of applicable access restrictions documented. SECTION 7 — EXPORT AUTHORIZATION MANAGEMENT: active license log (license number, authorized items, authorized parties, expiration, conditions, use tracking); TAA/MLA file (signed agreements, amendments, activities performed thereunder); exemption documentation (citation, items, parties, date, EO authorization signature); AES/EEI electronic filing verification and monthly reconciliation; pre-shipment compliance checklist signed by EO before each export. SECTION 8 — ANNUAL SELF-ASSESSMENT: audit checklist covering all TCP sections; independent review team (at least one member not responsible for day-to-day compliance); findings documented with corrective actions and due dates; EO and management sign-off; results retained ≥5 years; findings reported at annual management review. SECTION 9 — VIOLATION REPORTING AND VOLUNTARY DISCLOSURE: employee reporting procedure with anonymous option; EO notification within 24 hours of suspected violation; investigation procedure and legal hold on relevant records; voluntary disclosure initiation per 22 CFR 127.12 (DDTC) or 15 CFR 764.5 (BIS) upon EO determination of apparent violation; no disclosure to external parties without legal counsel concurrence. SECTION 10 — RECORDS RETENTION: all export-related records retained ≥5 years from date of export or expiration of license/TAA/MLA, whichever is later, per 22 CFR 122.5.
summary: TCP template: facility access controls, visitor/foreign national procedures, IT security for ITAR data, marking requirements, employee training (2-hr initial/1-hr annual), export authorization log, annual self-assessment, violation reporting, 5-year retention

label: Export License Application Package Checklist
content: Application package checklists for DSP-5, DSP-73 (ITAR via DTRADE), and BIS-748P (EAR via SNAP-R). DSP-5 (Permanent Export): □ All 22 blocks completed in DTRADE — applicant DDTC registration number, foreign consignee legal name/full address/country, USML category and paragraph, item description, quantity, unit price, total value; □ Technical description sufficient to verify USML classification and match to specific USML paragraph; □ Form DSP-83 (Nontransfer and Use Certificate) — required for all significant military equipment (22 CFR 120.45), classified technical data, and items DDTC designates in license conditions; must be executed by foreign consignee and, if required, foreign government authority; □ End-use statement: specific application, end-item system integration, and confirmation no re-transfer without DDTC authorization; □ Supporting technical documents: engineering drawings or data sheets, specifications, manufacturer letter, prior CJ determination letter; □ Purchase order or contract reference; □ TAA/MLA agreement number reference (if applicable); □ Empowered Official signature certifying accuracy and ITAR compliance of all statements. DSP-73 (Temporary Export/Import): □ All DSP-5 requirements above; □ Confirmed return date and return mechanism (carnet, re-import customs documentation); □ Foreign storage location (company name, address, country, security measures); □ Confirmation no title transfer to foreign party; □ Re-export restrictions cited. BIS-748P (EAR Export License via SNAP-R): □ Items 1–9: applicant information, other party authorization (if broker/freight forwarder signing); □ Items 10–20: ECCN, commodity/technology/software description, CCATS number (if applicable), quantity, unit of measure, value; □ Items 21–25: purchasing entity, intermediate consignee, ultimate consignee (legal entity name), end-user; □ License reason and basis (specific reason for control column from CCL); □ Form BIS-748P Supplement 1 (end-user certification letter) for transactions requiring additional documentation; □ Form BIS-711 (Statement by Ultimate Consignee and Purchaser) for commercial end-user transactions; □ Technical specifications covering all controlled ECCN parameters demonstrating classification accuracy; □ Explanatory cover letter on company letterhead describing transaction and end-use. ALL APPLICATIONS — Universal Requirements: □ Denied party screening documentation for all transaction parties — lists screened, date of screening (within 30 days of application), results; □ Internal jurisdiction determination memo or CJ letter on file; □ Empowered Official pre-application review and authorization record; □ Complete copy of application package retained in export compliance files ≥5 years. Post-Submission: □ Track application in DTRADE/SNAP-R; respond to RFIs within stated deadline (typically 10–30 days or per agency notice); □ Review license conditions thoroughly before shipment; □ File AES/EEI in ACE citing license number before export.
summary: DSP-5/DSP-73/BIS-748P application checklists: USML/ECCN technical description, DSP-83 triggers (SME/classified), BIS-711/748P supplements, EO signature, pre-application denied party screening, 5-year file retention

label: Voluntary Disclosure Format
content: Template for DDTC and BIS voluntary self-disclosure. DDTC VOLUNTARY DISCLOSURE (22 CFR 127.12 — submit to Director, Office of Defense Trade Controls Compliance): SECTION 1 — INTRODUCTION: identify document as voluntary disclosure under 22 CFR 127.12; company name, address, DDTC registration number; point of contact (name, title, phone, email); outside legal counsel contact if retained; date company became aware of potential violation. SECTION 2 — DESCRIPTION OF ALLEGED VIOLATION(S): for each violation — date(s) of occurrence; nature of violation (unauthorized export, unlicensed transfer of technical data, unauthorized deemed export, AES non-filing, license condition violation, unauthorized re-transfer); items involved (USML category and paragraph, item description, quantity, USD value); destination country; all foreign parties involved; ITAR provision(s) apparently violated (22 CFR citation). SECTION 3 — CHRONOLOGICAL NARRATIVE: detailed timeline from transaction initiation through discovery of potential violation; key personnel decisions and actions at each stage; discovery date and circumstances of discovery. SECTION 4 — ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS: primary root cause (classification error, process gap, training deficiency, IT system failure, supervision failure, ambiguous regulatory interpretation); contributing factors; assessment of whether violation is isolated or systemic. SECTION 5 — AFFECTED TRANSACTIONS TABLE: columns — transaction date | items/quantity | USD value | foreign recipient | destination country | authorization attempted (license number or exemption citation if any) | transfer method (shipment, email, server access). SECTION 6 — STATUS OF VIOLATIVE ITEMS/DATA: current location of controlled items or data; ability to retrieve; security measures applied by foreign recipient; any corrective action taken (recall, end-use verification letter, written security commitments). SECTION 7 — REMEDIAL AND PREVENTIVE ACTIONS: corrective actions implemented with completion dates (updated classification procedures, training completed, IT access controls modified, personnel actions); preventive actions to preclude recurrence; compliance program enhancements; management commitment statement signed by Empowered Official. SECTION 8 — REQUEST FOR MEETING: offer to meet with DDTC compliance staff (standard practice for complex disclosures; facilitates guidance on corrective actions). BIS VOLUNTARY DISCLOSURE (15 CFR 764.5 — submit to Director, Office of Export Enforcement): Same structure with adaptations — cite 15 CFR 764.5; reference EAR provisions violated (15 CFR 730–774); ECCN(s) and license exception claimed (if any). Timing: initial notification (summary) submitted within 30 days of discovery; full written disclosure within 60 days of initial notification (extensions available by request to OEE); submit to OEE field office with jurisdiction or OEE HQ. PROCESSING NOTES: Both DDTC and BIS treat voluntary disclosure and cooperation as significant mitigating factors; can result in warning letter, substantial civil penalty reduction, or no-action; place legal hold on all relevant records immediately upon awareness of potential violation; do not communicate details externally until legal counsel advises.
summary: DDTC/BIS VD template: violation description, chronological narrative, root cause, affected transactions table, item status, remedial actions, meeting request; BIS 30-day initial/60-day full submission; legal hold triggers

label: USML/CCL commodity jurisdiction determination
content: Classify items, software, and technology as subject to ITAR (USML) or EAR (CCL). Process: (1) Apply commodity jurisdiction analysis — USML categories I–XXI cover defense articles with predominant military application or no commercial counterpart; (2) Submit CJ request to DDTC (via DECCS/DTAG) when jurisdiction is ambiguous — required before export activities proceed; CJ requests must include technical description, comparable commercial items, and funding source; (3) For EAR-jurisdiction items, assign ECCN from CCL using product characteristics and reasons for control (NS, MT, RS, NP, CB, AT, EI); EAR99 applies to items not enumerated on the CCL but still subject to restrictions for embargoed destinations; (4) Document classification rationale with technical parameters supporting USML category or ECCN selection; (5) Maintain classification database by part number with USML category/ECCN, CJ determination date, and review cycle. USML category revisions (Export Control Reform) moved many items from ITAR to EAR — validate classifications against current USML text, not pre-2013 categories.
summary: Task: USML/CCL commodity jurisdiction determination — CJ requests to DDTC, ECCN assignment, and classification database maintenance

label: License application management (DSP-5, DSP-73, DSP-85, TAA, MLA)
content: Prepare and submit export authorization applications to DDTC (ITAR) and BIS (EAR). ITAR license types: DSP-5 (permanent export of defense articles and related technical data) — standard hardware export license; DSP-73 (temporary export) — items exported temporarily for demonstration, repair, or modification with return obligation; DSP-85 (temporary import) — foreign-origin USML items temporarily imported for repair and re-export; TAA (Technical Assistance Agreement) — for furnishing defense services or technical data to foreign persons, including foreign subsidiaries; MLA (Manufacturing License Agreement) — authorizes foreign person to manufacture USML end items. EAR licenses: submitted via SNAP-R system to BIS. Application package requirements: end-user and end-use statements, DSP-83 non-transfer certificate for significant military equipment, technical description, and supporting documentation. License register: maintain active licenses with expiration dates, authorized quantities, authorized parties, consumption records, and license condition tracking. 22 CFR 123.26 requires records of all exports under each license for 5 years.
summary: Task: Prepare and manage DSP-5, DSP-73, DSP-85, TAA, and MLA applications — license register, consumption tracking, and condition compliance

label: Technology Control Plan (TCP) development and maintenance
content: Develop and maintain facility-level Technology Control Plans establishing controls for foreign person access to ITAR-controlled technical data and defense articles. Required TCP components: (1) Classification inventory — all ITAR/EAR items and technical data on-site with USML categories and ECCNs; (2) Physical access controls — restricted areas, badge requirements, and escort procedures for foreign visitors; (3) Electronic access controls — IT security restricting network access to export-controlled data, encrypted storage, access logs, and separation of controlled data repositories; (4) Visitor procedures — pre-visit nationality screening, visa status verification, visit request/approval process, and escort requirements; (5) Employee procedures — annual ITAR training acknowledgment, TCP obligations, and foreign travel briefings; (6) Deemed export procedures — license determination workflow before any foreign person accesses controlled technology. TCP must be reviewed annually and updated within 30 days of material changes to facility, workforce, or product portfolio. TCP is required by 22 CFR 125 and reviewed by DDTC during compliance inquiries — paper TCPs not operationalized are a leading enforcement vulnerability.
summary: Task: Develop and maintain facility-level TCP covering physical controls, IT security, visitor procedures, and deemed export workflows per 22 CFR 125

label: ITAR registration and compliance program management
content: Maintain active DDTC registration and manage the enterprise export compliance program. Registration: (1) Initial registration — required before brokering, exporting, or manufacturing USML defense articles; submit Form DS-2032 with annual fee ($2,250–$2,750 depending on revenue tier as of current DDTC schedule); (2) Annual renewal — submit 60 days before expiration; lapsed registration creates strict liability exposure; (3) Update notifications — notify DDTC within 5 days of changes to registration data (ownership, address, primary ITAR officials per 22 CFR 122.4(b)). Compliance program elements: (a) Written compliance manual covering classification procedures, license management, TCP, training, recordkeeping, and VSD procedures; (b) Empowered official designation — individual with authority to bind the organization on export control matters; (c) Internal compliance audits — annual review of classification database, license consumption, and TCP implementation against regulatory requirements; (d) Recordkeeping — 22 CFR 122.5 requires 5-year retention of all ITAR export records from date of export or license expiration. Program maturity is a material factor in DDTC penalty mitigation.
summary: Task: Maintain DDTC registration, annual renewal, compliance manual, empowered official designation, and internal audit program per 22 CFR Part 122

label: Fundamental research exclusion assessment
content: Assess applicability of the Fundamental Research Exclusion (FRE) to research activities before applying ITAR/EAR controls. ITAR 120.11: research at accredited US institutions that is ordinarily published and shared broadly is excluded from ITAR technical data controls. EAR 734.8: parallel exclusion for EAR-controlled technology. FRE is LOST when: (1) contract or grant contains publication restrictions or pre-publication sponsor review rights beyond notice of co-inventor IP claims; (2) access controls restrict participation by foreign nationals; (3) research results are proprietary and not intended for publication; (4) work is applied research directed toward a specific government system (not basic or fundamental research). Assessment procedure: (a) Review every government contract, grant, and CRADA for publication restriction language; (b) Identify any foreign national access restrictions imposed by the sponsor; (c) If restrictions exist, document that FRE is unavailable and initiate export classification for all research outputs and foreign person access; (d) Communicate FRE determination to Principal Investigator and research team with specific compliance obligations. Document assessment in project compliance record. FRE mis-application is a common enforcement gap at aerospace R&D firms with university partnerships.
summary: Task: Assess FRE applicability per ITAR 120.11 and EAR 734.8 — review publication restrictions, foreign access controls, and document determination for each research program

label: Voluntary self-disclosure (VSD) process
content: Manage voluntary disclosure of potential ITAR or EAR violations to minimize penalty exposure. ITAR VSD (22 CFR 127.12): submit initial notification to DDTC Office of Defense Trade Controls Compliance within 60 days of discovering potential violation; full disclosure report due within 60 days of initial notification (extensions available upon request). EAR VSD (15 CFR 764.5): initial notification to BIS Office of Export Enforcement (OEE) followed by complete narrative submission; BIS treats timely VSD as a significant mitigating factor reducing civil penalties. VSD process: (1) Identify potential violation — unlicensed export, unauthorized deemed export, false statement on license application, failure to file Electronic Export Information (EEI via AES); (2) Conduct internal investigation — scope, timeline, parties, root cause, and all affected transactions; (3) Implement immediate remediation — cease ongoing violations, enhance controls; (4) Prepare disclosure — factual narrative, violation description, mitigating factors (compliance program existence, training, voluntary discovery), and corrective actions implemented and planned; (5) Submit with cover letter from senior official. Do not delay initial notification while awaiting full investigation — initial notification preserves mitigating factor credit. Engage export control counsel before submission.
summary: Task: Identify potential violations, conduct internal investigation, and prepare ITAR VSD to DDTC (22 CFR 127.12) or EAR VSD to BIS (15 CFR 764.5) with corrective action plan

label: ITAR/EAR training program development and delivery
content: Develop and deliver export compliance training for all personnel with access to export-controlled items or technology. Program structure: (1) New employee orientation — ITAR/EAR framework, company classification inventory, TCP obligations, reporting requirements, and consequences of violations; (2) Annual refresher — regulatory updates (USML category revisions, CCL amendments, new OFAC sanctions), internal policy changes, and case studies from recent DDTC/BIS enforcement actions; (3) Role-specific modules — Engineering: technical data controls, CJ request process; Contracts: license requirements and ITAR/EAR flow-down clauses; Shipping/Receiving: EEI filing, license presentation, and recordkeeping; HR: deemed export screening at hire; (4) Training records — maintain completion records with date, content version, and employee acknowledgment signature; retain per 22 CFR 122.5 (5 years); (5) Content currency — update materials within 30 days of significant regulatory changes. Training program quality and documentation are reviewed by DDTC in compliance audits and weighed as a mitigating factor in enforcement proceedings. Untrained personnel accessing controlled technology is a primary root cause cited in DDTC consent agreements.
summary: Task: Develop and deliver role-specific ITAR/EAR training — new hire orientation, annual refresher, role modules, and maintain completion records per 22 CFR 122.5

label: Foreign person access controls and restricted party screening
content: Screen and control foreign person access to export-controlled facilities, technology, and defense articles. Components: (1) Visitor pre-screening — obtain visitor nationality and visa status before facility visit; screen against ITAR 22 CFR 126.1 embargoed countries and EAR Part 740 country groups; require advance export compliance review for access to controlled areas; (2) Restricted party screening — screen all foreign visitors, suppliers, customers, and business partners against: SAM.gov exclusions, BIS Entity List (Supplement 4 to Part 744), BIS Denied Persons List (Supplement 1 to Part 764), OFAC SDN and Consolidated Sanctions lists (including Sectoral Sanctions Identifications List), DDTC Debarment list, UN Security Council Consolidated List; (3) Employee screening — verify citizenship and immigration status at hire and annually for employees with access to ITAR/EAR controlled technology; update deemed export determinations when status changes; (4) Screening tool — use a commercial automated screening platform (e.g., Visual Compliance, Descartes) with daily list updates; manual screening against static PDFs is insufficient for compliance; (5) Recordkeeping — retain screening results with date, list version, and disposition (cleared/flagged/denied) for 5 years; (6) FOREIGN SUBCONTRACTOR ONBOARDING SCREENING: When onboarding a foreign subcontractor, screen not only the entity but also its principals, beneficial owners, and parent company against all restricted party lists; screen the subcontractor's country against OFAC comprehensive and targeted sanctions programs (currently: Cuba, Iran, North Korea, Syria, Crimea/Donetsk/Luhansk regions; targeted programs for Russia, China military entities, and others — verify current OFAC country list at time of screening); re-screen quarterly during active subcontract and within 5 days of any OFAC list update affecting the subcontractor's country. ITAR 22 CFR 126.1 is an absolute prohibition — no license exception permits exports, including deemed exports, to nationals of embargoed countries.
summary: Task: Screen visitors and employees against restricted party lists, control facility access per TCP, and maintain screening records using automated screening platform

label: End-use monitoring and compliance verification
content: Monitor post-shipment end-use of exported defense articles and controlled dual-use items to verify compliance with license conditions. Activities: (1) License condition register — maintain all active licenses with authorized end-use, authorized end-user, geographic restrictions, re-export requirements, reporting obligations, and expiration dates; (2) Post-shipment verification — periodic checks with foreign consignees to confirm items remain with authorized end-user and are used for authorized purpose; document verification method and results; (3) Blue Lantern cooperation — respond promptly and completely to DOS Blue Lantern program pre-license and post-shipment end-use check requests; non-cooperation is an independent violation; (4) Re-export controls — ITAR 22 CFR 123.9 requires prior DDTC approval for re-export; EAR requires license or exception for re-exports based on ECCN and destination country; DSP-83 non-transfer and use certificate required for significant military equipment; (5) Red flag monitoring — identify and escalate diversion indicators: unusual payment terms, reluctance to describe end-use, orders inconsistent with recipient's business profile, requests to ship via freight forwarders to undisclosed third parties; document all red flag reviews per BIS guidance. License condition violations can result in revocation, debarment, and criminal referral independent of the underlying export authorization.
summary: Task: Track license conditions, conduct post-shipment end-use verification, support Blue Lantern checks, enforce re-export controls, and monitor for diversion red flags

label: Subcontractor capability gap analysis
content: Map anticipated RFP requirements (from SOW/PWS, Sources Sought, and FPDS-NG program history) against Yggdrasil Aero organic capability inventory by NAICS code, past performance category, security clearance level, and geographic delivery presence. Identify gaps by task area: technical disciplines, labor category depth, facility requirements (cleared facility, lab accreditation, ISO certification), and small business set-aside credit contribution. For each gap, assess fill options: subcontractor with relevant past performance, key personnel hire, or organic development. Prioritize gaps by Pwin impact—gaps in heavily weighted evaluation areas (technical approach, past performance) take precedence. Document gap analysis in capture plan teaming section with evidence from FPDS-NG and SAM.gov.
summary: System: Map RFP requirement gaps against organic capabilities by task area, evaluation weight, and SB credit contribution; document subcontractor fill options in capture plan

label: NDA execution and pre-negotiation preparation
content: Execute mutual non-disclosure agreements with prospective subcontractors before any substantive technical discussions, proposal strategy sharing, or pricing disclosure. Initiate NDA within 5 business days of confirming subcontractor qualification interest. Use Yggdrasil Aero standard bilateral NDA template covering: definition of confidential information (technical data, pricing, proposal strategy, customer intelligence), permitted use restrictions (bid preparation only for named opportunity and contract number), term (through contract performance if award received), and return or destruction of confidential information on termination. Track NDA execution status for all candidates in the opportunity teaming log. Do not share draft technical sections, work share details, or pricing parameters without an executed NDA on file. Log execution date and signatory in CRM opportunity record. Prepare pre-negotiation position summary before TA discussions begin: identify Yggdrasil Aero's minimum acceptable work share floor, exclusivity requirements, and IP provisions requiring non-negotiable protection.
summary: System: Execute bilateral NDA with candidate subcontractors within 5 days of qualification confirmation; prepare pre-negotiation position summary before TA discussions

label: Teaming agreement provision negotiation
content: Negotiate key TA provisions with selected subcontractors from Yggdrasil Aero standard template: (1) Exclusivity—specify whether bidding-only or extends through award, one-directional or mutual, and geographic or opportunity scope of restriction; (2) Work share—define percentages by task area or CLIN, minimum performance thresholds post-award, and conditions under which work share may be reduced or reallocated; (3) IP and data rights—identify background IP and ownership, allocate foreground IP, incorporate DFARS 252.227-7013/7014 flow-down, address ITAR-restricted technology handling and TAA requirements, and include no-reverse-engineering provisions; (4) FAR/DFARS flow-down clauses—attach mandatory flow-down clause list applicable to the anticipated contract type (FFP, CPFF, T&M) including small business reporting obligations; (5) Proposal participation obligations—deliverables, milestone deadlines, B&P cost-sharing terms; (6) Termination provisions for both proposal phase and post-award restructuring. Document all deviations from standard template with legal rationale before countersigning.
summary: System: Negotiate TA provisions covering exclusivity scope, work share floors, IP/DFARS flow-down, FAR mandatory clauses, proposal participation obligations, and termination terms

label: Legal review coordination for teaming agreements
content: Coordinate legal counsel review of all TA drafts before execution. Initiate review no later than 10 business days before planned TA execution deadline (Gate 3). Submit draft TA package to legal with: opportunity overview (agency, estimated value, contract type, set-aside), subcontractor identity and SAM.gov registration status, capability gap being filled, proposed exclusivity scope, work share percentages, and flagged IP or ITAR items requiring specific legal attention. Track legal review status and open comments in the opportunity teaming log. Address all legal comments before execution; escalate unresolvable issues to BD director for business judgment decision with documented risk acceptance. For TAs with unusual provisions (co-development obligations, shared key personnel, IP licensing back to subcontractor), request advance legal review of those provisions before full draft to avoid schedule delays. File fully executed TA in contract management system linked to CRM opportunity record.
summary: System: Initiate legal TA review 10 days before Gate 3 deadline with full context package; track comment resolution to execution and file executed TA in contract management system

label: Subcontractor selection decision
content: Select subcontractors to include on the teaming structure based on structured evaluation: (1) Capability fit—technical capabilities match identified gaps with relevant past performance evidence (CPARS ratings or references); (2) Past performance quality—Satisfactory or better on comparable scope; (3) Pricing compatibility—labor rates and overhead structure compatible with PTW-informed pricing strategy; (4) Exclusivity availability—subcontractor not already committed to a competing team on this opportunity; (5) SB set-aside compliance and SB credit contribution toward subcontracting plan goals; (6) Security posture—facility clearance level and cleared workforce size matching contract requirements; (7) Organizational conflict of interest screening. For each finalist, document evaluation rationale. Obtain BD director concurrence on selections involving unusual risk (sole-source capability fill, unproven subcontractor, significant IP sharing). Notify non-selected candidates without disclosing competing team composition.
summary: Decision: Select subcontractors based on capability fit, CPARS ratings, pricing compatibility, exclusivity status, SB contribution, clearance posture, and OCI screening

label: Executed teaming agreement checklist
content: Standardized pre-execution checklist verifying all required elements are complete before TA signature: (1) NDA executed and on file with date and signatory; (2) subcontractor SAM.gov registration active and not debarred (confirm at SAM.gov Exclusions); (3) capability gap justification documented in capture plan teaming section; (4) subcontractor selection decision rationale documented and BD director concurred; (5) work share percentages defined by task area and agreed by both parties in writing; (6) exclusivity scope agreed and documented; (7) IP provisions reviewed and approved by legal counsel; (8) DFARS 252.227-7013/7014 data rights flow-down provisions included; (9) FAR mandatory flow-down clause list attached as exhibit; (10) ITAR restriction provisions addressed where applicable; (11) key personnel commitment letters or letters of intent initiated where required; (12) legal review completed with all comments resolved; (13) BD director approval obtained; (14) executed TA filed in contract management system and CRM opportunity record updated with execution date; (15) subcontractor proposal contribution deliverables and deadlines confirmed in writing. Signed by capture manager upon full completion before Gate 3.
summary: Output: Pre-execution TA checklist verifying 15 required elements from NDA through CRM filing; signed by capture manager before Gate 3

label: FPDS-NG incumbent contract data gathering for PTW
content: Extract incumbent contract data from FPDS-NG as primary PTW input. Query by incumbent contractor CAGE code, agency, and NAICS/PSC to retrieve: all contract awards and modifications on the target program (contract number, award date, total obligated value, modification history, period of performance, contract type), option period exercise pattern (all options exercised on time vs. late or unexercised), cost growth indicators (CPFF ceiling increase modifications signaling cost overrun), task order history and average task order value for IDIQ vehicles, and subcontractors listed in FPDS-NG or SAM.gov records. Calculate contract ceiling utilization rate and average modification frequency. Supplement with USASpending.gov sub-award data to estimate subcontractor labor mix contributions. For IDIQ vehicles, calculate historical task order award velocity (obligations per fiscal year). Document all extracted data in a PTW input package with FPDS-NG contract number citations and extraction date for PTW analyst use at engagement kickoff.
summary: System: Extract FPDS-NG incumbent award history, modification patterns, option exercise record, and ceiling utilization into a dated PTW input package for analyst kickoff

label: Competitor rate structure analysis
content: Analyze competitor labor rates and indirect cost structures to inform the competitive pricing estimate component of PTW analysis. Data sources: GSA Schedule pricing data for firms with published rates on applicable schedules (GSA Advantage, eBuy); Forward Pricing Rate Agreements (FPRAs) or Forward Pricing Rate Recommendations (FPRRs) obtained via FOIA requests on prior comparable bid packages; subcontractor quote patterns from prior proposal experience; and prior government debrief data disclosing pricing gaps. Build competitor rate card estimates for top 2–3 ghost team primes by labor category tier: direct labor rates, composite wrap rate range (fringe + overhead + G&A), and fee/profit posture. Flag labor categories where Yggdrasil Aero's internal rates are materially above or below the competitor estimated range. Document rate analysis with data source citations, confidence level per source, and estimation methodology for PTW analyst use. Refresh rate analysis on draft RFP release if labor category definitions shift significantly.
summary: System: Build competitor rate card estimates from GSA schedules, FOIA-obtained FPRAs, and debrief data; flag Yggdrasil Aero rate differentials by category for PTW analyst

label: PTW analyst engagement and management
content: Engage price-to-win analyst early in capture—minimum 6 months before anticipated RFP release for major competitive bids. Internal pricing team: schedule kickoff within 30 days of Gate 1 approval; provide PTW input package (FPDS-NG data, competitor rate analysis, IGCE signals, RFI scope summary). External PTW firm: obtain BD director approval for engagement, issue task order with scope, PTW range estimate deliverable, methodology documentation, and timeline. Brief PTW analyst on: opportunity overview and evaluation approach (LPTA vs. best value), ghost team compositions with confidence levels, customer budget signals (budget exhibit PE#, Congressional appropriation history), and known competitive dynamics. Schedule preliminary PTW findings presentation at Gate 2 preparation milestone. On draft RFP release, brief PTW analyst on requirement changes and request a model refresh if scope shift is material. Ensure PTW analyst deliverable clearly states data sources, confidence levels, and PTW range low/mid/high estimates.
summary: System: Engage PTW analyst at Gate 1 with full input package; schedule Gate 2 preliminary findings; trigger refresh on draft RFP release for material scope changes

label: Cost gap assessment
content: Compare Yggdrasil Aero's internal cost build-up against the PTW analyst estimate to quantify the cost gap by component. Cost gap equals PTW mid-point estimate minus Yggdrasil Aero internal build-up (total cost plus target fee). Analyze gap by element: direct labor (rate differential by labor category), indirect cost structure (overhead and G&A rate gap vs. competitor estimates), subcontractor cost (prime vs. alternative sourcing options), fee/profit (market norms by contract type—FFP vs. cost-plus), and unique cost elements (transition costs, facilities, special tooling). For each gap component, assess closure options: labor rate re-benchmarking or reclassification, workforce sourcing mix adjustment (shift high-volume tasks to lower-cost subcontractor labor), indirect rate reduction initiatives, fee compression within acceptable floor, and subcontractor competitive re-quote. Document gap assessment with closure action, responsible owner, estimated savings, and feasibility rating (high/medium/low). Flag total gaps exceeding 15% of estimated contract value without a credible closure path as no-bid risk factors requiring BD director review.
summary: System: Compare internal build-up against PTW estimate by cost component; assess closure options per element; flag gaps >15% without viable closure as no-bid risk

label: Price-to-win vs. price-to-perform decision
content: Determine pricing strategy based on PTW gap assessment and opportunity strategic value. Options: (1) Price to Win—compress internal cost estimate to PTW range by executing identified cost gap closure actions; accept reduced fee/profit; requires BD director and CFO concurrence on margin impact; appropriate when opportunity is strategically essential (re-compete protection, new agency access, large reference contract value) or when LPTA evaluation makes pricing decisive; (2) Price to Perform—price at Yggdrasil Aero's fully-loaded build-up with standard fee target; appropriate when technical differentiation is sufficient to justify above-PTW pricing under best value trade-off evaluation and cost gap closure is not feasible without unacceptable quality risk; (3) No-Bid—cost gap is irresolvable within acceptable margin floor and competitive Pwin does not justify B&P investment. Decision criteria: evaluation basis (LPTA vs. best value weight distribution), cost gap magnitude and closure feasibility, strategic importance of win, contract type (FFP risk tolerance vs. cost-plus fee environment), and competitor pricing posture confidence. Document decision rationale with BD director signature; feed into capture plan pricing section and proposal cost volume strategy.
summary: Decision: Choose price-to-win, price-to-perform, or no-bid based on PTW gap feasibility, evaluation basis, margin floor, strategic priority, and competitor pricing confidence

label: PTW summary report
content: Standardized PTW summary report delivered at Gate 2 covering: (1) opportunity header—program name, agency, total estimated value, period of performance, contract type, evaluation approach (LPTA/best value and scoring weights); (2) methodology summary—data sources (FPDS-NG, GSA schedules, FOIA data, budget exhibits), estimation approach, and analyst confidence assessment (high/medium/low); (3) PTW estimate range—low/mid/high winning price estimate with associated Pwin implications at each price point; (4) incumbent pricing reference—estimated current contract cost structure and likely re-compete pricing posture; (5) competitor pricing matrix—estimated price range for top 2–3 ghost teams by direct labor cost, indirect rates, and fee; (6) Yggdrasil Aero build-up vs. PTW comparison table—delta by cost element (labor, indirect, subcontractor, fee); (7) cost gap closure analysis—specific levers with estimated savings and feasibility rating; (8) recommended pricing strategy—price to win, price to perform, or no-bid with rationale; (9) key assumptions, data limitations, and model risk flags; (10) analyst name and firm (if external), date, version, and next update trigger (draft RFP release). Filed in BD shared drive per opportunity; presented at Gate 2 review.
summary: Output: PTW summary report with PTW range, competitor pricing matrix, build-up gap table, closure options, and recommended pricing strategy; filed in BD shared drive and presented at Gate 2

label: Gate review documentation assembly
content: Assemble complete gate review documentation package at least 5 business days before the scheduled gate meeting. Required package by gate: Gate 1—opportunity qualification scorecard, initial Pwin assessment with rationale, customer relationship status summary, B&P rough order of magnitude estimate, and pursue/no-bid recommendation memo. Gate 2—current version capture plan, competitive assessment matrix, win strategy and discriminator framework, teaming status (TA execution status per partner), solution concept overview, PTW summary report, updated Pwin range with rationale, and B&P budget authorization request. Gate 3—final pre-proposal capture plan, B&P spend-to-date vs. authorized ceiling, color team planning schedule, proposal volume and section assignments, pricing strategy summary, and final bid/no-bid recommendation. Compile into standardized gate review briefing template. Distribute to BD director and required stakeholders (VP/EVP for Gates 2/3 on pursuits above $50M) via email with opportunity name, gate number, and review date in subject line. Retain all gate packages version-controlled in BD shared drive per opportunity folder.
summary: System: Assemble gate-specific documentation packages 5 days before review; distribute to BD director and required stakeholders; retain version-controlled copies in BD shared drive

label: Gate review briefing template completion
content: Complete the standardized gate review briefing template for the applicable gate. Slides required: (1) opportunity overview—agency, program, estimated value, set-aside type, contract vehicle, anticipated RFP date, NAICS/PSC, incumbent with FPDS-NG contract value; (2) strategic alignment—portfolio fit, growth objective supported, customer segment; (3) Pwin—five-factor scoring summary, current range, key drivers and detractors, trajectory since last gate; (4) competitive landscape—ghost team summary and priority counter-strategies; (5) win strategy—win themes and discriminators with proof points (Gates 2/3); (6) teaming status—partner roster, work share summary, TA execution status (Gates 2/3); (7) B&P budget—phase breakdown, authorized ceiling, spend-to-date, variance, new authorization request (Gate 3); (8) gate decision recommendation with rationale and proposed conditions. Tailor depth per gate: Gate 1 focuses on qualification rationale; Gate 2 on capture intelligence quality and strategy depth; Gate 3 on proposal readiness and bid risk. Submit draft to BD director at least 2 business days before gate review for pre-meeting feedback.
summary: System: Complete standardized gate briefing template with gate-appropriate depth; submit draft to BD director 2 days before review for feedback prior to distribution

label: Gate decision documentation and CRM entry
content: Document gate decision within 24 hours of gate review meeting completion.
