Required elements: gate number, opportunity name, and review date; decision outcome (approve / conditional approval / defer / no-bid); decision rationale narrative (minimum 2–3 sentences stating the primary basis); conditions attached to conditional approval (each condition with: specific description, responsible owner, target resolution date, and verification method required); BD director name and documented approval; and attendee list with names and roles. Enter gate decision record in CRM opportunity record within 24 hours under the gate documentation field. Update opportunity pipeline stage in CRM to reflect advance (Identify→Qualify, Qualify→Capture, Capture→Proposal) or hold for conditional decisions. Notify capture manager of decision and all conditions by email with 24-hour CRM entry confirmation required. File signed gate decision document in BD shared drive per opportunity gate folder. No-bid decisions require BD director signature on a formal no-bid record and pipeline closure actions in CRM (stage set to Closed-No Bid, reason code documented).
summary: System: Document gate decision within 24 hours with outcome, rationale, conditions, and owners; enter in CRM; update opportunity stage; file signed record in BD shared drive

label: Condition closure tracking
content: Track resolution of all conditions attached to conditional gate approvals. For each open condition, maintain a log entry with: condition description, responsible owner, target resolution date, current status (open / in-progress / resolved-pending-verification / closed), evidence of resolution required (e.g., executed TA, updated competitive assessment, additional customer meeting completed, PTW analysis refreshed), and actual closure date. Review condition log weekly with capture manager. Escalate conditions tracking more than 5 business days past resolution date to BD director. Before advancing to next gate, confirm all current-gate conditions are closed with documentation on file. If a condition cannot be closed before the next gate milestone, escalate to BD director for one of three actions: extend the resolution deadline (document new date and rationale), accept the open risk with written rationale and BD director sign-off, or initiate an off-cycle gate review to reassess opportunity stage. Report open condition summary at each pipeline review meeting. Update CRM opportunity record with condition closure dates and evidence references as each condition is resolved.
summary: System: Track conditional gate approval conditions weekly; escalate overdue items; confirm all conditions closed before next gate advance; report open conditions at pipeline reviews

label: Gate conditional approval follow-up decision
content: Determine the appropriate action when a gate review results in conditional approval and conditions come due. Decision options: (1) Advance—all conditions are resolved with documented evidence; capture manager confirms closure in writing; BD director approves stage advancement in CRM; (2) Hold—one or more conditions remain open but resolution is on track within the defined deadline; maintain current stage; capture manager provides status update to BD director within 7 days; (3) Extend—condition resolution requires a deadline extension due to external factors outside capture team control (government action pending, teaming partner negotiation delay, legal review queue); BD director grants a single extension with a new target date and documented rationale; no repeat extensions without full BD director review; (4) Off-cycle gate review—a condition cannot be resolved (partner withdrew, Pwin dropped below threshold, competitive development closes the window), or a new material development changes the opportunity picture; BD director calls an unscheduled gate review to reassess stage; may result in stage downgrade, B&P reauthorization change, or no-bid. BD director makes this decision based on capture manager written status report submitted at least 2 business days before the condition due date. Document outcome with rationale in CRM condition log.
summary: Decision: BD director determines advance, hold, extend, or off-cycle gate review based on condition resolution status; documents outcome in CRM condition log

label: Gate review briefing package template
content: Standardized gate review briefing package template (PowerPoint slide deck) covering all three primary gates, maintained in BD shared drive. Slide structure: (1) Cover—opportunity name, agency, gate number, BD director, capture manager, review date, classification marking; (2) Opportunity Overview—program name, NAICS/PSC, estimated total value, set-aside type, contract vehicle, anticipated RFP date, PoP length, incumbent with FPDS-NG contract value and performance summary; (3) Strategic Alignment—portfolio growth objective supported, technical capability fit assessment, rationale for pursuit investment; (4) Pwin Assessment—five-factor scoring table with scores per factor, total score, Pwin range, key drivers (positive) and detractors (negative), and trajectory since last gate; (5) Competitive Landscape—top 2–3 ghost teams with prime identity, strengths, vulnerabilities, and priority counter-strategies; (6) Win Strategy and Discriminators (Gates 2/3)—three to five win themes with hot button linkage and proof points, discriminator summary; (7) Teaming Status (Gates 2/3)—partner roster, work share summary by task area, TA execution status per partner; (8) B&P Budget—phase breakdown, authorized ceiling, spend-to-date, variance, new authorization request if applicable; (9) Gate Decision Recommendation—recommended decision (approve/conditional/defer/no-bid), supporting rationale, proposed conditions with owners and due dates. Speaker notes per slide include completion guidance. Template reviewed and updated by BD director annually.
summary: Output: Standardized nine-slide gate review briefing package template for all three gates with speaker note guidance; maintained in BD shared drive and reviewed annually

label: Customer hot button compilation from call logs
content: Compile and synthesize customer hot buttons from all intelligence sources accumulated during capture. Primary sources: customer call reports (Section 4—requirements intelligence), industry day feedback and Q&A session records, RFI and Sources Sought response signals, stakeholder profile hot button fields updated after each engagement, and BD director account notes. For each compiled hot button, record: specific customer concern or priority (e.g., schedule predictability, ITAR compliance, transition risk, data availability, cost control), source document and date, confidence level (confirmed/probable/possible), program stakeholder who expressed it, their authority level (PM, technical lead, end user, contracting officer), and recency of last reinforcement. Rank hot buttons by: frequency of citation across sources and by stakeholder authority level. Distinguish hot buttons by stakeholder type—end user operational priorities differ from PM cost and schedule priorities and contracting officer compliance priorities. Deliver compiled hot button register as the primary input to win theme development sessions. Update register within 24 hours of any new customer engagement adding or modifying a priority signal.
summary: System: Compile ranked hot button register from call reports, industry day records, and stakeholder profiles by source frequency and stakeholder authority; update within 24 hours of each engagement

label: Discriminator-to-competitor-gap mapping
content: Map each Yggdrasil Aero discriminator candidate to a specific, substantiated competitor gap using the competitive assessment matrix. Mapping process: for each proposed discriminator (past performance reference, technical capability, key personnel qualification, process or tool, certification), identify the competitor gap it exploits—competitor lacks comparable past performance in this domain; competitor has documented cost growth in this task area; competitor's likely key personnel are at retention risk; competitor's known approach diverges from customer-expressed preference. Assess substantiation feasibility for each discriminator claim: is it provable with publicly available evidence (FPDS-NG data, CPARS indicators, customer statements, certifications, published technical data)? Reject discriminators not substantiated by evidence—generic superiority claims are evaluated as marketing language and damage proposal credibility. Build discriminator-gap mapping table: (1) discriminator claim; (2) proof point and source; (3) customer hot button addressed; (4) competitor gap exploited; (5) risk the competitor closes this gap before proposal submission. Update table as competitive intelligence evolves through capture phase.
summary: System: Map each discriminator to a substantiated competitor gap with proof point, hot button linkage, and gap durability assessment; reject unsupported claims before Gate 2

label: Win theme validation against ghost teams
content: Validate each draft win theme against ghost team hypotheses to confirm the theme genuinely exploits a competitor vulnerability rather than claiming parity or unsubstantiated superiority. Four-test validation for each win theme: (1) Ghost team gap test—does the competitor's anticipated approach, past performance profile, or team composition create a credible gap this theme addresses? (2) Evaluator pressure test—is the claim defensible if an evaluator directly compares Yggdrasil Aero's proposal to the competitor's likely proposal? (3) Gap durability test—is the competitive gap durable and unlikely to be closed before RFP response (e.g., key personnel commitments already secured, past performance gap cannot be manufactured in time, certification cannot be obtained before proposal due date)? (4) Hot button alignment test—does the theme address a documented customer priority, not merely a capability Yggdrasil Aero happens to possess? Flag themes failing tests 1 or 2 for revision or rejection. Assign confidence rating (high/medium) to surviving themes based on ghost team assessment certainty and proof point strength. Document validation results with revision history. Present validated theme set at Gate 2 review.
summary: System: Apply four-test validation (gap, evaluator pressure, durability, hot button) to each win theme against ghost team hypotheses; flag failures for revision before Gate 2

label: Theme carry-forward to proposal storyboards
content: Carry approved win themes and discriminators forward into proposal storyboard and section outline assignments at proposal kickoff. Process: for each approved win theme, assign to proposal volumes and sections where it must appear (technical approach, management approach, past performance narrative, executive summary); identify the volume lead responsible; specify the proof points, discriminator language, and visual concept (graphic, call-out box, comparison table, or annotated diagram) to be integrated. Build a win theme tracking matrix: rows are win themes; columns are assigned proposal volume, section reference, volume lead name, proof points assigned, and integration status (planned / storyboarded / drafted / reviewed). Review tracking matrix at Pink Team to confirm each theme is reflected in storyboards before draft writing begins. Escalate missing or weak themes to proposal manager for remediation. Enforce messaging consistency across volumes—flag contradictory claims or themes that appear in one section but are not reinforced elsewhere, as inconsistent theme treatment is a common Red Team finding.
summary: System: Assign approved themes to proposal volumes at kickoff; build win theme tracking matrix; verify integration at Pink Team; enforce cross-volume messaging consistency

label: Win theme approval at Gate 2
content: BD director reviews and approves the win theme and discriminator framework at Gate 2 as a prerequisite to proposal kickoff authorization. Approval criteria: (1) each win theme is grounded in documented customer hot buttons—BD director validates theme sourcing against BD-level customer intelligence; (2) each discriminator is substantiated by evidence already in the capture plan—not aspirational claims requiring future proof development; (3) themes collectively address all major anticipated Section M evaluation factors without gaps; (4) themes exploit confirmed or probable competitor gaps—BD director validates against ghost team assessment confidence levels; (5) themes are expressed in customer-centric, mission-outcome language—not Yggdrasil Aero-centric capability claims. If win themes are insufficient at Gate 2, BD director options: (a) conditional approval—assign capture manager to address specific theme gaps within 10 business days, document as a condition; (b) defer Gate 2 pending additional customer engagement to fill intelligence gaps for specific themes; (c) flag critical theme gap as a Gate 3 pre-condition (theme must be resolved before proposal kickoff is authorized). Approved win theme framework is documented in the gate decision record and constitutes a binding messaging framework for the proposal team.
summary: Decision: BD director approves win themes at Gate 2 verifying hot button grounding, discriminator substantiation, evaluation factor coverage, and competitor gap validity; sets conditions for gaps

label: Win theme and discriminator matrix
content: Standardized win theme and discriminator matrix deliverable completed at Gate 2 and carried to proposal kickoff. Document structure: (1) header—opportunity name, agency, Gate 2 review date, capture manager, BD director approval signature, and version date; (2) win theme section (3–5 themes maximum)—for each theme: theme statement in customer-benefit language (1–2 sentences), customer hot button addressed with source citation (call report date or industry day reference), proof point and evidence base (specific past contract reference, technical certification, key personnel credential, or quantified performance metric), competitor gap exploited with supporting basis from competitive assessment matrix, confidence level (high/medium), and assigned proposal volume and section; (3) discriminator summary table—each discriminator with claim, proof point, competitor gap, hot button linkage, and proposal integration location; (4) evaluation factor coverage table—each anticipated Section M factor confirmed as addressed by at least one win theme or discriminator; (5) revision log—changes from initial draft through Gate 2 approval with date and rationale. Distributed to proposal manager and all volume leads at proposal kickoff as the binding messaging framework governing all proposal volumes.
summary: Output: Win theme and discriminator matrix with theme statements, hot button citations, proof points, competitor gaps, evaluation factor coverage table, and Gate 2 approval signature

label: Compliance matrix development
content: Develop Section L/M compliance matrices mapping every RFP requirement to a specific proposal response location (volume, section, page). Parse Section L (Instructions to Offerors) and Section M (Evaluation Criteria) to identify all mandatory response elements, submission requirements, and evaluation factors per FAR Part 15.204. Build compliance matrix as a binding internal control document: rows are RFP requirements (with paragraph citation), columns are assigned volume, section number, volume lead responsible, and compliance status (addressed/partial/gap). Circulate matrix to all volume leads at proposal kickoff and update through each color team review. At Gold Team, confirm zero open gaps before submission. Use matrix as the primary compliance verification tool during final production review.
summary: Task: Compliance matrix development mapping every RFP Section L/M requirement to proposal response locations per FAR Part 15 evaluation criteria

label: Volume management and page allocation
content: Manage page count budgets across all proposal volumes per RFP Section L page limitations. At proposal kickoff, allocate total page budget across technical, management, past performance, and cost volumes; assign section-level page targets to each volume lead based on evaluation factor weights (Section M) and content complexity. Track running page counts as drafts are submitted for color team reviews. Enforce reallocation decisions when sections exceed budget—prioritize page investment toward highest-weighted evaluation factors. Coordinate with desktop publishing lead to account for graphics, tables, and white space in page consumption estimates. Flag page overruns to volume leads within 24 hours of draft receipt. Confirm final page compliance across all volumes during Gold Team and production review before submission.
summary: Task: Managing page count budgets across technical, management, past performance, and cost volumes per RFP Section L page limitations

label: Technical writing standards
content: Establish and enforce proposal writing standards across all volumes to ensure evaluator readability, consistency, and persuasive impact. Produce a proposal style guide at kickoff covering: active voice requirements, sentence length guidelines, use of win themes and discriminator language, approved terminology for company name and program references, graphics standards (figure numbering, caption format, color palette, resolution requirements), table formatting conventions, callout box and sidebar usage, header and subheader hierarchy, and cross-volume messaging consistency rules. Distribute style guide to all volume leads and writers at kickoff. At Pink Team, review storyboards for theme consistency; at Red Team, conduct cross-volume messaging audit to flag contradictory claims or inconsistent discriminator language. Coordinate with desktop publishing lead to enforce formatting standards through final production.
summary: Task: Establishing proposal writing style guides, graphics standards, and cross-volume consistency rules for all proposal volumes

label: Past performance citation management
content: Maintain a corporate library of past performance citations for rapid proposal deployment. Library records for each contract: contract number, agency, period of performance, contract value (base and options), NAICS/PSC code, scope description narrative (200–400 words keyed to evaluation relevance), key personnel who supported the effort, performance metrics (schedule adherence, cost control, quality ratings), CPARS rating summary (Exceptional/Very Good/Satisfactory per category), customer reference contact (name, title, phone, email—verified annually), and relevance tags by capability domain, agency, and contract type. Pre-write relevance narratives tailored to the most common evaluation factor formulations. For each active proposal, select 3–5 citations most relevant to RFP evaluation criteria using relevance tags; customize relevance narratives to the specific opportunity language. Update library within 30 days of each contract award, option exercise, or CPARS rating receipt. Flag stale references (PoP ended >5 years prior) for review on relevance thresholds.
summary: Task: Maintaining a library of past performance citations, CPARS references, and relevance narratives for proposal reuse

label: Oral presentation preparation
content: Prepare the proposal team for oral proposal presentations when required by the RFP as part of the evaluation process. Activities: parse RFP oral presentation instructions (time limits, topic scope, question-and-answer format, evaluation criteria applicable to oral phase, materials allowed vs. prohibited); develop presentation outline aligned to oral evaluation factors and win themes; assign presenter roles (typically PM, deputy PM, technical lead, key personnel as specified in RFP); develop slide deck or permitted visual aids within RFP constraints; conduct minimum three full rehearsals with timed run-throughs—first internal, second with capture manager and BD director as evaluators, third as final dress rehearsal; simulate Q&A with challenging evaluator questions drawn from customer hot buttons and competitive vulnerabilities; document and resolve presentation gaps identified during rehearsals; confirm logistics (facility, travel, clearance access requirements, equipment). Debrief after each rehearsal with specific improvement actions. Align final presentation to confirmed evaluation criteria and discriminator framework.
summary: Task: Preparing for oral proposal presentations including rehearsals, evaluator question simulation, and evaluation criteria alignment

label: Post-submission activities and BAFO preparation
content: Manage all post-submission proposal activities through award decision. Evaluation Notice (EN) management: upon receipt of ENs from the contracting officer, parse each EN for type (clarification, deficiency, weakness), assess response strategy with capture manager and BD director, draft responses that resolve the evaluator concern without volunteering new weaknesses, coordinate legal review for any ENs touching pricing or past performance disputes, and submit within the specified response window. BAFO preparation (per FAR 15.307): when the contracting officer issues a competitive range determination and requests a Best and Final Offer, review all EN exchanges and evaluation feedback, update technical or management approach to address identified weaknesses, revise pricing per updated PTW analysis incorporating any scope changes, coordinate revised subcontractor proposals within BAFO timeline, produce BAFO submission per revised Section L instructions, and conduct an internal compliance check before submission. Document all post-submission communications in the proposal record. Coordinate debrief request per FAR 15.506 within applicable period following award decision.
summary: Task: Managing post-submission clarifications, Evaluation Notices (ENs), and Best and Final Offer preparation per FAR 15.307

label: Manage NCR containment and MRB coordination
content: Manage the end-to-end NCR workflow from detection through MRB disposition per AS9100D §8.7. CONTAINMENT: Upon detection, immediately quarantine all affected material/WIP; apply red quarantine tags (date, NCR number, location, quantity); move to designated hold area or mark in-place with physical barrier; update MES WO status to hold within same shift. DOCUMENTATION: Complete NCR within 2 hours of detection for Tier 0 items — defect description, affected part numbers/serials, quantity, operation where found, probable origin operation, operator, timestamp, initial hold disposition. Notify QC and Engineering within 2 hours for Tier 0; within shift for standard NCRs. MRB COORDINATION: Schedule MRB within 24 hours for Tier 0; within 5 business days for standard NCRs. Ensure Engineering, Quality, and Production are represented. Production Manager role: provide schedule impact assessment (hold time cost, downstream program risk) and cost-of-options analysis (rework labor estimate, scrap material cost, schedule recovery delta) to support Engineering and Quality disposition authority. Update WO routing to reflect hold status in MES immediately upon NCR opening; do not advance WO past held operation until MRB disposition is documented and approved.
summary: System: Manage NCR containment, documentation, and MRB coordination from detection through disposition per AS9100D §8.7

label: Determine NCR severity tier (Tier 0 critical vs standard) to set MRB cycle time targets
content: Classify each NCR as Tier 0 (program-critical) or standard at time of detection to trigger appropriate MRB cycle time targets and escalation path. TIER 0 CRITERIA (any one sufficient): (1) Defect on a safety-critical or flight-critical part or feature; (2) Affected unit has a firm customer delivery commitment within 30 days; (3) NCR blocks downstream production on a committed-delivery program (e.g., defective sub-assembly needed for final integration); (4) Potential systemic defect affecting ≥3 units currently in-process on the same operation. STANDARD CRITERIA: None of the Tier 0 criteria apply; WIP hold does not block critical-path production; schedule can absorb standard MRB cycle time without delivery impact. CYCLE TIME TARGETS: Tier 0 → Use-As-Is or Rework disposition: within 24 hours; Complex Engineering Analysis: within 72 hours (elevate to Chief Engineer if standard review path cannot meet target). Standard → disposition within 5 business days; no expedite required. ESCALATION: If Tier 0 disposition cannot be reached within 24 hours, Production Manager escalates to Program Manager with options assessment (cost and schedule of each disposition path) and recommended path. RECLASSIFICATION: If standard NCR investigation reveals systemic pattern (same defect on a 4th unit discovered), reclassify to Tier 0 retroactively and activate 24-hour clock from reclassification time.
summary: Decision: Classify NCR as Tier 0 (24-hr MRB target) or standard (5-day target) based on delivery commitment, safety criticality, production-block impact, and systemic pattern indicators

label: Monthly NCR metrics report — count by operation/root cause, MRB cycle time, rework cost, repeat NCR rate
content: Publish monthly NCR metrics report to Production Manager, Quality Manager, and Program Manager by the 5th business day of the following month. REPORT SECTIONS: (1) NCR COUNT BY OPERATION — table of operations/work centers with NCR count for the month and 3-month rolling average; highlight operations with count >3× their own average as systemic issue candidates. (2) NCR COUNT BY ROOT CAUSE — Pareto by category: design-tolerance, process-capability, operator-error, supplier-escape, tooling/fixture; root cause classification assigned by Quality Manager at NCR close. (3) MRB CYCLE TIME BY TIER — Tier 0: average and maximum days from NCR open to disposition (target <1 day for Use-As-Is/Rework, <3 days for engineering analysis); Standard: average and maximum (target <5 days); list individual NCRs that exceeded target with reason for delay. (4) REWORK COST AS % OF PRODUCTION COST — monthly rework labor hours × loaded labor rate ÷ total production cost for the period (target <5%; yellow alert at 7%, red escalation at >10%); 6-month rolling trend line. (5) REPEAT NCR RATE — NCRs with same defect type and same operation as a prior NCR within 6 months ÷ total NCRs (target <10%; >15% triggers mandatory CAPA escalation); list specific repeat NCR pairs with current CAPA status. (6) SYSTEMIC ESCALATION STATUS — defect types with ≥3 occurrences in 6 months escalated to formal CAPA; list each with CAPA owner and target close date.
summary: Output: Monthly NCR metrics report — NCR count by operation and root cause, MRB cycle time by tier, rework cost percentage, repeat NCR rate, and open CAPA escalation status

label: Configure MES OEE data collection at each work center with automated calculation and trending
content: Configure MES to capture and automatically calculate OEE at each work center. OEE FORMULA: OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality. AVAILABILITY: MES captures planned production time from the weekly schedule and downtime events via operator-entered reason codes (minimum 6 categories: equipment failure, setup/changeover, planned PM, awaiting material, awaiting inspection, quality hold); Availability = (Planned time − Downtime) ÷ Planned time. PERFORMANCE: MES calculates actual cycle time from operator clock-in/out per WO operation; Performance = (Ideal cycle time × units produced) ÷ actual run time; ideal cycle times loaded from engineering routings as standard hours per operation. QUALITY: MES captures first-pass yield at each operation (units passing first inspection ÷ total units started); integrates with NCR records for rejection logging at source. WORK CENTER CONFIGURATION REQUIREMENTS: Each work center must have (1) unique equipment ID, (2) planned production time template by shift, (3) standard cycle time per routed operation loaded, (4) downtime reason code library configured, (5) operator clock-in/out enabled and enforced. TRENDING: MES generates weekly OEE trend chart by work center; 4-week rolling average posted on area visual board; automated alert when single-week OEE drops >15 percentage points from 4-week rolling average — triggers supervisor review of root cause decomposition (which OEE factor drove the drop).
summary: System: Configure MES OEE data collection at each work center — availability (downtime codes), performance (cycle time vs. standard), quality (FPY) — with automated calculation, trending, and alert thresholds

label: Determine which MES data gaps need immediate correction vs. acceptable thresholds for EVMS reporting
content: When MES data integrity review reveals gaps (missing labor transactions, incomplete downtime records, unlogged material consumption), classify each by urgency based on EVMS reporting impact and AS9100D traceability requirements. IMMEDIATE CORRECTION REQUIRED (same-shift resolution, no exceptions): (1) Missing labor hours on closed or closing WOs — directly affects EVMS BCWP calculation and program cost actuals; retroactive MES entry within 4 hours of discovery or system restoration. (2) Material consumption not logged at point of use — affects WIP value, EAC, and BOM reconciliation; must be entered before WO close. (3) Inspection results missing on shipped or shipment-pending units — AS9100D §7.5.3 traceability gap; stop shipment if unresolvable within 24 hours. (4) OEE data unavailable for Tier 0 critical work centers for >2 consecutive shifts — EVMS performance data gap; escalate to MES admin; reconstruct from supervisor records and document manual calculation method. ACCEPTABLE THRESHOLD (correct within 5 business days, during weekly integrity audit): (1) Downtime reason codes uncategorized or missing — degrades root-cause analysis but does not affect EVMS financial calculations. (2) Performance cycle time data incomplete on non-EVMS Tier 1B prototype operations — not program-reportable; batch-correct weekly. (3) OEE data gaps on Tier 1B work centers — acceptable within limits; manual estimation from supervisor shift logs. ESCALATION TRIGGER: if EVMS-reportable work center data completeness falls below 95% for any reporting period, notify Finance and Program Manager within 24 hours and document compensating calculations used.
summary: Decision: Classify MES data gaps as immediate-correction (EVMS-critical, AS9100D traceability) vs. acceptable-threshold (correct within 5 days, non-reportable) based on EVMS impact and regulatory requirements

label: Weekly MES production metrics — OEE, cycle time vs. standard, yield loss Pareto, top 3 improvements
content: Publish weekly MES production metrics report each Monday for the prior week. Distribute to Production Manager, Quality Manager, Engineering (improvement owners), and Finance (EVMS data currency confirmation). REPORT SECTIONS: (1) OEE BY WORK CENTER — table with availability %, performance %, quality %, and composite OEE % vs. target (≥85% mature, ≥75% ramp-up); color-coded red/yellow/green; 4-week trend per work center; for any below-target work center, identify which OEE factor (availability, performance, or quality) is the primary driver to direct corrective action. (2) CYCLE TIME VS. STANDARD BY OPERATION — actual average cycle time vs. engineered standard time for key operations; ratio >1.20 flagged; 4-week trend to distinguish learning curve improvement from process drift. (3) YIELD LOSS PARETO — top 5 operations ranked by defect-driven yield loss (units scrapped + reworked ÷ units started, this week vs. 4-week average); Pareto by defect type within each top operation; flag any operation with yield loss >10% for immediate root-cause action. (4) TOP 3 IMPROVEMENT PRIORITIES — selected from OEE gaps, cycle time overruns, and yield loss Pareto; each entry includes: target metric, current value, improvement owner, improvement hypothesis, and measurable success criterion; status update on prior week's top 3 items. (5) DATA INTEGRITY STATUS — MES completeness rate by program (% of WO operations with complete labor, material, and inspection records); any program below 95% flagged with gap description and corrective action owner.
summary: Output: Weekly MES production metrics — OEE by work center, cycle time vs. standard, yield loss Pareto, top 3 improvement priorities with owners, and data integrity status

label: Manage production schedule freeze and change control process
content: Manage the schedule freeze window and formal change control process to protect committed-delivery production from late disruptions. FREEZE HORIZON DEFINITION: Inner freeze window — 1-2 weeks ahead (committed production horizon); changes inside freeze require Production Manager authorization per L4 decision logic. Rolling planning horizon — 3-8 weeks ahead; changes accepted through standard weekly replanning cycle with PM notification for any Tier 0 impact. CHANGE CONTROL PROCESS: All proposed schedule changes — from engineering (design change), supply chain (material slip), quality (NCR hold recovery), or customer (delivery shift) — submitted through a single change request. Change request fields: requestor, date submitted, affected WO numbers and programs, reason and urgency, proposed revised dates, impact assessment (labor reallocation required, material pull timing shift, downstream program cascade), alternatives considered, and authorization tier required. CHANGE LOG: Every accepted change logged in schedule change register with: change date, WO numbers, program/tier, change type, authorized-by, slip delta in days, inside or outside freeze horizon flag. FREEZE WINDOW INTEGRITY: Floor supervisors and engineers may not adjust WO sequence, planned dates, or operator assignments inside the freeze window without formal change control authorization. Real-time WO status updates (holds, completions, holds lifted) are not schedule changes and bypass this process — only changes to planned sequence, dates, or resource assignments require it.
summary: System: Manage production schedule freeze window and change control process — single-channel change requests, change log, freeze horizon definitions, and integrity rules

label: Decide when to authorize schedule changes inside the freeze horizon
content: Evaluate change requests inside the freeze horizon using a four-factor decision framework. AUTHORIZE inside freeze when ANY of the following applies: (1) MANDATORY DESIGN CHANGE — engineering change is safety-critical or affects form/fit/function on a unit already in process; continuing on superseded design would produce scrap or require retrofit; cost of disruption from change < cost of non-conforming units or downstream retrofit. (2) NCR RECOVERY RESEQUENCING — MRB disposition accepted, rework WO must be reprioritized to meet recovery target; delay in resequencing extends NCR hold time beyond Tier classification target. (3) CONFIRMED MATERIAL SUBSTITUTION — approved alternate material available and requires minor operation resequencing (e.g., adjusted machining parameters); change is self-contained within one WO with no cascade to downstream WOs. (4) DOWNSTREAM TIER 0 PROTECTION — a contractually committed delivery will be missed if change is deferred; assessed cost of slip (CPARS impact, penalty, customer relationship) exceeds assessed cost of freeze disruption. DO NOT AUTHORIZE inside freeze when: change is convenience-based (engineering preference without mandatory driver), change affects >3 concurrent WOs (requires PMO involvement, not Production Manager unilateral), change is speculative (anticipated problem, not confirmed), or change displaces Tier 0 committed production in favor of Tier 1/1B work. DOCUMENTATION: every inside-freeze authorization logged with explicit four-factor rationale, authorized-by name and title, and timestamp within the same shift as the decision.
summary: Decision: Authorize inside-freeze schedule changes only for mandatory design changes, NCR recovery, confirmed material substitution, or Tier 0 downstream protection — document four-factor rationale for every authorization

label: Weekly schedule change log with impact assessment
content: Publish weekly schedule change log each Friday COB for the week just completed. Distribute to Program Manager, Production Manager, Supply Chain Manager, and Quality Manager (for NCR-recovery changes). REPORT SECTIONS: (1) CHANGE SUMMARY TABLE — all schedule changes accepted during the week: date, WO number, program/variant, priority tier, change type (design change, supplier slip, NCR recovery, customer request, resource constraint), previous planned dates, revised dates, slip delta in days, authorized-by, inside/outside freeze horizon flag. (2) INSIDE-FREEZE CHANGE DETAIL — changes inside the freeze horizon listed separately with the four-factor authorization rationale per L4 decision logic; weekly count of inside-freeze changes (target ≤2/week for Tier 0 programs; sustained >3/week indicates freeze discipline breakdown and triggers PM review). (3) SCHEDULE IMPACT ASSESSMENT — for any change with ≥3-day slip delta: downstream WO cascade impact (which subsequent WOs are affected and by how many days), customer delivery commitment at risk (Y/N with delivery date), and recovery option identified (Y/N, with description if yes). (4) CUMULATIVE SLIP TRACKING — for each active Tier 0 program: cumulative schedule slip in the current quarter vs. approved schedule baseline; trend plot; programs with >10 cumulative slip days flagged for PM and PMO notification. (5) PENDING CHANGE REQUEST QUEUE — change requests received but not yet dispositioned: WO, program, requestor, date submitted, reason, and expected authorization decision date.
summary: Output: Weekly schedule change log — all changes by type with slip delta, inside-freeze changes with authorization rationale, impact assessments for ≥3-day slips, and cumulative Tier 0 slip tracking

label: MIL-STD-810H Test Tailoring Process
content: Systematic Part One methodology for tailoring environmental test methods to platform lifecycle environments. Step 1: Develop Life Cycle Environment Profile (LCEP) — document all environments encountered from manufacture through disposal (manufacturing, acceptance, storage, ground/air/sea transport, operational deployment, maintenance, disposal) with data sources (measured field instrumentation, analogous platform data, engineering estimates). Step 2: Identify significant climatic conditions (temperature extremes and rates, humidity, altitude, solar radiation, rain, sand/dust, salt fog, fungus) and dynamic conditions (vibration spectra, shock events, sustained acceleration, pyroshock) relevant to the platform and mission profile. Step 3: Derive test levels directly from LCEP measured or estimated data; standard method default levels are the option of last resort when platform-specific data is unavailable — document rationale for any default level use. Step 4: Select applicable test methods from Part Two/Three corresponding to each identified LCEP environment; methods covering absent conditions are omitted with documented rationale. Step 5: Tailor each selected method — adjust sequences, levels, durations, number of cycles, and combined environment combinations to replicate LCEP conditions; document each tailoring decision with traceability to specific LCEP data entries. Tailoring rationale documented and agreed with government technical representative before test execution.
summary: Part One LCEP development → climatic/dynamic condition identification → test level derivation from measured/estimated data → method selection → procedure tailoring with documented rationale

label: MIL-STD-461G Test Plan Development
content: Procedure for developing EMI/EMC test plans per MIL-STD-461G Section 4. Step 1: Define EUT configuration — identify all operational modes during testing, representative production cabling and harness routing, all externally connected support equipment, and power source configuration. Step 2: Establish test setup — minimum 2.25 m × 2.25 m bonded ground plane (copper or aluminum sheet, earthed); EUT placed 1 m from ground plane front edge (bench-top equipment per Section 4.3.2); bond all metal enclosures per drawing; define safety ground routing. Step 3: LISN placement — install 50 µH/5 Ω LISN on each power input lead within 50 cm of EUT power connector; bond LISN enclosure to ground plane; verify current LISN calibration certificate. Step 4: Antenna positioning — RE101 loop (25 Hz–100 kHz) at 30 cm; RE102 rod (10 kHz–30 MHz) at 1 m, biconical (30 MHz–200 MHz) at 1 m, log-periodic (200 MHz–18 GHz) at 1 m. Step 5: Ambient survey — measure EM environment with all support equipment operating but EUT de-energized; confirm ambient ≥6 dB below applicable limit at all frequencies; test cannot proceed if margin not met. Step 6: Limit tailoring — identify platform installation category per MIL-STD-461G Table I; apply any tailoring directed by acquisition specification; document all tailoring decisions with approval authority reference. Step 7: Draft compliance matrix and submit test plan to contracting authority and government witness for review and approval before test commencement.
summary: EUT configuration → ground plane/test setup → LISN placement → antenna positioning → ambient survey (6 dB margin) → limit selection/tailoring → plan review and approval

label: MIL-STD-882E Hazard Analysis Methodology
content: System safety process hierarchy per MIL-STD-882E Tasks 201–205. Step 1 — Preliminary Hazard List (PHL, Task 201): concept-phase identification of known hazards, hazardous materials, and energy sources from design concept documents and historical system data; uses energy source analysis and system safety checklists; output is a prioritized list driving subsequent analysis scope. Step 2 — Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA, Task 202): analyze each PHL entry — identify causal mechanisms, effects on system and personnel, severity class (I–IV), probability level (A–F), initial risk level per matrix; recommend risk reduction measures; output provides initial risk assessment to guide design trade decisions. Step 3 — Subsystem Hazard Analysis (SSHA, Task 203): detailed analysis of each subsystem design — component failures, tolerance degradations, human errors, and environmental conditions that create or contribute to hazards; integrates with FMEA; identifies failure modes requiring design controls at subsystem level. Step 4 — System Hazard Analysis (SHA, Task 204): evaluates hazards arising from subsystem interactions — interface failure propagation, common cause failures, fault transfer paths across subsystem boundaries; confirms system-level risk levels after integration and verifies adequacy of subsystem-level mitigations. Step 5 — Operating and Support Hazard Analysis (O&SHA, Task 205): examines operations, maintenance, logistics support, test, training, and disposal procedures; identifies procedural hazards and human factors risks; generates safety requirements for operator and maintenance technical orders. All analyses maintained in and traceable to the program hazard tracking log; risk levels updated as design matures through program milestones.
summary: PHL (concept) → PHA (initial risk assessment) → SSHA (subsystem detail) → SHA (system integration) → O&SHA (operations/support) with continuous hazard tracking log

label: MIL-STD-881F WBS Development Procedure
content: Procedure for developing contract and program WBS per MIL-STD-881F. Step 1: Identify the defense materiel item category for the program — select the matching commodity WBS appendix (Aircraft Systems, Electronic/Automated Software Systems, Missile Systems, Ordnance Systems, Sea Systems, Space Systems, Surface Vehicle Systems, Unmanned Maritime Systems). Step 2: Establish Level 1 (program name), Level 2 (major functional areas per commodity appendix), and Level 3 (standard WBS subelements) directly from the applicable 881F appendix; Level 1–3 element names must match the standard exactly without government authorization to deviate. Step 3: Develop contract WBS (CWBS) by extending standard elements to the level required for work authorization and reporting; programs subject to DFARS 252.234-7002 EVMS must extend to control account level (typically WBS Level 3–5) with work packages at the lowest planning level. Step 4: Author WBS dictionary entries for every CWBS element — scope description, included/excluded work, responsible organization, applicable SOW paragraph references, applicable specifications, and deliverables. Step 5: Cross-map CWBS elements to contract SOW paragraphs — every SOW paragraph must trace to at least one WBS element; every CWBS element must trace to at least one SOW paragraph. Step 6: Assign CWBS elements to performing organizations via OBS to create the responsibility assignment matrix (RAM); each control account must have a single responsible Control Account Manager (CAM). Step 7: Use finalized CWBS as the basis for IMS scheduling, performance measurement baseline (PMB) budgeting, and IPMR/CPR reporting.
summary: Commodity framework selection → Level 1-3 standard elements → CWBS extension per EVMS requirements → WBS dictionary → SOW cross-map → RAM assignment → IMS/PMB basis

label: 810H Test Method Selection Criteria
content: Decision framework for determining which MIL-STD-810H test methods apply based on the LCEP. STEP 1: Extract each distinct environmental condition documented in the LCEP across all lifecycle phases. STEP 2: Map each condition to the corresponding candidate test method: High Temperature → Method 501.7; Low Temperature → 502.7; Temperature Shock → 503.7; Solar Radiation → 505.7; Rain → 506.7; Humidity → 507.7; Fungus → 508.8; Salt Fog → 509.7; Sand and Dust → 510.7; Altitude → 500.6; Vibration → 514.8; Shock → 516.8; Acceleration → 513.8; Pyroshock → 517.2; Gunfire Vibration → 519.9. STEP 3: Apply selection decision for each candidate method: INCLUDE — condition is present in LCEP at levels that could affect form, fit, or function, and test levels are derivable from measured or estimated data; INCLUDE WITH MINIMUM LEVELS — condition present but LCEP data insufficient; use method minimum levels and document rationale; OMIT — condition demonstrably absent from LCEP for the platform and mission (e.g., no salt fog for sealed inland ground vehicle); document omission rationale in test plan with LCEP data citation. STEP 4: Combined environments assessment — if two or more conditions co-occur simultaneously in service (vibration during high-temperature operation, humidity during vibration), sequential single-environment tests may be inadequate; assess whether combined environment testing is required and document decision. STEP 5: Obtain government technical representative concurrence on method selection and omissions before test plan finalization; record concurrence in test plan approval block.
summary: LCEP condition extraction → method mapping table → include/omit/minimum-level criteria → combined environment assessment → government concurrence

label: 882E Risk Acceptance Criteria
content: Decision framework for determining mishap risk index and required acceptance authority per MIL-STD-882E Table III. RISK INDEX DETERMINATION: assign severity (I=Catastrophic, II=Critical, III=Marginal, IV=Negligible) and probability (A=Frequent, B=Probable, C=Occasional, D=Remote, E=Improbable, F=Eliminated) for each hazard; cross-reference in risk matrix to determine risk level. HIGH: cells IA, IB, IC, IIA, IIB, IIIA — requires PEO or DASA(SE) acceptance; typically necessitates redesign or system modification before acceptance; must be formally briefed at program milestone review. SERIOUS: cells ID, IIC, IID, IIIB, IIIC — Program Manager acceptance authority; document rationale, safeguards, warnings, and residual risk in hazard tracking log. MEDIUM: cells IE, IIF, IIID, IIIE, IVA, IVB, IVC — delegated acceptance authority (Chief Engineer or designated System Safety Engineer with PM concurrence); document mitigation measures. LOW: cells IIIF, IVD, IVE, IVF — engineering-level delegated acceptance authority; document in hazard tracking log. ACCEPTANCE PREREQUISITES (apply at all risk levels before closing a hazard): (1) all practicable risk reduction measures applied in mandatory precedence order (design elimination → design reduction → safety devices → warning devices → procedures → training); (2) residual risk documented with rationale for why further reduction is impractical; (3) acceptance authority records written formal acceptance in hazard tracking log with signature and date. Hazards may not be closed as accepted without written acceptance at the designated authority level.
summary: Severity×probability → High/Serious/Medium/Low index → tiered acceptance authority → prerequisite: all practicable mitigations applied per precedence before formal acceptance

label: 461G Limit Tailoring Criteria
content: Decision framework for when to apply standard versus tailored EMI limits per MIL-STD-461G Section 4.4.3. USE STANDARD LIMITS (default): apply standard limits when platform installation matches Table I applicability categories (Army, Navy, Air Force — shipboard/submarine/aircraft/ground/space); when no electromagnetic compatibility threat analysis data exists to justify deviation; when acquisition specification does not authorize tailoring. TAILOR TO MORE STRINGENT LIMITS when: (1) installation-specific electromagnetic environmental effects (E3) analysis or measured threat data demonstrates that standard limits do not provide adequate protection for sensitive receivers at the installation location (e.g., high-power radar proximity on shipboard, sensitive navigation receivers in cockpit environment); (2) system integrator or government customer provides documented technical basis via acquisition specification requiring more stringent limits; (3) internal susceptibility analysis identifies specific frequency bands where standard limits are insufficient. TAILOR TO LESS STRINGENT LIMITS when: (1) EUT operates in a demonstrably benign or isolated electromagnetic environment supported by measured field data; (2) standard limit is technically unachievable and trade study documents that the relaxed limit still provides acceptable platform-wide electromagnetic compatibility; (3) government contracting officer or designated EMC authority provides written approval via acquisition specification or engineering order. PROCESS FOR ALL TAILORING: prepare written technical justification with supporting data or analysis; obtain written approval from government contracting authority or designated EMC authority before applying tailored limit; record approval date and approving authority identity in test plan and test report; do not self-authorize limit relaxations.
summary: Standard limits = default; tailor stringent when E3 analysis or customer spec demands it; tailor relaxed only with documented technical basis and written government approval

label: 881F WBS Element Extension Criteria
content: Decision framework for extending the standard WBS beyond Level 3 per MIL-STD-881F. MANDATORY EXTENSION TRIGGERS: (1) EVMS required under DFARS 252.234-7002 (development contracts >$20M, production >$50M) — extend to control account level, typically WBS Level 3–5, with work packages defined at the lowest earned value reporting level; (2) CDRL/DD-1423 deliverable items requiring unique cost tracking must have a corresponding WBS element; (3) SOW work scope cannot be adequately bounded at Level 3 — lower-level elements needed to capture discrete, separately authorizable work. CRITERIA FOR EXTENDING A SPECIFIC ELEMENT: EXTEND when — work scope is discrete, schedulable, and budgetable separately from sibling elements; element has a single responsible performing organization; element is high-risk, high-value, or placed under a separate subcontract; government directs reporting at that level. DO NOT EXTEND when — work scope represents less than approximately 0.5% of total program budget without being high-risk or customer-directed (avoids excessive fragmentation); extension would split work that is naturally integrated across organizational boundaries; extension exceeds the reporting granularity specified in IPMR format or CDRL DD-1423. MAXIMUM DEPTH: MIL-STD-881F recommends WBS depth generally not exceed Level 6; extension beyond Level 6 requires documented justification in the program management plan. Every element extended below Level 3 must have a WBS dictionary entry completed before work authorization.
summary: EVMS/CDRL trigger mandatory extension; extend specific elements when discrete, high-risk, or single-org; avoid sub-threshold fragmentation; max ~Level 6 without justification

label: Life Cycle Environment Profile (LCEP) Template
content: LCEP document template per MIL-STD-810H Part One, Section 2.1. SECTION 1 — PROGRAM IDENTIFICATION: equipment name and part number; program name; platform type and mission description; LCEP document number and revision; date; author; reviewer; government technical representative. SECTION 2 — LIFECYCLE PHASE DEFINITIONS: table — Phase Name | Duration | Operating Location/Theater | Phase Description. Phases: Manufacturing/Assembly; Acceptance Testing; Storage (packaged/unpackaged, short/long-term); Ground Transportation; Air Transportation; Sea Transportation; Installation/Checkout; Operational (normal mission, combat, emergency); Maintenance/Overhaul; Disposal. SECTION 3 — CLIMATIC ENVIRONMENT DATA TABLE: rows = one per phase per condition; columns: Phase | Condition | Data Source (measured program reference / analogous platform / engineering estimate) | Minimum Value | Maximum Value | Representative Typical | Exposure Duration per Event | Total Accumulated Duration | Proposed Test Method. Conditions: operating temperature (°C); storage temperature (°C); temperature rate of change (°C/min); relative humidity (%RH); altitude (m); solar radiation intensity (W/m²); rain rate (mm/hr); sand/dust concentration (g/m³) and median particle size; salt fog (hours); fungal conditions (Y/N). SECTION 4 — DYNAMIC ENVIRONMENT DATA TABLE: rows = one per phase per condition; columns: Phase | Condition | Data Source | Level Description | Duration per Event | Total Accumulated Duration | Proposed Test Method. Conditions: random vibration ASD spectra (g²/Hz vs. Hz — attach plots); sinusoidal vibration (amplitude G, frequency Hz); shock (peak G, duration ms, waveform type, axis); sustained acceleration (G, axis, duration); pyroshock (SRS level G vs. Hz — attach plot). SECTION 5 — CHEMICAL/OTHER ENVIRONMENTS: fluid types and exposure conditions; explosive atmosphere designation (Y/N, concentration data); EMI/EMP environment notes if informing test levels. SECTION 6 — DATA SOURCES AND CONFIDENCE: for each data entry — program measurement reference or analogy basis; confidence level (High/Medium/Low); data gaps and conservative assumption applied. SECTION 7 — TEST TAILORING DECISION MATRIX: Test Method Number and Name | Applicable (Y/N) | If N — Omission Rationale | Tailored Test Level | 810H Standard Level | Tailoring Basis. SECTION 8 — APPROVAL BLOCK: author | responsible system engineer | system safety engineer | government technical representative | date approved. Revise LCEP when platform configuration, mission profile, or deployment theater changes; revision triggers re-evaluation of test method applicability and levels.
summary: Phase-by-phase climatic and dynamic environment data with sources and confidence levels, chemical environments, and tailoring decision matrix per 810H Part One

label: System Safety Program Plan (SSPP) Template
content: SSPP document template per MIL-STD-882E Task 101. SECTION 1 — INTRODUCTION: program name and number; system/platform description and primary mission; SSPP purpose and scope; applicable lifecycle phases (design through disposal); contract CDRL and DID reference. SECTION 2 — APPLICABLE DOCUMENTS: MIL-STD-882E; contract safety requirements specification; program system safety requirements; referenced standards (MIL-STD-1472H human engineering, software safety standards as applicable). SECTION 3 — SYSTEM SAFETY ORGANIZATION: organization chart showing System Safety Manager, System Safety Engineer(s); System Safety Working Group (SSWG) charter — membership (systems engineering, propulsion, structures, logistics, test, operations, human factors, government safety counterpart), meeting frequency, charter authority; reporting relationship of System Safety Manager to Program Manager; government safety authority identification and interface. SECTION 4 — SYSTEM SAFETY TASKS SCHEDULE: table — Task # | MIL-STD-882E Task Reference | Task Name | Lifecycle Phase(s) | Performing Organization | Deliverable / CDRL DID | Scheduled Start | Scheduled Completion. Minimum required tasks: T101 (SSPP), T201 (PHL), T202 (PHA), T203 (SSHA), T204 (SHA), T205 (O&SHA), T206 (Health Hazard Assessment if chemical/biological/radiological hazards identified). SECTION 5 — RISK ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA AND AUTHORITY: risk matrix (severity I–IV × probability A–F per 882E Table III); risk level definitions (High, Serious, Medium, Low); acceptance authority by risk level (aligned to 882E Table III — High = PEO/DASA(SE), Serious = PM, Medium = delegated, Low = delegated); escalation procedure for unresolved High-risk hazards. SECTION 6 — HAZARD TRACKING LOG FORMAT: required fields — Hazard ID; Description; Causal Mechanism; System/Subsystem; Severity Class; Probability Level; Risk Level; Mitigation/Control Measures; Residual Severity; Residual Probability; Residual Risk; Acceptance Authority; Acceptance Date; Status (Open/Accepted/Closed). Update frequency: monthly minimum; full status review required at each program milestone (PDR, CDR, PRR, FCA/PCA). SECTION 7 — SAFETY DESIGN PRECEDENCE ORDER: (1) eliminate hazard through design; (2) reduce risk through design (fail-safe, redundancy, margins); (3) incorporate safety devices (guards, interlocks, barriers, physical separation); (4) provide warning devices (caution/warning systems, alarms); (5) develop procedures; (6) provide training. Documentation required showing each precedence step was evaluated and rationale for not applying higher-precedence measures. SECTION 8 — MILESTONE SAFETY REVIEWS: review schedule aligned to PDR, CDR, PRR, FCA/PCA — entry/exit safety criteria; required briefing materials (updated PHA/SHA, open hazard count by risk level, risk acceptance status, top risk issues); designated acceptance authority for each milestone. SECTION 9 — SSWG MEETING SCHEDULE AND CHARTER: meeting frequency (monthly minimum); standing agenda topics; action item log format; minutes distribution list; government witness requirements and notice period.
summary: SSPP covering organization, task schedule per 882E Tasks 101-206, RAC levels with authority tiers, hazard tracking log format, design precedence, and milestone review criteria

label: EMI Test Report Template (MIL-STD-461G)
content: EMI/EMC test report template per MIL-STD-461G Section 4.6. SECTION 1 — ADMINISTRATIVE: report number and revision; EUT name, model, part number, serial number; contractor name and address; test facility name and NVLAP/A2LA accreditation number; test dates (start/end); report issue date; test engineer name; government witness name, agency, and witness dates. SECTION 2 — EUT DESCRIPTION: functional description and intended platform installation; list of all operational modes tested; input power source type and rated consumption; connector and cable configuration with harness descriptions; EUT photographs (overall, front panel, rear panel, representative cable dress). SECTION 3 — TEST SETUP: ground plane dimensions and bonding verification; EUT placement diagram with dimensions from ground plane edges; LISN model, serial number, and placement dimensions; support equipment identification and connection diagram; representative test setup photographs (one per major test configuration change). SECTION 4 — TEST REQUIREMENTS COMPLIANCE MATRIX: table — Requirement ID | Title | Applicable (Y/N) | N/A Rationale | Standard Limit (dBxx) | Tailored Limit (Y/N) | Tailoring Basis and Approval Authority Reference. SECTION 5 — AMBIENT SURVEY: pre-test ambient measurements for each RE and RS requirement; table of ambient levels vs. limit at worst-case frequencies; compliance confirmation (≥6 dB margin) or anomaly disposition with government witness concurrence. SECTION 6 — TEST RESULTS BY REQUIREMENT: for each applicable requirement — test procedure summary (configuration, frequency range, measurement parameters, EUT operating mode); data plot (measured level dBµV, dBµA, or dBµV/m vs. frequency with limit line overlaid); worst-case margin noted on plot; pass/fail determination; anomalies and dispositions. SECTION 7 — COMPLIANCE SUMMARY MATRIX: Requirement ID | Description | Applicable | Limit (dBxx) | Worst-Case Measured Level | Frequency of Worst Case | Margin (dB, positive = pass) | Result (PASS / FAIL / CONDITIONAL). SECTION 8 — TEST EXCEPTIONS AND DEVIATIONS: departures from MIL-STD-461G test procedures with technical basis and government witness concurrence documentation. SECTION 9 — CALIBRATION RECORDS: all instruments — name, model, serial, calibration laboratory, calibration date, due date; antenna factor tables used; LISN insertion loss verification record. SECTION 10 — SIGNATURE BLOCK: test engineer; lead EMC engineer; quality representative; government witness. Retain as program record per contract data management requirements.
summary: 461G test report covering EUT identification, test setup documentation, ambient survey, per-requirement limit plots with margins, compliance matrix, exceptions, and calibration records

label: Contract WBS Template (MIL-STD-881F)
content: Contract WBS template per MIL-STD-881F. LEVEL 1: 1.0 [Program Name] — single mandatory element. LEVEL 2 AND 3 EXAMPLE — Aircraft Systems (881F Appendix A): 1.1 Air Vehicle [1.1.1 Airframe; 1.1.2 Propulsion; 1.1.3 Vehicle Subsystems; 1.1.4 Avionics; 1.1.5 Armament/Weapons Delivery; 1.1.6 Auxiliary Equipment; 1.1.7 Furnishings and Equipment; 1.1.8 Air Vehicle Software]; 1.2 Mission Systems [1.2.1 C3I Systems; 1.2.2 Electronic Warfare Systems; 1.2.3 Intelligence/Reconnaissance Systems; 1.2.4 Navigation Systems; 1.2.5 Identification Systems; 1.2.6 Mission Systems Software]; 1.3 Weapon Delivery Systems; 1.4 Training [1.4.1 Equipment; 1.4.2 Services; 1.4.3 Facilities]; 1.5 Peculiar Support Equipment [1.5.1 Test and Measurement Equipment; 1.5.2 Support and Handling Equipment]; 1.6 Common Support Equipment; 1.7 Operational/Site Activation; 1.8 Industrial Facilities; 1.9 Initial Spares and Repair Parts; 1.10 Systems Engineering [1.10.1 Systems Engineering; 1.10.2 Integration, Assembly, Test, and Checkout (IATC)]; 1.11 Program Management [1.11.1 Program Management; 1.11.2 Data Management; 1.11.3 Configuration Management]. WBS DICTIONARY ENTRY FORMAT (one entry required per element before work authorization): Element Number | Element Name | Scope Definition | Work Included | Work Excluded | Responsible Organization | SOW Paragraph Reference(s) | Applicable CDRLs | Applicable Specifications | Control Account Manager (for control accounts). CROSS-MAP TABLE: SOW Paragraph | WBS Element(s) | Direction (1:1 / 1:Many / Many:1) — verify bidirectional coverage. NOTE: Use the applicable commodity appendix from 881F — adapt element names per appendix (Missile Systems App B, Electronic/SW Systems App C, Sea Systems App D, Space Systems App F, etc.); Level 1–3 element names must match 881F exactly without government-authorized deviation; extend below Level 3 per WBS extension criteria decision framework.
summary: Level 1-3 standard WBS structure per 881F Aircraft Systems appendix with WBS dictionary entry format and SOW cross-map; adapt to applicable commodity appendix

label: Manage constraint-based scheduling for shared resources across programs
content: Identify and schedule shared production equipment (autoclaves, CMMs, paint booths, ovens, specialty test equipment) across concurrent programs to prevent double-booking and maximize utilization. SHARED RESOURCE REGISTER: Maintain list of all shared assets with their capacity (e.g., autoclave cycle time, CMM throughput in parts/shift, paint booth spray window), planned PM windows, and current reservations by program and WO. SCHEDULING RULES: Allocate shared resources by program tier priority (Tier 0 committed delivery > Tier 1 pilot > Tier 1B prototype); within same tier, use schedule float to sequence (least float first). Block shared resource time in MES as a capacity constraint — WO release gating check must verify shared resource slot reserved before releasing. CONFLICT RESOLUTION: When two Tier 0 programs compete for the same shared resource slot, escalate to Program Manager within the same shift for priority arbitration; do not resolve unilaterally. UTILIZATION TRACKING: Track shared resource utilization weekly — target 70-80% for autoclaves and paint booths (reserve 20-30% buffer for rework cycles and unplanned runs); CMM utilization target 75-85%. Flag utilization sustained >85% as a capacity constraint requiring recovery action (subcontract CMM time, authorize overtime autoclave runs, add paint booth shift). BOOKING HORIZON: Reserve shared resource slots 3-4 weeks ahead for Tier 0 programs; 1-2 weeks ahead for Tier 1; prototype slots filled from remaining capacity. Publish shared resource reservation calendar weekly to all program supervisors.
summary: System: Schedule and manage shared production equipment (autoclaves, CMMs, paint booths) across concurrent programs to prevent double-booking and maximize utilization

label: Manage WIP aging and flow optimization to prevent chronic flow blockers
content: Track WIP age for every open WO against its standard cycle time to detect and resolve flow blockers before they cascade into schedule slips. WIP AGE CALCULATION: WIP age = today's date − WO release date (or last operation completion date if partially complete); compare against standard cycle time from engineering routing. FLAG THRESHOLDS: WOs at 1.0-1.5× standard cycle time → supervisor awareness; 1.5-2.0× → supervisor must document reason in MES and recovery plan; >2× standard cycle time → escalate to Production Manager with root cause and recovery date. INVESTIGATION PROTOCOL: When a WO exceeds 2× standard, categorize root cause: (1) Quality hold (NCR pending MRB) — apply Tier classification cycle time targets; (2) Material shortage — activate expedite protocol; (3) Tooling unavailable — assess repair/replace timeline, adjust schedule; (4) Capacity constraint — check if work center is bottleneck; resequence or authorize overtime; (5) Design change hold (open ECO) — escalate to Engineering for disposition deadline. CHRONIC FLOW BLOCKERS: Any WO root cause category appearing ≥3 times in 30 days is a systemic blocker; present to Production Manager and Quality Manager as a formal corrective action item with owner and 30-day close target. WIP AGE METRICS: Weekly report — WO count by aging bracket (<1×, 1-1.5×, 1.5-2×, >2× standard); trend chart by work center; WOs at >2× standard reviewed individually in weekly ops meeting. TARGET: Zero WOs open >2× standard cycle time without an active documented recovery plan.
summary: System: Track WIP age by WO against standard cycle time, investigate WOs exceeding 2x standard, and implement corrective actions for chronic flow blockers

label: Coordinate fixture CMM re-verification program against design datum requirements
content: Schedule and track periodic dimensional verification of all production fixtures against their original design datum requirements to detect wear, deformation, or drift before it causes production nonconformances. RE-VERIFICATION INTERVALS: Set per fixture criticality and use frequency — Tier 0 program fixtures: every 50 production cycles or 6 months, whichever comes first; Tier 1 fixtures: every 100 cycles or 12 months; Tier 1B prototype fixtures: annually or after any reported fixturing anomaly. TOOLING REGISTER INTEGRATION: Track re-verification due date per fixture in the tooling register (see L3 2232); upcoming re-verifications appear on monthly tooling status report. RE-VERIFICATION PROCEDURE: CMM measures all datum reference features (primary locating pin positions, datum face flatness, clamp contact surfaces) against the fixture qualification drawing; compare results against original qualification baseline with tolerance stack-up accounted for. ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA: Datum deviation within 50% of the tightest feature tolerance it locates (conservative); deviations exceeding 75% of feature tolerance require Engineering disposition before continued use. QUARANTINE ON FAILURE: Fixtures failing re-verification are immediately removed from service (status = quarantine in tooling register), tagged physically, and cannot support WO release until either repaired and re-verified, or Engineering issues a documented conditional-use disposition with compensating controls (e.g., 100% part CMM inspection while fixture is repaired). FAILURE ROOT CAUSE: Log failure cause in CMMS — wear at locating surface, thermal distortion, collision damage, fastener loosening; use root cause to adjust re-verification interval or initiate fixture redesign. COORDINATION: Facilities Manager coordinates CMM time as a shared resource (see L3 above); re-verifications scheduled during planned production holds where possible to avoid competing with production CMM demand.
summary: System: Schedule periodic CMM re-verification of production fixtures against design datum requirements; quarantine fixtures failing verification until repaired or Engineering-dispositioned

label: Manage external special process operator certification renewals (NADCAP NDT, AWS D17.1 welding)
content: Track and coordinate renewal of externally issued operator certifications required for NADCAP-audited special processes and AWS D17.1 welding operations. These certifications cannot be renewed internally — scheduling failures create production stoppages with no internal workaround. CERTIFICATION TYPES TRACKED: NADCAP NDT operators (Level II/III per NAS410 — PT, MT, UT, RT, ET); welding operators per AWS D17.1 (Specification for Fusion Welding for Aerospace Applications); any other externally governed process where customer flow-down or regulatory requirement mandates external certification body renewal. TRACKING SYSTEM: Maintain external certification register distinct from internal skill matrix — fields: operator name, certification type, external body, certification number, expiration date, renewal lead time (calendar months required for scheduling, exam prep, and external body processing — typically 2-6 months per cert type), renewal action initiated date, renewal status (not started / scheduled / exam pending / awaiting certificate). COLOR-CODE: Green = current >90 days to expiry; Yellow = 60-90 days, renewal action must be initiated; Red = <60 days with no renewal scheduled (production stoppage risk). SCHEDULING PROTOCOL: Initiate renewal scheduling at 2-6 months before expiry depending on certification type — NDT NAS410 Level II requires exam scheduling with authorized NDT Level III examiner and may require 40+ hours of refresher training; AWS D17.1 welding operator qualification requires coupon prep, test welder scheduling, and evaluation. Coordinate with external bodies (ASNT chapters, AWS QC1-authorized testing facilities) early — available slots fill quickly. PRODUCTION IMPACT ASSESSMENT: For any Red status certification, assess immediately: which programs and operations are affected, whether any other operator is certified (SPOF check), and what the production stoppage consequence is if renewal is not completed before expiry. Escalate Red status immediately to Production Manager and Quality Manager. DOCUMENTATION: Retain external certificate copies in operator qualification file per AS9100D §7.2; external cert expiry dates feed directly into skill certification matrix as hard expiry dates.
summary: System: Track external special process operator certification renewal timelines (NADCAP NDT, AWS D17.1) and coordinate scheduling with external bodies 2-6 months ahead to prevent production stoppages

label: Implement 5S workplace organization program with monthly supervisor and quarterly cross-area audits
content: Establish and sustain a scored 5S program with regular audits, visual management of results, and structured improvement tracking. 5S SCORING CRITERIA: Develop a 100-point scoring rubric for each of the five S categories (20 points each). Sort (20 pts): no unneeded items at workstation; red-tag area cleared weekly; tools and materials limited to current WO requirements. Set-In-Order (20 pts): all tools have dedicated labeled locations; shadow boards or foam inserts used for high-use tools; material staging areas clearly demarcated. Shine (20 pts): workstation and equipment surfaces clean and free of chips/fluids/debris; spill response materials present and accessible; floors in work area clear of trip hazards. Standardize (20 pts): visual controls present and current (procedure cards, tooling diagrams, kanban signals); labeling consistent with facility standards; color-coding matches facility standard. Sustain (20 pts): prior audit findings closed by next audit; operators can describe 5S standard for their workstation; improvement suggestions logged in current month. SCORING THRESHOLDS: ≥90 = Excellent; 80-89 = Acceptable (target); 70-79 = Needs Improvement (corrective action with 30-day close); <70 = Unacceptable (supervisor corrective action plan required within 5 business days). AUDIT CADENCE: Monthly supervisor self-audit of own area using standard rubric (1 hr); quarterly cross-area audit by rotating team of supervisors from other areas (2 hrs per area, provides fresh perspective). Quarterly audits weighted separately from monthly for trend tracking. VISUAL MANAGEMENT: Post area 5S score on visual board at area entrance within 24 hours of audit completion; trend chart showing last 6 months; corrective action items listed with owner and due date. IMPROVEMENT TRACKING: Each below-target item generates a corrective action with specific owner, due date, and expected score impact; verify closure at next audit; unclosed items from prior month reduce Sustain score. Aggregate scores reported in monthly lean metrics dashboard (L5 neuron 2243).
summary: System: Run scored 5S program with monthly supervisor audits and quarterly cross-area audits; post scores on visual boards and track improvement trends

label: Define and maintain MES downtime backup procedures and retroactive entry protocol
content: Establish and maintain a documented backup procedure enabling production to continue with full data capture when MES is unavailable, and ensure retroactive data entry restores MES continuity on system restoration. BACKUP TRIGGER: Activate paper backup when MES is unavailable for >15 minutes (unplanned) or for any planned maintenance window affecting data entry. PAPER TRAVELER PROCESS: Pre-printed paper traveler forms (kept as a supply at each work center) capture: WO number, part number, routing operation sequence, operator name, clock-in time, clock-out time, quantity started, quantity completed, quantity scrapped, hold reason (if applicable), and any nonconformance observations. Supervisor collects completed paper travelers per shift and signs them as the shift supervisor record. SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS: In-process inspection hold points still require QC sign-off on paper; supervisor and QC inspector both sign the paper traveler at hold point completion. Material transactions: operators note material lot numbers and quantities consumed on the traveler for ERP retroactive entry. RETROACTIVE ENTRY REQUIREMENTS: All paper traveler data must be entered into MES within 4 hours of system restoration — this is a hard target, not a target of convenience, because EVMS-reportable WOs require same-period actuals and AS9100D traceability requires complete records. Entry priority: (1) Closed or near-closing WOs (cost capture), (2) Tier 0 Tier program WOs, (3) Inspection hold point records, (4) All remaining by shift sequence. Assign retroactive entry responsibility to a designated operator per work center before each maintenance window; for unplanned outages, supervisor assigns on restoration. PROCEDURE LOCATION: Paper traveler forms and one-page backup activation instructions stored in a laminated binder at each work center supervisor station; reviewed and restocked quarterly. TRAINING: All supervisors trained on backup activation and retroactive entry as part of MES onboarding; refresher when procedure is updated.
summary: System: Paper traveler backup process when MES is unavailable; retroactive MES entry required within 4 hours of system restoration to maintain data continuity and EVMS/AS9100D traceability

label: Select earned value method per WBS control account: discrete, LOE, apportioned, milestone
content: Assign earned value method to each WBS control account before PMB establishment per ANSI/EIA-748 Criterion 7. DISCRETE EFFORT: Work producing a tangible, measurable deliverable — hardware fabrication, test execution, software increments, inspection. Prefer weighted milestones over % complete estimates to reduce subjectivity; each milestone has predefined % credit (e.g., design complete=30%, prototype built=60%, test passed=100%). LEVEL OF EFFORT (LOE): Ongoing support with no distinct deliverable — program management, configuration management, quality surveillance, logistics support. BCWP equals BCWS each period by definition; LOE never accumulates schedule variance and always shows SV = 0. CRITICAL RULE: Do NOT assign LOE to any work that can be measured discretely — LOE overuse masks schedule problems and is the most frequently cited DCMA EVM deficiency. APPORTIONED EFFORT: Budget tied as a fixed ratio to a related discrete base task — e.g., quality inspection budgeted at 15% of production labor hours. BCWP = ratio × BCWP_base_task. Requires a clearly identified base task; if base task is restructured, apportioned task must be re-evaluated. MILESTONE BILLING: 100% budget credit on occurrence of a contractually defined event — CDRL acceptance, PDR/CDR completion, test article delivery. Used when work cannot be measured incrementally but the completion event is unambiguous. AUDIT EXPOSURE: DCMA surveillances routinely cite (1) LOE used for work that has discrete outputs, (2) % complete estimates without objective milestone criteria, and (3) apportioned effort without documented base task relationship. Document EV method assignment in the control account plan or work authorization document.
summary: Decision: Assign EVM earned value method (discrete/LOE/apportioned/milestone) per WBS control account per ANSI/EIA-748 Criterion 7 — LOE misuse for discrete work is the most-cited DCMA surveillance deficiency

label: Forward pricing rate table with wrap rates as SCD Type 2 for EVM cost accumulation
content: Rate tables for EVM cost accumulation must support forward pricing rate agreements (FPRAs) and actuals reconciliation across fiscal years. RATE COMPONENTS: Direct labor rate by skill/grade ($/hour). Overhead rate applied to direct labor (fringe + facilities burden). G&A rate applied to total cost input.
