Fee/profit applied to target cost. Wrap rate = (direct labor + overhead + G&A) ÷ direct labor — the burdened multiplier applied to direct labor hours for EAC projections. FPRA GOVERNANCE: DoD agencies negotiate FPRAs annually; wrap rates change by fiscal year and mid-year via Forward Pricing Rate Recommendations (FPRRs). SCD TYPE 2 REQUIRED: Rate tables must be SCD Type 2 because historical BCWP must be reproducible at the rates in effect at time of performance — not retroactively repriced to current rates. SCHEMA: dim_rate_table(rate_sk BIGINT IDENTITY, skill_category STRING, labor_rate DECIMAL(10,4), overhead_rate DOUBLE, ga_rate DOUBLE, wrap_rate DOUBLE, fpra_reference STRING, effective_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL, expiry_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT '9999-12-31', is_current BOOLEAN). POINT-IN-TIME JOIN: When computing BCWP or repricing contractor EAC, join fact_labor_actuals to dim_rate_table using half-open interval on performance date: fact.period_date >= rate.effective_date AND fact.period_date < rate.expiry_date. REPRICING USE CASE: Finance may reprice historical BCWP at current FPRA rates to assess cost realism for EAC validation — requires joining at current rates (WHERE is_current = TRUE) rather than historical rates.
summary: System: Model FPRA/FPRR forward pricing rate tables as SCD Type 2 Delta dimension with wrap rates by skill/FY — historical BCWP must be reproducible at period-specific rates using half-open interval joins

label: ITAR/CUI row-level security in Unity Catalog for government program EVM data
content: Government program EVM data may carry ITAR (International Traffic in Arms Regulations) or CUI (Controlled Unclassified Information) markings requiring access controls beyond standard Unity Catalog ACLs. ROW-LEVEL SECURITY: Implement via CREATE ROW ACCESS POLICY or SQL views with IS_ACCOUNT_GROUP_MEMBER() filters — restricts program rows by cleared AD group without table proliferation. Example: WHERE IS_ACCOUNT_GROUP_MEMBER('itar-cleared-analysts') OR program_sensitivity = 'UNCONTROLLED'. COLUMN MASKING: CREATE COLUMN MASK on cost fields (acwp, bcwp, eac) to return NULL for users without clearance rather than omitting rows — preserves row count visibility while protecting cost data. CUI TAGGING: ALTER TABLE gold.fact_evm_daily SET TAGS ('sensitivity'='CUI', 'export_control'='ITAR', 'data_classification'='controlled'). Discover all tagged tables via information_schema.table_tags. ACCESS GROUP DESIGN: Provision AD groups by contract and clearance level (e.g., 'program-abc-cleared', 'program-abc-itar') — never grant access to individual users. FOREIGN NATIONAL SEGREGATION: Foreign nationals may require a separate Unity Catalog catalog or schema excluding ITAR-controlled data — consult legal/compliance before architecture. AUDIT: Unity Catalog audit logs capture every query against ITAR-tagged tables; export audit logs to SIEM for compliance evidence. CLASSIFICATION BOUNDARY: Never store classified (SECRET/TOP SECRET) data in commercial Databricks; ITAR/CUI is appropriate for IL4/IL5-authorized GovCloud environments only.
summary: System: Enforce ITAR/CUI controls on government EVM data via Unity Catalog row access policies, column masks, CUI sensitivity tags, and AD group provisioning — never store classified data without IL4/IL5 GovCloud authorization

label: WBS dictionary SCD Type 2 DDL and EVM fact schema with BCWS/BCWP/ACWP/CPI/SPI
content:
-- WBS Dictionary dimension: SCD Type 2 to capture baseline changes (WBS restructuring, BCP-driven scope transfers)
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS gold.dim_wbs_dictionary (
  wbs_sk           BIGINT GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY,
  wbs_element_id   STRING NOT NULL,   -- natural key: e.g., '1.1.2.3'
  program_id       STRING NOT NULL,
  wbs_level        INT,               -- 1=program 2=system 3=subsystem 4=element 5=control account
  wbs_title        STRING,
  ev_method        STRING,            -- 'DISCRETE' | 'LOE' | 'APPORTIONED' | 'MILESTONE'
  base_task_id     STRING,            -- for APPORTIONED: FK to related discrete element
  apportioned_pct  DOUBLE,            -- for APPORTIONED: ratio (e.g., 0.15 = 15% of base BCWP)
  responsible_cam  STRING,            -- control account manager
  bac              DECIMAL(18,2),     -- Budget at Completion for this element
  effective_date   TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  expiry_date      TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT TIMESTAMP '9999-12-31 00:00:00',
  is_current       BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE
)
USING DELTA
TBLPROPERTIES ('delta.enableChangeDataFeed'='true', 'delta.enableDeletionVectors'='true');

-- EVM fact table: period cost/schedule performance by WBS control account
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS gold.fact_evm_control_account (
  program_id        STRING NOT NULL,
  wbs_element_id    STRING NOT NULL,
  wbs_sk            BIGINT NOT NULL,       -- FK to dim_wbs_dictionary at time of period
  performance_date  DATE NOT NULL,
  bcws              DECIMAL(18,2),         -- Budgeted Cost of Work Scheduled
  bcwp              DECIMAL(18,2),         -- Budgeted Cost of Work Performed (earned)
  acwp              DECIMAL(18,2),         -- Actual Cost of Work Performed
  cost_variance     DECIMAL(18,2),         -- CV = BCWP - ACWP
  schedule_variance DECIMAL(18,2),         -- SV = BCWP - BCWS
  cpi               DOUBLE,
  spi               DOUBLE,
  eac               DECIMAL(18,2),
  vac               DECIMAL(18,2),         -- VAC = BAC - EAC
  tcpi              DOUBLE,                -- To-Complete Performance Index
  is_significant_variance BOOLEAN,         -- |CV| or |SV| > 10% BAC
  computed_ts       TIMESTAMP
)
USING DELTA
PARTITIONED BY (performance_date)
CLUSTER BY (program_id, wbs_element_id);

-- Point-in-time join: fact to WBS dimension at performance date
-- Half-open interval handles mid-program WBS restructuring
SELECT f.*, d.wbs_title, d.ev_method, d.bac, d.responsible_cam
FROM gold.fact_evm_control_account f
JOIN gold.dim_wbs_dictionary d
  ON f.wbs_element_id = d.wbs_element_id
 AND f.program_id     = d.program_id
 AND CAST(f.performance_date AS TIMESTAMP) >= d.effective_date
 AND CAST(f.performance_date AS TIMESTAMP) <  d.expiry_date;

-- Flag elements requiring CAP narrative (Format 5)
SELECT wbs_element_id, wbs_title, cost_variance, vac, cpi, ev_method
FROM gold.fact_evm_control_account f
JOIN gold.dim_wbs_dictionary d USING (wbs_sk)
WHERE performance_date = CURRENT_DATE() - INTERVAL 1 MONTH
  AND is_significant_variance = TRUE
ORDER BY ABS(cost_variance) DESC;
summary: Output: WBS dictionary SCD Type 2 DDL with ev_method column and EVM fact schema (BCWS/BCWP/ACWP/CPI/SPI/TCPI/VAC) partitioned by date, with half-open interval point-in-time join and IPMR Format 5 CAP-trigger query

label: MES/OT Environment NIST SP 800-171 Compliance Gaps
content:
MES AND OT-SPECIFIC NIST 800-171 COMPLIANCE CHALLENGES:

1. CCIS SCOPE DETERMINATION: MES is a Covered Contractor Information System if it processes CDI — work orders referencing export-controlled part numbers, NC programs for ITAR hardware, acceptance test records, or TDPs linked to routing. Scope extends to DNC servers, shop floor terminals, OT network segments, and historian connections.

2. DNC SERVER AS CUI VECTOR (3.1.20, 3.4.x): NC program files for defense parts are CDI. DNC servers distributing NC code to CNC machines must be within CCIS boundary. USB-based NC program transfer is a finding under external device controls. Mitigate: enforce DNC-only program delivery, disable USB ports on CNC controllers, log all program loads.

3. FIPS 140-2/3 VALIDATION GOTCHA (3.13.10): MES vendors frequently claim 'AES-256 encryption' without FIPS-validated module certification. Require vendor to provide CMVP certificate number. Common finding: encryption implemented using non-validated libraries despite correct algorithm. Verify via NIST CMVP database.

4. MFA ON SHOP FLOOR (3.5.3): MFA is required for all privileged access — operationally disruptive on shared shop floor terminals. Solutions: proximity card readers, PIN+badge combinations, role-specific terminal lockout. Document compensating controls in SSP if full MFA is deferred to POA&M.

5. LEGACY OS / EOL SYSTEMS (3.14.4): Shop floor HMIs and MES clients frequently run Windows 7/10 EOL or Windows Server 2008/2012. Cannot patch — requires compensating controls: network isolation (OT VLAN), application whitelisting (Microsoft WDAC or equivalent), enhanced monitoring. Document as POA&M items with remediation timeline.

6. OT/IT NETWORK SEGMENTATION (3.13.1, 3.13.2): Most MES deployments have flat networks (ERP → MES → PLC). C3PAO finding: documented network diagram required in SSP showing MES boundary; enforce firewall rules between OT and IT; use data diodes or unidirectional gateways for historian uplinks.

7. MES VENDOR REMOTE ACCESS (3.7.5, 3.13.9): Plex, Epicor, Infor routinely require persistent VPN access for support. Counts as covered contractor information system connection — must be session-limited, MFA-enforced, logged, and documented as external connection in SSP.

8. CLOUD MES (FedRAMP): SaaS MES (Plex cloud, Infor CloudSuite) must hold FedRAMP Moderate authorization or DoD Cloud SRG IL2+ equivalency. Verify via FedRAMP Marketplace — most commercial MES SaaS platforms are NOT authorized. Blocking issue for CUI in cloud MES.

PRIORITY POA&M ORDER: (1) MFA on privileged MES accounts, (2) FIPS validation confirmation with vendor, (3) OT network segmentation, (4) DNC server access controls, (5) vendor remote access logging, (6) EOL OS compensating controls.
summary: MES/OT-specific NIST SP 800-171 compliance challenges: DNC server CUI scope, FIPS validation verification, MFA on shop floor terminals, legacy OS compensating controls, OT/IT segmentation, vendor remote access, and cloud MES FedRAMP requirements

label: Receiving Inspection — Risk-Tiered AQL Sampling
content: Risk-tiered incoming inspection per supplier classification and AS9100D 8.4. TIER A SUPPLIERS (Post-FAI Ppk≥1.33, R&R<10%, clean 12-month history): AQL 2.5 per ANSI/ASQ Z1.4 Normal Level II, 5–10% sample; accept on zero defects in sample; trigger 100% inspection if any nonconformance found or supplier SPC exceeds upper action limit. TIER B SUPPLIERS (Conditional post-FAI, Ppk 1.0–1.33, immature QMS): AQL 1.0, 25% sample first 10 production lots; step down to 10% only after zero nonconformances sustained across 10 consecutive lots AND Ppk ≥1.33 confirmed via supplier SPC submission; require supplier SPC data pre-shipment before issuing receiving authorization. TIER C SUPPLIERS (Single-source critical, post-CAR risk, new supplier, no capability data): 100% incoming inspection first 50 units; step-down to 25% requires zero nonconformances AND Engineering+Quality Manager joint written sign-off. CRITICAL RULE: Never relax receiving plan below tier minimum without documented Ppk ≥1.33 sustained over ≥30 consecutive parts from current supplier SPC. LOT REJECTION TRIGGER: Reject entire lot when ≥2 nonconforming units found in sample; conduct 100% inspection or return to supplier; issue CAR before accepting replacement lot. DEFECT DOCUMENTATION: Photograph each nonconformance in-situ; record supplier lot ID, quantity received, sample size, nonconforming characteristics, measured values vs. spec limits, and disposition decision. ESCAPE TRACKING: Calculate monthly in-process escape rate (defects found in assembly that passed receiving inspection); if escape rate exceeds 0.5% for any supplier, immediately increase sample size and review tier assignment.
summary: Risk-tiered receiving inspection strategy using ANSI/ASQ Z1.4 AQL sampling for Tier A/B/C aerospace suppliers with lot rejection logic, step-down criteria, and monthly defect escape rate tracking

label: Supplier Root-Cause Investigation — Process vs. Manufacturing Anomaly
content: Structured 48-hour protocol distinguishing supplier process inadequacy from isolated manufacturing anomalies per AS9100D 10.2. STEP 1 — DEFINE DEFECT POPULATION: Count affected units across full production lot. ≥2 similar defects from same supplier lot = pattern → supplier process investigation required. Single defect with no similar priors in prior 50 units = anomaly → internal investigation first. STEP 2 — REQUEST SUPPLIER PROCESS DATA (24-hour deadline): Tool offset log (last 30 days), calibration records, SPC data for affected lot production dates, material cert, operator shift assignment. Response delay >48 hours = QMS weakness indicator — escalate to Quality Manager. STEP 3 — SUPPLIER PROCESS HYPOTHESIS TESTING: (a) Tool offset drift — cross-reference SPC with offset log; (b) Material batch variation — compare defect characteristic to material cert lot analysis; (c) Measurement system drift — compare current Gage R&R to last calibration (R&R increase >5 percentage points suggests measurement ambiguity, not actual defect); (d) Operator/process change — cross-reference defect timestamp with shift change, operator turnover, procedure revision, tool replacement. STEP 4 — MANUFACTURING ANOMALY HYPOTHESIS TESTING: (a) Procedure ambiguity — walk procedure with assembler who found defect; (b) Fixture wear — measure 5 consecutive assemblies; if variation >20% of characteristic tolerance → fixture problem, not supplier; (c) Handling damage — inspect for drop or impact evidence. STEP 5 — CLASSIFICATION DECISION: Supplier Process (≥70% evidence confidence) → issue CAR to supplier within 48 hours; Manufacturing Anomaly → internal corrective action; Design-Manufacture Interface → coordinate Engineering+Supplier joint investigation. REJECT as root causes: 'operator error,' 'will be more careful,' or 'retrained operators' without a documented process-level change eliminating the enabling condition.
summary: 48-hour evidence-chain protocol classifying supplier defects as process inadequacy, manufacturing anomaly, or design-manufacture interface through 5-step hypothesis testing — preventing superficial CAR closure and corrective action waste

label: Supplier CAR Effectiveness Verification — 30/90-Day Protocol
content: Two-phase post-CAR effectiveness verification replacing reliance on documented supplier response as proof of corrective action. PHASE 1 — 30-DAY DATA VERIFICATION (after corrective action implementation date): Supplier submits verification data package: (a) SPC data from first post-CAR production lot (n≥30; if lot <30, measure entire lot); (b) Ppk on verification lot — target ≥1.33; Ppk 1.0–1.32 → CAR incomplete, require updated plan within 5 business days; Ppk <1.0 → supplier production hold; (c) Gage R&R study confirming measurement system stable (<10% for critical tolerances); (d) Process records demonstrating procedure compliance (tool offset log showing documented approval pattern, CMM verification records, revised procedure training sign-offs). ACCEPT criteria: zero nonconformances AND Ppk ≥1.33 AND measurement system verified. PHASE 2 — 90-DAY ONSITE AUDIT (highest confidence — documentation can be fabricated, witnessed production cannot): (a) Revised procedure posted at workstation in current revision; (b) Operator knowledge spot-check (interview 2–3 randomly selected operators: can they explain the new requirement without prompting?); (c) Process records on file — 100% of documented events required by CAR present for past 30 days; (d) SPC chart current within last work week. AUDIT OUTCOMES: Pass → CAR formally closed in CAPA log; Conditional Pass (1–2 minor gaps) → 30-day re-check before closure; Fail → second CAR initiated OR escalate to supplier performance probation (monthly audits, 100% incoming indefinitely). RECURRENCE RULE: Same defect within 6 months of CAR closure → Tier 3 escalation + alternative supplier evaluation triggered immediately; receiving plan reverts to Tier C (100% incoming).
summary: Two-phase supplier CAR effectiveness verification: 30-day production data package (Ppk ≥1.33, Gage R&R, process records) and 90-day onsite audit of actual procedure compliance — with recurrence triggering alternative-supplier evaluation

label: Safety-Critical Supplier Defect Disposition — Class A/B/C
content: Risk-based disposition framework for safety-critical supplier defects with field failure and retrofit implications. CRITICALITY CLASSIFICATION: Class A (Critical) — failure endangers crew or causes aircraft loss (structural crack, control surface, brake system, flight-control software); Class B (Major) — significant performance degradation, field repair possible (sensor accuracy drift, corrosion in electrical connector, non-critical sealing failure); Class C (Minor) — negligible safety impact, cosmetic or operational (coating thickness, surface finish). CLASS A DISPOSITION: MANDATORY SCRAP all affected units in lot — no rework acceptable unless FEA confirms defect cannot propagate under max service load across full service life (structural margin analysis required); halt supplier production immediately; Engineering failure analysis within 48 hours; if any units delivered to customer → notify within 48 hours with retrofit plan; field retrofit mandatory if safety analysis cannot bound defect severity. No cost consideration overrides scrap for Class A when FEA is inconclusive. CLASS B DISPOSITION: 100% inspection of remaining lot; accept marginal nonconformance (e.g., sensor ±1.2% when spec ±1%) only with Engineering field-use analysis confirming downstream system tolerates drift; customer notification only if units exceed spec by >20% or field failure probability is non-negligible; document field-surveillance plan (increased inspection at next maintenance interval). CLASS C DISPOSITION: Accept with documentation; rework only if rework cost <10% of unit cost; CAR with 30-day deadline; no customer notification. SINGLE-SOURCE + CLASS A CONFLICT: Escalate to PM + customer within 24 hours; jointly develop field-retrofit plan with serial number traceability to affected supplier lot; do NOT release any suspected-lot units until Engineering disposition approved; accept schedule slip rather than release safety risk. FEA RETROFIT DECISION: If rework restores geometry but cannot restore original strength margin → scrap mandatory for Class A; if FEA confirms restored margin → rework acceptable with 100% NDT (dye-penetrant or X-ray) post-rework and documented traceability.
summary: Risk-based disposition decision for Class A/B/C safety-critical supplier defects covering scrap/retrofit/accept criteria, FEA requirements, customer notification thresholds, and single-source field retrofit planning for aerospace manufacturing

label: Supplier Quality Scorecard — LRIP Aerospace KPIs
content: Monthly supplier quality scorecard for LRIP aerospace programs with 6 metrics and action thresholds. METRIC 1 — INCOMING LOT ACCEPTANCE RATE: Target ≥98%; Yellow <95% (trend review + sample size increase); Red <90% (immediate audit + CAR + alternative-source activation). METRIC 2 — ROLLING Ppk (30-part basis from supplier SPC submissions): Target ≥1.33; Yellow 1.0–1.32 (100% incoming + CAR within 5 days); Red <1.0 (supplier production hold + alternative-source activation). METRIC 3 — DEFECT ESCAPE RATE (defects found in-process that passed receiving): Target <0.5%; Yellow ≥0.5% (sample size increase + require supplier pre-shipment SPC approval); Red ≥1% (receiving plan mandatory revision + 100% inspection pending investigation). METRIC 4 — CAR CLOSURE RATE (with verified effectiveness within 90 days): Target ≥80%; Yellow <80% (escalate to supplier Quality Manager); Red <60% (escalate to supplier VP + PM). METRIC 5 — CAR RECURRENCE RATE (same root cause within 6 months of closure): Target 0%; Yellow ≥1 recurrence (performance probation trigger — monthly audits, 100% incoming indefinitely); Red ≥2 recurrences in 12 months (alternative-supplier qualification mandatory, begin immediately). METRIC 6 — ON-TIME DELIVERY + LEAD-TIME VARIABILITY: Target ≥98% deliveries within ±3 days of promise; variability target ±10%; Red ±30% variability (secondary source qualification required to protect schedule). GOVERNANCE: Quality Manager presents monthly to PM + Supply Chain + Engineering; supplier receives scorecard for self-aware accountability. OVERALL RATING: Green (all metrics met) → maintain plan; Yellow (1–2 metrics off) → 30-day corrective plan with named owner; Red (any single Red or ≥3 Yellow) → Tier 3 escalation + make-vs-buy analysis + alternative-source qualification within 30 days. MANDATORY TREND: 3-month rolling Ppk and defect rate trend plotted monthly; declining trend triggers escalation before threshold breach.
summary: Six-metric monthly supplier quality scorecard (lot acceptance, Ppk, defect escape, CAR closure, CAR recurrence, on-time delivery) with Green/Yellow/Red thresholds, action triggers, and monthly governance cadence measuring whether supplier quality management actually works

label: Equipment RCFA Decision Tree and Failure Classification
content: Root cause failure analysis for production equipment unplanned downtime. MANDATORY RCFA TRIGGER: Any unplanned downtime >4 hrs on Tier 0 equipment requires RCFA completed within 48 hrs. FOUR FAILURE CATEGORIES: (1) Maintenance-preventable: Was last PM completed per schedule? Were acceptance criteria met? If PM was missed or criteria waved through — category 1. Cost code: Facility Maintenance account; triggers PM procedure review. (2) Operator-induced: Did operator deviate from setup procedure, exceed rated parameters, or load incorrect tooling? Category 2. Cost code: Training program budget. (3) Design-limited: Does OEM service manual identify a fatigue/wear life limit at this failure point? Is this a known failure mode documented in OEM bulletins? Category 3. Cost code: Equipment depreciation/obsolescence account; triggers modernization evaluation. (4) Age-related degradation: Does equipment exceed OEM design-life hours? Is failure mode consistent with end-of-life material fatigue? Category 4. Cost code: Equipment replacement reserve fund. DECISION TREE: (A) Was last PM completed on schedule? NO → Category 1. YES → (B) Were PM acceptance criteria satisfied? NO → Category 1. YES → (C) Does OEM document this as a known design-limited failure mode? YES → Category 3. NO → (D) Does equipment exceed OEM design-life hours? YES → Category 4. NO → (E) Did operator deviate from standard procedure? YES → Category 2. NO → Environmental/unforeseeable (document separately, no category charge). RCFA LOG: Equipment | Failure mode | Downtime hrs | Category | Cost coded to | Corrective action | Owner | Closure date. TRENDING THRESHOLD: If ≥3 failures in same category within 6 months on same equipment class → systemic corrective action required (PM procedure change, OEM consultation, or accelerated replacement evaluation). Monthly RCFA trending review at Production Control Review.
summary: Procedure: 48-hour RCFA requirement for Tier 0 unplanned downtime with four-category failure classification decision tree (maintenance-preventable, operator-induced, design-limited, age-related), cost-pool coding per root cause, and systemic trending escalation trigger

label: Maintenance Cost Allocation for Multi-Program Shared Equipment
content: Cost allocation methodology for shared production equipment maintenance across multiple LRIP programs. FOUR COST POOLS: (1) Baseline PM (scheduled preventive maintenance labor + materials) — allocate as overhead using equipment utilization rate; split proportionally across programs by hours used that month. (2) Corrective maintenance (non-failure deficiencies found during PM, opportunistic repairs) — allocate as facility overhead; do not charge to individual programs. (3) Failure-driven corrective maintenance — direct charge to responsible party per RCFA result: Category 1 (maintenance-preventable) → Facility Maintenance account; Category 2 (operator-induced) → charge back to program whose operator caused failure; Category 3 (design-limited) → Equipment depreciation/capital account; Category 4 (age-related) → Equipment replacement reserve. (4) Spare-parts carrying cost (on-hand inventory) — allocate as facility overhead proportional to equipment utilization. MONTHLY COST REPORT FORMAT: Equipment ID | Baseline PM cost | Corrective PM cost | Failure-repair cost | Spare-parts carrying | Allocated to (program %) | Disputed charges. DISPUTE RESOLUTION: Program Manager disputes maintenance charge → RCFA record is the adjudicating document. If RCFA shows Category 1 or 2, charge stands; if RCFA shows Category 3 or 4, reverse program charge and credit to equipment capital account. ERP IMPLEMENTATION: Set each equipment asset as a cost center; link PM work orders to facility overhead order; link failure-repair work orders to RCFA category code; run monthly allocation report to distribute overhead proportionally. CARRYING COST TARGET: On-hand spare-parts inventory carrying cost ≤5% of equipment acquisition value annually.
summary: System: Four-pool maintenance cost allocation (baseline PM as overhead, corrective PM as overhead, failure-driven as direct charge per RCFA category, spare-parts carrying as overhead), monthly cost report structure, RCFA-based dispute resolution, and ERP cost center implementation for multi-program shared equipment

label: Activity-Based Overhead Allocation for Multi-Program Manufacturing
content: Replace direct-labor-hour overhead allocation with cost-driver-based pools when overhead is driven by facility occupancy, lot frequency, or support-function volume rather than labor intensity. FOUR OVERHEAD POOLS: (1) Facility occupancy (rent, utilities, facility maintenance) — allocate by work-cell hours booked per program from MPS; rate = total facility overhead $ ÷ total facility hours available. (2) Material handling and logistics — allocate by build-lot count × material release events per program; rate = total material-handling overhead $ ÷ total material releases. (3) Quality and inspection — allocate by inspection sampling volume (# of parts inspected); rate = total inspection overhead $ ÷ total parts inspected. (4) Rework coordination and NCR management — allocate by NCR event count per program; rate = total rework-coordination overhead $ ÷ total NCR events. RATE CALCULATION (annual, updated quarterly): (a) Pull total actual overhead by pool from accounting system. (b) Pull cost-driver quantities from MPS + ERP (cell hours, lot count, parts inspected, NCR count). (c) Calculate $/driver unit. (d) Apply to each program based on their actual driver consumption. MONTHLY RECONCILIATION: Compare allocated overhead to actual; investigate >10% variance — indicates cost-driver measurement error or pool assumption shift. DISPUTE RESOLUTION: Program Manager challenges allocation → adjudicate using MPS cell-hours log and ERP lot-release records as objective evidence; direct-labor-hour allocation is not defensible when facility-hour consumption differs materially between programs.
summary: System: Four-pool activity-based overhead allocation (facility hours, lot count, parts inspected, NCR events) replacing direct-labor-hour allocation when overhead is facility-occupancy or support-function driven

label: Two-Component Labor Productivity Measurement
content: Separate manufacturing labor into process-dependent (inherent) and efficiency-driven (variable) components to isolate where improvement efforts have leverage. COMPONENT A — PROCESS-DEPENDENT (controlled by physics and engineering spec): CMM dimensional inspection (hrs/feature count × tolerance complexity); heat-treat transport and loading (fixed time + batch-size time); solvent wash throughput (lbs/hr by part mass); FAI testing per AS9102 (hrs = test plan scope). Measurement: Xbar-R control chart for actual hours vs. process baseline; >2σ above baseline = hidden process delay investigation trigger (queue, equipment downtime, fixture misalignment). Variability tolerance: ±10–20% depending on process; flag systematic drift, not random variation. COMPONENT B — EFFICIENCY-DRIVEN (controlled by technician procedure discipline): Assembly sequencing and kitting (baseline 6 hrs/standard kit + 1 hr/variant; variance +0% optimal to +40% poor planning); work-cell setup and changeover (baseline 30 min; variance to +60% if reactive staging); NCR triage and documentation (baseline 15 min diagnosis + 10 min documentation; variance to +50% without structured form); material handling (4 hrs/lot manual vs. 1.5 hrs/lot with scan-in). Measurement: monthly labor variance report by technician; flag >10% above baseline as retraining or process-investigation trigger. INDIRECT LABOR CATEGORIES: Track separately from direct labor — material handling, quality inspection, rework coordination, and setup consume significant labor without contributing to part value. Target indirect labor ≤15–20% of total labor; investigate if rising trend.
summary: System: Two-component labor productivity model separating process-dependent (physics-constrained, measured by control chart) from efficiency-driven (procedure-dependent, measured by variance report) labor with indirect labor category tracking

label: WIP Limits and Inventory Flow Control by Program Tier
content: Establish maximum WIP limits per program tier to prevent facility congestion, material-handling overhead, and rework cascade risk. WIP LIMITS: Tier 0 (committed) = max 3 build lots in-progress simultaneously; halt new kit release if exceeded. Tier 1 (pilot/FAI) = max 2 build lots + 1 FAI exploratory article; defer next lot if exceeded. Tier 1B (prototype) = max 1 build lot + active ECO evaluations for current design revision only; halt if design changes pending that would obsolete in-progress WIP. DAILY WIP DASHBOARD (updated 8 AM): Track per entity — Program, Lot, Current Stage, Parts Complete/Total, Queue Days, Hold Type (measurement backlog / design clarification / material wait / quality disposition). RELEASE GATES: (1) Queue days >3 AND hold type = measurement system → escalate to Metrology; activate portable gage or external lab. (2) Queue days >5 AND hold type = design clarification → escalate to Engineering + PM; unblock or defer lot. (3) Total Tier 0 + Tier 1 active entities >5 → halt all new kit releases until congestion clears. (4) Tier 1B WIP with pending ECO that changes part interface → quarantine WIP until design baselined; do not advance to avoid obsolescence scrap. MATERIAL HANDLING OVERHEAD CONTROL: Measure material-movement time as % of total cycle time; target <10% of cycle time. If >10%, assess facility layout or scheduling coordination before physical relocation (layout ROI typically low in low-rate production).
summary: System: WIP limits by program tier (Tier 0 ≤3 lots, Tier 1 ≤2+1 FAI, Tier 1B ≤1 lot), daily dashboard tracking, queue-day release gates, and congestion-halt triggers

label: Operator Cross-Training and Certification Management
content: Three-level certification system tied to manufacturing risk and task complexity for multi-program aerospace production. CERTIFICATION LEVELS: Level 1 Basic — 40 hrs classroom + 10 hrs supervised execution; authorized for Tier 1B prototype assembly (non-critical dimensions), material handling, basic setup under L2/L3 supervision; re-validate annually (4 hrs). Level 2 SPC and In-Process — 80 hrs (40 classroom + 40 supervised production + 10 independently inspected parts at ±5% tolerance agreement); authorized for Tier 1 pilot production, control-chart sign-off, Tier 0 non-critical geometry assembly; re-validate semi-annually (2 hrs SPC audit) + annual re-qualification 5-part sample. Level 3 FAI Lead and Tier 0 — 160 hrs (40 classroom + 80 supervised FAI + 40 independent Tier 0 at ≥95% first-pass dimensional accuracy); authorized for all work including Tier 0 critical deliveries, FAI planning and execution, operator certification sign-off; re-validate annually (8 hrs procedure update) + triennial full re-certification. SINGLE-POINT-OF-FAILURE PROTOCOL: If only 1 operator certified at Level 3 for a critical process (CMM FAI, hazmat handling, critical assembly), escalate immediately to PM; authorize 40-hr L3 acceleration training for next-in-pipeline L2 operator (compress 12-week rotation to 4 weeks via supervised Tier 0 work). DEPLOYMENT RULES: All Tier 0 work must be assigned ≥Level 3 (target 100%; flag if <95%). If total Level 3 availability <80% of Tier 0 demand: defer Tier 1B (Shop Floor Supervisor authority), authorize overtime (PM authority ≤$2K/week), or accelerate L2→L3 pipeline. TRACKING: Maintain certification-expiration log with automated 2-week advance alerts; remove operators from authorized task list immediately on expiration; do not allow work until re-qualification complete.
summary: System: Three-level operator certification (L1 Basic 50 hrs, L2 SPC 80 hrs, L3 FAI Lead 160 hrs), single-point-of-failure escalation protocol, Tier 0 deployment rules, and certification-expiration tracking

label: Aerospace Supplier Agreement Key Provisions Framework
content: Establishing Tier-2 visibility for raw material sourcing is the central control for counterfeit prevention when second-tier suppliers are beyond direct program sight. CONTRACTUAL MECHANISM: Insert in supplier quality agreement and on PO terms: supplier must disclose all Tier-2 raw material sources within 30 days of PO award; Tier-2 supplier changes require 30-day advance written notification and program QM approval; unauthorized substitution is grounds for lot rejection and CAR. SUPPLY-CHAIN DISCLOSURE FORM (issue with every PO for raw materials): (1) Tier-2 supplier name, contact, and material supplied; (2) Distributor franchise status (authorized vs. independent); (3) AS9100D or equivalent QMS status; (4) FAR/DFARS flow-down acknowledgment signed by Tier-2 supplier QM; (5) Statement that no salvage, recycled, or grey-market material is used without written program approval; (6) Country of origin for all materials (critical for foreign subcontractors — validates Buy American Act / DFARS 252.225 compliance). APPROVED DISTRIBUTOR LIST: Maintain per-material-category list of authorized mills, OEMs, and franchise distributors; include in supplier agreement as controlled appendix; update quarterly; any purchase from non-listed source requires QM approval and triggers enhanced receiving inspection (material composition testing required). SUPPLIER RELUCTANCE: If supplier refuses Tier-2 disclosure, treat as major audit finding; require written risk acceptance from Program Manager if program must continue sourcing; initiate backup supplier qualification. LOT-LEVEL TRACEABILITY REQUIREMENT: CoC must reference specific heat/lot number traceable to original mill or OEM — aggregate certs referencing 'per specification' without lot number do not satisfy traceability requirements and are grounds for lot hold. FOREIGN SUBCONTRACTOR ADDITIONAL CONTROLS: For foreign subcontractors, require (a) insurance/indemnification — product liability insurance with coverage minimums appropriate to flight-critical hardware (typically $5M–$25M per occurrence, program-dependent), commercial general liability, and professional liability; require certificate of insurance naming prime contractor and government as additional insureds; (b) business continuity plan — validated annually, with alternative facility identified and recovery-time commitments documented; (c) force majeure provisions — define scope narrowly, require 48-hour notification, cap relief period. RED FLAG INDICATORS: Cost proposal drops >15% vs. baseline (possible grey-market sourcing); supplier cannot provide original mill cert on request; CoC lot number format changes between shipments without explanation; distributor not on Approved List.
summary: System: Master framework for aerospace supplier agreement structure covering FAR/DFARS flow-down, ITAR controls, quality requirements, payment terms, design-change authority, capacity allocation, business continuity, and commercial risk allocation

label: Supplier Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery Contract Provisions
content: Contractual business continuity requirements for aerospace long-term supplier agreements. ANNUAL PLAN DELIVERABLES (supplier obligation): (1) Facility risk assessment: primary location, hazard exposure (flood zone, seismic, hurricane), criticality (% production loss if facility lost). (2) Alternative production capability: secondary facility location, equipment installed and validated (not theoretical), operator training currency, activation lead-time in days. (3) Inventory pre-positioning: safety stock % at backup facility, critical tooling replicated, FIFO rotation procedure. (4) Communication procedures: notify Contractor within 2 hours of disruption; daily status reports during recovery; escalation path QM → GM → PM direct. (5) Recovery-time commitments: non-critical <5 business days (buffer stock), standard <10 days (contingency activation), critical long-lead <15 days (alternative supplier pre-positioned). ACTIVATION TRIGGERS: facility-wide disruption (fire, flood, power loss >8 hours), primary equipment failure >2 days, workforce disruption >1 shift, financial distress (bankruptcy filing, covenant violation, credit downgrade). ACTIVATION DECISION AUTHORITY: PM + QM assess recovery feasibility within 4 hours of Supplier notification; if recovery >5 days likely, PM authorizes contingency supplier PO within 24 hours. SUPPLIER COOPERATION OBLIGATIONS: provide drawings/BOM within 6 hours of activation; transfer in-process WIP if feasible; waive proprietary process restrictions (NDA required); provide setup procedures within 12 hours. CERTIFICATION REQUIREMENT: Supplier QM certifies plan tested within 24 months, backup facility operationally validated, backup operators trained within 12 months. COMMON CONTRACT FAILURE: accepting theoretical backup facility with no installed equipment or trained operators — require operational validation evidence at contract award.
summary: Procedure: Contractual business continuity provisions requiring supplier disaster-recovery documentation, alternative facility validation, recovery-time commitments, and activation authority for aerospace supply-continuity protection

label: Supplier Payment Terms Invoice Discipline and Working Capital Protection
content: Payment and invoice procedures for aerospace supplier agreements. RECEIPT-BASED AUTHORIZATION: supplier may NOT invoice until Contractor physical receipt AND quality acceptance confirmed; no advance payments, deposits, or progress payments except specifically-negotiated long-lead material releases (Material Release Authorization letter required). INVOICE DOCUMENTATION REQUIREMENTS (mandatory; reject if missing): PO number + revision, part number + CI engineering revision, quantity shipped and received, unit price per PO (no unauthorized additions), invoice date + number, supplier name + tax ID, shipping documentation (date, carrier, tracking). PROCESSING FLOW: PO issued → supplier manufactures → delivery → Contractor receiving + QA acceptance → invoice submission → Finance approval (Net 30 from receipt). PAYMENT TERMS: Standard Net 30; early payment discount 2/10 available for suppliers with >95% quality and 100% on-time delivery ≥6 months sustained. DISCREPANCY HANDLING: quantity discrepancy—invoice reflects received quantity, shortage enters recovery procedure; price discrepancy—invoice rejected, 5-day correction window; missing documentation—invoice held, 2-day notification, 5-day resubmission window. PAYMENT HOLD AUTHORITY: Contractor may defer payment (maximum 30 days) for: confirmed quality defect rework costs, property loss during supplier custody, expedited recovery cost from supplier schedule miss; documented with supporting evidence; hold does not cancel obligation—defers timing. DEDUCTION AUTHORITY: deduct confirmed costs from payment; deduction held pending supplier dispute resolution (30-day window). SUPPLIER FINANCIAL MONITORING: annual audited financials; monthly D&B credit check; lien/bankruptcy screening; downgrade triggers PM review (may require letter of credit for continued production).
summary: Procedure: Supplier payment terms, PO-to-invoice linkage, receipt-based authorization, invoice documentation requirements, payment hold authority, and supplier financial health monitoring to protect program working capital while maintaining supplier stability

label: DFARS 252.246-7007 and 252.246-7008 counterfeit electronic parts requirements
content: DFARS 252.246-7007 (Counterfeit Electronic Part Detection and Avoidance System) applies to prime contractors on DoD contracts when the contract includes electronic parts. KEY REQUIREMENTS: (1) Establish and maintain a counterfeit electronic part detection and avoidance system covering design, procurement, inspection, reporting, and corrective action; (2) Only purchase electronic parts from the original component manufacturer (OCM), original equipment manufacturer (OEM), or authorized distributors — purchases from other sources (brokers, independent distributors) require enhanced inspection per 252.246-7008(c); (3) GIDEP (Government-Industry Data Exchange Program) reporting mandatory within 60 days of confirming counterfeit part — failure to report is itself a DFARS violation; (4) System must cover: training, inspection/test methods, making traceability records, identifying and segregating suspect parts, quarantine, corrective action, and flow-down to subcontractors. DFARS 252.246-7008 (Sources of Electronic Parts): requires prime to purchase electronic parts from OCM/OEM or authorized distributors; if purchasing from non-authorized sources, contractor must establish risk-based inspection processes. FLOW-DOWN: Both clauses must be flowed to all subcontractors providing electronic parts. IMPORTANT SCOPE LIMIT: These clauses govern electronic/electrical parts — they do NOT establish counterfeit requirements for raw structural materials (metals, composites, fasteners governed by different contract mechanisms and AS6174).
summary: DFARS 252.246-7007 requires DoD prime counterfeit electronic part detection system, GIDEP reporting within 60 days, authorized distributor sourcing; 252.246-7008 restricts electronic part sources; both require subcontractor flow-down

label: Counterfeit parts standards scope: AS5553 vs AS6081 vs AS6174 — correct application
content: CRITICAL SCOPE DISTINCTIONS — applying the wrong standard is a compliance error: AS5553/AS5553A (SAE): Counterfeit electronic/electrical/electromechanical (EEE) parts avoidance for MANUFACTURERS and contractors. Covers procurement, inspection, testing, traceability, and reporting. Applicable to: any company manufacturing or assembling products containing EEE parts. AS6081 (SAE): Counterfeit EEE parts avoidance for INDEPENDENT DISTRIBUTORS (brokers, aftermarket distributors) only. Governs how distributors authenticate, test, and document electronic parts before resale. NOT applicable to manufacturers sourcing directly from OCMs or to non-electronic material sourcing. Common misapplication: citing AS6081 as the governing standard for an aerospace manufacturer's raw material (metals, composites) counterfeit prevention — incorrect; AS6081 scope is explicitly limited to independent distributor operations. AS6174 (SAE): Counterfeit Materiel Assurance Countermeasures — applies to NON-ELECTRONIC parts and materials (structural metals, fasteners, composites, elastomers) at the manufacturer level. This is the correct reference for raw material counterfeit prevention programs covering metals and composites. AS6496 (SAE): Fraudulent/Counterfeit Parts; Avoidance, Detection, Mitigation, and Disposition in the Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul (MRO) Environment — aviation MRO context, not applicable to manufacturing. SELECTION GUIDE: Manufacturing EEE parts → AS5553; Buying from broker/distributor → require AS6081 compliance from distributor; Structural raw materials (non-electronic) → AS6174; DoD contract → DFARS 252.246-7007 overlays AS5553 requirements.
summary: AS5553 governs manufacturers for EEE parts; AS6081 governs independent distributors of EEE parts only (not manufacturers, not raw materials); AS6174 governs non-electronic material counterfeit avoidance; AS6496 is MRO-specific — applying AS6081 to raw material sourcing is scope error

label: Supplier Tier-2 supply-chain disclosure and counterfeit risk visibility
content: Establishing Tier-2 visibility for raw material sourcing is the central control for counterfeit prevention when second-tier suppliers are beyond direct program sight. CONTRACTUAL MECHANISM: Insert in supplier quality agreement and on PO terms: supplier must disclose all Tier-2 raw material sources within 30 days of PO award; Tier-2 supplier changes require 30-day advance written notification and program QM approval; unauthorized substitution is grounds for lot rejection and CAR. SUPPLY-CHAIN DISCLOSURE FORM (issue with every PO for raw materials): (1) Tier-2 supplier name, contact, and material supplied; (2) Distributor franchise status (authorized vs. independent); (3) AS9100D or equivalent QMS status; (4) FAR/DFARS flow-down acknowledgment signed by Tier-2 supplier QM; (5) Statement that no salvage, recycled, or grey-market material is used without written program approval. APPROVED DISTRIBUTOR LIST: Maintain per-material-category list of authorized mills, OEMs, and franchise distributors; include in supplier agreement as controlled appendix; update quarterly; any purchase from non-listed source requires QM approval and triggers enhanced receiving inspection (material composition testing required). SUPPLIER RELUCTANCE: If supplier refuses Tier-2 disclosure, treat as major audit finding; require written risk acceptance from Program Manager if program must continue sourcing; initiate backup supplier qualification. LOT-LEVEL TRACEABILITY REQUIREMENT: CoC must reference specific heat/lot number traceable to original mill or OEM — aggregate certs referencing 'per specification' without lot number do not satisfy traceability requirements and are grounds for lot hold. RED FLAG INDICATORS: Cost proposal drops >15% vs. baseline (possible grey-market sourcing); supplier cannot provide original mill cert on request; CoC lot number format changes between shipments without explanation; distributor not on Approved List.
summary: Supplier Tier-2 supply-chain disclosure via PO contract terms, 30-day change notification requirement, Approved Distributor List enforcement, lot-level CoC traceability requirement, and red-flag indicators for grey-market sourcing — the primary control for supply-chain depth visibility gap

label: Foreign subcontractor anti-corruption and FCPA due diligence
content: Decision framework for Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA, 15 USC §78dd) due diligence when onboarding foreign subcontractors on DoD programs. FCPA prohibits paying or offering anything of value to foreign government officials to obtain or retain business — applies to US companies and their agents/subcontractors regardless of where the conduct occurs. DUE DILIGENCE STEPS: (1) Beneficial ownership analysis — identify ultimate beneficial owners of foreign subcontractor; screen against PEP (Politically Exposed Persons) databases; flag any government ownership, military affiliation, or government-official board members. (2) Country risk assessment — evaluate Transparency International Corruption Perceptions Index for subcontractor's country; countries scoring <40 (out of 100) require enhanced due diligence. (3) Red flag review — unusual commission structures, requests for cash payments, use of shell companies, reluctance to provide financial transparency, third-party payment routing through non-contract parties, requests for payments to accounts in countries unrelated to the work. (4) Anti-corruption representations — subcontract must include FCPA compliance certification, anti-bribery clause, right to audit financial records, and immediate termination right upon FCPA violation. (5) Ongoing monitoring — annual FCPA compliance certification from subcontractor; re-screen principals against PEP lists at each contract modification. FAR 52.203-13 (Contractor Code of Business Ethics) requires contractor to exercise due diligence to detect criminal conduct including FCPA violations in the supply chain. FCPA violations carry criminal penalties up to $250K per violation for individuals and $2M for organizations, plus disgorgement of profits and potential debarment.
summary: FCPA due diligence decision framework for foreign subcontractor onboarding: beneficial ownership analysis, country risk assessment, red flag indicators, anti-corruption contract clauses, and ongoing monitoring requirements

label: Composite manufacturing special process qualification
content: Special process qualification requirements for composite manufacturing of flight-critical aerospace parts. PROCESS CATEGORIES REQUIRING QUALIFICATION: (1) Autoclave cure — temperature/pressure profile validation per material supplier-specified cure cycle; thermocouple survey demonstrating temperature uniformity within ±5°F (material-dependent) across tool surface; cure cycle recorder with continuous logging. (2) Hand layup / automated fiber placement (AFP) — operator certification per ply count, orientation accuracy, and compaction method; ply placement tolerance typically ±0.060" positional, ±2° angular (verify against engineering spec). (3) Resin transfer molding (RTM) / vacuum-assisted RTM (VARTM) — flow simulation validation, injection pressure/vacuum level monitoring, fill-time recording. (4) NDT for composites — ultrasonic (pulse-echo or through-transmission) per ASTM E2580 or program-specific spec; radiographic inspection for metallic inserts, fastener holes, and foreign object detection; acceptance criteria for void content (typically <2% by volume for flight-critical, program-dependent), delamination size limits per damage tolerance analysis. MATERIAL QUALIFICATION: Incoming material acceptance testing — resin content, fiber areal weight, volatile content, glass transition temperature (Tg), shelf life / out-time tracking (prepreg systems); material stored per manufacturer requirements (typically -0°F freezer for prepreg with cumulative out-time log). ENVIRONMENTAL CONTROLS: Layup room temperature (typically 65–77°F) and humidity (<60% RH for epoxy systems) monitored and recorded; deviations documented as process nonconformances. KEY CHARACTERISTICS FOR COMPOSITES: fiber volume fraction (target per design, typically 55–65% for structural aerospace), void content, interlaminar shear strength, glass transition temperature post-cure, cured ply thickness. NADCAP COMPOSITES ACCREDITATION: Covers layup, cure, bonding, and repair; audit evaluates process control documentation, operator training records, environmental monitoring, NDT capability, and traceability from raw material lot to cured part serial number.
summary: Composite manufacturing special process qualification: autoclave cure validation, layup operator certification, NDT acceptance criteria (void content, delamination limits), material incoming acceptance, environmental controls, and NADCAP composites accreditation scope

label: GD&T information flow from design through manufacturing to inspection
content:
UNIFYING PRINCIPLE: The datum reference frame (DRF) defined on the engineering drawing must be faithfully simulated in both the manufacturing fixture and the CMM inspection setup. This single chain — DRF on drawing → DRF in fixture → DRF on CMM — is the thread that connects design intent to produced geometry to verified conformance. When this chain breaks (fixture does not replicate datum hierarchy, or CMM alignment does not match drawing datum order), parts that conform in one reference may fail in another.

Cross-functional procedure for propagating GD&T design intent through manufacturing and inspection. PHASE 1 — DESIGN DEFINITION: Mechanical engineer selects DRF based on assembly mating interfaces (3-2-1 locating principle); applies geometric tolerances (position, profile, orientation, runout, form) to functional features per Y14.5-2018; performs tolerance stack-up analysis to verify assembly fit; selects material condition modifiers (MMC where clearance-fit assembly benefits from bonus tolerance; RFS where precision location is required regardless of feature size); releases drawing through PLM with GD&T review checklist sign-off. PHASE 2 — MANUFACTURING PROCESS PLANNING: Manufacturing engineer maps each GD&T callout to a production operation; designs fixtures that replicate the DRF — fixture locating surfaces act as physical datum feature simulators (flat plate for primary datum plane, precision bore or pin for secondary/tertiary datum features of size); sequences operations so datum features are established first (machine primary datum surface before features controlled relative to it); verifies each tolerance is achievable at Cpk ≥1.33 with planned process; develops control plan linking GD&T characteristics to process parameters, measurement equipment, sampling frequency, and reaction plans. PHASE 3 — INSPECTION: Quality engineer develops CMM measurement plan with datum qualification sequence matching drawing DRF — primary datum qualified first (e.g., plane fit), then secondary constrained to primary, then tertiary; selects measurement algorithms per datum feature type (least squares for planes, max inscribed for internal features, min circumscribed for external features per Y14.5-2018 datum feature simulator definitions); verifies measurement uncertainty satisfies 4:1 TUR against each tolerance; executes FAI per AS9102 on first production article; establishes in-process inspection gates at post-machining and post-assembly stages. KEY LINKAGES: (a) DRF replication chain — fixture locating scheme must mirror drawing DRF; CMM alignment must mirror drawing DRF; if either breaks, parts conforming in one setup may fail in the other. (b) Process sequencing follows datum precedence — machine primary datum surface first so subsequent features reference a stable, finished datum. (c) Material condition modifiers flow through all three phases — MMC/LMC bonus tolerance widens the manufacturing process window when features depart from MMC/LMC size; functional gage dimensions are derived from virtual condition; CMM must evaluate position at actual mating envelope size to determine conformance. (d) Cpk targets flow from tolerance stack-up analysis — tighter contributors in the stack-up require higher process capability.

NOTE: When answering general questions about GD&T flow, emphasize the DRF replication principle and the three-phase linkage rather than narrowing to a single application scenario. Scenario-specific examples should illustrate the general principle, not replace it.
summary: Primary neuron for answering how GD&T flows from design through manufacturing to inspection. Core principle: the DRF defined on the drawing must be faithfully replicated in the manufacturing fixture and the CMM alignment — this single chain is the unifying thread across all three phases. Covers DRF selection, tolerance allocation, fixture datum simulation, process sequencing by datum precedence, CMM datum qualification, and bonus tolerance effects.

label: DRF Replication Principle Across Design-Manufacturing-Inspection
content: The DRF replication principle is the fundamental link between GD&T on an engineering drawing, manufacturing fixture design, and CMM inspection programming. PRINCIPLE: The datum reference frame specified on the drawing defines the coordinate system in which all geometric tolerances are evaluated. This same DRF must be physically simulated by (1) the manufacturing fixture (fixture locating surfaces act as datum feature simulators — a flat plate simulates a primary datum plane, a precision pin simulates a datum axis), and (2) the CMM alignment (CMM software qualifies datum features in the same precedence order — primary first, secondary constrained to primary, tertiary constrained to both — using algorithms appropriate to each datum feature type). WHEN THE CHAIN BREAKS: If the fixture does not replicate the drawing DRF (e.g., fixture locates on a convenience surface instead of the functional datum), parts produced in-spec per fixture measurement may fail CMM inspection aligned to the drawing datums. Conversely, if the CMM alignment deviates from the drawing DRF (e.g., uses unconstrained best-fit instead of 3-2-1 constrained alignment), inspection results will not reflect how the part functions in the assembly. PRACTICAL IMPLICATION: During manufacturing process planning, the manufacturing engineer must verify that fixture locating surfaces correspond to the drawing datum features and that the fixture datum hierarchy matches the drawing datum precedence order (A-B-C). During inspection planning, the quality engineer must verify that CMM datum qualification sequence and algorithms match the drawing DRF. Any mismatch between these three instantiations of the DRF creates a systematic error that may not be detectable from any single measurement.
summary: The unifying principle that the datum reference frame on the drawing must be faithfully simulated in both the manufacturing fixture and the CMM alignment — the fundamental link connecting design intent, production, and inspection

label: AS9100D Facility Infrastructure and Work Environment Requirements
content: AS9100D §8.5.1 requires adequate infrastructure and controlled work environment — but does NOT mandate cleanrooms, ISO 14644 particle classifications, or specific temperature/humidity ranges. Cleanroom requirements are customer-contractual or process-driven, not AS9100D mandates. WHAT AS9100D ACTUALLY REQUIRES FOR FACILITIES: (1) Configuration segregation per §8.5.6 — physical separation of different design revisions, build states, and programs; floor markings, bin labels, and locked staging prevent mixing; FAI articles held in designated isolated zone with restricted access. (2) FAI area isolation per AS9102 (First Article Inspection standard — NOT AS9145, which governs APQP/PPAP) — FAI articles physically staged separately until AS9102 balloon-check and CMM disposition complete; QM signature on each FAI traveler before production release. (3) Receiving quarantine per §8.4 — physical hold zone for uninspected incoming material; rejected lot cage separated from accepted stock; hot-list posting for suppliers under active CAR. (4) Calibrated equipment identification per §7.1.5 — out-of-calibration tools visually segregated (red tag, locked drawer) from calibrated tooling; no co-mingling in shared tool cribs. (5) Work environment monitoring proportional to product sensitivity — standard machining shop requires only documented temperature log if Ppk study shows drift correlation; dedicated HVAC loop and continuous monitoring only if customer contract specifies or process capability data demands. AUDITOR EXPECTATION: Demonstrate configuration traceability and FAI isolation integrity. Cleanroom certification is not an AS9100D audit criterion unless customer flow-down requires it.
summary: AS9100D §8.5.1 mandates configuration segregation, FAI isolation per AS9102, receiving quarantine, and calibration identification — NOT cleanroom classifications or ISO 14644 compliance, which are customer-contractual requirements

label: Foreign Object Debris (FOD) Prevention — AS9100D §8.5.1
content: AS9100D §8.5.1 requires a documented FOD prevention program as a production control requirement. FOD causes catastrophic failures in engines, hydraulics, and flight control assemblies — it is not optional housekeeping. MANDATORY PROGRAM ELEMENTS: (1) Tool and hardware accountability — shadow boards at every tool station with silhouette cutouts; tool count verified at operation start and end; missing item triggers production hold until resolved; tool inventory reconciled on traveler before work cell transfers to next operation. (2) Work cell design for FOD risk reduction — install plugs/caps in all open ports and cavities during in-process storage; designate FOD-critical zones with painted floor boundaries and signage; prohibit overhead work directly above open assemblies; use smooth cleanable hard flooring (no carpet) in FOD-critical zones; install task lighting ≥50 foot-candles to detect dropped items. (3) Pre-close inspections — mandatory documented visual inspection by QA before sealing any cavity, connector, access panel, or assembly closure; QA signature on traveler at each pre-close point; retain photographs as objective evidence. (4) Personnel training — annual FOD awareness training documenting consequences, identification, and immediate supervisor notification protocol for any unaccounted item regardless of perceived significance. (5) FOD audits — periodic unannounced spot audits of work cells; findings entered into QMS NCR system; repeat findings trigger CAPA with root cause analysis. TOOLING DESIGN: Use magnetic parts trays for ferrous hardware; color-code tool sets by work cell to prevent cross-cell migration; tether tools used near open assemblies. STANDARDS: AS9100D §8.5.1 (production control — FOD prevention); NAS 412 (Foreign Object Damage/Debris Prevention Program guidelines).
summary: AS9100D §8.5.1 FOD prevention program: tool shadow boards with accountability at each operation, pre-close cavity inspections with QA signature, FOD-critical zone facility design, annual training, and unannounced audits tracked in QMS NCR system

label: Facility Segregation for Concurrent Configuration Builds
content: AS9100D §8.5.2 (Identification and Traceability) requires maintaining unique identification of outputs and retaining documented information enabling traceability throughout production. For facilities running concurrent builds with different configurations: (1) STAGING ZONE SEGREGATION: Dedicate physically separate staging areas per active configuration variant; floor markings, labeled shelving, or physical barriers prevent inadvertent commingling. Hardware kits labeled with Configuration Index (CI) revision, work-order number, and serial number range. Only the active kit for the current work order positioned at the assembly workstation. (2) COLOR-CODED IDENTIFICATION: Assign distinct colors per configuration for hardware bags, staging containers, and work-cell placards — immediate visual verification without document lookup; color assignment documented in quality plan. (3) KIT VERIFICATION HOLDPOINT: Pre-assembly audit of kit contents (quantity, part numbers, CI revision, lot IDs) by QA before release to assembly floor — eliminates wrong-part-to-build errors at the source. Discrepant kits quarantined and not released until engineering disposition. (4) WORK-INSTRUCTION VERSION CONTROL: Configuration-specific work instructions stored in labeled, controlled location at each work cell; obsolete revisions removed and destroyed upon ECO release; CI revision cross-referenced on work instruction header — prevents use of superseded procedures. (5) CHANGEOVER PROTOCOL (single-cell, multi-config): When one work cell assembles multiple configurations sequentially, document: (a) surface cleanliness verification, (b) fixture/jig changeover with installation verification, (c) measurement system recalibration to new configuration tolerances, (d) work-instruction changeover with old instruction removed, (e) hardware bag color and QR verification. Supervisor sign-off required before new configuration starts — sign-off is AS9100 documented evidence of changeover completion. (6) CONFIGURATION VERIFICATION CHECKPOINTS: Integrate holdpoints at pre-assembly (kit audit), post-assembly (physical configuration audit against CI), and pre-shipment (configuration baseline register entry). Each holdpoint produces signed record with technician/auditor identity, date, and declared CI revision. PITFALL: Storing hardware bags for different configurations in adjacent unlabeled locations is the primary commingling failure mode — proximity without physical barriers or explicit labeling is a root cause, not a configuration control.
summary: AS9100D §8.5.2 physical segregation implementation: staged kit zones, color coding, changeover protocol, and verification holdpoints for concurrent multi-configuration manufacturing builds

label: Technician Traceability and Documentary Evidence Requirements
content: AS9100D §8.5.2 requires 'documented information to enable traceability' — for manufacturing this means objective evidence that the correct technician, using correct procedures, assembled the correct configuration in the declared sequence. REQUIRED DOCUMENTED INFORMATION: (1) ASSEMBLY SEQUENCE LOG: Record of each assembly step completion with technician identity (name/badge) and timestamp — demonstrates sequence was followed per work instruction. ERP/MES step-by-step logging provides strongest evidence; paper travelers are acceptable but require strict holdpoint signature discipline. System must prevent step skipping or out-of-sequence recording. (2) TECHNICIAN IDENTIFICATION AND ACCOUNTABILITY: Each unit traveler or activity log identifies technician by name and badge number — supports failure investigation if configuration errors discovered post-delivery. Multiple technicians on one unit each sign/log their specific steps. (3) COMPETENCY RECORDS (AS9100D §7.2): Training records demonstrating authorization for specific configuration assembly required before solo work; competency validation includes written assessment plus supervised assembly observation; annual refresher training documented. Certification scope (authorized configurations) must be documented and available at work cell. (4) MEASUREMENT RECORDS: Tool ID, calibration validity date, measured value, acceptance criterion, and recording technician for each critical characteristic — enables analysis of measurement system contribution to nonconformance and provides objective evidence of conformity. (5) CONFIGURATION VERIFICATION SIGN-OFF: Quality technician sign-off at each holdpoint with name, date/time, and declared CI revision — provides independent verification chain separate from assembly technician. (6) RETENTION (AS9100D §7.5.3): Retain documented information per customer/regulatory/legal requirements; aerospace contracts typically specify 10 years minimum — verify contract CDRL requirements before establishing retention period. (7) CAPITAL PRIORITIZATION: ERP/MES sequence logging directly supports manufacturing execution (prevents out-of-sequence assembly) AND generates AS9100 evidence — dual-purpose justification for capital investment. Standalone documentation systems that only generate compliance reports without influencing manufacturing execution have lower ROI priority. PITFALL: Unsigned or partially completed travelers are the most common AS9100 audit finding on traceability — establish process locks preventing units from advancing past holdpoints without required signatures.
summary: AS9100D §8.5.2 and §7.2 documentary evidence requirements for manufacturing: assembly sequence logs, technician identity, competency records, measurement traceability, and holdpoint sign-off chains

label: ISO 14644 Cleanroom Classification Selection for Aerospace Work Cells
content: Classification selection based on product contamination sensitivity and customer flow-down requirements. ISO 14644-1:2015 particle concentration limits at ≥0.5 µm: ISO Class 5 = 3,520/m³; ISO Class 6 = 35,200/m³; ISO Class 7 = 352,000/m³; ISO Class 8 = 3,520,000/m³ (formula: Cn = 10^N × (0.1/D)^2.08). AEROSPACE WORK CELL MAPPING: (1) Optical assembly (lenses, windows, sensor windows) → ISO 5–6; single particle ≥10 µm causes optical scatter defect; NADCAP AC7117 may mandate class. (2) Precision electronics, wire bonding, solder assembly → ISO 6–7; particle entrapment causes IPC-A-610 voids and early failure. (3) RF/microwave cavity final assembly → ISO 6–7; interior surfaces inaccessible post-seal; particle scoring degrades RF performance. (4) Composite bonding/adhesive cure → ISO 7–8; BAC5555 composite cure specs typically mandate ISO 7 minimum; particle-induced delamination risk. (5) General precision mechanical assembly, fastener insertion → ISO 7–8; acceptable defect rate achievable under SPC control. (6) Material staging, kit prep, bag opening → ISO 8–9; prevents external contamination ingress only. CLASSIFICATION DECISION PROCESS: (a) Review customer technical spec flow-down and NADCAP audit criteria (AC7108 electronics, AC7117 optics) for mandatory class — do not assume before reading contract; (b) if no mandatory class, evaluate defect sensitivity via trial assemblies in candidate environment (inoculate at ISO class upper limit; accept classification if failure rate increase ≤2% vs. higher-class control); (c) do not over-specify without defect-sensitivity data — excess classification adds capital and operational cost without quality benefit. COMMON PITFALL: Selecting ISO 6 for all work uniformly — use mixed-class zoning where feasible to reduce HVAC and filtration capital investment.
summary: ISO 14644-1 particle limits by class (ISO 5=3,520/m³ through ISO 8=3,520,000/m³ at ≥0.5 µm) with aerospace work cell classification mapping from optical/electronics (ISO 5–6) through composite bonding (ISO 7–8) to staging (ISO 8–9), and decision process requiring customer flow-down review before class selection

label: ISO 14644 Cleanroom Design Specifications — HVAC, Filtration, and Environmental Control
content: Facility design parameters per ISO 14644-3 and IEST-RP-CC002. AIRFLOW TYPE: ISO 3–6 require unidirectional (laminar) flow — HEPA/ULPA ceiling supply array with floor or low-sidewall return. ISO 7–8 use non-unidirectional (turbulent mixing) flow. AIR VELOCITY (UNIDIRECTIONAL FLOW, WORK ZONE): 0.2–0.5 m/s (40–100 FPM) per IEST-RP-CC002 §5; below 0.2 m/s risks particle settling; above 0.5 m/s increases turbulence and particle resuspension. CRITICAL: Supply-plenum air velocity is higher than work-zone face velocity — specification and measurement must be at work-zone level, not duct. AIR CHANGE RATES (TURBULENT ROOMS): ISO 6 = 150–300 ACH typical; ISO 7 = 60–150 ACH; ISO 8 = 10–25 ACH. TERMINAL FILTRATION: ISO 3–6 — ULPA U15 (99.9995% ≥0.12 µm) or HEPA H14 (99.995% ≥0.3 µm); pre-filter F9 minimum. ISO 7–8 — HEPA H13 (99.97% ≥0.3 µm); pre-filter F7. Return-air: HEPA H13 minimum for all classes. PRESSURE DIFFERENTIAL: +7.5–12.5 Pa (+0.03–0.05 in. w.c.) positive pressure vs. adjacent lower-class areas; cascade from highest to lowest class; monitor continuously via building automation system (BAS); alert on drift >10% from baseline. TEMPERATURE: ISO 5–6 = ±0.5°C of setpoint; ISO 7–8 = ±1–2°C. HUMIDITY: ESD-sensitive electronics ±3–5% RH; mechanical assembly ±5–10% RH. MATERIAL RESTRICTIONS: Avoid PVC, standard polyurethane foam, uncured resins (outgassing risk per IEST-RP-CC031); use 316 SS or hard-anodized aluminum for work surfaces and shelving. MIXED-CLASS CASCADE DESIGN: Shared HVAC plant supplying ISO 8 → ISO 7 → ISO 6 zones in pressure cascade reduces capital ~25% vs. independent systems for each class; ISO 8 return air (filtered) becomes ISO 7 supply input; reduces total HVAC capacity requirement for lower-class zones.
summary: ISO 14644-3 / IEST-RP-CC002 design parameters: unidirectional work-zone velocity 0.2–0.5 m/s (40–100 FPM, not duct velocity) for ISO 3–6; turbulent ACH 10–25 (ISO 8) to 150–300 (ISO 6); HEPA H13 minimum terminal filter; +7.5–12.5 Pa pressure cascade; temperature ±0.5–2°C and humidity ±3–10% RH by class; mixed-class cascade saves ~25% HVAC capital

label: ISO 14644 Initial Qualification and Periodic Requalification Protocols
content: Qualification and requalification protocol per ISO 14644-2:2015. INITIAL QUALIFICATION SEQUENCE (pre-occupancy): (1) Geometric inspection — HEPA/ULPA array coverage, return-air vent placement, room envelope seal integrity; visual pass required before proceeding. (2) Filter integrity (DOP/PAO aerosol photometry per IEST-RP-CC001 §5) — acceptance: <0.01% penetration for HEPA H13; <0.003% for ULPA; conducted by third-party accredited lab, not facility staff. (3) Airflow measurement — unidirectional: measure work-zone face velocity at 0.2–0.5 m/s; turbulent: verify ACH meets design target. (4) Pressure differential across all zone boundaries. (5) Particle count — ISO 14644-1 Annex B sampling: N locations = √(room area in m²), minimum 2 locations; sample volume ≥2 L/min for ≥1 minute per location; 3 consecutive sampling events within ISO class limit required for qualification acceptance. THREE OCCUPANCY BASELINE STATES required per ISO 14644-1 §3: as-built (empty, no equipment), at-rest (equipped, no personnel), operational (occupied, work in progress) — document all three; operational baseline is typically 10–20× above at-rest; operational count must not exceed class limit. STATISTICAL ACCEPTANCE per ISO 14644-1 §B.5: 95% UCL of particle concentration must be within class limit; single location may exceed limit by ≤20% if all-location mean is within limit. PERIODIC REQUALIFICATION SCHEDULE: Filter integrity (DOP) — annually and after any filter replacement; Particle count survey — every 6 months for ISO 6–7; annually for ISO 8; Pressure differential — continuous automated BAS monitoring; Air velocity mapping — every 2 years or after major HVAC maintenance. REQUALIFICATION TRIGGERS (beyond scheduled): filter replacement, ductwork modification, room envelope modification, contamination event exceeding action limit, ISO class change. COMMON PITFALL: Qualifying cleanroom empty then beginning production without establishing occupied baseline — operational particle counts frequently exceed class limit when occupancy effect is not characterized during qualification.
summary: ISO 14644-2 five-step pre-occupancy qualification sequence (geometric→DOP→airflow→pressure→particle count), three required occupancy baseline states, 95% UCL statistical acceptance per ISO 14644-1 §B.5, and periodic requalification schedule (DOP annually; particle survey every 6–12 months by class)

label: Cleanroom Classification Capital Planning and Total Cost of Ownership
content: Capital investment decision framework for ISO 14644 classification selection in LRIP aerospace facilities (~2,000 sq ft reference, 2024 USD). CAPITAL COST BY CLASS: ISO 8 = $150K–300K (basic HEPA, standard HVAC); ISO 7 = $300K–500K (precision HVAC, HEPA terminal, pressure controls); ISO 6 = $450K–700K (high-ACH HVAC, automated monitoring, full gowning infrastructure); ISO 5 = $900K–1.5M (ULPA/H14 supply, redundant HVAC, air shower, precision BAS). ANNUAL OPERATING COST BY CLASS: ISO 8 = $25K–45K; ISO 7 = $60K–90K; ISO 6 = $100K–140K; ISO 5 = $180K–250K. MIXED-CLASS CASCADE BENEFIT: Shared HVAC plant with cascading zones (ISO 8→7→6) reduces capital ~25% vs. independent full-capacity systems for each class; reduces annual operating ~20–30% through lower airflow demand in lower-class zones; recommended approach for LRIP multi-product facilities. TCO DECISION RULE: Calculate (a) incremental classification cost/unit = (capital amortized over facility life ÷ annual units) + (annual operating ÷ annual units); (b) contamination defect cost/unit = defect rate at lower class × (unit rework cost + schedule impact + customer penalty exposure). Upgrade ISO class when (b) exceeds (a). WHEN CUSTOMER-MANDATED: Class is non-negotiable per NADCAP AC7108/AC7117 or contract flow-down — focus optimization on cascade zoning, preventive maintenance scheduling, and filter lifecycle extension rather than class downgrade. FILTER LIFECYCLE COST: Pre-filters (F7/F9) replaced every 3–6 months ($50–200 each); terminal HEPA/ULPA replaced on pressure-drop trigger (typically 3–7 years; $300–2,000 each depending on size and class); DOP retest required after each terminal filter change ($1,500–4,000 per room per third-party test). Budget filter replacement and retesting as recurring operational cost, not capital.
summary: ISO 14644 capital cost ranges by class (ISO 8 $150K–300K through ISO 5 $900K–1.5M); annual operating costs; mixed-class cascade saves ~25% capital and ~20–30% operating; unit-cost decision rule comparing incremental classification cost vs. contamination defect cost; mandatory vs. discretionary classification optimization strategy

label: Predictive Maintenance ML Failure Prediction Pipeline
content: PySpark ML pipeline predicting days-to-failure from high-frequency equipment telemetry. REQUIRED IMPORTS: `from pyspark.sql.functions import year, lead, first, lag, datediff, avg, stddev, count, when`. FEATURE ENGINEERING: rolling 30-day window averages of spindle TIR, thermal drift rate, vibration RMS, coolant ISO cleanliness code, and cumulative operating hours; compute `tir_trend = (current_tir - first_tir_in_window) / 30` and `days_above_threshold = count(when(metric_value > baseline * 1.1, 1)).over(window_30d)`. LABEL: join telemetry to next corrective work order per equipment; label = `datediff(next_corrective_date, measurement_date)` where label > 0 only. MODEL: RandomForestRegressor(numTrees=100, maxDepth=10, minInstancesPerNode=5, seed=42); target RMSE <15 days for tier-1 critical assets. VALIDATION: hold out most recent 6 months; log RMSE via MLflow. INTERVENTION THRESHOLDS: schedule PM when predicted_days_to_failure ≤ 14 (tier-1), 21 (tier-2), 28 (tier-3). RETRAINING: quarterly batch on rolling 3-year history; alert if actual corrective events deviate >20% from predictions in any quarter (model drift signal).
summary: System: PySpark ML failure prediction pipeline — correct imports, rolling feature engineering, label construction from work order history, RandomForest regression, RMSE targets, and quarterly retraining cadence

label: Maintenance Work-Order Scheduling Optimization
content: Constrained greedy scheduling of competing maintenance demands across technician capacity and production availability. PRIORITY RANKING: `Window.orderBy(criticality_tier.asc(), urgency.desc(), predicted_days_to_failure.asc())`; urgency = urgent (<7 days) > high (<14) > medium (<21) > low. CAPACITY ASSIGNMENT: cumulative labor hours via `sum('estimated_hours').over(Window.orderBy('rank').rowsBetween(unboundedPreceding, 0))`; assign to week bucket when cumulative ≤ N × available_tech_hours_per_week. PRODUCTION BLACKOUTS: exclude via LEFT ANTI JOIN against blackout_calendar on date overlap — do not filter in WHERE clause. DATE ARITHMETIC: schedule week start using `date_add(next_day(col('recommended_date'), 'Monday'), (week_num - 1) * 7)` — never call undefined helpers like `next_monday()`. CONCURRENT LIMIT: cap simultaneous equipment downtime at ≤3 assets. BACKLOG RISK COST: for work exceeding 4-week capacity, estimated_emergency_cost = labor_cost × (1.0 + deferred_weeks / 4.0) × criticality_multiplier (2.5× for tier-1, 1.5× for tier-3+). OUTPUT: assigned_week (1–4) or -1 (backlog), scheduled_week_start, urgency, estimated_hours.
summary: System: Greedy work-order scheduling with technician-hour budgets, anti-join production blackout exclusion, criticality priority ranking, and backlog risk-cost escalation formula

label: Spare Parts Inventory EOQ Optimization
content: Economic Order Quantity model minimizing holding cost + stockout cost for critical spares. EOQ FORMULA: EOQ = sqrt(2 × D × S / H); D = annual demand units from 24-month work order part consumption history; S = order cost per purchase ($50 admin + shipping); H = unit_cost × 0.20 annual holding rate. PYSPARK IMPLEMENTATION: use `pyspark.sql.functions.sqrt` in column expressions — `from pyspark.sql.functions import sqrt as spark_sqrt`; Python `math.sqrt` cannot operate on DataFrame columns.
