DURING CONSTRUCTION: (5) Sanitize classified area before uncleared construction workers enter; remove all classified material, equipment, and media — do not assume covering is sufficient if workers will be unattended. (6) Provide cleared escorts for uncleared construction workers at 1:1 ratio if classified-area entry is required; if 24/7 escort is impractical, complete sanitization and suspend classified operations in that area. (7) If IDS is disrupted: notify FSO; implement compensatory measures (security guard rounds, temporary alarms, close-area work suspension until IDS restored). (8) Log all construction worker access in visitor log daily. POST-CONSTRUCTION: (9) Conduct barrier-integrity walk-through before resuming classified operations: verify walls extend to true ceiling, doors meet NISPOM standards, all penetrations sealed. (10) Restore and functionally test IDS and ACS; verify audit-log continuity. (11) For SCIFs: DCSA re-accreditation inspection required before resuming classified operations. CAPITAL SCOPE CHANGES: Major facility expansion adding new classified areas requires FSO coordination and possibly new DCSA facility inspection before occupancy.
summary: Task: Plan and execute construction/modification security for classified areas — develop Construction Security Plan, manage uncleared worker access, maintain compensatory IDS/ACS measures, and restore/inspect barriers before resuming classified operations

label: Coordinate emergency egress with classified-area security lockdown procedures
content: Ensure classified-area security procedures remain compatible with life-safety emergency egress requirements under IFC, NFPA 101, and OSHA standards. CORE PRINCIPLE: Life safety always takes precedence over classified-information security during true emergencies — personnel must be able to evacuate immediately. Security procedures must be designed so emergency egress is never blocked. DESIGN REQUIREMENTS: (1) Emergency exit doors in classified areas must comply with IFC/NFPA 101 (panic hardware, swing direction, max travel distance); security mag-lock systems must fail-safe (unlock on power loss or fire-alarm signal). (2) Verify fail-safe configuration of every mag-lock: connect to fire-alarm panel output and backup UPS; loss of power OR fire-alarm activation must release lock immediately. (3) Do NOT use fail-secure (power-on-to-lock) on egress doors in classified areas — this violates life-safety codes. LOCKDOWN PROCEDURES: During security-threat lockdowns (not fire), differentiated response from fire evacuation: Fire emergency → Evacuate immediately; security concerns are secondary; classified material may be left in place — personnel safety is paramount. Security threat (intruder, unauthorized access) → Shelter-in-place if safe; lock classified-area doors; secure classified material if possible without delaying personal safety; contact security office and law enforcement. CLASSIFIED MATERIAL DURING EVACUATION: Establish pre-arranged sanitization priority list (what gets locked up first if time allows); designate personnel responsible for locking safes/vaults before egress; accept that in true fire emergencies, material security is secondary to personnel egress. POST-EVACUATION: Security personnel account for all classified materials upon re-entry; if any classified material may have been exposed, report to FSO immediately. TRAINING: Annual fire drills include classified-area personnel; drills test ability to evacuate without defeating life-safety systems; drills verify mag-lock fail-safe function.
summary: Task: Design classified-area security procedures to permit unimpeded emergency egress — fail-safe mag-locks, fire-alarm integration, differentiated lockdown vs. evacuation protocols, and classified material prioritization that preserves personnel safety above information security

label: Industrial wastewater pretreatment systems for aerospace manufacturing
content: Design and operate on-site pretreatment systems per EPA 40 CFR Part 403 general pretreatment standards and local POTW limits. OIL-WATER SEPARATORS: Design gravity separators per API Standard 421 ('Design and Operation of Oil-Water Separators') — the primary industry standard for industrial gravity separator sizing; key parameters are rise rate (surface loading, gal/min/ft²), specific gravity differential, and temperature. Corrugated-plate interceptors (CPI/PPI) provide enhanced separation in smaller footprint; select based on flow rate, oil droplet size distribution, and effluent target (<100 mg/L oil/grease typical POTW limit). HEAVY METAL PRECIPITATION (plating and surface treatment): Raise pH to 9–10.5 using NaOH or Ca(OH)₂ to precipitate Cr(OH)₃, Zn(OH)₂, Ni(OH)₂ — each metal has an optimal precipitation pH; hexavalent chromium requires reduction to Cr³⁺ first (sodium bisulfite at pH 2–3) before alkaline precipitation. Follow precipitation with flocculation (alum or polymer) and sedimentation. SUSPENDED SOLIDS REMOVAL (parts washing discharge): Dissolved-air flotation (DAF) or plate settler for emulsified systems; sand filtration for polishing. BOD/COD TREATMENT: Biological treatment (activated sludge) justified only if BOD >500 mg/L consistently; below that, evaluate source controls first. Reference: Metcalf & Eddy 'Wastewater Engineering: Treatment and Resource Recovery,' 5th ed. (2014) — not 'Treatment and Reuse,' which is a common citation error.
summary: System: On-site industrial wastewater pretreatment — oil-water separator design per API Standard 421, heavy-metal precipitation for plating discharge, suspended-solids removal, and BOD treatment selection for aerospace facility pretreatment compliance

label: NPDES permit compliance, discharge monitoring, and upset reporting
content: Manage NPDES permit lifecycle and ongoing compliance per Clean Water Act Section 402 and 40 CFR Part 122. PERMIT APPLICATION AND RENEWAL: Industrial facilities discharging to surface water require individual NPDES permit or coverage under Multi-Sector General Permit (MSGP — EPA 2021 version covers most manufacturing including Sector S for Transportation Equipment Manufacturing/Aerospace). Submit renewal application 180 days before permit expiration; permit cycle is typically 5 years. DISCHARGE MONITORING REPORTS (DMR): Submit monthly or quarterly DMRs to state environmental agency via NetDMR (EPA's electronic reporting system) documenting measured effluent values against permit limits; late or falsified DMRs trigger enforcement actions. EFFLUENT MONITORING: Collect samples per permit-specified method (grab vs. composite) and frequency; chain-of-custody to certified lab; retain records minimum 3 years. PERMIT LIMIT EXCEEDANCE: If monitoring result exceeds a permit limit, report to state environmental agency per permit conditions (typically within 24 hours for significant violations). UPSET PROVISION (40 CFR 122.41(n)): 'Upset' defined as exceptional, unintentional, temporary noncompliance caused by factors beyond reasonable control (equipment failure, natural disaster). To claim upset as affirmative defense: (1) upset occurred, (2) facility was being properly operated, (3) facility notified permitting authority within 24 hours of becoming aware, (4) corrective action taken. Document cause, duration, corrective action in writing. Upset defense does not apply to permit violations caused by operational error, improperly designed systems, or inadequate facility capacity.
summary: System: NPDES permit application, renewal, discharge monitoring report (DMR) submission via NetDMR, permit limit exceedance reporting, and upset-condition affirmative defense procedures per 40 CFR 122.41(n)

label: POTW pretreatment coordination and industrial user permit compliance
content: Coordinate with the local Publicly Owned Treatment Works (POTW) for industrial discharge compliance under EPA 40 CFR Part 403 (General Pretreatment Regulations). CATEGORICAL PRETREATMENT STANDARDS: Aerospace facilities with electroplating or metal finishing operations are subject to categorical standards under 40 CFR Part 413 (Electroplating) or 40 CFR Part 433 (Metal Finishing) — these set technology-based effluent limits for specific pollutants regardless of POTW capacity. INDUSTRIAL USER (IU) PERMIT: POTW issues an IU permit specifying local limits (more stringent than categorical if POTW has specific concerns), monitoring requirements, reporting schedule, and slug-load notification requirements. SIGNIFICANT INDUSTRIAL USER (SIU) DESIGNATION: Triggered if: discharge ≥25,000 gpd average, discharge ≥5% of POTW design flow or loadings, or facility subject to categorical standards — SIU designation means more stringent monitoring (minimum semi-annual compliance sampling), self-monitoring reports, and POTW inspection rights. BASELINE MONITORING REPORT (BMR): Required within 180 days of categorical standard becoming applicable — documents facility compliance status. SLUG-LOAD NOTIFICATION: Facility must notify POTW immediately if any discharge exceeds local slug-load threshold (typically 5× normal flow/concentration) to protect POTW biological treatment. LOCAL LIMITS: POTW may set limits more stringent than categorical standards based on receiving water quality standards; obtain current local limits document from POTW and compare against your characterization data before finalizing treatment system design.
summary: System: POTW pretreatment coordination under 40 CFR Part 403 — categorical standards (Parts 413, 433), Industrial User permit requirements, Significant Industrial User designation thresholds, Baseline Monitoring Reports, and slug-load notification procedures

label: Stormwater management, SWPPP, and industrial runoff control
content: Manage industrial stormwater per NPDES Multi-Sector General Permit (MSGP) and facility-specific Storm Water Pollution Prevention Plan (SWPPP). SWPPP REQUIREMENTS: Document facility drainage map showing all discharge points; identify potential pollutant sources (outdoor material storage, vehicle maintenance areas, loading docks, cooling tower blowdown); describe Best Management Practices (BMPs) preventing stormwater contamination; designate qualified SWPPP personnel and annual site inspection schedule. SECTOR APPLICABILITY: Aerospace manufacturing typically falls under Sector S (Air Transportation / Parts Manufacturing) — confirm applicable sector and benchmark pollutant limits. BMPS FOR AEROSPACE MANUFACTURING: (1) Secondary containment for outdoor bulk-chemical storage (minimum 110% of largest container); (2) Covered or enclosed storage for raw materials exposed to precipitation; (3) Stormwater inlet protection (silt fences, inlet filters) in areas with soil disturbance; (4) Parking lot and loading dock drainage routed through oil-water separator or vegetated swale before discharge; (5) Spill response kits at outdoor storage areas. MONITORING: Benchmark monitoring requires quarterly sampling of discharge from representative outfalls during first three years of permit coverage; analyze for sector-specific parameters (pH, TSS, oil/grease, metals if applicable). ANNUAL REPORTS: Submit annual report to state agency documenting BMP implementation status, any monitoring exceedances, and corrective actions. RAINWATER HARVESTING: Roof drainage is typically the cleanest stormwater source — route to cistern for non-potable facility use (toilet flushing, cooling tower makeup, irrigation); check local municipal code and state water law for harvesting permits.
summary: System: Industrial stormwater NPDES Multi-Sector General Permit (MSGP) compliance, SWPPP development, aerospace-sector BMP requirements, benchmark monitoring, and rainwater harvesting for facility reuse

label: Water distribution system maintenance and backflow prevention testing
content: Maintain facility water distribution system to prevent leakage, water quality degradation, and cross-connection contamination risk. LEAK DETECTION: Conduct water-balance audits monthly (compare master meter input vs. sum of sub-meter consumption; >10% unaccounted loss indicates significant leakage). Use acoustic leak detection equipment (correlators or listening rods) to locate underground leaks without excavation; schedule during nights/weekends when ambient noise is low. Prioritize repair based on loss volume and structural risk. PIPING CONDITION ASSESSMENT: For systems >25 years old, assess corrosion using visual inspection of exposed sections, pipe-wall thickness measurement, and water quality testing (elevated iron/manganese indicates internal corrosion). Schedule replacement of galvanized steel or cast-iron sections showing >20% wall-thickness loss or scale accumulation affecting flow. BACKFLOW PREVENTION DEVICE TESTING: All reduced-pressure principle assemblies (RPPA) and double-check valve assemblies (DCVA) require annual testing by a certified backflow prevention device tester per local plumbing code (typically ASSE 1013 for RPPA, ASSE 1015 for DCVA). Document test results and maintain records for minimum 3 years; submit to local water purveyor if required. Replace failed devices within 30 days; install temporary isolation if device fails catastrophically. PRIORITY LOCATIONS: Cross-connections requiring backflow prevention in aerospace manufacturing: boiler feed water, irrigation systems, cooling systems with chemical treatment, plating bath makeup water, and any hose-bib accessible to chemical areas. WATER SOFTENER AND FILTRATION MAINTENANCE: Replace resin bed per ion exchange capacity monitoring (test hardness breakthrough); regenerate on schedule per water quality testing, not fixed interval; replace sediment filters based on pressure differential (replace at 10 psid increase from clean).
summary: Procedure: Facility water distribution leak detection via water-balance audit and acoustic methods, piping corrosion assessment, annual backflow prevention device testing per ASSE 1013/1015, cross-connection control priorities, and water-treatment equipment maintenance

label: Water conservation strategy and utility incentive program participation
content: Develop and track water conservation program integrating efficiency improvements and municipal utility partnerships. CONSUMPTION BASELINE AND TARGETS: Establish baseline gal/unit-produced (normalize for production volume variation); set annual reduction target (industry benchmark: 10–20% reduction over 3 years through efficiency programs). Track monthly via sub-metering by process area. CONSERVATION MEASURES BY PRIORITY (ROI): (1) Cooling-water recirculation — convert single-pass machining coolant cooling to closed-loop chiller circuit; typical ROI 2–3 years at current water rates; also reduces wastewater volume by 60–80%. (2) Parts washing — implement counter-current cascade rinsing (clean water enters last tank, flows to dirtier tanks); reduces makeup water 30–50% with no capital. (3) Low-flow fixture retrofit — EPACT-compliant faucets (0.5 gpm), toilets (1.28 gpf), urinals (0.5 gpf); payback 1–3 years depending on fixture count and usage frequency. (4) Spray-booth conversion to dry-filter system — eliminates booth wash water entirely; capital ~$50–150K per booth; payback 4–7 years (water savings plus reduced wastewater treatment). UTILITY INCENTIVE PROGRAMS: Most municipal water utilities and water districts offer rebate programs for: metering and sub-metering installation ($100–500 per meter), water-efficient fixture replacement ($25–150 per fixture), cooling-system optimization studies (often free audits), and large industrial conservation projects (negotiate custom rates). Contact your municipal water utility's industrial conservation coordinator; some utilities offer tiered rate structures that reward consumption reduction with lower marginal rates. STORMWATER CREDIT PROGRAMS: Some municipalities offer stormwater fee reductions for facilities that manage runoff on-site through green infrastructure (bioswales, rainwater harvesting) — quantify potential annual fee reduction against capital cost of infrastructure. DOCUMENTATION: Track water reduction in annual sustainability report; utilities often require documentation of qualifying measures to receive rebates; document in CMMS or facility management system.
summary: Procedure: Water consumption baseline setting per unit-produced, conservation ROI prioritization (cooling recirculation, cascade rinsing, low-flow fixtures, dry-filter paint booths), municipal utility rebate program participation, and stormwater credit program coordination

label: ANSI RP-7 Illumination Standards and Measurement Protocol for Manufacturing Zones
content: Illumination targets and measurement methodology per ANSI/IES RP-7 for aerospace manufacturing. ZONE TARGETS (horizontal, at task plane): Precision machining/CNC: 500–750 lux (50–70 fc); Assembly/hand work: 300–500 lux (30–50 fc); Inspection/CMM: 750–1,000 lux (70–100 fc); Warehouse aisles: 200–300 lux; Warehouse shelf face (vertical): 100–150 lux; Office/administrative: 300–500 lux; Corridors/hallways: 150–300 lux; Stairwells (normal power): ≥100 lux. COLOR RENDERING: CRI ≥90 for inspection, color-match, and cosmetic review areas — below CRI 80, color-based defect detection degrades measurably; CRI ≥80 for general assembly; CRI ≥70 for warehouses and non-precision areas. CCT GUIDELINES: 4,000 K neutral white preferred for inspection/assembly (best color discrimination); 3,500–4,000 K general manufacturing; ≤3,500 K office comfort. MEASUREMENT PROTOCOL: Calibrated light meter (±5% accuracy, 0–5,000 lux range); task plane height = 30 in AFF for machining benches, 29 in AFF for desks; 9-point grid per 100 sq ft; record mean, minimum, and max. UNIFORMITY: Min/mean ratio ≥0.80 for manufacturing task areas; ≥0.50 acceptable for storage. GLARE LIMITS: UGR (Unified Glare Rating) ≤16 for precision tasks; ≤19 for offices (CIES 117). FLICKER: Flicker index <3% for task areas (imperceptible); >10% causes eye strain — replace magnetic ballasts or fluorescent fixtures. AUDIT FREQUENCY: Full illumination survey annually and following any major fixture change; verify at initial commissioning.
summary: ANSI RP-7 illumination targets by zone (500–750 lux CNC, 750–1,000 lux inspection, 300–500 lux office); CRI ≥90 for inspection/color-critical tasks; 9-point grid measurement protocol at task-plane height; uniformity min/mean ≥0.80; UGR ≤16 precision/≤19 office; flicker index <3% for task areas

label: NFPA 101 Emergency Lighting and Exit Sign Illumination Requirements
content: Emergency lighting and exit sign requirements per NFPA 101 Life Safety Code. EXIT SIGN ILLUMINATION — THREE CATEGORIES (NFPA 101 §7.10.4): (1) Externally illuminated exit signs (NFPA 101 §7.10.4.2): external light source must deliver ≥50 foot-candles (≈540 lux) on sign face — this is 50 fc, NOT 50 lux; confusing foot-candles with lux is a 10× error. (2) Internally illuminated exit signs including LED (NFPA 101 §7.10.4.3): must be listed to ANSI/UL 924; evaluated on luminance-based listing criteria, not the 50 fc requirement — this is the standard for new LED exit sign installations. (3) Self-luminous/photoluminescent signs: listed to ANSI/UL 924; no electrical power required; require minimum ambient illumination during occupancy to maintain charge. EGRESS ROUTE FLOOR ILLUMINATION (NFPA 101 §7.8.1.3): Minimum initial average ≥1.0 foot-candle (10.8 lux) at floor level along full egress path; minimum at any single point ≥0.1 foot-candle (1.1 lux); full illumination duration ≥90 minutes from emergency trigger. BACKUP POWER (NFPA 101 §7.9): Emergency lighting must remain functional ≥90 minutes during utility power failure; battery backup fixtures must sustain illumination through complete 90-minute test duration; generator-backed systems also governed by NFPA 110 (monthly run tests, annual load test). TESTING (NFPA 101 §7.9.3): Monthly — 30-second functional test; verify illumination and battery charging indicator; log results. Annual — 90-minute full-duration load test; replace battery if level drops before 90 minutes. Self-testing models auto-log both tests — preferred for multi-building facilities. STAIRWELL REQUIREMENT: Stair treads and landings ≥1 fc (10.8 lux) under emergency power; do NOT place stairwell luminaires on occupancy sensors — stairwells require always-on ambient or permanent emergency-circuit illumination.
summary: NFPA 101 exit sign illumination: externally illuminated signs require ≥50 foot-candles (~540 lux) on face — NOT 50 lux; LED internally illuminated signs (most new installations) are UL 924 listed with luminance-based criteria; egress floor illumination ≥1.0 fc (10.8 lux) average, ≥0.1 fc minimum, ≥90-min backup; monthly 30-sec + annual 90-min load test required; stairwells must not be on occupancy sensors

label: Deionized Water System Design and Capacity Planning
content:
DEMAND ASSESSMENT: Install totalizing flowmeters (magnetic, ±2% accuracy) on all process branches; log 12 months at hourly resolution. Size plant at 95th percentile peak demand + 15% safety margin. Typical aerospace facility: 500–800 gal/day baseline, 2,000–3,000 gal/day peak (validation campaigns + spacecraft flushing).

CENTRALIZED VS. POU DECISION: Centralized preferred when peak demand >1,500 gal/day, multiple process locations >50 ft apart, or ultrapure (Type I) water required. POU cartridge systems work for <1,000 gal/day episodic demand at clustered equipment. Hybrid (centralized generation + POU polishing cartridges at critical use points) is often optimal for aerospace.

SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE (Two-Stage Treatment Train):
1. 20 µm sediment pre-filter — protects RO membrane from particulates
2. Activated carbon — removes chlorine before RO membrane
3. Reverse osmosis (RO) — 95–98% TDS reduction; output 50–150 µS/cm at 150–225 psi; Silt Density Index (SDI) of feed water must be <4 (per ASTM D4189) to protect membranes
4. Two-bed ion-exchange (cation/anion) — output <1 µS/cm
5. Mixed-bed polishing — for ultrapure (Type I) applications requiring >10 MΩ·cm
6. 0.2 µm final membrane filter — removes particulates and microorganisms at use points

PIPING MATERIAL: 316L stainless (electropolished, main distribution); PVDF for ultrapure branches (electronics, spacecraft). Copper and uncoated carbon steel are prohibited — both leach ions that raise conductivity and contaminate sensitive processes.

STORAGE TANK: Stainless 304L/316L, sized for 1.5× peak-day demand; vent through 0.2 µm hydrophobic filter; continuous recirculation loop prevents stagnation and biofilm. Annual passivation per ASTM A967 (nitric acid treatment) restores oxide layer. RO upstream extends ion-exchange resin life 3–5× by reducing ion loading before DI beds.
summary: Task: Design and size centralized DI water systems — demand profiling (12-month hourly logging), centralized vs. POU decision criteria (>1,500 gal/day peak favors centralized), two-stage treatment train (sediment→carbon→RO→ion-exchange→polishing→0.2µm filter), material selection (316L SS/PVDF, no copper), storage and distribution design

label: Ion-Exchange Resin Maintenance and Regeneration
content:
REGENERATION TRIGGERS (initiate when ANY condition met):
• Inline conductivity >2 µS/cm at tank outlet (automatic alarm)
• Time-based: every 10–14 days (conservative default for 3,000 gal/day system on 300–400 ppm municipal feed)
• Volume-based: after 40,000–50,000 gallons throughput per 1.5 ft³ resin bed
• Pressure drop rise >5 psi from baseline (indicates bed fouling; inspect pre-filter first before assuming resin failure)

REGENERATION SOP (Two-Bed System):
Step 1 — Backwash (10 min): Upflow 6 gal/min·ft² to expand bed, dislodge fines; discard turbid effluent to waste.
Step 2 — Cation Acid Regeneration (30 min): Downflow 6–8 lb HCl per ft³ resin as ~4 lb/gal solution at 2 gal/min·ft²; effluent pH drops to 0.5–1.5.
Step 3 — Intermediate Rinse (15 min): Upflow DI water at 6 gal/min·ft² until effluent conductivity <50 µS/cm.
Step 4 — Anion Caustic Regeneration (30 min): Downflow 12–16 lb NaOH per ft³ resin as ~4 lb/gal solution at 2 gal/min·ft²; effluent pH rises to 12.5–13.
Step 5 — Final Rinse (20 min): Upflow DI water until conductivity <2 µS/cm AND pH 6.5–7.5.
Step 6 — Return to Service: Verify <1.0 µS/cm at tank outlet before releasing to distribution.
Total cycle: ~3 hours. Waste per cycle: ~300 gal concentrated acid/caustic — classified hazardous corrosive (EPA waste code D002); requires manifest per 40 CFR 262 if facility exceeds small-quantity generator thresholds.

ECONOMICS: Chemicals $3–8/cycle; 50 cycles/year = $150–400/yr chemicals. Labor $50–120/cycle = $2,500–6,000/yr. On-site neutralization (combine spent acid + caustic to reach pH 7, settle solids, discharge to POTW under pretreatment permit) cuts disposal cost ~80%; neutralization skid capital $45,000–75,000, payback typically 6–12 months if disposing >10,000 gal/yr.

RESIN LIFE AND FAILURE INDICATORS: Resin lasts 3–5 years. Replace when regeneration cannot restore <1.0 µS/cm after three consecutive cycles. Pre-filter bypass or failure (ΔP >10 psi) is leading cause of premature resin fouling by particulates — always replace pre-filter cartridge before concluding resin is spent.
summary: Procedure: Ion-exchange resin maintenance — regeneration triggers (conductivity >2 µS/cm, 10–14 day schedule, or 40,000–50,000 gal throughput), two-bed SOP with HCl/NaOH quantities and timing, waste stream as hazardous corrosive D002 requiring 40 CFR 262 manifest, economics ($3–8/cycle), on-site neutralization ROI, and resin failure indicators

label: Water Quality Specifications and Monitoring for Aerospace Manufacturing
content:
APPLICABLE STANDARDS (verified):
• ASTM D1193 — Standard Specification for Reagent Water
  - Type I: Resistivity ≥18 MΩ·cm (conductivity ≤0.056 µS/cm) — ultrapure; electronics and critical analytical
  - Type II: Resistivity ≥1 MΩ·cm (conductivity ≤1 µS/cm) — general laboratory, CMM calibration
  - Type III/IV: Lower purity for general process use (conductivity in 1–20 µS/cm range); consult current standard for exact thresholds
• ASTM D5127 — Standard Guide for Ultra-Pure Water Used in Electronics and Semiconductor Industries
• ISO 3696 — Water for analytical laboratory use (Grade 1: ≤0.1 µS/cm; Grade 2: ≤1 µS/cm; Grade 3: ≤5 µS/cm)
• ASTM A967 — Chemical Passivation Treatments for Stainless Steel Parts (for tanks and distribution piping)
• ASTM D4189 — Silt Density Index (SDI) of Water (RO membrane feed water qualification; SDI <4 required)

PROCESS-SPECIFIC REQUIREMENTS:
• CMM / Coordinate Metrology: ≤1 µS/cm (ASTM D1193 Type II minimum)
• Electronics Cleaning / PCB Wash: ≤0.5 µS/cm; particulates <0.1 µm (approach ASTM D5127)
• Paint Recirculation Water: ≤2 µS/cm; Cl⁻ <0.1 ppm to prevent coating adhesion failure
• Parts Washing / Surface Treatment: ≤5 µS/cm (ASTM D1193 Type III range)
• Spacecraft Fluid System Flushing: ≥10 MΩ·cm (Type I); CRITICAL — verify against program-specific system specification and contract requirements; do not substitute a generic industry spec; lab verification by ISO 17025-accredited laboratory is typically required

MONITORING PROGRAM:
• Continuous (inline): Conductivity cells at (1) post-RO, (2) post-DI tank, (3) distribution loop return, (4) POU filter outlets; alarm at >2 µS/cm at tank outlet, >3 µS/cm at loop return
• Monthly lab: pH, conductivity, total hardness, ion chromatography (Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, Na⁺)
• Quarterly microbial: Heterotrophic plate count per Standard Methods for Examination of Water and Wastewater (APHA/AWWA/WEF), Method 9215; acceptance criterion <100 CFU/mL
• Annual ICP-OES: trace metals (Fe, Cu, Zn, Pb) to verify no leaching from distribution piping

CRITICAL PITFALLS: (1) Conductivity and resistivity are inversely related — 1 µS/cm = 1 MΩ·cm; temperature-compensate all inline readings to 25°C. (2) ASTM D1193 types are frequently misapplied; confirm type against actual process failure mode, not just generic 'purity level.' (3) Spacecraft flushing water specs are program-specific — always reference the applicable system specification, never assume a generic MIL-SPEC covers the requirement.
summary: Reference: Aerospace manufacturing water quality standards — ASTM D1193 types (Type I ≤0.056 µS/cm, Type II ≤1 µS/cm), ASTM D5127 for electronics, ISO 3696 grades, ASTM D4189 for RO feed (SDI<4), process-specific requirements (CMM ≤1, electronics ≤0.5, paint ≤2, parts wash ≤5 µS/cm), APHA Method 9215 for microbial testing, and critical warnings against misapplying generic specs to spacecraft flushing
